topics.title: The Submersible System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Submersible System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Submersible (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *submersible* is a watercraft or underwater vehicle that is designed to operate submerged in the water column, supported entirely by the surrounding hydrostatic pressure of the fluid medium, and is independent of surface support for its routine submerged operations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Course Code: **OENST723**), the term **"submersible system"** refers not just to the pressure hull and its mechanical body, but to the **integrated engineering assembly** comprising the following subsystems:

1. **Structural Hull** — The pressure vessel that resists external hydrostatic compression.
2. **Propulsion and Maneuvering Unit** — Thrusters and control surfaces that enable 3-DOF/4-DOF motion.
3. **Ballast and Trim System** — Regulates buoyancy and longitudinal/c transverse attitude.
4. **Power and Energy Storage** — Batteries, fuel cells, or surface-supplied umbilicals.
5. **Command, Control, and Communication (C³) Suite** — Sensors, telemetry, and pilot interface.
6. **Life Support / Payload Interface** — Instruments, manipulators, or crew habitat (for manned systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Boards):** A **submarine** operates primarily on the surface and submerges only as required, while a **submersible** is *purpose-designed* to spend the majority of its operational life fully submerged.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a sealed **steel water bottle** underwater in a swimming pool. The water pushes in from all sides equally — that force is **hydrostatic pressure**. Now imagine the bottle is not just holding air, but is actually a tiny submarine carrying a camera, a small propeller, and ballast tanks. That, in essence, is a **submersible**.

A more precise engineering analogy:

| Real-World Object | Submersible Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Astronaut spacecraft capsule | Pressure hull / crew sphere | Protects occupants from hostile external pressure |
| Drone quadcopter | ROV/AUV thruster array | Provides controlled motion in 6 degrees of freedom |
| Hot-air balloon ballast bags | Variable ballast tanks | Controls depth by varying overall density |
| Computer data center | Power + C³ rack | Runs sensors, navigation, and mission logic |

## 1.3 Operational Metrics & Standard Constants

The following constants and benchmark metrics recur throughout KTU examinations for this topic:

- **Standard seawater density ($\rho_{sw}$):** **1025 kg/m³**
- **Freshwater density ($\rho_{fw}$):** **1000 kg/m³**
- **Atmospheric pressure (sea level):** **101.325 kPa**
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** **≈ 10.05 kPa per metre of depth** (seawater) or **9.81 kPa/m** (freshwater)
- **Speed of sound in seawater:** **≈ 1500 m/s**
- **Earth's gravity:** **9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **non-negotiable** in KTU board numericals. Memorize them on Day 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Submersible System

The submersible is best understood as a **layered architecture** where each subsystem solves one specific physical problem encountered in the underwater environment.

### 2.1.1 The Pressure Hull Subsystem
- **Problem solved:** External hydrostatic crushing force.
- **Operating principle:** A sealed, geometrically optimized (sphere > cylinder > cone) shell converts external pressure into **membrane (tensile) stress** rather than bending stress.
- **Why a sphere?** For a given internal volume and wall material, a sphere provides the **minimum surface area** for a given enclosed volume, minimizing both weight and the area exposed to crushing pressure.

### 2.1.2 The Buoyancy–Ballast Subsystem
- **Problem solved:** Gravity pulls the vehicle down; we need to control its *net vertical force*.
- **Operating principle:** Archimedes' principle. The **Weight (W)** is balanced against the **Buoyant Force (B)**.

If $B > W$ → vehicle rises (positive buoyancy).
If $B < W$ → vehicle sinks (negative buoyancy).
If $B = W$ → vehicle is *neutrally buoyant* (hovers).

### 2.1.3 The Propulsion Subsystem
- **Problem solved:** Overcoming hydrodynamic drag to translate through water.
- **Operating principle:** Newton's third law. Propellers, water jets, or thrusters expel water rearward; the reaction propels the vehicle forward.
- **Why is underwater propulsion so power-hungry?** Because water is **≈ 800 times denser than air** at sea level — drag scales with fluid density.

### 2.1.4 The Control Subsystem
- **Problem solved:** Steering and depth-keeping.
- **Operating principle:** Hydroplanes (planes/dive planes) and rudders generate hydrodynamic lift forces when the vehicle is moving, allowing depth and heading changes.

### 2.1.5 The Power Subsystem
- **Problem solved:** Sustained energy for propulsion, sensors, and life support.
- **Operating principle:** Onboard batteries (Li-ion, lead-acid), fuel cells (H₂–O₂), or shore-supplied power via an umbilical cable.

### 2.1.6 The C³ (Command, Control, Communication) Subsystem
- **Problem solved:** Knowing where you are, what is around you, and relaying that information.
- **Operating principle:** A fusion of sensors (sonar, INS, DVL, depth sensor, cameras) feeding into a central computer, with acoustic or fiber-optic data links to the surface.

## 2.2 Classification of Submersibles

A frequent KTU short-answer question asks you to *classify* submersibles. The two master categories are:

### A. Manned Submersibles
Carry human occupants inside a pressure hull. Examples: *Alvin (USA), Nautile (France, 6000 m rated), Jiaolong (China), Deepsea Challenger.*

### B. Unmanned Submersibles
Two principal sub-types:

- **ROV — Remotely Operated Vehicle:** Tethered to a surface ship via an umbilical that transmits power and data. Pilot controls from the surface. No onboard autonomy required for routine ops.
- **AUV — Autonomous Underwater Vehicle:** No tether. Runs pre-programmed missions using onboard batteries and intelligent navigation (INS/Doppler-aided). Surface retrieval or acoustic communication at mission end.

A third emerging class worth mentioning:

- **Hybrid AUV/ROV (HROV):** Like *Nereus* (WHOI), can operate tethered or untethered.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Submersibles are indispensable across:

- **Oceanographic research** — mapping seafloor, sampling hydrothermal vents.
- **Offshore oil & gas** — pipeline inspection, wellhead intervention.
- **Defense & security** — mine countermeasures, intelligence gathering.
- **Marine archaeology** — wreck documentation (e.g., *Titanic* surveys).
- **Search and Recovery (SAR)** — black box retrieval, downed aircraft.
- **Cables & telecommunications** — submarine cable laying and repair.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{abs}(z) = P_{atm} + \rho g z$ | Absolute hydrostatic pressure at depth $z$ | $P_{atm}$ = 101.325 kPa; $g$ = 9.81 m/s² |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta z$ | Differential pressure between two depths | kPa or Pa |
| 3 | $B = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{displaced}$ | Archimedes buoyant force | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $W = m \, g$ | Gravitational weight of vehicle | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \, r}{t}$ | Thin-walled cylinder hoop stress | Pa; valid if $t/r < 0.1$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{membrane} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 t}$ | Thin-walled sphere membrane stress | Pa |
| 7 | $SF_{buoyancy} = B - W$ | Net vertical force (positive = rise) | Newtons (N) |
| 8 | $\gamma_{submerged} = \gamma_{material} - \rho_{fluid} g$ | Effective (submerged) weight density | N/m³ |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flow past hull | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} C_D A$ | Hydrodynamic drag force (quadratic drag) | Newtons (N) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown safeguard applied:** In all formula-table entries above, the absolute-value or "divided by" symbol is rendered as $\rho g z$ or $\rho v L / \mu$ — never as `|...|` — to prevent breakage of the markdown table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth

We begin from the fundamental hydrostatic equilibrium of an incompressible fluid.

**Step 1.** Consider an infinitesimal fluid column of height $dz$ and cross-section $A$ at depth $z$. The pressure at the bottom of the slice exceeds the pressure at the top by the weight of the slice per unit area.

$$
\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g
$$

**Step 2.** Integrate from the free surface ($z = 0$, $P = P_{atm}$) down to depth $z$.

$$
\int_{P_{atm}}^{P(z)} dP = -\rho g \int_{0}^{z} dz
$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate both integrals.

$$
P(z) - P_{atm} = -\rho g z
$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to give the classical KTU formula.

$$
P_{abs}(z) = P_{atm} + \rho g z
$$

**Conversion logic:** $P_{atm}$ is added because pressure at the surface is *not* zero — it is the weight of the entire atmosphere above. The negative sign in Step 1 reflects that pressure *increases* as $z$ *increases* downward.

**Numerical sanity check.** At $z = 1000$ m in seawater:

$$
P_{abs} = 101{,}325 \; \text{Pa} + (1025)(9.81)(1000)
$$

$$
P_{abs} \approx 101{,}325 + 10{,}055{,}250 \approx 10.16 \; \text{MPa}
$$

This matches the **rule-of-thumb** value: ≈ 100 atm at 1000 m.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Buoyancy Force and Condition for Neutral Buoyancy

**Step 1.** Buoyancy from Archimedes (force upward).

$$
B = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V
$$

**Step 2.** Weight of the vehicle (force downward).

$$
W = m \, g
$$

**Step 3.** Net vertical force.

$$
F_{net} = B - W = (\rho_{fluid} V - m) g
$$

**Step 4.** Neutral buoyancy condition ($F_{net} = 0$).

$$
\rho_{fluid} \, V = m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_{vehicle,avg} = \rho_{fluid}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A vehicle whose **average density equals that of the surrounding fluid** will hover indefinitely without any thrust — this is the holy grail of submersible design because it minimizes energy expenditure for station-keeping.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Membrane Stress in a Thin Spherical Pressure Hull

This is a KTU board-favourite derivation. We isolate a hemisphere and apply equilibrium.

**Step 1.** Internal pressure $P_i$, external $P_e$. Net radial pressure pushing outward on the hemispherical cap:

$$
\Delta P = P_i - P_e
$$

**Step 2.** The projected area of the hemisphere is $A = \pi r^{2}$. The total force trying to split the sphere along its equator is therefore:

$$
F_{axial} = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** This force is resisted by the membrane tension $T$ (force per unit length) acting around the circumference of the cut ($2 \pi r$):

$$
T = \frac{F_{axial}}{2 \pi r} = \frac{\Delta P \, \pi r^{2}}{2 \pi r} = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2}
$$

**Step 4.** If the wall thickness is $t$, the membrane stress is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{T}{t} = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2 t}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is why spherical pressure hulls are favoured — the **factor of 2** in the denominator means a sphere can withstand **twice the pressure** of a comparable cylinder (where $\sigma = \Delta P \, r / t$) for the same $r$ and $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thin-wall assumption requires $t/r \le 0.1$ (or more strictly, $t/r \le 1/20$). For thick-walled hulls, the Lamé equations must be used instead.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Subsystem Exists

| Subsystem | Physical Threat Addressed | Governing Equation | Failure Mode If Absent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure hull | Hydrostatic crushing | $\sigma = \Delta P r / 2t$ | Implosion |
| Ballast system | Gravity-induced sinking | $F_{net} = B - W$ | Loss of depth control |
| Propulsion | Hydrodynamic drag | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} C_D A$ | Vehicle stranded |
| Control surfaces | Uncontrolled motion | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} C_L A$ | Cannot steer / hover |
| Power storage | Energy depletion | $E = \int P_{draw} \, dt$ | Mission abort |
| C³ suite | Sensory deprivation | Sonar equation (active/passive) | Blind operation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Submersible System

The following Mermaid block illustrates how information, power, and fluid mass flow between subsystems inside a typical AUV/ROV-class submersible.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph ENERGY["ENERGY DOMAIN"]
        nodePSU["Power Source: Battery Pack or Surface Umbilical"]
        nodeDC["DC Bus: 24V / 48V Distribution"]
    end

    subgraph STRUCTURAL["STRUCTURAL DOMAIN"]
        nodeHull["Pressure Hull: Titanium or Carbon Composite Sphere"]
        nodeFair["Fairing: Outer Hydrodynamic Shell"]
    end

    subgraph FLUID["FLUID DOMAIN"]
        nodeB1["Main Ballast Tank: Variable Volume"]
        nodeTrim["Trim Tank: Fore and Aft"]
        nodeComp["Compensator: Oil-filled Bladder"]
    end

    subgraph MOTION["MOTION DOMAIN"]
        nodeThr["Thruster Array: 4 to 7 units"]
        nodePlane["Control Planes: Rudder and Elevator"]
        nodeIMU["Inertial Measurement Unit IMU"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL DOMAIN"]
        nodeCPU["Mission Computer and Flight Controller"]
        nodeSensor["Sensor Stack: DVL Sonar CTD Camera"]
        nodeComms["Acoustic Modem and Fiber Optic Link"]
    end

    nodePSU --> nodeDC
    nodeDC --> nodeThr
    nodeDC --> nodeCPU
    nodeDC --> nodeSensor

    nodeHull --- nodeFair
    nodeB1 --> nodeCPU
    nodeTrim --> nodeCPU
    nodeComp --> nodeHull

    nodeCPU --> nodeThr
    nodeCPU --> nodePlane
    nodeIMU --> nodeCPU
    nodeSensor --> nodeCPU
    nodeComms --> nodeCPU

    nodeThr --> nodeHull
    nodePlane --> nodeFair
```

## 4.2 Mission Timeline of a Typical Submersible Dive

```mermaid
graph LR
    phaseA["Surface Launch and Checks"]
    phaseB["Descent Phase"]
    phaseC["Bottom Approach"]
    phaseD["Mission Execution"]
    phaseE["Ascent Phase"]
    phaseF["Surface Recovery"]

    phaseA --> phaseB
    phaseB --> phaseC
    phaseC --> phaseD
    phaseD --> phaseE
    phaseE --> phaseF

    phaseA -. "Pre-dive ballast trim" .- phaseA
    phaseD -. "Hover, sample, image, map" .- phaseD
    phaseE -. "Drop ballast, positive buoyancy" .- phaseE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Rectangular nodes represent **hardware subsystems or operational phases**. Solid arrows show energy, control, or data flow. Dotted arrows show feedback or recurring actions during that phase.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**
*Define the term "submersible". How is it functionally different from a "submarine"?*

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

A *submersible* is a watercraft purpose-designed to operate underwater, fully supported by the ambient hydrostatic pressure and (for untethered types) independent of surface support. A *submarine* is primarily a surface vessel that submerges temporarily; it has surface propulsion, larger free-flooding superstructures, and uses snort masts when running submerged near the surface. Submersibles are typically smaller, deep-rated, and mission-specific.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`**
*List any six major subsystems of a submersible system and state the function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pressure hull** — resists external hydrostatic pressure.
2. **Ballast system** — controls net buoyancy for depth change.
3. **Propulsion** — overcomes hydrodynamic drag.
4. **Control surfaces** — generate lift for steering.
5. **Power subsystem** — supplies electrical energy.
6. **C³ suite** — provides navigation, sensing, and data telemetry.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on Module 1 carry **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Answer both parts and present labelled diagrams where relevant to score full marks.

### Question A — Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the various subsystems of a typical deep-rated submersible. Explain how the subsystems interact during a routine dive cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the membrane stress in a thin-walled spherical pressure hull. A titanium sphere of inner radius 1.1 m and wall thickness 50 mm is rated for an external pressure of 60 MPa (internal atmospheric). Calculate the maximum hoop stress and the factor of safety if the yield strength of titanium is 880 MPa.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram (drawn freehand or using Mermaid in your answer sheet):**

```
                ┌────────────────────────┐
                │   C³ SUBSYSTEM         │
                │  (Sonar, INS, Camera)  │
                └──────────┬─────────────┘
                           │ control signals
                           ▼
   POWER ───► THRUSTERS ◄──── CONTROL SURFACES ───► PRESSURE HULL
   BUS          ▲                  ▲                    │
                │                  │                    │
            MOTORS            HYDROPLANES            CREW /
                                                      PAYLOAD
                ▲
                │
        BUOYANCY / BALLAST SYSTEM
```

**Operating sequence during a dive:**
1. **Surface phase:** All ballast tanks empty, vehicle is positively buoyant; pilot initiates systems check.
2. **Descent:** Main ballast tank (MBT) flooded with seawater → negative buoyancy → vehicle sinks.
3. **Bottom phase:** MBT partially blown with air from a high-pressure air bottle (HPAB) or, for modern subs, a variable-ballast pump shifts water between internal and external tanks. Vehicle achieves neutral buoyancy.
4. **Ascent:** MBT fully blown → positive buoyancy → vehicle rises.
5. **Surface recovery:** Pilot retrieves the vehicle; data is downloaded; batteries are recharged.

**Key point to write in the exam:** All four phases are coordinated through the **flight controller**, which receives depth feedback from the pressure sensor and adjusts ballast valves / thruster RPM accordingly.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:** (write the derivation as in Section 3.3 above, terminating at)

$$
\sigma = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2 t}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

Given:
- Inner radius $r = 1.1$ m
- Wall thickness $t = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- External pressure $P_e = 60$ MPa
- Internal pressure $P_i = 0.101$ MPa (atmospheric, negligible compared to 60 MPa)
- Yield strength $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa

**Step 1.** Net pressure differential.

$$
\Delta P = P_e - P_i \approx 60 - 0.101 \approx 59.9 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2.** Membrane stress.

$$
\sigma = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 t} = \frac{59.9 \times 10^{6} \times 1.1}{2 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{65.89 \times 10^{6}}{0.1} = 658.9 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3.** Factor of safety.

$$
SF = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma} = \frac{880}{658.9} \approx 1.336
$$

**Result:** The hull operates at a factor of safety of approximately **1.34**, which is acceptable for a one-time dive pressure vessel (typical KTU/FOS design range is 1.25–1.5 for yield, with an additional 1.5–2.0× for ultimate strength).

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of submersibles. Compare ROVs and AUVs in terms of endurance, depth capability, payload, communication, and cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A research submersible displaces 12 m³ of seawater. Its total mass, including crew and payload, is 11,800 kg. Determine: (i) its weight in air, (ii) the buoyant force when fully submerged in seawater ($\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³), and (iii) the additional mass of seawater it must take into its ballast tanks to just begin descending from the surface.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification tree:**

```
Submersibles
├── Manned
│   ├── Bathyscaphe (free-flooding + buoyancy sphere)
│   ├── Bathysphere (sphere only)
│   └── Deep-diving submarines (e.g., DSV Limiting Factor)
└── Unmanned
    ├── ROV (tethered, surface-controlled)
    ├── AUV (autonomous, no tether)
    └── Hybrid ROV/AUV (HROV)
```

**Comparison Table — Write in exam:**

| Parameter | ROV | AUV |
|---|---|---|
| Endurance | Unlimited (surface-powered) | Limited by onboard batteries (typ. 6–30 h) |
| Depth capability | Up to 11,000 m (e.g., *Kaiko*, Japan) | Up to 11,000 m (e.g., *Autosub*, Boaty McBoatface variants) |
| Payload | Cameras, manipulators, samplers | Science sensors (CTD, sonar, camera) |
| Communication | Real-time via umbilical | Acoustic modem (low bandwidth, delayed) |
| Cost | High (surface ship + crew + cable) | Lower operating cost, but expensive unit |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Displaced volume $V = 12$ m³
- Total mass $m = 11{,}800$ kg
- Seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Weight in air.**

$$
W = m g = 11{,}800 \times 9.81 = 115{,}758 \; \text{N} \approx 115.76 \; \text{kN}
$$

**(ii) Buoyant force when fully submerged.**

$$
B = \rho_{sw} V g = 1025 \times 12 \times 9.81
$$

$$
B = 120{,}663 \; \text{N} \approx 120.66 \; \text{kN}
$$

**(iii) Additional mass to begin descending.**

The vehicle is currently **positively buoyant** ($B > W$). To descend, we must flood the ballast tank with just enough seawater so that $W_{new} \ge B$.

Required **additional mass** $\Delta m$ satisfies:

$$
(W + \Delta m \, g) = B
$$

$$
\Delta m = \frac{B - W}{g} = \frac{120{,}663 - 115{,}758}{9.81}
$$

$$
\Delta m = \frac{4905}{9.81} \approx 500 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Result:** The submersible must flood approximately **500 kg of seawater** into its ballast tank to become just negatively buoyant and begin its descent.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up "submarine" and "submersible"** — Examiners deduct 1 mark every time these are used interchangeably. Memorize the distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure** in hydrostatic calculations. $P_{abs} \ne \rho g z$; it is $P_{atm} + \rho g z$.
> 3. **Wrong factor in the sphere-vs-cylinder stress formula.** Cylinder: $P r / t$. Sphere: $P r / 2 t$. Confusing these gives the wrong safety factor.
> 4. **Stating "buoyancy = weight" for neutral hovering** without explaining that it is the *average density equality* that matters.
> 5. **Using freshwater density (1000)** where seawater (1025) was specified, or vice versa. Read the problem carefully.
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always carry units explicitly through each step and box the final numeric answer with units.
> 7. **Forgetting to label diagrams.** Unlabelled diagrams fetch zero marks in KTU 2024 Scheme — every block needs a label.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A submersible is a purpose-built underwater vehicle supported by hydrostatic pressure, **not** a submarine.
- **Six core subsystems:** Pressure hull, ballast, propulsion, control, power, C³.
- **Three classes:** Manned, ROV, AUV (plus hybrid).
- **Hydrostatic pressure equation:** $P_{abs}(z) = P_{atm} + \rho g z$ — the single most important formula.
- **Pressure gradient:** ≈ **10 kPa/m in seawater**, ≈ **9.81 kPa/m in freshwater**.
- **Archimedes' buoyancy:** $B = \rho_{fluid} g V$ — must be compared with $W = mg$ to determine net vertical force.
- **Neutral buoyancy condition:** $\rho_{vehicle,avg} = \rho_{fluid}$.
- **Sphere membrane stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 t}$ — half that of a cylinder of equal radius and thickness.
- **Thin-wall validity:** $t/r \le 0.1$.
- **Re-use the constants:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa; $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $c_{sw} \approx 1500$ m/s.
- **Pressure hull material trends:** Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) for deep diving, HY-80/100 steel for moderate depths, carbon composite for lightweight AUVs.
- **Always label diagrams** in KTU exams. Always show units. Always state assumptions.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Strategy:** Memorize the **10 formula sheet rows** in Section 2.4 along with the six subsystems in Section 2.1. That single page, if reproduced in the exam, fetches **at least 6–7 marks** by itself across any Module 1 question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Design of Manned Submersibles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Design of Manned Submersibles # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Manned Submersible** is a free-swimming, self-propelled, watertight pressure vessel designed to transport one or more human occupants safely to a preselected operational depth in the ocean and return them to the surface, while maintaining an internal habitable environment at approximately **1 atmosphere (101.325 kPa)** of pressure. As per **SNAME T&R Bulletin 5-5 (Submersible Vehicle Systems Design)** and **KTU 2024 Ocean Engineering syllabus (OENST723)**, a manned submersible is fundamentally characterized by the **Pressure Hull (PH)** — a structural shell engineered to resist external hydrostatic compression while providing a life-sustaining volume for the human crew. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The "Basic Design" phase is a *feasibility and concept-stage* exercise. It involves translating mission requirements (depth, payload, endurance, observers) into a **first-pass engineering layout** with calculated estimates of weight, buoyancy, stability, and hull scantlings — NOT a detailed manufacturing drawing. ## 1.2 Classification of Manned Submersibles Manned submersibles are classified along three orthogonal design axes: ### (a) By Hull Architecture | Type | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **Single-Hull (Wet-Dry)** | One pressure hull encloses the crew; outer fairings are non-structural | *Pisces IV, Johnson Sea Link* | | **Double-Hull** | Outer fairing is structural; flooded ballast tanks surround inner pressure hull | *Aluminaut* | | **Bathyscaph** | Large float (filled with low-density fluid like gasoline) supports a small pressure sphere | *Trieste, Trieste II* | | **Submersible with Lock-Out Trunk (LOT)** | Crew can exit underwater into the ambient sea through a trunk and habitat | *DSV Alvin (post-2014)* | ### (b) By Power Plant - **Battery-Electric (BE):** Onboard lead-acid / Li-ion / NiMH batteries drive DC motors. Most common. Endurance 4–12 h. - **Nuclear:** Nuclear steam generator drives propulsion. Used in research submersibles for extreme endurance. Example: *NR-1 (US Navy)*. ### (c) By Mission Profile - **Shallow Water (≤ 300 m):** Scientific observation, archaeology, recreational. - **Medium Depth (300–2000 m):** Continental shelf work, marine biology. - **Deep Submergence (2000–6500 m):** Mid-ocean ridges, abyssal research. - **Full Ocean Depth (≥ 6500 m):** Hadal zone exploration. Example: *DSV Limiting Factor* (10,925 m). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Egg in a Wine Glass" Imagine placing a raw egg at the bottom of a deep swimming pool. - **The egg white** represents the **external ocean** — it pushes inward with crushing hydrostatic pressure (≈ 1 atmosphere for every **10.06 m** of depth). - **The eggshell** represents the **Pressure Hull** — its sole job is to keep the egg's interior at 1 atm so the "yolk" (the human crew) is not crushed. - **The egg's volume** represents the **Habitable Volume (V_hab)** — limited because the hull must be thicker (and heavier) the deeper you go, leaving less interior space. - **The orientation of the egg** (pointed end down or sideways) represents the **Hull Form**, which determines stability. > [!NOTE] > This is why modern deep submersibles like *Limiting Factor* use a **titanium sphere** — titanium has the best strength-to-weight ratio among metals at 1000 MPa-class strength, allowing the sphere to be the *minimum* weight while withstanding ~110 MPa (≈ 11,000 m depth). ## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Basic Design A KTU-style basic design is built upon four interdependent engineering pillars: 1. **Hull Geometry & Material** — Determines pressure resistance and internal volume. 2. **Weight & Buoyancy Balance** — Determines whether the vehicle floats, dives, or surfaces. 3. **Static Stability** — Determines whether it returns upright after being tilted. 4. **Human Factors** — Determines survivability of the crew (atmosphere, temperature, CO₂ scrubbing, ergonomics). > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "Hull Buoyancy Stability Humans" → H-B-S-H** > Always mention these four pillars when asked to list the design considerations of a manned submersible. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The Iterative Loop A manned submersible's basic design is *never* linear; it is an iterative convergence loop. The sequence is: 1. **Mission Statement → Depth (D), Payload (W_p), Crew (n), Endurance (t).** 2. **Hull Form Selection** — Sphere (uniform stress) vs Cylinder (more volume per material) vs Hybrid. 3. **Material Selection** — Steel (cheap, heavy), Titanium (mid-cost, strong), Acrylic (transparent, shallow only). 4. **Pressure Hull Scantling Calculation** — Wall thickness (t). 5. **Weight Estimation** — Hull weight + payload + systems + ballast. 6. **Buoyancy Calculation** — Reserve buoyancy must be positive at surface. 7. **Stability Verification** — Metacentric height must exceed minimum threshold. 8. **Loop until convergence.** ## 2.2 Hydrostatic Environment Ambient ocean pressure at depth $D$ (in meters) is given by: $$ P_{ext} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot D $$ Where the standard seawater density is **$\rho_{sw} = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$** and gravitational acceleration **$g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$**. The pressure differential the hull must resist is: $$ \Delta P = P_{ext} - P_{int} \approx \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot D \quad (\text{since } P_{int} = P_{atm}) $$ ## 2.3 Thin-Wall Pressure Hull Theory For design depths, hulls qualify as **thin-walled pressure vessels** if $t/R \leq 1/10$. KTU problems typically assume this regime. ### 2.3.1 Spherical Pressure Hull A sphere is the optimal hull geometry because the biaxial stress state is *uniform* — there are no stress concentrations. $$ \sigma_{hoop} = \sigma_{meridional} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t} $$ Required wall thickness for a sphere: $$ t_{sphere} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y} / \text{FS}} + c_a $$ Where $c_a$ is the **corrosion allowance** (typically 1.5–3 mm for steel, near-zero for titanium in seawater). ### 2.3.2 Cylindrical Pressure Hull (with Hemispherical Endcaps) The cylinder is preferred when *maximum internal volume* is desired for a given depth. $$ \sigma_{hoop} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{t}, \qquad \sigma_{long} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t} $$ Required wall thickness: $$ t_{cyl} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{\sigma_{y} / \text{FS}} + c_a $$ Notice $t_{cyl} = 2 \cdot t_{sphere}$ — that is, **a cylinder needs double the wall thickness of a sphere** for the same radius and depth, which is why most deep submersibles use spheres for the crew compartment. ## 2.4 Factor of Safety (FS) — Submersible Practice Unlike surface ships (FS ≈ 3–5 for steel), manned submersibles use **elevated** factors of safety because: - **Human life is at risk** (cannot evacuate mid-ocean). - **Inspection is difficult** after deployment. - **Material degradation** (fatigue, corrosion) is hidden. | Depth Category | Typical FS on Yield | |----------------|---------------------| | 0–300 m (Shallow) | 2.0 – 3.0 | | 300–2000 m (Medium) | 2.5 – 3.5 | | 2000–6500 m (Deep) | 3.0 – 4.0 | | > 6500 m (Hadal) | 4.0 – 5.0 | ## 2.5 Buoyancy & Weight Balance **Archimedes' Principle** (the defining equation of any submersible): $$ F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} $$ **Reserve Buoyancy** at the surface: $$ F_{RB} = F_B - W_{total} $$ For surface floatation, $F_{RB} > 0$ (typically **5–15 %** of displacement). To dive, variable ballast (seawater flooding) is added so that $W_{total} > F_B$. $$ \Delta W_{ballast} = W_{total,dive} - W_{total,surface} = F_B \cdot \eta_{dive} $$ A typical **dive margin** $\eta_{dive} = 1–3\%$ of displacement is used to ensure a positive sinking rate (~ 0.5 m/s) regardless of small leaks or gas evolution. ## 2.6 Static Stability — The Metacenter Concept For a submersible tilted by a small angle $\phi$, restoring moment is generated when the **center of buoyancy (B)** shifts laterally relative to the **center of gravity (G)**. $$ \text{Metacentric Height (GM)} = KB + BM - KG $$ For a manned submersible (which is narrow and tall), the **minimum acceptable GM ≈ 0.05 m to 0.15 m** — too high causes jerky rolling; too low risks capsizing. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Note | |---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------| | 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $\Delta P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot D$ | Pa | $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | | 2 | Spherical Hoop Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 t}$ | Pa | Uniform biaxial | | 3 | Cylindrical Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{t}$ | Pa | Doubles sphere's | | 4 | Cylindrical Long. Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 t}$ | Pa | Half hoop | | 5 | Sphere Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R \cdot FS}{2 \sigma_y} + c_a$ | m | Add $c_a = 1.5$–$3$ mm | | 6 | Cylinder Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R \cdot FS}{\sigma_y} + c_a$ | m | — | | 7 | Buoyancy Force | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$ | N | Archimedes | | 8 | Reserve Buoyancy | $F_{RB} = F_B - W$ | N | > 0 at surface | | 9 | Dive Margin | $\eta = \Delta W / F_B$ | — | 1–3 % | | 10 | GM (Stability) | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | m | ≥ 0.05 m | | 11 | Factor of Safety | $FS = \sigma_y / \sigma_{allow}$ | — | 2.0–5.0 (deep) | | 12 | Collapse Buckling | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^2.5}{(L/D)}$ | Pa | Cyl. ring-stiffened | > [!NOTE] > Always use **$\text{mid}$ or $\\vert$** (not the bare pipe character) for absolute-value notation to keep the markdown table renderer safe. ## 2.8 Engineering Utility — Where This Shows Up - **Offshore Oil & Gas** — ROVs and crewed submersibles inspect subsea manifolds at 2000–3000 m. - **Marine Biology** — Documenting hadal trench fauna (e.g., Mariana snailfish at 8000 m). - **Naval Archaeology** — Mapping and excavating shipwrecks (e.g., *Titanic* by *Alvin* and *Mir*). - **Mineral Prospecting** — Seafloor massive sulfide (SMS) exploration at 2500–4000 m. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — Spherical Pressure Hull Wall Thickness **Given:** - Operating depth $D = 2000$ m - Inner radius $R_i = 1.1$ m - Material: HY-80 steel with $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa - Factor of Safety $FS = 3.0$ - Corrosion allowance $c_a = 2.5$ mm **Find:** Required wall thickness $t$ of a spherical pressure hull. ### Step 1 — External Hydrostatic Pressure $$ \Delta P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot D = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2000 $$ We compute term by term: $$ 1025 \cdot 9.81 = 10055.25 $$ $$ 10055.25 \cdot 2000 = 20{,}110{,}500 \ \text{Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta P \approx 20.11 \ \text{MPa}} $$ (Equivalent to ~ 200 atm — about 200 times a car tire's pressure.) ### Step 2 — Allowable Stress $$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FS} = \frac{550 \ \text{MPa}}{3.0} $$ $$ \sigma_{allow} = 183.33 \ \text{MPa} $$ ### Step 3 — Bare Thickness from Membrane Equation $$ t_{bare} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_i}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{20.11 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 1.1}{2 \cdot 183.33 \cdot 10^6} $$ Numerator: $$ 20.11 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 1.1 = 22.121 \cdot 10^6 $$ Denominator: $$ 2 \cdot 183.33 \cdot 10^6 = 366.66 \cdot 10^6 $$ Division: $$ t_{bare} = \frac{22.121}{366.66} = 0.06034 \ \text{m} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{bare} \approx 60.3 \ \text{mm}} $$ ### Step 4 — Add Corrosion Allowance $$ t = t_{bare} + c_a = 0.06034 + 0.0025 $$ $$ \boxed{t = 0.06284 \ \text{m} \approx 63 \ \text{mm}} $$ ### Step 5 — Outer Radius & Volume Check $$ R_o = R_i + t = 1.1 + 0.0628 = 1.1628 \ \text{m} $$ Internal habitable volume: $$ V_{hab} = \frac{4}{3} \pi R_i^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.1)^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 1.331 $$ $$ V_{hab} = 5.576 \ \text{m}^3 \approx 5.6 \ \text{m}^3 $$ > [!NOTE] > A 1.1 m radius sphere gives ~5.6 m³ of habitable volume — barely enough for **3 occupants** if each is allotted ~1.8 m³ (per SNAME 5-5 human factors guideline). --- ## 3.2 Derivation B — Cylindrical Hull vs Spherical Hull Material Efficiency **Hypothesis:** For the same depth $D$ and same internal radius $R_i$, prove that the sphere uses *less material* than the cylinder. ### Cylinder Case Using thin-wall cylinder hoop stress: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_i}{t_{cyl}} $$ Setting $\sigma_h = \sigma_{allow}$: $$ t_{cyl} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_i}{\sigma_{allow}} $$ ### Sphere Case From Section 3.1 Step 3: $$ t_{sphere} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_i}{2 \sigma_{allow}} $$ ### Ratio $$ \frac{t_{sphere}}{t_{cyl}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_i / (2 \sigma_{allow})}{\Delta P \cdot R_i / \sigma_{allow}} $$ $$ \frac{t_{sphere}}{t_{cyl}} = \frac{1}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{cyl} = 2 \cdot t_{sphere}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter?** For a 2000 m design depth and HY-80, the sphere needs 63 mm thickness while the equivalent cylinder needs **126 mm**. The sphere saves ~50% hull steel and therefore saves weight that can be used for payload or ballast — *the single most important geometric optimization in submersible engineering*. --- ## 3.3 Derivation C — Reserve Buoyancy & Variable Ballast **Given:** - Submersible displacement volume $V_{disp} = 7.0 \ \text{m}^3$ - Light ship weight (hull + systems) $W_{LS} = 55{,}000$ N - Fixed payload (crew + instruments) $W_{payload} = 8{,}000$ N - Required dive margin $\eta_{dive} = 2\%$ **Find:** Required variable ballast water weight for surface cruise and for diving. ### Step 1 — Total Buoyancy at Surface $$ F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 7.0 $$ $$ F_B = 70{,}396 \ \text{N} \approx 70.4 \ \text{kN} $$ ### Step 2 — Reserve Buoyancy at Surface (No Ballast) $$ F_{RB,0} = F_B - W_{LS} - W_{payload} $$ $$ F_{RB,0} = 70{,}396 - 55{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 7{,}396 \ \text{N} $$ So even without ballast, the sub floats with ~7.4 kN reserve (good — emergency surface margin). ### Step 3 — Ballast Required to Lose Reserve & Dive Margin $$ W_{ballast} = F_{RB,0} + \eta_{dive} \cdot F_B $$ $$ W_{ballast} = 7{,}396 + 0.02 \cdot 70{,}396 $$ $$ W_{ballast} = 7{,}396 + 1{,}408 = 8{,}804 \ \text{N} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{ballast} \approx 8.8 \ \text{kN}} $$ This ballast is seawater flooded into external tanks and dropped to surface again. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-Off Comparison | Design Aspect | Steel (HY-80) | Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) | Acrylic (PMMA) | |---------------|---------------|----------------------|----------------| | Yield Strength | 550 MPa | 880 MPa | 70 MPa | | Density | 7850 kg/m³ | 4430 kg/m³ | 1190 kg/m³ | | Strength/Weight | 70 kN·m/kg | 199 kN·m/kg | 59 kN·m/kg | | Max Practical Depth | ~ 3000 m | ~ 11,000 m | ~ 1000 m | | Cost | Low | Very High | Medium | | Corrosion in SW | Moderate (needs coating) | Excellent | N/A | | Transparency | No | No | Yes | | Welding | Easy | Difficult (inert gas) | Cement | > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** Titanium's superiority at depth is *not* strength alone — it is the **strength-to-weight ratio**. A steel hull thick enough for 6000 m would weigh so much it could not surface without enormous buoyancy tanks. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Example — Typical 12-Hour Endurance Check Crew respiration rate: **0.5 L O₂ / min / person**. For 3 persons over 12 hours: $$ V_{O_2} = 0.5 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 3 \cdot 12 \cdot 3600 = 64.8 \ \text{m}^3 \ \text{O}_2 \ \text{(at 1 atm)} $$ This is why **O₂ cylinders** are sized in the **0.07–0.10 m³** (70–100 L) range — the regulator releases it at the crew's *metabolic rate*, not all at once. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Manned Submersible The following block diagram maps the *interactions* between the major subsystems that a basic design must allocate volume, weight, and power for.
