[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Differentiate between a manned submersible and a remotely operated vehicle (ROV). List any four key differences.** **Model Answer (KTU Marking Scheme — 3 marks):** A *manned submersible* carries human occupants inside a pressure hull; an *ROV* is unmanned and tethered to a surface ship via an umbilical cable. Key differences: | # | Manned Submersible | ROV | |---|--------------------|-----| | 1 | Has a pressure hull with life-support system | No pressure hull; electronics are housed in pressure-tolerant oil-filled or pressure-compensated chambers | | 2 | Limited by human endurance (CO₂ scrubbing, O₂ supply) | Endless endurance (powered via tether) | | 3 | Limited depth by human factors (~ 11 km max) | Operates to full ocean depth (e.g., 11 km) | | 4 | High cost per mission but real-time human decision-making | Lower risk to life but limited dexterity | *(Full marks: 4 differences × 0.5 + intro + conclusion = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper]** **Why is a spherical pressure hull preferred over a cylindrical one for the crew compartment of a deep submersible?** **Model Answer:** 1. A sphere develops **uniform biaxial membrane stress** — hoop stress equals meridional stress at every point. 2. For the same depth and radius, the wall thickness of a sphere is **half that of a cylinder** ($t_{cyl} = 2 t_{sphere}$), reducing structural weight. 3. The sphere has **no discontinuities** (no sharp corners between cylindrical shell and hemispherical endcaps), minimizing stress concentrations. 4. The sphere has the **minimum surface area for a given volume**, reducing welding, inspection, and corrosion surface. *(Full marks: 4 reasons × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark questions, students frequently lose 2–3 marks by: > (a) *Skipping units* in intermediate steps. > (b) *Forgetting corrosion allowance* even when the problem specifies operating in seawater. > (c) *Confusing hoop and longitudinal stress* in cylindrical hulls. > (d) *Not verifying the thin-wall assumption* ($t/R \leq 1/10$). > Always state units at every line, and add a closing sentence confirming the assumption validity. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Option A for Module 1 Internal Choice)* **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** A manned submersible is to operate at a maximum depth of 2500 m. The crew sphere has an inner radius of 1.2 m and is to be fabricated from a titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) with yield strength $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa and a corrosion allowance of 1 mm. Using a factor of safety of 3.5, determine: (i) The required wall thickness of the spherical hull. (ii) The maximum hoop stress if the hull were instead built as a cylinder of the same radius and thickness. (iii) State whether the thin-wall assumption holds. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Define **Reserve Buoyancy** and **Dive Margin** for a submersible. For a vehicle with displacement volume 8 m³ and lightship weight 60 kN, calculate the variable ballast water mass required to (i) achieve neutral buoyancy and (ii) achieve a 2% dive margin. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². #### **Solution — Question A** **(a)(i) Spherical Wall Thickness:** External pressure differential: $$ \Delta P = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2500 = 25{,}138{,}125 \ \text{Pa} \approx 25.14 \ \text{MPa} $$ Allowable stress: $$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{880}{3.5} = 251.43 \ \text{MPa} $$ Bare thickness: $$ t_{bare} = \frac{25.14 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot 251.43 \cdot 10^6} = \frac{30.168}{502.86} = 0.06000 \ \text{m} = 60.0 \ \text{mm} $$ With corrosion allowance: $$ \boxed{t = 60.0 + 1.0 = 61.0 \ \text{mm}} $$ **(a)(ii) Cylinder Hoop Stress (same thickness 61 mm):** Using thin-wall cylinder formula: $$ \sigma_{h,cyl} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{t} = \frac{25.14 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 1.2}{0.061} $$ Numerator: $$ 25.14 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 1.2 = 30.168 \cdot 10^6 $$ Division: $$ \sigma_{h,cyl} = \frac{30.168 \cdot 10^6}{0.061} = 494.56 \cdot 10^6 \ \text{Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_{h,cyl} \approx 494.6 \ \text{MPa}} $$ Comparison: $\sigma_{h,cyl} = 494.6$ MPa **exceeds** the yield strength of 880 MPa… wait, recheck — 494.6 MPa < 880 MPa, so it is **safe** but uses only ~56% of yield — overdesigned as a cylinder (because sphere was already FS × 3.5 conservative). **(a)(iii) Thin-Wall Assumption:** $$ \frac{t}{R} = \frac{0.061}{1.2} = 0.0508 \approx \frac{1}{19.7} $$ Since $1/19.7 < 1/10$, the thin-wall assumption is **valid**. ✓ **(b)(i) Neutral Buoyancy:** $$ F_B = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 8 = 80{,}442 \ \text{N} $$ Lightship weight $W = 60{,}000$ N. Reserve buoyancy without ballast: $$ F_{RB,0} = 80{,}442 - 60{,}000 = 20{,}442 \ \text{N} $$ To reach neutral buoyancy, ballast must equal this: $$ \boxed{W_{ballast,neutral} = 20{,}442 \ \text{N} \ \text{or} \ m_{ballast} = 2{,}084 \ \text{kg}} $$ **(b)(ii) With 2% Dive Margin:** $$ W_{ballast,dive} = F_{RB,0} + 0.02 \cdot F_B = 20{,}442 + 0.02 \cdot 80{,}442 $$ $$ W_{ballast,dive} = 20{,}442 + 1{,}609 = 22{,}051 \ \text{N} $$ $$ \boxed{m_{ballast,dive} = 2{,}248 \ \text{kg}} $$ --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Option for Internal Choice)* **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **four main design considerations** for the pressure hull of a manned submersible. Discuss why HY-80 steel is preferred over mild steel for deep submersibles. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A cylindrical pressure hull of a small observation submersible has an internal diameter of 1.0 m, wall thickness of 25 mm, and operates at 500 m depth. Compute: (i) The hoop stress and longitudinal stress. (ii) The factor of safety if the material is aluminum alloy 7075-T6 ($\sigma_y = 503$ MPa). (iii) Comment on the suitability of this design for human occupation. #### **Solution — Question B** **(a) Four Design Considerations:** 1. **Strength against hydrostatic collapse** — must withstand external pressure of $\rho g D$. 2. **Weight minimization** — every kg saved is ballast saved. 3. **Volume maximization** — life support and human factors need habitable volume. 4. **Fabrication feasibility** — welding, inspection, and repair in dry dock. **Why HY-80 over Mild Steel:** - HY-80 has **higher yield strength (550 MPa vs 250 MPa for mild steel)**, allowing thinner hulls and deeper operation. - HY-80 has **better low-temperature toughness** (critical at 4 °C deep-ocean temperature where mild steel becomes brittle). - HY-80 has **improved weldability** with controlled HAZ (heat-affected zone) toughness. *(7 marks: 4 points × 1 + sketch (1) + HY-80 discussion (2) = 7)* **(b)(i) Stresses:** $$ \Delta P = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 500 = 5{,}027{,}625 \ \text{Pa} \approx 5.03 \ \text{MPa} $$ Inner radius: $$ R_i = 0.5 \ \text{m} $$ Hoop stress: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R_i}{t} = \frac{5.03 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.5}{0.025} $$ $$ \sigma_h = \frac{2.515 \cdot 10^6}{0.025} = 100.6 \cdot 10^6 \ \text{Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_h \approx 100.6 \ \text{MPa}} $$ Longitudinal stress: $$ \sigma_l = \frac{\sigma_h}{2} = \frac{100.6}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_l \approx 50.3 \ \text{MPa}} $$ **(b)(ii) Factor of Safety:** $$ FS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_h} = \frac{503}{100.6} = 5.0 $$ $$ \boxed{FS \approx 5.0} $$ **(b)(iii) Suitability:** A factor of safety of 5.0 is **adequate for manned operation** (typical deep-submersible FS range 3.0–4.0, but for shallow observation at 500 m, FS = 5 is conservative and safe). However, the thin-wall check: $t/R = 0.025/0.5 = 1/20$ — valid. Suitable for **human occupation at 500 m depth**. ✓ --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Manned submersible** = pressure hull + life support + propulsion + crew. - [ ] Standard seawater density: **$\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³**; $g = 9.81$ m/s². - [ ] Hydrostatic pressure at depth $D$: $\Delta P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot D$. - [ ] **Sphere** wall thickness is **half** the **cylinder** for the same depth and radius. - [ ] Spherical stress (uniform biaxial): $\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 t}$. - [ ] Cylindrical hoop stress (governs): $\sigma_h = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{t}$. - [ ] Cylindrical longitudinal stress: $\sigma_l = \sigma_h / 2$. - [ ] **Factor of Safety** for manned submersibles: **2.0–5.0**, increasing with depth. - [ ] **Thin-wall validity:** $t/R \leq 1/10$. - [ ] **Reserve Buoyancy** = $F_B - W$; must be **positive at surface** (5–15% typical). - [ ] **Dive Margin** = 1–3 % of displacement (positive sinking). - [ ] **Static stability** via Metacentric Height $GM = KB + BM - KG \geq 0.05$ m. - [ ] **Material hierarchy:** Titanium (deepest, lightest, costliest) > HY-80 Steel (mid-depth) > Mild Steel (shallow) > Acrylic (transparent windows). - [ ] **Always add corrosion allowance** ($c_a = 1.5$–$3$ mm for steel; ~0 for titanium). - [ ] **Human factors:** 1.8 m³/person habitable volume; O₂ at 0.5 L/min/person; CO₂ scrubbing mandatory. - [ ] **Standards:** SNAME T&R 5-5, DNV-RP-F112, ABS Rules for Underwater Vehicles. - [ ] **Design is iterative** — Mission → Hull Form → Material → Scantling → Buoyancy → Stability → Loop. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always end a 14-mark question with a *design validation sentence* — e.g., "Since $t/R \approx 1/20 < 1/10$, the thin-wall assumption is valid, and the design is acceptable for the prescribed mission." Examiners reward this **professional closure**.

topics.title: Design Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Objectives** in the context of submersibles refer to the formalized set of performance, operational, and engineering goals that govern the conception, sizing, and configuration of an underwater vehicle. These objectives translate the mission requirement into quantifiable technical specifications such as operational depth, payload capacity, endurance, speed, maneuverability, stability, safety factor, and life-cycle cost.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OENST723, Module 1), the design objective function of a submersible can be expressed in a generic form as:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \max \left[ w_1 \cdot \text{Performance} + w_2 \cdot \text{Safety} + w_3 \cdot \text{Reliability} - w_4 \cdot \text{Cost} \right]$$

where $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]$ represents the vector of design variables (length, diameter, hull thickness, material grade, battery capacity, etc.), and $w_i$ are the weighting factors assigned according to mission priority.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of designing a submersible the way you would design a custom backpack for a mountaineer. The climber tells you: *"I need to carry 15 kg of gear, survive -30 °C, climb 8,000 m, and still have food for 5 days."* You cannot optimize for everything equally. You prioritize **survival (pressure hull)** over **comfort (ergonomics)**, but you also refuse to make it so heavy the climber cannot move. Submersible design works exactly the same way: **safety and depth** almost always dominate, while **cost and speed** are subordinate trade-off variables.

The **primary design objective** is to ensure the vehicle can:
1. Withstand the hydrostatic pressure at the rated operating depth (typically with a **1.5× to 2.0× safety factor**).
2. Perform the intended mission (scientific survey, cable inspection, military reconnaissance, oil-rig support, etc.).
3. Return safely with the crew (or data, in the case of AUVs/ROVs).

### Standard Design Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

| Metric Category | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Depth (Shallow / Deep) | 300 – 6,000 | m |
| Hull Safety Factor (Collapse) | 1.5 – 2.0 | dimensionless |
| Reserve Buoyancy (Submerged) | 5 – 10 | % |
| Endurance (Manned) | 8 – 72 | hours |
| Endurance (AUV) | 24 – 1,000+ | hours |
| Cruise Speed | 1 – 4 | knots |
| Hull Material Density (HY-80 Steel) | **7,850** | kg/m³ |
| Hull Material Density (Ti-6Al-4V) | **4,430** | kg/m³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Hierarchical Breakdown of Design Objectives

The design objectives of a submersible are conventionally classified into a **4-tier hierarchy**:

- **Tier 1 — Mission-Critical (Non-Negotiable)**
  - *Why:* Without these, the vehicle cannot exist or cannot be deployed.
  - *How:* They are derived directly from the operational requirement document (ORD) supplied by the customer.
  - *Includes:* Maximum operating depth, payload mass/volume, mission duration, geographic operating window.

- **Tier 2 — Performance Objectives (Optimizable)**
  - *Why:* They determine whether the submersible is competitive or efficient.
  - *How:* Tuned using hydrodynamic and system-level trade studies.
  - *Includes:* Cruise and burst speed, turning radius, hover capability, sensor resolution, data throughput.

- **Tier 3 — Safety & Regulatory Objectives (Mandatory Compliance)**
  - *Why:* Governed by class rules (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register) and KTU-aligned design codes.
  - *How:* Implemented as constraints in the optimization problem, not as objectives to maximize.
  - *Includes:* Collapse pressure margin, fire suppression, escape and rescue provisions, reserve buoyancy, EMC compatibility.

- **Tier 4 — Life-Cycle & Economic Objectives (Long-Term)**
  - *Why:* A technically perfect vehicle that no agency can afford is a design failure.
  - *How:* Evaluated through cost-of-ownership modeling.
  - *Includes:* Acquisition cost, maintenance interval, mean time between failures (MTBF), crew training cost, decommissioning plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common Part-A question is *"Differentiate between mission objectives and design objectives."* The crisp answer: **Mission objectives** are *what the customer wants done* (explore a wreck at 2,000 m); **design objectives** are *how the engineer plans to achieve it* (hull rated to 3,000 m, 12-h endurance, 4-knot cruise, ±0.1 m hover accuracy).

### The Multi-Objective Optimization Framework

Modern submersible design treats the problem as a **constrained multi-objective optimization**:

$$\text{Find } \mathbf{x} \quad \text{to minimize} \quad \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \left[ F_1(\mathbf{x}),\, F_2(\mathbf{x}),\, \dots,\, F_k(\mathbf{x}) \right]$$

subject to

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$
$$h_l(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad l = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

Where $F_1$ is typically **displacement (W)**, $F_2$ is **drag power ($P_D$)**, and $F_3$ is **acquisition cost**. The Pareto front gives the designer a family of "best" non-dominated solutions.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{hydro}} = \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ ($\rho_{\text{sw}} \approx 1,025$ kg/m³) | Pa |
| 2 | $P_{\text{collapse}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Elastic collapse pressure of a thin cylinder (von Mises) | Pa |
| 3 | $\text{SF}_{\text{collapse}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{collapse}}}{P_{\text{hydro,max}}}$ | Collapse safety factor (KTU: $\geq 1.5$) | — |
| 4 | $P_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot C_D \cdot A \cdot V^3$ | Useful propulsive power to overcome drag | W |
| 5 | $R_B = W - B$ | Net buoyant force (positive ⇒ floats) | N |
| 6 | $E_{\text{batt}} = V_{\text{batt}} \cdot C_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{tot}}$ | Available electrical endurance energy | Wh |
| 7 | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{L}{V \cdot \tan(\phi)}$ | Tactical turning radius at rudder angle $\phi$ | m |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in a thin-walled pressure cylinder | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Reminder:** Whenever you see absolute values in formulas inside a table, render them as `\vert x \vert` (e.g., $\vert R_B \vert$), **never** using the vertical bar `|` character — it breaks the markdown table parser.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **oil & gas sector**, deep-diving ROVs (working at 3,000 m) are designed primarily for *manipulator dexterity and tether management*; speed is irrelevant. In **defense**, the *Diesel-Electric Submersible (SSK)* prioritizes *stealth (low radiated noise)* and *endurance*, with payload as a constraint. This illustrates that **the weighting vector $w_i$ is mission-driven, not universal**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation A: Hull Safety-Factor Constraint from First Principles

**Goal:** Starting from the geometry of a cylindrical pressure hull, derive the minimum thickness required to meet a depth-based design objective.

**Step 1 — State the hydrostatic loading.**
For a cylindrical hull of outer radius $R$ operating at depth $h$ in seawater:

$$P_{\text{ext}} = \rho_{\text{sw}} \, g \, h$$

With $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1,025$ kg/m³, and $h$ in metres:

$$P_{\text{ext}} = 1,025 \times 9.81 \times h \approx 10{,}055 \cdot h \;\; \text{[Pa]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert the depth into a design pressure using a depth margin.**
KTU and DNV recommend a 10% depth safety allowance to account for wave action, sensor error, and dynamic overshoot:

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \cdot P_{\text{ext}} = 11{,}061 \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Apply the hoop-stress equilibrium on a thin-walled cylinder.**
For a closed thin-walled cylinder of mean radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$:

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{design}} \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 4 — Enforce the yield constraint with the global safety factor.**
Let $\sigma_y$ be the material yield stress and $\text{SF}$ the design safety factor (KTU specifies $\text{SF} \geq 1.5$ for manned hulls):

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\text{SF}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the required wall thickness.**

$$\dfrac{P_{\text{design}} \cdot r}{t} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\text{SF}}$$

Rearranging:

$$t \geq \dfrac{P_{\text{design}} \cdot r \cdot \text{SF}}{\sigma_y}$$

Substituting $P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \, \rho_{\text{sw}} \, g \, h$:

$$t_{\min} = \dfrac{1.10 \, \rho_{\text{sw}} \, g \, h \cdot r \cdot \text{SF}}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 6 — Verify dimensional consistency.**

$$[t] = \dfrac{(\text{kg/m}^3) \cdot (\text{m/s}^2) \cdot (\text{m}) \cdot (\text{m}) \cdot (-)}{(\text{Pa})} = \dfrac{\text{N/m}^2}{\text{N/m}^2} \cdot \text{m} = \text{m} \;\; \checkmark$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 1.2 m mean-radius cylindrical hull must operate at **1,000 m** depth. Material is HY-80 steel ($\sigma_y = 550$ MPa). Required global safety factor is **2.0**. Compute the minimum hull thickness.

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 1,000 m:**

$$P_{\text{ext}} = 1,025 \times 9.81 \times 1{,}000 = 10.055 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa} = 10.055 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Design pressure (10% margin):**

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times 10.055 = 11.061 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the thickness formula:**

$$t_{\min} = \dfrac{11.061 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times 2.0}{550 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 11.061 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times 2.0 = 26.546 \times 10^6$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 550 \times 10^6$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio:**

$$t_{\min} = \dfrac{26.546}{550} = 0.04827 \; \text{m} \approx 48.3 \; \text{mm}$$

**Engineering check:** $t/R = 0.0483 / 1.2 = 0.040 \ll 0.10$, so the **thin-wall assumption is valid**. If $t/R$ had exceeded 0.10, the thick-wall Lamé equations would have been required.

### Derivation B: Endurance Objective as a Function of Battery and Drag

**Step 1 — Energy balance for a constant-cruise mission:**

The useful propulsive power to overcome hydrodynamic drag is $P_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{\text{sw}} C_D A V^3$.

**Step 2 — Total electrical power drawn from the battery, accounting for propulsion motor efficiency $\eta_m$ and propeller efficiency $\eta_p$:**

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{P_D}{\eta_m \eta_p}$$

**Step 3 — Endurance (mission duration) at constant cruise speed:**

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{elec}}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}} \cdot \eta_m \eta_p}{P_D}$$

Substituting $P_D$:

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{2 \, E_{\text{batt}} \, \eta_m \, \eta_p}{\rho_{\text{sw}} \, C_D \, A \, V^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** Endurance scales with the **inverse cube of speed**. Doubling speed reduces endurance by a factor of 8. This is the single most important trade-off in submersible design.

### Analytical Matrix: Objective → Driver → Typical Value

| Design Objective | Governing Equation | Design Driver | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Depth | $h_{\max} = P_{\text{collapse}} / (\rho_{\text{sw}} g)$ | Hull material, $t/R$ | 300 – 6,000 m |
| Cruise Speed | $V = (P_{\text{avail}} / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A))^{1/3}$ | Battery power, $C_D$ | 1 – 4 knots |
| Endurance | $T_{\text{end}} = 2E_{\text{batt}} \eta / (\rho C_D A V^3)$ | Battery kWh, drag | 8 – 72 h |
| Payload | $W_{\text{pl}} = B - W_{\text{light}}$ | Reserve buoyancy | 5 – 15% of $W$ |
| Maneuverability | $\text{TR} = L / (V \tan \phi)$ | Control-surface area | TR $\leq$ 2 $L$ |
| Stealth | $\text{SL} = f(\text{cavitation, machinery})$ | RPM, propeller tip $\sigma$ | $\sigma > 2.5$ |
| Cost | $C_{\text{acq}} = f(W, h_{\max}, \text{material})$ | Material & complexity | $20$k – $2$M USD |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Design-Objective Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Submersible Design Objectives"]
    n1["Tier 1 Mission Critical"]
    n2["Tier 2 Performance"]
    n3["Tier 3 Safety and Regulatory"]
    n4["Tier 4 Life Cycle and Economic"]
    n1a["Operating Depth"]
    n1b["Payload Mass"]
    n1c["Mission Duration"]
    n1d["Operating Environment"]
    n2a["Cruise Speed"]
    n2b["Maneuverability"]
    n2c["Hover Accuracy"]
    n2d["Sensor Throughput"]
    n3a["Hull Collapse SF"]
    n3b["Reserve Buoyancy"]
    n3c["Fire Suppression"]
    n3d["Crew Escape"]
    n4a["Acquisition Cost"]
    n4b["MTBF"]
    n4c["Maintenance Cost"]
    n4d["Decommissioning"]

    rootA --> n1
    rootA --> n2
    rootA --> n3
    rootA --> n4
    n1 --> n1a
    n1 --> n1b
    n1 --> n1c
    n1 --> n1d
    n2 --> n2a
    n2 --> n2b
    n2 --> n2c
    n2 --> n2d
    n3 --> n3a
    n3 --> n3b
    n3 --> n3c
    n3 --> n3d
    n4 --> n4a
    n4 --> n4b
    n4 --> n4c
    n4 --> n4d
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Design Trade-Off Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["Mission Requirement ORD"] --> proc1["Translate to Technical Specs"]
    proc1 --> proc2["Feasibility and Concept Study"]
    proc2 --> proc3["Hull Sizing Equation"]
    proc2 --> proc4["Power and Endurance Loop"]
    proc2 --> proc5["Stability and Buoyancy Check"]
    proc3 --> out1["Required Thickness t"]
    proc4 --> out2["Battery Capacity kWh"]
    proc5 --> out3["Ballast and Trim Plan"]
    out1 --> merge["Integrated Design"]
    out2 --> merge
    out3 --> merge
    merge --> val["Validation against Tier 3 Safety"]
    val --> cost["Tier 4 Life Cycle Cost"]
    cost --> loop{"Meets Cost Target?"}
    loop -- Yes --> fin["Final Design Freeze"]
    loop -- No --> proc2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Objective-to-Constraint Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    obj1["Objective: Maximize Depth"] --> con1["Constraint SF greater than 1.5"]
    obj2["Objective: Maximize Endurance"] --> con2["Constraint Battery Volume less than Vmax"]
    obj3["Objective: Maximize Speed"] --> con3["Constraint Installed Power less than Pmax"]
    obj4["Objective: Minimize Cost"] --> con4["Constraint Material Grade must be Certifiable"]
    con1 --> hull["Pressure Hull Design"]
    con2 --> energy["Energy System Design"]
    con3 --> prop["Propulsion Design"]
    con4 --> proc["Manufacturing and Procurement"]
    hull --> integ["Integrated Submersible"]
    energy --> integ
    prop --> integ
    proc --> integ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In the trade-off flow, the feedback arrow from *"Meets Cost Target?"* back to the *Concept Study* node represents an iterative design loop. In real projects, this loop executes 5 – 15 times before the design freezes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between "mission objectives" and "design objectives" of a submersible, with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Mission Objectives** (1 mark) — *What the customer wants the vehicle to do.* Example: Survey a shipwreck at 1,500 m depth within 5 hours.
- **Design Objectives** (1 mark) — *How the engineer plans to achieve it.* Example: Hull rated to 1,650 m (1.1× margin), 6-hour battery endurance, 3-knot cruise speed, ±0.2 m hover accuracy.
- **Key distinction** (1 mark) — Mission objectives are **input parameters**; design objectives are the **output specifications** that flow from a feasibility study.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State any four "Tier-1 (mission-critical)" design objectives of a submersible.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ¾ marks each, 4 to be chosen):**

1. Maximum operating depth ($h_{\max}$)
2. Payload mass and volume ($W_{\text{pl}}$)
3. Mission duration / endurance ($T_{\text{end}}$)
4. Operating environment (sea state, water temperature, salinity)
5. Geographic / regulatory operating window (Exclusive Economic Zone, naval exclusion zones)

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the "hydrostatic pressure" formula with the "collapse pressure" formula — they are *loads* and *capacities* respectively, and examiners will deduct 2 marks if the safety factor definition is inverted.
> 2. **Do not** forget the 10% depth margin; absence of this margin is a 1-mark deduction across both Part A and Part B questions.
> 3. **Do not** present a formula without its units; dimensional consistency is part of the marks.
> 4. **Do not** write *"a thicker hull is always better"* — points are lost for ignoring the weight–endurance trade-off.

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define a *design objective function* for a submersible. List the four tiers of design objectives and explain the role of *weighting factors* $w_i$ in the optimization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure hull has a mean radius of 0.8 m and is fabricated from HY-80 steel (yield stress $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa). The vehicle is rated to operate at 1,500 m. Using a depth safety margin of 10% and a global safety factor of 1.75, compute: (i) the design pressure, (ii) the minimum hull thickness, and (iii) verify whether the thin-wall assumption holds. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A design objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a mathematical expression that quantifies the merit of a design as a function of design variables. In submersible design, it is multi-objective:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 \cdot \text{Depth Margin} + w_2 \cdot \text{Endurance} + w_3 \cdot \text{Speed} - w_4 \cdot \text{Cost}$$

subject to $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ for safety constraints.

2. **Four tiers (3 marks — ¾ marks each):**
   - **Tier 1 — Mission-Critical:** Non-negotiable parameters (depth, payload, duration).
   - **Tier 2 — Performance:** Optimizable parameters (speed, maneuverability, sensor quality).
   - **Tier 3 — Safety & Regulatory:** Compliance constraints (collapse SF, reserve buoyancy, DNV class).
   - **Tier 4 — Life-Cycle & Economic:** Long-term parameters (cost, MTBF, maintenance).

3. **Role of $w_i$ (2 marks):** The $w_i$ are mission-specific weights that prioritize competing objectives. For a *military SSK*, $w_3$ (stealth) is large; for an *oil-and-gas ROV*, $w_1$ (depth) and $w_2$ (manipulator dexterity) dominate. The weights are *not universal* and are fixed by the design review board based on the ORD.

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Design pressure [2 marks]:**

$$P_{\text{ext}} = 1,025 \times 9.81 \times 1{,}500 = 15.083 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$$

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times 15.083 = 16.591 \; \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Minimum thickness [3 marks]:**

$$t_{\min} = \dfrac{P_{\text{design}} \cdot r \cdot \text{SF}}{\sigma_y}$$

$$t_{\min} = \dfrac{16.591 \times 10^6 \times 0.8 \times 1.75}{550 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{\min} = \dfrac{23.227 \times 10^6}{550 \times 10^6} = 0.0422 \; \text{m} = 42.2 \; \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Thin-wall check [2 marks]:**

$$\dfrac{t}{r} = \dfrac{0.0422}{0.8} = 0.0528 < 0.10$$

Since $t/r \ll 0.10$, the **thin-wall assumption is valid**, and the Lamé thick-wall correction is not required.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain, with a sketch (or block diagram), how a *multi-objective trade-off* between **endurance** and **speed** is carried out in the preliminary design stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lithium-ion battery pack delivers $E_{\text{batt}} = 120$ kWh to a submersible with a total drag coefficient $C_D A = 0.35$ m². Combined propulsion efficiency is $\eta = 0.55$. Compute the endurance when cruising at **(i) 1.5 m/s** and **(ii) 3.0 m/s**, and comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Endurance–speed trade-off (2 marks):** Endurance is inversely proportional to the cube of speed. The trade-off is plotted on a single chart with speed on the X-axis and endurance on the Y-axis.
2. **Block diagram (3 marks):** The trade-study loop iterates: required speed → required thrust → required power → battery mass → buoyancy change → stability check → acceptable.
3. **Practical rule of thumb (2 marks):** Designers typically select the **square-cube optimum** — cruise at the speed that minimizes $P_D / V$, which for a given $C_D A$ is the velocity that maximises *range* $R = V \cdot T_{\text{end}}$. Differentiating $R$ w.r.t. $V$ and setting to zero gives the long-range cruise speed.

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

Use the endurance formula derived in Section 3:

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{2 \, E_{\text{batt}} \, \eta}{\rho_{\text{sw}} \, C_D A \, V^3}$$

**(i) At $V = 1.5$ m/s [3 marks]:**

$$V^3 = 1.5^3 = 3.375 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{2 \times 120{,}000 \times 0.55}{1,025 \times 0.35 \times 3.375}$$

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{132{,}000}{1,210.78} \approx 109.0 \; \text{hours}$$

**(ii) At $V = 3.0$ m/s [3 marks]:**

$$V^3 = 3.0^3 = 27.0 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{132{,}000}{1,025 \times 0.35 \times 27.0}$$

$$T_{\text{end}} = \dfrac{132{,}000}{9,686.25} \approx 13.6 \; \text{hours}$$

**Commentary [1 mark]:** Doubling the speed from 1.5 to 3.0 m/s reduced endurance by a factor of $109.0 / 13.6 \approx 8.0$, which exactly confirms the inverse-cube relationship $T_{\text{end}} \propto 1/V^3$ (since $2^3 = 8$).

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Design objectives are mission-driven**, not universal — always derive them from the ORD before sizing the vehicle.
- ⭐ **The four tiers** are Mission-Critical → Performance → Safety/Regulatory → Life-Cycle/Economic.
- ⭐ **The core hydrostatic equation** is $P_{\text{ext}} = \rho_{\text{sw}} g h$ with $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1,025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ⭐ **Always apply a 10% depth margin** to convert operating depth into design depth.
- ⭐ **The hull safety factor** $\text{SF} = P_{\text{collapse}} / P_{\text{hydro}}$ must be $\geq 1.5$ (manned) or $\geq 1.25$ (unmanned ROV).
- ⭐ **Thin-wall assumption** is valid when $t/R \leq 0.10$. Above that, switch to Lamé thick-wall equations.
- ⭐ **Endurance scales as $1/V^3$** — this is the single most important trade-off in submersible design.
- ⭐ **Stealth** (radiated-noise level) is a Tier-2 objective for military submersibles and a non-issue for most civilian ROVs.
- ⭐ **Reserve buoyancy** of 5 – 10% is required even when submerged, to recover from an emergency blow.
- ⭐ **KTU expects the use of the generic objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ in Part-B answers when the question mentions *design objectives*.
- ⭐ **Common mark-losing pitfalls:** forgetting the 10% depth margin, using $\rho_{\text{fresh}}$ instead of $\rho_{\text{seawater}}$, omitting units, and confusing *hoop stress* with *collapse pressure*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Development of Submersibles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Development of Submersibles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **submersible** is a self-propelled, free-swimming, waterborne vehicle that is designed to operate beneath the surface of the water for sustained periods. Unlike a *submarine*, which is a fully self-contained combatant vessel of significant size, a submersible is generally smaller, has limited independent endurance, and is supported by a surface "mother ship" (tender/handling platform) for power, navigation, communication relay, and crew transfer. The term *submersible* is derived from the Latin **sub** (*under*) and **mergere** (*to plunge*).

KTU 2024 taxonomy classifies submersibles into three operational classes:

| Class | Acronym | Description | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human-Occupied Vehicle | HOV | Carries crew inside a pressure hull | Manned Submersible |
| Remotely Operated Vehicle | ROV | Tethered, pilot on surface | Tethered Submersible |
| Autonomous Underwater Vehicle | AUV | Untethered, pre-programmed or intelligent | Untethered Submersible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The course *OENST723 - Basics of Design of Submersibles* treats the submersible primarily as a *manned (HOV) engineering system*. ROVs and AUVs are covered briefly for comparison, but the bulk of design theory, hydrostatics, and structural analysis in Module 1 concerns pressure hulls and buoyancy control of crewed vehicles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Bubble" Intuition

Imagine you hold a sealed **steel ball** under the kitchen tap and push it into a basin of water. The deeper you push, the harder the water "pushes back" on the ball. If the ball is hollow, the air trapped inside keeps it *buoyant* — it wants to spring back to the surface. If you puncture it, water floods in, the air is replaced, and it sinks.

A submersible is, in essence, exactly that **"steel bubble"** — but engineered to do the following simultaneously:

1. **Resist** the crushing hydrostatic pressure of the deep ocean (up to **~1100 atm** at 11,000 m).
2. **Trap** a breathable atmosphere of air at roughly **1 atm** for the crew.
3. **Control** its own weight vs. buoyancy with milligram precision using ballast systems.
4. **Navigate** in a lightless, GPS-denied, high-pressure environment using sound instead of radio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** The deepest point on Earth, the **Challenger Deep** in the Mariana Trench, lies at approximately **10,935 m**. A column of seawater exerts a hydrostatic pressure of about **1,086 bar (110 MPa)** at that depth — equivalent to ~1,100 kg resting on every square centimetre of the hull. Designing a "bubble" that can survive this is the central engineering challenge of submersible design.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants Used Throughout the Course

For all numerical problems in Module 1, the following constants are standard:

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Density of seawater (standard): $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of fresh water: $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Atmospheric pressure at sea level: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Environment — The Three "Enemies" of a Submersible

A submersible designer must defeat three hostile regimes simultaneously:

1. **Hydrostatic Compression** — uniform crushing pressure on the hull (governed by depth).
2. **Hydrodynamic Loading** — drag, slamming, and wave forces when on surface.
3. **Corrosive/Chemical Attack** — saltwater galvanic corrosion, biofouling, hydrogen embrittlement.

## 2.2 Buoyancy — The Heart of Submersible Operation

A submersible is a *floating body* governed by **Archimedes' Principle** and **Newton's Second Law**.

### 2.2.1 Archimedes' Principle (Buoyant Force)

The upward buoyant force equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the pressure hull and external volumes:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g$$

where $V_{disp}$ is the total displaced volume of seawater (sum of hull exterior, syntactic foam, external payloads).

### 2.2.2 Net Submerged Weight (Apparent Weight in Water)

$$W_{sub} = W_{dry} - F_B + F_{variable\ ballast}$$

### 2.2.3 Three Operating Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Physical Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Positively Buoyant** | $F_B > W$ | Submersible rises to surface (emergency blow) |
| **Neutrally Buoyant** | $F_B = W$ | Submersible hovers at constant depth (research mode) |
| **Negatively Buoyant** | $F_B < W$ | Submersible descends (controlled dive) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** Achieving *neutral buoyancy* is the holy grail of mid-water operations. A perfectly trimmed submersible can be made to hover hands-free by a skilled pilot, conserving thruster power for fine lateral station-keeping.

## 2.3 Hydrostatic Pressure and Hull Design

Pressure at depth $h$ in a static incompressible fluid:

$$P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Because seawater is nearly incompressible, the *linear* $\rho g h$ approximation is sufficient up to full ocean depth (error < 2% at 11,000 m).

### 2.3.1 Stress in a Spherical Pressure Hull (Thin-Wall Approximation)

For a sphere of inner radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$ with $t/r < 0.05$:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \sigma_{meridional} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a sphere?** A sphere has *equal* hoop and meridional stresses — the most efficient geometry for resisting external pressure, using the **least possible mass of steel** for a given depth rating. This is why the DSV *Limiting Factor* (Triton 36000/2) uses a **spherical titanium pressure hull** rated to 11,000 m.

### 2.3.2 Buckling vs. Yield — The Two Failure Modes

Under external pressure, a cylindrical hull typically fails by **elastic instability (buckling)** *before* the steel reaches its yield stress. The critical buckling pressure for a long cylinder is given by the **Bresse-Bryan-German formula**:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^2.5}{(L/D)}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $D$ the outer diameter, $t$ the wall thickness, and $L$ the unsupported length. The factor 2.6 is an empirical knockdown (sometimes 2.0–2.6) that accounts for initial ovality.

## 2.4 Variable Ballast Systems

Three classical methods are used to change the submerged weight:

| System | Mechanism | Speed of Response |
|---|---|---|
| **Droppable Ballast (Iron Shot)** | Releases solid steel balls from soft tanks | Very fast (emergency ascent) |
| **Water Ballast (Hard/Soft Tanks)** | Pumps seawater in/out using hydraulics | Slow (~minutes) |
| **Syntactic Foam** | Compressible glass microspheres bonded in epoxy | Fixed buoyancy aid only |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Pa | Linear approximation, valid < 11 km |
| 2 | Buoyant force | $F_B = \rho_{sw} V_{disp} g$ | N | Archimedes' principle |
| 3 | Submerged weight | $W_{sub} = W - F_B$ | N | Apparent weight in water |
| 4 | Vertical equilibrium | $F_B - W = m a$ | N | Positive = accelerating up |
| 5 | Thin-sphere stress | $\sigma = P r \vert (2t)$ | Pa | Hoop = meridional for sphere |
| 6 | Thin-cylinder hoop stress | $\sigma_h = P r \vert t$ | Pa | Cylinder only |
| 7 | Cylinder buckling | $P_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5} \vert (L/D)$ | Pa | Bryan-Bresse knockdown 2.6 |
| 8 | Gas compressibility | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (ideal gas) | — | Adiabatic: $P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma$ |
| 9 | Drag force | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho v^2 A$ | N | $C_D$ = 0.5 to 1.0 for submersible hull |
| 10 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v L \vert \mu$ | — | $L$ = characteristic length |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Equations 1–4 govern *operational* design (will it float/sink?). Equations 5–7 govern *structural* design (will the hull survive?). Equation 8 governs *internal atmosphere* and *buoyancy compensation* during descent. Equation 9–10 govern *propulsion* sizing and *thruster* selection. Every KTU problem on this module traces back to one of these ten equations.

## 2.6 A Note on Historical Depth Milestones

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 KTU syllabus specifically requires familiarity with the following depth records (frequently asked as 3-mark short notes): **Bathysphere (1934, 923 m) → Trieste (1960, 10,916 m) → Alvin (since 1964, 4,500 m) → Deepsea Challenger (2012, 10,908 m) → DSV Limiting Factor (2019–2020, all five ocean deeps) → Fendouzhe (2020, 10,909 m, China).**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure From First Principles

**Starting point:** Consider a vertical column of seawater of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ resting on a small patch of hull. The force on the patch must equal the weight of the column above it (since the fluid is static).

**Step 1 — Write the weight of the column:**

$$W_{column} = m_{column} \cdot g = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g = (\rho \cdot A \cdot h) \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Write the force on the hull patch (the definition of pressure):**

$$F_{patch} = P \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Equate them (static equilibrium of the column):**

$$P \cdot A = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P$:**

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Add the atmospheric pressure pushing down on the sea surface:**

$$P_{total} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

> This is the **fundamental hydrostatic law** used in every KTU depth-pressure problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thin-Wall Spherical Pressure Hull Stress

**Starting point:** A sphere of inner radius $r$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal gauge pressure $P$ (the pressure difference between the inside air at $1$ atm and the external seawater at $\sim 1100$ atm).

**Step 1 — Cut the sphere in half with an imaginary diametrical plane. Consider the hemispherical cap.**

The internal pressure pushes *outward* on the cap, trying to separate it from the other half. The resisting force is the *integrated* tensile stress acting around the circumference of the cut.

**Step 2 — Pressure force pushing the cap away from the other half:**

$$F_{pressure} = P \cdot \pi r^2$$

This is force = pressure × projected area of the cap.

**Step 3 — Tensile force holding the cap on:**

The wall material at the cut has cross-sectional area $A_{wall} = 2 \pi r \cdot t$ (a circular ring of radius $r$ and width $t$). The average tensile stress is $\sigma$.

$$F_{tension} = \sigma \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Equate them (the cap is in equilibrium):**

$$P \cdot \pi r^2 = \sigma \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and solve for $\sigma$:**

$$P \cdot r = 2 \sigma \cdot t$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t}$$

> This is **Lamé's equation specialised to a thin sphere**. Note the factor of 2 vs. a cylinder — a sphere uses *half* the stress for the same pressure, which is why it is the geometry of choice for ultra-deep submersibles.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buckling Pressure for a Long Cylinder (Bryan-Bresse Form)

**Starting point:** A long thin cylinder of outer diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and unsupported length $L$, made of an elastic material with Young's modulus $E$, subjected to external pressure.

**Step 1 — Classic elastic instability of a plate:**

A flat plate of width $b$ and thickness $t$ elastically buckles at a critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12 (1 - \nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

For $\nu \approx 0.3$ (steel/titanium), the prefactor simplifies to approximately $0.6 E$ per unit $(t/b)^2$.

**Step 2 — Apply to the curved cylinder wall:**

Treat the cylinder wall as a curved plate. The effective width is related to the diameter, and the boundary conditions (free vs. clamped ends) introduce a *knockdown factor* $k$ that accounts for initial imperfections.

**Step 3 — Express the critical pressure in terms of geometry:**

By energy methods (Bryan, 1888; Bresse, 1866), the elastic buckling pressure of a long, perfectly round cylinder is:

$$P_{cr,\ ideal} = \frac{2 E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 — Apply a knockdown factor to account for real imperfections:**

Submarines and submersibles are never perfectly round. Empirical studies (Windenburg-Trilling, 1934) give a knockdown factor that scales with the slenderness ratio $L/D$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponent of $t/D$ drops from **3.0** (ideal) to **2.5** (real), which is why *seamless* and *stress-relieved* titanium hulls (e.g., *Alvin*, *Limiting Factor*) are mandatory for ultra-deep diving.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Net Buoyancy and Vertical Acceleration

**Starting point:** A submersible of dry mass $m_{dry}$ and total displaced volume $V_{disp}$ at depth $h$.

**Step 1 — Identify forces (positive = up):**

- Buoyant force: $+F_B = +\rho_{sw} V_{disp} g$
- Gravitational weight: $-W = -m_{dry} g$
- Variable ballast contribution: $\pm F_{ballast}$ (water in = negative, water out = positive)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law ($\sum F = m a$):**

$$\rho_{sw} V_{disp} g - m_{dry} g + F_{ballast} = m_{dry} a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for vertical acceleration:**

$$a = g \left(\frac{\rho_{sw} V_{disp} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}\right) + \frac{F_{ballast}}{m_{dry}}$$

**Step 4 — Special cases:**

- **Hovering** ($a = 0$): $F_{ballast} = m_{dry} g - \rho_{sw} V_{disp} g$
- **Emergency ascent** (full drop-ballast): $F_{ballast}$ becomes very large positive, $a \gg 0$
- **Free descent** (no thrust, $F_{ballast} = 0$): $a = -g \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{sw} V_{disp}}{m_{dry}}\right)$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> A research submersible displaces $V_{disp} = 18 \text{ m}^3$ of seawater, has a dry mass of $17{,}500 \text{ kg}$, and carries $250 \text{ kg}$ of variable ballast. Operating at 2,000 m depth:
>
> 1. Compute the hydrostatic pressure.
> 2. Compute the buoyant force.
> 3. Compute the net vertical force and the resulting acceleration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 2,000 m:**

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h = 101{,}325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 20{,}110{,}500 = 20{,}211{,}825 \text{ Pa} \approx 202.1 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Buoyant force:**

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} V_{disp} g = 1025 \times 18 \times 9.81 = 180{,}994.5 \text{ N} \approx 181 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Net vertical force with no ballast deployed:**

$$F_{net} = F_B - W = 180{,}995 - (17{,}500 \times 9.81)$$

$$F_{net} = 180{,}995 - 171{,}675 = +9{,}320 \text{ N (upward)}$$

**Step 4 — Resulting acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{9{,}320}{17{,}500} = +0.533 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (upward)}$$

> The submersible is *positively buoyant* with the ballast empty — it will rise to the surface. To hover at 2,000 m, the pilot must flood $9{,}320 \div 9.81 \approx 950 \text{ kg}$ of water ballast (or trim with syntactic foam at the design stage).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Historical Development Timeline of Submersibles

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["1620 DREBBEL OAR-POWERED SUBMARINE FIRST MANNED DIVE LONDON THAMES"] --> B["1775 AMERICAN TURTLE DAVID BUSHNELL SINGLE CREW"]

    D --> E["1948 FNRS 2 BATHYSCAPHE PICCARD SWITZERLAND"]
    E --> F["1953 TRIESTE BUILT ITALY REFITTED USA"]
    F --> G["1960 TRIESTE II CHALLENGER DEEP 10916 M PIC CARD AND WALSH"]
    G --> H["1964 DSV ALVIN WOODS HOLE OCEANOGRAPHIC INST 4500 M"]
    H --> I["1985 ALVIN FINDS RMS TITANIC WRECK 3800 M"]
    I --> J["2012 DEEPSEA CHALLENGER JAMES CAMERON 10908 M SOLO"]
    J --> K["2019 DSV LIMITING FACTOR TRITON 36000 TWO VESC OVO FIVE DEEPS"]
    K --> L["2020 FENDOUZHE STRIVER CHINA 10909 M CREWED"]
    L --> M["FUTURE TITANIUM HULLS COMPOSITES BIO INSPIRED DESIGN 11000 M PLUS"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
