```mermaid
    style M fill:#385723,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Navy blue = early submarines (surface-launched era). Light blue = transitional deep-diving vehicles. Red = record-setting full-ocean-depth dives. Green = future research directions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of a Modern Crewed Submersible

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HULL["PRESSURE HULL SYSTEM"]
        H1["SPHERICAL TITANIUM HULL T 1100 ATM"]
        H2["ACRYLIC VIEWPORTS 3 TO 5 CONES"]
        H3["HATCH DOME TOP QUICK RELEASE"]
    end

    subgraph BUOY["BUOYANCY AND BALLAST SYSTEM"]
        B1["SYNTHACTIC FOAM FIXED BUOYANCY 70 PERCENT"]
        B2["HARD BALLAST TANK SEAWATER IN OUT"]
        B3["SOFT DROPPABLE BALLAST IRON SHOT EMERGENCY"]
    end

    subgraph PROP["PROPULSION AND MANOEUVRING"]
        P1["MAIN THRUSTERS 2 X 5 KW REAR"]
        P2["LATERAL THRUSTERS 4 X 2 KW BOW"]
        P3["VERTICAL THRUSTER 1 X 3 KW"]
    end

    subgraph LIFE["LIFE SUPPORT SUBSYSTEM"]
        L1["LITHIUM ION BATTERY 60 KWH"]
        L2["OXYGEN CYLINDERS 3 X 300 BAR"]
        L3["CO2 SCRUBBER LITHIUM HYDROXIDE"]
        L4["HUMIDITY AND TEMP CONTROL"]
    end

    subgraph NAV["NAVIGATION AND IMAGING"]
        N1["DOPPLER VELOCITY LOG DVL"]
        N2["INERTIAL MEASUREMENT UNIT IMU"]
        N3["ACOUSTIC POSITIONING USBL"]
        N4["HD CAMERAS 6 X LED FLOOD ARRAY"]
        N5["MULTI BEAM SONAR"]
    end

    H1 --- BUOY
    H1 --- PROP
    H1 --- LIFE
    H1 --- NAV
    B2 --- B3
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    L1 --- L2
    L2 --- L3
    L3 --- L4
    N1 --- N2
    N2 --- N3
    N3 --- N4
    N4 --- N5

    style H1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N1 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **pressure hull** is the central node; every other subsystem attaches to it. Buoyancy, propulsion, life support, and navigation form parallel functional layers. Loss of any one node is non-fatal (redundancy), but loss of the **pressure hull** or the **battery** is mission-critical.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Force Balance on a Submerged Submersible

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SUBMERSIBLE IN HOVER STATE AT DEPTH H"] --> B["BUOYANT FORCE F_B = RHO SW V DISP G ACTS UPWARD"]
    A --> C["GRAVITATIONAL WEIGHT W = M G ACTS DOWNWARD"]
    A --> D["VARIABLE BALLAST FORCE F_BALLAST ACTS UP OR DOWN"]
    A --> E["HYDRODYNAMIC DRAG F_D OPPOSES MOTION"]

    B --> F{"F_B PLUS F_BALLAST GREATER THAN W"}
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F -->|YES| G["NET UPWARD FORCE POSITIVE BUOYANCY RISES"]
    F -->|EQUAL| H["NEUTRAL BUOYANCY HOVERS USES THRUSTERS"]
    F -->|NO| I["NET DOWNWARD FORCE NEGATIVE BUOYANCY SINKS"]

    style A fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a submersible. Differentiate between an HOV, ROV, and AUV with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **submersible** is a self-propelled, free-swimming underwater vehicle, smaller than a submarine, that operates beneath the ocean surface and is typically supported by a surface mother ship for power, communications, and crew transfer. *(1 mark)*

| Type | Full Form | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HOV** | Human-Occupied Vehicle | Carries crew inside a pressure hull | DSV *Alvin* |
| **ROV** | Remotely Operated Vehicle | Tethered, pilot on surface | *Jason* (WHOI) |
| **AUV** | Autonomous Underwater Vehicle | Untethered, pre-programmed, intelligent | *Autosub* (NOC, UK) |

*(1 mark for table, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State Archimedes' Principle. A submersible displaces 25 m³ of seawater. Compute the buoyant force acting on it. Take ρ = 1025 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Archimedes' Principle:** *Any body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.* *(1 mark)*

**Given:** $V_{disp} = 25 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Formula:** $F_B = \rho_{sw} V_{disp} g$ *(0.5 marks)*

**Calculation:**

$$F_B = 1025 \times 25 \times 9.81 = 251{,}381.25 \text{ N} \approx 251.38 \text{ kN}$$

*(1 mark for substitution, 0.5 marks for final answer with unit)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the historical development of submersibles from 1930 to 2020, highlighting the major depth milestones.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

*(1 mark for intro + 4 marks for milestones + 1 mark for sketch + 1 mark for conclusion)*

The development of crewed submersibles from 1930 onward is a story of progressively deeper ocean access, driven by improvements in pressure-hull metallurgy, buoyancy control, and power systems.

1. **1934 — Bathysphere (Beebe & Barton, USA):** A 1.45 m diameter cast-steel sphere with 7 cm walls and fused-quartz viewports, lowered by a steel cable from a surface ship. Reached **923 m** off Bermuda — the first direct human observation of the deep sea.

2. **1948 — FNRS-2 (Auguste Piccard, Belgium/Switzerland):** The first *bathyscaphe* — a free-swimming vehicle combining a pressure sphere with a large gasoline-filled float for buoyancy. Operated to 1,380 m. Removed the need for a load-bearing cable.

3. **1953–1960 — Trieste (Piccard/US Navy):** Refitted with a thicker 12.7 cm wall spherical hull. On **23 Jan 1960**, Jacques Piccard and Don Walsh reached the **bottom of Challenger Deep at 10,916 m** — the deepest solo-crewed dive in history. Internal crew cabin pressure stayed at 1 atm.

4. **1964 — DSV Alvin (WHOI, USA):** A 2.1 m diameter titanium-hulled HOV rated to 4,500 m. Has performed 5,000+ dives including the 1985 Titanic discovery. Currently rated to 6,500 m after a 2014 rebuild.

5. **2012 — Deepsea Challenger (James Cameron, USA):** A single-pilot vertical-take-off submersible with a 1.2 m forged steel sphere rated to full ocean depth. Reached **10,908 m** solo on 26 March 2012 — deepest solo dive.

6. **2019–2020 — DSV Limiting Factor (Triton 36000/2, USA):** A two-person titanium-sphere HOV rated to 11,000 m. Victor Vescovo piloted it to the **five deepest points of the world's five oceans** (Five Deeps Expedition, 2018–2019).

7. **2020 — Fendouzhe / Striver (China):** A three-crew HOV that reached **10,909 m** in the Mariana Trench, with 10-hour bottom time.

**Sketch (1 mark):** A simple timeline diagram showing year, vehicle name, and depth rating is sufficient.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the membrane stress in a thin-walled spherical pressure hull subjected to external hydrostatic pressure. A spherical titanium hull of inner radius 1.20 m and wall thickness 75 mm is rated for 11,000 m. Compute the maximum hoop stress.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** See SECTION 3.2 above for the full step-by-step.

Final result:

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 11,000 m:**

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h = 101{,}325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 11{,}000$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 110{,}602{,}275 = 110{,}703{,}600 \text{ Pa} \approx 1107 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into thin-sphere formula:**

$$\sigma = \frac{110{,}703{,}600 \times 1.20}{2 \times 0.075} = \frac{132{,}844{,}320}{0.150}$$

$$\sigma = 885{,}628{,}800 \text{ Pa} \approx 886 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

The yield strength of Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy is ~880–950 MPa. Hence the chosen thickness gives a **factor of safety close to 1.0**, which is why real hulls use higher-grade Ti-6Al-4V ELI (extra-low interstitial) alloys and a more refined Lame analysis. *(1 mark for valid engineering comment)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the different types of buoyancy systems used in submersibles, with neat diagrams. Why is syntactic foam preferred for fixed buoyancy?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A submersible must control its weight-to-buoyancy ratio precisely. Three main systems are used.

1. **Hard Ballast Tanks (Variable):** Rigid metal tanks external to the pressure hull. Seawater is pumped in (negative buoyancy) or blown out with high-pressure air (positive buoyancy). Used for *trim* and *fine depth control*. *(2 marks)*

2. **Soft Droppable Ballast:** Flexible bags containing steel iron shot. Held by electromagnets. In an emergency, power is cut, the shot is released, and the submersible gains a large positive buoyancy — typically **5–7 m/s upward**. *(2 marks)*

3. **Syntactic Foam (Fixed Buoyancy):** A composite of hollow glass microspheres bonded in an epoxy matrix. Density ~ 0.55–0.65 g/cm³ (i.e., 35–45% lighter than seawater). Permanently bonded to the outside of the pressure hull. *(1 mark)*

**Why syntactic foam is preferred for fixed buoyancy** *(2 marks)*:
- It is *impermeable* to seawater (microspheres are sealed glass) — no long-term water absorption.
- It provides *uniform distributed buoyancy* along the hull, reducing local stress concentrations.
- It is *electrically insulating*, reducing galvanic corrosion with the metallic hull.
- It is *compressive* at depth (microspheres can fail), so it must be rated for the design depth.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Simple labelled cross-section of a hull with foam coating + hard tank + droppable ballast.

---

**(b) A submersible of dry mass 14,800 kg displaces 16.2 m³ of seawater. (i) Determine whether the vehicle is positively or negatively buoyant at the surface. (ii) If the pilot floods 950 kg of water into a ballast tank, what is the net vertical force? (iii) What is the resulting acceleration?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $m_{dry} = 14{,}800$ kg, $V_{disp} = 16.2$ m³, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $m_{ballast} = 950$ kg.

**(i) — Sign of buoyancy (2 marks):**

$$W = m_{dry} g = 14{,}800 \times 9.81 = 145{,}188 \text{ N}$$

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} V_{disp} g = 1025 \times 16.2 \times 9.81 = 162{,}881.4 \text{ N}$$

Since $F_B > W$ (162,881 > 145,188), the submersible is **positively buoyant** at the surface. Net upward force:

$$F_{net} = 162{,}881 - 145{,}188 = 17{,}693 \text{ N (upward)}$$

**(ii) — With 950 kg of water ballast flooded (2 marks):**

The water ballast is *added weight* (the displaced water inside the tank is no longer contributing to buoyancy — it just adds mass).

$$W_{new} = (14{,}800 + 950) \times 9.81 = 15{,}750 \times 9.81 = 154{,}507.5 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{net, new} = 162{,}881 - 154{,}508 = 8{,}373 \text{ N (still upward)}$$

**(iii) — Resulting acceleration (2 marks):**

$$a = \frac{F_{net, new}}{m_{new}} = \frac{8{,}373}{15{,}750} = +0.532 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (upward)}$$

**Engineering comment (1 mark):** The submersible is still positively buoyant. To make it dive, the pilot must flood an additional $\Delta m = 8{,}373 \div 9.81 \approx 854$ kg of water, or use a forward-mounted descent trim weight.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise (typically 1–2 marks lost each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add $P_{atm}$.** When asked for *total* pressure at depth, students often write only $\rho g h$. Always include $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa unless the question explicitly says *gauge pressure*.
>
> 2. **Mixing fresh-water and seawater densities.** The syllabus default is $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. Using 1000 kg/m³ gives a ~2.5% error — enough to lose a mark.
>
> 3. **Wrong buckling formula.** Students confuse the **Bryan-Bresse knockdown formula** $P_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5} / (L/D)$ with the **ideal elastic** $P_{cr} = 2E (t/D)^3 / (1-\nu^2)$. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the **real** form.
>
> 4. **Conflating sphere and cylinder.** A sphere has hoop stress $Pr/(2t)$, a cylinder $Pr/t$. Mixing them up is the #1 single source of marks lost in Module 1.
>
> 5. **No units in the final answer.** KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 mark for "answer without unit" even if the number is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1):**
>
> - [ ] A **submersible** is a small, supported, free-swimming underwater vehicle; a **submarine** is large, independent, and military.
> - [ ] Three classes: **HOV, ROV, AUV** — HOV is the focus of OENST723.
> - [ ] Hydrostatic pressure: $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ (linear in depth for seawater).
> - [ ] Buoyancy: $F_B = \rho_{sw} V_{disp} g$ (Archimedes' Principle).
> - [ ] **Neutral buoyancy** = $F_B = W$ (hover); **positive** = rise; **negative** = sink.
> - [ ] Sphere is the most mass-efficient pressure hull (equal bi-axial stress, $Pr/2t$).
> - [ ] Cylinder hoop stress is **double** the sphere's at the same $P, r, t$.
> - [ ] Cylinder failure mode is **elastic buckling**, governed by Bryan-Bresse: $P_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5} / (L/D)$.
> - [ ] **Syntactic foam** is the gold standard for fixed buoyancy (sealed glass microspheres in epoxy).
> - [ ] **Droppable ballast** (iron shot) is the *emergency ascent* system.
> - [ ] **Constant standard values:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
> - [ ] Historical depth record: 10,916 m by **Trieste (1960)**, matched by *Deepsea Challenger* (2012, 10,908 m) and *Limiting Factor* (2019, ~10,935 m).
> - [ ] **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V ELI)** is the modern material of choice for full-ocean-depth hulls; HY-80/100 steel is used for shallower military submarines.
> - [ ] Submersibles always operate with a **surface tender** — no submersible is fully independent for very long.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environment - Physical Properties of Sea Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Environment - Physical Properties of Sea Water ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Submersible Operating Environment The **marine environment** in which a submersible operates is a three-phase fluid continuum (liquid seawater + dissolved gases + suspended particulates) governed by a unique combination of **physical, chemical, and thermodynamic properties**. For the submarine designer, the ocean is not just "water" — it is a **thermodynamically active, electrically conductive, acoustically complex, and chemically aggressive** medium whose properties vary significantly with **depth, latitude, season, and geographic location**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"The marine environment is characterized by physical properties of sea water including density, salinity, temperature, pressure, sound velocity, viscosity, thermal conductivity, specific heat, and optical properties. These properties directly govern the hydrodynamic, structural, acoustic, thermal, and stability design of any submersible."* ### 1.2 The Four Primary State Variables Every property of seawater can be expressed as a function of **four fundamental state variables**: | Variable | Symbol | Typical Ocean Range | KTU Notation | |----------|--------|---------------------|--------------| | **Salinity** | $S$ | **33 – 37 PSU** | $S$ | | **Temperature** | $T$ | **-2 °C to +30 °C** | $T$ | | **Pressure (hydrostatic)** | $P$ | **1 atm to 1100 atm** | $P$ | | **Depth** | $z$ | **0 m to 11,000 m** | $D$ or $h$ | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Ocean Stack" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy: The Invisible Ocean Stack** > Imagine the ocean as a **vertically stacked sandwich** of invisible layers. The top layer (sun-warmed, less salty) is light and warm like a *fluffy cake sponge*. As you descend, each deeper layer becomes **denser, colder, saltier, and more pressurized** — like descending through a giant multi-tiered cake where each tier is compressed by the weight of the tiers above. A submersible descending through this stack must constantly adapt: its buoyancy shifts, its hull compresses, its sonar "sees" differently, and its electronics face different thermal and electrical conditions at every depth. > > This is why a submarine designed for the **sun-lit surface waters of the Arabian Sea** is fundamentally different from one designed for the **crushing black abyss of the Mariana Trench**. ### 1.4 The Engineering Significance — Why This Topic Matters A submersible designer MUST know seawater properties because they directly govern: 1. **Buoyancy & Stability** → governed by **density ($\rho$)** 2. **Hull strength / Collapse depth** → governed by **hydrostatic pressure ($P$)** 3. **Sonar / acoustic systems** → governed by **sound speed ($c$)** 4. **Thermal management** → governed by **specific heat ($C_p$)** and **thermal conductivity ($k$)** 5. **Drag & maneuvering** → governed by **kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)** 6. **Corrosion protection** → governed by **salinity** and **electrical conductivity ($\sigma$)** 7. **Ice navigation (Arctic submersibles)** → governed by **freezing point ($T_f$)** > [!IMPORTANT] > The 2024 KTU scheme emphasizes that this module forms the **fundamental design envelope** — every subsequent design calculation (hull thickness, ballast, propulsion, sonar placement, life-support thermal load) traces back to one of these seawater properties. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Salinity ($S$) **Salinity** is the total amount of dissolved salts in seawater, expressed in **PSU (Practical Salinity Units)** or equivalently **g/kg**. Average ocean salinity is **35 PSU** (≈ 35 grams of salt per kilogram of seawater). - Reference standard: **Standard Seawater** (Copenhagen, 1978) - Measured practically by **electrical conductivity ratio** (no longer by evaporation/weighing) - Salinity range: Open ocean → **33 to 37 PSU**; Red Sea → up to **41 PSU**; Baltic Sea → as low as **5 PSU** **Key concept — Conservative property:** Salinity does not change with depth due to heating/cooling, only by mixing/evaporation/precipitation. ### 2.2 Temperature ($T$) The ocean is **thermally stratified** into three layers: - **Surface Mixed Layer (0 – 200 m):** Temperature varies diurnally and seasonally (15°C – 30°C tropical, near 0°C polar) - **Thermocline (200 – 1000 m):** Rapid temperature drop (up to 20°C/km in tropics) - **Deep Layer (>1000 m):** Nearly isothermal at **2°C – 4°C** globally For Arctic/Antarctic operations, water can reach **-1.9°C** (freezing point of seawater) and ice formation becomes a critical design constraint. ### 2.3 Hydrostatic Pressure ($P$) Pressure at depth $D$ in seawater: $$P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot D$$ Where $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho \approx 1025$ kg/m³. **Engineering rule of thumb:** Pressure increases by approximately **1 atmosphere (101.325 kPa) every 10 metres** of depth. | Depth (m) | Pressure (atm) | Pressure (MPa) | |-----------|----------------|----------------| | 0 | 1 | 0.10 | | 100 | 11 | 1.13 | | 1000 | 101 | 10.23 | | 6000 | 601 | 60.84 | | 11000 (Mariana Trench) | 1101 | 111.50 | ### 2.4 Density ($\rho$) — The Most Critical Property Density of seawater is a function of $S$, $T$, and $P$: $$\rho = f(S, T, P)$$ Standard surface seawater: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (vs. fresh water: 1000 kg/m³). The **UNESCO 1983 (EOS-80) Equation of State** computes density as: $$\rho(S,T,P) = \frac{\rho(S,T,0)}{1 - P/K(S,T,P)}$$ where $K(S,T,P)$ is the **secant bulk modulus** of seawater. Approximate surface values: - Cold + Salty → **Dense** (sinks) - Warm + Fresh → **Light** (floats) ### 2.5 Sound Speed ($c$) Sound travels ~4.5× faster in seawater than in air (~1500 m/s vs. 343 m/s). The simplified **Mackenzie (1981) formula** is KTU-relevant: $$c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^2 + 2.374 \times 10^{-4}T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 1.630 \times 10^{-2}D - 1.025 \times 10^{-2}T(S-35) - 7.139 \times 10^{-13}TD^3$$ Where $c$ in m/s, $T$ in °C, $S$ in PSU, $D$ in m. This equation shows: - $c$ increases with $T$ and $S$ - $c$ increases with depth $D$ (pressure effect) ### 2.6 Freezing Point of Seawater Unlike pure water (0°C), seawater freezes below 0°C. The UNESCO formula: $$T_f = -0.0137 - 0.051990S - 7.225 \times 10^{-9} S^3$$ For $S = 35$ PSU → $T_f \approx -1.86°C$ (this is why seawater never freezes at the surface in temperate zones — the salt depresses the freezing point). ### 2.7 Viscosity ($\mu$ and $\nu$) - **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$: resistance to flow (shear) - **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$: ratio of viscous to inertial forces For seawater at 0°C: $\mu \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s At 25°C: $\mu \approx 0.97 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s Approximation (Siedler, 1971): $$\mu(S,T) = \mu_w(T) \cdot [1 + A(T)S + B(T)S^{3/2}]$$ where $\mu_w(T)$ is fresh water viscosity at temperature $T$. ### 2.8 Thermal Conductivity ($k$) & Specific Heat ($C_p$) - $k \approx 0.6$ W/m·K (only ~25× better insulator than air; 25× worse conductor than copper) - $C_p \approx 4000$ J/kg·K (vs. air = 1005; water stores enormous thermal energy) - **Implication:** Ocean is a massive thermal reservoir — submersible thermal design must dump heat against ~4000 J/kg·K heat capacity, but the *low* thermal conductivity of water itself means heat transfer is limited by the boundary layer. ### 2.9 Electrical Conductivity ($\sigma$) Seawater is **highly conductive**: - $\sigma \approx 4$ to 5 S/m (Siemens per metre) - Compared to fresh water ($\sim 0.01$ S/m) and copper wire ($5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m) — seawater is ~500 million× less conductive than copper, but **400,000× more conductive than pure fresh water**. **Implication:** Cathodic protection (zinc/sacrificial anodes) is mandatory for any submerged steel structure. ### 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — KTU 2024 ESE EXAM** > The table below consolidates every formula a student must memorize for this topic. | # | Property | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|----------|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_{atm} + \rho g D$ | $P$ [Pa], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $g$ [9.81 m/s²], $D$ [m] | Hull thickness design | | 2 | Sound Speed (Mackenzie) | $c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^2 + 2.374\!\times\!10^{-4}T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 1.630\!\times\!10^{-2}D$ | $c$ [m/s], $T$ [°C], $S$ [PSU], $D$ [m] | Sonar system design | | 3 | Density (UNESCO EOS) | $\rho(S,T,P) = \rho(S,T,0) / (1 - P/K)$ | $\rho$ [kg/m³], $K$ [Pa] | Buoyancy & stability | | 4 | Freezing Point | $T_f = -0.0137 - 0.051990S - 7.225\!\times\!10^{-9}S^3$ | $T_f$ [°C], $S$ [PSU] | Polar/Arctic design | | 5 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | $\nu$ [m²/s] | Reynolds number, drag | | 6 | Reynolds Number | $Re = VL/\nu$ | $V$ [m/s], $L$ [m] | Flow regime & drag | | 7 | Bulk Modulus (seawater) | $K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa | $K$ [Pa] | Hull compressibility | | 8 | Surface Tension (sea) | $\gamma \approx 0.072$ N/m | $\gamma$ [N/m] | Free-surface effects at periscope depth | | 9 | Specific Heat | $C_p \approx 4000$ J/kg·K | $C_p$ [J/kg·K] | Thermal management | | 10 | Electrical Conductivity | $\sigma \approx 4.8$ S/m | $\sigma$ [S/m] | Corrosion protection | | 11 | Pressure Rule of Thumb | $\Delta P \approx 1$ atm per 10 m | — | Quick depth estimation | | 12 | Sound Speed (at surface) | $c \approx 1500$ m/s | — | Order-of-magnitude check | > [!WARNING] > **EXAM PITFALL:** Students often confuse **dynamic viscosity $\mu$** (Pa·s) with **kinematic viscosity $\nu$** (m²/s). The Reynolds number uses $\nu$, NOT $\mu$. Mixing these up can cost **3 full marks** in a numerical problem. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth **Starting principle — Stevin's Law (Pascal's principle applied to a static fluid column):** Consider a vertical column of seawater of cross-sectional area $A$ and depth $D$. The weight of the water column: $$W = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot A \cdot D) \cdot g$$ The pressure exerted at the base of the column: $$P_{hydro} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot D \cdot g}{A} = \rho g D$$ **Adding the atmospheric pressure** acting on the top of the ocean: $$P_{total} = P_{atm} + P_{hydro}$$ $$\boxed{P_{total} = P_{atm} + \rho g D}$$ **Conversion to absolute pressure (Pa):** With $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², depth $D$ in metres: $$P_{total} = 101325 + (1025 \times 9.81 \times D)$$ $$P_{total} = 101325 + 10055.25 D \quad [\text{Pa}]$$ **Pressure per 10 m rule:** $\Delta P_{10m} = 10055.25 \times 10 \approx 100553$ Pa $\approx$ 1 atm. ✓ ### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Pressure at 500 m Depth **Given:** $S = 35$ PSU, $T = 10°C$, $D = 500$ m, surface $\rho \approx 1027$ kg/m³. **Step 1 — Hydrostatic component:** $$P_{hydro} = \rho g D = 1027 \times 9.81 \times 500$$ $$P_{hydro} = 1027 \times 4905 = 5{,}037{,}435 \text{ Pa} \approx 5.04 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Add atmospheric:** $$P_{total} = 101325 + 5{,}037{,}435 = 5{,}138{,}760 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to atm:** $$P_{total} = \frac{5{,}138{,}760}{101325} \approx 50.7 \text{ atm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Designer's check:** At 500 m, the hull must withstand ~50 atm. This is why even a small submersible requires a **spherical or cylindrical pressure hull** (geometrically optimal for pressure containment). ### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Sound Speed Calculation **Given:** $T = 20°C$, $S = 35$ PSU, $D = 1000$ m. Using the Mackenzie equation: $$c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^2 + 2.374 \times 10^{-4}T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 1.630 \times 10^{-2}D - 1.025 \times 10^{-2}T(S-35) - 7.139 \times 10^{-13}TD^3$$ **Step-by-step evaluation:** **Step A — Temperature terms:** $$4.591 \times 20 = 91.82$$ $$-0.05304 \times (20)^2 = -0.05304 \times 400 = -21.216$$ $$2.374 \times 10^{-4} \times (20)^3 = 2.374 \times 10^{-4} \times 8000 = 1.8992$$ **Step B — Salinity correction (S = 35 → term vanishes):** $$1.340 \times (35 - 35) = 0$$ **Step C — Depth term:** $$1.630 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000 = 16.30$$ **Step D — Cross-term (Temperature × Salinity):** $$-1.025 \times 10^{-2} \times 20 \times 0 = 0$$ **Step E — Depth-cubic cross term:** $$-7.139 \times 10^{-13} \times 20 \times (1000)^3 = -7.139 \times 10^{-13} \times 20 \times 10^9 = -1.4278 \times 10^{-2} \approx -0.0143$$ **Step F — Sum all components:** $$c = 1448.96 + 91.82 - 21.216 + 1.8992 + 0 + 16.30 + 0 - 0.0143$$ $$c = 1537.75 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{c \approx 1537.75 \text{ m/s at } D=1000 \text{ m}, T=20°C, S=35 \text{ PSU}}$$ **Verification:** This matches the standard reference value (~1538 m/s) within 0.02% — the Mackenzie equation is reliable for engineering estimates. ### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Freezing Point Calculation **Given:** A submersible operates in the **Arctic Ocean** at $S = 33$ PSU. **Step 1 — Apply UNESCO freezing point equation:** $$T_f = -0.0137 - 0.051990 \times 33 - 7.225 \times 10^{-9} \times (33)^3$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate each term:** $$-0.051990 \times 33 = -1.71567$$ $$(33)^3 = 35937$$ $$-7.225 \times 10^{-9} \times 35937 = -2.596 \times 10^{-4} = -0.0002596$$ **Step 3 — Sum:** $$T_f = -0.0137 - 1.71567 - 0.0002596 = -1.7296°C$$ $$\boxed{T_f \approx -1.73°C \text{ at } S = 33 \text{ PSU}}$$ **Engineering implication:** A surface-piercing periscope or air system in this water must handle ice formation at temperatures **above the actual ambient** — the seawater itself is the coldest stable fluid in the system. ### 3.5 Derived Parameter: Buoyancy Reserve For a submersible of volume $V$ and mass $m$: **Net buoyancy (force):** $$F_B = (\rho_{sea} - \rho_{sub}) \cdot V \cdot g$$ **Condition for neutral buoyancy:** $$F_B = 0 \implies \rho_{sub} = \rho_{sea}$$ **Practical design (manned submersible):** $$0.95 \rho_{sea} \leq \rho_{sub} \leq 1.05 \rho_{sea}$$ This requires a **variable ballast system** because $\rho_{sea}$ changes with depth by ~0.5% from surface to 6000 m. ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Surface vs. Deep Seawater > [!NOTE] > **KTU Comparison Table — Property Variation from Surface to Depth** | Property | Surface (0 m) | Deep (4000 m) | % Change | Design Implication | |----------|---------------|----------------|----------|---------------------| | Temperature | 20°C | 3°C | -85% | Electronics heat removal gets HARDER (smaller $\Delta T$) | | Pressure | 1 atm | 401 atm | +40000× | Hull design dominates all other concerns | | Density | 1025 kg/m³ | 1048 kg/m³ | +2.2% | Ballast tanks must compensate | | Sound Speed | 1525 m/s | 1538 m/s | +0.85% | Sonar refraction changes | | Viscosity | 1.00 × 10⁻³ | 1.65 × 10⁻³ | +65% | Drag increases at depth | | Electrical Cond. | 4.8 S/m | 4.0 S/m | -17% | Corrosion rate reduces slightly | | Light Penetration | Full spectrum | ZERO (<1000 m) | -100% | External cameras useless — must use sonar/light | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Ocean Stratification — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A1["SURFACE LAYER 0 to 200 m
Sun warmed mixed layer
Temperature 15 to 30 C
Salinity 33 to 37 PSU"]:::surf A2["THERMOCLINE 200 to 1000 m
Rapid temperature drop
Up to 20 C per km gradient
Sound channel SOFAR axis"]:::thermo A3["DEEP OCEAN below 1000 m
Near isothermal 2 to 4 C
High pressure 100 to 1100 atm
Zero sunlight aphotic zone"]:::deep A4["ABYSSAL ZONE 4000 to 6000 m
Near freezing temperatures
Pressure 400 to 600 atm
Hull collapse risk critical"]:::abyss A1 -->|"Buoyancy control
active range"| A2 A2 -->|"Hull compression begins"| A3 A3 -->|"Pressure hull is the
primary design driver"| A4 B1["Hull strength design
gated by P rho g D"]:::design1 B2["Thermal management
requires active cooling"]:::design2 B3["Acoustic systems
optimized for c profile"]:::design3 A2 -.->|"Informs"| B3 A3 -.->|"Informs"| B2 A4 -.->|"Informs"| B1 classDef surf fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef thermo fill:#7FB3D5,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000 classDef deep fill:#1B4F72,stroke:#0B1F3A,color:#FFF classDef abyss fill:#0B1F3A,stroke:#000000,color:#FFF classDef design1 fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#922B21,color:#000 classDef design2 fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000 classDef design3 fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#6C3483,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Property-Design Coupling Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Seawater Density rho"]:::p P2["Hydrostatic Pressure P"]:::p P3["Sound Speed c"]:::p P4["Temperature T"]:::p P5["Salinity S"]:::p P6["Viscosity mu nu"]:::p P7["Electrical Conductivity sigma"]:::p P8["Specific Heat Cp"]:::p D1["BUOYANCY AND STABILITY
Variable ballast system"]:::d D2["HULL STRENGTH
Collapse depth design"]:::d D3["SONAR AND ACOUSTICS
Refraction and ray paths"]:::d D4["THERMAL MANAGEMENT
Heat exchanger sizing"]:::d D5["CORROSION PROTECTION
Cathodic and coating"]:::d D6["HYDRODYNAMIC DRAG
Reynolds number analysis"]:::d D7["LIFE SUPPORT
O2 and CO2 cycling"]:::d P1 --> D1 P2 --> D2 P3 --> D3 P4 --> D3 P4 --> D4 P5 --> P7 P6 --> D6 P7 --> D5 P8 --> D4 P2 --> D7 P4 --> D7 classDef p fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000 classDef d fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#922B21,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Pressure-Depth-Density Coupling (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB STEP1["INPUTS
Salinity S in PSU
Temperature T in C
Depth D in m"]:::s1 STEP2["HYDROSTATIC P
P atmp plus rho g D
Increase 1 atm per 10 m"]:::s2 STEP3["IN-SITU DENSITY
Use UNESCO EOS 80
rho as f of S T P"]:::s3 STEP4["DESIGN OUTPUTS
Hull collapse depth
Ballast compensation
Sonar accuracy"]:::s4 STEP1 --> STEP2 STEP2 --> STEP3 STEP3 --> STEP4 FEED["FEEDBACK LOOP
Density at depth adjusts P
for compressible ocean"]:::fb STEP3 -.->|"Iterate for accuracy"| STEP2 classDef s1 fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000 classDef s2 fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#922B21,color:#000 classDef s3 fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000 classDef s4 fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#6C3483,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#F1948A,stroke:#641E16,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1.** *Define salinity of seawater. How is it practically measured? Why is it called a "conservative" property?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Salinity is the total mass of dissolved inorganic salts in one kilogram of seawater, expressed in **g/kg** or **PSU (Practical Salinity Units)**. Average ocean salinity is **35 PSU**. - **Measurement (1 mark):** Practically measured via **electrical conductivity ratio** using a CTD (Conductivity-Temperature-Depth) probe, referenced to **Standard Seawater (Copenhagen 1978)**. Pure water evaporative weighing is no longer the standard. - **Conservative nature (1 mark):** Salinity does not change with heating/cooling/pressure — only by **physical processes** like evaporation (↑), precipitation (↓), river inflow (↓), or ice formation (↑). It is a *tracer* of water masses. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q2.** *State the Mackenzie formula for sound speed in seawater. List the variables and their units, and explain the sign of the depth term.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Formula (2 marks):** $c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^2 + 2.374 \times 10^{-4}T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 1.630 \times 10^{-2}D - 1.025 \times 10^{-2}T(S-35) - 7.139 \times 10^{-13}TD^3$ - **Variables (½ mark):** $c$ in m/s, $T$ in °C, $S$ in PSU, $D$ in m. - **Sign of depth term (½ mark):** The coefficient $+1.630 \times 10^{-2}$ is **positive** — meaning sound speed *increases* with depth because higher pressure increases seawater's bulk modulus, making the medium "stiffer" acoustically. --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice, May 2024]** #### **Question A (14 marks)** **(a)** *Derive the expression for hydrostatic pressure at a depth D below the seawater surface. A spherical observation submersible operates at 2500 m depth in seawater of average density 1027 kg/m³. Calculate the absolute pressure on the hull and express it in (i) Pa, (ii) atm, and (iii) bar. State the design implication of this pressure on hull material selection.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Explain the vertical thermal structure of the ocean (surface mixed layer, thermocline, deep layer) with typical temperature values. Using the UNESCO freezing point equation, calculate the freezing point of seawater at S = 38 PSU (Red Sea). State two engineering implications for a polar-class submersible.* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (a)** **Step 1 — Derivation of hydrostatic pressure (3 marks):** Consider a cylindrical column of seawater of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $D$. The mass of the column: $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot D$ The weight of the column: $W = m \cdot g = \rho A D g$ The pressure at the base (force per unit area): $$P_{hydro} = \frac{W}{A} = \rho g D$$ Adding the atmospheric pressure acting on the free surface: $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g D$$ **Step 2 — Numerical calculation at 2500 m (3 marks):** Given: $D = 2500$ m, $\rho = 1027$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa. $$P_{hydro} = 1027 \times 9.81 \times 2500$$ $$P_{hydro} = 1027 \times 24525 = 25{,}187{,}175 \text{ Pa}$$ $$P_{abs} = 101325 + 25{,}187{,}175 = 25{,}288{,}500 \text{ Pa}$$ **Conversions (1 mark):** - (i) Pa: $\boxed{P = 2.53 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}}$ - (ii) atm: $P = 25{,}288{,}500 / 101325 = \boxed{249.6 \text{ atm}}$ - (iii) bar: $P = 25{,}288{,}500 / 100000 = \boxed{252.9 \text{ bar}}$ **Design implication (½ mark):** The hull must withstand ~250 atm external pressure. This mandates a **spherical titanium or HY-80/HY-100 steel pressure hull** with a safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0**, giving a yield strength requirement of ~600 MPa minimum. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (b)** **Step 1 — Ocean thermal structure (3 marks):** | Layer | Depth | Temperature | Characteristics | |-------|-------|-------------|-----------------| | **Surface Mixed Layer** | 0–200 m | 15°C – 30°C (varies with latitude/season) | Wind-mixed, diurnal/seasonal variation | | **Permanent Thermocline** | 200–1000 m | 20°C → 4°C (rapid drop, ~20°C/km) | Stable density gradient | | **Deep Layer** | > 1000 m | ~2°C – 4°C (nearly isothermal) | Polar-origin water masses | A diagram of the temperature-vs-depth profile (thermocline) is **mandatory** for full marks. **Step 2 — Freezing point at S = 38 PSU (2 marks):** Using $T_f = -0.0137 - 0.051990S - 7.225 \times 10^{-9}S^3$: - Term 1: $-0.0137$ - Term 2: $-0.051990 \times 38 = -1.97562$ - Term 3: $(38)^3 = 54872$; $-7.225 \times 10^{-9} \times 54872 = -3.964 \times 10^{-4} = -0.0003964$ $$T_f = -0.0137 - 1.97562 - 0.0003964 = -1.9897°C \approx -1.99°C$$ $$\boxed{T_f \approx -1.99°C \text{ at } S = 38 \text{ PSU}}$$ **Step 3 — Engineering implications (2 marks):** 1. **Hull and external fittings** (e.g., periscope, mast, hydroplanes) must use materials and coatings that remain ductile at $-2°C$ (e.g., avoid brittle fracture — specify ASTM A36 or equivalent low-temperature steel). 2. **Ballast and trim systems** must prevent ice formation in vents/valves — heating elements or freeze-resistant hydraulic fluids (e.g., phosphate-ester-based) are required in ballast tanks exposed to surface water. --- #### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** *Define density of seawater. Using the UNESCO EOS-80 framework, explain how in-situ density varies with depth for typical ocean conditions. Calculate the hydrostatic pressure at 6000 m depth assuming average seawater density of 1030 kg/m³. Why is this depth significant for submersible design?* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Explain the term "equation of state" for seawater. List four physical properties of seawater that depend on salinity and temperature. Compute the sound speed in seawater at T = 5°C, S = 34 PSU, D = 200 m using the Mackenzie equation. Discuss how this sound speed affects sonar performance in cold, mid-latitude waters.* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (a)** **Step 1 — Definition of density (1 mark):** Seawater density $\rho$ is the mass per unit volume, in kg/m³. For standard surface seawater ($S=35$ PSU, $T=0$°C), $\rho = 1028$ kg/m³. **Step 2 — UNESCO EOS-80 framework (2 marks):** The **Equation of State** relates density to the three state variables: $$\rho(S,T,P) = \frac{\rho(S,T,0)}{1 - P/K(S,T,P)}$$ where $\rho(S,T,0)$ is the surface density and $K(S,T,P)$ is the **secant bulk modulus**. As depth (and hence $P$) increases, the denominator decreases, so $\rho$ **increases** with depth. Typical increase: surface $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ → 6000 m depth $\rho \approx 1050$ kg/m³ (~2.4% increase). **Step 3 — Pressure calculation (2 marks):** $$P = 101325 + 1030 \times 9.81 \times 6000 = 101325 + 60{,}625{,}800 = 60{,}727{,}125 \text{ Pa} \approx 60.7 \text{ MPa}$$ $$P \approx \frac{60{,}727{,}125}{101325} \approx 599.3 \text{ atm} \approx 607 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 4 — Design significance (2 marks):** 6000 m is the **abyssal operating depth** for most modern deep-submergence vehicles (e.g., *Alvin*, *Shinkai 6500*). It represents: - The depth where the **compression strength of HY-80 steel** is fully utilized - The depth where most **oceanic trenches** (except the deepest 6) are accessible - A practical compromise between payload capacity and crush-depth safety margin --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (b)** **Step 1 — Equation of state definition (1 mark):** An **equation of state** is a thermodynamic relation expressing a state property (e.g., density) as a function of the independent state variables (salinity $S$, temperature $T$, pressure $P$) for a substance in equilibrium. **Step 2 — Four $S,T$-dependent properties (2 marks):** 1. **Density** $\rho$ (↑ with $S$, ↓ with $T$) 2. **Sound speed** $c$ (↑ with both $S$ and $T$) 3. **Freezing point** $T_f$ (↓ with $S$) 4. **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ (↑ with $S$, ↓ with $T$) 5. *(Bonus: surface tension, specific heat, electrical conductivity)* **Step 3 — Sound speed calculation (3 marks):** Given: $T=5°C$, $S=34$ PSU, $D=200$ m. $$c = 1448.96 + 4.591(5) - 0.05304(5)^2 + 2.374 \times 10^{-4}(5)^3 + 1.340(34-35) + 1.630 \times 10^{-2}(200) - 1.025 \times 10^{-2}(5)(34-35) - 7.139 \times 10^{-13}(5)(200)^3$$ **Term-by-term:** - $1448.96$ - $+22.955$ - $-1.326$ - $+0.0297$ - $-1.340$ - $+3.260$ - $+0.05125$ (note: $-1.025\times10^{-2}\times 5\times -1 = +0.05125$) - $-2.856\times10^{-5}$ (negligible) **Sum:** $$c = 1448.96 + 22.955 - 1.326 + 0.0297 - 1.340 + 3.260 + 0.05125 \approx 1472.59 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{c \approx 1472.6 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 4 — Sonar performance discussion (1 mark):** In cold water ($T=5°C$), the sound speed is **lower (~1473 m/s vs 1538 m/s in warm deep water)**. This causes: - **Increased sound absorption** at high frequencies (cold water absorbs HF faster) - **Strong downward refraction** near the surface (sound bends toward colder, slower layers) - **Reduced sonar detection range** for HF sonars; LF sonars perform relatively better --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > **EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS** | # | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | How to Avoid | |---|----------------|------------|--------------| | 1 | Forgetting to add $P_{atm}$ in pressure calculations | -2 to -3 | Always write $P = P_{atm} + \rho g D$ explicitly | | 2 | Confusing dynamic ($\mu$) and kinematic ($\nu$) viscosity in Reynolds number | -2 | Reynolds number uses $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | | 3 | Using density of fresh water (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³) | -1 to -2 | Memorize $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³ | | 4 | Sign error in Mackenzie equation depth cross-term | -1 | Re-derive carefully; check dimensions | | 5 | Not stating units in final answer | -1 to -2 | Always end with units in box | | 6 | Mixing up PSU and % for salinity | -1 | 1 PSU ≠ 1%; 35 PSU = 3.5% by mass | | 7 | Omitting the thermocline diagram in temperature question | -2 | Sketch the T-vs-D profile; examiners *expect* a diagram | | 8 | Not mentioning Standard Seawater (Copenhagen) for salinity definition | -1 | Always cite the reference standard | --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM** - ✅ The ocean is characterized by **four state variables**: $S, T, P, D$ (and their derivatives yield all other properties) - ✅ **Salinity** $S$ = 35 PSU average, measured by conductivity, conservative - ✅ **Temperature** $T$: Surface (15-30°C) → Thermocline (drop) → Deep (2-4°C) - ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure** = $P_{atm} + \rho g D$; rule of thumb: **1 atm per 10 m** - ✅ **Density** $\rho \approx 1025$ kg/m³ at surface, increases with depth (UNESCO EOS-80) - ✅ **Sound speed** $c \approx 1500$ m/s, varies with $S, T, D$ (Mackenzie) - ✅ **Freezing point** $T_f \approx -1.86°C$ at $S=35$ PSU (UNESCO) - ✅ **Viscosity** $\mu$ decreases with $T$, increases with $S$; $\nu = \mu/\rho$ for Reynolds number - ✅ **Specific heat** $C_p \approx 4000$ J/kg·K — ocean is a massive thermal reservoir - ✅ **Electrical conductivity** $\sigma \approx 4.8$ S/m — cathodic protection mandatory - ✅ The design chain: **Seawater property → Submersible design subsystem** - ✅ **Buoyancy** → Density; **Hull strength** → Pressure; **Sonar** → Sound speed; **Cooling** → $C_p$; **Corrosion** → $\sigma$ - ✅ Memorize the **12-row KTU Formula Cheat Sheet** in Section 2.9 - ✅ Always include units in boxed answers; always sketch the thermocline profile when asked about temperature structure - ✅ **Arctic/Polar submersibles** must handle $T_f$ ≈ -1.9°C and ice loading on external fittings - ✅ **Deep submersibles (>6000 m)** are governed by collapse depth → sphere/toroid geometry, Ti-6Al-4V or HY-100 steel --- > [!NOTE] > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — OENST723 BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES** > *Topic: Environment - Physical Properties of Sea Water* > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Standards*

topics.title: Dynamical Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamical Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
In the context of submersible (submarine, ROV, AUV) engineering, **Dynamical Processes** refer to the time-dependent response of an underwater vehicle subjected to hydrodynamic excitation forces, environmental disturbances, and control inputs across its **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)**. These processes encompass the coupled translational (surge, sway, heave) and rotational (roll, pitch, yaw) motions governed by Newton's second law in a fluid medium, where the surrounding water reacts by contributing **added mass**, **damping**, and **restoring** effects that fundamentally alter the vehicle's response compared to its in-air dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Dynamical Processes form the *foundation* for all subsequent modules covering stability, control, maneuvering, and structural loading. Mastery of 6-DOF notation and the form of the equation of motion is **non-negotiable** for ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Submerged Pendulum"
Imagine a wooden block suspended by a spring in air — it bounces with a natural frequency determined purely by its mass and the spring stiffness. Now submerge the same block in water. Suddenly, the block feels "heavier" to motion (the water must be pushed aside — this is **added mass**), the motion gets resisted as if dragging through molasses (this is **hydrodynamic damping**), and a new "spring" effect appears from buoyancy gradients (**hydrostatic restoring force**). This submerged-spring analogy captures the essence of submersible dynamics: every motion is a battle between inertia, damping, restoring, and external forcing — all happening in a fluid that "fights back."

### The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) — Standard Notation

| DOF | Translation / Rotation | Linear / Angular | Symbol |
|-----|------------------------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | Surge (forward) | Translation along X | $u$ |
| 2 | Sway (sideways) | Translation along Y | $v$ |
| 3 | Heave (vertical) | Translation along Z | $w$ |
| 4 | Roll (about X) | Rotation about X | $p$ |
| 5 | Pitch (about Y) | Rotation about Y | $q$ |
| 6 | Yaw (about Z) | Rotation about Z | $r$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coordinate Convention (SNAME / KTU Standard):**
> - Origin **O** is placed at the vehicle's center of gravity (CG) or geometric center.
> - **X-axis** points forward (bow), **Y-axis** points to starboard (right), **Z-axis** points downward.
> - Positive rotation follows the **right-hand rule**.

### Why Dynamical Processes Matter in Submersible Design
1. **Maneuvering Prediction** — Will the submersible turn in 30 seconds or 3 minutes?
2. **Stability Assessment** — Will it right itself after a disturbance, or capsize?
3. **Structural Integrity** — Peak dynamic loads drive hull thickness.
4. **Control System Design** — Autopilots and thrusters are designed based on dynamic models.
5. **Crew/Mission Safety** — Human occupants and sensitive equipment have tolerance limits for accelerations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generalized Equation of Motion (1-DOF Form)

For any single degree of freedom, a submersible's dynamic response is described by a **second-order linear differential equation** with constant coefficients:

$$(m + m_a)\,\ddot{x}(t) + B\,\dot{x}(t) + C\,x(t) = F(t)$$

Where:
- $m$ = **rigid-body mass** of the submersible
- $m_a$ = **hydrodynamic added mass** (inertial coupling with the fluid)
- $B$ = **hydrodynamic damping coefficient** (energy dissipation to the fluid)
- $C$ = **hydrodynamic restoring coefficient** (buoyancy/gravity gradient)
- $F(t)$ = **external excitation force** (waves, currents, control input)
- $x(t), \dot{x}(t), \ddot{x}(t)$ = displacement, velocity, acceleration

### 2.2 The Three Force Categories — A Logic Breakdown

**A. Inertial Forces** (resist acceleration)
- The fluid surrounding the vehicle has inertia. To accelerate, the vehicle must also accelerate a "virtual" mass of fluid.
- This is captured by $m_a$, called **added mass** or **virtual mass**.
- The total inertial term becomes $(m + m_a)$.
- *Analogy:* A heavy bowling ball feels light underwater initially, but accelerating it still requires effort because the water must move.

**B. Damping Forces** (dissipate energy)
- Energy is lost via **viscous drag**, **vortex shedding**, and **wave radiation** from the moving body.
- Represented by $B\dot{x}$ — proportional to velocity.
- *Analogy:* The "stickiness" of the fluid that slows the motion.

**C. Restoring Forces** (provide stability)
- For a *submerged* vehicle at constant depth, pure translation has **zero hydrostatic restoring force** in surge/sway (the buoyancy doesn't change).
- However, **heave** and **rotational DOFs** (roll, pitch) have strong restoring forces from metacentric stability.
- Represented by $C x$ — proportional to displacement.

**D. Excitation Forces** $F(t)$ (drive the motion)
- Environmental: wave forces, current forces, vortex-induced vibrations.
- Operational: propeller thrust, control surface deflections, winch tension.

### 2.3 Free Decay vs. Forced Oscillation

- **Free Decay Test** ($F(t) = 0$): The vehicle is given an initial displacement and released. The amplitude decays exponentially at the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$.
- **Forced Oscillation** ($F(t) = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$): The vehicle responds at the **forcing frequency** $\omega$. Resonance occurs near $\omega_n$ if damping is low.

### 2.4 Natural Frequency, Damping Ratio, and Period

The **undamped natural frequency** of the system is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{C}{m + m_a}}$$

The **damping ratio** (a dimensionless number) is:

$$\zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{C(m + m_a)}}$$

Classification:
- $\zeta = 0$ → Undamped (oscillates forever)
- $0 < \zeta < 1$ → **Underdamped** (oscillates with decay — typical for submersibles)
- $\zeta = 1$ → Critically damped (fastest return without oscillation)
- $\zeta > 1$ → Overdamped (sluggish, no oscillation)

The **damped natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $(m + m_a)\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Cx = F(t)$ | 1-DOF equation of motion | N (force balance) |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{C / (m + m_a)}$ | Undamped natural frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $\zeta = B / \left[2\sqrt{C(m+m_a)}\right]$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Damped natural frequency | rad/s |
| 5 | $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$ | Natural period | seconds |
| 6 | $x_p(t) = X_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$ | Particular (steady-state) solution | meters |
| 7 | $X_p / (F_0/C) = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | Magnification factor (where $r = \omega/\omega_n$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $m_a = k \cdot \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Added mass estimate (potential-flow) | kg |
| 9 | $C_z = \rho g A_w$ | Heave restoring force coefficient | N/m |
| 10 | $G M_T = K B + B M_T - K G$ | Transverse metacentric height (roll stability) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> In formula 8, $\rho$ is fluid density, $\nabla$ is displaced volume, and $k$ is an added-mass coefficient (depends on body shape — e.g., $k \approx 0.5$ for a sphere, $\approx 1.0$ for a flat plate in heave).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Submarine designers** use these equations to predict the **depth-keeping response** of a ballast system after disturbance.
- **ROV pilots** rely on dynamic models for **station-keeping** (holding position despite currents).
- **AUV mission planners** need decay times to know how quickly the vehicle stabilizes between waypoints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 1-DOF Equation of Motion in Heave (Vertical)

We will derive the equation of motion for a fully submerged body of revolution oscillating in the **vertical (heave) direction** under small-amplitude motion. This is a *classic KTU 14-mark derivation* and demonstrates the interplay of all three hydrodynamic coefficients.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram and Newton's Second Law**

Sum of vertical forces on the body = mass × vertical acceleration.

The forces acting vertically are:
1. Gravity: $-W$ (downward, by convention negative in our Z-down axis)
2. Buoyancy: $+B_f$ (upward, opposing weight at equilibrium)
3. Hydrodynamic damping: $-B_z \dot{z}$ (opposes velocity)
4. Hydrodynamic added-mass force: $-m_{a,z} \ddot{z}$ (opposes acceleration)
5. External excitation: $+F_z(t)$

At equilibrium (static), $W = B_f$, so these cancel.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law**

$$m \ddot{z} = -m_{a,z} \ddot{z} - B_z \dot{z} - C_z z + F_z(t)$$

The hydrostatic restoring force $C_z z$ arises because, as the body moves up by $z$, the displaced volume decreases, reducing buoyancy by $\rho g A_w z$ (where $A_w$ is the waterplane area projected horizontally).

**Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Form**

Move the added-mass term to the left side:

$$(m + m_{a,z}) \ddot{z} + B_z \dot{z} + C_z z = F_z(t)$$

This is the **standard 1-DOF heave equation** used universally in submersible dynamics.

**Step 4 — Solve the Homogeneous (Free-Decay) Equation**

Set $F_z(t) = 0$. Assume a solution of the form $z(t) = Z_0 e^{\lambda t}$:

$$(m + m_{a,z}) \lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + B_z \lambda e^{\lambda t} + C_z e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

Dividing through by $e^{\lambda t}$ (which is never zero):

$$(m + m_{a,z}) \lambda^2 + B_z \lambda + C_z = 0$$

This is the **characteristic equation**. Using the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{-B_z \pm \sqrt{B_z^2 - 4(m + m_{a,z})C_z}}{2(m + m_{a,z})}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Three Solution Regimes**

Define the **undamped natural frequency** and **damping ratio**:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{C_z}{m + m_{a,z}}} \quad ; \quad \zeta = \frac{B_z}{2\sqrt{C_z(m + m_{a,z})}}$$

Then the characteristic roots are:

$$\lambda = -\zeta \omega_n \pm \omega_n \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Regime A — Underdamped** ($0 < \zeta < 1$, typical for submersibles):

$$\lambda_{1,2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm i \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm i \omega_d$$

The displacement decays as:

$$z(t) = e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \left[ A \cos(\omega_d t) + B \sin(\omega_d t) \right]$$

The amplitude envelope decays as $e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}$, and the oscillation occurs at the **damped natural frequency** $\omega_d$.

**Regime B — Critically Damped** ($\zeta = 1$):

$$z(t) = (A + B t) e^{-\omega_n t}$$

The body returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without oscillating.

**Regime C — Overdamped** ($\zeta > 1$):

$$z(t) = A e^{\lambda_1 t} + B e^{\lambda_2 t}$$

Slow, non-oscillatory return. (Rarely encountered in well-designed submersibles.)

**Step 6 — Solve the Forced (Steady-State) Response**

Let $F_z(t) = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$. Assume a particular solution $z_p(t) = X_p \cos(\omega t - \phi)$.

Substituting back into the original ODE and matching coefficients:

$$X_p = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{[C_z - (m + m_{a,z})\omega^2]^2 + (B_z \omega)^2}}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \frac{B_z \omega}{C_z - (m + m_{a,z})\omega^2} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Non-Dimensional Magnification Factor**

Define the frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n$. The **magnification factor** (response amplitude / static deflection) is:

$$\frac{X_p}{F_0/C_z} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2 \zeta r)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** At $r = 0$ (quasi-static), the magnification is 1. At $r = 1$ (resonance, $\zeta$ small), the magnification peaks at $1/(2\zeta)$. For $\zeta = 0.05$ (lightly damped), the peak is **10× the static deflection** — this is why KTU examiners love this topic.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A submarine model of mass $m = 8000$ kg has a heave added mass $m_a = 4000$ kg, a damping coefficient $B = 6000$ N·s/m, and a restoring coefficient $C = 50{,}000$ N/m. Find (a) natural frequency, (b) damping ratio, and (c) the magnification factor when excited at $\omega = 2$ rad/s.

**Solution (a):**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{50{,}000}{8000 + 4000}} = \sqrt{\frac{50{,}000}{12{,}000}} = \sqrt{4.1667} \approx 2.041 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\zeta = \frac{6000}{2\sqrt{50{,}000 \times 12{,}000}} = \frac{6000}{2 \sqrt{6 \times 10^8}} = \frac{6000}{2 \times 24{,}495} \approx 0.1225$$

Since $\zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — oscillations will occur.

**Solution (c):**

First compute $r = \omega / \omega_n = 2 / 2.041 \approx 0.98$. This is very close to resonance.

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.98^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.1225 \times 0.98)^2}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.9604)^2 + (0.2401)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(0.0396)^2 + 0.0576}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.00157 + 0.0576}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.05917}} \approx \frac{1}{0.2432} \approx 4.11$$

**Interpretation:** Even well below resonance, the magnification is 4×. A careful KTU student should always comment on whether the system is **near-resonance** in their final answer to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Dynamical Process Flow for a Submersible

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["EXTERNAL EXCITATION F(t)"] --> B["SUBMERSIBLE DYNAMICAL SYSTEM"]
    C["CURRENT FORCES"] --> B
    D["WAVE FORCES"] --> B

    B1["MASS MATRIX M_plus_Ma"] --> B
    B2["DAMPING MATRIX B"] --> B
    B3["RESTORING MATRIX C"] --> B
    B --> F["EQUATION OF MOTION SOLVER"]
    F --> G["6-DOF RESPONSE x_dot_x_ddot"]
    G --> H1["SURGE u"]
    G --> H2["SWAY v"]
    G --> H3["HEAVE w"]
    G --> H4["ROLL p"]
    G --> H5["PITCH q"]
    G --> H6["YAW r"]
    H1 --> I["MOTION FEEDBACK TO PILOT_AND_CONTROLLER"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    I --> E
```

```mermaid
    A2["DAMPING TERM B_x_dot"] --> SUM

    SUM --> OUT["NET FORCE ON BODY"]

```
