$$F_n \;=\; \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \;=\; \dfrac{6.173}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 90}}$$

$$F_n \;=\; \dfrac{6.173}{\sqrt{882.9}} \;=\; \dfrac{6.173}{29.71} \;=\; 0.2078$$

**Step (iii) Frictional resistance:**

$$\log_{10} R_n \;=\; \log_{10}(4.668 \times 10^{8}) \;=\; 8.6693$$

$$C_F \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(8.6693 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{44.49} \;=\; 1.686 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (6.173)^{2} \times 1700 \times 1.686 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F \;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 38.10 \times 1700 \times 1.686 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F \;=\; 512.5 \times 38.10 \times 1700 \times 1.686 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 5.60 \times 10^{4}\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 56\,\text{kN}$$

**Step (iv) Wave-making resistance:**

$$C_W \;=\; 0.0003 \times (0.2078)^{4.5}$$

*Intermediate:* $(0.2078)^{4} = 0.001864$, $(0.2078)^{0.5} = 0.4559$ → product = 0.0008497

$$C_W \;=\; 0.0003 \times 0.0008497 \;\approx\; 2.55 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$R_W \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 38.10 \times 1700 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-7} \;\approx\; 8.47\,\text{N}$$

*Text row:* This is the textbook answer for **low-$F_n$ surface operation** — at $F_n = 0.21$, wave resistance is **negligible** (~0.015 % of $R_F$). This is why submarines can transit on the surface at low-to-moderate speeds with relatively little wave penalty.

**Step (v) Effective power (appendages neglected):**

$$P_E \;=\; (R_F + R_W) \cdot V \;=\; (56000 + 8.5) \times 6.173$$

$$P_E \;\approx\; 56008.5 \times 6.173 \;\approx\; 3.458 \times 10^{5}\,\text{W} \;\approx\; 346\,\text{kW}$$

*Text row:* The **V³ scaling** is clearly visible — for 18 knots (3× power), $P_E$ would rise to ~3.1 MW.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Surface vs. Submerged Performance

| Parameter | On the Surface | Fully Submerged |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Dominant resistance | Wave-making at high $F_n$ | Viscous (form + friction) only |
| Critical dimensionless | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | $R_n = VL/\nu$ |
| Hull-form $C_B$ optimum | 0.55–0.65 (typical ship) | 0.40–0.50 (slender sub) |
| Free surface effects (sloshing) | Significant (tanks, voids) | None |
| Cavitation risk | Severe (prop near surface) | Reduced (depth-pressure) |
| Radar/visual signature | High | Minimal |
| Speed–length optimum | $F_n \approx 0.40$ ceiling | Limited by propulsion only |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Surface Resistance Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Surface Resistance RT] --> B[Frictional Resistance RF]
    A --> C[Wave-Making Resistance RW]
    A --> D[Appendage Resistance RAPP]
    A --> E[Air Resistance RAA]
    A --> F[Bulbous Bow Resistance RB]

    B --> B1[ITTC 1957 Line]
    B --> B2[Wetted Surface S]
    B --> B3[Dynamic Pressure 0.5 rho Vsq]

    C --> C1[Froude Number Fn]
    C --> C2[Hull Slenderness L over B]
    C --> C3[Block Coefficient CB]

    D --> D1[Sail Drag]
    D --> D2[Fin and Rudder Drag]
    D --> D3[Propeller Hub Drag]

    E --> E1[Aerodynamic Hull Drag]
    E --> E2[Sail Wind Drag]

    F --> F1[Wave Breaking]
    F --> F2[Interference with Hull]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Surface Operation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Submarine Departs Port] --> A2{Battery Charged?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Submerge via Main Ballast]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Surface Transit on Diesels]
    A4 --> A5[Recharge Batteries via Snorkel]
    A5 --> A6[Ready for Dive]

    A4 --> A5b[Surface Drag Dominates]
    A5b --> A5c[Wave Making plus Friction plus Air]
    A5c --> A6

    A6 --> A7[Submerged Operation Begins]
    A7 --> A8[Low Drag Submerged Mode]
    A8 --> A9[High Endurance Transit]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Submarine at the Waterline (Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Pressure Hull Cylinder] --> L2[Free Flooding Outer Casing]
    L2 --> L3[Ballast Tanks Port and Stbd]
    L2 --> L4[Sail and Conning Tower]
    L2 --> L5[Control Surfaces Fairwater Planes]
    L2 --> L6[Propeller Shaft and Nozzle]
    L7[Waterline WL] --- L1
    L7 --- L2
    L7 --- L3
    L7 --- L5

    L1 --> L1a[Internal Payload and Crew]
    L4 --> L4a[Periscopes and Masts Retracted]
    L5 --> L5a[Stern Planes and Rudder]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Resistance Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input Submarine Geometry] --> S2[Compute Wetted Surface S]
    S1 --> S3[Compute Length L and Beam B]
    S2 --> S4[Compute Reynolds Number Rn]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Apply ITTC 1957 Correlation]
    S5 --> S6[Frictional Resistance RF]

    S3 --> S7[Compute Froude Number Fn]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Wave Regression CW]
    S8 --> S9[Wave Resistance RW]

    S6 --> S10[Sum Total Resistance RT]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Effective Power PE]
    S11 --> S12[Delivered Power PD via Efficiencies]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Faithfulness:** The Mermaid blocks map *cause → effect* in the resistance-calculation pipeline and the operational workflow. The cross-section block shows the geometric relationship between the pressure hull, free-flooding casing, and the waterline — all using safe alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `L1`, `S5`, `A1`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
*List the major components of total resistance experienced by a submarine when running on the surface. Which one dominates at low Froude numbers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The total surface resistance $R_T$ consists of **(1) frictional resistance $R_F$**, **(2) wave-making resistance $R_W$**, **(3) appendage resistance $R_{APP}$**, **(4) air resistance $R_{AA}$**, and **(5) bulbous-bow resistance $R_B$** (the last is negligible for submarines). At **low Froude numbers** ($F_n < 0.20$), wave-making resistance is negligible and **frictional resistance $R_F$ dominates**, accounting for typically 75–85 % of the total.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is the block coefficient of a submarine hull (≈0.45) significantly lower than that of a merchant ship (≈0.65)? What is the hydrodynamic consequence?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A submarine must minimise both **form drag (submerged)** and **wave-making drag (surfaced)** while maximising internal volume. A lower block coefficient means a **slender, finer hull** with length-to-beam ratio $L/B \approx 7$–$10$. The hydrodynamic consequence is a **substantial reduction in wave-making resistance** (per Havelock's thin-ship theory, $R_W \propto B^2$) but a **larger wetted surface area for the same displacement**, which slightly increases frictional resistance. The net trade-off favours the slender form because wave resistance grows much faster ($F_n^4$–$F_n^5$) than frictional resistance at high $F_n$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark Part-B question carries sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Full step-by-step working is mandatory. Marks are awarded for *method*, *formula*, *substitution*, *evaluation*, and *units*.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 2]**

> A submarine model has length **L = 6 m**, wetted surface **S = 23 m²**, and is tested at a speed **V = 2.2 m/s** in fresh water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s).
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the Reynolds number, the Froude number, the frictional resistance coefficient (ITTC 1957), and the frictional resistance of the model.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Using the regression $C_W = 0.0003 F_n^{4.5}$, compute the wave-making resistance, the total resistance (neglecting appendages and air), and the effective power. Comment on whether the model is in the low-wave-drag regime.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Reynolds number**

$$R_n \;=\; \dfrac{VL}{\nu} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2 \times 6}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.32 \times 10^{7}$$

*(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for evaluation)*

**Step 2: Froude number**

$$F_n \;=\; \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 6}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2}{\sqrt{58.86}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.2}{7.672}$$

$$F_n \;=\; 0.2868$$

*(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for evaluation)*

**Step 3: ITTC 1957 frictional coefficient**

$$\log_{10} R_n \;=\; \log_{10}(1.32 \times 10^{7}) \;=\; 7.1206$$

$$C_F \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(7.1206 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{26.22} \;=\; 2.860 \times 10^{-3}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Frictional resistance**

$$R_F \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S C_F \;=\; 0.5 \times 1000 \times (2.2)^{2} \times 23 \times 2.860 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F \;=\; 500 \times 4.84 \times 23 \times 2.860 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 159.2\,\text{N}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 5: Wave resistance coefficient**

$$C_W \;=\; 0.0003 \times (0.2868)^{4.5}$$

*Intermediate:* $(0.2868)^4 = 0.00677$, $(0.2868)^{0.5} = 0.5355$ → $(0.2868)^{4.5} \approx 0.003626$

$$C_W \;=\; 0.0003 \times 0.003626 \;=\; 1.088 \times 10^{-6}$$

*(1 mark for intermediate, 1 mark for final)*

**Step 6: Wave resistance**

$$R_W \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S C_W \;=\; 500 \times 4.84 \times 23 \times 1.088 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R_W \;\approx\; 0.0605\,\text{N}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 7: Total resistance and effective power**

$$R_T \;\approx\; R_F + R_W \;=\; 159.2 + 0.0605 \;\approx\; 159.26\,\text{N}$$

$$P_E \;=\; R_T \cdot V \;=\; 159.26 \times 2.2 \;\approx\; 350.4\,\text{W}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 8: Comment [1 mark]**

> The model is in the **transitional-to-low wave-drag regime**. $F_n = 0.287$ is moderate; $R_W$ is only 0.04 % of $R_F$. The result confirms that for submarines on the surface, **frictional resistance overwhelmingly dominates** until $F_n > 0.40$.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

> A full-scale submarine has **L = 75 m**, wetted surface **S = 1500 m²**, surface displacement **Δ = 4000 tonnes**. The vessel runs on the surface at **V = 10 knots** in sea water (ρ = 1025 kg/m³, ν = 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s). A 7 % appendage allowance and a 3 % air-resistance allowance must be applied.
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compute the Reynolds number, Froude number, frictional resistance, and the appendage + air-resistance components.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Using $C_W = 0.0003 F_n^{4.5}$, find the wave-making resistance, the total surface resistance, the effective power, and the **delivered power** assuming $\eta_H = 1.05$ and $\eta_R = 1.00$.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Convert speed**

$$V \;=\; 10\,\text{knots} \times 0.5144 \;=\; 5.144\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Reynolds number**

$$R_n \;=\; \dfrac{5.144 \times 75}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 3.242 \times 10^{8}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Froude number**

$$F_n \;=\; \dfrac{5.144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 75}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.144}{\sqrt{735.75}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.144}{27.13} \;=\; 0.1897$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4: ITTC coefficient**

$$\log_{10} R_n \;=\; 8.5109$$

$$C_F \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(8.5109 - 2)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{42.44} \;=\; 1.768 \times 10^{-3}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5: Frictional resistance**

$$R_F \;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times (5.144)^{2} \times 1500 \times 1.768 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F \;=\; 512.5 \times 26.46 \times 1500 \times 1.768 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 35.96\,\text{kN}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6: Appendage and air-resistance components**

$$R_{APP} \;=\; 0.07 \times R_F \;=\; 0.07 \times 35.96 \;=\; 2.517\,\text{kN}$$

$$R_{AA} \;=\; 0.03 \times R_F \;=\; 0.03 \times 35.96 \;=\; 1.079\,\text{kN}$$

*(2 marks — 1 each)*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 7: Wave coefficient and resistance**

$$(0.1897)^{4.5} \;\approx\; 0.000598$$

$$C_W \;=\; 0.0003 \times 0.000598 \;=\; 1.794 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$R_W \;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 26.46 \times 1500 \times 1.794 \times 10^{-7} \;\approx\; 3.65\,\text{N}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 8: Total resistance**

$$R_T \;=\; 35.96 + 2.517 + 1.079 + 0.00365 \;\approx\; 39.56\,\text{kN}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 9: Effective power**

$$P_E \;=\; R_T \cdot V \;=\; 39560 \times 5.144 \;\approx\; 203.5\,\text{kW}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 10: Delivered power**

$$P_D \;=\; \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_H \eta_R} \;=\; \dfrac{203.5}{1.05 \times 1.00} \;\approx\; 193.8\,\text{kW}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 11: Final comment [0.5 mark]**

> At $F_n = 0.19$, the submarine is in the **low-wave-drag regime** ($R_W / R_F \approx 10^{-4}$). Effective power demand of ~204 kW is comfortably within a 1–2 MW diesel-generator envelope for a 4000 t vessel at 10 knots.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic (Senior Examiner Insight):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversions:** Forgetting to convert knots → m/s, or tonnes → kg. This alone costs **1–2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Wrong $\log_{10}$ vs. $\ln$:** The ITTC formula uses **base-10 logarithm**. Using natural log gives $C_F$ off by a factor of ~2.3.
> 3. **Forgetting $V^{2}$ vs. $V^{3}$:** Frictional power scales as $V^3$, NOT $V^2$. Students frequently compute $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ correctly but then naively double it.
> 4. **Appendage allowance baseline:** Always take it as a **percentage of bare-hull $R_T$**, not as a fixed absolute value.
> 5. **Confusing surface and submerged regimes:** On the surface, **wave-making is the new term**; submerged, it disappears. Mixing up the two loses the *application mark* (~2 marks).
> 6. **Block coefficient $C_B$ vs. prismatic $C_P$:** $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$; $C_P = \nabla / (A_M L)$. Mark examiners *do* test this distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Recall — "Submarines on the Surface"**
>
> ☐ **On the surface, a submarine is a surface ship** — supported by hydrostatic buoyancy, displacement = weight, freeboard positive.
> ☐ **Total resistance** = $R_F + R_W + R_{APP} + R_{AA} + R_B$.
> ☐ **Frictional resistance** uses the **ITTC 1957 line**: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10}R_n - 2)^{2}$.
> ☐ **Wave resistance** dominates at high $F_n$; negligible below $F_n \approx 0.20$.
> ☐ **Froude number** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ is the key surface scaling parameter.
> ☐ **Reynolds number** $R_n = VL / \nu$ governs viscous similarity.
> ☐ **Effective power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ scales as **$V^3$** — beware!
> ☐ **Delivered power** $P_D = P_E / (\eta_H \eta_R)$ — back to the propeller.
> ☐ **Block coefficient** of a submarine: **0.40–0.55** (slender, sub-optimised for submerged running).
> ☐ **Speed-length ratio** $V / \sqrt{L}$: typical surface range **0.45–0.85** (in knots/ft units).
> ☐ **Appendage allowance:** 5–10 % of $R_T$.
> ☐ **Air-resistance allowance:** 2–4 % of $R_T$ at low wind conditions.
> ☐ **Hull-speed barrier** is much less restrictive for submarines than for surface ships (because $C_B$ is smaller).
> ☐ **Surface penalty in SNR:** High surface RPM → cavitation → loud submarine → vulnerable.
> ☐ **Practical relevance:** Modern SSNs still surface (or use snorkel) to recharge batteries — surface resistance directly impacts patrol endurance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangement of the Main Ballast Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Course Code:** OENST723
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arrangement of the Main Ballast Tanks
> - **Cognitive Level Targets:** Understand (L2) → Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4)
> - **Aligned Course Outcomes (CO):** CO2 – Submarine Buoyancy and Stability Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs)** are large, free-flooding, hard-shell or soft-shell tanks located external to the pressure hull (in single-hull configurations) or between the pressure hull and the outer (light) hull (in double-hull configurations) of a submarine. Their primary function is to provide **variable buoyancy** so that the vessel can transition between the surfaced and submerged conditions by controlled flooding (diving) and blowing (surfacing) with compressed air.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MBTs are the **primary buoyancy-control volume** of a submersible. They account for the entire difference between the submarine's **surfaced displacement (Δₛ)** and its **submerged displacement (Δ_sub)**. In simple words, **the water that fills these tanks is exactly the weight that makes the submarine sink.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sealed plastic water bottle with a small air-tight cap on top**.

- When the bottle is **empty of water (full of air)** → it floats easily on the surface of a bathtub. The trapped air makes it light relative to the water it displaces. This is the **surfaced condition**.
- When you **open the cap and let water in** while holding it under water → water floods in from the bottom and pushes the air out from the top. The bottle becomes heavy, and if you let go, it sinks. This is the **submerged condition**.
- To bring it back up, you push air into the bottle from the top (through a straw). The compressed air pushes the water out from the bottom, the bottle becomes light again, and floats up. This is the **blowing operation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The plastic bottle = the **MBT**, the air pushed out the top = the **vent**, the water entering from the bottom = the **flooding opening**, and the straw pushing air in = the **blowing valve**. The submarine is essentially a giant, well-engineered version of this exact system.

---

## 1.3 Design Constraints (KTU Highlight)

Every KTU textbook and the KTU 2024 Module-2 descriptor emphasise these design numbers for the MBT arrangement:

- **MBT volume ≈ 10 % to 15 %** of the total submarine displacement volume.
- **Flooding time** (full dive, surface to periscope depth): typically **30 seconds to 2 minutes**.
- **Emergency blow time** (full surfacing from deep depth): typically **2 to 4 minutes** using high-pressure air.
- **Operating depth ratio**: MBTs are designed for hydrostatic pressure at the **crush depth** with a safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Metric (KTU Board Favourite):**
> For a surfaced displacement $\Delta_s$ of mass $m_s$, the total MBT displaced water mass $W_{MBT}$ required to drive the submarine from surfaced to fully submerged is:
> $$W_{MBT} = \Delta_{sub} - \Delta_s$$
> where the submarine is in **neutral buoyancy** when the MBTs are partially flooded such that displaced water mass equals total mass.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Role of the MBT in a Submarine

The submarine is, fundamentally, a **two-state buoyancy machine**:

- **State 1 — Surfaced (Positive Buoyancy):** MBTs are full of low-pressure air. Net buoyancy is **positive** (typically 10–20 % reserve buoyancy by volume).
- **State 2 — Submerged (Near-Neutral / Slightly Negative Buoyancy):** MBTs are full of seawater. The vessel's overall density equals or slightly exceeds the surrounding seawater density ($\rho_{sw} \approx 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ at the surface).

The MBT system is the **only routine mechanism** that changes the vessel's average density for the entire dive–surface cycle. The compensating tanks, trim tanks, and variable-ballast tanks handle *fine* adjustments, but the **MBT handles the gross transition**.

---

## 2.2 Governing Physical Principle — Archimedes & Variable Mass

The volume $V_{MBT}$ of the MBT system, when filled with seawater of density $\rho_{sw}$, displaces a mass equal to $\rho_{sw}\ V_{MBT}$. The weight of seawater inside the MBT also equals $\rho_{sw}\ V_{MBT}\ g$, so by Archimedes' principle the net effect is that the *additional weight* added to the submarine when the MBT floods is exactly **the weight of water admitted minus the weight of air vented out**. In practice, because the air vented is at near-atmospheric pressure and the air admitted (during blow) is at high pressure, the system behaviour is dominated by the **seawater mass**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula set** for any numerical or conceptual problem on the arrangement of Main Ballast Tanks in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the symbol–meaning mapping and the unit conventions.

| # | Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surfaced Displacement | $\Delta_s = \rho_{sw}\ g\ V_{submerged,\ hull}$ | tonnes (t) or kg | Weight of water displaced at surfaced draft |
| 2 | Submerged Displacement | $\Delta_{sub} = \Delta_s + W_{MBT}$ | tonnes (t) or kg | Weight of water displaced at full submergence |
| 3 | MBT water mass | $W_{MBT} = \rho_{sw}\ V_{MBT}$ | tonnes (t) or kg | Mass of seawater that floods the MBTs |
| 4 | Reserve Buoyancy (surfaced) | $RB = \Delta_{sub} - \Delta_s = W_{MBT}$ | tonnes (t) | Extra buoyancy the submarine carries at surface |
| 5 | Reserve Buoyancy fraction | $RB / \Delta_s = V_{MBT} / V_{hull}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.10 – 0.15 |
| 6 | Neutral buoyancy condition | $\Delta_{sub} = W_{vessel,\ fully\ flooded}$ | tonnes (t) | Condition for hovering at fixed depth |
| 7 | MBT volume (design) | $V_{MBT} = \dfrac{RB}{\rho_{sw}}$ | m³ | Total geometric volume of all MBT compartments |
| 8 | Blowing air pressure (HP) | $P_{HP} \ge P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\ g\ H_{max} + \Delta P_{margin}$ | Pa | Required high-pressure air to expel water at depth |
| 9 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth H | $P_{depth} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\ g\ H$ | Pa | Pressure at depth $H$ below waterline |
| 10 | Tank free-surface effect on GM | $GG' = -\dfrac{\rho_{sw}\ i}{W_{vessel}}$ | m | Loss of metacentric height due to free water in partially-flooded tank |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the Boussinesq free-surface rule**: a partially-flooded MBT *during the transition* is a free-water surface inside the hull. It temporarily reduces transverse stability, which is why divers maintain a strict **flooding sequence** to keep the free-surface effect minimised.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The arrangement of MBTs is not merely a buoyancy problem — it directly influences:

1. **Submarine hull form** (single-hull vs. double-hull layout).
2. **Resistance and speed** at the surfaced condition (MBT external surface adds wetted area).
3. **Signature and acoustic damping** (external tanks shield the pressure hull).
4. **Damage-control philosophy** (an external MBT breach is a *non-catastrophic* flooding event, because the tank is by design open to the sea).
5. **Maintenance and docking** (external MBTs are easily inspected and repaired in dry-dock).

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU loves this question:** It is a *systems-engineering* topic. Marks are awarded for explaining the **interaction** between hull form, valve system, and the buoyancy equation — not just reciting definitions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required MBT Volume from a Given Displacement

This is the **most commonly asked 14-mark numerical** in the KTU 2024 ESE for Module 2.

**Problem Statement (typical form):**
> A submarine of surfaced displacement $\Delta_s = 2400$ tonnes has a reserve buoyancy of **12 %** of surfaced displacement. The seawater density is $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Determine: (a) the mass of water that must be admitted to fully submerge the vessel, and (b) the total MBT volume required.

**Step 1 — Write down the definition of reserve buoyancy.**

The reserve buoyancy (RB) is the *extra* weight the submarine can carry while still floating at the surface. In conventional design, RB is expressed as a fraction of the surfaced displacement:

$$RB = 0.12 \times \Delta_s$$

Substituting the numerical value:

$$RB = 0.12 \times 2400\ \text{t}$$

$$RB = 288\ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass in SI units (kg).**

$$RB = 288\ \text{t} = 288\,000\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that the reserve buoyancy is exactly the mass of water the MBT must hold at full submergence.**

When the MBTs are completely filled, the weight of seawater in the MBTs = $W_{MBT}$. The submarine then has *zero* reserve buoyancy and is at the threshold of full submergence. Therefore:

$$W_{MBT} = RB = 288\,000\ \text{kg}$$

This is the direct answer to **part (a)** of the KTU question.

**Step 4 — Apply the density relation to find the MBT volume.**

The mass of seawater in a tank of volume $V_{MBT}$ is:

$$W_{MBT} = \rho_{sw}\ V_{MBT}$$

Rearranging for the volume:

$$V_{MBT} = \dfrac{W_{MBT}}{\rho_{sw}}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$V_{MBT} = \dfrac{288\,000\ \text{kg}}{1025\ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{MBT} \approx 280.98\ \text{m}^3 \;\approx\; 281\ \text{m}^3}$$

This is the answer to **part (b)**.

**Step 5 — Cross-check the design number.**

For a surfaced displacement of 2400 t, the total hull volume is approximately:

$$V_{hull} \approx \dfrac{\Delta_s}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{2\,400\,000}{1025} \approx 2341.5\ \text{m}^3$$

The MBT-to-hull volume ratio is therefore:

$$\dfrac{V_{MBT}}{V_{hull}} = \dfrac{281}{2341.5} \approx 0.12 \;=\; 12\,\%$$

This confirms the assumed design number and validates the calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required High-Pressure Air Pressure to Blow the MBT

This is the **second-most popular KTU numerical** and tests understanding of the pressure hierarchy in the blowing system.

**Problem Statement:**
> Find the minimum high-pressure (HP) air pressure required to start blowing the MBT of a submarine at an operating depth of $H = 200$ m. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $P_{atm} = 101.3\ \text{kPa}$, and a margin of $\Delta P_{margin} = 200\ \text{kPa}$ to overcome flow losses and to ensure the column of water in the tank is positively displaced.

**Step 1 — Compute the hydrostatic seawater pressure at the MBT inlet (depth H).**

$$P_{depth} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\ g\ H$$

Substituting:

$$P_{depth} = 101.3 \times 10^3 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 200$$

$$P_{depth} = 101\,300 + 2\,011\,050\ \text{Pa}$$

$$P_{depth} \approx 2.112\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Add the required margin for dynamic losses and the safety head.**

$$P_{HP,\ min} = P_{depth} + \Delta P_{margin}$$

$$P_{HP,\ min} = 2.112\ \text{MPa} + 0.200\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{HP,\ min} \approx 2.312\ \text{MPa} \;\approx\; 23.12\ \text{bar}}$$

**Step 3 — Practical design note.**

In real submarines, HP air is stored at **250–300 bar** in flasks, and is regulated down to a manifold pressure of approximately **25–40 bar** to feed the MBT blow valves. This manifold pressure comfortably exceeds the value derived above for any operational depth.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of MBT Arrangement Options

> [!NOTE]
> The following comparison matrix is **the single most important table** to commit to memory for the 14-mark descriptive KTU questions on MBT arrangement.

| Design Feature | Saddle Tank (Single-Hull) | Wing Tank (Double-Hull) | Combined Saddle + Wing | Free-Flooding Soft Tank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Top of pressure hull, straddling the casing | Sides, between pressure hull and outer hull | Saddle over casing + wing tanks on both sides | External to the pressure hull, open at bottom |
| **Hull Type** | Single-hull submarines (older, e.g. Type VII U-boat) | Modern double-hull submarines | Modern nuclear submarines (e.g. Virginia class top tanks) | Older coastal / midget submarines |
| **Volume efficiency** | Moderate (limited by hull curvature) | High (rectangular cross-section possible) | Highest (uses all available envelope) | Highest packing factor, but limited depth |
| **Reserve Buoyancy fraction** | 8 – 12 % | 12 – 15 % | 13 – 18 % | 10 – 14 % |
| **Damage tolerance** | Poor (loss of a saddle tank capsizes stability) | Excellent (symmetric wing tanks preserve balance) | Excellent (redundancy across the vessel) | Moderate (symmetric layout but soft shell) |
| **Surfacing wave slam** | High (tank top impacts waves) | Low (tanks are shielded by outer hull) | Low | High |
| **Maintenance access** | Easy from inside pressure hull | Requires access via the free-flood space | Mixed | Hard (external) |
| **KTU Board Likelihood** | High (classic question) | Very High | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: MBT Flooding and Blowing Sequence

This is the **most often asked 7-mark sub-question** in KTU 2024 ESE.

**Flooding (Dive) Sequence:**

1. Submarine is at **periscope depth** or at the surface, MBTs **full of low-pressure air** at near-atmospheric pressure.
2. **Vent valve** at the top of each MBT is **opened** to the open atmosphere / safety-vent tank.
3. **Flooding valve** at the bottom of each MBT is **opened** to the sea.
4. Seawater enters from the bottom under hydrostatic head and pushes the air out from the top. Tank fills from bottom upward.
5. As MBTs fill, the vessel becomes **negatively buoyant** → it sinks.
6. When neutral buoyancy is reached at the desired depth, the **flooding valves are closed**. Trim is adjusted by the **trim tanks** and **fine ballast tanks**.

**Blowing (Surface) Sequence:**

1. Submarine is submerged at depth $H$, MBTs **full of seawater**.
2. **Vent valves are closed** (to prevent water from being blown into the casing).
3. **HP air is admitted** to the top of each MBT through the **blow valve**.
4. HP air pressure exceeds the hydrostatic pressure at the flooding opening, so water is **pushed out** through the bottom flooding opening to the sea.
5. As water leaves, the MBT is refilled with air. Buoyancy becomes **positive**, and the submarine rises.
6. When the submarine breaks the surface, **blow valves are closed** and the MBTs are vented to the atmosphere to bring them to the surfaced (air-filled) state.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams in this section are **functional block diagrams** that map the engineering relationships within the MBT system. They are KTU 2024 exam-hall-ready line-drawing substitutes for the physical cross-sectional sketches of a submarine.

---

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of the MBT Arrangement (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTER["OUTER HULL FREE-FLOODING ENVELOPE"]
        A1["SADDLE TANK L"]:::tank
        A2["SADDLE TANK R"]:::tank
        B1["WING TANK L UPPER"]:::tank
        B2["WING TANK L LOWER"]:::tank
        C1["WING TANK R UPPER"]:::tank
        C2["WING TANK R LOWER"]:::tank
        D["FAIRING TANK"]:::tank
    end

    subgraph INNER["PRESSURE HULL"]
        E1["CONTROL COMPARTMENT"]:::core
        E2["HP AIR FLASK BANK"]:::core
        E3["TRIM TANK"]:::core
        E4["MAIN LINE VALVE MANIFOLD"]:::core
    end

    V1["VENT VALVE TOP"]:::valve
    V2["FLOOD VALVE BOTTOM"]:::valve
    V3["HP BLOW VALVE"]:::valve
    V4["SAFETY RELIEF"]:::valve

    S["SEA"]:::sea
    ATM["ATMOSPHERE"]:::atm

    S -- bottom opening --> V2
    V2 -- to all MBT tanks --> A1
    V2 --> A2
    V2 --> B1
    V2 --> B2
    V2 --> C1
    V2 --> C2
    V2 --> D

    A1 -- top connection --> V1
    A2 --> V1
    B1 --> V1
    B2 --> V1
    C1 --> V1
    C2 --> V1
    D --> V1
    V1 --> ATM

    E2 -- high pressure air --> V3
    V3 -- to MBT top --> A1
    V3 --> A2
    V3 --> B1
    V3 --> B2
    V3 --> C1
    V3 --> C2
    V3 --> D

    V4 -- overpressure relief --> ATM

    classDef tank fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#0a2553
    classDef valve fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#58151c
    classDef core fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#0d3320
    classDef sea fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160,color:#032830
    classDef atm fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#332701
```

---

## 4.2 Operational State Machine of the MBT System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Surfaced
    Surfaced --> Venting: Open vent valves
    Venting --> Flooding: Open flood valves
    Flooding --> Submerging: Tanks fill with seawater
    Submerging --> Submerged: Neutral depth reached, close flood
    Submerged --> Blowing: Open HP blow valves
    Blowing --> VentingAgain: Air refills tanks, water expelled
    VentingAgain --> Surfaced: Break surface, close blow
    Surfaced --> [*]
    Submerged --> EmergencyBlow: HP air full surge
    EmergencyBlow --> Surfaced: Rapid ascent
```

---

## 4.3 Valve Hierarchy Block Diagram (Control Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD["DIVE ORDER FROM CONTROL ROOM"]:::core --> LOGIC["INTERLOCK LOGIC UNIT"]:::core
    LOGIC --> VENTCHK{"VENT OPEN QUESTION"}:::valve
    VENTCHK -- YES --> FLOODOPN["OPEN FLOOD VALVE"]:::valve
    VENTCHK -- NO --> VENTOPN["OPEN VENT VALVE FIRST"]:::valve
    VENTOPN --> FLOODOPN
    FLOODOPN --> FILLED{"TANK FULL"}:::valve
    FILLED -- YES --> CLOSE["CLOSE FLOOD AND VENT"]:::valve
    FILLED -- NO --> WAIT["WAIT 2 SECONDS"]:::core
    WAIT --> FILLED
    CLOSE --> TRIM["ACTIVATE TRIM TANKS"]:::core
    TRIM --> DONE["SUBMERGED READY"]:::core

    classDef core fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43
    classDef valve fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **interlock logic** is a KTU favourite: students who can describe why the vent must open *before* the flood valve (or simultaneously) score full marks. The reasoning is that without the vent, the trapped air would be **compressed** by the incoming water, eventually creating a back-pressure that prevents the tank from filling — the submarine would then fail to dive.

---

## 4.4 Schematic Comparison — Single Hull vs Double Hull MBT Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SH["SINGLE HULL SUBMARINE MBT LAYOUT"]
        SH1["PRESSURE HULL CENTER"]:::core
        SH2["SADDLE TANK OVER HULL"]:::tank
        SH3["DECK CASING TOP"]:::tank
    end

    subgraph DH["DOUBLE HULL SUBMARINE MBT LAYOUT"]
        DH1["PRESSURE HULL CENTER"]:::core
        DH2["INNER LIGHT HULL"]:::tank
        DH3["WING TANK PORT"]:::tank
        DH4["WING TANK STARBOARD"]:::tank
        DH5["OUTER LIGHT HULL"]:::tank
        DH6["SADDLE TANK TOP"]:::tank
    end

    classDef core fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43
    classDef tank fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
>
> **Q1. Define the term "Main Ballast Tank" (MBT) as used in submersible design. Mention its primary function and the typical location of MBTs in a double-hull submarine.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **Main Ballast Tank (MBT)** is a large free-flooding tank provided in a submarine for the purpose of controlling its buoyancy during dive and surfacing operations. (1 mark)
> Its primary function is to provide the **variable buoyancy** needed to submerge the submarine by allowing seawater to enter and to surface it by expelling the water with compressed air. (1 mark)
> In a **double-hull submarine**, the MBTs are located in the space between the **inner pressure hull** and the **outer light hull** — typically as **wing tanks** on the port and starboard sides and as a **saddle tank** on top of the pressure hull. (1 mark)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
>
> **Q2. State and explain the difference between "Reserve Buoyancy" and "Submerged Displacement" in the context of MBT design.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Reserve Buoyancy (RB)** is the *additional* weight a vessel can carry at the surface while remaining afloat. Numerically, it equals the **weight of seawater that fills all the MBTs** at full submergence. (1.5 marks)
> **Submerged Displacement ($\Delta_{sub}$)** is the total weight of the submarine when it is fully submerged, i.e. the surfaced displacement plus the weight of the water in the MBTs:
> $$\Delta_{sub} = \Delta_s + W_{MBT}$$ (1.5 marks)
> The two are linked by the relation that **$RB = \Delta_{sub} - \Delta_s$**, so the RB is the *extra* mass that the MBTs must accommodate for the vessel to dive.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "reserve buoyancy" with "submerged displacement".** RB is a *difference*, $\Delta_{sub}$ is a *total*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this slip.
> 2. **Always state the assumption of $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$** at the top of any numerical; otherwise the unit consistency will be challenged.
> 3. **In valve-sequence questions, always explain the *interlock* (vent must be open before flood)**. A bare list of steps scores only half marks.
> 4. **Mention both single-hull and double-hull arrangements** in any descriptive answer. The KTU board loves comparative questions.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — MBT Volume and Buoyancy Design**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q A.** (a) With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **arrangement of the main ballast tanks** in a typical double-hull submarine. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A double-hull submarine has a surfaced displacement of $\Delta_s = 3200$ tonnes and a reserve buoyancy of **14 %**. The seawater density is $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Determine:
> (i) the mass of water required to fully flood the MBTs,
> (ii) the total geometric volume of the MBTs, and
> (iii) the submerged displacement of the vessel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks):**

1. **Definition and role** of MBT (1 mark):
   In a double-hull submarine, the MBT system is the primary buoyancy-control volume and is housed in the space between the inner pressure hull and the outer light hull.

2. **Components of the arrangement** (2 marks):
   - **Saddle tanks** (top, straddling the casing).
   - **Wing tanks** (port and starboard, between pressure hull and outer hull).
   - **Group / auxiliary tanks** at fore and aft.
   - All connected to the sea at the bottom by **flooding valves** and to the atmosphere at the top by **vent valves**, with **HP blow valves** admitting compressed air for surfacing.

3. **Operational sequence** (2 marks):
   - **Flooding:** Vent opens → flood valve opens → seawater enters from bottom → air vents from top → submarine becomes negatively buoyant and submerges.
   - **Blowing:** Vent closes → HP air admitted at top → water expelled from bottom → submarine becomes positively buoyant and rises.

4. **Design features** (2 marks):
   - Symmetric port-starboard layout to maintain transverse stability.
   - Free-flooding on damage (external tanks are *designed* to take water).
   - HP air system sized for emergency blow at the maximum operational depth.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Mass of water in MBTs (1 mark):**

$$W_{MBT} = 0.14 \times 3200\ \text{t} = 448\ \text{t} = 448\,000\ \text{kg}$$

(ii) **Total MBT volume (3 marks):**

$$V_{MBT} = \dfrac{W_{MBT}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{448\,000}{1025} \approx 437.07\ \text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{MBT} \approx 437.1\ \text{m}^3}$$

(iii) **Submerged displacement (3 marks):**

$$\Delta_{sub} = \Delta_s + W_{MBT} = 3200 + 448 = 3648\ \text{t}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta_{sub} = 3648\ \text{tonnes}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — HP Air System and Valve Interlocks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q B.** (a) Explain, with a labelled block diagram, the **sequence of valve operations** during a normal dive and a normal surface (blow) of a submarine. Clearly state the **interlock logic** that prevents valve mis-operation. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A submarine operates at a depth of $H = 150$ m. The seawater density is $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, and atmospheric pressure is $P_{atm} = 101.3\ \text{kPa}$. The MBT flooding opening is at the bottom of the tank. Calculate:
> (i) the hydrostatic pressure at the flooding opening, and
> (ii) the minimum high-pressure air pressure required at the MBT top to start blowing, assuming a margin of $\Delta P = 250\ \text{kPa}$ for flow losses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks):**

1. **Dive sequence** (2 marks):
   - Step 1: Open **vent valves** at MBT top → connect to atmosphere.
   - Step 2: Open **flooding valves** at MBT bottom → admit seawater.
   - Step 3: Water enters from bottom; air is displaced upward and vented.
   - Step 4: At desired depth, close **flooding valves**, then close **vent valves** once tank is full of water.

2. **Surface (blow) sequence** (2 marks):
   - Step 1: Verify **flooding valves are closed**.
   - Step 2: **Close vent valves** (prevent water from being blown into casing).
   - Step 3: Open **HP blow valves** → admit high-pressure air at MBT top.
   - Step 4: Air pressure exceeds hydrostatic head → seawater is forced out through the bottom flooding opening.

3. **Interlock logic** (3 marks):
   - **Interlock 1:** Vent valve must be **open (or have been opened)** *before* the flooding valve is opened. If flood is opened with vent closed, the trapped air compresses and the tank does not fill.
   - **Interlock 2:** HP blow valve cannot be opened **unless** all flooding valves are confirmed **closed** (prevents blowing water into the sea chamber or external flooding path).
   - **Interlock 3:** Emergency blow overrides interlocks 1 and 2 — it forces simultaneous opening of all vents and blows using main air banks for rapid surfacing.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Hydrostatic pressure at the flooding opening (3 marks):**

$$P_{depth} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\ g\ H$$

$$P_{depth} = 101.3 \times 10^3 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 150$$

$$P_{depth} = 101\,300 + 1\,508\,287.5\ \text{Pa}$$

$$P_{depth} \approx 1\,609.6\ \text{kPa} \;\approx\; 1.61\ \text{MPa}$$

(ii) **Minimum HP air pressure (4 marks):**

$$P_{HP,\ min} = P_{depth} + \Delta P_{margin}$$

$$P_{HP,\ min} = 1609.6 + 250 = 1859.6\ \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{HP,\ min} \approx 1.86\ \text{MPa} \;\approx\; 18.6\ \text{bar}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design insight:** Real submarines store HP air at 250–300 bar, so the manifold pressure of 18–30 bar used for normal blow easily clears this minimum, with a large safety margin for the worst-case depth and flow losses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — MBT Arrangement (Module 2)**

- ☐ **MBT** = Main Ballast Tank. Its sole routine job is to convert the submarine from **positive** to **near-neutral/negative** buoyancy.
- ☐ MBT volume is typically **10 – 15 %** of hull volume.
- ☐ **Reserve Buoyancy (RB)** = $\Delta_{sub} - \Delta_s$ = weight of seawater the MBT must hold.
- ☐ **Submerged displacement** $\Delta_{sub} = \Delta_s + W_{MBT}$.
- ☐ In a **single-hull** submarine, MBTs are typically **saddle tanks** mounted over the pressure hull.
- ☐ In a **double-hull** submarine, MBTs are **wing tanks** (sides) and a **saddle tank** (top), located between pressure hull and outer hull.
- ☐ **Flooding valves** are at the **bottom** of the tank; **vent valves** are at the **top**; **HP blow valves** feed compressed air at the **top**.
- ☐ **Vent must open before flood** — this is the golden interlock rule.
- ☐ **HP blow pressure** must exceed the hydrostatic head at the flooding opening: $P_{HP} \ge P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\ g\ H + \Delta P$.
- ☐ During the **transition** (partially flooded), the free-water surface in the MBT **reduces transverse GM** (Boussinesq effect). Symmetric flooding sequence minimises this.
- ☐ **Soft free-flooding tanks** (no top vent — open to sea at bottom only) are not used on deep-diving submarines because they cannot be blown at depth.
- ☐ **Reserve Buoyancy fraction** of 12 % means $V_{MBT}/V_{hull} = 0.12$.
- ☐ KTU exam pattern: Part (a) descriptive + sketch (7 marks), Part (b) numerical with three sub-steps (7 marks).
- ☐ Always begin numericals with: **Given**, **To find**, **Assumption ($\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$)**, **Formula**, **Substitution**, **Result**.

---

### End of Module 2 Topic — Arrangement of the Main Ballast Tanks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Submarine Submerged
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Submarine Submerged

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Submerged Condition** of a submarine refers to the operational state in which the vessel is entirely below the free water surface (mean waterline), with all external hull openings sealed and the main ballast tanks (MBTs) completely flooded. In this state, the submarine behaves as a fully submerged body of revolution governed by Archimedes' principle, where the **buoyancy force equals the submerged weight** of the vessel for equilibrium at any operating depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Definition**
> A submarine is said to be in the "submerged condition" when the **waterplane area is zero**, i.e., the hull is fully immersed below the design waterline. Unlike surface ships, the **reserve buoyancy becomes zero** and depth control is achieved by manipulating small quantities of water in the **trim tanks** and **ballast tanks** against the variable pressure of the surrounding sea.

## 1.2 Key Parameters of the Submerged Condition

| Parameter | Symbol | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Submerged Displacement | $\Delta_S$ | tonnes (t) | Total mass of the submarine when fully submerged |
| Submerged Volume | $\nabla_S$ | m³ | Total external volume of the pressure hull + free-flooding spaces |
| Sea Water Density | $\rho_{sw}$ | t/m³ | Typically **1.025 t/m³** (standard seawater) |
| Submerged Depth | $h$ | m | Vertical distance from keel to free surface |
| Operating Pressure | $p$ | kPa | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ |
| Reserve Buoyancy | $RB$ | m³ | Becomes **0** when fully submerged |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Underwater Balloon

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students:**
> Imagine holding a sealed balloon underwater in a swimming pool. The deeper you push it, the harder the water pushes back (more pressure). The balloon neither sinks nor rises by itself — it "hovers." A submarine in the submerged condition behaves exactly like this balloon. By sucking in a tiny bit of water (or blowing it out) using small **trim tanks**, the submariner makes the submarine **slightly heavier or lighter** than the surrounding water, allowing precise depth control. The main ballast tanks (MBTs) handle the big change between surface and submerged; the trim tanks handle the fine tuning.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are mandatory for solving submerged condition problems in KTU examinations:

- **Density of Sea Water:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$
- **Density of Fresh Water:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.000 \text{ t/m}^3$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Hydrostatic Pressure Gradient:** $p(h) = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ (in Pa)

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why Reserve Buoyancy Vanishes:**
> On the surface, a ship has a reserve buoyancy equal to the volume of the hull above the waterline. Once the submarine submerges completely, this volume of air above water is gone — so $RB = 0$. From this point onward, the submarine must rely on **depth control systems** (trim pumps, main ballast blow) rather than buoyancy reserves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principle of Submerged Equilibrium

For a submarine hovering at a constant depth in still water, the fundamental equation of static equilibrium is:

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

This gives us the **buoyancy–weight balance**:

$$B = W$$

Where:
- $B$ = Buoyancy force acting upward = $\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_S$
- $W$ = Total submerged weight of the submarine (including flooded water in MBTs)

## 2.2 Logic Breakdown — The Submerged State

The transition from surface to submerged condition follows these sequential steps:

1. **Surface State (Initial):** MBTs are filled with air → overall density < seawater → submarine floats.
2. **Flooding Phase:** Main ballast tank vents are opened; seawater rushes in due to lower internal pressure → weight increases progressively.
3. **Periscope Depth:** Free surface still partially above water; the submarine is "awash."
4. **Fully Submerged:** Waterplane area = 0; reserve buoyancy = 0; only **trim and depth control** remain.
5. **Depth Stabilisation:** Hydroplanes (sail planes / stern planes) provide dynamic depth control while moving; trim tanks provide static control while hovering.

## 2.3 The Three Sub-Submerged States

| State | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Lightly Submerged** | Sail/conning tower still above water | Periscope depth, communications |
| **Fully Submerged (Hovering)** | All hull below water; no forward motion | Surveillance, mine-laying |
| **Running Submerged** | Forward motion engaged; hydroplanes active | Transit at operational depth |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 SCHEME — SUBMERGED CONDITION**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_S$ | Buoyancy in submerged condition | N |
| 2 | $\Delta_S = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_S$ | Submerged displacement (Archimedes) | tonnes |
| 3 | $W_{sub} = \Delta_{surface} + W_{flooded \text{ } MBT}$ | Total submerged weight | tonnes |
| 4 | $\Delta W = W_{sub} - B$ | Weight excess (negative → buoyancy excess) | tonnes |
| 5 | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{h}}$ | Equilibrium depth change for floodable tank | m |
| 6 | $p(h) = p_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Absolute pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{hull} = \dfrac{p(h) \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical pressure hull (thin-wall) | Pa |
| 8 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{p(h) \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum thickness of pressure hull plating | m |
| 9 | $\overline{GM}_T = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM}_T - \overline{KG}$ | Transverse metacentric height (submerged) | m |
| 10 | $\overline{BM}_T^{sub} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla_S}$ | Submerged transverse BM | m |
| 11 | $\overline{BM}_L^{sub} = \dfrac{I_L}{\nabla_S}$ | Submerged longitudinal BM | m |
| 12 | $\overline{KB}^{sub} = \dfrac{h_{pressure \text{ } hull}}{2}$ | Centre of buoyancy for cylindrical pressure hull (approx.) | m |
| 13 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Critical buckling pressure (stiffened cylinder) | Pa |
| 14 | $V_{trim} = \dfrac{\Delta W \cdot \overline{GM}_L}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}$ | Required trim tank volume (longitudinal) | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examiner's Note on Table:**
> In the submerged condition, the **centre of buoyancy (B)** shifts DOWN compared to the surface condition because the buoyancy is now uniformly distributed throughout the pressure hull volume (no air-filled freeboard above water). This causes **$\overline{KB}$ to increase**, which generally **improves transverse stability** — a key concept tested in KTU.

## 2.5 The Reserve Buoyancy Concept in Submerged Condition

$$\boxed{RB_{submerged} = 0}$$

This is a defining characteristic. Unlike surface ships (where $RB \approx 30\text{–}50\%$ of displacement), a fully submerged submarine has **zero reserve buoyancy**. To prevent sinking uncontrollably, submarines rely on:

- **Main Ballast Blow System:** High-pressure air (HPA) at typically **2.45 MPa (≈ 25 bar)** forces water out of MBTs to surface.
- **Emergency Ballast Blow:** Faster blow using stored compressed air.
- **Negative Tank Concept:** A small dedicated tank that can be flooded to add weight deliberately.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern naval architecture, the submerged condition analysis drives:

- **Pressure Hull Design:** Dictates plate thickness, ring stiffener spacing, and hull material grade (often **HY-80** or **HY-100** steel for naval subs).
- **Depth Rating Classification:** Shallow (~200 m), Deep (~600 m), or Abyssal (~1000+ m for research subs like *Alvin* or *Limiting Factor*).
- **Power Plant Selection:** Nuclear (large submerged endurance) vs Diesel-Electric (snort/air-dependent).
- **Signature Reduction:** Hull form optimised for submerged acoustics (anechoic tiles).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Submerged Displacement from Archimedes' Principle

**Starting Principle:**
By Archimedes' principle, a body fully immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.

**Step 1 — Express Buoyancy Force:**
The buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced seawater:

$$B = m_{displaced} \cdot g = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_S \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Express Submerged Weight:**
The submerged weight includes the lightweight of the submarine plus all flooded water inside the MBTs:

$$W_{sub} = W_{lightweight} + W_{flooded \text{ } MBT} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores} + W_{water \text{ } in \text{ } trim \text{ } tanks}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Static Equilibrium:**
For hovering at constant depth:

$$B = W_{sub}$$

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_S \cdot g = W_{sub} \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Submerged Displacement:**

$$\boxed{\Delta_S = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_S \quad \text{[tonnes]}}$$

This is the **KTU standard form** used in all submerged-condition numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoop Stress in Cylindrical Pressure Hull

A cylindrical pressure hull section is essentially a thin-walled pressure vessel. KTU frequently tests this derivation.

**Step 1 — Pressure at Operating Depth:**
At a depth $h$ below the sea surface:

$$p_{ext}(h) = p_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Since $p_{atm}$ acts on both inside and outside of the hull (internal air at ~1 atm), the **net collapsing pressure** is:

$$\Delta p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram of Half-Cylinder:**
Consider a longitudinal half-section of the cylindrical hull of radius $r$, length $L$, and wall thickness $t$. Forces acting on the cut section:

- **Outward radial pressure** acting on the projected area $2r \cdot L$
- **Inward hoop tension** acting on two wall sections, each of area $t \cdot L$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of Forces:**
Equating outward pressure force to inward tension force:

$$\Delta p \cdot (2r \cdot L) = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot (t \cdot L)$$

**Step 4 — Cancel Common Terms:**
Dividing both sides by $2L$:

$$\Delta p \cdot r = \sigma_h \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Hoop Stress:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot r}{t} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h \cdot r}{t}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Minimum Plate Thickness:**
Given allowable stress $\sigma_{allow}$ for the hull material:

$$\boxed{t_{min} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Submerged Transverse Stability ($\overline{GM}_T$)

**Step 1 — Recall General Metacentric Height Formula:**

$$\overline{GM}_T = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM}_T - \overline{KG}$$

**Step 2 — Submerged $\overline{KB}$:**
For a fully submerged pressure hull approximated as a uniform cylinder of depth $d$:

$$\overline{KB} = \dfrac{d}{2}$$

(measured from keel K)

**Step 3 — Submerged $\overline{BM}_T$:**

$$\overline{BM}_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla_S}$$

where $I_T$ is the second moment of waterplane area of the **horizontal cross-section** at the longitudinal centre. For a circular section of radius $r$:

$$I_T = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^4}{4}$$

So:

$$\overline{BM}_T^{sub} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^4}{4 \cdot \nabla_S}$$

**Step 4 — Final Submerged Stability Formula:**

$$\boxed{\overline{GM}_T^{sub} = \dfrac{d}{2} + \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^4}{4 \cdot \nabla_S} - \overline{KG}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Comparison Insight:** In the submerged state, $\overline{BM}_T$ is typically **larger** than in the surface state because $\nabla_S$ (submerged volume) is much greater than the surface displacement volume per unit beam consideration. This is why submarines are **more stable when submerged** — a frequently asked KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Trim Tank Volume for Static Depth Adjustment

**Step 1 — Define Trim Moment:**
A flooded forward trim tank creates a trimming moment about the longitudinal Centre of Gravity (LCG).

**Step 2 — Moment Equation:**
If the forward trim tank is at a longitudinal distance $\ell_{trim}$ from LCG, then the moment produced by flooding weight $w$:

$$M_{trim} = w \cdot \ell_{trim}$$

**Step 3 — Required Volume:**
Converting weight to volume using seawater density:

$$\boxed{V_{trim} = \dfrac{M_{trim}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \ell_{trim}}}$$

This is the volume of seawater that must be moved into (or out of) the trim tank to achieve a desired longitudinal trim.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Submerged vs Surface Condition

| Property | Surface Condition | Submerged Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Waterplane Area ($A_W$) | Non-zero | **Zero** |
| Reserve Buoyancy ($RB$) | ~30–50% of $\Delta$ | **Zero** |
| Centre of Buoyancy ($KB$) | Near waterline | **Mid-hull** (lower) |
| Transverse Stability ($\overline{GM}_T$) | Moderate | **Higher** |
| Longitudinal Stability | High | **Lower** (prone to bow-up/bow-down) |
| Depth Control | Passive (floats) | **Active (trim tanks + planes)** |
| Free Surface Effect | Important | **Minimal** (all flooded or pressed) |
| Compressibility Effect | Negligible | **Significant** at >300 m |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A submarine has a lightweight of **2400 tonnes**. When on the surface, 80 tonnes of fuel and 20 tonnes of stores are carried. To submerge, the main ballast tanks are flooded, adding **120 tonnes** of seawater. The pressure hull is cylindrical with radius $r = 5$ m, length $L = 70$ m. Sea water density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³.

**Find:** (a) Submerged displacement, (b) Submerged buoyancy volume required, (c) Whether the submarine will hover, rise, or sink.

### Solution:

**(a) Submerged Displacement:**

$$\Delta_S = W_{lightweight} + W_{fuel} + W_{stores} + W_{flooded \text{ } MBT}$$

$$\Delta_S = 2400 + 80 + 20 + 120 = 2620 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(b) Required Buoyancy Volume:**

$$\nabla_S = \dfrac{\Delta_S}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{2620}{1.025} = 2556.10 \text{ m}^3$$

**(c) Compare Actual Hull Volume to Required:**

The actual submerged volume equals the external hull volume:

$$V_{hull} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot L = \pi \cdot (5)^2 \cdot 70 = 5497.79 \text{ m}^3$$

Since $V_{hull} \gg \nabla_S$ required, the submarine has excess buoyancy and would **rise**. To hover, the **trim tanks must be flooded** with $(5497.79 - 2556.10) \cdot 1.025 \approx 3010$ tonnes of additional water.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Submerged Condition Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submarine Lightweight W_light] --> Z[Total Submerged Weight W_sub]
    B[Main Ballast Tank Flooded Water] --> Z
    C[Fuel and Stores] --> Z
    D[Trim Tank Water FWD and AFT] --> Z
    Z --> E{Static Equilibrium Check}
    E -- Equal --> F[HOVERING at Constant Depth]
    E -- W greater than B --> G[SINKING Activate Emergency Blow]
    E -- W less than B --> H[RISING Blow MBT or Flood Negative]
    F --> I[Depth Control Subsystem]
    I --> J[Static Trim Tanks FWD and AFT]
    I --> K[Dynamic Hydroplanes Sail and Stern]
    I --> L[Variable Ballast Tank VBT]
    J --> M[Precise Hovering Depth Achieved]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pressure Hull Stress Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sea Water Hydrostatic Pressure] --> B[Pressure at Operating Depth p_h]
    B --> C[Net Collapsing Pressure Delta_p]
    C --> D[Hoop Stress in Cylindrical Hull]
    C --> E[Longitudinal Stress]
    C --> F[Buckling Check on Stiffened Panels]
    D --> G[Check against sigma_allow]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Safe --> H[Operational Depth Approved]
    G -- Fail --> I[Reduce Depth or Increase Plate Thickness t]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Transition from Surface to Submerged

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Surface State MBTs filled with Air] --> S2[Open MBT Vent Valves]
    S2 --> S3[Sea Water Floods MBTs by Gravity]
    S3 --> S4[Submarine Loses Reserve Buoyancy Progressively]
    S4 --> S5[Periscope Depth Sail Tower Above Water]
    S5 --> S6[Flood Remaining MBTs Fully Submerged]
    S6 --> S7[Engage Trim Pumps for Hovering]
    S7 --> S8[Steady Submerged State Equilibrium Achieved]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Architecture — Submarine Hull Form Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submarine Hull Form] --> B[Single Hull Type]
    A --> C[Double Hull Type]
    A --> D[Teardrop Hull Optimised for Submerged Speed]
    B --> B1[Outer Light Hull Sacrificial]
    B --> B2[Inner Pressure Hull Cylindrical]
    C --> C1[Outer Ballast Tanks MBTs]
    C --> C2[Inner Pressure Hull with Circular Cross Section]
    D --> D1[Modern Nuclear Submarine Design]
    D --> D2[Minimum Drag Profile for Submerged Operation]
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow — Depth Control Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Target Operating Depth h_target] --> Q[Compare with Current Depth h_current]
    Q --> R{Depth Error}
    R -- Too Deep --> S[Flood Trim Tank to Reduce Buoyancy]
    R -- Too Shallow --> T[Blow Trim Tank to Increase Buoyancy]
    S --> U[Hydroplanes Correct Residual Error]
    T --> U
    U --> V[New Equilibrium Depth h_new]
    V --> Q
```

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** Examiners often award 2–3 marks for **clearly labelled free-body diagrams** of the pressure hull. Practice drawing the half-cylinder FBD with three force vectors: outward hydrostatic pressure, two inward hoop tension vectors, and the longitudinal axis annotation.
