# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "submerged condition" of a submarine. Why does the reserve buoyancy become zero in this state?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A submarine is said to be in the **submerged condition** when its entire pressure hull and outer hull are below the free water surface, with no portion of the vessel exposed to air. The waterplane area is zero, and only the **main ballast tanks** remain in flooded state.

**Reserve buoyancy becomes zero** because reserve buoyancy is defined as the volume of the hull above the waterline that can be flooded to submerge the vessel further without sinking. In the fully submerged state, this freeboard volume no longer exists — the hull is entirely underwater. Therefore:

$$RB_{submerged} = 0$$

Instead of reserve buoyancy, the depth of the submarine is controlled by the **trim tanks**, **variable ballast tanks**, and **hydroplanes**, which modulate buoyancy in small, precise increments. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**State Archimedes' principle as applicable to a fully submerged submarine and write the equilibrium equation for hovering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Archimedes' Principle (Submerged Form):**
> When a body is fully immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.

For a fully submerged submarine:

$$B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_S$$

**Hovering Equilibrium Equation:**

For the submarine to remain at a constant depth (hovering) without rising or sinking, the **buoyancy force must exactly balance the submerged weight**:

$$B = W_{sub}$$

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_S = W_{lightweight} + W_{fuel} + W_{stores} + W_{flooded \text{ } MBT} + W_{trim \text{ } tank \text{ } water}$$

If $B > W_{sub}$, the submarine rises; if $B < W_{sub}$, it sinks. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Submerged Condition & Pressure Hull Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** A submarine operates at a depth of **250 m** below the sea surface. The pressure hull is a cylinder of **inner radius** $r_i = 4.5$ m and wall thickness $t = 35$ mm. Calculate:
   1. The hydrostatic pressure at this depth.
   2. The hoop stress in the cylindrical hull.
   3. The minimum required thickness if the allowable stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 550$ MPa.

Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

**(b)** Discuss **three key differences** between the surface condition and the submerged condition of a submarine, with particular reference to **stability** and **depth control mechanisms**.

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Pressure Hull Analysis (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Operating depth: $h = 250$ m
- Inner radius: $r_i = 4.5$ m
- Wall thickness: $t = 35$ mm $= 0.035$ m
- Allowable stress: $\sigma_{allow} = 550$ MPa $= 550 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(1) Hydrostatic Pressure at 250 m Depth:**

Using the absolute pressure formula:

$$p_{abs}(h) = p_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$p_{abs}(250) = 101325 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 250$$

Computing stepwise:

$$1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25$$

$$10055.25 \times 250 = 2513812.5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$p_{abs} = 101325 + 2513812.5 = 2615137.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.615 \text{ MPa}$$

**Net collapsing pressure** (gauge) acting on the hull:

$$\Delta p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 2513812.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.514 \text{ MPa}$$

**(2) Hoop Stress in the Cylindrical Hull:**

Using the thin-walled pressure vessel formula:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot r}{t}$$

The mean radius is:

$$r = r_i + \dfrac{t}{2} = 4.5 + \dfrac{0.035}{2} = 4.5175 \text{ m}$$

So:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{2.514 \times 10^6 \cdot 4.5175}{0.035}$$

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{11.357 \times 10^6}{0.035} = 324.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 324.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(3) Minimum Required Thickness:**

Rearranging the hoop stress formula:

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{2.514 \times 10^6 \cdot 4.5175}{550 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{11.357 \times 10^6}{550 \times 10^6} = 0.02065 \text{ m} = 20.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The provided thickness (35 mm) is **safe** (factor of safety ≈ 1.7) against membrane hoop stress failure. However, **buckling** must also be checked, which is more critical for thin cylindrical shells. *(7 marks — full breakdown: 2 + 2 + 3)*

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Surface vs Submerged Condition (7 marks)

| S.No. | Aspect | Surface Condition | Submerged Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reserve Buoyancy** | Positive (~30–40% of $\Delta$) — provides safety margin | **Zero** — no buoyancy reserve; depends on active systems |
| 2 | **Transverse Stability** | $\overline{GM}_T$ smaller; depends on waterplane area | $\overline{GM}_T$ is **higher** because $\overline{BM}_T^{sub}$ uses full submerged volume $\nabla_S$ in denominator |
| 3 | **Depth Control** | Passive — vessel floats at waterline | **Active** — trim tanks (static) + hydroplanes (dynamic) + VBT |
| 4 | **Free Surface Effect** | Significant in partially filled tanks | **Minimal** since tanks are either full or empty |
| 5 | **Compressibility** | Negligible (atmospheric pressure) | **Significant** at depth > 300 m due to sea water compressibility |
| 6 | **Pressure Hull Stress** | Near zero (internal = external = 1 atm) | **Maximum** — drives structural design |

**Stability Discussion:**
In the submerged state, the **centre of buoyancy (B) lies at the centroid of the fully submerged volume**, which for a cylindrical hull is at the mid-depth — i.e., significantly lower than in the surface condition. Since $\overline{KG}$ changes only marginally, the net effect is that $\overline{GM}_T^{sub} > \overline{GM}_T^{surface}$, making the submerged submarine inherently **more stable in roll**.

**Depth Control Discussion:**
Surface vessels control depth passively. Submerged submarines require **three coordinated systems**:
- **Variable Ballast Tank (VBT):** coarse buoyancy adjustment for major depth changes.
- **Trim Tanks (Forward/Aft):** fine static longitudinal and depth adjustments.
- **Hydroplanes (Sail planes and Stern planes):** dynamic depth control during forward motion; create lift/dive moments based on angle of attack.

*(7 marks — full breakdown: 3 for differences + 2 for stability discussion + 2 for depth control)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Comprehensive Question*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **transformation from surface to fully submerged condition** of a submarine. List the **states of flooding** of the main ballast tanks during this transition.

**(b)** A submarine has the following data:
   - Lightweight displacement: $W_L = 3200$ tonnes
   - Surface displacement (full load): $\Delta_{surf} = 3800$ tonnes
   - MBT total volume: $V_{MBT} = 600$ m³
   - Pressure hull external radius: $r = 5.5$ m
   - Length of pressure hull: $L = 80$ m

Calculate:
   1. The weight of seawater that must flood the MBTs to fully submerge the submarine.
   2. The submerged displacement.
   3. The submerged metacentric radius $\overline{BM}_T$ assuming circular cross-section.

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Transition from Surface to Submerged (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Surface State:**
The submarine floats at the surface with **reserve buoyancy** equal to the volume above the waterline. The MBTs are filled with low-pressure air at approximately atmospheric pressure.

**Step 2 — Vent Operation:**
To submerge, the **MBT vent valves** at the top of the tanks are opened. Simultaneously, the **flooding valves** at the bottom of the tanks are opened.

**Step 3 — Gravity Flooding:**
Because internal tank pressure is less than the external hydrostatic pressure at the flooding port depth, seawater rushes into the MBTs by gravity. This increases the weight of the submarine progressively.

**Step 4 — Three Flooding States:**
   1. **Emerged (Surface):** MBTs dry → submarine floats high.
   2. **Partially Flooded (Awash/Periscope Depth):** MBTs ~80% full → free surface near waterline.
   3. **Fully Flooded (Submerged):** MBTs 100% full → waterplane area = 0 → fully submerged.

**Step 5 — Fine Depth Control:**
Once submerged, **trim pumps** transfer small amounts of water between forward and aft trim tanks to maintain the desired depth. **Hydroplanes** are used when moving forward.

**Sketch Outline (to be drawn by student):**
- Show hull with MBTs (port and starboard) coloured blue (flooded).
- Mark waterline moving from above the hull (surface) to below the hull (submerged).
- Annotate: vent valves, flooding valves, trim tanks, hydroplanes.

*(7 marks — 2 for sketch + 3 for sequential explanation + 2 for flooding states)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Submerged Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $W_L = 3200$ t
- $\Delta_{surf} = 3800$ t
- $V_{MBT} = 600$ m³
- $r = 5.5$ m, $L = 80$ m
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³

**(1) Weight of Seawater Flooding MBTs:**

The difference between surface and submerged displacement equals the seawater that floods the MBTs plus any other adjustments. Assuming the flooded MBT water equals the weight increase from surface to submerged:

$$W_{MBT \text{ } water} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$$

$$W_{MBT \text{ } water} = 1.025 \cdot 600 = 615 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Verification:** This means $\Delta_{sub} = W_L + W_{MBT \text{ } water} + \text{fuel/stores} = 3200 + 615 + \text{variable}$. The surface condition includes fuel/stores such that the net MBT flooding contributes to a part of the displacement shift. The **effective MBT flooding weight** required to take the vessel from the surface condition to fully submerged is **615 tonnes**.

**(2) Submerged Displacement:**

$$\Delta_{sub} = W_L + W_{fuel \text{ } and \text{ } stores} + W_{MBT \text{ } water}$$

Given $\Delta_{surf} = W_L + W_{fuel \text{ } and \text{ } stores} = 3800$ t:

$$\Delta_{sub} = 3800 + 615 = 4415 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(3) Submerged Metacentric Radius $\overline{BM}_T$:**

For a circular cross-section:

$$I_T = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (5.5)^4}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 915.0625}{4} = 718.69 \text{ m}^4$$

The submerged volume equals the external hull volume (cylindrical approximation):

$$\nabla_S = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot L = \pi \cdot (5.5)^2 \cdot 80 = \pi \cdot 30.25 \cdot 80 = 7602.65 \text{ m}^3$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{BM}_T^{sub} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla_S} = \dfrac{718.69}{7602.65} = 0.0945 \text{ m}$$

This very small $\overline{BM}_T^{sub}$ indicates that for a slender cylindrical hull, transverse stability in roll is dominated primarily by $\overline{KB}$ rather than $\overline{BM}_T$.

*(7 marks — full breakdown: 2 + 2 + 3)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $p_{atm}$:** When calculating hoop stress, many students use $p_{abs}$ instead of the **net gauge pressure** $\Delta p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$. The atmospheric pressure acts on both sides and cancels out. **Deduction: 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Using inner radius instead of mean radius:** For thick-walled cylinders, you must use $r = r_i + t/2$ in the thin-wall formula. Using $r_i$ gives a lower (incorrectly safe) stress. **Deduction: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 3. **Confusing submerged and surface displacement:** Students often use surface $\Delta$ in submerged condition formulae. Remember: **$\Delta_{sub}$ includes the flooded MBT water weight.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring unit conversions:** When thickness is given in mm, convert to m **before** applying the formula. A common error is computing $\sigma_h$ in Pa when stress should be in MPa.
>
> 5. **Writing "reserve buoyancy is zero" without explanation:** Examiners expect the *reason* — that reserve buoyancy is the volume above the waterline, which is zero when fully submerged.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the role of trim tanks:** Many students describe depth control only via hydroplanes. The **static trim tank system** is the primary depth-control mechanism when hovering.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Submarine Submerged Condition**

- ☐ Submerged condition = fully immersed hull with **waterplane area = 0**.
- ☐ **Reserve buoyancy $RB = 0$** in submerged state — this is the defining characteristic.
- ☐ Equilibrium for hovering: **$B = W_{sub}$**, where $B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_S$.
- ☐ Submerged displacement formula: **$\Delta_S = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_S$** (tonnes).
- ☐ Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$: **$p_{abs} = p_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$** — use **gauge** for hull stress.
- ☐ Hoop stress in thin cylindrical hull: **$\sigma_h = \Delta p \cdot r / t$**.
- ☐ Minimum plate thickness: **$t_{min} = \Delta p \cdot r / \sigma_{allow}$**.
- ☐ Submerged transverse stability: **$\overline{GM}_T^{sub} > \overline{GM}_T^{surf}$** (more stable submerged).
- ☐ **$\overline{BM}_T^{sub} = I_T / \nabla_S$** — uses *full submerged volume* in denominator.
- ☐ Depth control: **Trim tanks (static)** + **Hydroplanes (dynamic)** + **VBT (coarse)**.
- ☐ Sea water density: **$\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³** = 1025 kg/m³ — always remember.
- ☐ Submarine compressibility effects become **significant beyond ~300 m depth**.
- ☐ Pressure hull buckling is **more critical than yielding** in deep-diving submarines.
- ☐ Main ballast blow uses **HPA at ~2.45 MPa** for emergency surfacing.
- ☐ Three submerged sub-states: **Lightly Submerged / Hovering / Running Submerged**.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Submarine Submerged Condition**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Buoyancy Elements # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Buoyancy Elements** are dedicated structural components or material systems integrated into a submersible's hull architecture whose **sole engineering purpose is to generate a controllable positive buoyant force (uplift)** that counteracts the gravitational weight of the vehicle, its payload, crew, and systems, thereby determining the submersible's static equilibrium, depth-keeping ability, and surfacing safety margins. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a buoyancy element is classified as a **passive (or active, in some configurations) hydrostatic system** that exploits **Archimedes' Principle** — that any submerged body experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces. The defining performance metrics in board examinations are: - **Net Buoyancy (N)** - **Reserve Buoyancy (R)** - **Compressibility-induced buoyancy loss at depth** ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Pool Noodle:** Imagine you tie a heavy rock to a pool noodle and throw it into a swimming pool. The rock sinks, but the noodle (mostly air, very low density) pushes the rock UP because the noodle displaces a lot of water that weighs more than the noodle itself. A **buoyancy element is simply a meticulously engineered "pool noodle"** — except instead of foam, it uses **syntactic foam, ballast tanks, or hard-shell buoys** rated for thousands of meters of ocean pressure. The engineer must calculate exactly how much "upward push" is required to keep the heavy sub hovering, ascending, or descending on command. ## 1.3 Why Buoyancy Elements are Critical in Submersible Design | Function | Engineering Reason | |---|---| | Provide **static lift** | Counteract dry weight of pressure hull, machinery, crew | | Ensure **controlled ascent** in emergencies | Guarantee surfacing even if all power is lost (Safety-of-Life-At-Sea) | | Compensate for **payload variation** | Modular mission packages (sonar, manipulators, samples) | | Tune **trim and stability** | Distributed placement corrects pitch/roll moments | | Maintain **reserve buoyancy** | Margin against accidental flooding or compression at depth | ## 1.4 Classification of Buoyancy Elements Submersible buoyancy elements fall into three primary categories used in KTU curriculum: 1. **Hard-Shell Syntactic Buoyancy Modules** — rigid polymer-glass microsphere composites. 2. **Buoyancy Blocks / Floats** — solid blocks of low-density polymer (e.g., HDPE, syntactic foam). 3. **Variable Ballast / Trim Tanks** — actively pumped air/water systems that *change* effective buoyancy in real time. > [!NOTE] > The **classification depth (h)** at which a submersible operates is the single most important design driver. For every 10 m of seawater, hydrostatic pressure increases by approximately **1 atm (≈ 101.325 kPa)**. This pressure squeezes buoyancy elements and reduces their volume — a phenomenon called **compressibility loss**, central to KTU numerical problems. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic The buoyant force acting on a fully submerged element is governed by **Archimedes' Principle**. The logical derivation chain used by KTU examiners is as follows: - **Step 1 — Pressure differential:** A submerged element experiences higher pressure on its bottom face (P_bottom) than on its top face (P_top). The difference arises because hydrostatic pressure grows linearly with depth. - **Step 2 — Net upward force:** This pressure differential, integrated over the element's surface area, produces a net upward force — the **buoyancy force (F_b)**. - **Step 3 — Volume dependence:** Since pressure grows linearly with depth, the resulting F_b is independent of depth (in an incompressible fluid) and depends **only on the displaced fluid volume V**. - **Step 4 — Density dependence:** F_b is directly proportional to the **density of the surrounding seawater (ρ_sw)** and the **gravitational acceleration (g)**. - **Step 5 — Equilibrium condition:** The submersible is in **neutral buoyancy** when F_b = W_total (total weight). If F_b > W, the sub rises; if F_b < W, the sub sinks. - **Step 6 — Reserve margin:** Designers do NOT operate exactly at neutral buoyancy. They add a **Reserve Buoyancy (R)** as a safety margin for emergencies. ## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following table — every KTU board problem in Module 2 will draw from these equations.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g$ | N (Newtons) | Upward force on submerged element | | 2 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | Pressure at depth h | | 3 | Net Buoyancy | $F_{net} = F_b - W_{sub}$ | N | Excess or deficit of upward force | | 4 | Reserve Buoyancy | $R = F_b - W_{sub}$ | N | Safety margin for surfacing | | 5 | Reserve Buoyancy (Fraction) | $R^* = \dfrac{R}{F_b} = 1 - \dfrac{W_{sub}}{F_b}$ | dimensionless | Ratio form, used for design checks | | 6 | Compressibility Loss | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = -\beta \cdot \Delta P$ | dimensionless | Volume shrinkage under pressure | | 7 | Compressed Buoyancy | $F_b^{comp} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V(1 - \beta \cdot \Delta P) \cdot g$ | N | Effective buoyancy at operating depth | | 8 | Syntactic Foam Density | $\rho_{foam} = \rho_{matrix}(1-\phi) + \rho_{ms}\phi$ | kg/m³ | Density of composite buoyancy material | | 9 | Weight of Submerged Element | $W_{sub,eff} = W_{air} - F_b$ | N | Apparent (in-situ) weight underwater | | 10 | Pressure per 10 m depth | $\Delta P \approx 1 \text{ atm} \approx 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Pa | Quick estimation tool | | 11 | Seawater Density (standard) | $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | kg/m³ | Standard ocean reference value | | 12 | Specific Gravity Check | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{foam}}{\rho_{sw}}$ | dimensionless | Must be < 1 to float | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAMINATION NOTE:** In all numerical answers, retain **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. Do NOT round intermediate values. Standard seawater density is **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**, NOT 1000 kg/m³ (which is freshwater). Marks are commonly deducted for using freshwater density in saltwater problems. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Buoyancy element design is not an academic exercise — it underwrites the safety of every human-occupied vehicle (HOV) and many unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs). Examples in current operational use: - **DSV Alvin (WHOI):** Uses syntactic foam blocks mounted on the superstructure, rated to 4,500 m. - **Shinkai 6500 (JAMSTEC):** Uses approximately **8.4 m³ of syntactic foam** to provide reserve buoyancy at 6,500 m depth. - **Deepsea Challenger (James Cameron, 2012):** Used specialized syntactic foam to reach the Mariana Trench (~10,900 m). - **Oil & Gas ROVs:** Foam buoyancy modules enable work-class ROVs to operate neutrally buoyant at 3,000–4,000 m. ## 2.4 Material Properties of Common Buoyancy Materials | Material | Density (kg/m³) | Service Depth | Application | |---|---|---|---| | HDPE (solid plastic) | 950 | < 500 m | Shallow ROVs, floats | | Polymer syntactic foam | 450–700 | 500–3,000 m | Work-class ROVs | | Glass microsphere syntactic foam | 350–550 | 3,000–6,500 m | Deep submersibles | | Ceramic syntactic foam | 280–450 | 6,500–11,000 m | Hadal vehicles | | Hollow glass spheres + epoxy | 400–600 | 1,000–4,500 m | Modular buoyancy | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buoyant Force from First Principles (Archimedes' Principle) We begin with a fully submerged rectangular buoyancy block of horizontal cross-sectional area A and vertical height H, with its top face at depth h_1 and bottom face at depth h_2 = h_1 + H. **Step 1:** Write the hydrostatic pressure at the top face: $$ P_{top} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h_1 $$ **Step 2:** Write the hydrostatic pressure at the bottom face. The bottom is at greater depth, so pressure is higher: $$ P_{bot} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot (h_1 + H) $$ **Step 3:** Compute the force pushing DOWN on the top face (atmospheric and water pressure both push down): $$ F_{down} = P_{top} \cdot A = \left(P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h_1\right) A $$ **Step 4:** Compute the force pushing UP on the bottom face: $$ F_{up} = P_{bot} \cdot A = \left(P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g (h_1 + H)\right) A $$ **Step 5:** Take the net force (UP minus DOWN). Atmospheric pressure cancels out — a key insight: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{net,up} &= F_{up} - F_{down} \\ &= \left[P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g (h_1 + H)\right]A - \left[P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h_1\right] A \\ &= P_{atm} A - P_{atm} A + \rho_{sw} g (h_1 + H)A - \rho_{sw} g h_1 A \\ &= \rho_{sw} g H A \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Recognize that the volume of the block is V = A × H. Substituting: $$ F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g $$ **Physical Insight:** Notice that h_1 (the depth of the top face) has disappeared from the equation. **The buoyant force is independent of depth** — it depends only on the volume of fluid displaced. This is the formal statement of Archimedes' Principle. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reserve Buoyancy Fraction Reserve buoyancy is the **fraction of total buoyant force** that exceeds the weight. It is the designer's safety margin. **Step 1:** Start with the definition of reserve buoyancy R: $$ R = F_b - W_{sub} $$ **Step 2:** Divide both sides by F_b to obtain a dimensionless fraction: $$ R^* = \frac{R}{F_b} = \frac{F_b - W_{sub}}{F_b} = 1 - \frac{W_{sub}}{F_b} $$ **Step 3:** Recognize that the ratio W_sub / F_b is the **submergence ratio (σ)**. Therefore: $$ R^* = 1 - \sigma $$ **Step 4:** Typical KTU design requirement: a manned submersible should have **R\* ≥ 0.05 to 0.10** (i.e., 5% to 10% reserve buoyancy). This guarantees the sub can return to the surface if some buoyancy is lost to flooding. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compressibility-Induced Buoyancy Loss at Depth At great depths, the hydrostatic pressure crushes the buoyancy material, reducing its volume. This is the **single most important KTU board problem variant**. **Step 1:** Define the bulk compressibility (β) of the buoyancy material. By definition: $$ \beta = -\frac{1}{V} \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P} $$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to find the fractional volume change under a pressure increase ΔP: $$ \frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\beta \cdot \Delta P $$ **Step 3:** The new volume at depth is therefore: $$ V_{depth} = V_0 \left(1 - \beta \cdot \Delta P\right) $$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the buoyancy equation to find compressed buoyancy at depth: $$ \begin{aligned} F_b^{comp} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{depth} \cdot g \\ &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V_0 \left(1 - \beta \cdot \Delta P\right) \cdot g \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** The buoyancy loss is: $$ \Delta F_b = F_b^0 - F_b^{comp} = \rho_{sw} V_0 g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta P $$ **Numerical Reference (KTU standard):** For polymer syntactic foam, β ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ Pa⁻¹. At 6,500 m depth, ΔP ≈ 6,500 × 1025 × 9.81 ≈ 65.4 MPa. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Syntactic Foam Density (Rule of Mixtures) Syntactic foam is a composite of a polymer matrix filled with hollow glass microspheres. The KTU board often asks for the effective density of such a foam. **Step 1:** Let φ = volume fraction of microspheres (0 ≤ φ ≤ 1). The matrix occupies (1 − φ) of the volume. **Step 2:** Apply the **rule of mixtures** (mass-weighted average density): $$ \rho_{foam} = \rho_{matrix}(1 - \phi) + \rho_{ms} \phi $$ **Step 3:** For the foam to be buoyant in seawater, we require: $$ \rho_{foam} < \rho_{sw} $$ **Step 4:** Define the specific gravity (SG): $$ SG = \frac{\rho_{foam}}{\rho_{sw}} < 1 $$ **Design Example (KTU style):** If ρ_matrix = 1,200 kg/m³ (epoxy), ρ_ms = 250 kg/m³ (hollow glass), and φ = 0.65: $$ \rho_{foam} = 1200(0.35) + 250(0.65) = 420 + 162.5 = 582.5 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ This gives SG = 582.5 / 1025 ≈ **0.568**, so each cubic meter of foam lifts approximately 1025 − 582.5 = **442.5 kg** of seawater weight when submerged. This is an excellent buoyancy material. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Submersible Buoyancy System > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete buoyancy system in a manned submersible, showing how the buoyancy elements interact with the pressure hull, ballast system, and depth-control logic. This is the level of systems understanding expected at KTU Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[External Hydrostatic Pressure
P = Patm + rho_sw g h] A --> B[Buoyancy Element Array
Syntactic Foam Blocks] A --> C[Hard Pressure Hull
Titanium or HY Steel] B --> D[Effective Buoyancy Force
Fb comp = rho_sw V g 1 minus beta dP] C --> E[Hull Internal Volume
Crew and Systems] E --> F[Total Submerged Weight
Wsub = Wair minus Fb hull] D --> F F --> G{Buoyancy vs Weight Check} G -->|Fb greater than Wsub| H[Net Upward Force
Sub Ascends] G -->|Fb less than Wsub| I[Net Downward Force
Sub Descends] G -->|Fb equals Wsub| J[Neutral Buoyancy
Hover State] K[Variable Ballast Tank
Pumped Air or Water] --> F L[Emergency Drop Weight
Solid Mass Release] --> I H --> M[Surface Acquisition
Safety Critical Path] I --> N[Controlled Descent
Mission Operation] J --> O[Station Keeping
Hover for Task Work] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Subsystem Description > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram — what the examiner expects you to know:** - **Node A** represents the ocean environment. The hydrostatic pressure grows with depth h and acts on EVERY external surface of the submersible. - **Node B** is the syntactic foam array, which is deliberately placed on the **exterior of the pressure hull** (often in a "skirt" or "saddle bag" configuration) so it can be larger volume without taking up interior space. - **Node C** is the pressure hull — it is denser than water (sinks), so it must be paired with buoyancy elements to remain submerged in a controlled way. - **Node F** is the **weight integration point** — every force converges here to decide whether the vehicle rises, sinks, or hovers. - **Node G** is the **decision diamond** — this is the heart of static equilibrium analysis and the most-tested concept in KTU Module 2 problems. - **Node K** is the **variable ballast system** — actively controlled by the pilot to change effective weight. - **Node L** is the **emergency jettisonable weight** — a fail-safe system mandated by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's) for all human-occupied vehicles. It guarantees surfacing if all else fails. ## 4.3 Typical Buoyancy Element Placement Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Upper Bow Module] P2[Port Side Module] P3[Starboard Side Module] P4[Stern Module] P5[Lower Keel Module] P6[Top Hat Module] C[Central Pressure Hull
Crew Sphere] --> P1 C --> P2 C --> P3 C --> P4 C --> P5 C --> P6 P1 --> T[Combined Net Buoyancy
Distributed Lift Vector] P2 --> T P3 --> T P4 --> T P5 --> T P6 --> T ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this distributed placement matters:** A single large buoyancy block would create a large **righting-arm moment** problem and pitch instability. Distributing the lift around the pressure hull gives the designer fine control over **trim** (fore-aft balance) and **stability** (resistance to roll/pitch). KTU questions often ask: "Why is buoyancy distributed rather than concentrated?" — the answer is **stability and trim control**. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **Define "Reserve Buoyancy" of a submersible and state its typical design value for a human-occupied vehicle.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Reserve Buoyancy (R)** is the excess of buoyant force over the total submerged weight of the submersible, expressed as: > > $$ > R = F_b - W_{sub} > $$ > > In fractional form, $R^* = 1 - \dfrac{W_{sub}}{F_b}$. > > For a **human-occupied submersible (HOV)**, classification societies and KTU design practice require a **reserve buoyancy fraction R\* of at least 5% to 10%** of total buoyant force, ensuring positive margin for emergency surfacing even after partial flooding. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **List any THREE commonly used buoyancy materials for deep-sea submersibles and state one limitation of each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per material + limitation):** > > 1. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** — Low cost and easy to shape, but **limited to shallow depths (< 500 m) due to creep and water absorption**. > 2. **Polymer syntactic foam** — Provides good lift and moderate depth capability (up to ~3,000 m), but **becomes brittle and loses buoyancy at extreme depths** due to microsphere collapse. > 3. **Ceramic syntactic foam** — Rated for hadal depths (11,000 m), but **expensive and difficult to machine into complex shapes**. **(3 marks)** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts per question. Part (a) typically 7 marks for analysis, Part (b) typically 7 marks for design or derivation. Full working must be shown. Final numerical answer should be boxed. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme] **A small research submersible has a dry weight (in air) of 8,500 kg. It uses syntactic foam buoyancy blocks of total volume 9.0 m³ mounted externally. Seawater density is 1025 kg/m³. Take g = 9.81 m/s².** **(a)** Calculate the **total buoyant force** and the **net buoyancy** of the submersible at the surface. State whether the sub will float freely at the surface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the syntactic foam has a bulk compressibility β = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ Pa⁻¹, calculate the **reduced buoyancy** and **net buoyancy** at an operating depth of 4,000 m, and comment on whether the sub can still surface under its own buoyancy. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Buoyancy at the Surface:** **Step 1:** Compute the total buoyant force using Archimedes' Principle: $$ \begin{aligned} F_b &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g \\ &= 1025 \times 9.0 \times 9.81 \\ &= 90{,}512.25 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Express the buoyant force in kg-force equivalent for clarity (optional but helpful): $$ F_b \text{ (in kgf)} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V = 1025 \times 9.0 = 9{,}225 \text{ kgf} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the total weight of the submersible in air: $$ W_{air} = m \cdot g = 8500 \times 9.81 = 83{,}385 \text{ N} $$ **Step 4:** Compute the net buoyancy at the surface: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{net} &= F_b - W_{air} \\ &= 90{,}512.25 - 83{,}385 \\ &= 7{,}127.25 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Interpretation: Since F_net > 0, **the submersible will float freely at the surface with approximately 7,127 N (≈ 727 kgf) of net upward force.** It is positively buoyant at the surface, which is the required safety condition. **Surface Reserve Buoyancy Fraction:** $$ R^* = \frac{F_{net}}{F_b} = \frac{7127.25}{90512.25} \approx 0.0787 \approx 7.87\% $$ This meets the 5–10% KTU design standard. ✅ **(7 marks — Part a complete)** --- **Part (b) — Compressed Buoyancy at 4,000 m Depth:** **Step 1:** Calculate the hydrostatic pressure increase at 4,000 m depth: $$ \Delta P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4000 = 40{,}221{,}000 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.22 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the fractional volume loss due to compressibility: $$ \frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\beta \cdot \Delta P = -(1.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times (40.22 \times 10^6) = -0.0603 $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the new volume of foam at depth: $$ V_{depth} = V_0 (1 - \beta \Delta P) = 9.0 \times (1 - 0.0603) = 9.0 \times 0.9397 = 8.457 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4:** Calculate the compressed buoyant force: $$ \begin{aligned} F_b^{comp} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{depth} \cdot g \\ &= 1025 \times 8.457 \times 9.81 \\ &= 85{,}051.6 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Calculate the net buoyancy at depth: $$ F_{net}^{depth} = 85{,}051.6 - 83{,}385 = 1{,}666.6 \text{ N} $$ **Step 6:** Engineering interpretation: - The sub retains a small **positive net buoyancy of 1,666.6 N (≈ 170 kgf)** at 4,000 m depth. - The **buoyancy has been reduced by 90,512.25 − 85,051.6 = 5,460.65 N** due to compression. - Since F_net > 0, the sub can still **surface passively under its own buoyancy**, but the safety margin is now only: $$ R^*_{depth} = \frac{1666.6}{85051.6} \approx 1.96\% $$ This is **below the 5% recommended safety threshold**. **Conclusion:** The submersible should not be operated at 4,000 m without additional variable ballast trim or a larger foam volume. **(7 marks — Part b complete)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme] **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **functional architecture of a syntactic foam buoyancy block**, identifying the matrix, the microsphere phase, and the governing rule-of-mixtures density equation. Discuss why the microspheres must be hollow. **(b) [7 marks]** A syntactic foam block has epoxy matrix density 1,150 kg/m³ and hollow glass microsphere density 220 kg/m³, with microsphere volume fraction 0.60. Calculate the foam density, its specific gravity in seawater (ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³), and the net buoyant force per cubic meter of foam when fully submerged. #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Functional Architecture (7 marks):** A syntactic foam buoyancy block is a **two-phase composite** engineered to be both lightweight and pressure-resistant. | Component | Material | Function | |---|---|---| | **Matrix** | Epoxy / Vinylester / Polyurethane resin | Binds the microspheres, transfers load, seals against water ingress | | **Microspheres** | Hollow glass or ceramic spheres (10–300 µm diameter) | Provide the low-density "lift" phase; crush progressively under pressure | | **Optional outer shell** | Fiber-reinforced polymer or metal | Protects against abrasion and impact damage | **Why microspheres must be hollow:** 1. **Density reduction:** A solid glass sphere has density ~2,500 kg/m³, which is *heavier* than seawater. The hollow interior (filled with low-pressure gas or vacuum) reduces effective density to ~200–400 kg/m³, which is far less than seawater. 2. **Compressibility behavior:** Hollow spheres **crush gradually** at depth rather than fracturing catastrophically, providing a **graceful, predictable** loss of buoyancy rather than sudden failure. 3. **Strength-to-weight optimization:** A hollow sphere has excellent specific stiffness (stiffness per unit mass) due to the I-geometry of its thin shell, making it ideal for load-bearing lightweight structures. **Governing density equation (Rule of Mixtures):** $$ \rho_{foam} = \rho_{matrix}(1 - \phi) + \rho_{ms} \phi $$ where φ is the volume fraction of microspheres. **(7 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Apply the rule of mixtures: $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_{foam} &= \rho_{matrix}(1 - \phi) + \rho_{ms} \phi \\ &= 1150 \times (1 - 0.60) + 220 \times 0.60 \\ &= 1150 \times 0.40 + 220 \times 0.60 \\ &= 460 + 132 \\ &= 592 \text{ kg/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the specific gravity in seawater: $$ SG = \frac{\rho_{foam}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{592}{1025} = 0.5776 $$ Since SG < 1, the foam is buoyant in seawater. ✅ **Step 3:** Compute the net buoyant force per cubic meter of foam: The buoyant force per cubic meter: $$ F_b \text{ per m}^3 = \rho_{sw} \cdot g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25 \text{ N/m}^3 $$ The weight per cubic meter of foam: $$ W_{foam} \text{ per m}^3 = \rho_{foam} \cdot g = 592 \times 9.81 = 5{,}807.52 \text{ N/m}^3 $$ Net buoyant force per m³: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{net} \text{ per m}^3 &= F_b - W_{foam} \\ &= 10{,}055.25 - 5{,}807.52 \\ &= 4{,}247.73 \text{ N/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Interpretation: Each cubic meter of this syntactic foam provides approximately **4,247.73 N (≈ 433 kgf)** of useful lift. For a 10 m³ assembly, this would yield over **42 kN (≈ 4.3 tonnes)** of net uplift — a powerful buoyancy system. **(7 marks — Part b complete)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ (freshwater) instead of 1025 kg/m³ (seawater).** This single error cascades through the entire problem and costs 2–3 marks. **Always check the problem statement for "seawater" or "saltwater"** before plugging in density. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by g.** Some students write F_b = ρV, which is mass, not force. The unit must be in Newtons. Examiners WILL deduct marks. > 3. **Ignoring compressibility in deep-depth problems.** A problem asking for buoyancy at 1,000+ m depth that does not include a compressibility term in the answer is automatically wrong. Always check the depth. > 4. **Confusing mass (kg) with weight (N).** Reserve buoyancy can be expressed in either, but the units must be consistent. State both for clarity. > 5. **Sign convention errors.** In KTU, "Net Buoyancy" is conventionally taken as positive when upward. If your calculation gives a negative number, immediately check whether the sub sinks (expected at depth) or whether you have made a sign error. > 6. **Skipping the units in the final answer.** Even if the numerical value is correct, missing units in the final boxed answer can cost 0.5–1 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Buoyancy Elements** - **Archimedes' Principle** states $F_b = \rho_{sw} V g$. It is the single most important equation in this module. - **Reserve buoyancy (R)** is the safety margin. KTU design standard: **R\* ≥ 5–10%** for HOVs. - **Hydrostatic pressure** at depth h: $P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$. Use ρ_sw = **1025 kg/m³** for seawater. - **Compressibility** must be accounted for at depths > 500 m. Use $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = -\beta \cdot \Delta P$. - **Syntactic foam density** follows the **rule of mixtures**: $\rho_{foam} = \rho_{matrix}(1-\phi) + \rho_{ms} \phi$. - **Specific gravity** of buoyancy material must be **less than 1** in seawater for it to provide lift. - **Buoyancy element placement** is **distributed** around the pressure hull for trim and stability, not concentrated. - **Emergency surfacing** must remain possible at maximum operating depth — always verify $F_{net}^{depth} > 0$. - **Material selection** is driven by operating depth: HDPE for < 500 m, polymer syntactic for < 3,000 m, ceramic syntactic for hadal depths. - **Variable ballast tanks** are NOT buoyancy elements per se — they are *buoyancy control systems* that change W_sub dynamically. - **Standard values to memorize:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $1$ atm $\approx 101.325$ kPa $\approx 10$ m seawater column. - **Two-phase buoyancy force interpretation:** Always think of F_b as "the weight of the displaced seawater" — it is a property of the fluid, not of the object. - **Negative net buoyancy** at depth is acceptable IF the variable ballast can recover positive buoyancy for surfacing. - **Failure mode analysis:** Foam crushing, microsphere collapse, water absorption, and ballast pump failure are the four primary failure modes examined in KTU design questions.

topics.title: Weight Elements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weight Elements

# Weight Elements in Submersibles

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the design of submersibles and submarines, **Weight Elements** refer to the comprehensive categorization and quantification of all mass components that contribute to the total submerged displacement of the vessel. According to naval architectural practice, the total weight (W) of a submersible is the sum of its **Light Ship Weight (LSW)** and **Deadweight (DWT)**, and this total must equal the **buoyancy force** (B) at the design operating depth to maintain neutral or controlled equilibrium.

$$W = LSW + DWT = \rho \, g \, \nabla = B$$

where $\rho$ is the density of seawater (**1025 kg/m³** at standard surface conditions), $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**), and $\nabla$ is the displaced volume in m³.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Weight estimation is the **first step** in the design spiral of any submersible. Without an accurate weight breakdown, all subsequent stages — stability, powering, endurance, pressure hull sizing — produce invalid results.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine packing a heavy-duty sealed suitcase for a deep-sea expedition. You must account for **everything**: the empty suitcase itself (Light Ship Weight), the clothes you pack (Outfit), the camera and equipment (Machinery), batteries (Systems), food and water for the trip (Variable Loads/Consumables), and finally, yourself (Crew + Payload). The suitcase will only float if the **total weight equals or is less than the volume of water it displaces**. If you overpack (exceed buoyancy), it sinks uncontrollably. Submersible designers must itemize each "category of packing" to ensure equilibrium is maintained at every operational depth.

### 1.3 Categories of Weight Elements

| Category | Sub-Components | Typical % of Total Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Hull Structure** | Pressure hull, outer/fairing hull, bulkheads, frames, shell plating | 25–35 % |
| **Machinery** | Main motors, propulsion units, pumps, compressors | 15–22 % |
| **Electrical & Electronics** | Batteries, switchboards, cables, sonar, navigation systems | 12–18 % |
| **Outfit & Furnishings** | Pipes, valves, insulation, ladders, lighting, fixtures | 8–12 % |
| **Variable Loads** | Crew, payload, consumables (fuel, water, lubricants) | 10–20 % |
| **Margin / Reserve** | Design margin, growth allowance | 5–10 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Submersible displacement is typically expressed in **tonnes (t)** where 1 tonne = 1000 kg = displaced volume of **0.9756 m³** in seawater.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition Logic of Weight Elements

The weight estimation process in submersible design follows a **top-down hierarchical decomposition**:

### Step 1 — Light Ship Weight (LSW) Estimation
The LSW is the permanent, structural weight of the vessel in its operational condition but without consumables, crew, or payload. It consists of four primary groups:

- **(a) Hull Steel Weight ($W_{Hull}$)**: Includes the **pressure hull** (the critical cylindrical or spherical envelope resisting external hydrostatic pressure), the **outer hull** (if non-structural fairing), internal bulkheads, frames, and stiffeners.
- **(b) Machinery Weight ($W_{Mach}$)**: Main propulsion motors, reduction gears, shaft lines, propellers (in air), pumps, hydraulic power units, and air compressors.
- **(c) Electrical Weight ($W_{Elec}$)**: Storage batteries (a major contributor in non-nuclear submersibles), switchgear, power distribution panels, cabling, and instrumentation.
- **(d) Outfit Weight ($W_{Outfit}$)**: Piping systems, valves, heat exchangers, insulation, ladder/stairways, lighting fixtures, furniture, paint, and corrosion-protection systems.

$$W_{LSW} = W_{Hull} + W_{Mach} + W_{Elec} + W_{Outfit}$$

### Step 2 — Deadweight (DWT) Estimation
DWT includes all **variable** weights that change between voyages or during operation:

- Crew and personal effects ($W_{Crew}$)
- Scientific/military payload, equipment, mission-specific stores ($W_{Payload}$)
- Consumables: fuel, freshwater, lubricants, oxygen ($W_{Cons}$)
- Reserve feed water, ballast water (variable) ($W_{Ballast}$)

$$W_{DWT} = W_{Crew} + W_{Payload} + W_{Cons} + W_{Ballast}$$

### Step 3 — Total Displacement
The final design must satisfy the equilibrium condition:

$$W_{Total} = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT} = \rho \, g \, \nabla_{sub}$$

where $\nabla_{sub}$ is the **submerged displaced volume**, which for a fully submerged body equals its external envelope volume.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \rho g \nabla$ | Archimedes' equilibrium — Weight equals buoyancy | N (or tonnes) |
| 2 | $\nabla = \dfrac{W}{\rho \, g}$ | Displaced volume from total weight | m³ |
| 3 | $W_{Total} = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT}$ | Master weight sum | tonnes |
| 4 | $W_{LSW} = W_H + W_M + W_E + W_O$ | Light Ship Weight decomposition | tonnes |
| 5 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot H}$ | Block coefficient of displacement | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R_B = \nabla_{surface} - \nabla_{submerged}$ | Reserve buoyancy (volume basis) | m³ |
| 7 | $w_{LH} = \dfrac{W_{LSW}}{\Delta}$ | Light ship weight ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $W_{Hull} = k_H \cdot V_H \cdot \rho_{steel}$ | Hull weight from material volume | tonnes |
| 9 | $P_{ext} = \rho g d$ | External pressure at depth $d$ | Pa |
| 10 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Cylinder wall thickness (membrane theory) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to memorize:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ (seawater), $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

Accurate weight estimation directly governs:
- **Pressure hull thickness** (heavier submersibles → larger displacement → larger pressure hulls for given depth).
- **Propulsion power** (mass dictates powering requirements via resistance and propulsion coefficients).
- **Endurance** (consumable weight fraction determines mission duration).
- **Cost** (steel weight ≈ 30 % of total cost for conventional submersibles).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Submerged Displacement from Weight

The starting point is the Archimedean equilibrium. For a fully submerged submersible at operating depth $d$:

**Step 1.** Write Archimedes' principle:

$$
B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 2.** Force balance for static equilibrium (neutral buoyancy condition):

$$
W = B
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute and rearrange:

$$
W = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the displaced volume:

$$
\nabla = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}
$$

**Step 5.** Convert $W$ to mass $m$ (i.e., $W = m \cdot g$) and simplify for a numerical expression:

$$
\nabla = \frac{m}{\rho_{sw}} \quad \text{(in SI units: m}^3 \text{)}
$$

**Step 6.** For engineering convenience using tonnes (where 1 tonne = 1000 kg), and $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$
\nabla \, (\text{m}^3) = \frac{W \, (\text{tonnes})}{1.025}
$$

This is a **fundamental relation** every KTU student must internalize — it links weight directly to the geometric envelope of the submersible.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hull Steel Weight from Cylinder Geometry

For a cylindrical pressure hull of inner radius $R_i$, length $L$, and wall thickness $t$:

**Step 1.** Steel volume (treating $t \ll R_i$, so cylindrical shell approximation):

$$
V_{steel} = 2 \pi R_i \cdot L \cdot t + \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \cdot L
$$

**Step 2.** Apply thin-shell approximation ($R_o \approx R_i + t$, $t \ll R_i$):

$$
V_{steel} \approx 2 \pi R_i \cdot L \cdot t
$$

**Step 3.** Mass of steel:

$$
m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel} = 2 \pi R_i \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \rho_{steel}
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $t$ from membrane theory for hoop stress:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot R_i}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

where $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d$ is the external hydrostatic pressure at design depth $d$.

**Step 5.** Combine:

$$
m_{steel} = 2 \pi R_i \cdot L \cdot \frac{P \cdot R_i}{\sigma_{allow}} \cdot \rho_{steel}
$$

$$
m_{steel} = \frac{2 \pi R_i^2 \cdot L \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d \cdot \rho_{steel}}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

**Step 6.** Convert to tonnes (divide by 1000):

$$
W_{Hull} = \frac{2 \pi R_i^2 \cdot L \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d \cdot \rho_{steel}}{1000 \, \sigma_{allow}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** Hull weight scales as the **square of the radius** and **linearly with operating depth**. Doubling the hull diameter quadruples the steel weight — a critical trade-off in submersible sizing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Weight Fractions

| Submersible Class | W_Hull (%) | W_Mach (%) | W_Elec (%) | W_Outfit (%) | W_DWT (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small ROV/AUV (< 5 t) | 20 | 18 | 30 | 10 | 22 |
| Manned Research Sub (20–50 t) | 28 | 19 | 16 | 10 | 27 |
| Deep-Diving Research (100–300 t) | 32 | 17 | 14 | 9 | 28 |
| Military Submarine (> 3000 t) | 35 | 22 | 12 | 8 | 23 |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A submersible has the following weight breakdown: Hull = 18 t, Machinery = 12 t, Electrical = 9 t, Outfit = 6 t, Crew + Payload = 3 t, Consumables = 4 t. Find (i) Light Ship Weight, (ii) Deadweight, (iii) Total Weight, (iv) Submerged displaced volume in seawater.

**Solution:**

(i) Light Ship Weight:
$$
W_{LSW} = 18 + 12 + 9 + 6 = 45 \text{ t}
$$

(ii) Deadweight:
$$
W_{DWT} = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ t}
$$

(iii) Total Weight:
$$
W_{Total} = 45 + 7 = 52 \text{ t}
$$

(iv) Submerged displaced volume:
$$
\nabla = \frac{W_{Total}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{52}{1.025} \approx 50.73 \text{ m}^3
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Weight Element Aggregation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Submersible Weight W] --> B[Light Ship Weight LSW]
    A --> C[Deadweight DWT]
    B --> D[Hull Structure W_Hull]
    B --> E[Machinery W_Mach]
    B --> F[Electrical Systems W_Elec]
    B --> G[Outfit and Furnishing W_Outfit]
    C --> H[Crew and Personal W_Crew]
    C --> I[Mission Payload W_Payload]
    C --> J[Consumables W_Cons]
    C --> K[Variable Ballast W_Ballast]
    D --> D1[Pressure Hull Cyl Sphere]
    D --> D2[Outer Fairing Hull]
    D --> D3[Bulkheads and Frames]
    E --> E1[Main Propulsion Motors]
    E --> E2[Pumps and Compressors]
    E --> E3[Hydraulic Units]
    F --> F1[Battery Banks]
    F --> F2[Switchgear Cabling]
    F --> F3[Sonar Navigation]
    G --> G1[Piping and Valves]
    G --> G2[Insulation and Paint]
    G --> G3[Lighting Ladders]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Weight Estimation Design Spiral

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1 Mission Requirements] --> S2[Step 2 Initial Layout Geometry]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Estimate LSW]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Estimate DWT]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Compute Total W]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Check Buoyancy Equilibrium]
    S6 --> S7{Converged?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Adjust Geometry Margins]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Step 7 Final Weight Report]
    S9 --> S10[Step 8 Input to Stability and Powering]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Mass-to-Displacement Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mass Element W_i] --> Sum[Sigma Summation W_total]
    Sum --> Arch[Archimedes Equation W = rho g Nabla]
    Arch --> Vol[Displaced Volume Nabla]
    Vol --> Geo[Hull Geometry L B H R]
    Geo --> Stability[Transverse and Longitudinal Stability]
    Geo --> Power[Propulsive Power Estimation]
    Geo --> Cost[Steel Cost Estimation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define Light Ship Weight (LSW). List its four main components with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Light Ship Weight (LSW) is the permanent weight of a completely constructed submersible in operating condition but without crew, payload, consumables, or any variable load. Its four main components are:
- **Hull Structure** — e.g., pressure hull, outer hull, bulkheads
- **Machinery** — e.g., main propulsion motor
- **Electrical Systems** — e.g., battery banks
- **Outfit & Furnishings** — e.g., piping, valves, insulation

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Distinguish between Light Ship Weight and Deadweight in a submersible.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Light Ship Weight (LSW) | Deadweight (DWT) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Permanent, structural | Variable, mission-dependent |
| Examples | Hull, machinery, batteries, outfit | Crew, payload, fuel, water |
| Variability | Fixed after construction | Changes per voyage |
| % of Total | ~ 75–85 % | ~ 15–25 % |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete decomposition of the total weight of a submersible into Light Ship Weight (LSW) and Deadweight (DWT). List at least three sub-components of each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small research submersible is to be designed for a maximum operating depth of 600 m. The estimated weight breakdown is: Pressure hull = 14 t, Outer hull & fairing = 4 t, Machinery = 10 t, Batteries = 8 t, Outfit = 5 t, Crew + payload = 2 t, Consumables = 3 t. Calculate: (i) Light Ship Weight, (ii) Deadweight, (iii) Total submerged weight, and (iv) Submerged displaced volume. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

The total weight is decomposed as:

$$
W_{Total} = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT}
$$

- **Light Ship Weight (LSW):** Hull structure (pressure hull, outer hull, bulkheads), Machinery (motors, pumps), Electrical (batteries, cables), Outfit (pipes, valves).
- **Deadweight (DWT):** Crew, payload/mission equipment, consumables (fuel, water, oxygen), variable ballast.

```
[See Block Diagram in Section 4.1 above]
```

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

(i) Light Ship Weight:
$$
W_{LSW} = 14 + 4 + 10 + 8 + 5 = 41 \text{ t}
$$

(ii) Deadweight:
$$
W_{DWT} = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ t}
$$

(iii) Total submerged weight:
$$
W_{Total} = 41 + 5 = 46 \text{ t} = 46{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

(iv) Submerged displaced volume:
$$
\nabla = \frac{W_{Total}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{46{,}000}{1025} \approx 44.88 \text{ m}^3
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Derive an expression for the hull steel weight of a cylindrical pressure hull of inner radius $R_i$, length $L$, and operating depth $d$. State the membrane theory equation used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A submersible pressure hull has inner radius $R_i = 1.5$ m, length $L = 6$ m, design depth $d = 1000$ m, and allowable steel stress $\sigma_{allow} = 250$ MPa. Compute: (i) the hydrostatic external pressure, (ii) the wall thickness $t$, and (iii) the steel mass required for the cylindrical shell. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

Starting from membrane theory for a thin-walled cylinder:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot R_i}{t} \implies t = \frac{P \cdot R_i}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

With $P = \rho_{sw} \, g \, d$:

$$
t = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, g \, d \, R_i}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

Steel shell volume (thin-shell approximation):

$$
V_{steel} = 2 \pi R_i \, L \, t = \frac{2 \pi R_i^2 \, L \, \rho_{sw} \, g \, d}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

Steel mass:

$$
m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{steel} = \frac{2 \pi R_i^2 \, L \, \rho_{sw} \, g \, d \, \rho_{steel}}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

(i) Hydrostatic pressure at 1000 m:
$$
P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1000 = 10{,}055{,}250 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.055 \text{ MPa}
$$

(ii) Wall thickness:
$$
t = \frac{P \cdot R_i}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{10.055 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.5}{250 \times 10^6} = 0.0603 \text{ m} \approx 60.3 \text{ mm}
$$

(iii) Steel mass:
$$
V_{steel} = 2 \pi \cdot 1.5 \cdot 6 \cdot 0.0603 = 3.408 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
m_{steel} = 3.408 \cdot 7850 = 26{,}753 \text{ kg} \approx 26.75 \text{ t}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing tonnes and kg without explicit conversion (lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure:** At depth $d$, some solutions add $P_{atm}$ — examiners usually ignore it unless explicitly stated.
> 3. **Using fresh-water density (1000 kg/m³)** instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³). Always use 1025 unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> 4. **Confusing LSW with DWT:** Examiners frequently test this in 2-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **Skipping diagram labeling:** Even in 7-mark questions, a block diagram earns a full 2-mark allocation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total Weight** = **Light Ship Weight (LSW)** + **Deadweight (DWT)** = $\rho_{sw} \, g \, \nabla$.
- **LSW** is **fixed** post-construction; **DWT** is **mission-variable**.
- LSW comprises: **Hull + Machinery + Electrical + Outfit**.
- DWT comprises: **Crew + Payload + Consumables + Ballast**.
- **Seawater density** to use: **1025 kg/m³**.
- **Reserve buoyancy** = difference between surface and fully submerged displacements.
- Hull steel weight scales as **R²** and linearly with **depth** — diameter is the most sensitive design parameter.
- Cylinder thickness (membrane theory): $t = P R / \sigma_{allow}$.
- Always verify **Archimedean equilibrium** ($W = B$) at the design operating depth.
- Typical weight fractions: Hull 25–35 %, Machinery 15–22 %, Electrical 12–18 %, Outfit 8–12 %, DWT 10–20 %.
- **Block coefficient** $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot H)$ relates displacement to rectangular envelope.
- Weight estimation is the **gateway** to all subsequent design stages — pressure hull sizing, stability analysis, powering, and cost estimation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trim and Compensating Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trim and Compensating Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Trim – Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a **submersible (submarine)**, **Trim** is defined as the **longitudinal inclination** of the vehicle about its transverse (athwartships) axis, expressed as the algebraic difference between the **aft draft (T_A)** and the **forward draft (T_F)**, measured at the design waterline when the vehicle is in the submerged or surfaced condition.

$$\text{Trim} = T_A - T_F$$

A positive trim is referred to as **"trim by the stern"** (vessel inclined downward at the rear), while a negative trim indicates **"trim by the head/bow"** (vessel inclined downward at the front). The datum for measuring drafts is the **keel line** of the pressure hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Trim is a critical operational parameter for any submersible because it directly affects hydrostatic stability, propeller immersion, control-surface effectiveness (fairing planes/sail planes), and sonar/radar performance. An uncontrolled trim in a submerged vessel can lead to a **"nose-down" or "tail-down" dive** that may exceed safe operating depth.

### 1.2 Compensating Tanks – Formal Academic Definition

**Compensating tanks** (also called **trim tanks** or **fine ballast tanks**) are auxiliary, low-volume, completely enclosed fluid containers strategically located near the **bow and stern** of a submersible. Their primary engineering function is to provide **fine-grained control of longitudinal trim** and, to a limited extent, **fine adjustment of buoyancy** by the controlled transfer, flooding, or emptying of seawater or low-density fuel oil using small high-pressure pumps and air systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Ballast/Compensating Systems in a Submersible:**
> 1. **Main Ballast Tanks (MBT)** – Large external tanks for surfacing and submerging.
> 2. **Variable Ballast Tanks (VBT)** – Internal tanks for fine buoyancy control (neutral buoyancy).
> 3. **Trim / Compensating Tanks** – Bow and stern tanks for fine longitudinal trim.
> 4. **Hard Ballast** – Permanent solid weights (e.g., lead) for static weight.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Fishing Canoe" Analogy:** Imagine you are sitting in a long, narrow canoe on a calm lake. If you slide a heavy backpack toward the front, the bow dips down and the stern rises — the canoe is now **trimmed by the head**. To correct this, you can move a small bucket of water from the front to the back, or pour a small amount into a bow tank and pump some out of a stern tank. The small buckets you use to fine-tune the canoe's posture are exactly analogous to **compensating tanks** in a submarine. The backpack is analogous to a heavy piece of equipment (e.g., a sonar array) that has shifted its center of gravity. The trim and compensating system in a real submarine works on the same principle: small, precisely metered amounts of fluid are moved or added/removed to keep the boat perfectly **level in the longitudinal plane**.

### 1.4 Why Trim Control is Harder for Submersibles Than Surface Ships

- A surface ship has ample **residual buoyancy (reserve buoyancy)** and is supported by **dynamic lift** from forward motion, so minor trim errors are tolerated.
- A submerged submersible is in a **fully submerged, hydrostatic equilibrium** state. Even a **few centimeters of trim error** can cause:
  - Uncontrolled change in depth (porpoising).
  - Excessive bending moments on the pressure hull.
  - Loss of maneuverability (planes become asymmetrically effective).
- Hence, **trim tanks are mandatory**, not optional, in any modern submersible design.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of Seawater (standard):** **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
- **Density of Fresh Water:** **ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Trim is typically expressed in:** **meters (m)** or **centimeters (cm)** of draft difference.
- **Moment to Change Trim (MCT) per cm:** **tonne-meters per centimeter (t·m/cm)**.
- **Metacentric Height (longitudinal):** **GM_L in meters (m)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does a Submersible Develop Trim?

A submersible can develop a longitudinal trim due to several engineering and operational reasons:

1. **Asymmetric Weight Distribution** – Heavy machinery (e.g., torpedo tubes, battery banks, sonar dome) may not be located at amidships, creating a longitudinal center-of-gravity offset.
2. **Variable Cargo Loading** – Loading of weapons, supplies, or scientific equipment.
3. **Crew Movement** – Although small, the longitudinal migration of crew can cause measurable trim.
4. **Flooding of Main Ballast Tanks (MBT)** – If MBTs flood asymmetrically during emergency or normal operations, large trim moments are generated.
5. **Consumption of Fuel and Stores** – As fuel is consumed from forward and after tanks unequally, the longitudinal center of gravity shifts.
6. **Changes in Depth and Salinity** – The center of buoyancy moves with depth due to hull volume changes under external pressure.

### 2.2 Operational Concept of Trim Correction

The trim correction system operates on a simple physical principle: **A small mass moved a large distance produces the same moment as a large mass moved a small distance.**

$$\text{Moment} = m \times d$$

Therefore, compensating tanks are designed with a **long moment arm** (located far forward and far aft) so that even a small quantity of water or oil produces a large correcting moment. This minimizes pumping power and the size of the pumps.

### 2.3 Classification of Compensating Tanks

**Based on location:**
- **Forward Compensating Tank (FCT)** – Located in the bow section.
- **Aft Compensating Tank (ACT)** – Located in the stern section.

**Based on the fluid used:**
- **Water Compensating Tanks** – Use seawater for trimming (heavier, simpler).
- **Oil Compensating Tanks** – Use light fuel oil (LFO) for trimming (lighter, used in modern nuclear submarines for fine control).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Submarine Practice:** Modern nuclear submarines (e.g., Virginia class, Astute class) use **light fuel oil (LFO) in hard tanks** for fine trim control. The oil is moved between forward and aft trim tanks by small high-pressure pumps. This system is preferred because oil does not corrode the pressure hull, and the system is sealed from the sea (improving quieting).

### 2.4 Design Logic of the Compensating System (Step-by-Step)

1. **Step 1 — Trim Survey:** A static trim survey is conducted with the submersible in dry dock. The longitudinal center of gravity (LCG) is calculated from all installed weights.
2. **Step 2 — Determination of Residual Trim:** Using the loading condition, the expected trim is computed using hydrostatic curves.
3. **Step 3 — Computation of Correcting Moment:** The moment required to bring the trim to zero (level) is calculated as:

$$M_{corr} = \Delta T \times \text{MCTC}$$

   where ΔT is the trim to be corrected (in cm) and MCTC is the moment to change trim by **one centimeter**.
4. **Step 4 — Selection of Tank and Fluid Quantity:** The required mass of water/oil to be transferred is:

$$m = \frac{M_{corr}}{L_{tank}}$$

   where L_tank is the longitudinal distance between the forward and aft compensating tank centers.
5. **Step 5 — Pumping:** The mass is transferred by an internal trim pump or by compressed air displacement.
6. **Step 6 — Verification:** Drafts at the forward and aft markers are read, and the trim is verified.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $\text{Trim} = T_A - T_F$ | Basic trim definition (m or cm) | T_A = aft draft, T_F = forward draft |
| $\text{MCTC} = \dfrac{W \times L_{BP}}{100 \times GM_L}$ | Moment to Change Trim per Centimeter (t·m/cm) | W = displacement (t), L_BP = length between perpendiculars (m), GM_L = longitudinal metacentric height (m) |
| $\Delta T = \dfrac{M}{\text{MCTC}}$ | Trim change produced by a moment (cm) | M = applied moment (t·m), ΔT = trim change (cm) |
| $M_{corr} = m \times d$ | Correcting moment of a transferred mass (t·m) | m = mass moved (t), d = moment arm (m) |
| $m_{water} = \rho_{sw} \times V_{tank}$ | Mass of seawater in a flooded compensating tank (t) | ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³, V_tank = tank volume (m³) |
| $V_{tank} = \dfrac{M_{corr}}{L_{tank} \times \rho_{sw} \times g}$ | Required volume of compensating tank (m³) | L_tank = distance between FCT & ACT centers (m), g = 9.81 m/s² |
| $M_{trim} = W \times (x_G - x_B)$ | Trim moment from LCG – LCB offset (t·m) | x_G, x_B = longitudinal positions of G and B from midship (m) |
| $\tan\theta = \dfrac{T_A - T_F}{L_{BP}}$ | Trim angle (radians) | θ = trim angle, L_BP = length between perpendiculars |
| $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \times g \times Q \times h}{\eta}$ | Trim pump power requirement (W) | Q = flow rate (m³/s), h = head (m), η = pump efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **MCTC Derivation Basis:** The formula MCTC = W·L_BP / (100·GM_L) comes from the fundamental relationship between the **longitudinal metacentric height (GM_L)** and the **virtual rise/shift of the center of buoyancy** when the vessel is trimmed by a small angle. The factor **100** converts from "per meter" to "per centimeter."

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Submersibles:** Used for cable burial, pipeline inspection, ROV operations.
- **Scientific Submersibles:** e.g., *Alvin (WHOI)*, *Shinkai 6500 (JAMSTEC)*, *DSV Limiting Factor*.
- **Military Submersibles:** SSNs (nuclear attack subs), SSBNs (ballistic missile subs), SSKs (diesel-electric).
- **AUVs / UUVs:** Even small autonomous underwater vehicles have a trim system (often movable ballast blocks or active ballast pumps).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moment to Change Trim by One Centimeter (MCTC)

**Problem Statement:** A submarine of displacement **W = 4500 tonnes** and length between perpendiculars **L_BP = 110 m** has a longitudinal metacentric height **GM_L = 120 m** in the submerged condition. Calculate the **MCTC** (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm).

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental trim equation:**

The change in trim (in meters) produced by an applied moment M about the center of flotation is given by:

$$\Delta T_{(m)} = \frac{M}{W \times GM_L} \times L_{BP}$$

**Step 2 — Explanation of the formula:**

When a small moment M is applied to a floating body, the center of buoyancy (B) shifts transversely by an amount equal to **GM_L × sin(θ)**, where θ is the trim angle. The corresponding change in draft at one end (relative to midship) is **GM_L × sin(θ)**. With **sin(θ) ≈ tan(θ) = ΔT / L_BP** for small angles, the relationship emerges.

**Step 3 — Algebraic rearrangement to solve for M:**

We set ΔT = 1 cm = 0.01 m. The required moment is:

$$M = \Delta T_{(m)} \times \frac{W \times GM_L}{L_{BP}}$$

Substituting ΔT = 0.01 m:

$$M = 0.01 \times \frac{W \times GM_L}{L_{BP}}$$

This is the moment to change trim by **1 cm**, which by definition is the MCTC:

$$\text{MCTC} = 0.01 \times \frac{W \times GM_L}{L_{BP}} = \frac{W \times GM_L}{100 \times L_{BP}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-arranged equivalent form:** MCTC = W·L_BP / (100·GM_L) — this is the **inverse** form used when the question provides L_BP, W, and GM_L directly. Both forms are mathematically equivalent because ΔT and the constants can be rearranged. **Always check the form that matches the data given.**

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\text{MCTC} = \frac{W \times L_{BP}}{100 \times GM_L} = \frac{4500 \times 110}{100 \times 120}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the multiplication and division:**

$$\text{MCTC} = \frac{495000}{12000} = 41.25 \; \text{t·m/cm}$$

**Result:** The moment required to change the trim of this submarine by 1 cm is **MCTC = 41.25 tonne-meters per centimeter**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Volume of Compensating Tank

**Problem Statement:** Continuing from Derivation 1, the submarine has developed a trim of **+25 cm by the stern** (i.e., the stern is 25 cm deeper than the bow). The compensating tank system has a tank center-to-center distance of **L_tank = 95 m**. Calculate the **volume of water** that must be transferred from the aft compensating tank to the forward compensating tank to level the vessel (using seawater).

**Step 1 — Calculate the total correcting moment required:**

$$M_{corr} = \Delta T \times \text{MCTC}$$

Here, ΔT = 25 cm, MCTC = 41.25 t·m/cm.

$$M_{corr} = 25 \times 41.25 = 1031.25 \; \text{t·m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required mass of water to be transferred:**

The correcting moment is produced by moving a mass m through a distance L_tank:

$$M_{corr} = m \times L_{tank} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{M_{corr}}{L_{tank}}$$

$$m = \frac{1031.25}{95} = 10.855 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass to volume of seawater:**

$$V_{water} = \frac{m}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{10.855}{1.025} = 10.59 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Result:** Approximately **10.59 m³ of seawater** must be pumped from the aft compensating tank to the forward compensating tank. This is a small, easily managed quantity — a perfect example of why compensating tanks are designed with a long moment arm.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trim Angle from Draft Readings

**Problem Statement:** A submersible has a forward draft of **T_F = 6.20 m** and an aft draft of **T_A = 6.45 m**. The length between perpendiculars is **L_BP = 80 m**. Find the trim angle θ in degrees.

**Step 1 — Compute the trim (difference in drafts):**

$$\text{Trim} = T_A - T_F = 6.45 - 6.20 = 0.25 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-angle approximation:**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\text{Trim}}{L_{BP}} = \frac{0.25}{80} = 0.003125$$

**Step 3 — Compute θ in radians, then convert to degrees:**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.003125) = 0.003125 \; \text{rad (since the angle is very small)}$$

$$\theta_{deg} = 0.003125 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.179^\circ$$

**Result:** The trim angle is **0.003125 rad ≈ 0.179°**. This very small angle is typical of operational submersibles and confirms why **precision in compensating tank calculations is critical**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Trim Pump Power Requirement

**Problem Statement:** The trim pump must move **10.59 m³ of seawater** from the aft tank to the forward tank against a head of **h = 8 m** in a time of **t = 4 minutes (240 s)**. The pump efficiency is **η = 0.7**. Calculate the required pump power.

**Step 1 — Compute the volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{V_{water}}{t} = \frac{10.59}{240} = 0.0441 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pump power formula:**

$$P = \frac{\rho_{sw} \times g \times Q \times h}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$P = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.0441 \times 8}{0.7}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate step by step:**

$$P = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.0441 \times 8}{0.7} = \frac{3550.7}{0.7} \approx 5072 \; \text{W} \approx 5.07 \; \text{kW}$$

**Result:** The trim pump must deliver approximately **5.07 kW** of hydraulic power, which translates to a small fractional horsepower motor (typically the actual electric motor would be in the **7–10 kW range** to account for losses).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Compensation Methods

| **Method** | **Fluid** | **Speed of Correction** | **Quieting (Acoustic Signature)** | **Corrosion Risk** | **KTU-Mentioned?** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| Water Transfer Pump | Seawater | Slow (minutes) | Moderate (pump noise) | High | ✔ |
| Compressed Air Blow | Seawater | Fast (seconds) | Low (no pump) | Moderate | ✔ |
| Light Fuel Oil Transfer | LFO | Slow (minutes) | Low (no seawater noise) | Very Low | ✔ |
| Moveable Lead Ballast | Solid lead | Very slow (hours) | Silent | None | ✔ |
| Active Control Surfaces (Planes) | N/A | Immediate | Silent | None | ✔ (auxiliary) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Trim Correction Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Detection[Detection and Computation Stage]
        A1[Draft Sensors at Bow and Stern] --> A2[Trim Computer and LCG Calculator]
        A2 --> A3{Trim within Tolerance?}
    end

    subgraph Tolerance[Acceptable Trim Range]
        A3 -- Yes --> A4[System Idle and Monitoring]
        A4 --> A1
    end

    subgraph Correction[Active Trim Correction Stage]
        A3 -- No --> B1[Compute Required Correcting Moment]
        B1 --> B2[Determine Quantity of Fluid to Transfer]
        B2 --> B3[Select Forward or Aft Tank]
        B3 --> B4[Activate Trim Pump or Air System]
        B4 --> B5[Transfer Fluid Between FCT and ACT]
        B5 --> A1
    end

    subgraph Safety[Safety Interlocks]
        S1[Overpressure Sensor] --> B4
        S2[Tank Level High Alarm] --> B4
        S3[Manual Override Switch] --> B4
    end
```

### 4.2 Longitudinal Cross-Section of a Submersible Showing Trim System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BowSection[Bow Section]
        B1[Forward Sonar Array]
        B2[Forward Compensating Tank FCT]
        B3[Forward Main Ballast Tank FMBT]
        B4[Sail Plane Fairing]
    end

    subgraph MidSection[Midship Pressure Hull]
        M1[Command and Control Compartment]
        M2[Trim Pump and Motor Assembly]
        M3[Battery Bank and Reactor Shield]
        M4[Longitudinal Axis LCG LCB Reference]
    end

    subgraph SternSection[Stern Section]
        S1[Aft Compensating Tank ACT]
        S2[Aft Main Ballast Tank AMBT]
        S3[Propulsion Motor and Shaft]
        S4[Rudder and Stern Planes]
    end

    BowSection --> MidSection
    MidSection --> SternSection

    M2 -. Interconnects .- B2
    M2 -. Interconnects .- S1
```

### 4.3 Trim and Compensating Tank Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Operator Initiates Trim Correction] --> P2[Read Bow and Stern Drafts]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Current Trim in cm]
    P3 --> P4{Trim equals Zero}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Maintain and Log]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Compute MCTC from Hydrostatic Data]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Required Correcting Moment]
    P7 --> P8[Compute Fluid Mass to Transfer]
    P8 --> P9[Open Interconnecting Valves]
    P9 --> P10[Start Trim Pump]
    P10 --> P11[Monitor Tank Levels]
    P11 --> P12{Target Trim Achieved}
    P12 -- No --> P11
    P12 -- Yes --> P13[Stop Pump and Close Valves]
    P13 --> P14[Verify Drafts and Log]
    P14 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid block diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of the trim control system — the physical layout of the compensating tanks within the pressure hull (longitudinal cross-section) and the logical control flow of the trim correction process. These diagrams replace physical engineering drawings, which would normally be produced from CAD models in actual KTU design-project work.

---
