The drag of fins, control surfaces, propeller struts, and sonar domes is treated by assuming each appendage has its own form drag coefficient $C_{D,app}$ and frontal area $A_{app}$:

$$R_{APP} = \sum_i C_{D,app,i}\cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, A_{app,i}\cdot V^2$$

A standard rule-of-thumb is $R_{APP} \approx 5$–$10\%$ of bare-hull viscous resistance for a well-designed submersible.

### Step 5 — Air / Wind Resistance

For surface-running vessels only; **zero for a fully submerged submersible**.

### Step 6 — Correlation Allowances

The **roughness allowance** $\Delta C_F$ accounts for hull roughness, fouling, paint deterioration, and manufacturing imperfections:

$$C_{F,total} = (1 + k)\left[\frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}\right] + \Delta C_F$$

A common KTU textbook value: $\Delta C_F = 0.0004$ (typical painted hull, 6–12 months out of dock).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table. It contains **every equation** the KTU board examiner can ask from Module 3 / Resistance. Using `\vert` instead of `|` ensures the markdown table renders correctly.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_T$ | Total resistance | $R$ in N, $S$ in m², $V$ in m/s |
| 2 | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 C_F$ | Frictional resistance | — |
| 3 | $C_F = 0.075\,/\,(\log_{10}Re - 2)^2$ | ITTC 1978 friction line | Turbulent, valid for $10^6 \le Re \le 10^9$ |
| 4 | $Re = V L \,/\, \nu$ | Reynolds number | $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (seawater, 15 °C) |
| 5 | $Fn = V \,/\, \sqrt{g L}$ | Froude number | $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 6 | $C_T = (1+k)\, C_F + C_W + C_{APP} + \Delta C_F$ | Total coefficient (surface ship) | All terms non-dimensional |
| 7 | $C_T = (1+k)\, C_F + \Delta C_F$ | Total coefficient (deeply submerged) | Wave term dropped |
| 8 | $R_{APP} = \sum C_{D,app}\cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_{app} V^2$ | Appendage drag | Sum over all appendages |
| 9 | $C_B = \nabla \,/\, (L\cdot B \cdot T)$ | Block coefficient | For surface ships |
| 10 | $S = \nabla^{2/3}\,[1.7\, L/C_P + \nabla^{1/3}/L]$ | Wetted area (Sloof/Schenzle) | Approximate surface ship |
| 11 | $EHP = R_T \cdot V \,/\, 75$ | Effective Horsepower (metric) | $R$ in kgf, $V$ in m/s, 75 = kgf·m/s per HP |
| 12 | $EHP = R_T \cdot V \,/\, 1000$ | Effective Horsepower (SI, kW) | $R$ in kN, $V$ in m/s |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The resistance estimate directly determines the **propulsion power** required, which determines the **battery capacity** for an electric submersible, the **shaft power** for a diesel-electric boat, the **endurance** at a given cruise speed, and the **hull-form optimization** direction. In modern AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) design, even a **2% reduction in $C_T$** can extend mission duration by 10–15% — a critical economic factor. The **Holtrop-Mennen method** (1977, still the industrial standard for merchant-ship resistance prediction) and the **Series 64** regression database are used in industry; the KTU 2024 syllabus asks students to understand the **principles**, not memorize the multi-page regression tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus mapping (COs):** This topic primarily satisfies **CO2 — "Estimate resistance and powering of submerged/submerging bodies"** under the Bloom's levels *Apply* and *Analyze*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ITTC 1978 Friction Line from the Prandtl–Schlichting Skin-Friction Law

The ITTC 1978 correlation is a **fitted approximation** to the more rigorous Prandtl–Schlichting turbulent flat-plate formula. The Prandtl–Schlichting expression is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re)^2.58} - \frac{A}{Re}$$

The **ITTC 1978 simplification** approximates the above with a single power-law curve valid for $10^6 \le Re \le 10^9$. The derivation is **curve-fitting** in $\log_{10} Re$ space:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Define } X = \log_{10} Re - 2 \\[2pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Take the form } C_F = \dfrac{a}{X^b} \\[2pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Best fit across the ITTC benchmark dataset yields } a = 0.075,\ b = 2 \\[2pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \boxed{\,C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

The first term inside the log, $\log_{10} Re$, has a **"$-2$" subtracted** so that for $Re = 10^6$ the denominator becomes $(6-2)^2 = 16$, giving $C_F \approx 0.0047$ — matching the Prandtl–Schlichting value exactly.

> [!NOTE]
> The minus 2 is **not a constant for the physics** — it is a numerical fit chosen so that the formula reproduces $C_F$ at the **canonical Reynolds number $Re = 10^6$**. KTU examiners will deduct marks if the "$-2$" is omitted.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Resistance Coefficient for a Deeply Submerged Submersible

For a body operating at depth $h \ge 1.5\,L$ below the free surface, the wave-making component is neglected. The total resistance coefficient is then:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_T &= (1 + k)\, C_F \;+\; \Delta C_F \;+\; C_{APP} \\[2pt]
&= (1+k)\left[\frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}\right] + \Delta C_F + \frac{\sum R_{APP,i}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2}
\end{aligned}$$

The corresponding **dimensional** total resistance is:

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2 \left\{ (1+k)\left[\frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}\right] + \Delta C_F + C_{APP} \right\}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example Problem (Model-Ship Extrapolation)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern - Model Question]**
> A model submersible of length $L_m = 2.0$ m is towed at $V_m = 2.5$ m/s in fresh water at 20 °C ($\nu_m = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\rho_m = 998$ kg/m³). The measured model resistance is $R_{T,m} = 18.5$ N. The prototype has $L_p = 20$ m and is intended to operate at depth $h = 35$ m in seawater at 15 °C ($\nu_p = 1.189 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³). The wetted area scale ratio is $S_p / S_m = (L_p/L_m)^2 = 100$. Determine: **(a)** the prototype speed $V_p$ from Froude similarity, **(b)** the prototype frictional resistance using the ITTC 1978 line, and **(c)** the effective horsepower at the prototype.

**Solution:**

### Part (a) — Prototype Speed by Froude Similarity

Froude similarity demands $Fn_m = Fn_p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} &= \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g L_p}} \\[2pt]
V_p &= V_m \sqrt{\frac{L_p}{L_m}} = 2.5 \sqrt{\frac{20}{2.0}} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{10} \\[2pt]
V_p &= 2.5 \times 3.1623 = 7.906 \;\text{m/s} \quad (\approx 15.37 \text{ knots})
\end{aligned}$$

> Because the submersible is deeply submerged ($h/L_p = 35/20 = 1.75 > 1.5$), wave-making resistance is **negligible** at the prototype, and Froude scaling governs only the inertial-coupling term — which here is zero. **Strictly speaking**, for a deeply submerged body, **only Reynolds similarity** is required. We retain the Froude calculation because the KTU 2024 syllabus includes both.

### Part (b) — Prototype Frictional Resistance

**Step 1 — Reynolds number at model:**

$$Re_m = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{2.5 \times 2.0}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.98 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2 — Model friction coefficient (ITTC 1978):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{F,m} &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(4.98\times 10^{6}) - 2)^2} \\[2pt]
\log_{10}(4.98 \times 10^{6}) &= 6.697 \\[2pt]
C_{F,m} &= \frac{0.075}{(6.697 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(4.697)^2} = \frac{0.075}{22.06} = 3.40 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number at prototype:**

$$Re_p = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p} = \frac{7.906 \times 20}{1.189 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.330 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Prototype friction coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(1.330 \times 10^{8}) &= 8.124 \\[2pt]
C_{F,p} &= \frac{0.075}{(8.124 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.124)^2} = \frac{0.075}{37.50} = 2.00 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Prototype frictional resistance:**

$$R_{F,p} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_p\, S_p\, V_p^2\, C_{F,p}$$

The wetted area is $S_p = S_m (L_p/L_m)^2$. If model wetted area is $S_m = 3.6$ m² (typical for a 2 m submersible), then $S_p = 360$ m².

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{F,p} &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 360 \times (7.906)^2 \times 2.00 \times 10^{-3} \\[2pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 360 \times 62.50 \times 2.00 \times 10^{-3} \\[2pt]
&= 23{,}062.5 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 23.06 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (c) — Effective Horsepower

$$EHP = \frac{R_{F,p} \cdot V_p}{1000} = \frac{23{,}062.5 \times 7.906}{1000} = 182.3 \;\text{kW}$$

If appendage and roughness allowances add 15%:

$$EHP_{total} \approx 1.15 \times 182.3 = 209.6 \;\text{kW}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of Total Resistance for a Submersible

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resistance R sub T] --> B[Viscous Resistance]
    A --> C[Wave Making Resistance]
    A --> D[Appendage Resistance]
    A --> E[Air Resistance]

    B --> B1[Frictional R sub F]
    B --> B2[Form R sub VP]
    B --> B3[Roughness Allowance delta C sub F]

    B1 --> F1[ITTC 1978 Line]
    B2 --> F2[Form Factor 1 + k]
    B3 --> F3[Hull Condition]

    C --> G1{Negligible if depth h >= 1.5 L}
    G1 -->|YES| G2[Drop from equation]
    G1 -->|NO| G3[Apply Michell thin ship theory]

    D --> H1[Control Surfaces]
    D --> H2[Sail Fairings]
    D --> H3[Sonar Dome]

    E --> I1{Submerged}
    I1 -->|YES| I2[Set to ZERO]
    I1 -->|NO| I3[Add wind drag term]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Resistance Prediction Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Define Geometry L B T] --> P2[Compute Re and Fn]
    P2 --> P3{Operating Mode}
    P3 -->|Surface Ship| P4[Apply Holtrop Mennen or Series 64]
    P3 -->|Submerged Body| P5[Apply ITTC 1978 plus form factor]
    P4 --> P6[Sum R sub F R sub W R sub APP]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Compute EHP]
    P7 --> P8[Add propulsive eta for BHP]
    P8 --> P9[Final Powering Estimate]
```

## 4.3 Process Diagram: Froude-Reynolds Scaling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Model Test at Towing Tank] --> S2[Measure R sub T model]
    S2 --> S3[Compute C sub F model from ITTC 1978]
    S3 --> S4[Find C sub R model = C sub T minus C sub F model]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Froude Scaling to get C sub R prototype]
    S5 --> S6[Compute C sub F prototype from Re sub p]
    S6 --> S7[Total C sub T prototype = C sub F prototype + C sub R + delta C sub F]
    S7 --> S8[Multiply by half rho S V squared to get R sub T prototype]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block in the Mermaid charts represents a *computational step* in the resistance estimation procedure. The decision diamonds (e.g., `Submerged?`, `Depth threshold?`) implement the **branching logic** between surface-ship and submersible resistance formulations — a critical distinction the KTU examiner tests through the form-factor and wave-term sub-questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define frictional resistance. State the ITTC 1978 correlation formula and explain the meaning of the term "$-2$" in the denominator.**

**Model Answer:**

Frictional resistance $R_F$ is the tangential drag generated by viscous shear stresses acting within the **turbulent boundary layer** that envelops a body moving through a fluid. It is the largest single component of total resistance for slender, streamlined submersibles.

The **ITTC 1978** correlation is:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$

The constant **"$-2$"** is **not a physical parameter** — it is a numerical fitting offset chosen so that the formula reproduces the exact Prandtl–Schlichting value of $C_F \approx 0.004687$ at the canonical reference Reynolds number $Re = 10^6$. Without this offset, the formula would not align with experimental benchmark data at $10^6$.

**(3 marks)** ✅

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is wave-making resistance neglected in the design of a deeply submerged submersible? Justify with a scaling argument.**

**Model Answer:**

Wave-making resistance arises from the **radiation of surface gravity waves** generated by the pressure field of a moving body near the free surface. Michell's thin-ship theory shows that the wave-amplitude envelope decays **exponentially** with depth $h$:

$$\eta(x,h) \;\propto\; e^{-k_0 h}, \quad k_0 = g/V^2$$

For a deeply submerged submersible where $h \ge 1.5\,L$ (depth at least 1.5 times body length), the exponent $k_0 h$ is large, making $\eta \to 0$. Quantitatively, $R_W < 1\%$ of $R_T$ under this condition, so $R_W$ is **safely neglected** in preliminary resistance estimates. The total resistance then reduces to the **viscous** terms only.

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

A streamlined submersible of length $L = 30$ m and wetted surface area $S = 540$ m² operates at $V = 8.0$ m/s in seawater at 15 °C. Given: form factor $(1+k) = 1.15$, roughness allowance $\Delta C_F = 0.0004$, appendage drag $R_{APP} = 4.5$ kN. The vehicle cruises at depth $h = 50$ m.

#### (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the total resistance $R_T$ and effective horsepower (EHP). Use the ITTC 1978 friction line. Assume $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ and $\nu = 1.189 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{VL}{\nu} = \frac{8.0 \times 30}{1.189 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.018 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — ITTC 1978 friction coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(2.018 \times 10^{8}) &= 8.305 \\[2pt]
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(8.305 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.305)^2} = \frac{0.075}{39.75} = 1.887 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance coefficient** (deeply submerged, so no wave term):

$$C_T = (1+k)\,C_F + \Delta C_F + C_{APP}$$

where

$$C_{APP} = \frac{R_{APP}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho S V^2} = \frac{4500}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 540 \times 64} = 2.54 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_T &= 1.15 \times 1.887\times 10^{-3} + 0.0004 + 2.54 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 2.170 \times 10^{-3} + 0.400 \times 10^{-3} + 0.254 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 2.824 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total resistance:**

$$R_T = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 540 \times 64 \times 2.824 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}034 \;\text{N} \approx 50.03 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Effective horsepower:**

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{1000} = \frac{50{,}034 \times 8.0}{1000} = 400.3 \;\text{kW}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

#### (b) — 7 Marks
**If the operational depth were reduced to $h = 10$ m, qualitatively discuss how the resistance estimate would change. Identify the new dominant resistance component and recommend two hull-form modifications to reduce it.**

**Model Answer:**

At $h = 10$ m, the ratio $h/L = 10/30 = 0.33 < 1.5$. The body is now **near-surface**, and wave-making resistance **can no longer be neglected**. The free-surface proximity allows the pressure field to radiate significant gravity waves. Wave-making becomes a **new dominant component**, and the total resistance may increase by **30–80%** depending on $Fn$, $B/T$, and hull form.

Two hull-form modifications to reduce wave resistance:

1. **Increase length-to-diameter ratio $L/D$** (e.g., from 8 to 10) — a slimmer hull creates a smaller pressure peak at the bow, reducing the wave-source strength.
2. **Adopt a parabolic or ogival entrance** (replacing a blunt or wedge-shaped bow) — this spreads the pressure rise over a longer length, weakening the wave system (this is the basis of the **Havelock-form** minimum-wave-resistance hulls).

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

A 1:25 scale model of a submersible is tested in a towing tank. Model length $L_m = 0.8$ m, model wetted area $S_m = 1.6$ m², model speed $V_m = 1.5$ m/s, fresh water ($\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). Measured total resistance $R_{T,m} = 4.2$ N. Prototype operates in seawater at 15 °C ($\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu_p = 1.189 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s), $L_p = 20$ m.

#### (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the frictional resistance of the prototype using the ITTC 1978 correlation.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Froude scaling for prototype speed:**

$$V_p = V_m \sqrt{L_p / L_m} = 1.5 \sqrt{20/0.8} = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Model Reynolds number:**

$$Re_m = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.8}{1.0\times 10^{-6}} = 1.2 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Prototype Reynolds number:**

$$Re_p = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p} = \frac{7.5 \times 20}{1.189\times 10^{-6}} = 1.262 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Prototype friction coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(1.262 \times 10^{8}) &= 8.101 \\[2pt]
C_{F,p} &= \frac{0.075}{(8.101 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.101)^2} = \frac{0.075}{37.22} = 2.015 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Prototype wetted area** (geometric similarity):

$$S_p = S_m (L_p / L_m)^2 = 1.6 \times (25)^2 = 1.6 \times 625 = 1000 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Prototype frictional resistance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{F,p} &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1000 \times (7.5)^2 \times 2.015 \times 10^{-3} \\[2pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1000 \times 56.25 \times 2.015 \times 10^{-3} \\[2pt]
&= 58{,}083 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 58.08 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

#### (b) — 7 Marks
**If the form factor is $k = 0.12$ and the appendage resistance is 8% of the frictional resistance, compute the total resistance of the prototype. State any assumptions.**

**Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** Vehicle operates at sufficient depth ($h/L \ge 1.5$), so wave-making is neglected.

**Assumption 2:** Roughness allowance $\Delta C_F = 0.0004$ is included.

**Step 1 — Viscous (form-corrected frictional) coefficient:**

$$C_{visc} = (1+k) C_{F,p} = 1.12 \times 2.015 \times 10^{-3} = 2.257 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Appendage coefficient** (8% of $R_{F,p}$):

$$C_{APP} = 0.08 \times C_{F,p} = 0.08 \times 2.015 \times 10^{-3} = 1.612 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Total coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_T &= (1+k) C_{F,p} + \Delta C_F + C_{APP} \\
&= 2.257 \times 10^{-3} + 0.400 \times 10^{-3} + 0.161 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 2.818 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total resistance:**

$$R_T = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1000 \times 56.25 \times 2.818 \times 10^{-3} = 81{,}221 \;\text{N} \approx 81.22 \;\text{kN}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert** ⚠️
>
> 1. **The "$-2$" trap:** Many students write $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re)^2$ — this is **wrong** and will cost **0.5 mark instantly** in Part A and full credit in Part B derivations.
> 2. **Confusing the reference area:** For submersibles, use **wetted surface area $S$**. For aircraft-style aerodynamic drag, use frontal area. Mixing them up is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting Froude scaling for speed:** $V_p$ is **NOT** equal to $V_m (L_p/L_m)$. It is $V_p = V_m \sqrt{L_p/L_m}$ (square root, not linear). A common error costing **2 marks**.
> 4. **Unit slip:** Always convert $\nu$ to **m²/s** (not mm²/s or stokes) and $V$ to **m/s** (not knots) before computing $Re$. One inconsistent unit loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Wave term for submerged bodies:** Examiners explicitly check whether you *recognise* the depth threshold. If $h/L < 1.5$, you must include a discussion of $R_W$. Writing "negligible" without justification costs **1 mark**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Total resistance** $R_T$ for a marine vehicle = sum of viscous, wave, appendage, and air-drag components.
- [x] For a **submersible at depth $h \ge 1.5\,L$**, the dominant component is **viscous (frictional + form) resistance**; wave-making is **negligible**.
- [x] **ITTC 1978 line** is the universal friction-line formula: $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$ — valid for $10^6 \le Re \le 10^9$.
- [x] **Form factor** $(1+k)$ accounts for 3D pressure drag; $k \approx 0.1$–$0.2$ for streamlined bodies.
- [x] **Roughness allowance** $\Delta C_F \approx 0.0004$ is added for service-condition hulls.
- [x] **Reynolds similarity** governs viscous scaling; **Froude similarity** governs wave scaling.
- [x] **Froude speed scaling** for prototype: $V_p = V_m \sqrt{L_p / L_m}$.
- [x] **Wetted area scaling**: $S_p / S_m = (L_p / L_m)^2$ under geometric similarity.
- [x] **EHP (SI)** = $R_T \cdot V / 1000$ when $R_T$ is in kN and $V$ in m/s, giving kW.
- [x] **Seawater properties** (KTU standard): $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.189 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [x] **Michell's exponential decay** $\eta \propto e^{-k_0 h}$ is the physical reason submerged bodies have no wave drag.
- [x] **Appendage resistance** is typically 5–10% of bare-hull viscous resistance.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** explicitly in KTU 14-mark answers — depth regime, hull condition, similarity criterion used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appendage Resistance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Appendage Resistance

# 🔱 APPENDAGE RESISTANCE IN SUBMERSIBLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **APPENDAGE RESISTANCE (R_APP)** is defined as the additional hydrodynamic resistance offered to a submerged vehicle by all protruding fittings, projections, and auxiliary bodies attached externally to the bare hydrodynamic hull. It is a component of the **viscous resistance** family and arises due to the pressure drag and skin friction of these secondary bodies operating within the boundary layer and wake of the main hull.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with Hoerner's *Fluid-Dynamic Drag* (1965) and the U.S. Navy Submarine Design Manual, appendage resistance is mathematically expressed as:

$$
R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^{2} \cdot S_{APP} \cdot C_{D,APP}
$$

where the symbols carry their standard meanings, and the equation is integrated over the wetted surface area of each appendage. Appendages on a typical submersible include **rudders, diving planes, sail planes, bow planes, stern planes, sonar domes, propeller struts, shaft fairings, dive planes, stabilizer fins, towed-body brackets, hull-mounted masts, and capstan wells**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hand in the River"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are swimming in a river and you hold your hand flat, slicing the water — this is your "bare body." Now imagine another swimmer holds onto your leg as you swim. You immediately feel more drag. That extra drag is precisely what **appendage resistance** means to a submersible. The bare hull is the "swimmer," and the rudders, planes, sail, sonar dome, and struts are the "passengers" being dragged through the water, each creating its own little pocket of disturbance.

### 1.3 Classification of Appendages on a Submersible

Submersible appendages are broadly classified into **five functional categories** as per the KTU Module-3 syllabus:

| Sl. No. | Category | Examples | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Control Surfaces** | Rudders, diving planes, bow planes, stern planes, X-stern planes | Manoeuvring in 3-DOF (yaw, pitch, depth) |
| 2 | **Sail & Superstructure** | Conning tower, sail planes, fairwater, masts | Housing electronics, periscope, communication |
| 3 | **Propulsion Appendages** | Propeller struts, shaft brackets, Kort nozzles, shroud rings | Supporting shaft line and propeller |
| 4 | **Sensor Appendages** | Sonar domes, hydrophone blisters, towed-sonde booms, transducer pods | Acoustic sensing and reconnaissance |
| 5 | **Auxiliary Appendages** | Capstan wells, hull valves, limber holes, docking keels, cable fairings | Operational and structural support |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Sea water density (ρ):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard) or **1027 kg/m³** (deep ocean, 4 °C)
> - **Kinematic viscosity (ν):** **1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 10 °C
> - **Appendage resistance share (empirical):** typically **3 % to 12 %** of total bare-hull resistance
> - **Appendage drag coefficient (C_D,APP):** range **0.4 to 1.2** for typical sub appendages
> - **Interference factor (k_i):** **1.2 to 1.8** (amplification when appendage sits in hull wake)

### 1.5 Why Appendage Resistance Matters in Submersible Design

A modern deep-diving submersible may carry **20 to 40 individual appendages**. Although each is geometrically small relative to the hull, the cumulative effect is non-trivial. For an operational submersible cruising at 3–6 knots, the appendage contribution can amount to **3–8 % of total drag**, but at higher speeds (above 8 knots) and when appendages are placed inside the **viscous wake or turbulent boundary layer** of the hull, the interference factor can amplify the effective drag by **80–100 %**. This is why KTU Module-3 dedicates explicit treatment to it.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Body Problem — Hull + Appendage

A submersible in motion experiences a coupled hydrodynamic interaction between the **bare hull (body 1)** and the **appendages (body 2)**. The flow over the appendage is strongly influenced by the **boundary layer state** at the hull surface where the appendage is mounted. Three regimes are possible:

- **Regime A — Laminar boundary layer attachment:** Appendage sees clean inflow; drag is purely geometric.
- **Regime B — Turbulent boundary layer attachment:** Boundary layer has thickened; appendage experiences local velocity defect.
- **Regime C — Wake attachment:** Appendage sits directly in the separated wake; drag can be 2× to 3× the freestream value.

### 2.2 Component Decomposition of Appendage Resistance

Appendage resistance is decomposed into the standard viscous drag components:

$$
R_{APP} = R_{F,APP} + R_{P,APP} + R_{I,APP}
$$

- **R_F,APP** = Skin-friction (frictional) component — dominant for streamlined, fully-wetted appendages.
- **R_P,APP** = Pressure (form) component — dominant for bluff bodies such as sonar domes or exposed struts.
- **R_I,APP** = Interference (induced) component — accounts for the proximity and wake interaction with the hull.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA BANK** for Appendage Resistance. Master every row — at least one formula from this table is guaranteed in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Key Constants |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $R_{APP} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP} C_{D,APP}$ | Standard drag equation applied to appendage wetted area | $C_{D,APP}$ from Hoerner chart |
| 2 | $C_{D,APP} = C_{F,APP} \cdot (1 + k_{APP}) + C_{D,form}$ | Total drag coefficient = friction + form + interference | $k_{APP}$ = appendage form factor |
| 3 | $C_{F,APP} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^{2}}$ | ITTC-78 friction line for turbulent flow on appendage | $R_n = \dfrac{V L_{APP}}{\nu}$ |
| 4 | $R_{APP} = \eta \cdot R_{HULL}$ | Empirical shortcut — appendage drag as % of hull drag | $\eta$ = 0.03 to 0.12 |
| 5 | $C_{D,0} = C_{D,\infty} \left( 1 + \dfrac{0.4}{\left(R_n/10^{5}\right)^{0.43}} \right)$ | Hoerner's low-Reynolds correction for finite appendage | For $R_n < 10^{6}$ |
| 6 | $k_i = 1 + 0.6 \left(\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d}\right) + 0.2 \left(\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d}\right)^{2}$ | Interference factor when appendage sits in hull boundary layer | $\delta^{*}$ = displacement thickness |
| 7 | $L/D_{APP} = \dfrac{C_{L,APP}}{C_{D,APP}}$ | Lift-to-drag ratio (hydrodynamic efficiency) of control surface | Used in stability–drag trade-off |
| 8 | $C_{D,p} = 0.18 \cdot \dfrac{t}{c} + 0.60\cdot (C_L^{2}) / (\pi \lambda)$ | Pressure drag of finite-span lifting surface (lifting-line) | $t/c$ = thickness ratio, $\lambda$ = aspect ratio |
| 9 | $S_{APP,wet} = \sum_{i} 2 \cdot (s_i \cdot c_i)$ | Total wetted area of all appendages (sum over $i$ components) | $s$ = span, $c$ = chord |
| 10 | $R_{n,L} = \dfrac{V \cdot c_{mean}}{\nu}$ | Chord-based Reynolds number of a lifting surface | For plane / rudder |
| 11 | $R_{n,APP} = \dfrac{V \cdot L_{APP}}{\nu}$ | Length-based Reynolds number for body-of-revolution appendage | $L_{APP}$ = appendage length |
| 12 | $W_{APP} = \rho g V_{APP} \cdot k_{mat}$ | Weight penalty (for design trade-off) | $V_{APP}$ = displaced volume of appendage |

### 2.4 The 3-σ Design Rule of Thumb for Submersibles

> [!NOTE]
> As per Hoerner (1965) and the David Taylor Naval Ship R&D Center reports, a well-streamlined operational submersible typically carries an **appendage resistance quota of 5–7 %** of bare-hull resistance. A poorly designed one can exceed **15 %**. The KTU Module-3 numerical problems will often quote $\eta = 0.05$ to $\eta = 0.10$ as the working range.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the design office of an actual submersible (think *DSV Alvin*, *Pisces VI*, *DRDO Sindhu Sadhana*, or *NR-1*), the appendage resistance budget is a **hard constraint** that drives several design decisions:

- Whether to recess the sonar dome flush with the hull or accept a blister fairing.
- Placement of control surfaces in the **parallel mid-body** versus the stern (where the boundary layer is thicker but the wake is also more energetic).
- Material choice for sonar domes (rubber vs. composite) — affects both acoustic transparency and surface roughness, hence $C_{F,APP}$.
- Whether to use **end-plate fences** on stern planes to suppress tip-vortex induced drag.
- Selection of a free-flooding or sealed appendage (affects added mass and form factor).

### 2.6 The Interference Phenomenon — Why It Cannot Be Ignored

The bare-hull boundary layer has a typical **displacement thickness** of $\delta^{*} \approx 5$–$20$ mm for a mid-sized submersible at cruise. When an appendage of thickness $d$ is mounted on the hull:

- If $d \ll \delta^{*}$ → the appendage is **fully immersed in the BL** → interference maximum.
- If $d \gg \delta^{*}$ → the appendage **pierces the BL** → interference minimum.

This is why large sonar domes mounted on otherwise clean hulls are notoriously drag-inefficient, and why modern submersibles use **conformal domes** (flush-mounted) wherever possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Appendage Drag from the Standard Drag Equation

**Problem Statement:** Express the appendage resistance as a percentage of the bare-hull frictional resistance using dimensional reasoning.

**Starting point (dimensional analysis of drag):**

$$
[R] = [M L T^{-2}]
$$

Drag must depend on fluid density $\rho$, velocity $V$, and a characteristic area $S$. The Buckingham π-theorem gives:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S \cdot \phi(R_n, M, Fr)
$$

For a deeply submerged, low-speed submersible, the **Mach number M → 0** and **Froude number Fr → 0** (no free surface), so the functional collapses to a single non-dimensional coefficient:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S \cdot C_D(R_n)
$$

**Application to an appendage:**

$$
R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP} \cdot C_{D,APP}(R_{n,APP})
$$

This is **Equation (1)** — the working definition used throughout KTU Module-3 problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Hoerner's Low-Reynolds Correction for Appendages

Hoerner observed that small appendages (control surfaces, struts) often operate at $R_n$ in the range $10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$, where the standard ITTC friction line is not accurate. He proposed:

$$
C_{D,APP} = C_{D,\infty} \left[ 1 + \left(\dfrac{0.4}{(R_n/10^{5})^{0.43}}\right) \right]
$$

**Step-by-step demonstration** — at $R_n = 5 \times 10^{5}$ and $C_{D,\infty} = 0.65$ (typical of a streamlined strut):

$$
C_{D,APP} = 0.65 \left[ 1 + \left(\dfrac{0.4}{(5)^{0.43}}\right) \right]
$$

Computing the inner bracket:

$$
(5)^{0.43} = e^{0.43 \cdot \ln 5} = e^{0.43 \cdot 1.6094} = e^{0.6920} \approx 1.998
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.4}{1.998} \approx 0.2002
$$

$$
C_{D,APP} = 0.65 \times (1 + 0.2002) = 0.65 \times 1.2002 = 0.7801
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{D,APP} \approx 0.78}
$$

So at this moderate Reynolds number, the drag coefficient is **20 % higher** than the infinite-Reynolds value — a non-negligible correction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — The Interference Factor (Wake Multiplier)

**Conceptual setup:** When a cylindrical appendage of diameter $d$ sits inside the boundary layer of the hull, the effective velocity at the appendage's leading edge is reduced by the velocity defect of the BL, but the appendage is now operating in a **sheared, accelerated, often turbulent** environment.

Hoerner's interference model:

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.6 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d}\right) + 0.2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Worked example:** $\delta^{*} = 12$ mm, $d = 30$ mm.

$$
\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d} = \dfrac{12}{30} = 0.40
$$

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.6 \cdot 0.40 + 0.2 \cdot (0.40)^{2}
$$

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.24 + 0.2 \cdot 0.16
$$

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.24 + 0.032 = 1.272
$$

$$
\boxed{k_i = 1.272}
$$

This means the bare appendage drag is amplified by **27.2 %** purely due to boundary-layer interaction — a real engineering penalty.

**Interference-corrected appendage drag:**

$$
R_{APP}^{*} = k_i \cdot R_{APP} = 1.272 \cdot R_{APP}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Total Appendage Drag of a Submersible (Sum Over All Appendages)

For a submersible with $N$ appendages indexed $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$:

$$
R_{APP,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP,i} \cdot C_{D,APP,i} \cdot k_{i,i} \right]
$$

**Worked numerical example:**

A research submersible operates at **V = 4 m/s** in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). It has four appendages:

| Appendage | $S_{APP}$ (m²) | $C_{D,APP}$ | $k_i$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Stern planes (pair) | 0.60 | 0.42 | 1.10 |
| Sail plane | 0.45 | 0.55 | 1.25 |
| Sonar dome | 0.80 | 0.85 | 1.35 |
| Propeller struts (pair) | 0.25 | 0.50 | 1.20 |

**Step 1 — Compute the dynamic pressure head:**

$$
q = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (4)^{2}
$$

$$
q = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16 = 8200 \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute appendage-by-appendage drag (with interference):**

- Stern planes: $R_1 = 8200 \times 0.60 \times 0.42 \times 1.10 = 2273$ N
- Sail plane: $R_2 = 8200 \times 0.45 \times 0.55 \times 1.25 = 2537$ N
- Sonar dome: $R_3 = 8200 \times 0.80 \times 0.85 \times 1.35 = 7528$ N
- Prop struts: $R_4 = 8200 \times 0.25 \times 0.50 \times 1.20 = 1230$ N

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$
R_{APP,total} = 2273 + 2537 + 7528 + 1230 = 13{,}568 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{APP,total} \approx 13.57 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a percentage of bare-hull drag (assume $R_{HULL} = 200$ kN):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{13{,}568}{200{,}000} \times 100\% = 6.78\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 6.78\%}
$$

This is well within the **5–7 % design budget** — the appendage design is *acceptable* but the **sonar dome is the dominant contributor (≈ 55 % of appendage drag)**. A design review should target the dome.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Appendage Resistance Estimation

| Step | Question | If YES | If NO |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Is the appendage slender (streamlined)? | Use $C_{D,APP} = C_{F,APP}(1 + k_{form})$ | Use bluff-body $C_D$ from chart |
| 2 | Does it sit inside the hull boundary layer? | Apply interference factor $k_i > 1$ | Use freestream $C_D$ |
| 3 | Is $R_{n,APP} < 10^{6}$? | Apply Hoerner's low-$R_n$ correction | Use ITTC-78 friction line |
| 4 | Are there multiple appendages in proximity? | Use mutual-interference lumped factor | Treat as independent |
| 5 | Is total $R_{APP,total} / R_{HULL} < 7\,\%$? | Design is *within budget* | Re-design appendages |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Submersible Appendage Inventory — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional classification of appendages** on a typical deep-diving submersible and their inter-relationship with hull resistance components. This is the Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE for topics involving complex physical systems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Submersible Total Resistance R_T] --> B[Bare Hull Resistance R_HULL]
    A --> C[Appendage Resistance R_APP]
    A --> D[Wave Resistance R_W]
    A --> E[Interference Drag R_INT]

    B --> B1[Frictional R_F]
    B --> B2[Pressure R_P]
    B --> B3[Form Factor K]

    C --> C1[Control Surfaces]
    C --> C2[Sail and Conning Tower]
    C --> C3[Sonar Domes and Sensors]
    C --> C4[Propulsion Appendages]
    C --> C5[Auxiliary Fittings]

    C1 --> C1A[Rudders]
    C1 --> C1B[Diving Planes]
    C1 --> C1C[Stern Planes]
    C1 --> C1D[Bow Planes]

    C2 --> C2A[Sail Body]
    C2 --> C2B[Fairwater]
    C2 --> C2C[Mast Housings]

    C3 --> C3A[Sonar Dome Blister]
    C3 --> C3B[Hydrophone Pods]
    C3 --> C3C[Towed Body Bracket]

    C4 --> C4A[Propeller Struts]
    C4 --> C4B[Shaft Brackets]
    C4 --> C4C[Nozzle Shroud]

    C5 --> C5A[Capstan Wells]
    C5 --> C5B[Docking Keels]
    C5 --> C5C[Cable Fairings]

    C --> F[Interference Factor k_i]
    F --> F1[BL Immersion Check]
    F --> F2[Wake Proximity Check]
    F --> F3[Mutual Interference Check]

    F1 --> G[Corrected R_APP]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Appendage Drag Budget: 3 to 12 percent of R_HULL]
```

### 4.2 Resistance Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    RT[Total Resistance R_T] --> A1[Pressure Resistance R_P]
    RT --> A2[Viscous Resistance R_V]
    RT --> A3[Wave Resistance R_W]
    RT --> A4[Appendage Resistance R_APP]
    RT --> A5[Interference R_INT]

    A2 --> A2A[Frictional R_F]
    A2 --> A2B[Form Drag Component]

    A4 --> A4A[Skin Friction on Appendage]
    A4 --> A4B[Form Drag on Appendage]
    A4 --> A4C[Interference Multiplier k_i]

    A4A --> Z[Sum Across All Appendages]
    A4B --> Z
    A4C --> Z
    Z --> A4D[Total R_APP]

    A4D --> RES[Combined with R_HULL gives R_T]
```

### 4.3 Appendage Resistance Calculation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Inventory all appendages] --> S2[Step 2: Measure wetted area S_APP_i]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Reynolds number R_n,APP_i]
    S3 --> S4{Is R_n less than 10^6?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Apply Hoerner low-R_n correction]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Use ITTC-78 friction line]
    S5 --> S7[Step 4: Determine drag coefficient C_D,APP_i]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 5: Check BL immersion depth ratio delta over d]
    S8 --> S9{Is appendage inside hull BL?}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Compute interference factor k_i]
    S9 -->|No| S11[Set k_i equals 1]
    S10 --> S12[Step 6: Compute R_APP for each appendage]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Step 7: Sum over all appendages]
    S13 --> S14[Step 8: Compute ratio eta percent of R_HULL]
    S14 --> S15{Is eta within 3 to 12 percent?}
    S15 -->|Yes| S16[Design ACCEPTED]
    S15 -->|No| S17[Re-design appendages]
```

### 4.4 Appendage Geometry Reference (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Rudder: Rectangular or Cruciform] --> P1A[Aspect Ratio lambda equals 1.2 to 2.0]
    P2[Diving Plane: Symmetric NACA 0015 Profile] --> P2A[Thickness Ratio t over c equals 0.12 to 0.18]
    P3[Sail: Teardrop Body of Revolution] --> P3A[Length to Beam Ratio equals 4 to 6]
    P4[Sonar Dome: Hemispherical or Conformal] --> P4A[Radius equals 0.3 to 0.5 m typical]
    P5[Strut: Elliptical Cross Section] --> P5A[Thickness equals 12 to 18 percent chord]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1. Define appendage resistance in the context of a submersible. Mention any two appendages commonly found on a deep-diving submersible.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Appendage resistance is the additional hydrodynamic resistance experienced by a submersible due to the fittings, projections, and auxiliary bodies attached externally to the bare hull, when the vehicle moves through water. It arises from the skin friction and pressure drag of these secondary bodies, modified by interference effects with the hull's boundary layer. (2 marks)*
>
> *Examples: (i) Control surfaces such as rudders and diving planes, and (ii) the sail (conning tower) housing periscopes and masts. Sonar domes and propeller struts are other examples. (1 mark)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q2. State the standard drag equation applied to an appendage and explain the role of the drag coefficient $C_{D,APP}$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *The standard drag equation for an appendage is* $R_{APP} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP} C_{D,APP}$ *(1 mark),* *where* $\rho$ *is fluid density,* $V$ *is the speed of the submersible,* $S_{APP}$ *is the wetted surface area of the appendage, and* $C_{D,APP}$ *is the appendage drag coefficient. (1 mark)*
>
> *The drag coefficient* $C_{D,APP}$ *encapsulates the geometry, Reynolds number, surface roughness, and orientation of the appendage, and is determined from experiments, Hoerner's charts, or empirical correlations such as the ITTC-78 friction line augmented by a form factor. (1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE — Module 3 Internal Choice, 14-Mark Pattern]**

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive, starting from the standard drag equation, the working formula for **appendage resistance** of a submersible. Explain each term and state the typical range of the drag coefficient for streamlined and bluff appendages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submersible of length 18 m and hull diameter 3 m operates at a cruising speed of 4.5 m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The bare-hull frictional resistance is estimated as $R_{HULL} = 145$ kN. The submersible has the following appendages:

| Appendage | $S_{APP}$ (m²) | $C_{D,APP}$ (infinite $R_n$) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Stern planes (pair) | 0.75 | 0.40 |
| Sail plane | 0.50 | 0.55 |
| Sonar dome (blister) | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| Rudder | 0.35 | 0.45 |
| Propeller struts (pair) | 0.30 | 0.50 |

The boundary layer displacement thickness at the sonar dome location is $\delta^{*} = 15$ mm; for all other appendages, assume $k_i = 1.05$. Compute:

- (i) the corrected $C_{D,APP}$ for the sonar dome using Hoerner's low-$R_n$ correction, with $R_{n,APP} = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$ for the dome. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) the interference factor for the sonar dome using $d_{dome} = 0.40$ m. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) the total appendage resistance $R_{APP,total}$ in kN. **(2 marks)**
- (iv) the appendage resistance as a percentage of $R_{HULL}$. **(1 mark)**

**(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question A):**

**(a) Derivation of the appendage resistance formula (7 marks):**

The drag force on any body immersed in a fluid stream is fundamentally a function of the fluid inertia and the body's geometric and Reynolds-number characteristics. Dimensional analysis (Buckingham π-theorem) gives:

$$
R = f(\rho, V, S, \mu, L)
$$

Yielding two non-dimensional groups — a Reynolds number and a drag coefficient:

$$
C_D = \dfrac{R}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S}
$$

For a deeply submerged, low-speed submersible, compressibility and free-surface effects vanish (M → 0, Fr → 0), so the drag depends only on the Reynolds number. For an appendage:

$$
R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP} C_{D,APP}(R_{n,APP})
$$

Each term is defined:
- $\rho$ = sea-water density, **1025 kg/m³** (1 mark)
- $V$ = free-stream velocity (m/s) (1 mark)
- $S_{APP}$ = wetted surface area of the appendage (m²) (1 mark)
- $C_{D,APP}$ = drag coefficient; typically **0.4–0.6** for streamlined, **0.8–1.2** for bluff bodies (1 mark)

Accounting for interference and Reynolds-number effects:

$$
C_{D,APP}^{eff} = C_{D,APP}^{\infty} \left[ 1 + \left(\dfrac{0.4}{(R_n/10^{5})^{0.43}}\right) \right] \cdot k_i
$$

(2 marks for form factor and interference integration)

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Hoerner low-Reynolds correction for sonar dome (2 marks):**

$$
C_{D,APP} = 0.80 \left[ 1 + \left(\dfrac{0.4}{(2.5)^{0.43}}\right) \right]
$$

$$
(2.5)^{0.43} = e^{0.43 \cdot \ln 2.5} = e^{0.43 \cdot 0.9163} = e^{0.394} \approx 1.483
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.4}{1.483} = 0.2697
$$

$$
C_{D,APP} = 0.80 \times 1.2697 = 1.0158
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{D,APP} \approx 1.016 \text{ (dome)}}
$$

**(ii) Interference factor for sonar dome (2 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d} = \dfrac{0.015}{0.40} = 0.0375
$$

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.6 \times 0.0375 + 0.2 \times (0.0375)^{2}
$$

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.0225 + 0.000281 = 1.0228
$$

$$
\boxed{k_i \approx 1.023}
$$

**(iii) Total appendage resistance (2 marks):**

Dynamic pressure:

$$
q = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (4.5)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 20.25 = 10{,}378 \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

Appendage-wise:

- Stern planes: $R_1 = 10{,}378 \times 0.75 \times 0.40 \times 1.05 = 3269$ N
- Sail plane: $R_2 = 10{,}378 \times 0.50 \times 0.55 \times 1.05 = 2996$ N
- Sonar dome: $R_3 = 10{,}378 \times 1.00 \times 1.016 \times 1.023 = 10{,}790$ N
- Rudder: $R_4 = 10{,}378 \times 0.35 \times 0.45 \times 1.05 = 1716$ N
- Prop struts: $R_5 = 10{,}378 \times 0.30 \times 0.50 \times 1.05 = 1635$ N

Sum:

$$
R_{APP,total} = 3269 + 2996 + 10{,}790 + 1716 + 1635 = 20{,}406 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{APP,total} \approx 20.4 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(iv) Percentage of hull resistance (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{20.4}{145} \times 100\% = 14.07\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 14.07\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> 1. *Forgetting to apply Hoerner's correction when $R_n < 10^6$* — costs **2 marks** in part (b)(i).
> 2. *Using $k_i = 1$ for all appendages instead of computing it for the dome* — costs **2 marks** in part (b)(ii).
> 3. *Expressing percentage as a decimal (0.14) instead of percent (14.07 %)* — costs **1 mark** in part (b)(iv).
> 4. *Mixing up length-based and chord-based Reynolds numbers* — costs **2 marks** if the wrong characteristic length is used.
> 5. *Ignoring unit conversion (N → kN)* — partial loss of **1 mark** at the final answer.
> 6. *Forgetting to multiply by the pair count for stern planes, rudder, and struts* — costs **1–2 marks**.

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the **interference effect** in appendage resistance? Derive the Hoerner interference factor formula and explain the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small research submersible is fitted with two identical stern planes, each of wetted area 0.30 m², drag coefficient (corrected) 0.50, and a single propeller strut of wetted area 0.20 m² with corrected $C_D = 0.65$. The submersible moves at 3.5 m/s in seawater. Estimate:

- (i) the dynamic pressure on each appendage. **(1 mark)**
- (ii) the drag on a single stern plane. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) the total drag from the three appendages. **(2 marks)**
- (iv) the appendage drag as a percentage of the total resistance, given $R_{TOTAL} = 95$ kN. **(2 marks)**

**(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question B):**

**(a) Interference effect and Hoerner formula (7 marks):**

The interference effect refers to the modification of the drag of an appendage caused by its proximity to the main hull. The appendage operates inside the velocity gradient of the hull's boundary layer, which is non-uniform, often turbulent, and partially separated near the stern. This causes:

- **Local velocity defect** (lower $V_{local}$ reduces friction, but the local shear is amplified)
- **Turbulence amplification** (higher Reynolds stresses increase form drag)
- **Cross-flow contamination** (streamline curvature from the hull is imposed on the appendage)

Hoerner's empirical interference factor is (3 marks for derivation):

$$
k_i = 1 + 0.6 \left(\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d}\right) + 0.2 \left(\dfrac{\delta^{*}}{d}\right)^{2}
$$

Term-by-term physical meaning:
- The **unity term (1)** represents the bare-appendage drag in freestream.
- The **linear term** $0.6 \cdot (\delta^{*}/d)$ captures the velocity gradient in the BL.
- The **quadratic term** $0.2 \cdot (\delta^{*}/d)^{2}$ captures the nonlinear amplification when the appendage is deeply immersed.

(2 marks for term explanation; 2 marks for physical interpretation)

The interference-corrected appendage drag is:

$$
R_{APP}^{*} = k_i \cdot R_{APP} = k_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP} C_{D,APP}\right)
$$

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Dynamic pressure (1 mark):**

$$
q = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (3.5)^{2}
$$

$$
q = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 12.25 = 6278.125 \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{q \approx 6278 \text{ N/m}^{2}}
$$

**(ii) Drag on a single stern plane (2 marks):**

$$
R_{plane} = q \cdot S \cdot C_D = 6278 \times 0.30 \times 0.50
$$

$$
R_{plane} = 941.7 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{plane} \approx 942 \text{ N}}
$$

**(iii) Total appendage drag (2 marks):**

Two stern planes:

$$
R_{2,planes} = 2 \times 942 = 1884 \text{ N}
$$

Single strut:

$$
R_{strut} = 6278 \times 0.20 \times 0.65 = 816.1 \text{ N}
$$

Total:

$$
R_{APP,total} = 1884 + 816 = 2700 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{APP,total} \approx 2.70 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(iv) Percentage of total resistance (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{2700}{95{,}000} \times 100\% = 2.84\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 2.84\%}
$$

The appendage drag is well within the **3–12 % design budget** (slightly below) — the appendage package is *aerodynamically efficient*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — APPENDAGE RESISTANCE**

- ✅ **Appendage resistance** is the additional viscous+pressure drag from external fittings on the hull (rudders, planes, sail, sonar, struts).
- ✅ Standard equation: $R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} S_{APP} C_{D,APP}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ **Typical range** of $R_{APP}$: **3 % to 12 %** of $R_{HULL}$ — design target ≈ 5–7 %.
- ✅ **Streamlined** appendages: $C_D$ = **0.4–0.6**; **bluff** appendages: $C_D$ = **0.8–1.2**.
- ✅ **Hoerner's low-$R_n$ correction** applies when $R_n < 10^{6}$ — formula uses $(R_n/10^{5})^{0.43}$.
- ✅ **Interference factor** $k_i$ = Hoerner's BL-immersion formula — increases with $\delta^{*}/d$.
- ✅ **Sonar domes** are typically the **largest single contributor** to appendage drag.
- ✅ **Conformal (flush-mounted) domes** drastically reduce appendage drag — preferred in modern design.
- ✅ **Sum appendage drag** over all $N$ appendages, applying the per-appendage $C_D$ and $k_i$.
- ✅ **ITTC-78** friction line is used for $R_n > 10^{6}$: $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$.
- ✅ **Froude and Mach numbers are zero** for submerged operation — collapse drag to $C_D(R_n)$ only.
- ✅ **Stern planes and rudders** are control surfaces — design trade-off is L/D ratio vs. drag.
- ✅ **Boundary layer state** (laminar, turbulent, separated) at appendage root decides interference severity.
- ✅ **Appendage wetted area** is computed as $2 s c$ per lifting surface (both sides wetted).
- ✅ **Design budget check**: $R_{APP,total}/R_{HULL}$ must lie in **0.03–0.12** for KTU Module-3 acceptance.
- ✅ **End-plate fences** on stern planes reduce tip-vortex drag — a recognized KTU design point.
- ✅ **Sea water density** at 4 °C deep ocean: **1027 kg/m³** (vs. 1025 at surface).
- ✅ **Kinematic viscosity** of seawater at 10 °C: **1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** — use this in $R_n$ computations.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Appendage Resistance**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Ocean Engineering, Course OENST723.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Defining the Six Degrees of Freedom Context

Before isolating sway and heave, it is essential to recognize that a fully submerged vehicle (submersible) possesses **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** in the fluid medium. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these into **3 Translational (Surge, Sway, Heave)** and **3 Rotational (Roll, Pitch, Yaw)** motions, all referenced to a standard body-fixed coordinate system with origin typically at the midship center of gravity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sway** and **Heave** are the two translational motions of a submersible perpendicular to its principal longitudinal axis of motion (Surge direction). They govern the **lateral** and **vertical** maneuvering/stability characteristics, respectively.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions Aligned to KTU Syllabus

### Sway (Lateral Translational Motion)
**Sway** is the sidewise (lateral) translational motion of a submersible along the **Y-axis** of the body-fixed coordinate frame, perpendicular to the longitudinal centerline. The hydrodynamic force resisting this motion is termed **Sway Resistance (F_Y)** or **Lateral Resistance**.

### Heave (Vertical Translational Motion)
**Heave** is the vertical translational motion of a submersible along the **Z-axis** (positive downward per naval architecture convention, or positive upward per aeronautical convention). The hydrodynamic force opposing this motion is termed **Heave Resistance (F_Z)** or **Vertical Resistance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The resistance to sway and heave is NOT primarily due to forward-speed pressure drag (as in surge), but is overwhelmingly governed by **added mass effects**, **cross-flow drag (Hoerner's principle)**, and **viscous damping forces** generated by the lateral/vertical velocity components through the surrounding water mass.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy for Instant Clarity

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The Underwater Broomstick:**
> 
> Imagine pushing a long, smooth broomstick vertically down into a full bucket of water. You will feel a sudden "kick" of resistance the moment it starts moving sideways or up-down. This is NOT friction on the surface (the broom is smooth!) — it is the water's own inertia that you must overcome. The water is being *displaced sideways* and *accelerated out of the way*. This extra inertia is the **Added Mass**, and the turbulent pushing-aside of water is the **Cross-Flow Drag**. Together, they constitute sway/heave resistance.
> 
> Now imagine doing the same with a *squat, flat disc*. The resistance is enormous! Why? Because the disc sweeps a large projected area perpendicular to motion. This is the **Projected Area (A_p)** effect.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Submersible) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea Water Density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **1.0 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s |
| Acceleration due to Gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Standard Drag Coefficient (submerged body) | $C_{D\infty}$ | **0.6 – 1.2** | dimensionless |
| Added Mass Coefficient (typical slender body) | $C_A$ | **0.5 – 1.0** | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Components of Sway/Heave Resistance

For a submersible undergoing sway ($v$) or heave ($w$) motion at instantaneous velocities, the total hydrodynamic reaction force decomposes into **two physically distinct contributions**:

### (A) Inertia (Added Mass) Force — $F_I$
This is the force required to **accelerate the surrounding water mass** along with the body. Since the body is accelerating water, by Newton's Third Law, the water exerts an equal and opposite reaction force on the body. The coefficient is called the **Added Mass Coefficient ($C_A$)** or **Inertia Coefficient ($C_M$)**.

$$F_I = \rho \, C_A \, \nabla \, \dot{v} \quad \text{(sway)}$$

$$F_I = \rho \, C_A \, \nabla \, \dot{w} \quad \text{(heave)}$$

where $\nabla$ = displaced volume of the submersible, $\dot{v}$ and $\dot{w}$ are the sway/heave accelerations.

### (B) Damping (Cross-Flow Drag) Force — $F_D$
This is the force resisting **steady-state (constant velocity)** lateral/vertical motion. It arises because the body sweeps water sideways like a 2D cylinder, shedding vortices and creating low-pressure wake regions. Per **Hoerner's Cross-Flow Drag Principle**, each infinitesimal strip of the hull behaves as a 2D cross-section moving through the cross-stream.

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{DC}(x) \, dA(x) \, v^2(x) \quad \text{(sway, strip-wise)}$$

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{DC}(x) \, dA(x) \, w^2(x) \quad \text{(heave, strip-wise)}$$

## 2.2 Hoerner's Cross-Flow Drag Principle (The Heart of the Module)

A slender submersible hull (Length/Beam ratio > 6) is divided into **N thin strips** of length $dx$, each with its local cross-sectional area $A(x)$ and cross-section diameter $D(x)$. Each strip acts **independently** as a 2D cylinder in cross-flow. The local cross-flow drag coefficient is:

$$C_{DC}(x) = C_{D\infty} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\text{Re}(x)}{1000}\right) \right] + k \, C_{D\infty} \frac{D(x)}{L}$$

where:
- $C_{D\infty}$ = 2D cylinder drag coefficient at infinite Reynolds number
- $\text{Re}(x) = \frac{V(x) \, D(x)}{\nu}$ = local cross-flow Reynolds number
- $k$ = 3D end-effect correction factor (typically 0.0 to 0.3)

## 2.3 Strip Theory Integration (From Local to Global)

The total sway or heave resistance is obtained by integrating the strip-wise forces along the hull length $L$:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{DC}(x) \, A(x) \, V^2(x) \, dx}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Important:** The total cross-flow force is **NOT** $C_D \times \frac{1}{2}\rho A_p V^2$ with a single drag coefficient. That simplistic form is only valid for bluff bodies of revolution. For slender submersibles, the **strip integration is mandatory** because the cross-section profile $D(x)$ varies along the length (e.g., bow taper, parallel mid-body, stern taper).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_I = \rho \, C_A \, \nabla \, a$ | Inertia (Added Mass) Force | N | Sway/Heave |
| 2 | $C_A = \frac{m_A}{\rho \nabla}$ | Definition of Added Mass Coefficient | — | Sway/Heave |
| 3 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{DC} A V^2$ | Cross-Flow Drag (local strip) | N | Sway/Heave |
| 4 | $C_{DC} = C_{D\infty}\left[1 - e^{-\text{Re}/1000}\right]$ | Hoerner's local drag correlation | — | Sway/Heave |
| 5 | $F_{total} = \int_0^L \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{DC}(x) A(x) V^2 dx$ | Strip theory global resistance | N | Sway/Heave |
| 6 | $\text{Re}_x = \frac{V(x) D(x)}{\nu}$ | Local cross-flow Reynolds number | — | Sway/Heave |
| 7 | $A_p = \int_0^L D(x) \, dx$ | Total projected area | m² | Sway/Heave |
| 8 | $C_{A,\text{ellipsoid}} = \alpha$ (per axis) | Analytical added mass for ellipsoids | — | Heave |
| 9 | $C_{A,\text{sphere}} = 0.5$ | Sphere added mass (all axes) | — | Sway/Heave |
| 10 | $C_{A,\text{prolate}} \approx \frac{\alpha_0}{2 - \alpha_0}$ | Slender prolate body inertia coeff. | — | Sway |

## 2.5 Why This Knowledge Matters in Real Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Maneuvering Simulation:** Submersibles like DSV *Alvin* or nuclear SSNs use sway/heave coefficients in 6-DOF simulators to predict turning circles and depth-change responses.
> - **Control Surface Design:** Stern planes (heave control) and bow planes need sufficient authority to overcome the *heave-added-mass* inrush during rapid depth changes.
> - **Station-Keeping:** ROVs hovering near seabed installations must counter sway drift caused by underwater currents; the cross-flow drag determines thruster power required.
> - **Launch & Recovery:** Knowing heave resistance is critical for safe docking of submersibles in rough surface conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Added Mass Coefficient for an Ellipsoid (Used for Sway & Heave)

A **prolate spheroid** is the standard analytical shape used to model a submersible hull mathematically. We derive the heave (and sway, for the transverse axis) added mass coefficient.

**Starting Point — Potential Flow Past an Ellipsoid:**
An ellipsoid with semi-axes $a$ (longitudinal), $b$ (transverse), $c$ (vertical) translating with velocity $U$ in the $i$-th direction. The velocity potential is given by classical potential theory.

The kinetic energy of the fluid in irrotational flow around the ellipsoid is:

$$T_{\text{fluid}} = \frac{1}{2} m_i U^2$$

where the **virtual (added) mass** $m_i$ in the $i$-th direction is given by the **ellipsoidal inertia coefficient**:

$$m_i = \rho \, \nabla \, \alpha_i$$

The geometric coefficient $\alpha_i$ for an ellipsoid depends on eccentricity $e$. For motion along the $a$-axis (surge), $e = \sqrt{1 - b^2/a^2}$:

$$\alpha_a = \frac{(1 - e^2)}{e^3} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right) - e \right]$$

For the transverse axes ($b$ and $c$), the inertia coefficient satisfies:

$$\alpha_a + 2\alpha_b = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha_b = \frac{1 - \alpha_a}{2}$$

**Step-by-Step Transition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_b &= \rho \, \nabla \, \alpha_b \\
&= \rho \, \nabla \, \left(\frac{1 - \alpha_a}{2}\right) \\
&= \frac{\rho \, \nabla}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{(1-e^2)}{e^3}\left[\frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right) - e\right] \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **added mass coefficient** is then defined as:

$$C_A = \frac{m_b}{\rho \, \nabla} = \frac{1 - \alpha_a}{2}$$

**Sanity Check (Sphere):** For a sphere, $a = b = c$, so $e = 0$. Expanding the logarithm for small $e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_a &\approx 1 - \frac{e^2}{5} - \frac{e^4}{15} - \ldots \\
\Rightarrow C_A &= \frac{1 - (1 - e^2/5)}{2} = \frac{e^2}{10} \to 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — for a sphere, the standard result is $C_A = 0.5$ for *each* transverse axis. The correct limit (without truncation) yields:

$$\boxed{C_{A,\text{sphere}} = 0.5 \quad \text{(all translational axes)}}$$

This is a foundational result used in **KTP (Kutta-Joukowski-type) potential flow textbooks** and forms the basis for slender-body theories.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Standard Format)

> **PROBLEM (KTU 2024 Style):** A small submersible has a prolate ellipsoidal hull with semi-axes $a = 6$ m, $b = c = 1.2$ m. It is undergoing **sway motion** at instantaneous velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s with acceleration $\dot{v} = 0.5$ m/s². The hull is approximated as moving in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Compute the **total sway hydrodynamic force**.

### Given Data
- $a = 6$ m, $b = 1.2$ m
- $v = 1.5$ m/s, $\dot{v} = 0.5$ m/s²
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³

### Step 1: Compute the Eccentricity
$$e = \sqrt{1 - \frac{b^2}{a^2}} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{1.2}{6}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.04} = \sqrt{0.96} \approx 0.9798$$

### Step 2: Compute $\alpha_a$ (Longitudinal Inertia Coefficient)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_a &= \frac{(1 - e^2)}{e^3} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right) - e \right] \\
&= \frac{0.04}{0.9411} \left[ \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1.9798}{0.0202}\right) - 0.9798 \right] \\
&= 0.04250 \left[ \frac{1}{2} \ln(98.01) - 0.9798 \right] \\
&= 0.04250 \left[ \frac{1}{2}(4.585) - 0.9798 \right] \\
&= 0.04250 \left[ 2.2925 - 0.9798 \right] \\
&= 0.04250 \times 1.3127 \\
&\approx 0.0558
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Transverse Inertia Coefficient
$$\alpha_b = \frac{1 - \alpha_a}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.0558}{2} = \frac{0.9442}{2} = 0.4721$$

### Step 4: Compute the Displaced Volume
$$\nabla = \frac{4}{3} \pi a b^2 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (6)(1.2)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (8.64) \approx 36.19 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5: Compute the Added Mass Force (Inertia Component)
$$F_I = \rho \, C_A \, \nabla \, \dot{v} = 1025 \times 0.4721 \times 36.19 \times 0.5$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_I &= 1025 \times 0.4721 \times 36.19 \times 0.5 \\
&= 1025 \times 0.4721 \times 18.095 \\
&= 1025 \times 8.544 \\
&\approx 8757.6 \text{ N} \quad \approx \mathbf{8.76 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Cross-Flow Drag (Damping Component)
Assume $C_{D\infty} = 1.0$ (typical for ellipsoidal cross-section). The average projected area in the sway direction for an ellipsoid is:
$$A_p = \pi a b \quad \text{(projected area in transverse plane)}$$

$$A_p = \pi \times 6 \times 1.2 = 22.62 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_p v^2 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.0 \times 22.62 \times (1.5)^2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 22.62 \times 2.25 \\
&= 512.5 \times 50.895 \\
&\approx 26{,}083.7 \text{ N} \quad \approx \mathbf{26.08 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Total Sway Force
$$F_{\text{total}} = F_I + F_D = 8.76 + 26.08 \approx \mathbf{34.84 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The damping (cross-flow drag) component **dominates** the inertia component for sustained sway motion. This is the standard design lesson in submersible maneuvering: **steady-state sway motion is drag-limited, not inertia-limited.**

## 3.3 Heave Resistance — Direct Analogous Derivation

For a body heaving vertically with $w(t)$, the *heave added mass coefficient* for a prolate spheroid (since the submersible is symmetric about horizontal mid-plane) is **identical** to the transverse added mass coefficient:

$$C_{A,\text{heave}} = C_{A,\text{sway}} = \alpha_b \quad \text{(for axisymmetric hulls)}$$

The heave damping force uses the **deck/bottom projected area**, not the side projected area:

$$F_{D,\text{heave}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_{DC,\text{heave}} A_{p,\text{deck}} w^2$$

where $A_{p,\text{deck}}$ is the *plan-view* area of the deck (top) or bottom of the hull — whichever is being moved *towards*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the projected area used in sway vs. heave. Sway uses the **side (transverse) projection**; heave uses the **top (plan) projection**. For a slender prolate body, the side projection is much larger than the deck projection, so **sway damping is greater than heave damping** for the same body.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sway & Heave Resistance Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submersible 6-DOF Motion] --> B[Translational Modes]
    A --> C[Rotational Modes]

    B --> D[Surge: along X-axis]
    B --> E[Sway: along Y-axis]
    B --> F[Heave: along Z-axis]

    E --> G[Sway Resistance F_Y]
    F --> H[Heave Resistance F_Z]

    G --> I[Inertia Component F_I_sway]
    G --> J[Damping Component F_D_sway]

    H --> K[Inertia Component F_I_heave]
    H --> L[Damping Component F_D_heave]

    I --> M[rho x C_A x nabla x a_y]
    J --> N[0.5 rho C_DC A_side v^2]
    K --> O[rho x C_A x nabla x a_z]
    L --> P[0.5 rho C_DC A_deck w^2]

    M --> Q[Strip Theory Integration along L]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q

    Q --> R[Total Sway and Heave Forces]
    R --> S[Input to Maneuvering Simulator and Control Design]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Strip Theory Logic Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hull Geometry Definition] --> P2[Discretize into N Strips]
    P2 --> P3[For each strip i at position x_i]
    P3 --> P4[Compute local diameter D of x i]
    P3 --> P5[Compute local projected area dA of x i]
    P3 --> P6[Compute local Reynolds number Re of x i]
    P4 --> P7[Evaluate C_DC at x i using Hoerner]
    P5 --> P8[Evaluate local drag force dF]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Numerical Integration over 0 to L]
    P9 --> P10[Total Cross Flow Resistance]
    P10 --> P11[Add Inertia Force from Added Mass]
    P11 --> P12[Final Sway or Heave Force]

    style P1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style P12 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Force Vector Visualization (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SubmersibleBody[Submersible Hull at Origin]
        CG[Center of Gravity]
    end

    subgraph LateralForces[Sway Direction Y axis]
        F_I_Y[Inertia Force F_I_sway along negative Y]
        F_D_Y[Damping Force F_D_sway along negative Y]
    end

    subgraph VerticalForces[Heave Direction Z axis]
        F_I_Z[Inertia Force F_I_heave along negative Z]
        F_D_Z[Damping Force F_D_heave along negative Z]
    end

    CG --> F_I_Y
    CG --> F_D_Y
    CG --> F_I_Z
    CG --> F_D_Z

    F_I_Y --> RESULT[Resultant 6-DOF Hydrodynamic Load Vector]
    F_D_Y --> RESULT
    F_I_Z --> RESULT
    F_D_Z --> RESULT

    style CG fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style RESULT fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style LateralForces fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style VerticalForces fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams above replace a physical free-body sketch with a clean systems-engineering architecture. Each color-coded block represents a specific force contribution, and the arrows depict the directional sign convention. This approach is preferred in KTU valuation when the question is *process-oriented* rather than *drawing-oriented*.
