<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q: Define sway and heave resistance in a submersible. Why are they not governed by the same physics as surge (forward) resistance?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 Marks):**
Sway resistance is the hydrodynamic force opposing **lateral** (sideways) motion of a submersible along the Y-axis. Heave resistance opposes **vertical** (up-down) motion along the Z-axis. **(1 Mark)**

Unlike surge resistance — which is dominated by **frictional and form drag from forward translation** — sway and heave resistance are predominantly governed by **added mass inertia effects** and **cross-flow drag (Hoerner's principle)**. This is because the cross-section presented to the flow is not the streamlined bow profile but a *bluff* 2D cylinder-like section. **(2 Marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q: State Hoerner's Cross-Flow Drag Principle. Write the formula for the local cross-flow drag coefficient.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 Marks):**
Hoerner's Principle states that for a **slender body** moving laterally or vertically through a fluid, each infinitesimal cross-sectional strip behaves as an **independent 2D cylinder in cross-flow**, and the total cross-flow resistance is obtained by integrating the strip-wise contributions along the hull length. **(2 Marks)**

The local cross-flow drag coefficient:

$$C_{DC}(x) = C_{D\infty} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\text{Re}(x)}{1000}\right) \right]$$

where $\text{Re}(x)$ is the local cross-flow Reynolds number. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing projected areas:** Students often use the **same** projected area for sway and heave. They are different (side projection vs. deck projection). 
> 2. **Forgetting the integration sign:** Strip theory requires **integration over the length**, not multiplication by total area. Marks are deducted when a single force coefficient is incorrectly applied globally.
> 3. **Mixing up added mass with displaced mass:** $C_A$ is the *added* (hydrodynamic) mass coefficient — the actual body mass $m$ is **separate** and does not appear in hydrodynamic added mass force.
> 4. **Ignoring sign convention:** Sway/heave forces oppose velocity, so they are written in the direction of $-\vec{V}$ in Newton's Second Law form.

---

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q: (a) Derive the expression for the added mass coefficient of a prolate spheroid in transverse (sway) motion. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A small submersible has a length $L = 10$ m, maximum beam $B = 1.5$ m, and is approximated as a prolate spheroid. It is heaving with velocity $w = 1$ m/s and acceleration $\dot{w} = 0.4$ m/s² in sea water ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Compute the total heave resistance if the heave drag coefficient $C_{D,\text{heave}} = 0.8$ and the deck projected area equals 70% of the maximum transverse cross-section. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition of Added Mass:** When a body accelerates through a fluid, it must also accelerate a portion of the surrounding fluid. This effective additional inertia is called the *added (virtual) mass*, $m_A$. The added mass coefficient is $C_A = m_A / (\rho \nabla)$, where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Potential Flow Around a Prolate Spheroid:** For an ellipsoid with semi-axes $a, b, c$ translating in the $i$-th direction with velocity $U$, the fluid kinetic energy is $T = \frac{1}{2} m_i U^2$, where $m_i = \rho \nabla \alpha_i$. The geometric coefficient is: **(2 Marks)**

$$\alpha_a = \frac{(1-e^2)}{e^3} \left[ \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right) - e \right] \quad \text{with} \quad e = \sqrt{1 - b^2/a^2}$$

**Step 3 — Identity for Ellipsoid:** By the ellipsoidal identity $\alpha_a + 2\alpha_b = 1$: **(2 Marks)**

$$\alpha_b = \frac{1 - \alpha_a}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Transverse Added Mass Coefficient:** The sway added mass coefficient is therefore: **(2 Marks)**

$$\boxed{C_{A,\text{sway}} = \alpha_b = \frac{1 - \alpha_a}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ 1 - \frac{(1-e^2)}{e^3}\left(\frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1+e}{1-e} - e\right)\right]}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Eccentricity:** $a = L/2 = 5$ m, $b = B/2 = 0.75$ m. **(1 Mark)**

$$e = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{0.75}{5}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0225} = \sqrt{0.9775} \approx 0.9887$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\alpha_a$:** **(2 Marks)**

$$\alpha_a = \frac{0.0225}{0.9663}\left[\frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1.9887}{0.0113}\right) - 0.9887\right] \approx 0.0233 \times [5.122 - 0.9887] \approx 0.0963$$

**Step 3 — Sway/Heave Added Mass Coefficient:** **(1 Mark)**

$$C_A = \frac{1 - 0.0963}{2} \approx 0.4519$$

**Step 4 — Displaced Volume:** $\nabla = \frac{4}{3}\pi a b^2 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (5)(0.75)^2 \approx 11.78$ m³. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 — Heave Force Components:** Inertia: $F_I = 1025 \times 0.4519 \times 11.78 \times 0.4 \approx 2183$ N. Damping (with deck area $A_p = 0.7 \times \pi b^2 \times 2 = 0.7 \times \pi(0.75)^2 \times 2 \approx 2.47$ m²): **(2 Marks)**

$$F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.8 \times 2.47 \times (1)^2 \approx 1013 \text{ N}$$

**Total Heave Force** $\approx 2183 + 1013 \approx \mathbf{3196 \text{ N} \approx 3.2 \text{ kN}}$. **(Full 7 Marks achieved)**

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a) Explain with neat block diagrams the strip theory approach for computing sway resistance of a slender submersible hull. State Hoerner's correlation for the local cross-flow drag coefficient. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A cylindrical submersible of length $L = 8$ m and diameter $D = 1$ m is moving laterally at $v = 2$ m/s in sea water. Using strip theory with 4 equal strips and Hoerner's correlation ($C_{D\infty} = 1.2$, $\nu = 10^{-6}$ m²/s), calculate the total sway cross-flow drag force. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Strip Theory Concept:** A slender body (L/D > 6) is divided longitudinally into N thin strips of width dx. Each strip's local cross-section $D(x)$ is treated as a 2D cylinder. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 2 — Local Force Computation:** For strip at position $x$:
- Local Reynolds number: $\text{Re}(x) = V(x) D(x) / \nu$
- Local drag coefficient: $C_{DC}(x) = C_{D\infty} [1 - \exp(-\text{Re}(x)/1000)]$
- Local force: $dF = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_{DC}(x) D(x) dx \cdot v^2$ **(2 Marks)**

**Step 3 — Global Integration:** $F = \int_0^L dF = \int_0^L \frac{1}{2}\rho C_{DC}(x) D(x) v^2 dx$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4 — Hoerner's Correlation:** As written in formula 4 of the cheat sheet. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 — Block Diagram (verbal):** Geometry Input → Discretization → Local Strip Evaluation → Integration → Total Force. **(1 Mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Geometry:** Cylinder, $L = 8$ m, $D = 1$ m, 4 strips → $\Delta x = 2$ m each. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Local Reynolds (same for all strips since D and v constant):** **(1 Mark)**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{v D}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 1}{10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Local Drag Coefficient:** **(1 Mark)**

$$C_{DC} = 1.2 \times \left[1 - e^{-2 \times 10^6 / 1000}\right] = 1.2 \times [1 - e^{-2000}] \approx 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.2$$

(The exponential is essentially zero at $\text{Re} = 2 \times 10^6$.) **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4 — Per-Strip Force:** **(1 Mark)**

$$dF_{\text{strip}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times (1 \times 2) \times (2)^2 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2 \times 4 = 4920 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Total Force (4 strips):** $F_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 4920 = \mathbf{19{,}680 \text{ N} \approx 19.68 \text{ kN}}$ **(2 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Things to Remember Before the Exam:**

- **Sway = lateral (Y-axis); Heave = vertical (Z-axis)** — *translational motions only.*
- **Sway/Heave resistance has TWO components:** (1) Inertia from **Added Mass** ($F_I = \rho C_A \nabla a$) and (2) Damping from **Cross-Flow Drag** ($F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{DC} A_p V^2$).
- **Hoerner's Cross-Flow Principle** treats a slender hull as a stack of 2D cross-flow cylinders; **strip theory integration** is mandatory.
- **Hoerner's local drag correlation:** $C_{DC} = C_{D\infty}\left[1 - \exp(-\text{Re}/1000)\right]$.
- **Ellipsoidal added mass identity:** $\alpha_a + 2\alpha_b = 1$ (translates to $C_{A,\text{sway}} = C_{A,\text{heave}} = (1-\alpha_a)/2$ for axisymmetric prolate bodies).
- **Sphere added mass coefficient is 0.5** on all three translational axes.
- **Sway projected area** = side (transverse) projection $\pi a b$ for an ellipsoid.
- **Heave projected area** = deck/keel (plan) projection — **different from sway!**
- **Damping usually dominates** inertia force for steady sway/heave; inertia dominates during rapid maneuvering transients.
- **Sway > Heave damping** for the same slender body because the side profile is larger than the deck profile.
- **Standard sea water properties to memorize:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
- **Sign convention:** Hydrodynamic resistance forces oppose velocity — they enter equations of motion as **negative** terms.
- **Numerical value to remember:** For slender prolate spheroids with $a/b \geq 5$, $C_A \approx 0.45$–$0.50$ (close to sphere value).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Resistance Estimation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Resistance Estimation** in the context of submersible design refers to the quantitative prediction and analytical breakdown of the hydrodynamic forces opposing the motion of an underwater vehicle when it is fully submerged and propelled at a given speed through a fluid medium (typically seawater). The **Total Resistance (R_T)** is the vector sum of all retarding forces acting on the hull and appendages, expressed in Newtons (**N**) or kiloNewtons (**kN**). For a fully submerged body, the resistance components differ fundamentally from a surface ship because the free-surface (wave) effects are largely eliminated. The dominant contributions are: $$R_T = R_F + R_P + R_{APP} + R_{RES}$$ where: - $R_F$ = Frictional (viscous) resistance - $R_P$ = Pressure (form) resistance - $R_{APP}$ = Appendage resistance - $R_{RES}$ = Residuary resistance (small at deep submergence) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike surface ships, submerged vehicles do not experience significant wave-making resistance, especially when operating below a critical depth. The primary resistance contributors are **viscous (frictional)** and **pressure (form)** components, which together account for nearly **95%** of the total drag. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are swimming underwater in a pool. When you push your body forward: - The water "sticks" to your skin and drags along with you — this is **frictional resistance** (like rubbing sandpaper across a surface). - The shape of your body (head, shoulders, feet) forces water to separate and create low-pressure wakes behind you — this is **form (pressure) resistance** (like sticking your hand out a car window palm-first vs. edge-first). - Your arms and legs add extra drag — this is **appendage resistance**. A submersible behaves the same way. The designer must estimate these forces accurately because they directly determine the **propulsion power** required, which in turn dictates **battery capacity**, **endurance**, and **mission range**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favorites):** > - Kinematic viscosity of seawater: $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s** at 20°C > - Density of seawater: $\rho = 1025$ **kg/m³** > - Reference area for submarines: Wetted Surface Area (**WSA**) in m² - Reference area for appendages: frontal area in m² --- ### The Resistance Triad — A Visual Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Submersible Resistance:** > 1. **Friction** (skin drag) — proportional to wetted area and $V^{1.7}$ to $V^{1.8}$ > 2. **Form/Pressure** (shape drag) — dependent on hull geometry and separation points > 3. **Appendage** (lifting surfaces, control fins) — analogous to aircraft wing drag ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Decomposition of Total Resistance The resistance of a fully submerged body is conventionally split into two broad categories: $$R_T = R_V + R_W$$ where: - $R_V$ = Viscous resistance (friction + form-drag due to viscous effects) - $R_W$ = Wave resistance (negligible for deep submergence) For submersibles, the **ITTC (International Towing Tank Conference)** recommends the splitting: $$R_T = R_F + R_P + R_{APP}$$ ### 2.2 Frictional Resistance ($R_F$) Frictional resistance arises from the **viscous shear stresses** in the turbulent boundary layer flowing over the wetted hull. The standard **ITTC 1957 correlation** is universally used: $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$ $$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot C_F$$ where: - $C_F$ = Frictional resistance coefficient (dimensionless) - $R_n$ = Reynolds number based on length $L$ (dimensionless) - $S$ = Wetted Surface Area (WSA) in m² - $V$ = Submerged speed in m/s - $\rho$ = Mass density of water in **kg/m³** > [!NOTE] > **Why the 0.075 constant?** It is the empirical ITTC-57 line that approximates the turbulent flat-plate friction data of Schoenherr. The denominator $(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ ensures $C_F$ decreases as $R_n$ increases — a hallmark of turbulent boundary layers. ### 2.3 Pressure (Form) Resistance ($R_P$) Form resistance depends on the **shape, length-to-beam ratio, and hull fullness**. It is estimated from the **form factor (k)** which accounts for the difference between 2D flat-plate and 3D body friction: $$R_P = R_F \cdot (1 + k) - R_F = R_F \cdot k$$ The form factor $k$ is determined experimentally (from model tests) or by empirical correlations. For typical submarine hulls: | Hull Type | Form Factor $k$ | |------------|:--:| | Myring-like (axisymmetric, streamlined) | **0.10 – 0.15** | | Tear-drop (Pellissippi/Ben Franklin) | **0.08 – 0.12** | | Blunt-bow research submersibles | **0.20 – 0.30** | | Cylindrical with hemispherical ends | **0.15 – 0.25** | ### 2.4 Appendage Resistance ($R_{APP}$) Appendages include control surfaces, sail/fairing, propeller hub, and sonar domes. Standard estimate: $$R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \sum A_i \cdot C_{D,i}$$ where $A_i$ and $C_{D,i}$ are the frontal area and drag coefficient of the $i^{th}$ appendage. ### 2.5 Reynolds Number Scaling For model-to-prototype extrapolation: $$R_{n,m} = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu}, \quad R_{n,p} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Submersible resistance tests are typically run at **Froude-matched** (but Reynolds-mismatched) conditions. For deeply submerged bodies, Froude scaling is irrelevant and **Reynolds similarity** is the governing criterion. ### 2.6 Froude Number for Submerged Bodies For surface-piercing operation or near-surface maneuvers: $$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$$ > [!WARNING] > A common mistake: applying wave-resistance corrections (Hughes, Havelock) to fully submerged vehicles. For depth $h \geq 0.5L$, wave resistance is below **5%** of total and may be neglected. --- ### KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless; $L$ = length | | 2 | Froude Number | $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Dimensionless; surface ops only | | 3 | ITTC-57 Friction Coefficient | $C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | Turbulent flat plate | | 4 | Frictional Resistance | $R_F = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$ | **N** | | 5 | Form Resistance | $R_P = k \cdot R_F$ | $k$ = form factor | | 6 | Appendage Resistance | $R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \sum A_i C_{D,i}$ | Sum over all appendages | | 7 | Total Resistance | $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_{APP}$ | Submerged case | | 8 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | **Watts (W)** | | 9 | WSA (Submarine) | $S = \pi D L (1 - \frac{1}{k_1^2})$ (Myring) | m², $D$ = diameter, $k_1$ = length ratio | | 10 | Reynolds Scaling | $\frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p}$ | Same fluid: $V_m L_m = V_p L_p$ | --- ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Resistance estimation is the **starting point** of the entire propulsion system design chain: $$R_T \rightarrow P_E = R_T V \rightarrow P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_H \eta_R} \rightarrow \text{Battery Sizing / Reactor Power}$$ - **Deep-sea AUVs (Autosub, Hugin):** endurance is battery-limited → minimizing $R_T$ is critical. - **Military SSNs/SSBNs:** acoustic signature is partly speed-dependent → resistance sets the natural speed envelope. - **Human-Occupied Vehicles (DSV Alvin):** maneuverability vs. drag trade-off drives control-surface sizing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the ITTC-57 Friction Line (Foundation) The ITTC-57 formula is an empirical fit to the turbulent flat-plate data. The **Schoenherr skin-friction line** gives: $$\frac{0.242}{\sqrt{C_F}} = \log_{10}(R_n \cdot C_F)$$ Iterating this transcendental equation is cumbersome for board exams. The **ITTC simplified form** is obtained by linearizing in $\log_{10} R_n$ space. The full derivation: **Step 1:** Define the local slope from turbulent boundary-layer theory (1/7th power law): $$\tau_w = 0.0288 \rho U^2 R_n^{-0.2}$$ **Step 2:** Integrate over the plate to get the average skin-friction coefficient: $$C_F = \frac{0.074}{R_n^{0.2}}$$ **Step 3:** Re-write the power-law form as a logarithmic approximation. Taking $\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$\log_{10}(R_n^{0.2}) = 0.2 \log_{10} R_n$$ **Step 4:** The ITTC-57 committee fitted the curve to be: $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$ **Text-row conversion logic:** This is mathematically equivalent to the power-law form for $R_n \approx 10^7$ to $10^9$ but is more accurate in the mid-Reynolds range typical of model submarines. --- ### 3.2 Complete Worked Example: AUV Resistance Prediction **Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A 5 m long AUV of wetted surface area $S = 12$ m² operates fully submerged at $V = 4$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The form factor is $k = 0.12$ and total appendage drag area is $A_{app} = 0.4$ m² with $C_D = 1.1$. Estimate the total resistance and effective power. **Step 1 — Reynolds Number:** $$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{4 \times 5}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.0 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 2 — ITTC Friction Coefficient:** $$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(2.0 \times 10^{7}) = 7.301$$ $$\log_{10} R_n - 2 = 5.301$$ $$(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 = 28.10$$ $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{28.10} = 2.668 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Frictional Resistance:** $$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 4^2 \times 12 \times 2.668 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16 \times 12 \times 2.668 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16 \times 0.03202$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.5123$$ $$R_F = 262.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Form Resistance:** $$R_P = k \cdot R_F = 0.12 \times 262.6 = 31.5 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Appendage Resistance:** $$R_{APP} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_{app} C_D$$ $$R_{APP} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16 \times 0.4 \times 1.1$$ $$R_{APP} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 7.04 = 3608 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Total Resistance:** $$R_T = R_F + R_P + R_{APP}$$ $$R_T = 262.6 + 31.5 + 3608 = 3902 \text{ N} \approx 3.90 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 7 — Effective Power:** $$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 3902 \times 4 = 15{,}608 \text{ W} \approx 15.6 \text{ kW}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What | Operating Condition | Dominant Resistance | Preferred Method | |---------------------|--------------------|--------------------| | Fully submerged, deep ($h > 0.5L$) | Friction + Form (no waves) | ITTC-57 + form factor $k$ | | Near surface ($h < 0.5L$) | All three, plus wave drag | Add Michell integral estimate | | Appendage-rich vehicle (sail, fins) | Appendage drag dominant | Component-by-component $C_D$ table | | High-speed (supercavitating) | Cavity drag, not viscous | Use Logvinovich or experimental data | | Low-speed maneuvering | Viscous + cross-flow drag | Use 6-DOF coefficients instead | ### 3.4 Derivation: Power-Reduction Ratio for Submerged Operation For a surface ship and a fully submerged vehicle of identical hull form, the **submerged resistance** is **lower** because wave drag vanishes. A typical ratio is: $$\frac{R_{sub}}{R_{surf}} = \frac{R_F + R_P}{R_F + R_P + R_W} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.8$$ at the same speed and length. **Step-by-step text logic:** The wave resistance $R_W$ is roughly 20–50% of surface ship drag. Removing it directly reduces $R_T$, but $R_F$ increases slightly because the wetted length is now the full body (vs. surface ship where some appendages are partially submerged). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** If the question says "fully submerged" or "operating at depth," you can **safely set $R_W = 0$** unless told otherwise. This is a guaranteed mark in Part A and Part B alike. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Resistance Component Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Resistance RT] --> B[Viscous Resistance RV] A --> C[Wave Resistance RW] A --> D[Appendage Resistance RAPP] B --> E[Frictional RF
ITTC 57 Line] B --> F[Pressure Form RP
Form Factor k] F --> G[k times RF] E --> H[0.5 rho V2 S CF] C --> I[Michell Integral] C --> J[Slender Body Theory] D --> K[Sail Fairing] D --> L[Control Fins] D --> M[Sonar Dome] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Submersible Resistance Estimation Methodology Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD step1[Step 1: Define Geometry
L B D WSA] --> step2[Step 2: Select Speed V and Depth h] step2 --> step3{Depth Check
h gt 0.5L} step3 -->|Yes| step4[Set Wave Drag RW = 0] step3 -->|No| step5[Compute Michell Wave Drag] step4 --> step6[Compute Reynolds Rn] step5 --> step6 step6 --> step7[Compute CF via ITTC 57] step7 --> step8[Compute RF = 0.5 rho V2 S CF] step8 --> step9[Apply Form Factor k] step9 --> step10[Compute RP = k RF] step10 --> step11[Sum Appendage RAPP] step11 --> step12[RT = RF + RP + RAPP] step12 --> step13[PE = RT V Effective Power] style step12 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style step13 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Boundary Layer and Drag Mechanism Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR BL[Body Leading Edge] --> LP[Laminar Layer] LP --> TR[Transition Point] TR --> TB[Turbulent Layer] TB --> SEP[Flow Separation] SEP --> WAKE[Low Pressure Wake] WAKE --> DR[Form Drag Originates Here] S1[Friction Acts] --> LP S1 --> TB S2[Pressure Acts] --> DR style BL fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style SEP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style DR fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.4 Resistance vs. Speed Characteristic Curve (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW["Low Speed Region"] L1[Friction Dominant] --> L2[RT proportional to V 1.8] end subgraph MID["Mid Speed Region"] M1[Friction plus Form] --> M2[RT proportional to V 2.0] end subgraph HIGH["High Speed Submerged"] H1[Friction plus Form plus Wave] --> H2[RT proportional to V 2.5 to 3.0] end LOW --> MID MID --> HIGH style L1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style M1 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style H1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid for students:** The exponents of $V$ are conceptual. In reality, $C_F$ decreases with $R_n$, so $R_F$ grows as $V^{1.7}$ to $V^{1.85}$ depending on the length range — not as $V^2$. This subtlety is what makes resistance prediction an art, not just a formula. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List the four main components of resistance acting on a fully submerged submersible and state which one can be neglected at deep submergence.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four components are: 1. **Frictional resistance ($R_F$)** — viscous shear on wetted hull 2. **Pressure (form) resistance ($R_P$)** — shape-dependent pressure drag 3. **Appendage resistance ($R_{APP}$)** — drag of control surfaces, sail, sonar dome 4. **Wave resistance ($R_W$)** — energy radiated as surface gravity waves At deep submergence ($h > 0.5L$, where $L$ is hull length), **wave resistance is negligible** because the hull is too far below the free surface to generate significant wave systems. The remaining resistance is dominated by viscous and form components. (3 marks — 1 mark per correct point, 1 mark for the depth condition) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the form factor $k$ in the resistance estimation of submersibles. State a typical value range for a streamlined myring hull.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **form factor $k$** is the ratio of the form (pressure) resistance of a 3D body to the frictional resistance of an equivalent flat plate of the same wetted area: $$k = \frac{R_P}{R_F}$$ It accounts for the additional pressure drag arising from 3D geometry, surface curvature, and flow separation. For a **streamlined Myring hull**, typical values are **$k = 0.10$ to $0.15$**. (3 marks) --- ### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** *Derive, with a clear sketch, the ITTC-57 formula for frictional resistance coefficient. State the assumptions. (7 marks)* **(b)** *A submersible of length $L = 8$ m, wetted surface area $S = 22$ m², and form factor $k = 0.14$ operates at $V = 3$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The total appendage drag area is 0.6 m² with average $C_D = 0.9$. Calculate the total resistance and effective power. Neglect wave drag. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of ITTC-57 formula:** Starting from the turbulent flat-plate assumption (1/7th power velocity profile): $$\frac{u}{U} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$ The wall shear stress is: $$\tau_w = \mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\bigg|_{y=0} = \frac{\mu U}{7 \delta}$$ Using the boundary-layer momentum integral and the 1/7-power law, the local skin-friction coefficient becomes: $$c_f = 0.0592 R_x^{-0.2}$$ Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = L$: $$C_F = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L c_f \, dx = 0.074 \, R_L^{-0.2}$$ The ITTC-57 committee re-fitted this in a logarithmic form for the model-to-full-scale correlation: $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Fully turbulent boundary layer from the leading edge (no laminar region) 2. 2D flat plate (no 3D form effects — these go into $k$) 3. Smooth, hydraulically rough-free surface 4. Incompressible, steady flow (7 marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for sketch, 2 for assumptions) **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Reynolds number: $$R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 8}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.286 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 2:** Friction coefficient: $$\log_{10} R_n = 7.359$$ $$\log_{10} R_n - 2 = 5.359$$ $$(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 = 28.72$$ $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{28.72} = 2.612 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3:** Frictional resistance: $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9 \times 22 \times 2.612 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9 \times 22 \times 0.002612$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.5170$$ $$R_F = 265.1 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4:** Form resistance: $$R_P = k \cdot R_F = 0.14 \times 265.1 = 37.1 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5:** Appendage resistance: $$R_{APP} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 2491.9 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6:** Total resistance: $$R_T = 265.1 + 37.1 + 2491.9 = 2794.1 \text{ N} \approx 2.79 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 7:** Effective power: $$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 2794.1 \times 3 = 8382 \text{ W} \approx 8.38 \text{ kW}$$ (7 marks — 1 mark per correctly computed step) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** *Explain the differences in resistance components between a surface ship and a fully submerged submersible. Why is wave resistance often neglected for submersibles? (7 marks)* **(b)** *A research submersible of length 6 m cruises at 4 knots fully submerged. Estimate the frictional resistance using ITTC-57. Given: wetted surface area = 14 m², $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Surface Ship vs. Submerged Submersible (7 marks):** | Aspect | Surface Ship | Submerged Submersible | |--------|--------------|----------------------| | Wave resistance | **Significant** (15–50% of $R_T$) | **Negligible** at depth $h > 0.5L$ | | Wetted area | Partial hull only (waterline girth) | **Full hull** always wetted | | Free-surface effects | Dominant (Froude scaling required) | None (only Reynolds scaling) | | Appendages | Shafts, rudders above water | All submerged | | Spray / wind drag | Present | **Absent** | | Reynolds number | Based on waterline length $L_{WL}$ | Based on **total length** $L$ | **Why wave resistance is neglected for submersibles:** - At a depth $h > 0.5L$, the energy radiated to the surface decays **exponentially** as $e^{-2kh}$ where $k$ is the wave number. - For typical submarine speeds, the Froude number is low ($F_n < 0.3$), and the wave system generated is too weak to produce measurable drag. - For $h > 0.5L$, wave resistance is empirically observed to be less than **5%** of total drag and is conservatively set to zero in design estimation. (7 marks — 4 for comparison, 3 for wave-resistance justification) **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Convert speed: $$V = 4 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 2.058 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Reynolds number: $$R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu} = \frac{2.058 \times 6}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.235 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 3:** Friction coefficient: $$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.235 \times 10^7) = 7.092$$ $$\log_{10} R_n - 2 = 5.092$$ $$(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 = 25.93$$ $$C_F = \frac{0.075}{25.93} = 2.893 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 4:** Frictional resistance: $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (2.058)^2 \times 14 \times 2.893 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 4.235 \times 14 \times 0.002893$$ $$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.1715$$ $$R_F = 87.9 \text{ N}$$ (7 marks — distributed across unit conversion, $R_n$, $C_F$, $R_F$ with each step earning marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion of speed** (knots → m/s) — easy 1-mark loss if missed. > 2. **Using $V^2$ directly** in $C_F$ calculation — wrong; $C_F$ depends only on $R_n$, not $V$ alone. > 3. **Forgetting to multiply $R_F$ by $k$** for form drag — common error. > 4. **Neglecting appendage drag** when problem states "control surfaces are fitted" — appendage term is **mandatory**. > 5. **Including wave drag for a deeply submerged vehicle** — penalised as over-engineering. > 6. **Not specifying units** in the final answer — KTU deducts 0.5 marks for "N" or "kW" omissions. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **$R_T = R_F + R_P + R_{APP}$** for fully submerged bodies (wave term dropped). - **ITTC-57 line** is the universal frictional-resistance estimator; remember the **0.075** numerator and the **$(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$** denominator. - **Form factor $k$** quantifies the 3D shape penalty: streamlined bodies have $k \approx 0.1$, blunt bodies $k \approx 0.3$. - **Appendage drag** is computed component-wise using frontal area and 2D $C_D$ tables. - **Depth matters:** $h > 0.5L$ → wave drag negligible; $h < 0.5L$ → Michell integral required. - **Reynolds scaling** is the **only** scaling law needed for deep submergence; Froude scaling is for surface operations. - **Standard seawater constants to memorize:** $\rho = 1025$ **kg/m³**, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s**. - **Effective power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ is the **starting point** for the propulsion system design chain. - **Speed unit traps:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 m/s = 1.944 knots. - **Result check:** $R_F$ for a typical 5 m AUV at 4 m/s should be in the range **200–400 N** — if your answer is in kN, you have a unit error. - **Practical tip:** For deeply submerged bodies, **friction and form are about 60–80%** of total drag; appendages can dominate in fin-heavy designs.

topics.title: Propulsion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propulsion

# Propulsion Systems for Submersibles

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3, Topic: Propulsion)**
> **Propulsion** in the context of submersibles refers to the integrated engineering system responsible for converting stored energy (electrical, chemical, or mechanical) into controlled translational and rotational motion of an underwater vehicle through a surrounding fluid medium. It encompasses the prime mover, transmission, thrust-generating device (propeller/pump-jet/MHD), and the hydrodynamic interface with the hull.

### 1.1 Formal Terminology Block

A submersible propulsion plant consists of four mandatory sub-systems operating in series:

| Sub-System | Function | KTU Standard Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Prime Mover** | Converts stored energy → mechanical/electrical work | kW, hp (1 hp = **745.7 W**) |
| **Transmission** | Couples prime mover speed to thrust device | Gear ratio (dimensionless) |
| **Thrust Generator** | Accelerates fluid mass rearward → reaction force | Newtons (**N**) |
| **Hull Interface** | Wake, thrust deduction, cross-coupling | Wake fraction $w$, $t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Density:** Seawater at **4 °C** has $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$. Fresh water at 4 °C has $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$. These are KTU-referenced benchmark values for all B-series naval architecture problems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Scuba Diver on a Scooter**
> Imagine a scuba diver wearing a small handheld scooter underwater. The scooter has a spinning propeller (like a fan) that pushes water backwards. By **Newton's Third Law**, the water pushes the diver forward. Now scale this up to a 50-tonne submersible: the propeller must accelerate **tons of water per second** to generate tens of kiloNewtons of thrust. The bigger the propeller, the more "slugs of water" it can shove backwards per revolution — but only if it avoids cavitation (vapor bubbles) and excessive tip-speed losses.

### 1.3 Classification of Submersible Propulsion

Based on KTU Module 3 learning outcomes, submersible propulsion is classified as:

- **A. Mechanical-Propeller Based**
  - Open fixed-pitch propeller (FPP) — most common on civilian submersibles
  - Controllable-reversible pitch (CRP) propeller — naval/military use
  - Ducted (Kort nozzle) propeller — improves bollard thrust by **20–30%**
- **B. Water-Reaction Based**
  - Pump-jet (fully shrouded ducted rotor)
  - Water-jet (partial duct with intake/outlet)
- **C. Novel / Non-Conventional**
  - Magneto-Hydrodynamic (MHD) — silent, no moving parts
  - Bio-inspired (oscillating foil, undulating body)
  - Buoyancy-vector (gravity-glider for AUVs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 – Topical Weightage Note**
> The dominant share of marks in this module is awarded to **Propeller Theory** (Wageningen B-Series, $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$) and **Hull-Propeller Interaction** ($w$, $t$, $\eta_H$, $\eta_D$, $\eta_R$). Mastery of these is non-negotiable for ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an Open-Propeller System

When the prime mover rotates the propeller shaft at $n$ revolutions per second, the following **sequence of events** unfolds at the propeller disc:

- **Step 1 — Inflow Acceleration:** Water enters the propeller disc with the local wake velocity $V_A = V_\infty(1 - w)$, where $w$ is the axial wake fraction.
- **Step 2 — Energy Transfer:** Blades impart a tangential swirl velocity $\Delta V_\theta$ and an axial velocity jump $\Delta V_A$ across the disc.
- **Step 3 — Thrust Generation:** Per Newton's 2nd law, the rate of change of momentum in the axial direction produces thrust: $T = \dot{m}(V_e - V_0)$.
- **Step 4 — Power Dissipation:** Useful power $P_{useful} = T \cdot V_\infty$; remainder is the rotational kinetic energy lost to the wake swirl (rotative loss).

## 2.2 Ideal Actuator Disc Theory (Rankine-Froude Momentum Model)

This is the **analytical foundation** for all KTU propeller questions. Consider an infinitely thin disc of area $A_e$ that imparts a uniform pressure jump $\Delta p$ across itself.

### The Three Velocity Regions

- Region 1 (far upstream): $V_\infty$ — undisturbed flow
- Region 2 (at the disc): $V_A = V_\infty(1 - w)$ — axial inflow
- Region 3 (far downstream wake): $V_e$ — exit velocity

From continuity and momentum balance:

$$V_A = \frac{V_\infty + V_e}{2}$$

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the disc velocity the *average* of upstream and downstream?**
> Because the disc acts like a control surface. By continuity, mass flow is constant everywhere in the slipstream. By momentum, thrust equals pressure jump × area. Solving both yields the **Froude axial assumption** — the disc sees exactly the average of upstream and downstream velocities.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — These formulas appear in 70%+ of KTU Module 3 propulsion questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disc Area | $A_e = \pi D^2/4$ | m² | $D$ = propeller diameter |
| 2 | Thrust (ideal) | $T = \rho A_e V_A (V_e - V_\infty)$ | N | Actuator disc model |
| 3 | Ideal Efficiency | $\eta_i = \dfrac{2}{1 + V_e/V_\infty}$ | — | Always $\leq 1$ |
| 4 | Advance Velocity | $V_A = V_\infty(1 - w)$ | m/s | $w$ = wake fraction |
| 5 | Slip Ratio | $V_s/V_\infty$ | — | Indicates loading |
| 6 | Advance Coefficient | $J = V_A/(nD)$ | — | Dimensionless speed |
| 7 | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$ | — | B-Series standard |
| 8 | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = Q/(\rho n^2 D^5)$ | — | B-Series standard |
| 9 | Open-Water Efficiency | $\eta_0 = (J/2\pi)(K_T/K_Q)$ | — | Best on-line efficiency |
| 10 | Hull Efficiency | $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ | — | $t$ = thrust deduction |
| 11 | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$ | — | Overall figure of merit |
| 12 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$ | — | $\sigma < \sigma_{crit}$ ⇒ cavitation |
| 13 | Reynolds Number | $Re = V L / \nu$ | — | $\nu_{sw} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| 14 | Tip-Speed | $V_{tip} = \pi n D$ | m/s | Must $\leq V_{cav}$ limit |
| 15 | Tip Mach Limit | $M_{tip} = V_{tip}/c_{sw}$ | — | $c_{sw} \approx 1500$ m/s |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Warning:** All absolute-value / vertical-bar notations inside this table use `\vert` or `\mid`. KTU examiners reject answers where LaTeX tables break due to unescaped pipes. Always substitute `\|` with `\mid` when writing inline.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Theory

The actuator disc model is **engineering gold** because:

- It gives the **maximum theoretical efficiency** for any thrust-producing device in inviscid flow.
- Real propellers, pump-jets, and even MHD thrusters are benchmarked against this limit.
- It enables **scaling laws**: a model propeller tested at 1/10 scale can predict full-scale thrust using Reynolds-corrected coefficients.
- For AUVs operating at depth, it predicts how **ambient pressure** raises the cavitation ceiling, allowing more aggressive blade-tip speeds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Froude Ideal Efficiency of the Actuator Disc

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot Derivation #1 — Memorise every step.**

### Given
- A thin disc of area $A_e = \pi D^2/4$ immersed in fluid of density $\rho$.
- Steady incompressible flow at far-field velocity $V_\infty$.
- Pressure jump $\Delta p$ across the disc.

### Find
- Ideal efficiency $\eta_i$ in terms of $V_e$ and $V_\infty$.

### Step 1 — Continuity Across the Disc
Mass flow entering upstream must equal mass flow leaving downstream:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_e V_\infty = \rho A_e V_e = \rho A_e V_A$$

> *Logic*: Steady-state, incompressible, no leakage through the disc → the mass crossing any cross-section of the slipstream per unit time is identical.

### Step 2 — Momentum Theorem on the Control Volume
Apply Newton's 2nd law to the slipstream bounded by far-upstream and far-downstream stations. The net pressure force on the control volume equals the rate of momentum change:

$$T = \Delta p \cdot A_e = \dot{m}(V_e - V_\infty)$$

> *Logic*: The disc imparts a higher pressure on its downstream face than its upstream face. The integral of this pressure difference × area is thrust.

### Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli on Either Side of the Disc
Since the disc is idealized as having no thickness and no rotational wake, the flow upstream and downstream is irrotational. Bernoulli's equation applies on both streamlines:

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_{u} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2$$

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_e^2 = p_{d} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2$$

> *Logic*: The slipstream contracts after the disc; the streamlines outside the slipstream are unaffected by the disc.

### Step 4 — Solve for the Pressure Jump
Subtracting the two Bernoulli equations yields:

$$\Delta p = p_d - p_u = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_\infty^2 - V_e^2)$$

> *Logic*: The downstream pressure is lower than upstream pressure when $V_e > V_\infty$ — this is the **suction effect** that an ideal propeller exploits.

### Step 5 — Express $V_A$ as the Average
By the momentum + continuity argument, the disc sees the arithmetic mean:

$$V_A = \frac{V_\infty + V_e}{2}$$

> *Logic*: At the disc, the kinetic energy is half the change between upstream and downstream kinetic energies. This is the *Froude axial assumption*.

### Step 6 — Thrust in Terms of Velocities
Substituting Step 5 into Step 2:

$$T = \rho A_e \cdot \frac{V_\infty + V_e}{2} \cdot (V_e - V_\infty) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_e (V_e^2 - V_\infty^2)$$

> *Logic*: Simplification — the $(V_e + V_\infty)(V_e - V_\infty)$ difference-of-squares.

### Step 7 — Useful Power vs. Power Input

- **Useful power** (work done on the vehicle): $P_u = T \cdot V_\infty$
- **Input power** (rate of work done by pressure jump): $P_i = T \cdot V_A = T \cdot \dfrac{V_\infty + V_e}{2}$

> *Logic*: Input power is thrust × the velocity at which the thrust force physically does work on the fluid (which is the disc velocity).

### Step 8 — Define Ideal Efficiency

$$\eta_i = \frac{P_u}{P_i} = \frac{T \cdot V_\infty}{T \cdot V_A} = \frac{V_\infty}{(V_\infty + V_e)/2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_i = \frac{2}{1 + V_e/V_\infty}}$$

> *Logic*: This is the famous Froude ideal efficiency. Note that as $V_e/V_\infty \to 1$ (i.e., the wake disturbance vanishes), $\eta_i \to 1$. But then thrust also goes to zero. There is an inherent trade-off.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Four Coupled Efficiencies

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked: "Explain the components of $\eta_D$."**

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Range | Cause of Loss |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\eta_0$ | Open-water (rotative) | $\dfrac{J K_T}{2\pi K_Q}$ | 0.55–0.72 | Friction, profile drag on blades |
| $\eta_H$ | Hull efficiency | $\dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}$ | 1.05–1.30 | Wake non-uniformity, thrust deduction |
| $\eta_R$ | Relative rotative | $\dfrac{1 - s}{1}$ | 0.97–1.00 | Difference between model and ship $K_Q$ |
| $\eta_D$ | Propulsive (overall) | $\eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$ | 0.55–0.70 | Combined system figure of merit |

### Worked Numerical Example — KTU Module 3 Style

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** A submarine model has $D = 1.2$ m, $n = 12$ rps, $V_\infty = 5$ m/s. Measured: $T = 1800$ N, $Q = 350$ N·m. Seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Wake fraction $w = 0.18$, thrust deduction $t = 0.15$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{5(1-0.18)}{12 \times 1.2} = \frac{4.10}{14.4} = 0.285
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K_T &= \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{1800}{1025 \times 144 \times 2.074} = \frac{1800}{306{,}012} = 0.00588
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K_Q &= \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = \frac{350}{1025 \times 144 \times 2.488} = \frac{350}{367{,}214} = 0.000953
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_0 &= \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.285}{6.2832} \cdot \frac{0.00588}{0.000953} = 0.0454 \times 6.170 = 0.280
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_H &= \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{0.85}{0.82} = 1.037
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_D &= 0.280 \times 1.037 \times 1.00 = 0.290
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Student Pitfall:** Many candidates forget that $J$ uses **wake-corrected** velocity $V_A = V_\infty(1-w)$, not the bare $V_\infty$. This single error shifts $\eta_0$ by ~10%.

## 3.3 Cavitation Derivation (Quick Form)

Cavitation inception occurs when local static pressure drops to vapor pressure $p_v$. The incipient cavitation number:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^2}$$

> *Logic*: When $\sigma < \sigma_i$, the blade's suction surface sees pressure $\leq p_v$ and vapor cavities nucleate. For submerged vehicles at depth, $p_0$ increases linearly with depth (≈ 1 atm per **10 m** of seawater), so depth-immersed submersibles can run **much higher tip speeds** without cavitation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Submersible Propulsion Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete submersible propulsion plant, showing signal flow and physical power transmission.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat[DC Battery Bank] --> conv[Power Converter]
    conv --> ctrl[Motor Controller VFD]
    ctrl --> em[Electric Motor PMSM or IM]
    em --> gearbox[Gear Reduction Box Ratio n1 to n2]
    gearbox --> shaft[Drive Shaft with Sealing Gland]
    shaft --> prop[Propeller or Pump Jet]
    prop --> wake[Slipstream Wake]
    wake --> hull1[Hull Stern Pressure Field]
    hull1 --> body[Submersible Body Translational Motion]
    em -.feedback.-> ctrl
    body -.speed and depth.-> ctrl

    style bat fill:#fce570,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    style em fill:#80c8ff,stroke:#003a66,color:#000
    style prop fill:#ff9090,stroke:#660000,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph upstream[Far Upstream Flow]
        A[Free Stream Velocity V infinity]
    end

    subgraph wakefield[Ship Wake Region]
        B[Wake Fraction w reduces V to V times 1 minus w]
    end

    subgraph propdisc[Propeller Disc]
        C[Advance Velocity V A]
        D[Disc Thrust T generated]
        E[Disc Efficiency factor]
    end

    subgraph downfield[Far Downstream Slipstream]
        F[Exit Velocity V e greater than V infinity]
    end

    subgraph interaction[Hull Stern Interaction]
        G[Thrust Deduction t reduces effective thrust]
        H[Hull Efficiency eta H equals 1 minus t over 1 minus w]
    end

    A --> B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    G --> H
    B --> H

    style A fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#444
    style D fill:#ff9090,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    style H fill:#90ff90,stroke:#1a5c1a,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cavitation Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Operate Propeller] --> Q{Ambient Pressure p0}
    Q -->|Shallow less than 50 m| R[High Sigma — Suction Cavitation Risk]
    Q -->|Deep greater than 200 m| S[Low Sigma — Supercavitating Regime]
    R --> T{V tip greater than V cav limit}
    T -->|Yes| U[Sheet and Tip Vortex Cavitation]
    T -->|No| V[Pressure Face Cavitation Only]
    S --> W{V tip and blade loading}
    W -->|Moderate| X[Stable Super Cavity Pattern]
    W -->|Aggressive| Y[Ventilated Cavity — Loss of Thrust]

    style P fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003366
    style U fill:#ffaaaa,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    style Y fill:#ffaaaa,stroke:#660000,color:#000

```

```mermaid

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU):** When asked to "draw the propeller slipstream" or "label the actuator disc model," always include the **three velocity regions** ($V_\infty$, $V_A$, $V_e$) and indicate the **pressure jump** $\Delta p$ across the disc with an arrow. Examiners explicitly look for these annotations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]
> *Define the term "wake fraction" $w$ in the context of submersible hull-propeller interaction. State its typical range for a streamlined submersible hull.*

**Model Answer:**

The wake fraction $w$ is the fractional reduction in axial velocity at the propeller disc compared to the free-stream velocity, caused by the boundary layer and potential flow effects of the hull.

$$w = \frac{V_\infty - V_A}{V_\infty}$$

For a well-streamlined torpedo-shaped submersible, $w$ typically ranges from **0.10 to 0.25**. Higher values indicate a "fatter" stern that drags more fluid with it. The wake fraction directly enters the hull efficiency formula:

$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}$$

> *(3 marks: definition 1, equation 1, range + connection 1)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> *What is the cavitation number? Why is cavitation more easily avoided on deep-diving submersibles than on surface ships?*

**Model Answer:**

The cavitation number $\sigma$ is defined as:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$$

where $p_0$ is ambient static pressure, $p_v$ is vapor pressure, and $V$ is the local flow velocity.

Cavitation is avoided when $\sigma > \sigma_{critical}$ for the propeller geometry. For a deep-diving submersible, $p_0$ increases linearly with depth at approximately **1 bar per 10 m of seawater**. Hence for a vehicle at 300 m depth, $p_0 \approx 31$ bar, allowing blade tip speeds far higher than at the surface without $\sigma$ dropping below critical. This is precisely why deep-submergence vehicles like ALVIN or DSV Limiting Factor can operate efficiently at high RPM.

> *(3 marks: definition with formula 1, comparison logic 1, deep-vehicle advantage 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Q8(a)]**

**(a)** Derive the **Froude ideal efficiency** of a propeller modelled as an actuator disc. Start from the momentum equation and show every algebraic step clearly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A model propeller of diameter $D = 0.6$ m is tested in a cavitation tunnel at $n = 20$ rps. The following data are recorded at one operating point: thrust $T = 850$ N, torque $Q = 95$ N·m, axial velocity at disc $V_A = 4.5$ m/s. The water density is $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.
   - Compute the thrust coefficient $K_T$, torque coefficient $K_Q$, advance coefficient $J$, and open-water efficiency $\eta_0$.
   - If the model's wake fraction and thrust deduction are $w = 0.12$ and $t = 0.10$, compute the hull efficiency $\eta_H$ and propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ (assume $\eta_R = 1.00$).
   - Comment on whether this propulsive efficiency is acceptable for a service submersible. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1–8** of these notes. The final boxed result:

$$\boxed{\eta_i = \frac{2}{1 + V_e/V_\infty}}$$

This derivation must be reproduced in full on the answer sheet. *(7 marks: continuity 1, momentum 1, Bernoulli 2, Froude assumption 1, efficiency definition 2)*

**(b)** Numerical Evaluation:

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{4.5}{20 \times 0.6} = \frac{4.5}{12} = 0.375
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K_T &= \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{850}{1000 \times 400 \times 0.1296} = \frac{850}{51{,}840} = 0.01640
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K_Q &= \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = \frac{95}{1000 \times 400 \times 0.07776} = \frac{95}{31{,,104} \text{(approx)}} = 0.003054
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_0 &= \frac{J}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.375}{6.2832}\cdot\frac{0.01640}{0.003054} = 0.0597 \times 5.370 = 0.3205
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_H &= \frac{1 - t}{1 - w} = \frac{0.90}{0.88} = 1.0227
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_D &= \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R = 0.3205 \times 1.0227 \times 1.00 = 0.3278
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** A propulsive efficiency of **0.328** (32.8%) is on the lower side for a service submersible. Modern well-designed submersibles achieve $\eta_D$ of **0.55–0.70**. The low value here may indicate either an off-design operating point, excessive blade-area ratio, or significant rotational losses. The designer should consider a re-pitch or geometry optimization.

> *(7 marks: 1 each for $J, K_T, K_Q$, 2 for $\eta_0$, 1 for $\eta_H$, 1 for $\eta_D$, 1 for comment)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Q8(b)]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear block diagram, explain the **hull–propeller interaction** in a submersible. Define wake fraction, thrust deduction, and hull efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A deep-submergence vehicle operates at 600 m depth. The ambient pressure at this depth is approximately **61 bar**. If the local absolute velocity over a propeller blade suction surface is $V = 18$ m/s and the vapor pressure of seawater at the operating temperature is $p_v = 0.025$ bar, compute the **cavitation number**. If the critical cavitation number for the chosen blade section is $\sigma_c = 0.40$, determine whether cavitation will occur. Seawater density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Required Components of the Answer:

1. **Block diagram** showing: free-stream $V_\infty$ → wake region → propeller disc → slipstream exit.
2. **Wake fraction** $w = (V_\infty - V_A)/V_\infty$: definition + typical value 0.10–0.30 for streamlined hulls.
3. **Thrust deduction** $t = (T - T_E)/T$: definition; caused by low-pressure wake accelerating the hull.
4. **Hull efficiency** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$: definition + physical meaning.
5. **Connection**: $\eta_H > 1$ is normal and **beneficial** — it represents the propeller working in the hull's accelerated wake.

> *(7 marks: diagram 2, three definitions 3 each worth 1, hull efficiency formula 1)*

**(b)** Numerical Evaluation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2} = \frac{61 - 0.025}{\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 18^2} = \frac{60.975}{166{,}050} = 3.67 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — let me recompute carefully. $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 324 = 166{,}050$ Pa (in Pascals, since $p_0$ and $p_v$ are in bar, we must convert).

Convert numerator to Pascals: $60.975 \text{ bar} = 60.975 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 6{,}097{,}500$ Pa.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{6{,}097{,}500}{166{,}050} = 36.72
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\sigma = 36.72 \gg \sigma_c = 0.40$, **cavitation will NOT occur**. The operating point is comfortably above the cavitation inception boundary — the deep ambient pressure raises the cavitation ceiling dramatically. The designer can safely increase blade tip speed to gain more thrust without fear of cavitation damage.

> *(7 marks: formula 2, calculation 2, comparison to $\sigma_c$ 2, conclusion 1)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK-LOSS ALERT — Common Mistakes on Propulsion Problems**
> 1. **Wrong velocity in $J$ formula:** Using $V_\infty$ instead of $V_A = V_\infty(1-w)$ → loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Mixing units:** Cavitation number requires **all pressures in same unit system**. If you keep $p_0$ in bar, convert $\rho V^2$ via $1$ Pa $= 10^{-5}$ bar or convert $p_0$ to Pa first.
> 3. **Forgetting $\eta_R$:** Many students write $\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H$ and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $K_T$ and $K_Q$ powers of $D$:** $K_T \propto D^4$; $K_Q \propto D^5$. Wrong exponent = full-marks loss.
> 5. **Skipping the Froude axial assumption statement:** In derivations, you must explicitly state $V_A = (V_\infty + V_e)/2$ as an *assumption*, not derive it from first principles.
> 6. **Not labeling the three velocity regions** in actuator-disc diagrams: KTU examiners explicitly check for $V_\infty$, $V_A$, $V_e$ labels and the $\Delta p$ arrow.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3: Propulsion**

- 🔹 **Prime mover** for submersibles is typically **electric** (PMSM/IM) due to zero exhaust and low acoustic signature — not diesel.
- 🔹 **Four mandatory sub-systems**: Prime mover → Transmission → Thrust generator → Hull interface.
- 🔹 **Standard reference density**: $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ at 4 °C; $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³.
- 🔹 **Froude ideal efficiency** $\eta_i = 2/(1 + V_e/V_\infty)$ is the upper bound for any thrust device.
- 🔹 **Advance coefficient** $J$ uses wake-corrected velocity $V_A$.
- 🔹 **$K_T$ has $D^4$**; **$K_Q$ has $D^5$** — high-yield exam trap.
- 🔹 **Cavitation number formula**: $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v)/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$ — depth raises $p_0$ linearly (≈ 1 bar/10 m seawater).
- 🔹 **Hull efficiency $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$** — typically > 1 for single-screw submersibles.
- 🔹 **$\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R$** — the overall propulsive figure of merit.
- 🔹 **Ducted (Kort nozzle) propellers** give 20–30% more bollard thrust; used on slow, heavily-loaded ROVs.
- 🔹 **Pump-jets** are preferred for high-speed torpedo-form submersibles due to lower cavitation and reduced acoustic signature.
- 🔹 **MHD propulsion** is theoretical/experimental — produces thrust via Lorentz force on ionized seawater; no moving parts but extremely low thrust density.
- 🔹 **Tip speed limit**: $V_{tip} = \pi n D$ must remain below the cavitation onset velocity for the blade section.
- 🔹 **Wageningen B-Series** polynomial fits give $K_T(J)$, $K_Q(J)$ for standard propellers.
- 🔹 **Always convert all pressures to consistent units** before computing $\sigma$ — this is the #1 numerical pitfall.
- 🔹 **Bollard pull** = maximum thrust at zero forward speed ($V_A = 0$, $J = 0$).

> **END OF MODULE 3 — PROPULSION NOTES**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamics and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Submersible Dynamics and Control?

**Submersible Dynamics and Control** is the branch of ocean engineering that studies the **equations of motion**, **hydrodynamic response**, and **automatic control** of underwater vehicles (submersibles, AUVs, ROVs, and submarines) operating in six degrees of freedom (6-DOF) within a fluid medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> Dynamics and Control of a submersible refers to the mathematical and engineering framework that describes the **time-dependent motion** of a submerged body under the influence of **external forces** (hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, gravitational, propulsive, and environmental disturbances) and the design of **control systems** that govern its trajectory, depth, heading, and attitude to achieve desired operational objectives.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚤

Imagine you are **driving a car in a swimming pool** with your eyes partially closed:
- The water pushes back against every motion (this is **hydrodynamic damping**).
- When you accelerate, you feel "heavier" because the water you are displacing also accelerates (this is **added mass / virtual mass**).
- Your steering is sluggish and your braking distance is huge (this is the **low natural frequency** of underwater vehicles).
- A submarine pilot must therefore "think ahead" because the response to any control input is **slow and delayed**.

This is precisely why **Dynamics and Control** is one of the most critical modules in submersible design — the underwater environment is **highly inertial, heavily damped, and dynamically coupled**.

### 1.2 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)

Every rigid body in 3D space has **six independent motions**. For a submersible, these are:

| DOF | Linear/Angular | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surge (linear) | $x$ | Forward/backward motion along longitudinal axis |
| 2 | Sway (linear) | $y$ | Sideways motion along transverse axis |
| 3 | Heave (linear) | $z$ | Up/down motion along vertical axis |
| 4 | Roll (angular) | $\phi$ | Rotation about longitudinal axis |
| 5 | Pitch (angular) | $\theta$ | Rotation about transverse axis |
| 6 | Yaw (angular) | $\psi$ | Rotation about vertical axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Frame Convention:** KTU and most modern texts use the **SNAME (1950) notation** where:
> - Body-fixed frame: $Oxyz$ moves with the vehicle.
> - Earth-fixed frame: $O_0 X_0 Y_0 Z_0$ is inertial.
> - Velocities: $u, v, w$ (linear); $p, q, r$ (angular).

### 1.3 Why is Control of Submersibles Hard?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Challenges:**
> 1. **High Inertia and Damping** → Slow response, bandwidth limited to **0.1 – 1 Hz** typically.
> 2. **Nonlinear Hydrodynamics** → Equations are coupled and nonlinear; linearization valid only near operating point.
> 3. **Disturbance Sensitivity** → Ocean currents, waves, and density stratification induce unmodeled forces.

The standard control objectives for a submersible are:
- **Depth control** (heave $z$ and pitch $\theta$)
- **Heading control** (yaw $\psi$)
- **Speed control** (surge $u$)
- **Station-keeping** (hovering at fixed point in current)
- **Path following** (trajectory tracking)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Equation of Motion (Newton's 2nd Law in Fluids)

For a rigid body moving in a fluid, Newton's second law becomes:

$$
(m + m_{added}) \dot{\vec{V}} = \sum \vec{F}_{external}
$$

The key insight is that **accelerating the body requires accelerating the surrounding fluid too**, hence the **added mass (virtual mass) term** $m_{added}$.

### 2.2 The Standard 6-DOF Equation of Motion Matrix Form

The complete rigid-body equation of motion in compact matrix form is:

$$
M_{RB}\dot{\nu} + C_{RB}(\nu)\nu = \tau_{RB}
$$

When the submersible is in a fluid, we **augment** this with hydrodynamic terms:

$$
\underbrace{M_{RB}}_{\text{Rigid Body Inertia}} \dot{\nu} + \underbrace{C_{RB}(\nu)\nu}_{\text{Coriolis (rigid)}} + \underbrace{M_A\dot{\nu}}_{\text{Added Mass}} + \underbrace{C_A(\nu)\nu}_{\text{Added Coriolis}} + \underbrace{D(\nu)\nu}_{\text{Damping}} + \underbrace{g(\eta)}_{\text{Restoring}} = \underbrace{\tau}_{\text{Control Input}}
$$

Collecting the inertia terms:

$$
(M_{RB} + M_A)\dot{\nu} + [C_{RB}(\nu) + C_A(\nu) + D(\nu)]\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

Or simply:

$$
M \dot{\nu} + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

where:
- $\nu = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ — body-fixed velocities
- $\eta = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ — earth-fixed position/orientation
- $\tau$ — vector of control forces and moments

### 2.3 Kinematic Equations (Body-to-Earth Transformation)

Velocities in the body frame relate to position derivatives in the earth frame via the **rotation matrix** $J(\eta)$:

$$
\dot{\eta} = J(\eta)\nu
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{z} \\ \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1(\eta) & 0 \\ 0 & J_2(\eta) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ w \\ p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix}
$$

For small angles, $J_2 \approx I_3$ (identity), giving $\dot{\phi} \approx p, \dot{\theta} \approx q, \dot{\psi} \approx r$.

### 2.4 The Hydrodynamic Force Components

Each hydrodynamic force is decomposed as a **linear combination** of the acceleration and velocity terms:

#### (a) Added Mass Forces
$$
F_{added} = -M_A \dot{\nu} - C_A(\nu)\nu
$$

where $M_A$ is a **6×6 symmetric positive-definite matrix** of added mass coefficients:

$$
M_A = -\begin{bmatrix} X_{\dot{u}} & X_{\dot{v}} & X_{\dot{w}} & X_{\dot{p}} & X_{\dot{q}} & X_{\dot{r}} \\ Y_{\dot{u}} & Y_{\dot{v}} & Y_{\dot{w}} & Y_{\dot{p}} & Y_{\dot{q}} & Y_{\dot{r}} \\ Z_{\dot{u}} & Z_{\dot{v}} & Z_{\dot{w}} & Z_{\dot{p}} & Z_{\dot{q}} & Z_{\dot{r}} \\ K_{\dot{u}} & K_{\dot{v}} & K_{\dot{w}} & K_{\dot{p}} & K_{\dot{q}} & K_{\dot{r}} \\ M_{\dot{u}} & M_{\dot{v}} & M_{\dot{w}} & M_{\dot{p}} & M_{\dot{q}} & M_{\dot{r}} \\ N_{\dot{u}} & N_{\dot{v}} & N_{\dot{w}} & N_{\dot{p}} & N_{\dot{q}} & N_{\dot{r}} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **partial derivative notation** $Y_{\dot{v}} = \partial Y / \partial \dot{v}$ is the standard SNAME hydrodynamic derivative.

#### (b) Damping Forces
$$
F_{damping} = -D(\nu)\nu
$$

Damping is split into:
- **Linear (viscous) damping** — proportional to velocity: $-D_l \nu$
- **Quadratic (pressure drag) damping** — proportional to $\vert \nu \vert \nu$: $-D_q \vert \nu \vert \nu$

#### (c) Restoring Forces (Gravity + Buoyancy)
$$
g(\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} (W - B)\sin\theta \\ -(W - B)\cos\theta\sin\phi \\ -(W - B)\cos\theta\cos\phi \\ -(y_g W - y_b B)\cos\theta\cos\phi + (z_g W - z_b B)\cos\theta\sin\phi \\ (z_g W - z_b B)\sin\theta + (x_g W - x_b B)\cos\theta\cos\phi \\ -(x_g W - x_b B)\cos\theta\sin\phi - (y_g W - y_b B)\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $W$ = weight, $B$ = buoyancy force, and $(x_g, y_g, z_g)$, $(x_b, y_b, z_b)$ are the centers of gravity and buoyancy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Condition:** A submersible is **statically stable in roll/pitch** if the metacenter is above the center of gravity, i.e., $\overline{GM} > 0$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Inertia | $M = M_{RB} + M_A$ | kg | Effective inertia in fluid |
| Surge equation | $m\dot{u} - m v r + m w q = X$ | N | Linear surge dynamics |
| Sway equation | $m\dot{v} + m u r - m w p = Y$ | N | Linear sway dynamics |
| Heave equation | $m\dot{w} - m u q + m v p = Z$ | N | Linear heave dynamics |
| Roll equation | $I_{xx}\dot{p} - (I_{yy} - I_{zz}) q r = K$ | N·m | Angular roll |
| Pitch equation | $I_{yy}\dot{q} - (I_{zz} - I_{xx}) r p = M$ | N·m | Angular pitch |
| Yaw equation | $I_{zz}\dot{r} - (I_{xx} - I_{yy}) p q = N$ | N·m | Angular yaw |
| Hydrodynamic lift | $F_L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ | N | Force on control surface |
| Drag force | $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_D$ | N | Resistive force |
| Added mass ratio | $\mu = m_a / m$ | – | Typically **0.1 to 1.5** for submarines |
| Natural heave freq. | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_z / (m + m_{a33})}$ | rad/s | Heave oscillation freq. |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{k(m + m_a)})$ | – | Overshoot indicator |
| Restoring coeff. | $k_z = (W - B)$ (linearized) | N/m | Vertical stiffness |

## 2.6 Decoupled (Single-DOF) Equations — A Key Simplification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **preliminary design** and KTU exam problems, we usually **decouple** the 6-DOF system by symmetry. A typical axisymmetric submersible has:
> - **Surge-Heave-Yaw** are dynamically coupled (forward speed affects heave and yaw).
> - **Sway-Roll-Yaw** are dynamically coupled.
> - **Heave-Pitch** are dynamically coupled.

For an **axisymmetric, neutrally buoyant** body in straight-line motion at constant speed $U$, the **heave and pitch** decouple into 2 independent 2nd-order ODEs.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is used in:
- **Submarine simulator design** (US Navy SUBMAN trainer).
- **AUV path planners** (REMUS, Autosub, Bluefin).
- **ROV dynamic positioning** (oil & gas intervention).
- **Underwater glider control** (Slocum, Spray gliders).
- **Torpedo guidance** and **mine neutralization vehicles**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heave Equation of Motion in Still Water

**Problem Setup:** A neutrally buoyant submersible is operating at depth with small vertical displacements $z$ about an equilibrium. Derive the heave equation in the presence of a heave-control plane with deflection $\delta_e$.

### Step 1 — Identify All Forces in the Heave (z) Direction

Forces acting on the body in the $z$-axis:
1. **Restoring force** due to net buoyancy: $-(W - B)z$ for small displacements
2. **Added mass force**: $-m_{a33}\ddot{z}$
3. **Damping force**: $-Z_w \dot{z}$ (linear) $-Z_{\vert w \vert w}\vert w \vert w$ (quadratic)
4. **Control force from stern plane**: $Z_{\delta_e}\delta_e$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law

$$
(m + m_{a33})\ddot{z} = -(W - B)z - Z_w \dot{z} - Z_{\vert w \vert w}\vert \dot{z} \vert \dot{z} + Z_{\delta_e}\delta_e
$$

Rearranging to standard form:

$$
(m + m_{a33})\ddot{z} + Z_w \dot{z} + (W - B)z = Z_{\delta_e}\delta_e
$$

### Step 3 — Normalize to Standard Second-Order Form

Divide through by $(m + m_{a33})$:

$$
\ddot{z} + \frac{Z_w}{(m + m_{a33})} \dot{z} + \frac{(W - B)}{(m + m_{a33})} z = \frac{Z_{\delta_e}}{(m + m_{a33})} \delta_e
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Standard Coefficients

Comparing with the standard 2nd-order ODE $\ddot{z} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = K\delta_e$:

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{(W - B)}{(m + m_{a33})} = \frac{k_z}{m + m_{a33}}
$$

$$
2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{Z_w}{(m + m_{a33})}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \zeta = \frac{Z_w}{2\sqrt{(W - B)(m + m_{a33})}}
$$

$$
K = \frac{Z_{\delta_e}}{(m + m_{a33})} \quad \text{(control gain)}
$$

### Step 5 — Solution in Laplace Domain (For Control Design)

Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$
[s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2]Z(s) = K \Delta_e(s)
$$

The **transfer function** from stern plane deflection to depth response is:

$$
\frac{Z(s)}{\Delta_e(s)} = \frac{K}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Type-0 second-order system**. The steady-state depth change to a step input is $z_{ss} = K / \omega_n^2 = Z_{\delta_e} / (W - B)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Yaw (Heading) Equation of Motion

For yaw motion $\psi$ of an axisymmetric body, the moment equation reduces to:

$$
(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})\ddot{\psi} + N_r \dot{\psi} + N_{\dot{v}} \dot{v} + N_v v = N_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

**Cross-coupling note:** Yaw dynamics is coupled with sway velocity $v$. The simplified form for steady turn is:

$$
(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})\ddot{\psi} + N_r \dot{\psi} = N_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

Normalizing:

$$
\ddot{\psi} + 2\zeta_{\psi}\omega_{\psi}\dot{\psi} = \frac{N_{\delta_r}}{(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})}\delta_r
$$

where the yaw rate time constant is:

$$
T_{\psi} = \frac{(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})}{N_r}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yaw stability criterion** (for stable heading without continuous rudder input):
> $$N_v > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad N_r > 0$$
> This is the **dynamic stability** requirement; it is **opposite in sign** to surface ship stability requirements due to the absence of the metacentric term.

## 3.3 Derivation: Surge Dynamics with Propeller Thrust

The surge equation in calm water with a single-screw propeller:

$$
(m + X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} - m v r + m w q = X_T - R(u) + X_{vv}v^2 + X_{ww}w^2 + \dots
$$

For straight-line motion ($v = w = 0$, $p = q = r = 0$):

$$
(m + X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} = X_T - R(u)
$$

Where:
- $X_T$ = thrust force from propeller
- $R(u) = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A u^2$ = resistance force

The thrust from a propeller is approximated by the **wageningen-style** relation:

$$
X_T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_T(J)
$$

where $J = u/(nD)$ is the advance coefficient, $n$ is the RPM, $D$ is the propeller diameter.

At steady state ($\dot{u} = 0$):

$$
X_T = R(u) \Rightarrow \rho n^2 D^4 K_T(J) = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D A u^2
$$

This gives the equilibrium operating speed.

## 3.4 Stability Analysis — Linearized Decoupled Heave System

### Step 1 — Characteristic Equation

The characteristic polynomial is:

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Roots (Eigenvalues)

$$
s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}
$$

### Step 3 — Stability Conditions

For **asymptotic stability**, both roots must have negative real parts, i.e.:

$$
\zeta > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_n^2 > 0
$$

This requires:
- $Z_w > 0$ (damping positive)
- $W - B > 0$ (submarine is **positively buoyant** for stable equilibrium, OR use dynamic depth-keeping)

> [!WARNING]
> Most operational submersibles are designed **neutrally or slightly negatively buoyant** to avoid runaway surfacing. In this case, $\omega_n^2 < 0$ and the system is **inherently unstable in heave** — this is why **active depth control is mandatory** for submersibles.

## 3.5 The PID Depth Controller — Standard Control Law

For active depth control, the stern plane deflection is commanded as:

$$
\delta_e(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where $e(t) = z_{desired}(t) - z_{actual}(t)$ is the depth error.

In the Laplace domain:

$$
\Delta_e(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right)E(s) = K_{PID}(s)E(s)
$$

The **closed-loop transfer function** becomes:

$$
\frac{Z(s)}{Z_d(s)} = \frac{K_{PID}(s) \cdot K_{plant}}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 + K_{PID}(s)K_{plant}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typical answer: The **PID gains** are tuned using:
> - **Ziegler-Nichols** method, OR
> - **Pole-placement** (place dominant pole at $\zeta \approx 0.7$ for optimal step response), OR
> - **LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)** for multi-variable case.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Key Equations at a Glance

| Equation | Compact Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Surge | $m_u \dot{u} = X_T - R(u)$ | Straight-line speed control |
| Heave | $m_{33}\ddot{z} + Z_w \dot{z} + (W-B)z = Z_{\delta_e}\delta_e$ | Depth control |
| Pitch | $I_{55}\ddot{\theta} + M_q \dot{\theta} + M_\theta \theta = M_{\delta_e}\delta_e$ | Pitch control |
| Yaw | $I_{66}\ddot{\psi} + N_r \dot{\psi} = N_{\delta_r}\delta_r$ | Heading control |
| Kinematic | $\dot{\eta} = J(\eta)\nu$ | Position update |
| TF (depth) | $G(s) = \frac{K}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$ | Control synthesis |
