# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the six degrees of freedom of a submersible. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**
The six degrees of freedom of a rigid-body submersible in 3D space, as per **SNAME (1950) notation**, are:

| # | DOF | Symbol | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surge | $u$ | Linear | Forward motion along $x$-axis |
| 2 | Sway | $v$ | Linear | Sideways motion along $y$-axis |
| 3 | Heave | $w$ | Linear | Vertical motion along $z$-axis |
| 4 | Roll | $p, \phi$ | Angular | Rotation about $x$-axis |
| 5 | Pitch | $q, \theta$ | Angular | Rotation about $y$-axis |
| 6 | Yaw | $r, \psi$ | Angular | Rotation about $z$-axis |

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU):**
- 6 DOF correctly named: **2 marks**
- Symbol and type (linear/angular): **1 mark**

---

### Question 2: What is added (virtual) mass in the context of submersible dynamics? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Added mass (virtual mass)** is the inertia added to the system because a body accelerating in a fluid must also accelerate the surrounding fluid particles. Mathematically, the added mass $m_a$ is defined through the relation:
> $$\text{Force on fluid} = -m_a \dot{V}$$

**Key Points to Score Full Marks:**
1. **Definition** (1 mark): Inertia of the fluid that must be accelerated along with the body.
2. **Mathematical expression** (1 mark): $F_{added} = -m_a \dot{V}$ or appears in the equation as $m_a \dot{u}$.
3. **Practical significance** (1 mark): For a typical submarine, the **surge added mass ratio** $\mu = m_a/m \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$; effective inertia in water is **10–30% higher** than in air.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. The pattern is (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Below are TWO complete alternative questions.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): Depth Control of a Submersible `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the equation of motion for the heave (depth) direction of a neutrally buoyant submersible, clearly identifying the added mass, hydrodynamic damping, restoring, and control terms.

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram and Force Identification (2 marks)
Forces on the body in the heave ($z$) direction:
- Gravity (weight) $W$ acting downward
- Buoyancy $B$ acting upward
- Hydrodynamic added mass reaction: $-m_{a33}\ddot{z}$
- Viscous damping: $-Z_w \dot{z}$
- Control force from stern plane: $Z_{\delta_e} \delta_e$

#### Step 2 — Application of Newton's Second Law (2 marks)
For small vertical displacement $z$ from equilibrium depth:

$$
(m + m_{a33})\ddot{z} = -(W - B)z - Z_w \dot{z} + Z_{\delta_e}\delta_e
$$

#### Step 3 — Rearrangement to Standard Form (1 mark)
$$
(m + m_{a33})\ddot{z} + Z_w \dot{z} + (W - B)z = Z_{\delta_e}\delta_e
$$

#### Step 4 — Identification of Coefficients (2 marks)
- Effective mass: $m_{eff} = m + m_{a33}$
- Damping coefficient: $Z_w > 0$
- Restoring stiffness: $K_z = (W - B)$
- Control derivative: $Z_{\delta_e}$

Final standard form:

$$
\ddot{z} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = \frac{Z_{\delta_e}}{m_{eff}}\delta_e
$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{(W-B)/m_{eff}}$ and $\zeta = Z_w / (2\sqrt{(W-B)m_{eff}})$.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For the heave equation derived above, with the following data:
- $m = 5000$ kg, $m_{a33} = 4500$ kg
- $W = 49000$ N, $B = 49050$ N
- $Z_w = 1200$ N·s/m, $Z_{\delta_e} = 800$ N/rad

Compute: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the damping ratio, and (iii) comment on the stability of the heave response.

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Effective Mass (1 mark)
$$
m_{eff} = m + m_{a33} = 5000 + 4500 = 9500 \text{ kg}
$$

#### Step 2 — Natural Frequency (2 marks)
$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{(W - B)}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{49000 - 49050}{9500}}
$$

Note that $W - B = -50$ N. The net buoyancy is **negative** (the body is slightly heavy).

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{-50}{9500} = -5.263 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_n = j \cdot 0.0726 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\text{imaginary})}
$$

This is a **purely imaginary natural frequency**, indicating an **inherently unstable** heave mode.

#### Step 3 — Damping Ratio (2 marks)
$$
\zeta = \frac{Z_w}{2\sqrt{(W - B)(m_{eff})}}
$$

Since $(W - B) < 0$, the term under the square root is **negative**:

$$
\zeta = \frac{1200}{2 \times j\sqrt{50 \times 9500}} = \frac{1200}{j \times 1378.4}
$$

Damping ratio is **imaginary** → system is **unstable**.

#### Step 4 — Stability Comment (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The heave response of this submersible is **open-loop unstable** because the net buoyancy is **negative** ($W > B$). The body will sink continuously without active depth control. A **closed-loop PID controller** is mandatory to maintain depth.

Forced controller design:
- Compute $K_{PID}$ via pole-placement targeting $\omega_d = 0.5$ rad/s, $\zeta_{cl} = 0.7$.
- Stern plane authority must be sufficient: $Z_{\delta_e}\delta_{e,max} > \vert W - B \vert = 50$ N.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Yaw Dynamics and Heading Control `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the simplified yaw equation of motion for an axisymmetric submersible moving at constant forward speed, and identify the conditions for **dynamic heading stability**.

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Starting Point: 6-DOF Yaw Equation (1 mark)
$$
(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})\dot{r} + N_r r + N_v v + N_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + (\text{Coriolis terms}) = N_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Steady Forward Speed Assumption (1 mark)
For steady forward surge $u_0 = U$ and small yaw rate $r$, Coriolis terms simplify. The sway $v$ couples to yaw via the centrifugal term.

#### Step 3 — Substitute Sway Equilibrium (2 marks)
For steady turn at constant $\dot{\psi} = r$ and $v \approx 0$ (no sideslip), the **yaw equation** reduces to:

$$
(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})\ddot{\psi} + N_r \dot{\psi} = N_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

#### Step 4 — Identify Time Constant (1 mark)
$$
T_r = \frac{I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}}{N_r}
$$

#### Step 5 — Dynamic Heading Stability Conditions (2 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **dynamic heading stability** (the submarine returns to straight-line motion after a disturbance), the following must hold:
> $$N_v > 0 \quad \text{(positive weather-vane stability)}$$
> $$N_r > 0 \quad \text{(positive yaw damping)}$$
> The submarine behaves like a "weather vane" — any yaw rate produces a restoring moment that aligns the body with the flow.

If $N_v < 0$, the submarine exhibits **directional instability** (similar to a Dart missile that needs spin for stability).

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For an axisymmetric submersible with $I_{zz} = 1500$ kg·m², $N_{\dot{r}} = 800$ kg·m², $N_r = 2500$ N·m·s/rad, and rudder control derivative $N_{\delta_r} = 1500$ N·m/rad, determine:
(i) the yaw time constant $T_r$,
(ii) the steady-state yaw rate for a step rudder input of 10°,
(iii) whether the system requires active heading control.

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Time Constant (2 marks)
$$
T_r = \frac{I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}}{N_r} = \frac{1500 + 800}{2500} = \frac{2300}{2500}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_r = 0.92 \text{ s}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Steady-State Yaw Rate (3 marks)
At steady state ($\ddot{\psi} = 0, \dot{\psi} = r_{ss}$):

$$
N_r \cdot r_{ss} = N_{\delta_r} \delta_r
$$

$$
r_{ss} = \frac{N_{\delta_r}}{N_r} \delta_r = \frac{1500}{2500} \times \left(10 \times \frac{\pi}{180}\right)
$$

$$
r_{ss} = 0.6 \times 0.1745
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{ss} = 0.1047 \text{ rad/s} \approx 6°/\text{s}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Active Control Requirement (2 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The yaw dynamics is **open-loop stable** (since $N_r > 0$ and $N_v > 0$ assumed), so the heading will settle naturally after a disturbance. However, for **precise heading control** during missions, a **closed-loop autopilot** is still used. The time constant of **0.92 s** is very fast for a submarine, indicating good maneuverability.

The transfer function from rudder angle to yaw rate is:

$$
\frac{\Psi(s)}{\Delta_r(s)} = \frac{N_{\delta_r}/(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})}{s[s + 1/T_r]} = \frac{0.652}{s(s + 1.087)}
$$

This is a **Type-1 system** (integrator in the open loop), so **ramp-free** heading error is achievable in closed loop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing frame of reference**: Always specify whether velocity is in body frame ($\nu$) or earth frame ($\dot{\eta}$). Mixing them is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting added mass**: Writing the heave equation as $m\ddot{z}$ instead of $(m + m_{a33})\ddot{z}$ loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Sign convention error in restoring force**: The heave restoring is $-(W-B)z$, not $+(W-B)z$. The negative sign indicates the **direction of force**.
> 4. **Skipping units**: Always state units (N, rad/s, kg·m²) in derivations. Unit errors cost **1 mark**.
> 5. **Linearization justification**: When using the small-angle assumption, **explicitly state it**. Examiners expect phrases like "for small perturbation $\theta \ll 1$, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$".
> 6. **PID tuning without stating method**: Specify **Ziegler-Nichols** OR **Pole-placement** OR **LQR** when designing controllers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dynamics and Control of Submersibles**

- ☐ **6-DOF** are: Surge, Sway, Heave, Roll, Pitch, Yaw (SNAME 1950 notation).
- ☐ **General EOM**: $M\dot{\nu} + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau$
- ☐ **Kinematics**: $\dot{\eta} = J(\eta)\nu$
- ☐ **Added mass** = inertia of fluid that moves with the body; **always positive** for stable configuration.
- ☐ **Damping** has **linear** ($D_l \nu$) and **quadratic** ($D_q \vert\nu\vert \nu$) components.
- ☐ **Restoring forces** depend on **weight ($W$), buoyancy ($B$), and their centers of action (CG, CB)**.
- ☐ **Static stability** requires $\overline{GM} > 0$ (metacenter above CG).
- ☐ **Dynamic stability in yaw** requires $N_v > 0$ and $N_r > 0$ (weather-vane effect).
- ☐ **Heave equation** in standard form: $\ddot{z} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{z} + \omega_n^2 z = K\delta_e$
- ☐ **Transfer function** for depth: $G(s) = K / (s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$ — a second-order underdamped system.
- ☐ **Natural frequency** of heave: $\omega_n = \sqrt{(W - B)/m_{eff}}$
- ☐ Most operational submersibles have **$W \approx B$** (near-neutral buoyancy) → heave is **inherently marginally stable** → **closed-loop depth control is mandatory**.
- ☐ **Control surfaces**: Stern planes → pitch + heave; Bow planes → trim + depth hold; Rudder → yaw.
- ☐ **PID controller** is the workhorse; tuning methods include Ziegler-Nichols, pole-placement, and LQR.
- ☐ **Ocean disturbances** (currents, waves) enter as **additive disturbances** in the equation of motion.
- ☐ **SNAME hydrodynamic derivatives**: $X_{\dot{u}}, Y_v, Z_w, K_p, M_q, N_r$ are diagonal-dominant terms; off-diagonal terms represent **cross-coupling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mantra for KTU Viva:**
> *"Submersible dynamics is a coupled, nonlinear, six-DOF problem dominated by added mass and damping — and most submersibles are designed neutrally buoyant, making active control absolutely essential."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of Motion of a Submarine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion of a Submarine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equation of Motion (EOM) of a Submarine** is the set of six coupled, non-linear, second-order ordinary differential equations that completely describe the dynamic behaviour of a free-flooding, axi-symmetric, buoyant body translating and rotating under water. These six equations correspond to the **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)**: three translations along the body-fixed axes (*Surge, Sway, Heave*) and three rotations about those axes (*Roll, Pitch, Yaw*).

In the **SNAME (Society of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers) 1950 notation**, the equations are written in vector–matrix form as:

$$ \mathbf{M}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + \mathbf{C}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\,\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\,\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} $$

where $\boldsymbol{\nu} = [u,\,v,\,w,\,p,\,q,\,r]^T$ is the body-fixed velocity vector and $\boldsymbol{\eta} = [x,\,y,\,z,\,\phi,\,\theta,\,\psi]^T$ is the Earth-fixed position/orientation vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The EOM of a submarine is the *foundational design tool* used in manoeuvring, control-system design, stability analysis, and trajectory simulation. Every subsequent module (control surfaces, ballast systems, hydroplanes) builds upon these equations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Kite"

Imagine a **kite** tethered to your hand on a windy beach. The kite has:
- A **mass** (the body of the kite).
- An **invisible water-balloon** of water that has to be pushed aside every time the kite moves (**added mass**).
- **Air resistance** proportional to its speed (**damping**).
- A **string tension** that tries to keep it in place (**restoring force** from weight & buoyancy).
- Your hand pulling it (**propulsion thrust**).
- Six ways you can move it — push it left, right, up, down, twist it, or tilt it.

A submarine is the **same physics, underwater, in 3-D**. The EOM is the *mathematical receipt* that balances all these effects — inertia of the hull, inertia of the displaced water, hydrodynamic resistance, gravitational pull, buoyant lift, propeller thrust, and rudder/hydroplane forces — to predict exactly how the vessel will accelerate, decelerate, and turn.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Submarine Class) | Unit |
|----------|--------|-----------------------------------|------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | $\mathrm{m/s^2}$ |
| Sea-water density | $\rho$ | **1025** | $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ |
| Submerged displacement | $\Delta$ | 1,000 – 30,000 | tonnes |
| Submerged speed | $U$ | 8 – 35 | knots |
| Length overall | $L_{OA}$ | 60 – 170 | $\mathrm{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The EOM is valid for any rigid body operating in a *Newtonian fluid* under the assumption of an *Earth-fixed, inertial reference frame* combined with a *body-fixed, non-inertial reference frame* moving with the vessel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Reference Frames

To derive the EOM, two Cartesian frames are used:

| Frame | Origin | Axes | Purpose |
|-------|--------|------|---------|
| **Earth-Fixed (E-frame)** | Arbitrary point on Earth | $X_E,\,Y_E,\,Z_E$ (with $Z_E$ pointing **upward**) | Tracks absolute position; measures position/attitude |
| **Body-Fixed (B-frame)** | Vehicle centre of gravity (CG) | $x_B,\,y_B,\,z_B$ (with $x_B$ along the longitudinal axis pointing **forward**) | Measures velocities, accelerations, and applies forces |

The transformation between the two frames uses the **Euler-angle rotation matrix** $R_z(\psi)R_y(\theta)R_x(\phi)$:

$$ \boldsymbol{\dot{\eta}} = \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\eta})\,\boldsymbol{\nu} $$

where $\mathbf{J}$ is the **kinematic Jacobian**.

## 2.2 Six Degrees of Freedom — SNAME Notation

| DOF | Translation / Rotation | Velocity Symbol | Force / Moment |
|-----|-----------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Surge** — forward translation along $x_B$ | $u$ | $X$ |
| 2 | **Sway** — lateral translation along $y_B$ | $v$ | $Y$ |
| 3 | **Heave** — vertical translation along $z_B$ | $w$ | $Z$ |
| 4 | **Roll** — rotation about $x_B$ | $p$ | $K$ |
| 5 | **Pitch** — rotation about $y_B$ | $q$ | $M$ |
| 6 | **Yaw** — rotation about $z_B$ | $r$ | $N$ |

## 2.3 Components of the EOM — A Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Apply Newton's Second Law in the B-frame.**
   The acceleration of the CG in the B-frame is *not* simply $\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}}$ because the B-frame itself is rotating. We must include **Coriolis and centripetal terms**.
2. **Account for Added Mass.**
   A submarine accelerating underwater must also accelerate a *virtual mass of water* around it. This is modelled by the **added-mass matrix** $\mathbf{M}_A$, which is symmetric and positive-definite.
3. **Account for Damping.**
   Water resists motion. The **damping matrix** $\mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ is non-linear and may be a function of speed (linear, quadratic, or higher).
4. **Account for Restoring Forces.**
   Weight $W$ acts downward; buoyancy $B$ acts upward. Any separation between the centre of gravity (G) and the centre of buoyancy (C) creates a **restoring moment** when the submarine tilts.
5. **Account for Control Inputs.**
   Thrust from the propeller, deflections of the **stern planes** and **fairwater planes** (rudder, hydroplanes, dive planes).
6. **Sum to zero** — the total force/moment on the body must equal mass × acceleration.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M}\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + \mathbf{C}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | **General 6-DOF EOM** | Always the starting point |
| 2 | $X = m(\dot{u} - vr + wq)$ | **Surge** | Solve for $\dot{u}$ |
| 3 | $Y = m(\dot{v} + ur - wp)$ | **Sway** | Couple with roll/yaw |
| 4 | $Z = m(\dot{w} - uq + vp)$ | **Heave** | Couple with pitch |
| 5 | $K = I_{xx}\dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy})qr$ | **Roll** | Subscripts $x$ refer to body axis |
| 6 | $M = I_{yy}\dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz})pr$ | **Pitch** | Couples with heave |
| 7 | $N = I_{zz}\dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})pq$ | **Yaw** | Couples with sway |
| 8 | $X_H = X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + X_{uu}u^2 + X_{vv}v^2 + X_{ww}w^2 + \cdots$ | **Hydrodynamic surge force** | Express as polynomial in velocities |
| 9 | $X_{\dot{u}} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\rho \pi L^2 \cdot k_1$ | **Surge added-mass coefficient** | $k_1 \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ for slender bodies |
| 10 | $\mathbf{M}_A = \mathbf{M}_A^T > 0$ | **Added-mass matrix properties** | Symmetric, positive-definite |
| 11 | $\mathbf{C}(\boldsymbol{\nu}) = -\mathbf{C}^T(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ | **Coriolis matrix skew-symmetry** | Energy-conservation check |
| 12 | $W = mg$, $\quad B = \rho g \nabla$ | **Weight & Buoyancy** | Used in $\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta})$ |
| 13 | $GB = \overline{GC}$ (metacentric) | **Stability condition** | Restoring moment $\propto GB\sin\phi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Trap:** The *added mass* always appears with a **negative sign** in hydrodynamic force models, because it represents the **reaction** of the fluid on the body. Forgetting this sign is a 7-mark killer in ESE.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 6-DOF EOM is the backbone of:
- **Manoeuvring simulations** (STANDARD Manoeuvres — turning circle, zig-zag, crash-stop).
- **Control-system design** — autopilot algorithms for depth, heading, and pitch.
- **Stability analysis** — both **static** (restoring from tilt) and **dynamic** (oscillation damping).
- **Crew safety** — predicting maximum depth, emergency-surge capability, and crash survival.
- **Stealth design** — minimising radiated noise requires understanding the EOM-derived accelerations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Newton's Second Law in a Rotating (Body-Fixed) Frame

### Step 1: Position vectors in the B-frame

Let the position of a generic point *P* on the submarine (mass $dm$) in the B-frame be:
$$ \mathbf{r}_P = x\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + y\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} + z\,\hat{\mathbf{k}} $$

The unit vectors $\hat{\mathbf{i}},\hat{\mathbf{j}},\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ are **rotating** with the body, so their time derivatives in the E-frame are not zero.

### Step 2: Velocity of point P

Taking the time derivative, the velocity of *P* relative to the E-frame, expressed in the B-frame, is:

$$ \mathbf{v}_P = \frac{{}^{E}d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt} = \frac{{}^{B}d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P $$

where $\boldsymbol{\omega} = p\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + q\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} + r\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ is the angular velocity of the B-frame.

Since $\frac{{}^{B}d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt} = \dot{x}\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \dot{y}\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \dot{z}\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}$, we have:

$$ \mathbf{v}_P = (\dot{x} + qz - ry)\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + (\dot{y} + rx - pz)\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} + (\dot{z} + py - qx)\,\hat{\mathbf{k}} $$

### Step 3: Acceleration of point P

Differentiating once more:

$$ \mathbf{a}_P = \frac{{}^{E}d\mathbf{v}_P}{dt} = \frac{{}^{B}d\mathbf{v}_P}{dt} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}_P $$

Working out all 12 terms component-by-component, the **i-component** of $\mathbf{a}_P$ becomes:

$$ a_{Px} = \ddot{x} + \dot{q}z - \dot{r}y + q(\dot{z} + py - qx) - r(\dot{y} + rx - pz) $$

Grouping terms for the centre of mass ($x=y=z=0$ at the CG):

$$ a_{Px}\vert_{CG} = \ddot{x} + q\dot{z} - r\dot{y} $$

### Step 4: Newton's Second Law for translation

Multiplying by $dm$ and integrating over the entire mass $m$:

$$ \int a_{Px}\,dm = m(\ddot{x} + q\dot{z} - r\dot{y}) $$

Equating to the total external force $X$ acting along $x_B$:

$$ X = m(\dot{u} + qw - rv) $$

> This is the **Surge Equation of Motion** (Equation (2) in the cheat-sheet table).

### Step 5: Repeat for sway and heave

By cyclic permutation of $(u,v,w)$ and $(p,q,r)$:

$$ Y = m(\dot{v} + ru - pw) $$

$$ Z = m(\dot{w} + pv - qu) $$

### Step 6: Euler's Equations for rotation

For the rotational EOM, the **angular momentum** about the CG is $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}$. The general form of **Euler's equations** in the B-frame is:

$$ \frac{{}^{E}d\mathbf{H}}{dt} = \frac{{}^{B}d\mathbf{H}}{dt} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{M}_{ext} $$

Expanding $\frac{{}^{B}d\mathbf{H}}{dt} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$, and noting that for a body without products of inertia $\mathbf{I} = \mathrm{diag}(I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz})$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
K &= I_{xx}\dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy})qr \\
M &= I_{yy}\dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz})pr \\
N &= I_{zz}\dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})pq
\end{aligned} $$

### Step 7: Adding hydrodynamic reaction terms

The "external" force $X$ is the **net** force on the hull — the sum of **gravity, buoyancy, propulsion, control-surface, and hydrodynamic reaction**. We decompose the hydrodynamic part using a **Taylor series expansion** in the velocities and accelerations:

$$ \begin{aligned}
X_H &= X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + X_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + X_{\dot{w}}\dot{w} + X_{\dot{p}}\dot{p} + X_{\dot{q}}\dot{q} + X_{\dot{r}}\dot{r} \\
    &\quad + X_{uu}u^2 + X_{vv}v^2 + X_{ww}w^2 + X_{rr}r^2 + X_{qq}q^2 + \cdots
\end{aligned} $$

Where:
- $X_{\dot{u}}, X_{\dot{v}}, \ldots$ = **added-mass derivatives** (units of mass or moment of inertia).
- $X_{uu}, X_{vv}, \ldots$ = **damping derivatives** (units of force per unit velocity²).

### Step 8: Substituting into the rigid-body EOM

For the surge direction, for example, the **complete** equation becomes:

$$ \begin{aligned}
&(m - X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} + \text{(coupling terms)} + \text{(damping)} + \text{(restoring)} \\
&\quad = X_{P} + X_{C}
\end{aligned} $$

where $X_P$ is the propeller-thrust contribution and $X_C$ is the control-surface (rudder/stern-plane) contribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign on $X_{\dot{u}}$ reflects the **opposition** of the fluid to acceleration. Hence the **effective surge mass** becomes $m - X_{\dot{u}} = m + m_{11}$, where $m_{11} = -X_{\dot{u}}$ is the **surge added mass** (always positive in SI units).

### Step 9: Final compact matrix form

Grouping all 6 equations:

$$ (\mathbf{M}_{RB} + \mathbf{M}_A)\dot{\boldsymbol{\nu}} + \mathbf{C}_{RB}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{C}_A(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})\boldsymbol{\nu} + \mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} $$

With:
- $\mathbf{M}_{RB}$ = rigid-body mass matrix.
- $\mathbf{M}_A$ = added-mass matrix.
- $\mathbf{C}_{RB}, \mathbf{C}_A$ = rigid-body and added-mass Coriolis-centripetal matrices.
- $\mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$ = hydrodynamic damping matrix.
- $\mathbf{g}(\boldsymbol{\eta})$ = gravity-buoyancy restoring vector.
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ = vector of external control forces and moments.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coordinate Frame and 6-DOF Layout

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the *functional architecture* of how the body-fixed frame, the 6-DOF variables, and the force-moment vector interact to form the closed-loop EOM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Submarine CG, Origin of B-frame] --> B1[Surge DOF: u, X]
    A0 --> B2[Sway DOF: v, Y]
    A0 --> B3[Heave DOF: w, Z]
    A0 --> B4[Roll DOF: p, K]
    A0 --> B5[Pitch DOF: q, M]
    A0 --> B6[Yaw DOF: r, N]

    B1 --> C1[Rigid Body Inertia, m]
    B2 --> C2[Rigid Body Inertia, m]
    B3 --> C3[Rigid Body Inertia, m]
    B4 --> C4[Rigid Body Inertia, Ixx]
    B5 --> C5[Rigid Body Inertia, Iyy]
    B6 --> C6[Rigid Body Inertia, Izz]

    C1 --> D1[Coriolis Centripetal Coupling]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Added Mass MA]
    E1 --> F1[Hydrodynamic Damping D nu]
    F1 --> G1[Restoring Forces, Weight minus Buoyancy]
    G1 --> H1[External Inputs: Propeller Thrust, Rudder, Hydroplanes]
    H1 --> I1[Final 6 DOF Equation of Motion]
    I1 --> J1[Acceleration Vector: u-dot, v-dot, w-dot, p-dot, q-dot, r-dot]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Force Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HULL[Submarine Hull]
        H1[G, Centre of Gravity]
        H2[C, Centre of Buoyancy]
    end

    W[Weight, W = mg, downward] --> H1
    BU[Buoyancy, B = rho g nabla, upward] --> H2

    H1 --> MOM[Restoring Moment, M_restoring = W times GC-bar times sin phi]
    H2 --> MOM

    PROP[Propeller Thrust, T, along xB] --> NET[Net Force, F_net]
    RUD[Rudder Deflection, delta_r, yaw moment] --> NET
    SPL[Stern Plane Deflection, delta_s, pitch moment] --> NET
    FWP[Fairwater Plane, delta_f, pitch moment] --> NET

    NET --> EOM[Equation of Motion, M nu-dot + C nu + D nu + g eta = tau]
    EOM --> OUT[Submarine Trajectory, position and attitude]
```

## 4.3 Coupling Map Between DOFs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURGE[Surge, u] --- SWAY[Sway, v]
    SURGE --- HEAVE[Heave, w]
    SURGE --- YAW[Yaw, r]
    SWAY --- ROLL[Roll, p]
    SWAY --- YAW
    HEAVE --- PITCH[Pitch, q]
    ROLL --- YAW
    PITCH --- HEAVE
    PITCH --- YAW
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows above show the **strongest dynamic couplings**. Notice that *surge is weakly coupled*, but *sway–roll–yaw* and *heave–pitch* form **two strongly coupled subsystems**. This is why control engineers design **two independent autopilots** (depth-pitch and heading-yaw) for submarines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Define the equation of motion of a submarine. List the six degrees of freedom considered in the analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Equation of Motion (EOM) of a submarine is the set of six coupled, non-linear, second-order differential equations that describe the translational and rotational dynamics of the vessel in a fluid medium. The six degrees of freedom (per SNAME 1950 notation) are:
1. **Surge** — forward translation $u$ along $x_B$.
2. **Sway** — lateral translation $v$ along $y_B$.
3. **Heave** — vertical translation $w$ along $z_B$.
4. **Roll** — rotation $p$ about $x_B$.
5. **Pitch** — rotation $q$ about $y_B$.
6. **Yaw** — rotation $r$ about $z_B$.

*(1 Mark — Definition; 1 Mark — Translation DOFs; 1 Mark — Rotational DOFs.)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*What is added mass? Why is it significant in submarine dynamics?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Added mass** (also called *hydrodynamic mass* or *virtual mass*) is the inertial resistance offered by the fluid that must be accelerated together with the body when it changes its motion. It is a consequence of the pressure distribution that builds up in the surrounding water.

**Significance:**
- It **increases the effective inertia** of the submarine — sometimes by 10–30 % of the rigid-body mass.
- It introduces **velocity-dependent coupling** between the DOFs.
- It is **symmetric and positive-definite** — a property exploited in stability proofs.
- Forgetting the added mass leads to a **stiff, oscillatory, non-physical** simulation response.

*(1 Mark — Definition; 2 Marks — Significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

> **(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a submarine in the **surge** direction, starting from Newton's second law applied in a body-fixed, rotating reference frame. Clearly state each term and explain its physical meaning. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A submarine of mass $m = 5{,}000\,\mathrm{tonnes}$ undergoes a steady acceleration. The surge added-mass coefficient is $X_{\dot{u}} = -2.5 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{kg}$, and the quadratic damping coefficient is $X_{uu} = -4.0 \times 10^3\,\mathrm{N \cdot s^2 / m^2}$. The propeller develops a thrust of $T = 200\,\mathrm{kN}$ at a steady speed of $u = 6\,\mathrm{m/s}$. Compute the **steady-state acceleration** of the submarine. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

Starting with the B-frame acceleration of the CG obtained in **Section 3.1 (Step 4)**:

$$ X = m(\dot{u} + qw - rv) $$

Expanding $X$ as the sum of *propulsion*, *control*, *hydrodynamic*, and *gravity-buoyancy* components:

$$ X = X_P + X_C + X_H + X_{g-b} $$

In straight-line motion (no roll, pitch, or yaw), $v = w = p = q = r = 0$, so the equation reduces to:

$$ m\dot{u} = X_P + X_C + X_H $$

Expanding $X_H$ with the relevant added-mass and damping terms:

$$ X_H = X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + X_u\,u + X_{uu}\,u^2 $$

Substituting:

$$ m\dot{u} = T + X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + X_u\,u + X_{uu}\,u^2 $$

Rearranging to isolate $\dot{u}$:

$$ (m - X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} - X_u\,u - X_{uu}\,u^2 = T $$

Therefore, the **surge equation of motion** of a submarine is:

$$ (m + m_{11})\dot{u} + X_u\,u + X_{uu}\,u^2 = T $$

where $m_{11} = -X_{\dot{u}}$ is the **surge added mass**.

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $(m + m_{11})\dot{u}$ → total inertia (rigid-body + fluid) × acceleration.
- $X_u\,u$ → linear damping (viscous skin friction).
- $X_{uu}\,u^2$ → quadratic damping (form drag, vortex shedding).
- $T$ → external thrust.

*(7 Marks — 3 for derivation, 2 for substitution, 2 for physical interpretation.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $m = 5{,}000\,\mathrm{tonnes} = 5 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{kg}$
- $X_{\dot{u}} = -2.5 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{kg}$, so $m_{11} = 2.5 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{kg}$
- $X_{uu} = -4.0 \times 10^3\,\mathrm{N \cdot s^2 / m^2}$
- $T = 200\,\mathrm{kN} = 2 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{N}$
- Steady speed $u = 6\,\mathrm{m/s}$
- Assuming $X_u \approx 0$ for simplicity (problem statement does not provide it).

**Equation (from part a):**

$$ (m + m_{11})\dot{u} = T + X_{uu}\,u^2 $$

**Step 1 — Compute the effective mass:**

$$ m + m_{11} = 5 \times 10^6 + 2.5 \times 10^5 = 5.25 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{kg} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the damping force:**

$$ X_{uu}\,u^2 = (-4.0 \times 10^3)(6)^2 = -4.0 \times 10^3 \times 36 = -1.44 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{N} $$

**Step 3 — Net accelerating force:**

$$ F_{net} = T + X_{uu}\,u^2 = 2 \times 10^5 - 1.44 \times 10^5 = 5.6 \times 10^4\,\mathrm{N} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the steady-state acceleration:**

$$ \dot{u} = \frac{F_{net}}{m + m_{11}} = \frac{5.6 \times 10^4}{5.25 \times 10^6} = 0.01067\,\mathrm{m/s^2} $$

$$ \boxed{\dot{u} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-2}\,\mathrm{m/s^2} } $$

*(7 Marks — 2 for data extraction, 2 for correct effective mass, 1 for damping term, 2 for final answer with correct units.)*

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

> **(a)** Write the **complete 6-DOF equations of motion** of a submarine in the body-fixed frame using SNAME notation. For each equation, identify and explain the **rigid-body**, **Coriolis-centripetal**, **added-mass**, **damping**, and **restoring** terms. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A submarine is executing a steady **turn at constant speed and constant yaw rate**, with $u = U$, $v = w = 0$, $p = q = 0$, $r = r_0$ (constant). The lateral added-mass derivative in sway is $Y_{\dot{v}} = -1.0 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{kg}$, and the mass is $m = 4{,}000\,\mathrm{tonnes}$. Determine the **lateral force** $Y$ required to sustain the steady turn. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Translational DOFs:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
X &= m(\dot{u} - vr + wq) + X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + X_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + X_{\dot{w}}\dot{w} + X_{D} + X_R \\
Y &= m(\dot{v} + ur - wp) + Y_{\dot{u}}\dot{u} + Y_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + Y_{\dot{r}}\dot{r} + Y_{D} + Y_R \\
Z &= m(\dot{w} - uq + vp) + Z_{\dot{w}}\dot{w} + Z_{\dot{q}}\dot{q} + Z_{D} + Z_R
\end{aligned} $$

**Rotational DOFs:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
K &= I_{xx}\dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy})qr + K_{\dot{p}}\dot{p} + K_{D} + K_R \\
M &= I_{yy}\dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz})pr + M_{\dot{q}}\dot{q} + M_{D} + M_R \\
N &= I_{zz}\dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})pq + N_{\dot{r}}\dot{r} + N_{D} + N_R
\end{aligned} $$

**Term-by-term identification (table form):**

| Term Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Origin |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Rigid-body inertia** | $m\dot{u},\,m\dot{v},\,m\dot{w}$ | Newton's second law for the hull |
| **Rigid-body rotation** | $I_{xx}\dot{p},\,I_{yy}\dot{q},\,I_{zz}\dot{r}$ | Angular momentum change of hull |
| **Coriolis–centripetal** | $m(-vr+wq),\,m(ur-wp),\, \ldots$ | B-frame rotation effect |
| **Gyroscopic** | $(I_{zz}-I_{yy})qr$ | Rotational inertia coupling |
| **Added mass** | $X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u},\,Y_{\dot{v}}\dot{v},\,N_{\dot{r}}\dot{r}$ | Inertia of accelerated water |
| **Damping** | $X_D, Y_D, \ldots$ | Viscous + form drag |
| **Restoring** | $X_R, Y_R, \ldots$ | Weight–buoyancy couple |

*(7 Marks — 2 for translation equations, 2 for rotation equations, 2 for table, 1 for completeness.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $m = 4{,}000\,\mathrm{tonnes} = 4 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{kg}$
- $Y_{\dot{v}} = -1.0 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{kg}$ → $m_{22} = 1.0 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{kg}$
- $u = U$, $v = w = 0$, $p = q = 0$, $r = r_0 = $ const.

**Steady-turn condition:** $\dot{v} = 0$, $\dot{u} = 0$, $\dot{w} = 0$.

**Sway equation (from part a):**

$$ Y = m(\dot{v} + ur - wp) + Y_{\dot{v}}\dot{v} + \cdots $$

Under the steady-turn assumptions:

$$ Y = m\,(0 + u\,r - 0) + Y_{\dot{v}}\,(0) = m\,u\,r $$

**Substituting the given numerical values** (with $r = r_0$ retained as a symbol for the constant yaw rate):

$$ \boxed{Y = m\,U\,r_0 = 4 \times 10^6 \cdot U \cdot r_0 \;\mathrm{N} } $$

**Example numerical check:** if $U = 10\,\mathrm{m/s}$ and $r_0 = 0.05\,\mathrm{rad/s}$:

$$ Y = 4 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 0.05 = 2.0 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{N} = 2{,}000\,\mathrm{kN} $$

This is the **centripetal force** that the rudder must supply (indirectly, through the water pressure on the hull) to keep the submarine in a circular path of radius $R = U/r_0 = 200\,\mathrm{m}$.

*(7 Marks — 2 for steady-turn assumptions, 2 for substitution, 2 for final expression, 1 for numerical verification.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $Y_{\dot{v}}$:** Students often write $Y_{\dot{v}} = +1.0 \times 10^6$ kg. In hydrodynamic derivative tables, the derivative is **defined as the rate of change of force with acceleration**, *not* the added mass itself. Hence $Y_{\dot{v}}$ is **negative** (opposing the acceleration).
> 2. **Confusing $r$ and $\dot{r}$:** In a *steady* turn, $\dot{r} = 0$ but $r \neq 0$. Many students mistakenly drop the $ur$ term, ending up with $Y = 0$ — **lose 4 marks**.
> 3. **Units:** Always convert tonnes to kg (×1000) and knots to m/s (×0.5144) before plugging in. A unit mismatch is a guaranteed **-1 mark** penalty.
> 4. **Coupling omitted:** Sway–yaw and roll–sway are strongly coupled in submarines. Truncating them leads to **non-physical** answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **EOM Definition:** Six coupled, non-linear ODEs describing a submarine's full dynamic behaviour in 3D space.
- **6-DOF:** Surge ($u,X$), Sway ($v,Y$), Heave ($w,Z$), Roll ($p,K$), Pitch ($q,M$), Yaw ($r,N$).
- **Two frames:** Earth-fixed (inertial, for position) and Body-fixed (non-inertial, for velocities & forces).
- **Newton's law in B-frame** gives the Coriolis terms $qw - rv$, $ru - wp$, $pv - qu$.
- **Euler's equations** give the gyroscopic terms $(I_{zz}-I_{yy})qr$, etc.
- **Added mass matrix $\mathbf{M}_A$** is **symmetric, positive-definite**; appears with a **negative** sign in hydrodynamic force models.
- **Damping matrix $\mathbf{D}(\boldsymbol{\nu})$** is generally **non-linear** (linear + quadratic terms).
- **Restoring forces** come from the **weight-buoyancy couple** when the centre of buoyancy shifts relative to the centre of gravity.
- **Control inputs** include **propeller thrust**, **rudder** (yaw), **stern planes** (pitch), and **fairwater planes** (pitch in some classes).
- **Subsystem decoupling:** For control design, submarines are usually split into *vertical plane* (heave-pitch) and *horizontal plane* (sway-roll-yaw) — these are only weakly coupled.
- **Steady-turn simplification:** $\dot{u} = \dot{v} = \dot{w} = 0$; only the $mur$ term survives in sway.
- **Units to remember:** $1\,\mathrm{tonne} = 1000\,\mathrm{kg}$; $1\,\mathrm{knot} = 0.5144\,\mathrm{m/s}$; $\rho_{sw} = 1025\,\mathrm{kg/m^3}$; $g = 9.81\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always write the **vector-matrix form** first, then specialise. Examiners reward students who *state the assumptions clearly* before deriving.
- **Anti-trap:** Do not forget that for an *axi-symmetric* submarine, $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$ — this simplifies the roll-yaw coupling block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic — Equation of Motion of a Submarine.** You now have a complete, KTU 2024-scheme-aligned treatment suitable for both internal assessments and the End-Semester Examination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrodynamic Derivatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Derivatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrodynamic Derivatives** (also called **Hydrodynamic Maneuvering Coefficients** or simply **Maneuvering Derivatives**) are the partial derivatives of the hydrodynamic forces and moments acting on a submerged body with respect to the corresponding components of motion (linear/angular velocities, accelerations) and control-surface deflections. They are the fundamental mathematical quantities that linearize (or quasi-linearize) the otherwise highly nonlinear equations of motion of a marine vehicle, making stability and control analysis tractable.

For a submarine/submersible, the general hydrodynamic force $F$ (or moment $M$) is expanded in a Taylor series about the steady (equilibrium) operating point:

$$F = F_0 + \sum_i \left.\frac{\partial F}{\partial x_i}\right|_0 x_i + \sum_{i,j} \left.\frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\right|_0 x_i x_j + \cdots$$

Each partial derivative term — when multiplied by the corresponding kinematic variable — represents a specific physical phenomenon (e.g., added mass, damping, cross-coupling).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus-Aligned Definition:**
> *Hydrodynamic derivatives are the coefficients in the Taylor-series expansion of hydrodynamic forces and moments on a submersible, expressed as partial derivatives of force/moment with respect to local motion variables (velocities, accelerations, control angles). They quantify how the surrounding fluid reacts to — and reacts upon — the motions of the vehicle.*

### 1.2 Symbolic Framework — SNAME Notation

The international standard adopted by the **Society of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers (SNAME)** uses:

| Symbol | Quantity |
|---|---|
| $X, Y, Z$ | Surge, sway, heave forces (N) |
| $K, M, N$ | Roll, pitch, yaw moments (N·m) |
| $u, v, w$ | Surge, sway, heave linear velocities (m/s) |
| $p, q, r$ | Roll, pitch, yaw angular velocities (rad/s) |
| $\delta_r, \delta_b, \delta_s$ | Rudder, bow-plane, stern-plane deflection angles (rad) |

The general force/moment is therefore written as $F_i = F_i(u,v,w,p,q,r,\dot u,\dot v,\dot w,\dot p,\dot q,\dot r,\delta_r,\delta_b,\delta_s)$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Flywheel" Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine pushing a heavy shopping cart through still water. Three things happen simultaneously:
> 1. The water **resists** your push (this is the **velocity derivative** — a damping-like effect).
> 2. A portion of the water is **dragged along** with the cart, increasing its effective inertia (this is the **acceleration derivative** — also called *added mass*).
> 3. As the cart moves sideways, it creates a small rotating flow that also tries to **twist** the cart (this is the **cross-coupling derivative**).
>
> A submersible underwater behaves exactly like this — only in six degrees of freedom simultaneously. Each "hidden push" or "hidden twist" from the surrounding fluid is quantified by one hydrodynamic derivative.

### 1.4 Classification of Hydrodynamic Derivatives

The complete set is partitioned into **six physical families**:

1. **Acceleration Derivatives** (Added-Mass): $X_{\dot u}, Y_{\dot v}, Z_{\dot w}, K_{\dot p}, M_{\dot q}, N_{\dot r}$ (diagonal) and $Y_{\dot r}, N_{\dot v}, \dots$ (off-diagonal / cross-coupled added mass).
2. **Linear Velocity Derivatives**: $X_u, Y_v, Z_w, K_p, M_q, N_r$.
3. **Angular Velocity Derivatives**: $Y_r, Y_p, Z_q, \dots$.
4. **Quadratic Velocity Derivatives** (nonlinear damping): $X_{vv}, X_{ww}, Y_{v\vert v\vert}, N_{r\vert r\vert}, \dots$.
5. **Cross-Coupling Derivatives**: $Y_{uv}, N_{ur}, M_{uw}, \dots$.
6. **Control Derivatives**: $Y_{\delta_r}, M_{\delta_b}, N_{\delta_r}, \dots$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask students to **classify** a given derivative (e.g., "What type is $Y_{\dot r}$?"). Memorize this six-fold classification — it carries **2 marks** alone in short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Taylor Expansion Foundation

The hydrodynamic force/moment on a submarine moving in calm water is a smooth, differentiable function of the motion variables. About the equilibrium (straight-running at $u = U$, all other motions = 0), the function is expanded. Each derivative term has a strict physical meaning:

- **First-order in velocity** → linear viscous damping.
- **First-order in acceleration** → added inertia (fluid inertia reacting to acceleration).
- **Higher-order velocity products** → nonlinear damping (form drag, vortex shedding, stall effects).
- **Mixed velocity-velocity products** → cross-coupling between two simultaneous motions.
- **Control-angle terms** → force/moment due to deflected control surfaces (rudders, planes).

### 2.2 Why the Derivative Concept Is So Powerful

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering Utility:**
> 1. **Linearization** of the 6-DOF equations for stability analysis (eigenvalue / Routh-Hurwitz).
> 2. **Controller design** — gain-scheduling and MIMO control require derivative values.
> 3. **CFD and PMM validation** — measured derivatives are the benchmark against which CFD codes and planar-motion-mechanism (PMM) tests are validated.
> 4. **Maneuvering simulation** — derivative-based models run faster than full Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes simulations.

### 2.3 Standardized 6-DOF Equations

#### Surge ($X$)
$$X = X_u\, u + X_{uu}\, u^2 + X_{vv}\, v^2 + X_{ww}\, w^2 + X_{uw}\, uw + X_{\dot u}\, \dot u + X_{\delta_s}\, \delta_s + X_{\delta_b}\, \delta_b$$

#### Sway ($Y$)
$$Y = Y_v\, v + Y_r\, r + Y_{\dot v}\, \dot v + Y_{\dot r}\, \dot r + Y_{vv}\, v\vert v\vert + Y_{vr}\, vr + Y_{uv}\, uv + Y_{\delta_r}\, \delta_r$$

#### Heave ($Z$)
$$Z = Z_w\, w + Z_q\, q + Z_{\dot w}\, \dot w + Z_{qq}\, q\vert q\vert + Z_{uw}\, uw + Z_{\delta_s}\, \delta_s + Z_{\delta_b}\, \delta_b$$

#### Roll ($K$)
$$K = K_p\, p + K_v\, v + K_{\dot p}\, \dot p + K_{uv}\, uv + K_{\delta_r}\, \delta_r$$

#### Pitch ($M$)
$$M = M_w\, w + M_q\, q + M_{\dot q}\, \dot q + M_{uw}\, uw + M_{ww}\, w\vert w\vert + M_{\delta_s}\, \delta_s + M_{\delta_b}\, \delta_b$$

#### Yaw ($N$)
$$N = N_r\, r + N_v\, v + N_{\dot r}\, \dot r + N_{\dot v}\, \dot v + N_{uv}\, uv + N_{rr}\, r\vert r\vert + N_{vr}\, vr + N_{\delta_r}\, \delta_r$$

### 2.4 Symmetry Properties (Submarines)

A modern submarine has three planes of approximate symmetry: **port–starboard, top–bottom, fore–aft**. These symmetries cause specific derivatives to vanish identically.

| Symmetry | Vanishing Derivatives | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Port-Starboard (y-z plane) | All odd in $v$ or $r$: $Y_0 = 0$, $K_0 = 0$, $N_0 = 0$; terms with odd power of $v$ or $r$ vanish | Forces/moments cannot prefer one side |
| Top-Bottom (x-y plane) | All odd in $w$ or $q$: $Z_0 = 0$, $M_0 = 0$; odd-$w$ terms vanish | Vertical symmetry of hull |
| Fore-Aft (x-z plane) | Couples $v$ ↔ $-v$, $r$ ↔ $-r$, $p$ ↔ $-p$: many cross terms zero | Submarine is bi-symmetric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** For an ideally symmetric submarine, the non-zero "restoring" derivatives such as $Z_w$ and $M_w$ are the only stiffness-like terms that produce pure heaving/pitching motion.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Derivative | Type | Dimensional Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X_{\dot u}, Y_{\dot v}, Z_{\dot w}$ | Added mass (linear) | $\text{kg}$ | Fluid mass dragged in pure translation |
| $K_{\dot p}, M_{\dot q}, N_{\dot r}$ | Added inertia (angular) | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ | Fluid rotational inertia dragged in rotation |
| $Y_{\dot r}, N_{\dot v}$ | Cross added mass | $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}$ | Sway acceleration producing yaw moment, etc. |
| $X_u, Y_v, Z_w$ | Linear damping | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$ | Force per unit linear velocity |
| $K_p, M_q, N_r$ | Angular damping | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s/rad}$ | Moment per unit angular velocity |
| $Y_r, N_v$ | Cross damping | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/rad}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$ | Sway→Yaw coupling |
| $X_{uu}, Y_{v\vert v\vert}, N_{r\vert r\vert}$ | Quadratic damping | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}^2/\text{m}^2$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s}^2/\text{rad}^2$ | Nonlinear drag |
| $Y_{uv}, N_{ur}, M_{uw}$ | Cross-coupling (vel.) | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}^2/\text{m}^2$ | Interaction damping |
| $Y_{\delta_r}, N_{\delta_r}$ | Rudder force/moment | $\text{N/rad}$, $\text{N}\cdot\text{m/rad}$ | Rudder effectiveness |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the literal pipe symbol `\|` in markdown tables** — it breaks the column separator. In LaTeX, use `\vert` (or `\mid`) for absolute-value notation, e.g., $Y_{v\vert v\vert}$.

### 2.6 Numerical Scale — Typical Submarine Values

For a 100 m long, 10 m diameter submarine at $U = 6$ m/s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³:

- $Y_v \approx -0.5\,\rho\, L^2 U$ → side force per unit sway velocity
- $N_{\dot r} \approx 0.02\,\rho\, L^5$ → yaw added inertia
- $Y_{\dot r} \approx 0.005\,\rho\, L^4$
- $N_r \approx -0.3\,\rho\, L^3 U$
- $Y_{\delta_r} \approx 0.005\,\rho\, L^2 U^2$
- $N_{\delta_r} \approx -0.003\,\rho\, L^3 U^2$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule-of-thumb estimate** (slender-body theory): any linear derivative scales with $\rho\, L^{n}\, U^{m}$ where $n, m$ are chosen so that the result has the correct force/moment units. This is the basis of **strip theory** (Hoerner's formula).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Quasi-Static Sway Derivative $Y_v$

**Starting point:** Consider a submarine moving with steady surge speed $U$ and a small steady sway velocity $v$, with no rotation. The lateral hydrodynamic side force $Y$ is by definition a function $Y(v)$.

**Step 1 — Symmetry Argument**
Because the hull is symmetric about the $x$–$z$ plane, the function $Y(v)$ is *odd*: a positive $v$ to port must produce the same magnitude of side force (with opposite sign) as a negative $v$ to starboard. Therefore $Y(0) = 0$ and $Y(v)$ has no constant term.

**Step 2 — Taylor Expansion about $v = 0$**

$$Y(v) = Y(0) + \left.\frac{\partial Y}{\partial v}\right|_0 v + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^2 Y}{\partial v^2}\right|_0 v^2 + \frac{1}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 Y}{\partial v^3}\right|_0 v^3 + \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Apply Symmetry**
Since $Y$ is an odd function, all even-order derivatives vanish at the origin:

$$\left.\frac{\partial^2 Y}{\partial v^2}\right|_0 = 0, \quad \left.\frac{\partial^4 Y}{\partial v^4}\right|_0 = 0, \;\dots$$

**Step 4 — Retain Leading Non-Zero Terms**

$$Y(v) \approx Y_v\, v + Y_{v\vert v\vert}\, v\vert v\vert + \cdots$$

where the conventional coefficient notation is used:

$$Y_v \;\triangleq\; \left.\frac{\partial Y}{\partial v}\right|_0, \qquad Y_{v\vert v\vert} \;\triangleq\; \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^3 Y}{\partial v^3}\right|_0$$

**Step 5 — Slender-Body (Hoerner) Estimate**
For a slender body of length $L$, projected lateral area $S_v$, and form-factor $C_f$:

$$Y_v \;\approx\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, U\, S_v\, C_{Df}\, \pi$$

The factor $-\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U$ has units $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$; multiplied by area it gives $\text{N·s/m}$, confirming the dimensional consistency of a damping derivative.

**Physical interpretation:** $Y_v$ is **negative** (restoring against sway) — water resists lateral motion. This is the dominant damping term that stabilizes low-speed sway.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Cross-Coupling Derivative $N_v$ (Sway → Yaw)

**Starting point:** With $u = U$ (constant) and $r = 0$, the submarine is given a small steady sway velocity $v$. A yaw moment $N$ develops because of asymmetric pressure on the fore and aft regions of the hull.

**Step 1 — Symmetry**
$N(v)$ is again an odd function (port/starboard symmetry).

**Step 2 — Expansion**

$$N(v) = N_v\, v + N_{v\vert v\vert}\, v\vert v\vert + \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Geometric Interpretation**
Decompose the hull into cross-sectional strips. A strip at longitudinal position $x$ experiences a local side force per unit length $\mathrm{d}Y/\mathrm{d}x$. Its contribution to the yaw moment about the CG is:

$$\mathrm{d}N_v \;=\; -x \cdot \frac{\partial Y}{\partial v}(x)\, \mathrm{d}x$$

The minus sign arises because port-positive $y$ creates a *negative* yaw moment (right-hand rule).

**Step 4 — Integrate along the Hull**

$$N_v \;=\; -\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} x \, \frac{\partial Y}{\partial v}(x)\, \mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 5 — For a Symmetric Hull (fore-aft)**
The integrand is anti-symmetric: $\partial Y/\partial v (x) = \partial Y/\partial v (-x)$. Therefore the integral of $x$ times an even function over symmetric limits is **non-zero**, giving $N_v \neq 0$. This is the famous **weathercock tendency** — submarines naturally turn their bow into the relative wind/current.

**Step 6 — Dimensional Check**

$$[N_v] = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m/s}} \cdot \text{m}\;=\; \text{N·m·s/m} \;\equiv\; \text{N·(rad·s/m)}$$

which is the correct unit for a moment derivative with respect to a linear velocity (using rad as a dimensionless angle).

### 3.3 Derivation III — Yaw Added Inertia $N_{\dot r}$

**Starting point:** The submarine, initially at rest, is given an angular acceleration $\dot r$ about the vertical axis (no translation).

**Step 1 — Angular Momentum of the Fluid**
Each fluid element at radius $\rho_f$ from the rotation axis acquires tangential velocity $v_\theta = \rho_f\, r$. Its angular momentum per unit mass is $\rho_f^2\, \dot r$.

**Step 2 — Total Fluid Angular Momentum**

$$h_{fluid} \;=\; \int_{V_{fluid}} \rho_f^{\,2}\, \dot r\, \rho_{water}\, \mathrm{d}V$$

**Step 3 — Define $N_{\dot r}$**
The hydrodynamic yaw moment required to enforce $\dot r$ (against the inertia of entrained water) is:

$$N_{\dot r} \;\triangleq\; \left.\frac{\partial N}{\partial \dot r}\right|_0 \;=\; \int_{V_{fluid}} \rho_f^{\,2}\, \rho_{water}\, \mathrm{d}V$$

**Step 4 — Slender-Body Approximation**
For a hull of length $L$ with equivalent radius $R(x)$:

$$N_{\dot r} \;\approx\; \frac{\pi\,\rho}{8}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} R(x)^4\, \mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 5 — For an Ellipsoidal Hull** (semi-axes $a, b, c$ with $L = 2a$):

$$N_{\dot r,ellipsoid} \;=\; \frac{\rho}{75}\,(2a)^3\,(2b)^2\, \frac{(2b)^2 + (2c)^2}{(2b)^2\,(2c)^2}\cdot\text{(correction factor)}$$

In practice, for slender bodies $b, c \ll a$, the dominant estimate is:

$$N_{\dot r} \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{20}\,\rho\, L^3\, b^2$$

where $b$ is the hull radius.

> [!TIP]
> **For exam answers:** Show that *added-inertia derivatives are independent of $U$* — they describe the fluid mass, not the flow speed. This is the **single most important conceptual difference** between acceleration derivatives ($X_{\dot u}, Y_{\dot v}, N_{\dot r}$) and velocity derivatives ($X_u, Y_v, N_r$).

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Rudder Yaw Moment Derivative $N_{\delta_r}$

**Starting point:** A rudder of area $A_R$, deflected by $\delta_r$ at steady speed $U$, generates a side force $L_R$.

**Step 1 — Rudder Lift (Slender-Foil Theory)**

$$L_R \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, U^2\, A_R\, C_{L\alpha}\, \delta_r$$

where $C_{L\alpha}$ is the rudder lift-curve slope (≈ $2\pi$ per radian for thin foils).

**Step 2 — Arm of Action**
The rudder is located at longitudinal distance $x_R$ from the CG. Its yaw moment arm is $x_R$ (with negative sign for a stern rudder — by convention, positive rudder deflection to port produces negative yaw moment, i.e., turns to starboard).

**Step 3 — Moment**

$$N \;=\; -L_R \cdot x_R \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, U^2\, A_R\, C_{L\alpha}\, x_R\, \delta_r$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Derivative**

$$N_{\delta_r} \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, U^2\, A_R\, C_{L\alpha}\, x_R$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional Check**

$$[N_{\delta_r}] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}\cdot\frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}^2}\cdot\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m} = \frac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2}{\text{s}^2} = \text{N·m/rad}\;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical insight:** $N_{\delta_r}$ scales with $U^2$. A submarine at 3 knots has only $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of the rudder authority it has at 6 knots. This is why submarines reduce speed dramatically (or use bow planes) for tight maneuvers near the surface.

### 3.5 Cross-Reference Matrix — Which Derivative Governs Which Maneuver

| Maneuver | Dominant Derivatives |
|---|---|
| Straight-running resistance | $X_u$, $X_{uu}$ |
| Steady turn | $Y_v, N_r, Y_r, N_v, Y_{\delta_r}, N_{\delta_r}$ |
| Zig-zag (overshoot analysis) | $Y_v, N_r, Y_r, N_v, N_{\dot v}, Y_{\dot r}, N_{\dot r}$ |
| Vertical-plane maneuver | $Z_w, M_q, Z_q, M_w, M_{\delta_b}, M_{\delta_s}$ |
| Roll oscillation | $K_p, K_{\dot p}, K_v, Y_v$ (coupled) |
| Accelerating from rest | $X_{\dot u}, Y_{\dot v}, N_{\dot r}$ |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 — Define hydrodynamic derivatives and distinguish between velocity derivatives and acceleration derivatives. Give one example of each.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrodynamic derivatives are the partial derivatives of hydrodynamic forces and moments on a submersible with respect to motion variables (velocities, accelerations) or control deflections, obtained by Taylor-series expansion of the force/moment function about the equilibrium operating point.

**Velocity derivatives** are partial derivatives of force/moment with respect to the *velocity* components of motion. They quantify the damping that the fluid exerts on the moving body. They have units of force·time/length (linear) or moment·time (angular) and **scale with the steady forward speed $U$**. Example: $Y_v = \partial Y/\partial v$ — the side force per unit sway velocity; it is **negative** and damps lateral oscillation.

**Acceleration derivatives** are partial derivatives of force/moment with respect to *acceleration* components. They quantify the **added mass** — the inertia of the surrounding fluid that must be accelerated together with the hull. They have units of mass (linear) or moment of inertia (angular) and are **independent of $U$**. Example: $Y_{\dot v} = \partial Y/\partial \dot v$ — the added mass in sway direction; it is **positive** and represents fluid mass dragged sideways during a sway acceleration.

#### Question 2 — State the symmetry properties of hydrodynamic derivatives for a submarine. Which derivatives vanish due to port-starboard symmetry?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
A modern submarine has three planes of symmetry: port–starboard ($x$–$z$ plane), top–bottom ($x$–$y$ plane), and fore–aft ($x$–$z$ plane offset from CG).

Due to **port–starboard symmetry** (no preferred side), the following vanish:
- All even-order force terms in $v$ at $v = 0$: $Y_0 = 0$, $K_0 = 0$, $N_0 = 0$.
- All odd-order *linear* derivatives in $v$ that would prefer one side: $Y_{vv}$ (the even, quadratic term survives), and odd-order coupling terms like $Y_{vvv}$ vanish.
- Similarly for $r$ (odd by symmetry): $K_{rr} = 0$ but $N_{rr}$ survives.

Physically: water cannot distinguish port from starboard if the hull itself is symmetric, so any "drift force" or "drift moment" must be **odd in the lateral motion** (i.e., proportional to $v$ or $v^3$, etc.).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** Derive the standard SNAME notation for the sway and yaw equations of motion of a submarine in 6-DOF. Clearly identify each term and classify it as linear damping, added inertia, cross-coupling, or control derivative. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**Model Solution:**

**Sway equation** — All forces in the lateral $y$-direction:

$$Y = Y_{\dot v}\,\dot v + Y_{\dot r}\,\dot r + Y_v\,v + Y_r\,r + Y_{vv}\,v\vert v\vert + Y_{vr}\,vr + Y_{uv}\,uv + Y_{\delta_r}\,\delta_r$$

| Term | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $Y_{\dot v}\,\dot v$ | Added mass (acceleration) | Fluid mass dragged during sway acceleration |
| $Y_{\dot r}\,\dot r$ | Cross added mass | Sway force due to yaw angular acceleration |
| $Y_v\,v$ | Linear damping (negative) | Resistance to sway velocity |
| $Y_r\,r$ | Cross damping | Sway force from yaw rate |
| $Y_{vv}\,v\vert v\vert$ | Quadratic damping | Nonlinear sway drag |
| $Y_{vr}\,vr$ | Cross-coupling | Coupling damping between sway and yaw |
| $Y_{uv}\,uv$ | Cross-coupling | Forward-speed-coupled sway damping |
| $Y_{\delta_r}\,\delta_r$ | Control derivative | Rudder-induced side force |

**Yaw equation** — All moments about vertical $z$-axis:

$$N = N_{\dot v}\,\dot v + N_{\dot r}\,\dot r + N_v\,v + N_r\,r + N_{vv}\,v\vert v\vert + N_{vr}\,vr + N_{uv}\,uv + N_{\delta_r}\,\delta_r$$

| Term | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{\dot v}\,\dot v$ | Cross added inertia | Yaw moment during sway acceleration |
| $N_{\dot r}\,\dot r$ | Added inertia | Fluid rotational inertia during yaw acceleration |
| $N_v\,v$ | Cross damping (weathercock) | Yaw moment from sway velocity — positive, restoring |
| $N_r\,r$ | Linear damping (negative) | Yaw rate damping |
| $N_{vv}\,v\vert v\vert$ | Quadratic cross-coupling | Nonlinear weathercock moment |
| $N_{vr}\,vr$ | Cross-coupling damping | Yaw damping from combined sway and yaw |
| $N_{uv}\,uv$ | Forward-speed coupling | Rudder-like moment from sway at forward speed |
| $N_{\delta_r}\,\delta_r$ | Control derivative | Rudder-induced yaw moment |

The system is decoupled into port-starboard and heave/pitch subsystems (due to symmetry), allowing independent analysis.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** A submarine cruises at $U = 7.5$ m/s. Its rudder has area $A_R = 6$ m², located at $x_R = -28$ m (aft of CG). The rudder lift-curve slope is $C_{L\alpha} = 2.4$ rad⁻¹. Seawater density is $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Calculate the rudder yaw-moment derivative $N_{\delta_r}$. Comment on how $N_{\delta_r}$ changes when the submarine reduces speed to $U = 3$ m/s. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the rudder moment equation derived earlier:**

$$N_{\delta_r} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, U^2\, A_R\, C_{L\alpha}\, x_R$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values at $U = 7.5$ m/s:**

$$N_{\delta_r} = -\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (7.5)^2 \times 6 \times 2.4 \times (-28)$$

$$N_{\delta_r} = -512.5 \times 56.25 \times 6 \times 2.4 \times (-28)$$

$$N_{\delta_r} = -512.5 \times 56.25 \times 6 = -173{,}062.5$$

$$N_{\delta_r} = -173{,}062.5 \times 2.4 = -415{,}350$$

$$N_{\delta_r} = -415{,}350 \times (-28) = +11{,}629{,}800 \;\text{N·m/rad}$$

$$N_{\delta_r} \approx +1.163 \times 10^{7}\;\text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 3 — At $U = 3$ m/s:**

$$N_{\delta_r,\,3} = -\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9 \times 6 \times 2.4 \times (-28)$$

$$N_{\delta_r,\,3} = -512.5 \times 9 \times 6 \times 2.4 \times (-28)$$

$$N_{\delta_r,\,3} = -4612.5 \times 6 = -27{,}675$$

$$N_{\delta_r,\,3} = -27{,}675 \times 2.4 = -66{,}420$$

$$N_{\delta_r,\,3} = -66{,}420 \times (-28) = +1{,}859{,}760 \;\text{N·m/rad}$$

$$N_{\delta_r,\,3} \approx +1.86 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{N_{\delta_r,\,3}}{N_{\delta_r,\,7.5}} = \frac{(3)^2}{(7.5)^2} = \frac{9}{56.25} = 0.16 = 16\%$$

**Comment:** At 3 m/s, the rudder yaw moment derivative drops to only 16 % of its value at 7.5 m/s — the rudder has $\sim 6.25\times$ less authority at low speed. This confirms the $U^2$ scaling and explains why submarines use **bow planes** for low-speed depth/heading control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Forgetting that $x_R$ is *negative* (rudder is aft of CG), then losing the sign. The sign of $N_{\delta_r}$ **must** be **positive** for a stern rudder by the standard convention. Losing this sign costs **1 mark**.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** Derive the expressions for the added mass $Y_{\dot v}$ and added inertia $N_{\dot r}$ for an ellipsoidal hull of length $L = 80$ m, diameter $D = 8$ m, submerged in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Compare the magnitude of fluid inertia to the inertia of the hull itself (steel hull mass $\approx 2 \times 10^6$ kg, $I_{zz} \approx 4 \times 10^8$ kg·m²). **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sway Added Mass $Y_{\dot v}$ for an Ellipsoid**
For an oblate spheroid (length $L$, beam $B$) translating sideways, the added mass coefficient is:

$$k_y \;=\; \frac{\pi\,B^2\,L}{16}\,\rho \cdot (\text{elliptic integral correction})$$

The leading-order slender-body result (since $B \ll L$):

$$Y_{\dot v} \;\approx\; \tfrac{\pi}{8}\,\rho\, L\, B^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$Y_{\dot v} = \frac{\pi}{8} \times 1025 \times 80 \times (8)^2$$

$$Y_{\dot v} = \frac{\pi}{8} \times 1025 \times 80 \times 64$$

$$Y_{\dot v} = \frac{\pi}{8} \times 5{,}248{,}000$$

$$Y_{\dot v} \approx 0.3927 \times 5{,}248{,}000 \approx 2{,}060{,}000 \;\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{\dot v} \approx 2.06 \times 10^{6}\;\text{kg}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Hull Mass**

$$\frac{Y_{\dot v}}{m_{hull}} = \frac{2.06 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} \approx 1.03$$

**Interpretation:** The **fluid mass dragged sideways** is approximately **equal** to the hull mass itself. When the submarine accelerates sideways, it must accelerate twice its own mass. This is a fundamental reason why submarines have poor lateral maneuverability.

**Step 4 — Yaw Added Inertia $N_{\dot r}$**

$$N_{\dot r} \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{20}\,\rho\, L^3\, (B/2)^2 \;\cdot\; \text{ellipsoidal correction factor}$$

For an oblate spheroid, the more accurate formula:

$$N_{\dot r} \;=\; \frac{\rho\,\pi\, L^3 B^2}{128}\,\left[\frac{(B/L)^2}{1 - (B/L)^2}\cdot\frac{1}{e^3}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1+e}{1-e} - e\right)\right]$$

With $B/L = 0.1$, $e = \sqrt{1 - 0.01} \approx 0.995$:

$$N_{\dot r} \approx \frac{1025 \times \pi \times 80^3 \times 8^2}{128} \times 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation**

$$N_{\dot r} = \frac{1025 \times 3.1416 \times 512{,}000 \times 64}{128}$$

$$= \frac{1025 \times 3.1416 \times 32{,}768{,}000}{128}$$

$$= \frac{1025 \times 102{,}960{,}000}{128}$$

$$\approx \frac{1.0553 \times 10^{11}}{128}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\dot r} \approx 8.24 \times 10^{8}\;\text{kg·m}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to Hull Yaw Inertia**

$$\frac{N_{\dot r}}{I_{zz,\,hull}} = \frac{8.24 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^8} \approx 2.06$$

**Interpretation:** The yaw added inertia is **twice** the hull's own yaw inertia. Combined with hull inertia, the effective rotational inertia is **3 times** the bare-hull value. This dramatically slows any yaw maneuver — a 30° turn takes ~3× longer than a non-friction-vacuum estimate would suggest.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** Using dimensional analysis, show that the derivative $N_r$ has dimensions of $\text{N·m·s/rad}$. Justify that $N_r$ must scale as $\rho L^4 U$ in the leading-order slender-body estimate. Given $N_r = -1.5 \times 10^8$ N·m·s/rad at $U = 6$ m/s for a hull with $L = 80$ m, compute the non-dimensional coefficient $C_N = N_r / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^4 U)$. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dimensional Analysis**
A moment derivative with respect to angular velocity has the form:

$$[N_r] \;=\; \frac{\text{Moment}}{\text{Angular velocity}} = \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{rad/s}} = \text{N·m·s/rad}$$

since the radian is dimensionless. ✓

**Step 2 — Scaling Argument**
For a slender hull in cross-flow, the dominant physical variables are:
- Fluid density: $[\rho] = \text{kg/m}^3$
- Length scale: $[L] = \text{m}$
- Forward speed: $[U] = \text{m/s}$

The moment derivative must scale as:

$$N_r \;\sim\; \rho^a \, L^b \, U^c$$

Solving for dimensional consistency:

$$\text{kg}^{1-a} \cdot \text{m}^{-3a+b+c} \cdot \text{s}^{-c} = \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

Equating exponents:
- Mass: $-a + 1 = 1 \Rightarrow a = 0$... *wait, let us reconsider.* Actually $N_r$ has dimensions $\text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}$, but with hidden factors of $\rho$ the equation is:

$$\rho^a L^b U^c \;:\; \text{kg}^a \cdot \text{m}^{-3a+b+c} \cdot \text{s}^{-c}$$

Equating to $\text{kg·m}^2\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$:
- kg: $a = 1$
- s: $c = 1$
- m: $-3(1) + b + 1 = 2 \Rightarrow b = 4$

Therefore $N_r \sim \rho\, L^4\, U$. ✓

**Step 3 — Non-dimensional Coefficient**

$$C_N \;=\; \frac{N_r}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,L^4\,U}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Calculation**
Given: $N_r = -1.5 \times 10^8$ N·m·s/rad, $L = 80$ m, $U = 6$ m/s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

$$L^4 = 80^4 = (80^2)^2 = 6400^2 = 40{,}960{,}000 \;\text{m}^4$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^4 U = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 40{,}960{,}000 \times 6$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 245{,}760{,}000$$

$$= 512.5 \times 245{,}760{,}000$$

$$= 1.2595 \times 10^{11}\;\text{N·m·s/rad}$$

**Step 5 — Final Coefficient**

$$C_N = \frac{-1.5 \times 10^8}{1.2595 \times 10^{11}} \approx -1.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{C_N \approx -1.19 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**
The negative sign confirms **damping**: positive yaw rate produces a negative (opposing) yaw moment. Typical submarine values of $C_N$ range from $-5 \times 10^{-4}$ to $-3 \times 10^{-3}$ depending on hull form, fins, and Reynolds number. The result $-1.19 \times 10^{-3}$ is consistent with published slender-body data for clean hull forms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Forgetting the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the denominator when defining non-dimensional coefficients. Examiners consistently deduct **1 mark** for this. The KTU board expects the standard $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2$ normalization, just as in aerodynamic lift/drag coefficients.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition** ✓ — Hydrodynamic derivatives are Taylor-series coefficients in the 6-DOF force/moment expansion.
- **SNAME notation** ✓ — $X,Y,Z$ for forces; $K,M,N$ for moments; $u,v,w,p,q,r$ for motions; $\delta$ for controls.
- **Six families** ✓ — Acceleration, linear velocity, angular velocity, quadratic, cross-coupling, control.
- **Symmetry** ✓ — Port–starboard symmetry eliminates odd-in-$v$ terms at equilibrium; fore–aft symmetry eliminates many roll derivatives.
- **Slender-body scaling** ✓ — $X_u \sim \rho L^2 U$, $Y_v \sim \rho L^2 U$, $N_r \sim \rho L^4 U$, $N_{\dot r} \sim \rho L^5$, $Y_{\delta_r} \sim \rho L^2 U^2$.
- **Rudder** ✓ — $N_{\delta_r} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 A_R C_{L\alpha} x_R$; scales with $U^2$.
- **Added mass is independent of $U$** ✓ — A diagnostic property separating acceleration from velocity derivatives.
- **Weathercock** ✓ — $N_v > 0$ — sway induces restoring yaw moment; this is what makes submarines directionally stable.
- **Typical values** ✓ — $C_N \in [-3\times 10^{-3}, -5\times 10^{-4}]$; $Y_{\dot v} \approx m_{hull}$; $N_{\dot r} \approx 2 I_{zz,\,hull}$.
- **Methodology** ✓ — Strip theory, captive-model tests (PMM), CFD, system identification — all yield derivatives for simulation and design.
- **Units** ✓ — Always verify dimensional consistency: force derivatives per unit velocity are $\text{N·s/m}$; moment derivatives per unit angular velocity are $\text{N·m·s/rad}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Advice:**
> 1. Always begin each Part B answer with a **clear diagram** of the hull, axes (right-handed, $x$ forward, $y$ port, $z$ down), and sign convention. This sets the stage for the entire solution.
> 2. **Sign every derivative** — examiners award marks for correct sign as much as for magnitude.
> 3. **State assumptions** (symmetric hull, slender body, steady $U$, etc.) explicitly before any derivation.
> 4. End with a **physical interpretation sentence** — e.g., "Thus the rudder loses 84 % of its authority at 3 m/s, justifying the use of bow planes for low-speed control." This converts a purely mathematical answer into a high-scoring engineering one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Compliant)

**Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane** of a submersible refers to the study of the vehicle's ability to maintain and control its heading (yaw) and lateral position (sway) while moving forward, with the center of gravity (CG) acting primarily in the longitudinal-vertical symmetry plane. The horizontal plane comprises two translational degrees of freedom (surge + sway) and one rotational degree of freedom (yaw), making it a **3-DOF (Three Degrees of Freedom) problem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the horizontal plane, the only active control surface on a conventional submersible is the **rudder** (mounted on the sail or stern). Stern planes and fairwater planes operate in the *vertical plane* and are NOT part of horizontal-plane control.

Mathematically, the state of a submersible in the horizontal plane is fully described by the vector:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ r \\ \psi \\ x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $u$ = surge velocity, $v$ = sway velocity, $r = \dot{\psi}$ = yaw rate, $\psi$ = heading angle, and $(x, y)$ = position in the horizontal plane.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle**:
- **Static stability (directional)** = the bicycle naturally "wants" to keep pointing forward when pushed sideways (like a weathervane aligning with the wind). Submersibles are designed to be **directionally stable** like an arrow or a weathervane.
- **Dynamic stability** = after being disturbed (e.g., a sideways gust), how quickly and smoothly the vehicle returns to its original heading without oscillating wildly.
- **Control** = the steering handle (rudder) that lets you deliberately change direction.

For a submersible, the **rudder** is analogous to the bicycle's handlebars, while the body's hydrodynamic shape provides the natural "weathervane" tendency.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in the Horizontal Plane

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hydrodynamic Variables (KTU Notation Convention)**
> - **Surge velocity ($u$)** — forward speed (m/s)
> - **Sway velocity ($v$)** — sideways slip (m/s)
> - **Yaw rate ($r$)** — rate of heading change (rad/s)
> - **Sideslip angle ($\beta$)** — $\beta = \arctan(v/u)$ (radians or degrees)
> - **Rudder deflection ($\delta_r$)** — control input (radians or degrees)
> - **Yaw moment ($N$)** — moment about the vertical (z) axis (N·m)
> - **Sway force ($Y$)** — lateral hydrodynamic force (N)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Horizontal-Plane Behavior

The horizontal-plane dynamics of a submersible are governed by **three coupled nonlinear differential equations**. Since analytical solutions are intractable, the equations are **linearized** about an equilibrium condition (steady forward motion at constant speed $U$, with $v = r = \beta = 0$). Linearization is the cornerstone of KTU 2024 module-level derivations.

**Step 1 — Identify the equilibrium operating point:**
A submersible cruising at depth with constant speed $U$, constant heading $\psi_0$, and no sideslip.

**Step 2 — Express all forces and moments as Taylor series expansions** about the equilibrium point, retaining only first-order terms. This is the **"small-perturbation theory"** of submersibles.

**Step 3 — Define hydrodynamic stability derivatives** as the partial derivatives of forces/moments with respect to the kinematic variables at the equilibrium.

**Step 4 — Assemble the 3-DOF linear state-space model** and analyze the eigenvalues to determine stability.

**Step 5 — Apply control inputs** ($\delta_r$) and design the heading-keeping autopilot.

## 2.2 The Three Coupled Equations of Motion (Horizontal Plane)

Following standard KTU textbook notation (per SNAME 1950 and Fossen conventions):

**Surge equation (decoupled in many designs):**
$$
m(\dot{u} - v r) = X(u) + X_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

**Sway equation:**
$$
m(\dot{v} + u r) = Y_{\beta}\beta + Y_r r + Y_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

**Yaw equation:**
$$
I_{zz}\dot{r} = N_{\beta}\beta + N_r r + N_{\delta_r}\delta_r
$$

The sway and yaw equations are **strongly coupled** through the cross-coupling derivatives $Y_r$ and $N_{\beta}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Sign (Stable Sub) | Units |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------------:|:-----:|
| $Y_{\beta}$ | Sway force due to sideslip | $< 0$ (restoring) | N/rad |
| $Y_r$ | Sway force due to yaw rate | $< 0$ (damping) | N·s/rad |
| $Y_{\delta_r}$ | Sway force due to rudder | $> 0$ | N/rad |
| $N_{\beta}$ | Yaw moment due to sideslip | $> 0$ (weathercock) | N·m/rad |
| $N_r$ | Yaw moment due to yaw rate | $< 0$ (damping) | N·m·s/rad |
| $N_{\delta_r}$ | Yaw moment due to rudder | $> 0$ | N·m/rad |
| $I_{zz}$ | Moment of inertia about z-axis | $> 0$ | kg·m² |
| $m$ | Submersible mass | $> 0$ | kg |
| $U$ | Equilibrium surge speed | $> 0$ | m/s |
| $T_{1,2}$ | Time constants of yaw mode | real & positive | s |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio (turning mode) | $0.1$ to $0.3$ (sub) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion (KTU High-Yield Result):** A submersible is **directionally stable** in the horizontal plane if and only if:
> $$N_{\beta} > 0 \quad \text{(weathercock stability)}$$
> This means the yaw moment created by a small sideslip angle is **restoring** — pushing the bow back into the wind. This is achieved by placing the **center of lateral resistance (CLR)** *behind* the **center of gravity (CG)** when viewed from above, exactly like a weathervane.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Submersible Engineering

- **Course-keeping** at low speeds (periscope depth operations) is critically dependent on directional stability — unmanned AUVs use this principle.
- **Maneuverability vs. Stability Trade-off**: A *highly stable* submersible is sluggish to turn; a *highly maneuverable* one may oscillate. Designers tune $N_{\beta}$ and $N_r$ to balance both.
- **Autopilot Design**: Modern submersibles use **PID controllers** where the rudder command $\delta_r$ is a function of heading error $\psi_e$ and yaw rate $r$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linearized 3-DOF Horizontal-Plane Equations

Starting from Newton's second law in the body-fixed frame for a rigid body submerged in a fluid:

**Step 1.1 — Write the general nonlinear equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\dot{u} - v r + w q) &= X \\
m(\dot{v} + u r - w p) &= Y \\
m(\dot{w} - u q + v p) &= Z \\
I_{xx}\dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy})qr &= K \\
I_{yy}\dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz})pr &= M \\
I_{zz}\dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})pq &= N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.2 — Restrict to the horizontal plane:** In the horizontal plane, $w = 0$, $p = 0$, $q = 0$, and the depth $z$ is constant. The terms $w q$, $w p$, $pq$ all vanish, leaving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\dot{u} - v r) &= X \\
m(\dot{v} + u r) &= Y \\
I_{zz}\dot{r} &= N
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the reduction from 6-DOF to 3-DOF.

**Step 1.3 — Linearize about the equilibrium point** $(u_0 = U, v_0 = 0, r_0 = 0)$:
Replace each variable with its equilibrium value plus a small perturbation: $u = U + \tilde{u}$, $v = \tilde{v}$, $r = \tilde{r}$, and expand the right-hand-side forces/moments in a first-order Taylor series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= X(u) + X_{\delta_r}\delta_r \approx X_u \tilde{u} + X_{\delta_r}\delta_r \\
Y &= Y_{\beta}\beta + Y_r r + Y_{\delta_r}\delta_r \\
N &= N_{\beta}\beta + N_r r + N_{\delta_r}\delta_r
\end{aligned}
$$

where the partial derivative notation $Y_{\beta} = \partial Y/\partial\beta$ is taken at the equilibrium.

**Step 1.4 — Convert sideslip angle to velocity:** Using $\beta = \arctan(v/u) \approx v/U$ for small $\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\beta}\beta &\approx \frac{Y_{\beta}}{U}\tilde{v} \\
N_{\beta}\beta &\approx \frac{N_{\beta}}{U}\tilde{v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.5 — Substitute into the 3-DOF equations and neglect higher-order products** like $\tilde{v}\cdot \tilde{r}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\dot{\tilde{u}} &= X_u \tilde{u} + X_{\delta_r}\delta_r \\
