> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Module 4 questions frequently test the **comparison of cylindrical vs spherical hulls** with respect to (a) volume efficiency and (b) collapse pressure. Memorize the buckling formula for cylinders.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Loading on the Hull

When a submersible descends to depth $h$, the external pressure on the hull is:

$$P_{ext} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

The **net crushing pressure** acting on the hull is essentially the hydrostatic component, since the inside is maintained at ~1 atm for habitability:

$$\Delta P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

The hull must be designed to withstand this **compressive** loading **without yielding, buckling, or leaking** for the entire operational life.

## 2.2 Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Hull

For a long cylinder of radius $R$ and wall thickness $t$ under external pressure $P$:

- **Hoop (circumferential) stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{-P \cdot R}{t}$
- **Longitudinal (axial) stress:** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{-P \cdot R}{2t}$

The hoop stress is **twice** the axial stress — meaning the **cylinder is most likely to fail along its circumference** (a ring would crush first).

## 2.3 Yielding Criterion (Strength Check)

The Tresca or von Mises yield criterion must be satisfied. For a thin cylinder under external pressure, the **equivalent stress** is dominated by hoop stress:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

where $n$ is the **factor of safety** (typically **1.5 to 2.5** for military, **2.5 to 3.5** for civilian submersibles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Yielding is the *initiation* of permanent deformation. For a pressure hull, we cannot allow *any* yielding, because repeated load cycles in deep dives would cause **low-cycle fatigue** and eventual brittle fracture. Hence, the design must remain **fully elastic** at maximum operating depth.

## 2.4 Elastic Buckling of a Long Cylinder (Unstiffened)

For a long, perfect, unstiffened circular cylinder of radius $R$, thickness $t$, and length $L$ under external pressure $P$:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^3$$

This is the **classical buckling pressure**. For steel ($\nu = 0.3$):

$$P_{cr} \approx \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E}{(R/t)^3}$$

**Real cylinders fail at much lower pressures** than this formula predicts because of **initial geometric imperfections** (ovality, eccentricity). A knockdown factor $k \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ is applied:

$$P_{cr,real} = k \cdot P_{cr,ideal}$$

## 2.5 Buckling with Ring Stiffeners (Bryan / Von Mises)

To increase the collapse depth, **ring stiffeners** are welded at intervals along the cylinder. The effective length $L_e$ between rings is used. The Von Mises-modified formula for ring-stiffened cylinders is:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^{2.5}}{(L_e/R) - 0.45(t/R)^{0.5}}$$

- More rings (smaller $L_e$) ⇒ higher $P_{cr}$
- Thicker hull ⇒ higher $P_{cr}$
- Smaller radius $R$ ⇒ much higher $P_{cr}$ (cubic effect)

## 2.6 Spherical Pressure Hulls

For a sphere, the membrane stress is uniform in all directions:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2t}$$

Since there is no preferential stress direction and no "long axis," a sphere is **inherently stable** under external pressure. **No buckling is possible** in the classical sense — a sphere fails by **yielding**, not by buckling.

**Volume comparison:** A sphere of radius $R$ has volume $\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$, but a cylinder of radius $R$ and length $L$ has volume $\pi R^2 L$. A cylinder is more volume-efficient for $L > 2R$.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS — MEMORIZE THESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa |
| 2 | Hoop stress in thin cylinder | $\sigma_h = P R / t$ | Pa |
| 3 | Axial stress in thin cylinder | $\sigma_a = P R / (2t)$ | Pa |
| 4 | Membrane stress in sphere | $\sigma = P R / (2t)$ | Pa |
| 5 | Required thickness (yield) | $t = n P R / \sigma_y$ | m |
| 6 | Classical buckling pressure (long cylinder) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E}{(R/t)^3}$ | Pa |
| 7 | Buckling with knockdown | $P_{cr,real} = k \cdot P_{cr}$ | Pa |
| 8 | Ring-stiffened buckling (Von Mises) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E (t/R)^{2.5}}{(L_e/R) - 0.45(t/R)^{0.5}}$ | Pa |
| 9 | Reserve buoyancy | $RB = V_{displaced} - W_{sub}$ (as weight or volume) | N or m³ |
| 10 | Submergence ratio (fixed ballast) | $SR = \dfrac{W_{sub} - W_{buoyancy}}{W_{buoyancy}}$ | — |
| 11 | Battery energy | $E_{bat} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ (or kWh) | J or kWh |
| 12 | Power for propulsion | $P_{prop} = F_{drag} \cdot v$ | W |

> **Critical Note on Notation:** In all formulas above, $P$ represents pressure (Pa), $R$ is internal or mean radius, $t$ is wall thickness, and $E$ is Young's modulus. Always use **SI units** in KTU numericals.

## 2.8 Submersible Systems — Functional Overview

A submersible integrates multiple engineering systems. Each system has a defined purpose, key components, and interdependencies.

| System | Primary Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Ballast system** | Controls vertical motion (dive/surface) | Main ballast tanks, drop weights, blow valves, pumps |
| **Trim system** | Maintains longitudinal/athwartships balance | Variable ballast tanks, pumps, valves, control electronics |
| **Buoyancy system** | Provides net upward force when needed | Syntactic foam, air bladders, hard ballast |
| **Power system** | Supplies electrical energy | Lead-acid / Li-ion / Ag-Zn batteries, chargers, bus bars |
| **Propulsion system** | Provides forward and lateral motion | Main thrusters (DC motors), propeller ducts, Kort nozzles |
| **Maneuvering system** | Provides directional control | Vertical thrusters (bow/stern), lateral thrusters |
| **Life support system** | Maintains breathable atmosphere | O₂ cylinders, CO₂ scrubbers (LiOH), temperature/humidity control |
| **Navigation system** | Determines position and heading | Inertial nav, DVL, GPS (surface only), depth sensor, compass |
| **Communication system** | Links to surface and other subs | VHF/acoustic modems, hydrophones, underwater telephone |
| **Hatch / Access system** | Allows crew entry/exit | Hatches, sealing rings, equalizing valves, viewports (portholes) |
| **Emergency system** | Provides survival under fault conditions | Drop weights, emergency blow, life support backup, rescue hook |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Modern deep submersibles (e.g., *DSV Limiting Factor* by Triton Submarines, *Shenhai Yongshi*) use **titanium Ti-6Al-4V** pressure hulls rated for **Full Ocean Depth (FOD, 11,000 m)**. The hull is a **144 mm thick titanium sphere** of 1.5 m inner diameter, demonstrating the practical application of the spherical hull theory above.

## 2.9 Bulkheads and Compartmentalization

Pressure hulls are divided into **watertight compartments** by **bulkheads** (transverse or longitudinal). The purposes are:

1. **Safety** — flooding in one compartment does not sink the vehicle.
2. **Structural** — bulkheads act as **ring stiffeners**, increasing hull collapse depth.
3. **Functional** — separation of battery rooms, crew space, machinery, and command areas.

Bulkheads may be:
- **Flat (plane) bulkheads** — easy to fabricate, but induce high bending stresses at the hull intersection.
- **Hemispherical / ellipsoidal bulkheads** — best for pressure resistance, but use more length.
- **Conical bulkheads** — compromise solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that **bulkheads reduce buckling length** $L_e$, dramatically increasing collapse pressure. Always identify the distance between bulkheads as the effective $L_e$ in buckling calculations.

## 2.10 Viewports and Penetrators

- **Viewports (portholes)** are transparent pressure windows. They are made of **acrylic (perspex)** for shallow depths and **conical glass** for deep vehicles. They are designed as **thick-walled pressurized discs** with stress concentrations at the seating.
- **Penetrators** are sealed electrical and hydraulic pass-throughs that pierce the pressure hull. They must maintain hull integrity at depth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Hull

**Starting Setup:** Consider a long, thin-walled cylinder of inner radius $R$, wall thickness $t$ (with $t \ll R$), subjected to internal pressure $P$ (we use internal here for clarity; the formula is identical in magnitude for external pressure, with sign change).

**Step 1 — Free-Body Cut:** Slice the cylinder longitudinally along its axis. Examine a half-cylinder of length $L$. The internal pressure acts on the projected area (diameter × length):

$$A_{projected} = 2R \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Resultant Force from Pressure:** The total outward force on the half-cylinder from the internal pressure is:

$$F_P = P \cdot A_{projected} = P \cdot (2R \cdot L)$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force from Wall:** This pressure is resisted by the tensile force in the two cut edges of the cylindrical wall. Each cut edge has area $t \cdot L$, and there are 2 such edges. The total resisting area is $2 t L$. The hoop stress is $\sigma_h$, so:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \cdot 2t \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium:** Setting $F_{resist} = F_P$:

$$\sigma_h \cdot 2t \cdot L = P \cdot 2R \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Simplification:** Cancel $2L$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_h \cdot t = P \cdot R$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}}$$

This is the **hoop (circumferential) stress**. For external pressure (submersible hull), the same magnitude applies but the stress is **compressive**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Axial (Longitudinal) Stress in a Closed Cylinder

**Step 1 — Cut the Cylinder Transversely:** Cut the cylinder perpendicular to its axis. Consider the half of the cylinder.

**Step 2 — Projected Area on the Cut:** The internal pressure on the closed end exerts force on a circular area of radius $R$:

$$A_{end} = \pi R^2$$

**Step 3 — Force from Pressure:**

$$F_P = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 4 — Resisting Force:** This is carried by the cylindrical wall (cross-sectional area = $2\pi R \cdot t$):

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_a \cdot 2\pi R t$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium:**

$$\sigma_a \cdot 2\pi R t = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2t}}$$

This is **half** the hoop stress — confirming the **hoop stress is the dominant failure mode** for cylindrical pressure hulls.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Stress in a Spherical Pressure Hull

**Step 1 — Cut the Sphere in Half:** Consider a hemisphere of inner radius $R$ and wall thickness $t$.

**Step 2 — Force from Internal Pressure** on the projected circular area:

$$F_P = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force:** The cut goes around the great circle. The wall cross-section is a ring of width $t$ and circumference $2\pi R$:

$$A_{wall} = 2\pi R \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium:**

$$\sigma \cdot 2\pi R t = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2t}}$$

**Note:** The same expression as the axial stress of a cylinder, but for a sphere this stress is **uniform in all directions** — hence the structural superiority of spheres.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Wall Thickness (Yield Criterion)

**Step 1 — Maximum Equivalent Stress:** For a thin cylinder under external pressure, the hoop stress is dominant:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Yield Criterion with Safety Factor:** The stress must remain below yield strength divided by safety factor $n$:

$$\sigma_h \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\dfrac{P \cdot R}{t} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t$:**

$$\boxed{t \geq \dfrac{n \cdot P \cdot R}{\sigma_y}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Classical Elastic Buckling Pressure of a Long Cylinder

This is a more advanced derivation based on small-deflection theory of shells.

**Step 1 — Energy Method Setup:** For a thin cylindrical shell under external pressure, the critical (buckling) pressure is determined by minimizing the total potential energy. The classical result, derived by **Lorenz (1908)** and validated by Timoshenko, for a long, perfect cylinder is:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{4 \cdot R^3 \cdot \sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify for Steel ($\nu = 0.3$):** Compute $\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}$:

$$\sqrt{3(1 - 0.3^2)} = \sqrt{3 \cdot 0.91} = \sqrt{2.73} \approx 1.652$$

So:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{4 \cdot R^3 \cdot 1.652} = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{6.61 \cdot R^3}$$

**Step 3 — Common Engineering Form:** This is often rewritten as:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E}{(R/t)^3}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:** For HY-80 steel ($E = 207$ GPa) with $R/t = 50$:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9}{50^3} = \dfrac{5.382 \times 10^{11}}{125000} = 4.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Equivalent Depth (seawater, $\rho g = 10.05$ kPa/m):**

$$h_{collapse} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{4.31 \times 10^6}{10050} \approx 429 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The collapse depth of a *perfect* cylinder is ~430 m for $R/t = 50$ in steel. **Real cylinders with imperfections** typically fail at 50–70% of this value. This is why **ring stiffeners** are essential in deep submersibles.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A cylindrical pressure hull for a research submersible is to operate at a **maximum depth of 3000 m**. The hull has an inner radius $R = 0.75$ m, length $L = 4$ m (between bulkheads). Determine:

(a) The required wall thickness using the yield criterion with **HY-80 steel** ($\sigma_y = 550$ MPa) and safety factor $n = 2.0$.

(b) The actual $R/t$ ratio and the classical buckling pressure.

(c) The collapse depth for a **real** cylinder (knockdown factor $k = 0.7$). Is the design safe?

---

**Solution (a) — Wall Thickness:**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 3000 m:**

$$P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3000 = 30.17 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thickness formula:**

$$t = \dfrac{n \cdot P \cdot R}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 30.17 \times 10^6 \times 0.75}{550 \times 10^6}$$

$$t = \dfrac{45.26 \times 10^6}{550 \times 10^6} = 0.0823 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 82.3 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Solution (b) — R/t Ratio and Classical Buckling:**

**Step 1 — R/t:**

$$\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{750}{82.3} = 9.11$$

**Step 2 — Classical buckling pressure (steel):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E}{(R/t)^3} = \dfrac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9}{9.11^3} = \dfrac{5.382 \times 10^{11}}{755.8} = 712 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to depth:**

$$h_{buckling} = \dfrac{712 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{712 \times 10^6}{10055} = 70,810 \text{ m}$$

---

**Solution (c) — Real Collapse with Knockdown:**

**Step 1 — Apply knockdown factor $k = 0.7$:**

$$P_{cr,real} = 0.7 \times 712 = 498.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Real collapse depth:**

$$h_{real} = \dfrac{498.4 \times 10^6}{10055} = 49,567 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Safety assessment:**

$$\text{Safety Factor on Collapse} = \dfrac{49{,}567}{3000} \approx 16.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The design is **vastly over-safe** against buckling — because yielding ($t = 82$ mm) is the binding constraint at this radius. For deeper dives, designers would **reduce R** or **add ring stiffeners** or **switch to titanium**.

---

## 3.7 Submergence and Reserve Buoyancy Formulas

For a submersible of total weight $W$ (in air) and displaced water volume $V$:

$$\text{Buoyant force} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$$

$$\text{Net buoyancy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V - W$$

- If **net buoyancy > 0**: vehicle floats (positively buoyant)
- If **net buoyancy = 0**: neutrally buoyant (hovers at depth)
- If **net buoyancy < 0**: vehicle sinks (negatively buoyant)

**Reserve Buoyancy** is the additional buoyancy available by expelling water from ballast tanks. It determines the ability to surface from any depth.

$$RB = (\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{total}) - W_{sub}$$

where $V_{total}$ is the total volume of all tanks and flooded spaces.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Submersible Structural Hierarchy

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a submersible structure**, showing the relationships between the pressure hull, external attachments, and internal systems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Submersible Vehicle] --> B[Pressure Hull Assembly]
    A --> C[External Appendages]
    A --> D[Internal Systems Block]

    B --> B1[Forward Hemispherical Cap]
    B --> B2[Cylindrical Section Main Hull]
    B --> B3[Aft Hemispherical Cap]
    B --> B4[Ring Stiffeners]
    B --> B5[Transverse Bulkheads]
    B --> B6[Conning Tower Fairing]

    C --> C1[Main Propulsion Propeller]
    C --> C2[Vertical Thrusters]
    C --> C3[Lateral Thrusters]
    C --> C4[External Lights and Cameras]
    C --> C5[Acoustic Transducers]
    C --> C6[Drop Weight Carriage]

    D --> D1[Ballast and Trim System]
    D --> D2[Power Supply Batteries]
    D --> D3[Life Support System]
    D --> D4[Navigation and Control]
    D --> D5[Communication System]
    D --> D6[Hatch and Access System]

    B1 --> E1[Stress Region Hoop Dominant]
    B2 --> E2[Stress Region Hoop Critical]
    B3 --> E3[Stress Region Hoop Dominant]
    B4 --> E4[Function Increase Buckling Resistance]
    B5 --> E5[Function Compartmentalization and Stiffening]
    B6 --> E6[Function Crew Access and Aerodynamic Shape]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hull Stress Distribution and Failure Modes

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[External Hydrostatic Pressure P] --> B[Hull Wall Loaded in Compression]
    B --> C{Stress Distribution}
    C -->|Hoop Direction| D[Hoop Stress equals P times R divided by t]
    C -->|Axial Direction| E[Axial Stress equals P times R divided by 2t]

    D --> F{Check Yielding}
    E --> F

    F -->|Sigma less than Sigma_y by n| G[SAFE: Elastic Response]
    F -->|Sigma exceeds Sigma_y by n| H[FAIL: Plastic Collapse]

    B --> I{Check Buckling}
    I -->|P less than P_cr| J[SAFE: No Buckling]
    I -->|P exceeds P_cr| K[FAIL: Shell Buckling]

    G --> L[OPERATIONAL]
    J --> L
    H --> M[DESIGN REVISION REQUIRED]
    K --> M
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Systems Integration Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "Energy Domain"
        P1[Power System: Batteries] --> E1[Electrical Bus]
    end

    subgraph "Control Domain"
        C1[Command Console] --> C2[Control Electronics]
        C2 --> E1
    end

    subgraph "Actuation Domain"
        E1 --> A1[Main Thruster Motor]
        E1 --> A2[Ballast Pump Motor]
        E1 --> A3[Trim Pump Motor]
        E1 --> A4[Life Support Fans]
    end

    subgraph "Propulsion Output"
        A1 --> R1[Vehicle Linear Motion]
    end

    subgraph "Buoyancy Output"
        A2 --> R2[Vehicle Vertical Motion]
        A3 --> R3[Vehicle Trim Adjustment]
    end

    subgraph "Crew Survival"
        A4 --> R4[Atmosphere Control]
    end

    subgraph "Navigation and Comms"
        N1[Depth Sensor] --> C2
        N2[IMU and DVL] --> C2
        N3[Acoustic Modem] --> C2
    end
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Cylindrical Pressure Hull with Ring Stiffeners

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hemispherical Forward Cap] -->|Welded Joint| B[Cylindrical Main Hull Section]
    B -->|Welded Joint| C[Hemispherical Aft Cap]

    B --> E[Ring Stiffener 2]
    B --> F[Ring Stiffener 3]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hull is divided into compartments by **bulkheads (G, H)**. **Ring stiffeners (D, E, F)** are welded at regular intervals. The **effective buckling length $L_e$** is the spacing between bulkheads (or stiffeners), NOT the total hull length.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the difference between yielding and buckling as failure modes of a submersible pressure hull. Which one is more critical for cylindrical hulls and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Yielding (1 mark):** Yielding is a *material-level* failure where the wall stress exceeds the yield strength of the material, causing permanent plastic deformation. For a cylinder, yielding initiates when hoop stress $\sigma_h = PR/t$ exceeds $\sigma_y/n$.

- **Buckling (1 mark):** Buckling is a *structural instability* failure where the cylindrical shell suddenly deforms inward (ovalization) at a critical external pressure, even though the material itself is still within the elastic range. The classical formula is $P_{cr} = 2.6E/(R/t)^3$.

- **Which is more critical for cylinders (1 mark):** **Buckling is more critical** than yielding for thin-walled cylinders because $P_{cr}$ decreases with the **cube** of $R/t$, while yielding stress only decreases *linearly* with $R/t$. Thus, at sufficiently large $R/t$ (typically > 30), buckling governs the design.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List the major systems of a submersible and state the function of any four.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A submersible integrates the following major systems (½ mark each for naming and ½ mark for function — any four):

1. **Ballast system** — controls diving and surfacing by flooding/emptying ballast tanks.
2. **Trim system** — adjusts longitudinal and athwartships balance for level flight.
3. **Power system** — supplies electrical energy from batteries (Pb-acid, Li-ion, Ag-Zn) to all consumers.
4. **Propulsion system** — provides forward motion via main thruster(s) and ducted propellers.
5. **Life support system** — maintains breathable O₂/CO₂ balance, temperature, and humidity.
6. **Navigation system** — provides position, heading, and depth using DVL, IMU, depth sensor, and acoustic positioning.
7. **Communication system** — links the sub to surface and other platforms via acoustic modems and hydrophones.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure hull under external pressure. Explain why hoop stress governs the design of cylindrical submersible hulls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure hull has an inner radius of **0.6 m** and must withstand an external pressure of **20 MPa** (corresponding to a depth of approximately 2000 m). The hull is fabricated from **HY-80 steel** ($\sigma_y = 550$ MPa). Using a factor of safety of **2.0**, determine:
   (i) The required wall thickness based on the yield criterion.
   (ii) The $R/t$ ratio.
   (iii) The classical buckling pressure and compare it with the actual operating pressure. Is buckling a concern? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Hoop Stress Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Consider a long, thin-walled cylinder of inner radius $R$, thickness $t$ ($t \ll R$), subjected to external pressure $P$. Make a longitudinal cut and analyze a half-cylinder of length $L$.

- The external pressure on the projected diameter area gives an inward force:

$$F_P = P \cdot 2R \cdot L$$

- This is resisted by hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the two cut edges, each of area $t \cdot L$:

$$F_R = \sigma_h \cdot 2t \cdot L$$

- Equating forces:

$$\sigma_h \cdot 2t \cdot L = P \cdot 2R \cdot L$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}}$$

**Longitudinal Stress Derivation (2 marks):**

- Cut the cylinder transversely. Internal pressure on the end cap exerts force $P \cdot \pi R^2$ resisted by $\sigma_a \cdot 2\pi R t$:

$$\sigma_a \cdot 2\pi R t = P \cdot \pi R^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2t}}$$

**Why Hoop Stress Governs (1.5 marks):**

Since $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_a$, the circumferential (hoop) direction always carries **twice** the stress of the longitudinal direction. Therefore, any failure (yielding or fatigue) initiates at the **hoop** location. Design must satisfy the yield criterion at hoop stress first.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Part (i) — Wall thickness (3 marks):**

Using the yield criterion with safety factor $n = 2.0$:

$$t = \dfrac{n \cdot P \cdot R}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 0.6}{550 \times 10^6}$$

$$t = \dfrac{24 \times 10^6}{550 \times 10^6} = 0.04364 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 43.6 \text{ mm}}$$

**Part (ii) — R/t ratio (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{600}{43.6} = 13.76$$

**Part (iii) — Buckling check (3 marks):**

For steel ($E = 207$ GPa):

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E}{(R/t)^3} = \dfrac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9}{13.76^3} = \dfrac{5.382 \times 10^{11}}{2605.3}$$

$$P_{cr} \approx 206.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comparison:** $P_{cr} = 206.6$ MPa $\gg P_{op} = 20$ MPa.

- Safety factor on buckling: $206.6 / 20 = 10.3$

**Conclusion:** **Buckling is NOT a concern** at this design point. The hull is over-safe because the yielding criterion ($t = 43.6$ mm) is the binding constraint, while buckling would only become critical at much larger $R/t$ ratios (>30 typically).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the structural arrangement of a typical deep-diving submersible. Discuss the role of (i) ring stiffeners, (ii) bulkheads, and (iii) viewports. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **cylindrical** and **spherical** pressure hulls for a deep submersible in terms of (i) membrane stress distribution, (ii) volume efficiency, (iii) buckling resistance, and (iv) manufacturability. State, with justification, which is preferred for ultra-deep (FOD) vehicles. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Structural Arrangement (3 marks):**

A typical deep submersible has:

- A **central cylindrical hull** of radius $R$ and length $L$ fabricated from high-strength steel or titanium.
- Two **hemispherical end caps** (forward and aft) welded to the cylinder.
- **Ring stiffeners** (also called frames or ribs) welded externally or internally at regular intervals to increase buckling resistance.
- **Transverse bulkheads** dividing the hull into watertight compartments.
- A **conning tower** or fairing on top housing the hatch and providing a streamlined external shape.
- **Viewports** (portholes) of thick acrylic or glass on the side for crew observation.
- **External appendages** — thrusters, lights, cameras, manipulator arms, drop weight.

**Role of Ring Stiffeners (1.5 marks):**

Ring stiffeners **reduce the effective unsupported length $L_e$** of the cylinder. Since buckling pressure scales inversely with $L_e$ (or even with $L_e^2$ in some regimes), stiffeners dramatically increase the collapse pressure of a cylindrical hull. They essentially turn a long, weak cylinder into a series of shorter, stronger cylinders.

**Role of Bulkheads (1.5 marks):**

Bulkheads provide:
- **Watertight compartmentalization** — a flood in one compartment does not sink the vehicle.
- **Structural stiffening** — they act as very large ring stiffeners at their locations.
- **Functional separation** — they divide the hull into battery, machinery, command, and crew sections.

**Role of Viewports (1 mark):**

Viewports are **transparent pressure windows** providing visual access to the external environment. They are made of thick acrylic (shallow) or conical glass (deep) and are designed as **thick-walled pressurized discs** with stress concentrations carefully managed at the seating (sealing cone) with the hull.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

| # | Criterion | Cylindrical Hull | Spherical Hull |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Membrane stress distribution | Non-uniform: hoop = $PR/t$, axial = $PR/2t$ | Uniform: $\sigma = PR/2t$ in all directions |
| 2 | Volume efficiency | **High** for $L > 2R$ (long, roomy interior) | **Low** — sphere wastes space for crew comfort |
| 3 | Buckling resistance | Susceptible to buckling at moderate $R/t$ | No classical buckling — fails only by yielding |
| 4 | Manufacturability | Easier to roll/form cylindrical plates; standard welding | Requires forging/forming hemispheres; complex welding |
| 5 | Hydrodynamic drag | Higher drag due to cylindrical shape | Lower drag (sphere is more streamlined per unit volume) |
| 6 | Internal layout | Long, modular, easy to add equipment | Cramped, single volume, no clear compartments |

**Preferred shape for FOD vehicles (1 mark):**

For **Full Ocean Depth (FOD, ~11,000 m) vehicles**, the **sphere is preferred** because:

- The hydrostatic pressure at FOD is approximately **110 MPa**, and the sphere's uniform stress state makes it the **only geometry that can be designed with reasonable safety margins** at such pressures.
- Cylinders would require impractically thick walls or extreme ring stiffener density.
- Examples: *DSV Limiting Factor* (Triton), *Fendouzhe* (China), *Shenhai Yongshi* (China) all use **titanium spherical pressure hulls** for FOD operation.

The sphere's lack of a long axis and absence of a buckling mode means it is the **inherently safest geometry** for ultra-deep diving.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Sign error in hoop stress** — Forgetting that external pressure produces **compressive** hoop stress. The magnitude $PR/t$ is the same, but the sign is negative. KTU examiners check this.
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $L_e$** — The total hull length is NOT used in buckling formulas; the **effective unsupported length between stiffeners/bulkheads** is used. Using the wrong $L$ overestimates $P_{cr}$ and underestimates risk.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — KTU strictly requires SI units (Pa, m, N, kg). Mixing MPa with mm is a frequent error.
> 4. **Mixing up "hoop" and "axial"** — Hoop is circumferential (around the cylinder); axial is along the length. Hoop = $PR/t$, Axial = $PR/2t$.
> 5. **Ignoring knockdown factor** — A real cylinder fails at 50–80% of the classical buckling pressure. KTU problems often specify the knockdown factor; do not skip it.
> 6. **Spherical hulls do NOT buckle** — In KTU 14-mark answers, students often write "spherical hull buckling" which is **wrong**. Spheres fail by yielding, not buckling.
> 7. **Forgetting safety factor** — The factor of safety $n$ must appear in every thickness calculation. Omitting it is a 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 4: Structure and Systems**

- ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ — use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ for seawater.
- ☐ **Hoop stress** in cylinder: $\sigma_h = PR/t$ — **2× the axial stress**.
- ☐ **Axial stress** in cylinder: $\sigma_a = PR/2t$.
- ☐ **Membrane stress** in sphere: $\sigma = PR/2t$ — **uniform** in all directions.
- ☐ **Yield-based thickness:** $t = nPR/\sigma_y$.
- ☐ **Classical buckling (long cylinder):** $P_{cr} = 2.6E/(R/t)^3$ (steel).
- ☐ **Knockdown factor** $k = 0.6$ to $0.8$ is applied for real cylinders.
- ☐ **Ring stiffeners** reduce effective length $L_e$ and increase $P_{cr}$.
- ☐ **Bulkheads** are both watertight compartments AND structural stiffeners.
- ☐ **Spheres** are preferred for ultra-deep (FOD) diving — no buckling, only yielding.
- ☐ **Cylinders** are preferred for moderate depth — better volume efficiency.
- ☐ **HY-80 steel:** $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa; **HY-100:** $\sigma_y = 690$ MPa; **Ti-6Al-4V:** $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa.
- ☐ **Material selection logic:** Steel for depth ≤ 6000 m (cost-effective); Titanium for depth > 6000 m (high strength-to-weight).
- ☐ **Major systems** to memorize: Ballast, Trim, Power, Propulsion, Life Support, Navigation, Communication, Hatch, Emergency.
- ☐ **Reserve buoyancy** = $W_{buoyancy,max} - W_{sub}$ — the ability to surface from any depth.
- ☐ **Submergence ratio** describes the fraction of displacement used for permanent ballast.
- ☐ **Conning tower** houses the hatch; **viewports** are thick acrylic/glass pressure windows; **penetrators** are sealed electrical pass-throughs.
- ☐ **Full Ocean Depth (FOD):** 11,000 m = Mariana Trench — only spherical titanium hulls can reach this reliably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, **always include a sketch or block diagram** — even a simple one earns you 1–2 extra marks. Use the diagrams from Section 4 as templates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Submarine Structure — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Submarine Structure (KTU Definition):**
> The submarine structure is a **compound, compartmented, pressure-resistant assembly** composed of an internal **pressure hull (strong hull / inner hull)** that resists hydrostatic collapse, an external **light hull (outer casing / free-flooding casing)** that shapes the vehicle, internal **ring frames, longitudinal girders, bulkheads, and conical/ellipsoidal end closures**, all fabricated from **high-yield quenched-and-tempered steels (HY-80 / HY-100) or titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** to provide safe, dry habitability and a controlled buoyancy envelope at operating depths.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term *Submarine Structure* is not a single member but an **integrated load-bearing system**. The structure must simultaneously satisfy:

- **Strength** against hydrostatic pressure-induced hoop and longitudinal stress
- **Stability** against elastic buckling (shell, ring, and stiffener modes)
- **Stiffness** for hydrodynamic line and silencing requirements
- **Survivability** against shock, fire, and flooding (residual strength)
- **Manufacturability** using curved plate rolling, automatic welding, and modular block construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A designer must understand (i) the functional role of the pressure hull vs. light hull, (ii) the choice of cylindrical vs. spherical geometry, (iii) ring-stiffened shell analysis, and (iv) the **scantling** (minimum required plate thickness & frame spacing) methodology prescribed by classification rules (DNV / NAVSEA / Lloyd's).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Deep-Diving Egg"

Imagine you are trying to take a **raw egg** to the bottom of a swimming pool without breaking it. If you simply paint the egg waterproof, the water pressure will crush it — eggshells are *thin spheres*, but our submarines are very large. The solution used in nature (and in submarines) is a **two-skin sandwich**:

| Component | Egg Analogy | Submarine Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Outer wet shell** | Thin painted film, lets water flow smoothly | **Light hull / outer casing** — fairing only |
| **Inner pressure vessel** | The actual membrane holding yolk & white | **Pressure hull** — holds crew, systems, air |
| **Stiffening ring** | Cardboard ring around the egg | **Ring frames** — prevent buckling of the pressure hull |
| **End caps** | Two tapered ends of the egg | **Ellipsoidal / conical end closures** |
| **Compartments** | White vs. yolk | **Bulkheads** dividing hull into watertight sections |

The crucial insight: **the pressure hull does not need to be shaped like a submarine.** A perfect cylinder or sphere is the strongest, cheapest shape. The submarine's iconic "torpedo" outline is provided by the **outer light hull**, which carries **no** pressure load at all — it simply streamlines the vehicle and houses ballast tanks.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The following metrics are the **numerical backbone** of every KTU numerical on submarine structure. Memorize them.

- **Sea-water density:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** $p \approx \mathbf{0.101\ MPa\ per\ 10\ m\ depth}$ (≈ 1 atm / 10 m)
- **Design safety factor (NAVSEA):** $SF = \mathbf{1.5\ to\ 2.0}$ on yield
- **Material yield strengths (typical):**
   * HY-80 steel: $\sigma_y = \mathbf{552\ MPa}$ (80 ksi)
   * HY-100 steel: $\sigma_y = \mathbf{690\ MPa}$ (100 ksi)
   * Ti-6Al-4V titanium: $\sigma_y = \mathbf{880\ MPa}$
- **Submarine operating depth envelope:**
   * Coastal / shallow: 200 m
   * Ocean-going (typical): 300 – 500 m
   * Deep-diving research: 1000 m+ (e.g., DSV *Limiting Factor*, 10 928 m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Hull Architecture — Functional Rationale

A modern military submarine (Type 214, Scorpène, Virginia-class) employs a **double-hull** configuration, while older designs (Type 209, many WWII U-boats) use a **single-hull** arrangement. The KTU syllabus requires understanding both.

### 2.1.1 Pressure Hull (Inner / Strong Hull)
- **Geometry:** Right circular cylinder with ellipsoidal or hemispherical end closures. Cylindrical midships maximises payload volume; spheres are used for high-pressure compartments (e.g., escape trunks, battery wells).
- **Load:** Carries the **entire hydrostatic pressure differential** between inside (1 atm) and outside (depth-induced hydrostatic pressure).
- **Failure modes the designer must defeat:**
   1. Material yielding (hoop stress exceedance)
   2. Elastic shell buckling (axisymmetric "chessboard" pattern)
   3. Ring-stiffener local buckling
   4. Inter-frame shell buckling between rings
   5. General instability (column-like)
   6. Fatigue from cyclic diving
- **Design philosophy:** **Stiffened shell** — the bare cylinder would buckle at very low pressure; adding closely-spaced **ring frames** raises the critical buckling pressure by an order of magnitude.

### 2.1.2 Light Hull / Outer Casing
- **Geometry:** Hydrodynamic teardrop, with sail (conning tower fairing) atop.
- **Load:** **Zero hydrostatic differential** in normal operation (vented to sea or flooded to free-flooding tanks).
- **Function:**
   * Streamlines the vehicle (drag reduction)
   * Houses **main ballast tanks (MBT)**, **free-flooding spaces**, **sonar arrays**
   * Provides crush depth protection (sacrificial outer layer)
   * Reduces radiated noise by isolating the pressure hull from turbulent boundary layer
- **Material:** Mild or HSS steel, lower strength grade — cost-optimised.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why double hull?** A single-hull boat must end the pressure hull at the ends of the cylinder with a flat or curved end cap that *is* the outer skin. A double-hull boat can extend the outer skin into a long taper, creating a much longer, quieter, more hydrodynamic vehicle — at the cost of larger surface area and weight.

## 2.2 Material Selection Logic

| Material | Yield Strength (MPa) | Density (kg/m³) | Specific Strength | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (ASTM A36) | 250 | 7850 | 31.8 | Light hull, non-pressure structures |
| HSS (A514) | 690 | 7850 | 87.9 | Some pressure hulls |
| HY-80 | 552 | 7850 | 70.3 | Most common NATO pressure hull steel |
| HY-100 | 690 | 7850 | 87.9 | Deeper-diving US submarines |
| HY-130 | 896 | 7850 | 114 | Deep-diving experimental hulls |
| Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5) | 880 | 4430 | 198.6 | Deep submersibles, Russian Alfa-class |

The **specific strength** (yield/density) is the key metric — it tells you how much pressure you can resist per unit of weight penalty. Titanium's specific strength is **2.5× that of HY-80**, which is why deep-diving submersibles use it.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum formula set** you must memorise for KTU Module 4. Practise solving at least one problem per row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $d$ | $p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d$ | $d$ in m, $p$ in Pa | $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$ |
| 2 | Hoop (circumferential) stress in thin cylinder | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{t}$ | $R$ = inner radius (m), $t$ = wall thickness (m) | Dominant stress; sets thickness |
| 3 | Longitudinal (axial) stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{2t}$ | — | Half the hoop stress |
| 4 | Required wall thickness (yield criterion) | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot R \cdot SF}{\sigma_y}$ | $SF$ = safety factor (1.5 – 2.0) | **Scantling** equation |
| 5 | Classical elastic buckling pressure (infinite cylinder, no rings) | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^{2.5}}{L/R}$ — *Bryan/Levy* | $E$ = Young's modulus, $L$ = cylinder length | Valid when $L/R$ is large |
| 6 | Critical buckling stress (von Mises) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{E \cdot t}{R \sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$ | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | For long cylinders |
| 7 | Critical pressure for ring-stiffened cylinder (Allen-Halgrave / DNV) | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^{2.5}}{(L_{eff}/R) - 0.45 \cdot (t/R)^{0.5}}$ | $L_{eff}$ = effective frame spacing | $L_{eff}$ replaces bare $L$ |
| 8 | Minimum frame spacing for shell stability | $L_{eff} = 0.75 \cdot R \cdot (t/R)^{0.5}$ (optimum) | — | Gives highest $p_{cr}$ for given $t$ |
| 9 | Ring frame section modulus (required) | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot R^2 \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{y,f} \cdot 1.5}$ | $Z$ in m³, $L_{eff}$ = frame spacing | Ring must yield before shell buckles |
| 10 | Ring frame moment of inertia (for local stability) | $I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot R^3 \cdot L_{eff}}{0.6 \cdot E}$ | $I$ in m⁴ | Prevents ring ovalisation |
| 11 | Hemispherical end cap thickness | $t_{hemi} = \dfrac{p \cdot R_{hemi}}{2 \sigma_y}$ | $R_{hemi}$ = sphere radius | Sphere is 2× stronger than cylinder |
| 12 | Ellipsoidal (2:1) end cap thickness | $t_{ell} = \dfrac{p \cdot R_{ell}}{\sigma_y}$ | — | Intermediate geometry |
| 13 | Submerged weight / buoyancy reserve | $W_{sub} = W_{dry} - \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume | Must be near zero, slightly negative |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** When writing equations inline, never use the bare vertical bar `|` for absolute value or shell thickness. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables. Example: write $\vert t \vert$, not $|t|$.

## 2.4 Why a Cylinder and Not a Sphere?

A sphere is the most efficient pressure vessel (volume-to-surface ratio is highest, and membrane stresses are uniform). So why do submarines use cylinders?

- **A sphere of equivalent interior volume** would have a much smaller radius → thinner walls are needed, BUT the **length is lost** — you cannot fit missile tubes, a long propulsion shaft, a control room, berths, etc., inside a sphere.
- A **long cylinder** plus **spherical end caps** is the optimal compromise: the cylinder uses the entire hull length for payload; the spheres handle the most stressed regions (ends) with the strongest shape.
- The penalty is a heavier hull — the cylinder must be stiffened by ring frames, which a sphere does not need.
- **The Teller–Hale limit:** the optimum blend places 60 % sphere + 40 % cylinder for purely spherical, and 20 % sphere + 80 % cylinder for the modern submarine (where length dominates).

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Pressure Cylinder

We derive the circumferential (hoop) stress in the pressure hull of a submarine operating at depth $d$.

**Step 1 — Determine the external pressure on the hull wall.**

The internal pressure is the atmospheric pressure $p_i = p_{atm}$ (1 atm). The external pressure at depth $d$ below the free surface is given by the hydrostatic equation. The **net compressive pressure** acting radially inward on the hull wall is therefore:

$$
p = p_{ext} - p_{int} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d
$$

where $p_{ext} - p_{int}$ denotes the net hydrostatic head acting on the hull. (For $d = 300\ m$ and $\rho_{sw} g = 10.05\ kPa/m$, this gives $p \approx 3.02\ MPa$.)

**Step 2 — Cut the cylinder with a diametral plane and apply force equilibrium.**

Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$ cut along a vertical diametral plane. The internal pressure acts radially outward on the curved surface. The vertical (longitudinal) components cancel by symmetry, but the **horizontal components** add up to a net outward force $F_{pressure}$ acting on the two cut edges:

$$
F_{pressure} = p \cdot (2R) \cdot L
$$

Here $2R$ is the projected diameter onto the cut plane, and $L$ is the length of the half-shell under consideration. The two straight edges of the cut are each of area $t \cdot L$ (shell thickness × length), so the total resisting cross-sectional area of the hull wall is $A_{wall} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L$.

**Step 3 — Equate the pressure force to the wall resisting force.**

The wall carries this load in hoop tension (or compression, since the outside pressure pushes inward). For static equilibrium of the cut section:

$$
p \cdot (2R) \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L)
$$

The factors of $L$ cancel from both sides, simplifying to:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot R}{t}
$$

This is the **hoop stress** equation. For the numerical example with $p = 3.02\ MPa$, $R = 3.3\ m$, $t = 0.04\ m$:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{3.02 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.3}{0.04} = 249.2\ MPa
$$

**Step 4 — Verification using the longitudinal stress.**

Cutting the cylinder with a transverse plane and balancing axial forces gives:

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{p \cdot R}{2 t} = 124.6\ MPa
$$

The ratio $\sigma_h / \sigma_L = 2$ is the classical result. **Hoop stress always governs pressure hull design** because it is twice the longitudinal stress for a closed cylinder.

**Step 5 — Apply the safety factor to obtain the design thickness.**

$$
t_{req} = \frac{p \cdot R \cdot SF}{\sigma_y}
$$

With $SF = 1.5$, $\sigma_y = 552\ MPa$ (HY-80), and using $p \cdot R = 9.97\ MN/m$:

$$
t_{req} = \frac{9.97 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.5}{552 \times 10^6} = 0.0271\ m \approx 27\ mm
$$

A fabrication thickness of ~32 mm (with corrosion + wear allowance) would be specified.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Buckling Pressure of a Ring-Stiffened Cylinder (Bryan / Allen-Halgrave Form)

A bare (unstiffened) long cylinder under external pressure fails by elastic shell buckling at a stress far below the material yield. We derive the critical pressure for the realistic case of a cylinder stiffened by equally spaced ring frames.

**Step 1 — Buckling mode of a long unstiffened cylinder.**

A long thin cylinder under external pressure buckles into a "chessboard" pattern of dimples. The number of circumferential waves $n$ and axial half-waves $m$ that form is governed by minimisation of the total strain energy. The classical result (Bryan, 1888; Lévy, 1884) for a long cylinder with simply-supported ends and clamped radial edge is:

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^{2.5}}{L/R}
$$

valid in the regime $L/R > 4 \cdot (R/t)^{0.5}$ (long-cylinder limit). For our example with $E = 207\ GPa$, $R/t = 82.5$, $L/R = 6$:

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot 207 \times 10^9 \cdot (0.0121)^{2.5}}{6} = 0.74\ MPa
$$

This is **only ~25 %** of the operating pressure 3.02 MPa. Without ring frames, the hull would buckle!

**Step 2 — Introduce ring stiffeners.**

Ring frames effectively **break the cylinder into shorter panels** of length $L_{eff}$ equal to the frame spacing. If $L_{eff} = 0.5\ m$ in our example, the new aspect ratio is $L_{eff}/R = 0.15$, far below the long-cylinder limit.

**Step 3 — Use the ring-stiffened cylinder formula.**

The classical Bryan/Lévy expression, modified for finite length panels, is the **Bryan-Halgrave** form, where the effective length $L_{eff}$ replaces the bare cylinder length $L$:

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^{2.5}}{(L_{eff}/R) - 0.45 \cdot (t/R)^{0.5}}
$$

This modification accounts for the boundary layer of stress concentration near the stiffener. Note the minus sign: the term in the denominator shrinks as $L_{eff}$ is reduced, so $p_{cr}$ rises rapidly. The optimum frame spacing that maximises $p_{cr}$ for a given thickness is:

$$
L_{opt} = 0.75 \cdot R \cdot (t/R)^{0.5}
$$

For our example, $L_{opt} = 0.75 \cdot 3.3 \cdot 0.11 = 0.272\ m$. Adopting $L_{eff} = 0.5\ m$ (slightly above optimum for fabrication ease):

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot 207 \times 10^9 \cdot (0.0121)^{2.5}}{(0.5/3.3) - 0.45 \cdot 0.11} = 31.7\ MPa
$$

The critical buckling pressure has jumped from **0.74 MPa to 31.7 MPa** — a factor of **43× improvement**. The hull now has a buckling reserve of more than 10× the operating pressure.

**Step 4 — Check material yield vs. buckling.**

The hoop stress at operating pressure is $\sigma_h = 249\ MPa$, well below HY-80's $\sigma_y = 552\ MPa$. The **governing failure mode is now neither yielding nor buckling** but **fatigue** and **residual stress from welding** — the real-world design drivers.

**Step 5 — Design the ring frame's moment of inertia and section modulus.**

To prevent the ring itself from ovalising under external pressure (which would then load the shell asymmetrically), the ring's moment of inertia must satisfy:

$$
I_{req} = \frac{p \cdot R^3 \cdot L_{eff}}{0.6 \cdot E} = \frac{3.02 \times 10^6 \cdot 35.94 \cdot 0.5}{0.6 \cdot 207 \times 10^9} = 4.37 \times 10^{-4}\ m^4
$$

A typical T-section ring (flange 100 mm × 30 mm, web 200 mm × 25 mm) achieves $I \approx 6 \times 10^{-4}\ m^4$, providing an adequate margin.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Submerged Weight and Buoyancy Reserve

A submarine must be in near-neutral buoyancy when submerged so that small changes in water density (thermocline) do not cause uncontrolled depth excursions.

**Step 1 — Define the dry weight and displaced volume.**

Let the dry weight (hull + systems + crew) be $W_{dry}$ and the total displaced volume (external envelope) be $\nabla$. The submarine displaces a volume of water whose weight is:

$$
W_{buoy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 2 — Define the net submerged weight (apparent weight in water).**

$$
W_{sub} = W_{dry} - W_{buoy} = W_{dry} - \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the neutral buoyancy condition.**

For a hovering submarine (sustaining depth with no up/down motion):

$$
W_{sub} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{dry} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small negative reserve for safety.**

Conventionally, the submarine is designed to be **slightly heavy** ($W_{sub} \approx -0.5\ \%\ W_{dry}$) so that emergency blows to surface are required, not flooding. This reserve can be countered by blowing the **main ballast tanks** (MBT) to surface, or by using **trim tanks** to adjust fore-aft attitude.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world rule of thumb:** for a typical 2 000-tonne submarine with a 9 m × 70 m envelope, $\nabla \approx 4 500\ m^3$, giving a displaced-water weight of ~46 MN, while $W_{dry}$ is matched to this with a ~0.3 % margin (≈ 140 kN). This is roughly the weight of 14 tonnes of water — a single tank or pump action.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Submarine External Profile]:::ext --> B[Light Hull / Outer Casing]
    A --> C[Fairing Sail Conning Tower]
    A --> D[Stern Hydroplanes and Propeller Shaft]

    B --> B1[Free Flooding Outer Void]
    B --> B2[Main Ballast Tank MBT Port]
    B --> B3[Main Ballast Tank MBT Starboard]
    B --> B4[External Sonar Array Dome]

    E[Pressure Hull Inner Strong Hull]:::prs --> E1[Cylindrical Mid Section]
    E --> E2[Forward Ellipsoidal Endcap]
    E --> E3[Aft Ellipsoidal Endcap]

    E1 --> F1[Ring Frames at 0.5 m Spacing]
    E1 --> F2[Longitudinal Stringers]
    E1 --> F3[HY-80 Steel Shell Plates]
    E1 --> F4[Internal Lead Lining for Radiation Shield]

    E1 --> G1[Forward Torpedo Compartment]
    E1 --> G2[Control Room and Operations]
    E1 --> G3[Machinery and Diesel Generators]
    E1 --> G4[Reactor Compartment Nuclear Subs]
    E1 --> G5[Aft Electric Motor and Pump Room]

    F1 -.->|Transfer hoop load| F3
    F2 -.->|Resist longitudinal bending| F3
    G1 --> H1[Watertight Bulkheads between Compartments]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1
    G5 --> H1

    classDef ext fill:#dfe8ff,stroke:#3357c8,color:#0a1f5c
    classDef prs fill:#ffe1d6,stroke:#c43a17,color:#5a0e00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Blue blocks = external/free-flooding components. Orange blocks = pressure-resisting internal structure. Note how the **ring frames** and **bulkheads** subdivide the pressure hull into discrete cells — this is the **crack-arrestor** principle, where any single flooding event is contained to one cell.

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Block — Load Path Through the Submarine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Hydrostatic Pressure at Operating Depth]:::ld --> P1[Net Compressive Radial Load on Shell]

    S --> R2[Longitudinal Stress Carried by Stringers and Endcaps]
```
