# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the pressure hull and the light hull of a submarine. Why are both required?**

**Model Answer:**

The **pressure hull** is the inner, fully-watertight cylindrical shell fabricated from high-yield quenched-and-tempered steel (HY-80) that carries the entire hydrostatic load at operating depth. The **light hull** is the outer, free-flooding casing fabricated from mild steel that shapes the vehicle hydrodynamically and houses ballast tanks, sonar arrays and free-flooding voids, but carries **no** pressure differential.

Both are required because the **optimal pressure shape** (sphere or short cylinder) is incompatible with the **optimal hydrodynamic shape** (long teardrop with appendages). The double-hull design decouples these two functions: the pressure hull is engineered purely for strength, while the light hull is engineered purely for hydrodynamics, stealth, and tankage. The single-hull alternative is shorter and lighter but cannot achieve the same length, speed, and quieting envelope as a double hull.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**Define "scantling" in submarine hull design. Write the equation for required pressure hull thickness based on yield criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Scantling** is the minimum required structural dimensions (plate thickness, frame spacing, section modulus) of a ship's or submarine's hull, as prescribed by classification society rules, to ensure the structure can withstand the maximum expected loads without failure.

The yield-based scantling equation for a cylindrical pressure hull is:

$$
t_{req} = \frac{p \cdot R \cdot SF}{\sigma_y}
$$

where $p$ = external hydrostatic pressure, $R$ = inner radius, $SF$ = safety factor (1.5 to 2.0 for submarine pressure hulls), and $\sigma_y$ = material yield strength.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure hull. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ arises in the circumferential direction of a cylindrical shell under internal or external pressure. For a long thin-walled cylinder of inner radius $R$ and wall thickness $t$ under external hydrostatic pressure $p$, the derivation proceeds in five steps.

**Step 1:** Cut the cylinder along a diametral plane and consider a half-shell of length $L$.

**Step 2:** The net pressure force on the half-shell acts radially outward (or inward for external pressure) and its horizontal component sums to a resultant force $F_p = p \cdot 2R \cdot L$ acting on the two diametral cut edges.

**Step 3:** The two cut edges of the cylinder wall each have cross-sectional area $t \cdot L$, so the total resisting area is $2tL$.

**Step 4:** Equate the pressure force to the wall tension (force balance):

$$
p \cdot 2R \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2tL
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $L$ from both sides and solve for $\sigma_h$:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot R}{t}
$$

The hoop stress is the **dominant membrane stress** in a pressure cylinder and is twice the longitudinal stress.

**(b) A submarine pressure hull has an inner radius of 3.5 m and is fabricated from HY-80 steel (yield 552 MPa). If the design depth is 350 m, determine: (i) the hydrostatic pressure, (ii) the required wall thickness using a safety factor of 1.6, and (iii) the hoop stress in the actual hull if the as-built thickness is 40 mm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Hydrostatic pressure at 350 m depth:**

Using $p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d$ with $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$, $d = 350\ m$:

$$
p = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 350 = 3.519 \times 10^6\ Pa \approx 3.52\ MPa
$$

**(ii) Required wall thickness (yield-based scantling):**

$$
t_{req} = \frac{p \cdot R \cdot SF}{\sigma_y} = \frac{3.52 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 1.6}{552 \times 10^6} = 0.0357\ m \approx 36\ mm
$$

**(iii) Hoop stress in the actual 40 mm hull:**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot R}{t} = \frac{3.52 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.5}{0.040} = 308\ MPa
$$

This is 55.8 % of HY-80's yield — the hull is well within elastic regime, with adequate margin against yielding.

**Validation:** A ring-stiffened buckling check (not required here) would further verify that the 40 mm hull with ~0.5 m frame spacing can sustain this pressure without elastic instability. The result confirms the design is safe.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**(a) Explain the need for ring stiffeners in a submarine pressure hull. With a neat sketch, describe the layout of a typical ring-stiffened cylinder. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A long unstiffened cylindrical pressure hull under external pressure buckles elastically at a pressure far below the material yield. For a typical 7 m diameter × 30 mm wall cylinder, the classical Bryan critical pressure is on the order of 0.5 MPa, while the operating pressure at 300 m is ~3 MPa. The hull would collapse long before the material yields. **Ring stiffeners** (closely-spaced circumferential frames welded to the inside of the shell) break the cylinder into short panels, dramatically raising the buckling pressure — typically by a factor of 20 to 50.

**Layout (textual sketch):**

```
         ┌──── ring frame T-section ────┐
         ▼                              ▼
        ╱ ╲                            ╱ ╲
       │   │  L_eff ≈ 0.5 m between   │   │
       │   │  successive ring frames  │   │
       │   │                          │   │
        ╲ ╱                            ╲ ╱
         ▲                              ▲
         └──── ring frame T-section ────┘
              (shell wrapped around)
```

The ring frames are typically T-sections or flat bars welded to the inside of the shell, oriented perpendicular to the hull axis, with spacing $L_{eff}$ chosen to be near the optimum value $0.75 \cdot R \cdot (t/R)^{0.5}$ to maximise the critical buckling pressure for a given shell thickness.

**(b) For a submarine pressure hull of radius 4 m, shell thickness 35 mm, and material HY-100 (E = 207 GPa, ν = 0.3), determine the optimum ring-frame spacing and the corresponding critical buckling pressure using the Bryan-Halgrave formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric ratios.**

$R/t = 4 / 0.035 = 114.3$. For HY-100: $E = 207\ GPa$, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 2 — Optimum frame spacing.**

$$
L_{opt} = 0.75 \cdot R \cdot (t/R)^{0.5} = 0.75 \cdot 4 \cdot (1/114.3)^{0.5} = 0.75 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.0935 = 0.281\ m
$$

Round to $L_{eff} = 0.28\ m$ for fabrication practicality.

**Step 3 — Apply Bryan-Halgrave formula.**

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/R)^{2.5}}{(L_{eff}/R) - 0.45 \cdot (t/R)^{0.5}}
$$

Compute each term:
- $t/R = 1/114.3 = 8.75 \times 10^{-3}$
- $(t/R)^{0.5} = 0.0935$
- $(t/R)^{2.5} = (8.75 \times 10^{-3})^{2.5} = 7.21 \times 10^{-6}$
- $L_{eff}/R = 0.28/4 = 0.07$

Substitute:

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot 207 \times 10^9 \cdot 7.21 \times 10^{-6}}{0.07 - 0.45 \cdot 0.0935} = \frac{3.881 \times 10^6}{0.07 - 0.0421} = \frac{3.881 \times 10^6}{0.0279}
$$

$$
p_{cr} \approx 139.1\ MPa
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and check.**

The critical buckling pressure is **139 MPa** — vastly higher than the operating hydrostatic pressure of ~3 to 5 MPa. The buckling reserve factor is $139 / 3.5 \approx 40$, confirming the design is exceptionally safe against elastic shell buckling.

**Cross-check via hoop stress:** At the operating pressure, the hoop stress is $\sigma_h = p \cdot R / t = 3.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 4 / 0.035 = 400\ MPa$, which is 58 % of HY-100's yield (690 MPa) — well within elastic regime.

**Conclusion:** Both yielding and buckling are well-controlled. Fatigue and weld residual stress are the governing design drivers in this regime, not primary strength.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in KTU Module 4 (Submarine Structure):**
>
> 1. **Confusing hoop and longitudinal stress.** Hoop stress is $pR/t$, longitudinal is $pR/(2t)$. Writing them backwards costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor.** Scantling equations in KTU papers always include a 1.5 to 2.0 SF. Submitting $t = pR/\sigma_y$ without SF is treated as a "non-engineering" answer and docked a mark.
> 3. **Using $R$ as outer radius instead of inner radius.** In thin-wall theory, $R$ is the **inner** radius. Mixing $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ in different parts of a derivation is a common error.
> 4. **Omitting ring-stiffener discussion in design questions.** A bare cylinder design is **incomplete** in any KTU Part B answer. You must justify frame spacing using the Bryan-Halgrave or DNV formulation.
> 5. **Stating "stainless steel" without grade.** Specify HY-80 / HY-100 / Ti-6Al-4V with yield strength. Generic terms lose marks.
> 6. **Missing the role of bulkheads.** Bulkheads serve as **flooding boundaries** (compartmentalisation) AND as **longitudinal stiffeners**. Mention both.
> 7. **Forgetting the free-flooding function of the light hull.** Stating that the outer hull "carries pressure" is a fundamental error. It carries **no** pressure differential.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Submarine Structure (Module 4):**
>
> - [ ] Submarine has **two hulls**: pressure hull (carries hydrostatic load) + light hull (streamlines, tanks).
> - [ ] Pressure hull geometry: **cylinder + ellipsoidal/spherical endcaps** (not pure sphere, because length matters).
> - [ ] Hoop stress = $pR/t$; **twice** the longitudinal stress → hoop stress **governs** design.
> - [ ] Scantling: $t_{req} = p \cdot R \cdot SF / \sigma_y$ with $SF \approx 1.5 - 2.0$.
> - [ ] Bare cylinder buckles far below yield → **ring frames are mandatory**.
> - [ ] **Bryan-Halgrave** formula: $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/R)^{2.5} / \big[ (L_{eff}/R) - 0.45 (t/R)^{0.5} \big]$.
> - [ ] **Optimum frame spacing**: $L_{opt} = 0.75 R (t/R)^{0.5}$.
> - [ ] Ring frames must satisfy **moment of inertia** ($I_{req} = pR^3 L_{eff} / 0.6E$) AND **section modulus** ($Z_{req} = pR^2 L_{eff} / 1.5 \sigma_{y,f}$) checks.
> - [ ] **Materials**: HY-80 (552 MPa), HY-100 (690 MPa), Ti-6Al-4V (880 MPa). **Specific strength** is the key metric for deep diving.
> - [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure gradient**: ≈ **0.101 MPa per 10 m** of depth.
> - [ ] **End caps**: hemispherical thickness = $pR/(2\sigma_y)$ — half the cylinder thickness; ellipsoidal (2:1) thickness = $pR/\sigma_y$.
> - [ ] **Bulkheads** divide the pressure hull into watertight compartments; serve as load-distributing transverse frames and as flooding boundaries.
> - [ ] **Submerged weight** must be near-neutral (slightly negative): $W_{sub} = W_{dry} - \rho_{sw} g \nabla \approx 0$ for hovering.
> - [ ] **Double hull** (modern) vs **single hull** (older) — trade-off: double hull gives longer, quieter boats at the cost of weight and surface area.
> - [ ] **Real-world failure modes** the designer must defeat: material yielding, elastic buckling, ring-stiffener instability, inter-frame shell buckling, general instability, fatigue, weld residual stress, and shock/blast loading.
> - [ ] **Specific strength** (yield/density) of Ti-6Al-4V is 2.5× that of HY-80 — this is why deep-diving submersibles (DSV *Alvin*, *Limiting Factor*) use titanium.
> - [ ] **Teller–Hale sphere-cylinder blend** is a classical optimisation result: most efficient pressure-vessel volume minimisation places 20 % sphere + 80 % cylinder for submarine proportions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational Requirement for Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operational Requirement for Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Operational Requirement for Depth

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Operational Depth Requirement** for a submersible is the formally specified maximum depth, expressed in **meters (m)** or **pounds per square inch (psi)**, to which a submersible is certified to safely conduct its intended mission profile (scientific survey, military patrol, search-and-recovery, pipeline inspection, etc.) without exceeding its structural, mechanical, or human-physiological limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Operational Depth is the deepest working depth at which the submersible can perform its designated mission repeatedly, safely, and reliably over its service life, while maintaining all safety margins against the external hydrostatic pressure.

In engineering practice, depth is **never** treated as a single number. Instead, KTU-aligned naval architecture standards define a **Depth Hierarchy** consisting of four critical depth levels:

| Depth Level | Standard Relationship to Operating Depth (OD) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Depth (OD)** | $1.0 \times OD$ | The rated, repeatable working depth |
| **Design Depth (DD)** | $1.25 \times OD$ | Structural design baseline |
| **Test Depth (TD)** | $1.10 \times DD$ (or $1.375 \times OD$) | Hydrostatic pressure test certification |
| **Collapse / Crush Depth (CD)** | $1.5 \times DD$ to $2.0 \times DD$ | Theoretical hull failure point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Concept:** The *Operational Requirement for Depth* is the starting point of the entire hull-girder design process. Every plate thickness, stiffener spacing, and material selection flows downstream from this single specification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **commercial passenger elevator** with a maximum safe load capacity of **1,000 kg**.

- The **rated capacity** is 1,000 kg — this is the *Operating Depth* for our submersible.
- The elevator's steel cables and motor are **engineered to handle** 1,250 kg — this is the *Design Depth* (a 25% safety margin).
- Before certification, the cables are **proof-tested** in a laboratory at 1,375 kg — this is the *Test Depth* (10% over-design).
- If you somehow loaded **2,000 kg**, the cables would **snap** — this is the *Collapse Depth*.

A submersible's *Operational Requirement for Depth* is exactly the same philosophy applied to ocean water pressure. The deeper the required mission, the higher the hydrostatic pressure, and the more robust the pressure hull must be engineered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Reality:** Every **10 meters** of seawater adds roughly **1 atmosphere (101.325 kPa)** of external pressure. A submersible rated for **1,000 m** operating depth must therefore survive an external pressure of approximately **100 atmospheres** pushing inward on every square inch of its hull.

### 1.3 Why Operational Depth Drives the Entire Design

The operational depth requirement is the **single most influential design driver** for a submersible because it directly determines:

1. **Hull material selection** (aluminum alloys for shallow, titanium/hy-80 steel for deep).
2. **Hull geometry** (spherical for deep — optimal pressure resistance; cylindrical for shallow — better payload volume).
3. **Wall thickness** (scales roughly linearly with depth for spheres).
4. **Buoyancy reserve** (sphere volume is fixed, so floatation systems must compensate).
5. **Life-support endurance** (deeper missions require longer descent/ascent, hence more life support).
6. **Cost** (depth and cost grow roughly **exponentially**, not linearly).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Foundation

The most fundamental physical principle governing operational depth is **Pascal's Law of Hydrostatic Pressure**. The external pressure on a submerged body is given by:

$$P_{ext} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $P_{ext}$ = Total external pressure acting on the hull, in Pascals (Pa)
- $P_{atm}$ = Atmospheric pressure at the sea surface ≈ **101,325 Pa**
- $\rho_{sw}$ = Density of seawater ≈ **1,025 kg/m³** (standard KTU exam value)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity ≈ **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = Depth below the sea surface, in meters (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In most board exam problems, $P_{atm}$ is **neglected** and only the **gauge pressure** is used: $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$. Always read the question carefully — if it says "gauge pressure" or "hydrostatic pressure", drop $P_{atm}$.

## 2.2 The Four-Tier Depth Hierarchy (KTU Standard)

### Tier 1 — Operating Depth (OD)
- The **rated, repeatable, certified** maximum working depth.
- The mission is guaranteed to function at this depth over the entire design life.
- Example: The *Aluminaut* was rated for an operating depth of **4,500 m**.

### Tier 2 — Design Depth (DD)
- Set at **1.25 × OD** per **DNV, ABS, and KTU-aligned** standards.
- The structural design (plate thickness, stiffener spacing, weld details) is calculated against the pressure at DD.
- This 25% margin absorbs: material property scatter, fatigue cycling, corrosion wastage, and accidental overruns.

### Tier 3 — Test Depth (TD)
- Set at **1.10 × DD** (equivalently **1.375 × OD**).
- Every pressure hull is **hydrostatically proof-tested** at TD in a pressure vessel facility **before** being installed in the submersible.
- Passing the TD test certifies the hull for service at OD.

### Tier 4 — Collapse Depth (CD)
- The theoretical depth at which the pressure hull undergoes **catastrophic elastic buckling** (for spheres) or **plastic collapse** (for cylinders).
- Set at typically **1.5 × DD to 2.0 × DD** as the ultimate safety floor.
- The submersible is **never designed to operate** at this depth — it is the failure boundary.

## 2.3 Factors That Define the Operational Depth Requirement

The operational depth is **not chosen arbitrarily**. It is the output of a **Mission Engineering Trade Study**, considering:

### A. Mission-Profile Factors
- **Survey depth** of the target seabed (e.g., abyssal plain ≈ 4,000 m; hadal trench ≈ 11,000 m).
- **Bottom time** required at depth (longer bottom time → larger life support → larger volume → larger pressure hull).
- **Transit time** to/from the depth (shorter if using a tow cable, longer if self-propelled).

### B. Environmental Factors
- **Sea state** (rough weather limits launch/recovery at depth).
- **Water density gradients** (thermocline effects on buoyancy).
- **Current speeds** at depth (deep currents are slower, aiding station-keeping).

### C. Regulatory & Class Factors
- **Classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) prescribe minimum safety factors.
- **Flag state regulations** (e.g., US Coast Guard, IMO).

### D. Economic Factors
- Going from **1,000 m to 6,000 m** operating depth increases hull cost by roughly **8 to 12 times**.
- This is why submersibles are built **only as deep as they must be**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Compute external hull pressure |
| 2 | Pressure in atmospheres | $P_{atm} \approx h / 10$ | atm | Quick rule of thumb (1 atm per 10 m) |
| 3 | Design Depth | $DD = 1.25 \times OD$ | m | Structural design baseline |
| 4 | Test Depth | $TD = 1.10 \times DD = 1.375 \times OD$ | m | Hydrostatic proof-test value |
| 5 | Collapse Depth | $CD \geq 1.5 \times DD$ | m | Buckling failure boundary |
| 6 | Safety Factor (Hull) | $SF = \sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{working}$ | dimensionless | Material margin against yielding |
| 7 | Sphere Hoop Stress | $\sigma = P \cdot r / (2t)$ | Pa | Stress in spherical pressure hull |
| 8 | Cylinder Hoop Stress | $\sigma = P \cdot r / t$ | Pa | Stress in cylindrical pressure hull |
| 9 | Sphere Wall Thickness | $t = P \cdot r / (2 \sigma_{allow})$ | m | Required thickness for spherical hull |
| 10 | Cylinder Wall Thickness | $t = P \cdot r / \sigma_{allow}$ | m | Required thickness for cylindrical hull |
| 11 | Buoyancy Force | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$ | N | Net upward force on hull |
| 12 | Reserve Buoyancy | $RB = F_B - W_{sub}$ | N | Positive value = will surface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - For **spherical hulls**: $\sigma_{sphere} = \sigma_{cylinder} / 2$ (sphere is twice as efficient).
> - For **operational depth > 3,000 m**: always prefer a **spherical pressure hull** (no stress concentration at ends).
> - For **operational depth < 1,000 m**: a **cylindrical hull with hemispherical end caps** is acceptable and gives better payload volume.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Depth-Hierarchy System

This four-tier system is not academic — it is a **legal, contractual, and insurance framework**. When a navy buys a submarine or an oil company hires an ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle), the contract specifies:
- "The vehicle shall operate to a depth of X meters."
- This X is the OD. Everything else (DD, TD, CD) flows from it, and **deviation in any tier is grounds for contract rejection**.
- The hierarchy also gives the **dive officer** a clear decision tree: "Approaching OD → begin ascent; approaching DD → abort mission; approaching TD → emergency surfacing."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure from First Principles

We start from the fundamental principle that **pressure in a static fluid varies linearly with depth**.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on a Fluid Column
Consider a vertical column of seawater with a cross-sectional area $A$ and a height $h$. The column is in static equilibrium, so the upward force from the pressure at the bottom must balance the weight of the fluid plus the atmospheric pressure pushing down on the top.

$$P_{bottom} \cdot A = P_{atm} \cdot A + W_{column}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Column Weight
The weight of the fluid column is its volume times density times gravity:

$$W_{column} = A \cdot h \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify
Substituting the weight into the force balance:

$$P_{bottom} \cdot A = P_{atm} \cdot A + A \cdot h \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g$$

Dividing both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$P_{bottom} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

### Step 4 — Define Gauge Pressure
The **gauge pressure** (which is what pressure gauges and most engineering calculations use) is the pressure **above atmospheric**:

$$P_{gauge} = P_{bottom} - P_{atm} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

This is the **canonical formula** for the external pressure acting on a submersible hull at depth $h$.

### Step 5 — Quick-Reference Numerical Form
For seawater with $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$P_{gauge} = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times h \approx 10{,}055 \cdot h \;\; \text{[Pa]}$$

A useful rule of thumb: **$P_{gauge} \approx 0.1 \text{ MPa per meter of depth}$**, or equivalently **1 atm per 10 m**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> **[Problem Statement]**
> A scientific submersible is specified with an **Operating Depth (OD) of 600 m**. The hull is a **spherical titanium pressure vessel** with an internal radius of **1.2 m**. The allowable working stress for the titanium alloy is **$\sigma_{allow} = 350$ MPa**. Using the standard KTU safety factors:
>
> **(a)** Calculate the hydrostatic gauge pressure at the Operating Depth.
> **(b)** Calculate the Design Depth and the Test Depth in meters.
> **(c)** Calculate the required minimum wall thickness of the spherical hull.

### Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at OD

Using the gauge pressure formula with $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$P_{OD} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 600$$

$$P_{OD} = 1{,}025 \times 5{,}886 = 6{,}033{,}150 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{OD} \approx 6.03 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Step 2 — Design Depth and Test Depth

Applying the KTU-standard depth hierarchy:

$$DD = 1.25 \times OD = 1.25 \times 600 = 750 \text{ m}$$

$$TD = 1.10 \times DD = 1.10 \times 750 = 825 \text{ m}$$

Equivalently, in pressure terms:

$$P_{DD} = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 750 = 7{,}541{,}438 \text{ Pa} \approx 7.54 \text{ MPa}$$

$$P_{TD} = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 825 = 8{,}295{,}581 \text{ Pa} \approx 8.30 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Wall Thickness for Spherical Hull

The **structural design** must be performed at the **Design Depth pressure**, not the Operating Depth pressure. This is the 25% safety margin in action.

For a spherical pressure vessel, the membrane (hoop) stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t}$$

Solving for the minimum required wall thickness $t$:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P_{DD} \cdot r}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$$

Substituting the values $P_{DD} = 7.54 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $r = 1.2 \text{ m}$, and $\sigma_{allow} = 350 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$:

$$t_{min} = \frac{7.54 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{2 \times 350 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{9.048 \times 10^6}{7.0 \times 10^8}$$

$$t_{min} = 0.01293 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{min} \approx 12.93 \text{ mm} \;\; (\text{say, 13 mm for procurement})}$$

### Step 4 — Verification of Safety Factor

Let us verify the actual safety factor of the design at the Operating Depth:

$$\sigma_{actual} = \frac{P_{OD} \cdot r}{2 \cdot t_{min}} = \frac{6.03 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{2 \times 0.01293}$$

$$\sigma_{actual} = \frac{7.236 \times 10^6}{0.02586} = 279.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 279.8 \text{ MPa}$$

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{actual}} = \frac{350}{279.8} \approx 1.25$$

The safety factor of **1.25** matches the **Design-Depth-to-Operating-Depth** ratio, confirming the consistency of the KTU hierarchy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Depth Hierarchy Decision Grid

| Parameter | Operating Depth | Design Depth | Test Depth | Collapse Depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Depth (m)** | 600 | 750 | 825 | ≥ 1,125 |
| **Pressure (MPa)** | 6.03 | 7.54 | 8.30 | ≥ 11.32 |
| **Hull Stress (MPa)** | 279.8 | 350.0 | 384.7 | ≥ 525 (yield) |
| **Mission Status** | ✅ Normal ops | ⚠️ Design limit | 🛑 Test only | ❌ Catastrophic failure |
| **Pilot Action** | Continue | Abort & ascend | Emergency surface | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Operational Depth Requirement System

This block diagram maps the **decision flow** by which an operational depth requirement is translated into a hull design specification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Profile Definition] --> B[Identify Maximum Required Seabed Depth]
    B --> C[Add Safety and Margin Buffers]
    C --> D{Operating Depth OD Fixed?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute Design Depth DD = 1.25 x OD]
    D -- No --> A
    E --> F[Compute Test Depth TD = 1.10 x DD]
    F --> G[Compute Collapse Depth CD = 1.5 to 2.0 x DD]
    G --> H[Select Hull Geometry]
    H --> I{Depth Greater than 3000 m?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Select Spherical Pressure Hull]
    I -- No --> K[Select Cylindrical Hull with End Caps]
    J --> L[Compute Required Wall Thickness]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Select Material and Allowable Stress]
    M --> N[Check Buoyancy and Stability]
    N --> O{Safety Factor Satisfied?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Finalize Design Specification]
    O -- No --> Q[Increase Thickness or Change Material]
    Q --> L
    P --> R[Fabricate and Hydrostatically Test at TD]
    R --> S[Certify for Service at OD]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Mission Depth to Certified Hull

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end certification flow**, showing how the operating depth requirement cascades through engineering analysis, manufacturing, and acceptance testing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1 Mission Engineering] --> step2[Step 2 Set Operating Depth]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Derive Design Depth]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Set Test Depth]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Calculate Hull Stresses]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Size Plate Thickness]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Weld and Assemble]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Hydrostatic Proof Test at TD]
    step8 --> step9{Test Passed?}
    step9 -- Yes --> step10[Step 9 Certify and Deliver]
    step9 -- No --> step11[Step 9b Reject and Rework]
    step11 --> step6
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Pressure Variation with Depth for a Spherical Hull

This block diagram is a functional analog of the physical pressure distribution experienced by a spherical pressure hull as it descends.

```mermaid
graph TD
    surfA[Sea Surface] -->|P atm 101 kPa| level1[0 m]
    level1 -->|Hydrostatic Column| level2[Operating Depth OD]
    level2 -->|Pressure P OD| level3[Design Depth DD]
    level3 -->|Pressure 1.25 P OD| level4[Test Depth TD]
    level4 -->|Pressure 1.375 P OD| level5[Collapse Depth CD]
    level5 -->|Hull Buckles| fail[Hull Failure]
    surfA --- desc[DESCENT PROFILE]
    desc --- stepA[Pressure increases linearly with depth]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **Operating Depth** of a submersible and state how it differs from **Design Depth** and **Test Depth**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Operating Depth (OD)** is the maximum depth at which a submersible is certified to safely and repeatedly perform its intended mission over its design service life **(1 mark)**.
- **Design Depth (DD)** is set at **1.25 × OD** and is the pressure level used as the structural design baseline for the pressure hull. This 25% margin accounts for material property scatter, fatigue, corrosion, and accidental overruns **(1 mark)**.
- **Test Depth (TD)** is set at **1.10 × DD (= 1.375 × OD)** and is the hydrostatic pressure at which every pressure hull is **proof-tested** in a workshop pressure vessel before installation. Passing the TD test certifies the hull for service at OD **(1 mark)**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A submersible is designed for an operating depth of **2,000 m**. Calculate the external hydrostatic pressure (in MPa) acting on its hull. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Write the hydrostatic pressure formula:

$$P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the given values:

$$P = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 2{,}000$$

Step 3 — Compute the result:

$$P = 20{,}110{,}500 \text{ Pa} \approx 20.11 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> **Final Answer:** The hydrostatic pressure at 2,000 m operating depth is **≈ 20.11 MPa** **(1 mark)**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four-tier depth hierarchy** used in submersible design. State the standard multiplication factors and the engineering purpose of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submersible is rated for an operating depth of **1,200 m**. The pressure hull is a **cylinder of internal radius 1.5 m** with hemispherical end caps. The allowable stress of the hull material is **$\sigma_{allow} = 280$ MPa**. Calculate: **(i)** the hydrostatic pressure at the **Design Depth**, and **(ii)** the required **wall thickness of the cylindrical section**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) The Four-Tier Depth Hierarchy (7 marks):**

The KTU-aligned depth hierarchy is a nested safety system that translates a single mission requirement (Operating Depth) into a fully engineered and tested pressure hull. The four tiers are:

1. **Operating Depth (OD)** — The rated, certified, and repeatable maximum working depth **(1.5 marks)**. The submersible is guaranteed by the designer and the classification society to operate safely at this depth for the entire service life. All mission planning, dive tables, and pilot briefs are based on OD.

2. **Design Depth (DD)** — Set at **1.25 × OD** **(1.5 marks)**. The pressure at DD is used as the structural design load for sizing plate thickness, stiffener spacing, and weld details. This 25% margin absorbs material property variation, fatigue accumulation, corrosion wastage over the hull's life, and the statistical risk of incidental over-dives.

3. **Test Depth (TD)** — Set at **1.10 × DD = 1.375 × OD** **(1.5 marks)**. Every fabricated pressure hull is hydrostatically proof-tested in a workshop pressure chamber at TD before being accepted. The test is non-destructive — it proves the hull can survive 10% beyond the design load without yielding or leaking. Passing TD is the legal basis for certification at OD.

4. **Collapse Depth (CD)** — The theoretical depth of catastrophic hull failure, set at **≥ 1.5 × DD** **(1.5 marks)**. For a spherical hull, this is the elastic-buckling pressure; for a cylindrical hull, it is the plastic-collapse pressure. The submersible is **never intended** to reach this depth. CD is the safety floor that ensures even if the dive control system fails completely, the hull retains residual strength.

A final concluding point: the hierarchy gives the dive officer a **clear decision tree** at sea — approach OD triggers routine ascent preparation, approach DD triggers mission abort, approach TD triggers emergency blow of all ballast tanks. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Pressure at Design Depth:**

First compute the design depth in meters:

$$DD = 1.25 \times OD = 1.25 \times 1{,}200 = 1{,}500 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Hydrostatic pressure at DD:

$$P_{DD} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot DD = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 1{,}500 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$P_{DD} = 15{,}082{,}875 \text{ Pa} \approx 15.08 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Cylindrical Wall Thickness:**

The hoop stress formula for a thin-walled cylinder is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{t} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Solving for wall thickness $t$:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P_{DD} \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{15.08 \times 10^6 \times 1.5}{280 \times 10^6} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{22.62 \times 10^6}{280 \times 10^6} = 0.0808 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> **Final Answer:** $t_{min} \approx \textbf{80.8 mm} \;\; (\text{or 81 mm for procurement})$ **(1 mark)**

Note: The hemispherical end caps would require **half** this thickness ($\approx 40.4 \text{ mm}$), illustrating the engineering efficiency of sphere geometry.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **mission, environmental, regulatory, and economic factors** that determine the operational depth requirement of a submersible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deep-submergence vehicle has a **spherical titanium pressure hull** with internal radius **1.0 m** and is rated for an operating depth of **4,500 m**. The allowable stress of the titanium is **$\sigma_{allow} = 450$ MPa**, and the seawater density is **$1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. Calculate: **(i)** the hydrostatic pressure at the **Test Depth**, and **(ii)** the **minimum required wall thickness** of the spherical hull. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Factors Determining the Operational Depth Requirement (7 marks):**

1. **Mission-Profile Factors (2 marks):** The primary driver is the depth of the target — e.g., surveying an abyssal plain (~4,000 m) versus inspecting a shallow pipeline (~300 m). The required **bottom time** (hours spent at depth) and **transit time** (descent + ascent) together dictate the size of the pressure hull, life-support system, and energy storage, all of which scale with depth.

2. **Environmental Factors (1.5 marks):** Local sea state at the surface governs launch and recovery conditions. Thermoclines and haloclines create density gradients that affect buoyancy control. Deep currents are generally slower than surface currents, but their direction and magnitude must be considered for station-keeping.

3. **Regulatory and Class Factors (1.5 marks):** Classification societies (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register) publish rules prescribing minimum safety factors, non-destructive testing protocols, and proof-test pressures. The **flag state** (country of registration) and **port state** authorities also impose their own rules, particularly for human-occupied vehicles.

4. **Economic Factors (2 marks):** Hull cost scales **roughly exponentially** with operating depth — going from 1,000 m to 6,000 m can multiply the hull cost by 8 to 12 times. The operator therefore specifies the **minimum depth** that meets the mission, and never "over-specifies" depth. A common engineering trade-off is: build for the *deepest practical* depth, or use a **tethered ROV** instead of a free-swimming submersible.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Pressure at Test Depth:**

First compute the test depth in meters:

$$TD = 1.375 \times OD = 1.375 \times 4{,}500 = 6{,}187.5 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Hydrostatic pressure at TD:

$$P_{TD} = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 6{,}187.5 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$P_{TD} = 62{,}222{,}719 \text{ Pa} \approx 62.22 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Spherical Wall Thickness:**

The hoop stress formula for a thin-walled sphere is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r}{2t} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Structural design is performed at the **Design Depth**, not the Test Depth. The Design Depth is:

$$DD = 1.25 \times OD = 1.25 \times 4{,}500 = 5{,}625 \text{ m}$$

$$P_{DD} = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 5{,}625 = 56{,}565{,}938 \text{ Pa} \approx 56.57 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Solving for the required thickness:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P_{DD} \cdot r}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{56.57 \times 10^6 \times 1.0}{2 \times 450 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{56.57 \times 10^6}{900 \times 10^6} = 0.0629 \text{ m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> **Final Answer:** $t_{min} \approx \textbf{62.9 mm} \;\; (\text{say, 65 mm for procurement})$ **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing OD with DD**: The most common error. Wall-thickness calculations must use **Design Depth pressure** ($1.25 \times OD$), not the Operating Depth pressure. If you compute at OD, you will under-design the hull and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for spheres**: A common trap. Cylinder stress is $P \cdot r / t$, but sphere stress is $P \cdot r / (2t)$. Mixing them up gives a hull that is **half as thick as required** — catastrophically unsafe.
> 3. **Including atmospheric pressure when not asked**: If the question says "hydrostatic" or "gauge" pressure, use $P = \rho g h$ only. If it says "absolute", then add $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa. Reading the wording carefully is worth 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong multiplication factors**: Memorize the KTU standard ratios — $DD = 1.25 \times OD$ and $TD = 1.375 \times OD$ (or $1.10 \times DD$). The examiner expects these exact factors.
> 5. **No unit consistency**: Always carry units through the calculation. Writing "MPa" when the answer is in "Pa" (or vice-versa) is a frequent 1-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Operating Depth (OD)** is the **rated, certified, repeatable** working depth.
- [x] **Design Depth (DD) = 1.25 × OD** is the structural design load.
- [x] **Test Depth (TD) = 1.10 × DD = 1.375 × OD** is the hydrostatic proof-test pressure.
- [x] **Collapse Depth (CD) ≥ 1.5 × DD** is the theoretical buckling/failure boundary.
- [x] Hydrostatic pressure formula: $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ with $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- [x] Quick rule: **1 atm per 10 m** of seawater depth.
- [x] **Sphere** stress = $P \cdot r / (2t)$ — sphere is **twice as strong** as a cylinder of the same radius and thickness.
- [x] **Cylinder** stress = $P \cdot r / t$ — preferred for shallow depths (better payload volume).
- [x] For depth **> 3,000 m**, always prefer a **spherical pressure hull**.
- [x] Wall thickness is computed at the **Design Depth**, not the Operating Depth.
- [x] Mission depth, environment, class rules, and economics jointly define the OD.
- [x] Hull cost scales **roughly exponentially** with operating depth.
- [x] The dive officer uses the four-tier system as a **decision tree** during the dive.
- [x] The TD test is **non-destructive** but certifies the hull for service at OD.
- [x] Reserve buoyancy must be **positive** at all times to ensure the sub can surface.
- [x] KTU board exams expect you to state the standard **multiplication factors verbatim** (1.25 and 1.10/1.375).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Hull Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Hull Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Hull** is the watertight, load-bearing structural shell of a submersible (submarine or deep-diving vehicle) that encloses all crew, payload, and vital machinery, and is designed to resist the **external hydrostatic pressure** acting on it at the maximum operating depth while maintaining a **negligible internal pressure** (typically 1 atm surface pressure).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Pressure Hull Design** is the systematic engineering process of proportioning the geometry, selecting the material, sizing the shell plating, and configuring the stiffening arrangement of the cylindrical/spherical shell such that it safely withstands the combined effects of:
1. **Hoop membrane stress** (circumferential)
2. **Longitudinal membrane stress** (axial)
3. **Local / overall elastic instability** (shell buckling)
4. **Material yielding and rupture**

without failure, throughout the design life of the vessel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Deep-Sea Egg**
> Think of a pressure hull as a **hard-boiled egg** lowered into a deep lake. The deeper you go, the more the water tries to **crush** the egg from all sides. The shell of the egg is the "hull plate." A sphere is the strongest natural shape (no corners for stress to concentrate), just like an egg's curved surface. A long cylinder acts like a **metal can** — strong across its belly (hoop direction) but weaker when squeezed from the ends. Engineers therefore either use **spherical hulls** (deep-diving research subs like *Alvin* and *DSV Limiting Factor*) for extreme depths, or **ring-stiffened cylindrical hulls** (most military submarines) because cylinders are practical to build, equip, and sail through the water.

## 1.3 Key Design Pressures and Terminology

| Design Depth Term | Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Depth** | Maximum depth at which the sub can perform missions | $D_{op}$ |
| **Crush Depth** | Depth at which the hull is expected to collapse catastrophically | $D_{crush} \approx 1.5 \times D_{op}$ to $2.0 \times D_{op}$ |
| **Design (Collapse) Pressure** | External pressure used as design load | $p_d = 1.5 \times p_{op}$ |
| **Test Pressure** | Pressure applied during hydrostatic test (≈ 1.25 × design) | $p_t$ |
| **Yield Strength of Material** | Stress at which permanent deformation begins (e.g., HY-80 ≈ **550 MPa**, HY-100 ≈ **690 MPa**, HY-130 ≈ **895 MPa**, Titanium Ti-6Al-4V ≈ **880 MPa**) | $\sigma_y$ |
| **Young's Modulus** | For steel ≈ **2.07 × 10⁵ MPa**; for Titanium ≈ **1.14 × 10⁵ MPa** | $E$ |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | For steel ≈ **0.3**; for Titanium ≈ **0.34** | $\nu$ |
| **Density of Seawater** | Used to convert depth to pressure ≈ **1025 kg/m³** | $\rho_{sw}$ |
| **Gravitational Acceleration** | **9.81 m/s²** | $g$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Pressure from Depth:**
> $$p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$
> where $h$ is depth in metres. For seawater, the rule of thumb is **1 atm ≈ 10 m of seawater**, so pressure at 1000 m ≈ 100 atm ≈ **10.13 MPa**.

## 1.4 Why Pressure Hull Design is the Heart of Submersible Engineering

The pressure hull is **THE** critical safety item. Every other system (propulsion, life support, control) is a "black box" mounted inside it. Failure of the hull = total loss of vessel + crew. Hence KTU 2024 Scheme treats it as a **failure-critical** design problem governed by:
- **Defence standards (e.g., DDS 102, NAVSEA 0900-LP-077-1010)** for military subs
- **ASME BPVC Section VIII, Div 2** for pressure vessels
- **Classification society rules (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register)** for civilian subs
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Four Failure Modes to Check

A pressure hull must be sized such that **NONE** of the following occur:

1. **Material Yielding (Strength criterion)** — Hoop/longitudinal stresses must remain below allowable stress (σ_allow = σ_y / SF, where SF is the safety factor, typically 1.5 to 2.0).
2. **Elastic / Plastic Buckling (Stability criterion)** — The cylindrical shell may "wrinkle" or "oil-can" long before yielding. This is the **dominant failure mode** for thin-walled cylinders.
3. **Fatigue / Crack Propagation** — Repeated pressure cycles during dives cause fatigue; must be checked using fracture mechanics.
4. **Ultimate Rupture** — Bursting pressure must be ≥ 1.5 × design pressure with a high safety factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **For thin-walled cylindrical pressure hulls, BUCKLING (not yielding) is the controlling design criterion.** This is a high-yield exam point.

## 2.2 Thin-Walled Cylindrical Hull — Membrane Stresses

For a long cylinder of **diameter D, length L, and wall thickness t**, subjected to external pressure p:

### 2.2.1 Hoop (Circumferential) Stress
Arises from balancing the radial pressure on a diametral strip:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} \quad \text{(MPa)}$$

This is the **LARGEST** stress in the hull and dominates the design.

### 2.2.2 Longitudinal (Axial) Stress
From end-cap force balance on a hemispherical/ellipsoidal closure:

$$\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t} \quad \text{(MPa)}$$

Note that $\sigma_L = 0.5 \sigma_h$.

### 2.2.3 Von Mises (Equivalent) Stress
Since the cylinder is in **biaxial stress state** (hoop + longitudinal), the equivalent stress for yielding check is:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_L + \sigma_L^2}$$

Substituting the above relations:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{pD}{2t}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{pD}{2t}\right)\left(\frac{pD}{4t}\right) + \left(\frac{pD}{4t}\right)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{pD}{2t}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \frac{pD}{t}$$

## 2.3 Spherical Pressure Hull

For a **sphere** of radius R and thickness t under external pressure p:

$$\sigma_{sphere} = \frac{pR}{2t}$$

For equal pressure and material, a sphere has **half the stress** of a cylinder — which is why deep submersibles prefer spheres. The hoop stress formula for cylinder can be obtained by substituting $R = D/2$:

$$\sigma_{h,cyl} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{sphere}$$

## 2.4 Elastic Buckling of Unstiffened Cylindrical Shells

A long thin cylinder under external pressure buckles elastically. Two regimes exist based on the **Batdorf parameter** $Z = \frac{L^2}{Dt}\sqrt{1 - \nu^2}$:

### 2.4.1 Long Cylinder ($Z \geq 2.85$, i.e., $L/D \geq $ large)
Failure occurs by buckling of the shell as a whole (ring-type buckle):

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$$

This is the **Bryan-Bryan formula** simplified form. More precisely, per **ASME BPVC** (external pressure chart for cylinders):

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3 \cdot \frac{2.6}{L/D}$$

### 2.4.2 Short Cylinder ($Z < 2.85$)
Failure occurs by local panel buckling between the heads:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)} \quad \text{transitioning to:}$$

$$p_{cr} = 0.92 \, E \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2.5} \cdot \frac{1}{L/D} \quad \text{(short cyl, head-loaded buckling)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Most authoritative form (DNV / ABS) for unstiffened cylinder under external pressure:**
> $$p_{cr} = \frac{2 \cdot 2.6 \cdot E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$
> for long cylinders. The factor **2.6** arises from the **Bresse-Timoshenko solution** for simply-supported shells.

## 2.5 Ring-Stiffened Cylinders (Real Submarine Hulls)

Real submarine pressure hulls are **ring-stiffened** (T-frames or H-frames placed circumferentially) to raise the buckling pressure dramatically. The critical buckling pressure for a ring-stiffened cylinder is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{1}{4} \left(N_E + p_L h\right) \frac{t}{R}\sqrt{1 + \frac{p_L h}{N_E}}$$

where:
- $N_E = \frac{E \, t^3}{4(1-\nu^2) R^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(L_{st}/R)}$ — shell stiffness term
- $p_L = \frac{3 E I}{R^3 L_{st}}$ — ring stiffness term (Bresse formula for ring buckling)
- $L_{st}$ = spacing between ring stiffeners
- $I$ = moment of inertia of ring-stiffener cross-section

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "effective" cylinder between rings behaves like a short cylinder with $L = L_{st}$**, while the rings act as supports. This is the key idea behind ring stiffening.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hoop stress in cylinder | $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$ | MPa | Largest primary stress |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_L = pD/(4t)$ | MPa | = 0.5 × σ_h |
| 3 | Von Mises stress | $\sigma_{VM} = (\sqrt{3}/4)\cdot pD/t$ | MPa | Biaxial yield check |
| 4 | Stress in sphere | $\sigma = pR/(2t)$ | MPa | Half of cylinder hoop |
| 5 | Pressure from depth | $p = \rho g h$ | Pa | $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³ |
| 6 | Long cylinder buckling | $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}/(L/D)$ | MPa | Bryan-Bryan |
| 7 | Short cylinder buckling | $p_{cr} = 0.92 E (t/R)^{2.5}/(L/D)$ | MPa | Head-loaded |
| 8 | Ring-stiffened shell | $p_{cr} = 0.25 (N_E + p_L h)(t/R)\sqrt{1+p_L h/N_E}$ | MPa | DNV / ABS form |
| 9 | Ring (Bresse) buckling | $p_L = 3EI / (R^3 L_{st})$ | MPa | Ring must not buckle first |
| 10 | Required shell thickness (yield) | $t = pD/(2\sigma_{allow})$ | m | σ_allow = σ_y/SF |
| 11 | Required thickness (buckling) | $t \geq D \left(p/(2.6 E \cdot f_{SF})\right)^{0.4}$ | m | Iterative |
| 12 | Bursting pressure | $p_{burst} = 2 \sigma_{uts} t / D$ | MPa | Ultimate check |
| 13 | Safety factor on buckling | $SF = p_{cr}/p_{design}$ | – | Typically ≥ 1.5 |
| 14 | Stress in cone (half-angle α) | $\sigma = pD/(2t \cos\alpha)$ | MPa | For conical hulls |
| 15 | End-cap (hemispherical) | $\sigma = pD/(4t)$ | MPa | = longitudinal stress |

## 2.7 Material Selection Rationale

- **HY-80, HY-100, HY-130** (High Yield steels) — Quenched & tempered low-alloy steels. HY-130 used for deepest military hulls.
- **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** — Used in *Alvin* and *DSV Limiting Factor* for > 6000 m dives. Lower $E$ → less buckling resistance, but lower density, zero corrosion, and very high yield.
- **Aluminum alloys (5083, 5456)** — Used in older deep submergence vehicles (DSCV) because of non-magnetic requirement.
- **Glass / Acrylic** — Used only for **viewports**, not hulls.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **ring-stiffened cylindrical hull** is used in virtually every conventional submarine (Type 214, Virginia, Scorpène).
- The **sphere** is the shape of choice for the **personnel sphere** of DSVs (Alvin, Limiting Factor, Shinkai 6500).
- **Conical hull transitions** are used between the cylindrical hull and the bow/sonar dome.
- **End closures** are usually hemispherical (most efficient) or ellipsoidal (2:1 ratio) — flat closures waste material.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress in a Cylindrical Pressure Hull

### Step 1: Consider a unit length of the cylinder
Take a small section of the cylindrical shell of length $L = 1$ (unit length), radius $R$, and thickness $t$. The diameter is $D = 2R$.

### Step 2: Free Body Cut
Imagine cutting the cylinder in half along a longitudinal (axial) plane. The two halves are held together by hoop tension in the shell.

### Step 3: Force Balance on the Half-Cylinder
The external pressure $p$ acts on the projected area (a rectangle of length $L$ and width $D$):

$$F_{pressure} = p \cdot D \cdot L = p \cdot D \cdot 1 = pD \quad \text{(per unit length)}$$

This outward force is resisted by hoop tension acting in **two cut edges**, each of cross-sectional area $t \times L = t \times 1 = t$:

$$F_{hoop} = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot 1 = 2 \sigma_h t$$

### Step 4: Equating forces for equilibrium
$$2 \sigma_h t = p D$$

Solving for the hoop stress:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}}$$

### Step 5: Verification by dimensional check
- Numerator: $pD$ → (Pa)(m) = N/m
- Denominator: $t$ → m
- Result: N/m² = Pa ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal Stress in a Cylindrical Pressure Hull

### Step 1: Consider a section at the end-cap
Imagine cutting the cylinder transversely (perpendicular to its axis). One half is closed by an end dome, the other half is supported by longitudinal tension in the shell.

### Step 2: Projected area at the cut
For external pressure $p$ acting on the closed end, the projected area is a circle of diameter $D$:

$$F_{pressure,end} = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3: Resistance from longitudinal stress
The longitudinal stress $\sigma_L$ acts over the entire cross-section of the shell (a ring of thickness $t$ and mean circumference $\pi D$):

$$F_{longitudinal} = \sigma_L \cdot \pi D \cdot t$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium
$$\sigma_L \cdot \pi D t = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi D$:

$$\sigma_L t = \frac{p D}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t}}$$

### Step 5: Comparison
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_L} = \frac{pD/(2t)}{pD/(4t)} = 2$$

So hoop stress is **exactly twice** the longitudinal stress. This is why the **hoop direction is critical** for cylindrical pressure hulls.

## 3.3 Derivation of Von Mises Stress for Cylinder under External Pressure

### Step 1: Biaxial stress state
The cylinder experiences two normal stresses:
- $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$
- $\sigma_L = pD/(4t)$
- $\sigma_r \approx 0$ (radial stress is small in thin shells)

### Step 2: Von Mises criterion
The general Von Mises equivalent stress for plane stress is:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

where $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$ and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_L$.

### Step 3: Substitute
Let $k = pD/t$. Then $\sigma_h = k/2$ and $\sigma_L = k/4$.

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{k}{2}\right)\left(\frac{k}{4}\right) + \left(\frac{k}{4}\right)^2}$$

### Step 4: Factor out k²/16
$$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{k}{4}\sqrt{4 - 2 + 1} = \frac{k}{4}\sqrt{3}$$

### Step 5: Final expression
$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \frac{pD}{t} \approx 0.433 \cdot \frac{pD}{t}}$$

### Step 6: Yield criterion
The cylinder is safe from yielding if:

$$\sigma_{VM} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{SF}$$

$$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \frac{pD}{t} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{SF}$$

Solving for the minimum thickness:

$$\boxed{t_{min,yield} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot SF \cdot p \cdot D}{4 \sigma_y}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Bryan-Bryan Buckling Pressure (Long Cylinder)

### Step 1: Geometric idealization
A long thin cylinder buckles into a pattern of **diamond-shaped lobes** (typically $m = 2$ circumferential waves) and $n$ longitudinal half-waves. The critical mode minimizes $p_{cr}$.

### Step 2: Energy method (Timoshenko)
Using the classical Donnell-type shell equation and assuming a sinusoidal buckling mode, the critical external pressure for a long cylinder is:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2 E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$$

### Step 3: Refined form (von Mises)
Von Mises derived a more accurate expression including Poisson's effect:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

for $L \gg D$ (truly long cylinder, axisymmetric mode dominates).

### Step 4: Numerical evaluation for a typical submarine
Take HY-80 steel, $D = 8$ m, $t = 0.06$ m, $E = 2.07 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 2.07 \times 10^5}{1 - 0.09} \times \left(\frac{0.06}{8}\right)^3$$

$$= \frac{5.382 \times 10^5}{0.91} \times (0.0075)^3$$

$$= 5.914 \times 10^5 \times 4.219 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$= 0.2495 \, \text{MPa} \approx 2.5 \, \text{atm}$$

This corresponds to a depth of about **25 m** — clearly too low! Hence the need for **ring stiffening** to raise $p_{cr}$ by an order of magnitude.

## 3.5 Analytical Problem Walkthrough — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (KTU typical 14-mark type):**
> A submersible's pressure hull is a ring-stiffened cylinder of diameter 6 m, length 40 m, with shell thickness 32 mm. Rings are spaced at 0.8 m intervals. Material is HY-80 ($E = 2.07 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa). Find the design depth if safety factor on buckling is 1.5.

**Step 1: Effective length (between rings)**
$$L_{st} = 0.8 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Check long vs. short cylinder**
Compute Batdorf parameter $Z$:
$$Z = \frac{L^2}{Dt}\sqrt{1-\nu^2} = \frac{(0.8)^2}{6 \times 0.032}\sqrt{0.91}$$
$$Z = \frac{0.64}{0.192} \times 0.954 = 3.18$$

Since $Z \geq 2.85$, the panel between rings behaves as a **long cylinder**. Use the **long cylinder** formula.

**Step 3: Long cylinder buckling pressure**
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E}{1-\nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$
$$= \frac{2.6 \times 2.07 \times 10^5}{0.91} \times \left(\frac{0.032}{6}\right)^3$$
$$= 5.914 \times 10^5 \times (0.00533)^3$$
$$= 5.914 \times 10^5 \times 1.516 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$= 0.0897 \, \text{MPa} = 89.7 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor**
$$p_{design} = \frac{p_{cr}}{SF} = \frac{89.7}{1.5} = 59.8 \, \text{kPa} = 0.0598 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5: Convert to depth**
$$h = \frac{p}{\rho g} = \frac{59800}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{59800}{10055.25} = 5.95 \, \text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Wait — this depth is unrealistically shallow! This shows that **unstiffened shell between rings is NOT the design**. We need to also check the **ring buckling (Bresse formula)**. The lower of the two $p_{cr}$ values governs.

**Step 6: Check yielding (Von Mises)**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \frac{pD}{t} = 0.433 \times \frac{0.0598 \times 6}{0.032}$$
$$= 0.433 \times 11.21 = 4.85 \, \text{MPa}$$

Since $4.85 \ll 550$ MPa, **yielding is not an issue** for this thin shell. Buckling dominates — confirming the rule stated in §2.1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This analytical example illustrates that for thin-walled cylindrical pressure hulls, **the buckling (stability) check ALWAYS controls over the yielding (strength) check.** A correctly sized submarine hull has huge reserve strength against material yielding but is on the brink of buckling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pressure Hull Design Process

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **end-to-end engineering workflow** for designing a submersible pressure hull, as required by KTU 2024 Scheme and classification society rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Mission Profile Definition] --> B[Step 2: Determine Maximum Operating Depth D_op]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Design Collapse Pressure p_d = 1.5 rho g D_op]
    C --> D[Step 4: Select Hull Geometry: Cylinder Sphere Cone Composite]
    D --> E[Step 5: Select Material: HY-80 HY-100 HY-130 Titanium Al5083]
    E --> F[Step 6: Initial Sizing by Yielding t_min = sqrt3 SF p D / 4 sigma_y]
    F --> G[Step 7: Buckling Check - Unstiffened Cylinder Bryan-Bryan Formula]
    G --> H{Step 8: Buckling p_cr < p_d?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Step 9: Add Ring Stiffeners T-frames H-frames]
    I --> J[Step 10: Recompute p_cr for Ring Stiffened Cylinder]
    J --> K{Step 11: p_cr / SF >= p_d?}
    H -- No --> K
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[Step 12: Check Ring Bresse Buckling p_L = 3EI / R^3 L_st]
    L --> M{Step 13: p_L >= p_cr?}
    M -- No --> N[Step 14: Increase Ring Size or Reduce Spacing]
    N --> J
    M -- Yes --> O[Step 15: Check End Closures Hemispherical Ellipsoidal 2:1]
    O --> P[Step 16: Check Penetrations and Openings Reinforcement]
    P --> Q[Step 17: Fatigue and Fracture Mechanics Check]
    Q --> R[Step 18: Hydrostatic Test at 1.25 p_d]
    R --> S[Step 19: Classification Society Certification DNV ABS Lloyd]
    S --> T[Step 20: Final Approved Pressure Hull Design]
```

## 4.2 Geometry and Stress Distribution Schematic (Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps how external pressure is transmitted to the cylindrical shell and how stress flows through the structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Seawater Hydrostatic Pressure p] --> B[External Hull Surface]
    B --> C[Ring Stiffener T-frame]
    B --> D[Shell Panel between Rings]
    D --> E[Hoop Stress sigma_h = pD/2t]
    D --> F[Longitudinal Stress sigma_L = pD/4t]
    E --> G[Von Mises Stress = sqrt3/4 x pD/t]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Sigma_VM less than sigma_y / SF}
    H -- Yes --> I[Safe from Yielding]
    H -- No --> J[Increase t or Use Higher Yield Material]
    C --> K[Ring Bresse Buckling p_L = 3EI / R cubed L_st]
    K --> L{Ring Stiffness Adequate}
    L -- Yes --> M[Ring Stiffened Buckling p_cr computed]
    L -- No --> N[Increase Ring Moment of Inertia I]
    M --> O{Final p_cr / SF greater than p_d}
    O -- Yes --> P[Design Accepted]
    O -- No --> Q[Iterate Geometry and Material]
```

## 4.3 Cylinder vs Sphere Comparison (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submersible Pressure Hull] --> B{Depth Requirement}
    B -- less than 1000 m --> C[Cylindrical Ring Stiffened Hull]
    B -- greater than 4000 m --> D[Spherical Personnel Hull]
    C --> E[Hoop stress dominant sigma_h = pD/2t]
    C --> F[Buckling controlled p_cr by ring stiffeners]
    D --> G[Uniform stress sigma = pR/2t]
    D --> H[No buckling issue at moderate t]
    E --> I[Submarines: Type 214, Virginia, Scorpene]
    F --> I
    G --> J[DSVs: Alvin, Limiting Factor, Shinkai 6500]
    H --> J
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Submarine Pressure Hull (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Submarine Cross Section]
        A[Outer Light Hull fairing] --> B[Pressure Hull Shell]
        B --> C[Ring Stiffener T-frames]
        B --> D[Longitudinal Stringer optional]
        B --> E[Internal Deck and Bulkheads]
    end
    C --> F[Resists Hoop Buckling]
    D --> G[Resists Longitudinal Buckling]
    E --> H[Compartmentalisation and Pressure Boundary]
    B --> I[Primary Load Bearing Path]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
