# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define "Pressure Hull" of a submersible. Why is it considered the most safety-critical component?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pressure hull** is the watertight, load-bearing structural envelope of a submersible that encloses the crew, payload, and vital machinery, and is designed to resist the **external hydrostatic pressure** at the maximum operating depth while maintaining near-atmospheric internal pressure (typically 1 atm).

It is the **most safety-critical component** because:
1. It is the **only structural barrier** between the high-pressure external seawater and the life-supporting internal atmosphere.
2. Failure of the pressure hull results in **instantaneous, catastrophic implosion** with total loss of the vessel and crew (no time for escape at depth).
3. All other systems (propulsion, control, life support) are mounted inside it and depend on its integrity.
4. It experiences the **most severe loading** of any structural component on the vessel.

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Why are deep-diving submersibles (e.g., DSV Limiting Factor) designed with spherical hulls rather than cylindrical ones?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Spherical hulls are preferred for **deep-diving submersibles** because:

1. **Lower stress:** For equal pressure and material, the membrane stress in a sphere ($\sigma = pR/2t$) is **half** that of a cylinder ($\sigma = pD/2t = pR/t$).
2. **No buckling concern:** A sphere has no preferential buckling direction; its critical buckling pressure is several times higher than a cylinder of equal mass.
3. **Material efficiency:** For a given internal volume and pressure, a sphere requires less steel/titanium thickness than a cylinder, reducing weight.
4. **High payload volume / weight ratio** at extreme depths (> 6000 m).

The **trade-off** is that spheres are not hydrodynamic and are unsuitable for long-range, high-speed submarines — hence military submarines use ring-stiffened cylinders.

**(1 mark per point, total 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice** between Question A and Question B is provided.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**A (a)** With neat sketches, derive expressions for the **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress** in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure hull subjected to external hydrostatic pressure. Explain why hoop stress is the dominant design stress. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Definition and assumption (1 mark)**
A thin-walled cylinder is defined as one with $D/t \geq 20$. The shell experiences biaxial stress under external pressure.

**Step 2: Hoop stress derivation (3 marks)**

> Refer to the derivation in **§3.1** of Section 3.

Consider a unit length of cylinder of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$. Cut the cylinder longitudinally into two halves. The external pressure $p$ on the projected area $D \times 1 = D$ is balanced by hoop tension on two cut edges of area $t \times 1 = t$ each.

$$2 \sigma_h t = p D \implies \sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t}$$

**Step 3: Longitudinal stress derivation (2 marks)**

> Refer to **§3.2**.

Cutting the cylinder transversely at the end-cap. Pressure on the end-cap projected area $\pi D^2/4$ is balanced by longitudinal stress on the shell cross-section $\pi D t$.

$$\sigma_L \cdot \pi D t = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \implies \sigma_L = \frac{pD}{4t}$$

**Step 4: Dominance of hoop stress (1 mark)**

$$\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$$

Hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress, so it is the dominant design stress for cylindrical pressure hulls.

---

**A (b)** A submersible's pressure hull is a cylinder of **diameter 5 m**, **length 30 m**, with **shell thickness 40 mm**. The material is **HY-100 steel** ($E = 2.07 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 690$ MPa). The hull is **ring-stiffened at 1.0 m spacing** with rings of moment of inertia $I = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴. Determine: (i) the **Von Mises stress** at the design pressure corresponding to 750 m depth, and (ii) the **critical ring buckling (Bresse) pressure**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Design pressure (1 mark)**
$$p_d = \rho_{sw} g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 750 = 7.541 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 7.541 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2: Hoop stress (1 mark)**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{p_d D}{2t} = \frac{7.541 \times 5}{2 \times 0.04} = \frac{37.705}{0.08} = 471.3 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Longitudinal stress (1 mark)**
$$\sigma_L = \frac{p_d D}{4t} = \frac{7.541 \times 5}{4 \times 0.04} = \frac{37.705}{0.16} = 235.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Von Mises stress (2 marks)**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \frac{p_d D}{t} = 0.433 \times \frac{7.541 \times 5}{0.04}$$
$$= 0.433 \times 942.6 = 408.1 \, \text{MPa}$$

Check: $\sigma_{VM} = 408.1$ MPa $< \sigma_y = 690$ MPa ✓ (Safe from yielding with SF = 690/408.1 = 1.69)

**Step 5: Bresse ring buckling pressure (2 marks)**
$$p_L = \frac{3 E I}{R^3 L_{st}} = \frac{3 \times 2.07 \times 10^{11} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{(2.5)^3 \times 1.0}$$
$$= \frac{7.452 \times 10^7}{15.625} = 4.769 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 4.77 \, \text{MPa}$$

The ring buckling pressure is **4.77 MPa**, which is **less than** the design pressure of 7.54 MPa. Therefore, the **ring design is inadequate** and must be increased in size or closer spacing must be used. **(0 marks if not flagged)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to check the **Bresse ring buckling** and assume the shell between rings is the only failure mode. Marks are lost when the Bresse check is omitted.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**B (a)** Explain the **four primary failure modes** that must be checked during pressure hull design. Compare the **yielding criterion** with the **buckling criterion** for a typical submarine hull. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: The four primary failure modes (4 marks)**

1. **Material Yielding (Strength)** — The Von Mises equivalent stress must be below allowable: $\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \frac{pD}{t} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{SF}$
2. **Elastic / Plastic Buckling (Stability)** — The critical external pressure $p_{cr}$ must exceed design pressure: $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}/(L/D) \geq SF \cdot p_d$
3. **Fatigue / Crack Propagation** — Cyclic loading during dives causes fatigue; must be checked using Paris Law: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$
4. **Ultimate Rupture** — Bursting pressure $p_{burst} = 2 \sigma_{uts} t/D$ must be > 2 × design pressure

**Step 2: Comparison table (2 marks)**

| Criterion | Yielding | Buckling |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Strength | Stability |
| Governing formula | $\sigma_{VM} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \frac{pD}{t}$ | $p_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}/(L/D)$ |
| For thin hulls ($\sigma_{VM} \ll \sigma_y$) | Not critical | **CRITICAL** |
| Depends on | Material yield $\sigma_y$ | Material modulus $E$ and geometry |
| Failure appearance | Plastic deformation | Sudden shell wrinkling |

**Step 3: Conclusion (1 mark)**

For typical thin-walled submarine hulls ($D/t \approx 100$ to $200$), the **buckling criterion ALWAYS governs** over yielding. Hence ring stiffeners are mandatory.

---

**B (b)** A research submersible's **spherical titanium personnel hull** has an **inner radius 1.1 m** and **wall thickness 90 mm**. The material is **Ti-6Al-4V** ($E = 1.14 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.34$, $\sigma_y = 880$ MPa, $\sigma_{uts} = 950$ MPa). Determine: (i) the **maximum operating depth** with a safety factor of 1.8 against yielding, and (ii) the **bursting depth**. Seawater density = 1025 kg/m³. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Stress in spherical hull (1 mark)**
$$\sigma = \frac{pR}{2t} \quad \text{where } R = 1.1 \text{ m}, \, t = 0.09 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Allowable stress with SF (1 mark)**
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{SF} = \frac{880}{1.8} = 488.9 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum operating pressure (1 mark)**
$$p_{op} = \frac{2 \sigma_{allow} t}{R} = \frac{2 \times 488.9 \times 0.09}{1.1} = \frac{88.0}{1.1} = 80.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Maximum operating depth (1 mark)**
$$h_{op} = \frac{p_{op}}{\rho g} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{10055.25} = 7956 \, \text{m} \approx 7960 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Bursting pressure (1 mark)**
$$p_{burst} = \frac{2 \sigma_{uts} t}{R} = \frac{2 \times 950 \times 0.09}{1.1} = \frac{171}{1.1} = 155.45 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6: Bursting depth (1 mark)**
$$h_{burst} = \frac{155.45 \times 10^6}{10055.25} = 15,460 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 7: Interpretation (1 mark)**

The hull can safely operate to about **7960 m** (full ocean depth — Challenger Deep is ~10,928 m, so this hull design has margin to spare due to SF = 1.8). Bursting only occurs at the **extreme depth of ~15.5 km**, providing a margin of safety against brittle fracture scenarios.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly use $D$ instead of $R$ in the spherical stress formula. Always use **R** (radius), not D (diameter). Also forgetting the factor of **2** in $p = 2\sigma t/R$ loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Pressure Hull Design (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Pressure hull** = safety-critical watertight shell resisting external hydrostatic pressure.
> 2. **Design pressure** = $p_d = 1.5 \times \rho g h_{op}$ (factor of 1.5 on operating depth).
> 3. **Hoop stress** in cylinder: $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$ — **largest** and **dominant** primary stress.
> 4. **Longitudinal stress**: $\sigma_L = pD/(4t)$ = half the hoop stress.
> 5. **Von Mises** for biaxial state: $\sigma_{VM} = (\sqrt{3}/4)(pD/t)$.
> 6. **Spherical hull stress** = $pR/(2t)$ = **half** of cylinder hoop stress for same D.
> 7. **Buckling is the controlling failure mode** for thin-walled cylinders, not yielding.
> 8. **Long cylinder buckling (Bryan-Bryan):** $p_{cr} = 2.6E(t/D)^{2.5}/(L/D)$.
> 9. **Ring-stiffened buckling (DNV):** $p_{cr} = 0.25(N_E + p_L h)(t/R)\sqrt{1 + p_L h/N_E}$.
> 10. **Bresse ring buckling pressure:** $p_L = 3EI/(R^3 L_{st})$.
> 11. **Safety factor** on buckling ≥ 1.5; on yielding ≥ 1.5–2.0.
> 12. **Bursting pressure (ultimate):** $p_{burst} = 2\sigma_{uts} t/D$.
> 13. **End closures:** Hemispherical is most efficient; ellipsoidal 2:1 also common.
> 14. **Material choices:** HY-80, HY-100, HY-130 (steel); Ti-6Al-4V (titanium for > 6000 m).
> 15. **Young's Modulus:** Steel = $2.07 \times 10^5$ MPa; Ti = $1.14 \times 10^5$ MPa.
> 16. **Yield strength typical:** HY-80 = 550 MPa; HY-100 = 690 MPa; HY-130 = 895 MPa; Ti = 880 MPa.
> 17. **Buckling is independent of yield strength** — depends only on $E$ and geometry. So choosing higher-yield steel does NOT prevent buckling!
> 18. **Ring stiffeners** are required to raise $p_{cr}$ from ~0.1 MPa to several MPa.
> 19. **Design code references** to cite: ASME BPVC Sec VIII Div 2, DNV-RU-SHIP, ABS rules, DDS-102 (NATO).
> 20. **Crush depth** ≈ 1.5–2.0 × operating depth — gives ultimate margin of safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exam:** Students often check only **yielding** and forget **buckling**. For thin-walled submarine hulls ($D/t > 20$), **buckling ALWAYS governs**. Always do the buckling check first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape of the Pressure Vessel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shape of the Pressure Vessel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Pressure Vessel** (also called the **Pressure Hull**) of a submersible is the watertight structural envelope that isolates the internal habitable or equipment space from the surrounding seawater. Unlike surface pressure vessels that resist *internal* pressure, a submersible pressure hull must resist *external* hydrostatic pressure and is therefore designed against **collapse/buckling** rather than bursting.

The **Shape** of the pressure vessel is the geometric form of this hull, and it is the single most important design variable because it determines:
- Membrane stress distribution
- Buckling resistance
- Internal volume per unit of structural weight
- Manufacturability and payload integration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The shape of a submersible pressure hull is selected to maximize the collapse pressure-to-weight ratio, with the sphere being theoretically optimal, followed by cylinders with hemispherical or ellipsoidal end closures."*

---

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Deep-Sea Egg"

Imagine trying to crush a raw egg by squeezing it from all sides with your hands. The eggshell cracks easily along its long axis (cylinder) but resists immense force when pressure is applied uniformly (sphere). A submarine hull behaves identically: the **sphere** is the strongest shape against external crushing, just as a **sphere** is also the strongest against internal pressure. The **cylinder** is acceptable only when length must be greater than diameter (to fit equipment and crew), and it must be reinforced at the ends where the geometry transitions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Truth:**
> For the same mass of material and the same internal volume, a **sphere can go roughly twice as deep** as a cylinder before collapsing. This is why early bathyscaphes (*Trieste*, 1960) and modern deep-diving ROV housings use spheres.

---

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sea water density (ρ):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Hydrostatic pressure at depth h:** **P = ρ·g·h** (in Pa, with h in metres)
- **Pressure increase per metre depth:** approximately **0.1 atm/m** or **10.06 kPa/m**
- **Young's Modulus of HY-80 steel:** **E ≈ 207 GPa**
- **Young's Modulus of Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V):** **E ≈ 114 GPa**
- **Poisson's ratio (steel/titanium):** **ν ≈ 0.3**
- **Yield strength HY-80:** **550 MPa**
- **Yield strength Ti-6Al-4V:** **880 MPa**
- **1 atm = 101.325 kPa ≈ 1.013 bar ≈ 10.06 m of seawater**

---

## Why Shape Matters — The Physics of External Pressure

When a thin-walled shell is subjected to **external** pressure *p*:
1. **Membrane (in-plane) compressive stresses** develop (hoop and meridional).
2. Beyond a critical value, the shell **bifurcates** into a buckled configuration (ovalisation, dimpling, lobe formation).
3. The critical pressure $P_{cr}$ is governed by **stiffness, not strength**, and is highly sensitive to the shell's geometry and imperfections.

Hence, shape selection directly governs *how deep* the vessel can safely operate.

---

## The Three Principal Candidate Shapes

| Shape | Geometry | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Sphere** | Closed surface of constant radius R | Deep-diving human-occupied vehicles (HOVs), e.g. *Alvin* (rebuilt), *Limiting Factor* |
| **Cylinder + 2 Hemispherical Ends** | L/D ≈ 1.5 to 4 | Mid-depth submarines, *Nautile*, *Pisces* class |
| **Cylinder + 2 Ellipsoidal/Conical Ends** | Variable L/D | Military submarines, where length > diameter is mandatory |
| **Pure Cylinder** (rare) | Long thin tube | Unmanned ROVs, drop sondes |

> [!NOTE]
> A **pure cylinder is never used as a stand-alone pressure hull** at depth — it is always fitted with end closures (hemispherical, ellipsoidal, torispherical, or conical) because the open ends must be closed and the cylinder alone is mechanically poor at the open end.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Hydrostatic Loading

For a submersible operating at depth *h* (in metres of seawater):

$$P_{ext} = \rho \, g \, h$$

With $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$P_{ext}\ \text{[kPa]} \approx 10.06 \times h\ \text{[m]}$$

The design rule of thumb: for every **100 m** of depth, add **~1 MPa (10 atm)** of crushing pressure.

---

## 2.2 Membrane Stress Analysis (Thin-Wall Theory)

A "thin wall" is defined by $t/R \le 0.05$ for a sphere or $t/R \le 0.05$ for a cylinder. Beyond this, thick-wall theory (Lamé equations) must be used.

### (A) Sphere under External Pressure

For a thin spherical shell of radius *R* and wall thickness *t*, equilibrium of a hemispherical cap gives:

$$\sigma_{meridional} = \sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \, R}{2t}$$

Both principal stresses are **equal and compressive**. This is the only shape in which hoop and meridional stresses are identical.

### (B) Cylinder under External Pressure

A long thin cylinder of radius *R* and wall thickness *t*:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \, R}{t}\quad \text{(circumferential, compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_{longitudinal} = \frac{p \, R}{2t}\quad \text{(axial, compressive)}$$

The **hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress** — the most highly stressed direction. This is why cylindrical pressure vessels split *lengthwise*, not *across* (the opposite of internally pressurised vessels, which split along the axis).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick (KTU 2024):** Students often confuse which stress is larger. Remember: for **external** pressure on a cylinder, hoop stress > longitudinal stress. For **internal** pressure, hoop stress > longitudinal stress too — but for a different reason (force balance on a half-cylinder vs end cap).

---

## 2.3 Critical (Buckling) Pressure Analysis

Membrane stress alone is insufficient — at depth, the hull buckles *before* yielding. The relevant parameter is the **Critical Collapse Pressure** $P_{cr}$.

### (A) Sphere — Critical Buckling Pressure (von Mises, 1914)

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \, E}{\sqrt{3(1 - \nu^{2})}} \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

For steel with $\nu = 0.3$:

$$P_{cr} \approx 0.365 \, E \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

Notice the critical dependency: $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^{2}$, **quadratic** in slenderness. Doubling *t* increases collapse pressure by a factor of **4**.

### (B) Cylinder — Critical Buckling Pressure

The cylinder formula depends strongly on the **L/D ratio**:

**Long Cylinder** (L > ~5D, length-dominant buckling):
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E}{(L/D)} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}$$

(From Bresse-Bryant; valid for $\nu = 0.3$)

**Very Long Cylinder** (L > ~10D, approaching the asymptotic value):
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E}{(D/t)^{3}} \quad \text{(per unit gauge)}$$

A more compact form for the asymptotic long-cylinder value:
$$P_{cr} \approx 2.6 \, E \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

**Short Cylinder** (L < ~5D, dominated by end constraints):
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$$

(Timoshenko's simplified expression; the rigorous form is the **Bryan formula**.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the contrast: for a **sphere**, $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^{2}$, while for a **long cylinder**, $P_{cr} \propto (t/D)^{3}$. The cylinder's buckles *more* sensitive to slenderness, but it is also much weaker for the same $t/D$ because the exponent is higher AND the coefficient is smaller for a given t/D.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Sphere | Cylinder (long) | Cylinder (short) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hoop stress | $pR/(2t)$ | $pR/t$ | $pR/t$ |
| 2 | Meridional/Long. stress | $pR/(2t)$ | $pR/(2t)$ | $pR/(2t)$ |
| 3 | Membrane stress ratio | 1 : 1 | 2 : 1 | 2 : 1 |
| 4 | Critical buckling $P_{cr}$ | $\frac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}}\left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$ | $\frac{2.6E}{L/D}\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}$ | $\frac{2.6E(t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ |
| 5 | $P_{cr} \propto$ | $(t/R)^{2}$ | $(t/D)^{3}$ (asymptotic) | $(t/D)^{2.5}$ |
| 6 | Sensitivity to imperfection | Low | **High** | High |
| 7 | Volume efficiency | **Best (100%)** | ~90–95% | ~90–95% |
| 8 | Weight per unit volume | **Best** | 1.4–1.6× sphere | 1.4–1.6× sphere |
| 9 | Collapse margin factor | 1.0 (reference) | ~0.4–0.6 | ~0.6–0.8 |
| 10 | Buckling mode | Axisymmetric dimples | Lobe pattern (n=2 in short, many in long) | Mixed |

> [!NOTE]
> The "collapse margin factor" in row 9 means: for the *same* $t/R$ ratio, the sphere will resist roughly 1.5–2.5× the external pressure that a cylinder will resist.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Ocean Engineering** | All submersibles, ROV housings, instrument capsules |
| **Aerospace** | Crew capsule pressure vessels (Apollo, Orion) |
| **Nuclear** | Reactor containment spheres |
| **Pipelines** | Deep-water oil & gas risers (external pressure) |
| **Civil** | Underwater tunnels, caissons |
| **Hyperbaric Medicine** | Decompression chambers (reverse — internal pressure, but the same math applies with sign reversal) |

> [!TIP]
> The **sphere is the universal optimum** for any closed pressure containment, irrespective of whether the pressure is internal or external. Cylinders are a *compromise* made to fit elongated equipment (people, batteries, sensors, manipulator arms).

---

## 2.6 Comparison Logic: Why Sphere > Cylinder

1. **Equal biaxial stress state** in a sphere = no preferred failure direction.
2. **Lower hoop stress** for same $p$ and $t/R$ (factor of 2 lower than a cylinder's hoop stress).
3. **No longitudinal seams** to fail.
4. **Smaller surface area per unit volume** = less material, less weight.
5. **Higher $P_{cr}$** for the same $t/R$ ratio.

The cylinder wins on **internal layout** (better packing of long equipment) and **manufacturability** (rolled and welded plate is cheap), but loses on **collapse resistance per kg of steel**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Stress in a Thin Sphere (External Pressure)

**Step 1 — Define Geometry & Load**
Take a complete thin spherical shell of mean radius $R$ and uniform wall thickness $t$, subjected to external pressure $p$ that acts uniformly normal to the surface.

**Step 2 — Free-Body Cut**
Cut the sphere in half by an imaginary diametral plane. Consider the equilibrium of the upper hemisphere. The forces acting on it are:
- The external pressure acting on the projected circular area $\pi R^{2}$ (downward on the upper half).
- The internal pressure acting on the same area (upward, but smaller since we are *outside* the vessel).
- The net compressive thrust force $F$ in the shell wall, acting along the cut, distributed around the circumference $2\pi R$.

Wait — let us do it for **external pressure** correctly.

For external pressure $p$ (compressing the sphere), the net inward force on the hemispherical cap from the pressure difference is:

$$F_{pressure} = p \cdot \pi R^{2}$$

This force is balanced by the membrane force per unit length $N$ acting around the cut circumference:

$$F_{membrane} = N \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation**
$$N \cdot 2\pi R = p \cdot \pi R^{2}$$

$$N = \frac{p R}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Stress**
The membrane force per unit length $N$ relates to the membrane stress $\sigma$ as $\sigma = N / t$:

$$\sigma = \frac{p R}{2 t}$$

**Step 5 — Result**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{hoop} = \sigma_{meridional} = \frac{p R}{2 t}}$$

**Interpretation:** Both principal stresses are **equal and compressive** — the shell is in **equibiaxial compression**. The factor 1/2 in the denominator (versus 1 in the cylinder) reflects the curvature in *two* directions sharing the load.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Membrane Stress in a Thin Cylinder (External Pressure)

**Step 1 — Geometry & Load**
A long thin cylinder of mean radius $R$ (=$D/2$), wall thickness $t$, length $L$, subjected to external pressure $p$.

**Step 2 — Hoop (Circumferential) Stress**
Cut the cylinder with a longitudinal diametral plane. Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$. The projected area on which pressure acts is $2R \cdot L$. The membrane force in the two wall cuts (each of length $L$) balances this:

$$2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t \cdot L = p \cdot (2R) \cdot L$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p R}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal (Axial) Stress**
Cut the cylinder with a transverse plane. The pressure acts on the end cap of area $\pi R^{2}$:

$$\sigma_{long} \cdot (2\pi R t) = p \cdot \pi R^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{long} = \frac{p R}{2 t}$$

**Step 4 — Result**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p R}{t}, \quad \sigma_{long} = \frac{p R}{2 t}, \quad \frac{\sigma_{hoop}}{\sigma_{long}} = 2}$$

**Interpretation:** The hoop direction is **twice as stressed** as the longitudinal direction. This is the *opposite* of a pressurized gas cylinder on land (which also has hoop > longitudinal, but the ratio is also 2 — the *ratio* is the same; the *sign* is reversed for external pressure).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Buckling of a Sphere (von Mises Equation)

The classical derivation uses **small-deflection shallow-shell theory** with the Donnell-type stability operator. A more accessible engineering derivation:

**Step 1 — Strain Energy Stored in Bending**
When a sphere buckles into a small dimple, additional strain energy is stored in bending. The bending strain energy per unit area for a dimple of amplitude $w$ and wavelength $\lambda$ scales as:

$$U_{bending} \propto \frac{E t^{3} w^{2}}{\lambda^{4}}$$

**Step 2 — Work Done by External Pressure**
The external pressure does work as the shell moves inward by $w$:

$$W_{pressure} \propto p w \lambda^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium at Bifurcation**
Setting $\partial U_{bending} / \partial \lambda = \partial W_{pressure} / \partial \lambda$ yields the critical wavelength:

$$\lambda_{cr} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{R t}{\sqrt{12(1 - \nu^{2})}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back**
After substituting the optimal wavelength into the equilibrium, von Mises (1914) obtained:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1 - \nu^{2})}} \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in Steel Values**
For $\nu = 0.3$, $\sqrt{3(1 - 0.09)} = \sqrt{2.73} \approx 1.652$:

$$P_{cr} \approx 1.21 \, E \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

(Some texts quote $0.365 E$ for the coefficient, but the exact form depends on whether $P_{cr}$ is in $\text{Pa}$ or normalised — the key is the **quadratic** dependence.)

**Step 6 — Numerical Example**
For a steel sphere with $R = 1\ \text{m}$, $t = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $E = 207\ \text{GPa}$:

$$\frac{t}{R} = 0.05, \quad (t/R)^{2} = 0.0025$$

$$P_{cr} \approx 1.21 \times 207 \times 10^{9} \times 0.0025 = 6.26 \times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} = 626\ \text{MPa}$$

This corresponds to a depth of $\sim$62 km in seawater — well beyond the deepest ocean (Mariana Trench, ~11 km). Real hulls are limited by **yield, fatigue, and imperfections**, not the ideal buckling.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Critical Buckling of a Long Cylinder

**Step 1 — Bryan-Donnell Stability Equation**
For a long cylinder, the buckling mode is a ring with $n$ circumferential lobes. The critical pressure is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{E \, t}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\frac{n^{2} - 1 + \lambda^{2}/R^{2}}{n^{2}}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{n^{2} R^{2}}{12(1 - \nu^{2}) t^{2}} + \frac{\lambda^{4}}{R^{4} n^{2}(n^{2} - 1)^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{12(1-\nu^{2})}}$$

where $\lambda$ is the axial half-wavelength of the buckle. (This is the full Bryan equation, simplified.)

**Step 2 — Long-Cylinder Limit**
For a long cylinder, the axial half-wavelength can be optimised. Timoshenko showed that for a long cylinder the asymptotic value is:

$$P_{cr} \approx \frac{2.6 \, E \, t^{3}}{R^{3}} = 2.6 \, E \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{3}$$

For steel with $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 3 — Compare with the Sphere**
For the **same $t/R = 0.05$** as before:
- Sphere: $P_{cr} \propto (0.05)^{2} = 0.0025$
- Long cylinder: $P_{cr} \propto (0.05)^{3} = 0.000125$

Ratio: $\frac{P_{cr,\ sphere}}{P_{cr,\ long\ cyl}} = \frac{0.0025}{0.000125} = 20$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **same $t/R$**, the sphere is theoretically **~20× stronger** against collapse than a long cylinder! In practice (with imperfections and end effects), the ratio is smaller (~3–6×), but the sphere is still decisively superior.

---

## 3.5 Volume Efficiency Analysis

For a given internal volume $V$, compute the surface area $A$ (which scales with structural weight):

**Sphere:**
$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \Rightarrow R = \left(\frac{3V}{4\pi}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$A_{sphere} = 4\pi R^{2} = (36\pi)^{1/3} V^{2/3} \approx 4.836 \, V^{2/3}$$

**Cylinder with Two Hemispherical Ends** (length $L$ + 2 hemispheres of radius $R$):
$$V = \pi R^{2} L + \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} = \pi R^{2}\left(L + \frac{4R}{3}\right)$$

For $L = 2R$ (equal length of cylinder and hemisphere diameter):
$$V = \pi R^{2} \cdot \frac{10R}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \pi R^{3}$$

$$A = 2\pi R L + 4\pi R^{2} = 2\pi R \cdot 2R + 4\pi R^{2} = 8\pi R^{2}$$

Solving for $R$ in terms of $V$: $R = \left(\frac{3V}{10\pi}\right)^{1/3}$, and substituting:

$$A_{cyl+hem} = 8\pi \left(\frac{3V}{10\pi}\right)^{2/3} \approx 4.926 \, V^{2/3}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{A_{cyl+hem}}{A_{sphere}} = \frac{4.926}{4.836} \approx 1.019$$

> A cylinder with hemispherical ends uses only **~2% more surface area** than a pure sphere for the same volume. This is why practical submarines use cylinder-with-hemispheres: very little weight penalty, but vastly more usable internal length.

---

## 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix (Summary Table)

| Property | Pure Sphere | Cyl + 2 Hemispheres | Cyl + 2 Ellipsoids (2:1) | Pure Cylinder |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A/V^{2/3}$ factor | **4.836** | 4.926 | 5.14 | 5.30 |
| Hoop stress ($pR/t$) | 0.5 | 1.0 (in cyl) | 1.0 (in cyl) | 1.0 |
| Long. stress ($pR/t$) | 0.5 | 0.5 (in cyl) | varies | 0.5 |
| $P_{cr}$ formula | $\propto (t/R)^{2}$ | sphere caps $\propto (t/R)^{2}$; cyl $\propto (t/D)^{3}$ | as cylinder | $\propto (t/D)^{3}$ |
| Use case | Very deep HOV | Mid-depth HOV/ROV | Long submarines | Internal pipes only |
| Real example | *Limiting Factor* (DSV) | *Alvin* (rebuilt) | *Nautile* | — |
