# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Why is a spherical shape preferred for the pressure hull of a deep-ocean submersible, even though a cylindrical shape is easier to manufacture?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A spherical shape is preferred for deep-ocean submersible pressure hulls for three primary reasons:

1. **Lower membrane stress:** In a thin sphere under external pressure, both the hoop and meridional stresses are equal to $\sigma = pR/(2t)$. In a cylinder, the hoop stress is twice as large: $\sigma_h = pR/t$. For the same pressure, radius, and thickness, the sphere carries only half the peak stress of the cylinder.

2. **Higher critical buckling pressure:** The von Mises critical collapse pressure of a sphere is $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^{2}$, while for a long cylinder it is $P_{cr} \propto (t/D)^{3}$. For a typical $t/R = 0.05$, the sphere has a theoretical collapse pressure roughly **20 times greater** than a long cylinder.

3. **Lower surface area per unit volume:** A sphere encloses maximum volume for minimum surface area, which means less structural weight per unit of habitable or payload volume.

Although a cylinder is easier to roll, weld, and integrate with elongated machinery, its lower collapse resistance makes it unsuitable for the deepest vehicles (where spheres like those of *Trieste* and *DSV Limiting Factor* are mandatory). For mid-depth vehicles, the small weight penalty (~2% extra surface) of a cylinder with hemispherical ends is acceptable. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**"Distinguish between the failure modes of a pressure vessel under internal and external pressure. What is the corresponding design criterion for a submersible hull?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Pressure (Land/Tank) | External Pressure (Submersible) |
|---|---|---|
| Dominant failure mode | **Rupture / bursting** (material yield) | **Buckling / collapse** (geometric instability) |
| Critical parameter | Yield stress of material | Stiffness (E) and geometry (t/R, L/D) |
| Design criterion | $\sigma \le \sigma_{yield}/SF$ | $P_{cr} \ge SF \cdot P_{external}$ |
| Failure signal | Crack propagation | Sudden dimpling / lobe formation |
| Shell response | Hoop stress highest in cylinder | Hoop stress highest in cylinder, but buckling is the *first* limit reached |

For a submersible hull, the design criterion is therefore that the **critical collapse pressure $P_{cr}$** must exceed the design external pressure (which equals $\rho g h$ at operating depth, multiplied by a safety factor of typically 1.5 to 2.0). The membrane stress check $\sigma = pR/2t \le \sigma_{yield}/SF$ is secondary and usually satisfied automatically once buckling is prevented. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A thin-walled spherical pressure hull of **mean radius R = 1.2 m** and **wall thickness t = 60 mm** is made of HY-80 steel (E = 207 GPa, ν = 0.3). Calculate the critical collapse pressure using the von Mises equation. What is the corresponding safe operating depth, applying a safety factor of 2.0? State any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin-walled cylindrical hull segment of **radius R = 1.2 m, thickness t = 60 mm, length L = 6 m** is fabricated from the same steel. Calculate the critical buckling pressure for the long-cylinder case using Timoshenko's asymptotic formula. Compare the result with the sphere of part (a) and explain which shape is more efficient for the same material weight. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (a)

**Given:** Sphere, $R = 1.2\ \text{m}$, $t = 0.060\ \text{m}$, $E = 207 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\text{SF} = 2.0$.

**Step 1 — von Mises Critical Pressure:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1 - \nu^{2})}} \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the coefficient:**

$$\sqrt{3(1 - \nu^{2})} = \sqrt{3(1 - 0.09)} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.91} = \sqrt{2.73} = 1.6523$$

$$\frac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1 - \nu^{2})}} = \frac{2 \times 207 \times 10^{9}}{1.6523} = 2.5058 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the slenderness ratio:**

$$\frac{t}{R} = \frac{0.060}{1.2} = 0.05, \quad \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2} = 0.0025$$

**Step 4 — Final $P_{cr}$:**

$$P_{cr} = 2.5058 \times 10^{11} \times 0.0025 = 6.2645 \times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa} \approx 626\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to depth in seawater:**
The pressure equivalent in metres of seawater is:

$$h = \frac{P}{\rho g} = \frac{6.2645 \times 10^{8}}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{6.2645 \times 10^{8}}{10055} \approx 62{,}300\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Apply safety factor of 2.0:**

$$P_{design} = \frac{P_{cr}}{2.0} = 313\ \text{MPa}$$

$$h_{safe} = \frac{313 \times 10^{6}}{10055} \approx 31{,}130\ \text{m} \approx 31.1\ \text{km}$$

**Assumptions:**
- The shell is geometrically perfect (no out-of-roundness, no wall-thickness variation).
- The material is linearly elastic up to collapse.
- Hydrostatic pressure is uniform over the hull.
- Safety factor 2.0 is applied uniformly.

**Answer:** The sphere will theoretically withstand an external pressure of **626 MPa** (theoretically ~62 km depth) and, with a safety factor of 2.0, a safe operating depth of **~31.1 km** — well beyond the deepest ocean point. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:** Long cylinder, $R = 1.2\ \text{m}$, $D = 2.4\ \text{m}$, $t = 0.060\ \text{m}$, $L = 6\ \text{m}$, same steel.

**Step 1 — Verify long-cylinder assumption:**
$L/D = 6/2.4 = 2.5$. This is *not* in the long-cylinder regime ($L/D > 5$). But following the question's instruction, we apply the asymptotic long-cylinder formula. (We also compute a short-cylinder correction below.)

**Step 2 — Asymptotic long-cylinder $P_{cr}$:**

$$P_{cr} = 2.6 \, E \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{0.060}{2.4} = 0.025$$

$$\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3} = (0.025)^{3} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P_{cr} = 2.6 \times 207 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P_{cr} = 2.6 \times 207 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P_{cr} = 8.408 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \approx 8.41\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with the sphere:**

$$\frac{P_{cr,\ sphere}}{P_{cr,\ cyl}} = \frac{626}{8.41} \approx 74.4$$

> (Note: theoretical ratio from the formulas alone is $(0.05)^{2}/(0.025)^{3} \times$ coefficient ratio. Here, the t/R values are different, so the ratio is even larger. In a fair comparison at the *same* t/R = 0.05 for both, the ratio would be ~20.)

**Step 4 — Conclusion on Efficiency:**

For the **same mass of steel** (same volume of material, since $t$ and $R$ are the same), the sphere resists **~75× more pressure** than a long cylinder. Equivalently, to achieve the **same collapse depth**, the cylinder would need to be **~3× thicker** (cubic dependency), vastly increasing weight and reducing internal volume. The sphere is therefore the more structurally efficient shape per kg of material. The cylinder is chosen only when internal geometry demands elongation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, derive the membrane hoop and longitudinal stresses in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure hull subjected to **external** hydrostatic pressure. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A research submersible is designed to operate at **4000 m** depth. The cylindrical pressure hull segment has **D = 1.8 m, t = 35 mm, L = 5.5 m**, made of HY-80 steel. Calculate the membrane hoop stress and the long-cylinder critical buckling pressure. Comment on the safety margin. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $E = 207\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (a)

*(Refer to the derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes.)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Shell is **thin-walled**: $t/R \le 0.05$ (so wall thickness $\ll$ radius).
2. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
3. The shell is **closed** (so that longitudinal stress is well defined and end caps balance the load).
4. **Membrane theory** applies — bending stresses are negligible away from discontinuities (end closures, stiffeners, openings).
5. Pressure is **uniform** over the entire surface.
6. **No initial imperfections** (perfectly circular, uniform thickness).

**Hoop stress derivation** (longitudinal cut, two wall pieces each of length $L$ balance the pressure on a projected area $2R \times L$):

$$2 \, \sigma_h \, t \, L = p \cdot 2R \cdot L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_h = \frac{pR}{t}$$

**Longitudinal stress derivation** (transverse cut, the wall in the cross-section balances pressure on the end cap $\pi R^2$):

$$\sigma_l \cdot 2\pi R t = p \cdot \pi R^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_l = \frac{pR}{2t}$$

**Conclusion:** For external pressure, both stresses are **compressive** and $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1 — External Pressure at 4000 m Depth:**

$$p = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4000 = 4.022 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} \approx 40.22\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Membrane Hoop Stress:**

$$R = D/2 = 0.9\ \text{m}, \quad t = 0.035\ \text{m}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pR}{t} = \frac{4.022 \times 10^{7} \times 0.9}{0.035} = 1.034 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa} \approx 1034\ \text{MPa}$$

> **Check:** This is well above the yield strength of HY-80 (550 MPa) — the membrane stress *alone* would cause yield. This indicates that the design is **not viable as a pure cylinder** at 4000 m. The shell will collapse by **buckling** long before yielding.

**Step 3 — Long-Cylinder Critical Buckling Pressure:**

$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{0.035}{1.8} = 0.01944, \quad \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3} = 7.36 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{cr} = 2.6 \times 207 \times 10^{9} \times 7.36 \times 10^{-6} = 3.96 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \approx 3.96\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Safety Margin:**

$$\text{Safety factor} = \frac{P_{cr}}{p_{external}} = \frac{3.96}{40.22} \approx 0.098$$

The safety factor is **0.098 — far below 1.0**. The hull will collapse at this depth.

**Step 5 — Comment and Recommendation:**

The cylinder is grossly under-designed for 4000 m operation. Three corrective actions are possible:
- **Increase t:** Required $(P_{cr}/p)^{1/3} = (40.22/3.96)^{1/3} = (10.16)^{1/3} \approx 2.16$. So t must be increased to at least $0.035 \times 2.16 \approx 75.6$ mm — almost doubling the wall thickness.
- **Reduce D:** Buckling scales as $(D/t)^{3}$, so a smaller hull would help, but it reduces internal volume.
- **Change geometry to a sphere** (or cylinder with hemispherical ends), which gives $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^{2}$ instead of $(t/D)^{3}$, and is far stronger per unit weight.

**Final comment:** The design is unsafe and must be re-engineered before deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Shape of Pressure Vessel" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing internal vs external pressure formulas** — the form is the same, but the **sign** and **failure mode** differ. Always state clearly whether the stress is tensile or compressive.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for each missing unit (Pa vs MPa vs GPa, m vs mm, etc.).
> 3. **Using thick-wall formulas where thin-wall is asked** (or vice versa) — check the $t/R$ ratio first.
> 4. **Confusing $P_{cr}$ for a sphere with $P_{cr}$ for a cylinder** — the dependencies are fundamentally different: $(t/R)^2$ vs $(t/D)^3$.
> 5. **Not applying the safety factor** — KTU board questions frequently test that you apply a factor of 1.5–2.0 to the critical pressure before comparing with operating depth.
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** — every derivation in KTU 2024 scheme questions carries **1 mark** for "clearly stated assumptions". Don't skip this.
> 7. **Mixing up L/D regimes** — for cylinders, $L/D < 5$ is "short" and $L/D > 5$ is "long". The formula used must match the regime.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shape of the Pressure Vessel**

- ☐ A submersible pressure hull resists **external hydrostatic pressure**, so it is designed against **collapse/buckling**, not bursting.
- ☐ Hydrostatic pressure at depth *h*: $P = \rho g h \approx 10.06 h$ kPa (for $h$ in metres of seawater with $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$).
- ☐ **Membrane stress in a sphere:** $\sigma = pR/(2t)$ — equibiaxial compression.
- ☐ **Membrane stresses in a cylinder:** $\sigma_h = pR/t$, $\sigma_l = pR/(2t)$ — hoop is twice the longitudinal.
- ☐ **Critical buckling of a sphere (von Mises):** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$.
- ☐ **Critical buckling of a long cylinder (Timoshenko):** $P_{cr} \approx 2.6 E (t/D)^3$ (asymptotic).
- ☐ For a sphere, $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^2$; for a long cylinder, $P_{cr} \propto (t/D)^3$.
- ☐ The **sphere is the most efficient shape** per unit weight of material.
- ☐ The **cylinder with hemispherical ends** is the practical compromise: only ~2% extra surface area vs a pure sphere, but vastly more usable internal length.
- ☐ Volume efficiency ordering: **Sphere > Cyl + Hemispheres > Cyl + Ellipsoids > Pure Cylinder**.
- ☐ Real hulls are limited by **imperfections, fatigue, welds, openings, and material yield**, not the ideal buckling formula — a knockdown factor of 0.5–0.7 is typically applied to $P_{cr}$.
- ☐ Deepest-diving human vehicles (HOVs) almost universally use **spherical or near-spherical** pressure hulls (*DSV Limiting Factor*, *Trieste*).
- ☐ Military submarines use cylinders because **length is mandatory** for hydrodynamics, propulsion, and payload — collapse resistance is sacrificed for performance.
- ☐ **Safety factor** on $P_{cr}$: typically **1.5 to 2.0** in KTU/Defence standard practice.
- ☐ **Always state the following in derivations:** thin-wall assumption, isotropic homogeneous material, closed shell, uniform pressure, membrane theory, no imperfections.
- ☐ The cylinder formula depends on **L/D ratio**: $L/D < 5$ is "short", $L/D > 5$ is "long", with the buckling mode changing from ovalisation (2-lobe) to many-lobe diamond patterns.
- ☐ **End closures matter:** Hemispherical > Ellipsoidal (2:1) > Torispherical > Flat. End closures are typically the first to fail if not properly designed.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4: Shape of the Pressure Vessel**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buckling Deformation of the Shell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buckling Deformation of the Shell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Buckling Deformation of the Shell** is defined as the *sudden, large-amplitude out-of-plane lateral deflection* experienced by a thin-walled cylindrical pressure hull (shell) when the external hydrostatic pressure exceeds a critical threshold known as the **Critical Buckling Pressure ($P_{cr}$)**. Unlike material yielding (which is strength-governed), shell buckling is a *stability-governed* failure mode dominated by geometric stiffness, shell curvature, and boundary conditions.

In the context of submersible design, the cylindrical pressure hull is the **primary load-bearing structure** resisting the hydrostatic pressure of the deep ocean. As a vehicle descends, the external pressure $P_{ext}$ increases linearly with depth ($P_{ext} = \rho g h$). At a certain depth, the hull suddenly deforms — typically by collapsing inward in a characteristic diamond-shaped, polygonal, or "oil-can" pattern — long before the material itself reaches its yield stress.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding an empty **aluminum soda can** vertically on a table and pressing straight down on the top with your palm. The can will hold a reasonable amount of force. However, at a specific load, the can will suddenly buckle — its sidewalls will collapse inward with a loud *crack* and a single, large dent forms. The aluminum didn't melt, didn't tear, and didn't really "stretch" in the normal sense — it simply **lost geometric stability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for submersibles:** The operational depth of a submersible is often limited not by the *strength* of its hull material, but by the *stability* (buckling) of its shell geometry. This is why deep-diving vehicles use thick-walled cylinders, ring stiffeners, or spherical pressure hulls — geometry governs survival depth.

### 1.3 The Three Geometric Regimes of Shell Buckling

The buckling behavior of a cylindrical shell is classified by the **length-to-diameter ratio ($L/D$)** and **thickness-to-diameter ratio ($t/D$)**:

| Regime | Geometry | Dominant Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Long Cylinder** | $L/D > 4.0$ | Multiple circumferential waves, classic column-like Euler-type buckling |
| **Intermediate Cylinder** | $1.0 \le L/D \le 4.0$ | Mixed mode — local ring bending + axial half-waves |
| **Short Cylinder / Ring** | $L/D < 1.0$ | Pure ring bending, single circumferential lobe between stiffeners |

> [!NOTE]
> The **classical critical pressure** assumes a *perfect* shell — perfectly circular, perfectly concentric loading, and zero initial geometric imperfections. Real (as-built) shells exhibit knockdown factors of **0.5 to 0.7** relative to the classical prediction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physical Assumptions (Classical Linear Buckling Theory)

The classical buckling equations are derived under the following idealized assumptions:

- The shell material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law valid up to buckling).
- The shell is **initially perfect** (no geometric imperfections, no residual stresses).
- The loading is **axisymmetric and uniform** external hydrostatic pressure.
- **Membrane (in-plane) stresses** dominate the pre-buckling state.
- The wall is **thin** ($t/D \le 1/20$) so Kirchhoff-Love thin-shell theory applies.
- **Linearized stability theory (eigenvalue problem)** governs the bifurcation point.

### 2.2 The Two Classical Failure Equations (Donnell-Mushtari Theory)

The critical buckling pressure is governed by combining two classical equations:

**Equation 1 — Ring Bending (short cylinder):**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.59 \, E}{(1 - \nu^2)^{3/4}} \cdot \frac{(t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$$

This applies when the **axial half-wave length** would be shorter than the actual cylinder length, i.e., for short/intermediate shells.

**Equation 2 — Long Cylinder / Column Buckling:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \, E}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

This applies when the cylinder is long enough that a single axial half-wave would be shorter than $L$, i.e., the Euler-column limit is reached.

**Equation 3 — Mises-Kempner Unified Formula (Most Widely Used):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}}{\dfrac{L}{D} - 0.45 \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{0.5}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 - \nu^2)^{0.75}}$$

The Mises-Kempner equation provides a smooth, continuous transition between the short-cylinder ring-bending regime and the long-cylinder Euler regime, and is the **standard textbook equation for KTU submersible design problems**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| External Hydrostatic Pressure | $P_{ext} = \rho g h$ | Pa | $\rho$ = seawater density $\approx$ **1025 kg/m³** |
| Critical Buckling Pressure (Mises) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \, E \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}}{\dfrac{L}{D} - 0.45 \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{0.5}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1-\nu^{2})^{0.75}}$ | Pa | Classical, perfect shell |
| Long Cylinder Limit | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ | Pa | Applies when $L/D > 4$ |
| Short Cylinder Limit | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.59 \, E}{(1-\nu^{2})^{3/4}} \cdot \dfrac{(t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$ | Pa | Applies when $L/D < 1$ |
| Knockdown Factor | $k_d = P_{cr,actual} / P_{cr,classical}$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.5$ to $0.7$ for steel |
| Number of Circumferential Lobes | $n^{2} = \dfrac{2 \, P_{cr} (1-\nu^{2}) \, D^{3}}{E \, t^{3}}$ | — | Determines buckle pattern |
| Inelastic Buckling (inelastic modulus $E_t$) | $P_{cr,i} = P_{cr,e} \cdot \sqrt{E_t / E}$ | Pa | For $P_{cr}$ above proportional limit |
| Critical Depth (Collapse Depth) | $h_{cr} = P_{cr,actual} / (\rho g)$ | m | Operational limit |
| Volumetric Collapse Strain | $\Delta V / V = 0.5 \, (P_{cr} / E) \cdot (1 - \nu) \cdot (D/t)$ | — | Used in volume-change tests |
| Plastic Collapse Pressure (Von Mises) | $P_p = 2 \, \sigma_y \cdot \ln(R_o/R_i)$ | Pa | Strength failure limit |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Submersible Depth Rating:** A vehicle's maximum rated operating depth is determined by the **minimum** of (a) elastic buckling pressure, (b) inelastic buckling pressure, and (c) material yield pressure. For a typical 5 m long, 1.5 m diameter HY-80 steel hull with $t = 25$ mm, classical $P_{cr}$ may predict ~30 MPa, but the knockdown-adjusted operational pressure is closer to ~18 MPa — corresponding to roughly **1800 m depth**.

Other engineering applications of cylindrical shell buckling theory include:
- **Submarine pressure hulls** (military and research)
- **Deep-sea pipeline** collapse under external pressure
- **Aerospace fuselage** skin under cabin pressure differential
- **Nuclear reactor containment** vessels
- **Offshore riser pipes** in oil & gas industry
- **Aerospace rocket motor cases** (internal pressure) — the inverse problem

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Long-Cylinder Critical Buckling Pressure (Euler-Type)

We derive the asymptotic lower bound for an infinitely long cylinder, where buckling occurs as a sequence of circumferential waves of half-wavelength $\pi/\beta$, with axial wavenumber $\beta$.

**Step 1 — Pre-buckling membrane stress state**

For a long, closed-end cylinder under uniform external pressure $P$, the pre-buckling membrane stresses are:
- **Hoop (circumferential) stress:** $\sigma_{\theta} = -\dfrac{PD}{2t}$
- **Longitudinal (axial) stress:** $\sigma_{x} = -\dfrac{PD}{4t}$ (closed ends)

**Step 2 — Strain energy of bending**

A small radial deflection $w(\theta, x)$ introduces additional strain energy from plate bending:
$$\Delta U_{bending} = \frac{D_s}{2} \int\!\!\int \left[(\nabla^{2} w)^{2} - 2(1-\nu)\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial \theta^{2}} - \left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x \partial \theta}\right)^{2}\right)\right] dx \, d\theta$$

where $D_s = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the shell wall.

**Step 3 — Work done by external pressure**

When the shell deflects inward by volume $\Delta V$, the external pressure does work:
$$\Delta W = \frac{P}{2} \int\!\!\int \left[\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial \theta}\right)^{2}\right] dx \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Assume sinusoidal mode shape**

For a long cylinder, assume:
$$w(\theta, x) = A \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)\cos(n\theta)$$

where $n$ is the number of full circumferential waves.

**Step 5 — Solve the eigenvalue problem**

Setting the total potential energy's first variation to zero ($\partial \Pi / \partial A = 0$) yields the classical Donnell-Mushtari critical pressure:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{E \, t^{3}}{4(1-\nu^{2}) R^{3}} \cdot \frac{n^{2}}{(n^{2}-1)\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{n^{2} L^{2}}{\pi^{2} R^{2}}\right)^{2}\right]}$$

where $R = D/2$ is the mean radius.

**Step 6 — Optimize over mode number $n$**

In the **long-cylinder limit** ($L \to \infty$), the term $\left(\dfrac{n^{2} L^{2}}{\pi^{2} R^{2}}\right)^{2} \gg 1$, and the denominator simplifies. The critical mode number $n \to 2$ (two circumferential lobes), and we obtain:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

This is the **Euler long-cylinder buckling pressure** — independent of length $L$, depending only on $t/D$ and material properties.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Short-Cylinder Critical Pressure (Ring Bending)

For a **short cylinder** where $L/D < 1$, the buckled shape is essentially a **ring** with two circumferential lobes ($n = 2$).

**Step 1 — Treat each cross-section as a thin ring**

A ring of mean radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, and width $L$ (the stiffener spacing) under uniform external pressure $P$ buckles when the ring's bending resistance is exceeded.

**Step 2 — Curvature change in the buckled state**

The buckled ring of $n = 2$ lobes has effective bending rigidity $EI = \dfrac{E \, t^{3} \, L}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$.

**Step 3 — Critical pressure from ring stability**

Equating the destabilizing pressure work to the stabilizing ring bending energy:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.59 \, E}{(1-\nu^{2})^{3/4}} \cdot \frac{(t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$$

The exponent **2.5** on $(t/D)$ and the **inverse** dependence on $(L/D)$ are the two hallmark features of the short-cylinder regime.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A submersible pressure hull has the following properties:
- Diameter $D = 1.5$ m
- Wall thickness $t = 0.025$ m
- Length $L = 5.0$ m
- Material: HY-80 steel, $E = 207$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$
- Knockdown factor $k_d = 0.65$

Find the collapse depth.

**Step 1 — Compute geometric ratios**

$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{0.025}{1.5} = 0.01667, \qquad \frac{L}{D} = \frac{5.0}{1.5} = 3.333$$

**Step 2 — Apply Mises-Kempner equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{2.6 \times (207 \times 10^{9}) \times (0.01667)^{2.5}}{3.333 - 0.45 \times (0.01667)^{0.5}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 - 0.3^{2})^{0.75}}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute intermediate values:
$$(0.01667)^{2.5} = 0.01667^{2} \times 0.01667^{0.5} = 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1291 = 3.587 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$(0.01667)^{0.5} = 0.1291, \qquad 0.45 \times 0.1291 = 0.0581$$

$$3.333 - 0.0581 = 3.275$$

$$(1 - 0.09)^{0.75} = (0.91)^{0.75} = 0.9299$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{2.6 \times 2.07 \times 10^{11} \times 3.587 \times 10^{-5}}{3.275} \times \frac{1}{0.9299}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{1.931 \times 10^{7}}{3.275} \times 1.0753
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} = 5.896 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0753 = 6.341 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 6.34 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply knockdown factor**

$$P_{cr,actual} = 0.65 \times 6.34 = 4.12 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to collapse depth**

$$h_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr,actual}}{\rho g} = \frac{4.12 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{4.12 \times 10^{6}}{10055.25} \approx 410 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** This hull has a predicted collapse depth of approximately **410 m**. The factor-of-safety (typically 1.5–2.0 in KTU design problems) would further reduce the operating depth to ~205–275 m.

---

### 3.4 Summary of Derivations

| Regime | Critical Pressure Formula | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Long Cylinder | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ | $L/D \to \infty$, $n = 2$ lobes |
| Short Cylinder | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.59 E}{(1-\nu^{2})^{3/4}} \cdot \dfrac{(t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$ | $L/D \to 0$, pure ring mode |
| Unified (Mises) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D) - 0.45(t/D)^{0.5}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1-\nu^{2})^{0.75}}$ | Smooth transition |
| Inelastic | $P_{cr,i} = P_{cr,e} \sqrt{E_t / E}$ | Above proportional limit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Shell Buckling Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submersible Hull Geometry] --> B[Compute t/D and L/D ratios]
    B --> C{Regime Classification}
    C -- Long L/D > 4 --> D[Apply Long Cylinder Equation]
    C -- Short L/D < 1 --> E[Apply Short Cylinder Equation]
    C -- Intermediate 1 to 4 --> F[Apply Mises Kempner Equation]
    D --> G[Classical Pcr]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Knockdown Factor kd]
    H --> I[Inelastic Check using Et]
    I --> J[Compute Actual Collapse Pressure]
    J --> K[Convert to Collapse Depth hcr]
    K --> L[Apply Factor of Safety FS]
    L --> M[Final Operating Depth Rating]
```

### 4.2 Buckling Mode Shape Visualization (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Hydrostatic Pressure Pext] -- RHO G H --> N2[Membrane Stress State]
    N2 -- Hoop Stress -- N3[Compressive Hoop Stress]
    N2 -- Axial Stress -- N4[Compressive Axial Stress]
    N3 -- N5[Circumferential Lobes n=2]
    N4 -- N6[Axial Half Waves]
    N5 -- N7[Characteristic Diamond Pattern]
    N6 -- N7
    N7 -- N8[Sudden Loss of Geometric Stiffness]
    N8 -- N9[Hull Collapse]
```

### 4.3 Buckling Mode Number Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Pre buckling Equilibrium] --> S1[Apply Radial Perturbation w theta x]
    S1 --> S2{Is L/D Greater Than Critical?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Long Cylinder n = 2 lobes]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Short Cylinder n = 2 ring mode]
    S3 --> S5[Compute Pcr via Euler Limit]
    S4 --> S6[Compute Pcr via Ring Bending]
    S5 --> S7[Compare with Actual Pext]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 -- Pext less than Pcr --> S8[Safe Operation]
    S7 -- Pext greater than Pcr --> S9[Buckling Failure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams above map the *analytical decision flow* an engineer follows when assessing shell buckling. In practice, this analysis is implemented in classification society software (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register) using **non-linear finite element methods** (Abaqus, ANSYS) with measured initial imperfection data, replacing the classical eigenvalue calculation.

### 4.4 Schematic of Critical Pressure vs. L/D (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[L/D Small 0 to 1] -- A2[Short cylinder regime Pcr decreases with L/D]
    A2 -- A3[Inverse relation Pcr proportional to 1 over L/D]
    A3 -- A4[Transition at L/D around 4 to 5]
    A4 -- A5[Long cylinder regime Pcr independent of L/D]
    A5 -- A6[Constant asymptote at 2E over 1 minus nu squared times t over D cubed]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Define the term "critical buckling pressure" of a submersible cylindrical pressure hull. Why is it important in determining the operating depth of a submersible?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **critical buckling pressure ($P_{cr}$)** is the minimum external hydrostatic pressure at which a thin-walled cylindrical shell (pressure hull) suddenly loses its geometric stability and undergoes a large-amplitude radial inward collapse, even though the material stresses may still be well below the yield strength. It is a *stability failure*, not a strength failure.

It is computed classically from equations such as the Mises-Kempner formula:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}}{\dfrac{L}{D} - 0.45 \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{0.5}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1-\nu^{2})^{0.75}}$$

**Importance for operating depth:** The maximum safe operating depth of a submersible is set by the **collapse depth** $h_{cr} = P_{cr,actual} / (\rho g)$ (modified by knockdown factor and factor of safety). Operating beyond this depth results in catastrophic hull collapse, since buckling is sudden, non-recoverable, and typically non-symmetric. Hence, the **stability limit** — not the strength limit — is often the governing design criterion for deep-diving vehicles.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Distinguish between long cylinder buckling and short cylinder buckling of a pressure hull. Identify which one applies for a hull with $L/D = 5.0$ and $t/D = 0.02$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Long Cylinder | Short Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Geometric condition | $L/D > 4$ | $L/D < 1$ |
| Dominant mode | Multiple axial half-waves | Pure ring bending |
| Buckling pattern | Diamond/ovaling pattern | Two-lobe ovaling |
| $P_{cr}$ depends on | $t/D$ only | Both $t/D$ and $L/D$ |
| Governing equation | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.59 E (t/D)^{2.5}}{(1-\nu^{2})^{3/4} (L/D)}$ |

**For the given hull** $L/D = 5.0$ and $t/D = 0.02$:

Since $L/D = 5.0 > 4.0$, this hull falls in the **long cylinder regime**. The critical buckling pressure is given by the long-cylinder formula:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3} = \frac{2E}{0.91} (0.02)^{3} = 1.76 \times 10^{-5} \, E$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the critical buckling pressure expression for a long cylindrical shell under external hydrostatic pressure, starting from the Donnell stability equation. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A steel submersible pressure hull has the following specifications: $D = 1.2$ m, $t = 22$ mm, $L = 4.0$ m, $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. Compute: (i) the classical critical buckling pressure using the Mises-Kempner equation, (ii) the actual critical pressure using a knockdown factor of 0.65, and (iii) the collapse depth. Take seawater density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions (2 marks):*
- Shell is **thin** ($t/D \le 1/20$), isotropic, linearly elastic.
- **Initially perfect** geometry (no imperfections).
- **Axisymmetric loading** (uniform external pressure).
- **Membrane pre-buckling state** (no bending prior to buckling).
- **Linearized stability theory** (eigenvalue bifurcation).
- **Kirchhoff-Love thin shell theory** applies.

*Derivation (5 marks):*

The **Donnell-Mushtari stability equation** for a cylindrical shell under external pressure is:
$$D_s \nabla^{8} w + \frac{E \, t}{R^{2}} \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + P \nabla^{4} \left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\right) = 0$$

For a long cylinder, assume a sinusoidal mode shape:
$$w = A \sin(\beta x) \cos(n\theta)$$

where $\beta = m\pi/L$ is the axial wavenumber and $n$ is the circumferential mode number.

Substituting into the stability equation and applying the long-cylinder approximation ($m = 1$ axial half-wave, $n = 2$ lobes), the critical pressure reduces to:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

The buckling is **independent of length** in this limit — the long cylinder buckles as a column under hoop compression.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Geometric ratios**
$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{0.022}{1.2} = 0.01833, \qquad \frac{L}{D} = \frac{4.0}{1.2} = 3.333$$

**Step 2: Intermediate values**
$$(0.01833)^{2.5} = 4.51 \times 10^{-5}, \qquad (0.01833)^{0.5} = 0.1354$$

$$0.45 \times 0.1354 = 0.0609, \qquad 3.333 - 0.0609 = 3.272$$

$$(1 - 0.3^{2})^{0.75} = (0.91)^{0.75} = 0.9299$$

**Step 3: Mises-Kempner critical pressure**
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 4.51 \times 10^{-5}}{3.272} \times \frac{1}{0.9299}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.462 \times 10^{7}}{3.272} \times 1.0753 = 7.524 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0753 = 8.092 \text{ MPa}$$

**(i) Classical $P_{cr} \approx 8.09$ MPa (1 mark)**

**Step 4: Apply knockdown factor**
$$P_{cr,actual} = 0.65 \times 8.092 = 5.260 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Actual $P_{cr} \approx 5.26$ MPa (1 mark)**

**Step 5: Collapse depth**
$$h_{cr} = \frac{5.260 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{5.260 \times 10^{6}}{10055.25} = 523.1 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Collapse depth $\approx 523$ m (1 mark)**

> **Final Answer (1 mark):** $P_{cr,classical} = 8.09$ MPa, $P_{cr,actual} = 5.26$ MPa, $h_{cr} = 523$ m.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Mises-Kempner equation** for the critical buckling pressure of a cylindrical shell. Discuss how it unifies the long-cylinder and short-cylinder regimes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A ring-stiffened submersible hull has stiffener spacing $L = 0.8$ m, diameter $D = 1.5$ m, and $t = 18$ mm. Material: Titanium alloy with $E = 115$ GPa, $\nu = 0.32$. If the knockdown factor is 0.6, determine: (i) the mode of buckling (long or short cylinder), (ii) the actual critical pressure, and (iii) the maximum allowable operating depth using a factor of safety of 2.0. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Mises-Kempner Theory (7 marks):**

The **Mises-Kempner equation** is given by:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}}{\dfrac{L}{D} - 0.45 \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{0.5}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1-\nu^{2})^{0.75}}$$

This equation was developed to **bridge the gap** between the long-cylinder Euler-type buckling and the short-cylinder ring-bending buckling. (2 marks for stating equation and significance)

**Unification of regimes (5 marks):**

- **For $L/D \to \infty$ (long cylinder):** The denominator $\left[\dfrac{L}{D} - 0.45(t/D)^{0.5}\right]$ is dominated by $L/D$, and the $0.45(t/D)^{0.5}$ term becomes negligible. The expression asymptotically approaches the long-cylinder limit:
$$P_{cr} \approx \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D} \cdot \frac{1}{(1-\nu^{2})^{0.75}}$$
However, as $L/D$ grows, the **mode number $n$** changes from 2 to higher values, and the actual long-cylinder limit converges to $\dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$.

- **For $L/D \to 0$ (short cylinder):** The denominator is dominated by the $-0.45(t/D)^{0.5}$ term (the $L/D$ term vanishes), and the expression approaches the short-cylinder ring-bending limit with exponent 2.5 on $t/D$ and inverse dependence on $L/D$.

The Mises-Kempner equation thus provides a **continuous, smooth transition** across the entire geometric spectrum, making it the most widely used formula in classification society rules (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Geometric ratios**
$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{0.018}{1.5} = 0.012, \qquad \frac{L}{D} = \frac{0.8}{1.5} = 0.533$$

**Step 2: Regime classification (2 marks)**
Since $L/D = 0.533 < 1.0$, this is a **short cylinder** buckling problem, governed by ring-bending between stiffeners. The short-cylinder formula is appropriate.

**Step 3: Intermediate values**
$$(0.012)^{2.5} = 1.58 \times 10^{-5}, \qquad (0.012)^{0.5} = 0.1095$$
$$0.45 \times 0.1095 = 0.0493, \qquad 0.533 - 0.0493 = 0.484$$
$$(1 - 0.32^{2})^{0.75} = (1 - 0.1024)^{0.75} = (0.8976)^{0.75} = 0.920$$

**Step 4: Classical critical pressure**
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 1.15 \times 10^{11} \times 1.58 \times 10^{-5}}{0.484} \times \frac{1}{0.920}$$
$$P_{cr} = \frac{4.722 \times 10^{6}}{0.484} \times 1.087 = 9.756 \times 10^{6} \times 1.087 = 10.60 \text{ MPa}$$

**(i) Mode: Short cylinder (ring bending) (1 mark)**

**(ii) Actual critical pressure:**
$$P_{cr,actual} = 0.6 \times 10.60 = 6.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Allowable operating pressure with FS = 2.0:**
$$P_{allow} = \frac{6.36}{2.0} = 3.18 \text{ MPa}$$

$$h_{op} = \frac{3.18 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{3.18 \times 10^{6}}{10055.25} = 316.2 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Mode = short cylinder; $P_{cr,actual} = 6.36$ MPa; $h_{op} \approx 316$ m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Shell Buckling Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^{2})$ term** — The factor $\frac{1}{(1-\nu^{2})^{0.75}}$ or $\frac{1}{1-\nu^{2}}$ is often omitted by students. For steel ($\nu = 0.3$), this represents a **9.9% to 15% error** in $P_{cr}$ — easily worth 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong regime selection** — Using the long-cylinder formula for a short hull (or vice versa). Always check $L/D$ first; if $1 < L/D < 4$, use **Mises-Kempner**.
>
> 3. **Not applying the knockdown factor** — Classical theory overpredicts $P_{cr}$ by 30–50%. Examiners will deduct marks if the knockdown factor is not applied when computing *actual* collapse depth.
>
> 4. **Confusing $R$ vs $D$** — Some formulas use mean radius $R = D/2$. Mixing up these by a factor of 2 propagates an **8× error** in $(t/D)^{3}$ terms.
>
> 5. **Missing the factor of safety in Part (b)** — The operating depth is NOT the collapse depth. Always divide by FS = 1.5 to 2.0.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units consistently** — Mix-up of mm, m, GPa, MPa is a classic trap. Always convert $E$ to Pa and $D, t$ to meters before substitution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Buckling Deformation of the Shell**

- **Buckling is a stability failure, not a strength failure** — the material need not yield.
- **Governing parameter** is the **critical buckling pressure $P_{cr}$**, which depends on $E$, $\nu$, $t/D$, and $L/D$.
- **Three regimes**: Long ($L/D > 4$), Short ($L/D < 1$), Intermediate ($1 \le L/D \le 4$).
- **Mises-Kempner equation** is the *unified* and most commonly tested formula in KTU exams.
- **Long cylinder:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$ — *independent of $L$*.
- **Short cylinder:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.59 E (t/D)^{2.5}}{(1-\nu^{2})^{3/4} (L/D)}$ — *inversely proportional to $L$*.
- **Knockdown factor** $k_d \in [0.5, 0.7]$ for real shells, accounts for initial imperfections.
- **Inelastic buckling** uses $\sqrt{E_t / E}$ correction when $P_{cr}$ exceeds the proportional limit.
- **Collapse depth:** $h_{cr} = P_{cr,actual} / (\rho g)$ with $\rho_{seawater} = 1025$ kg/m³.
- **Operating depth** = Collapse depth $\div$ Factor of Safety (1.5 to 2.0).
- **Buckling mode number** $n = 2$ for typical cylindrical hulls.
- **Shell failure is sudden, non-symmetric, and catastrophic** — no warning deformation.
- **Strengthening methods**: thicker walls, ring stiffeners, spherical hulls, higher-stiffness materials.
- **Numerical practice**: Always compute $t/D$ and $L/D$ first, then choose the appropriate formula.

> **Key Constants to Memorize:**
> - Seawater density: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
> - Steel: $E = 207$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$
> - HY-80/Ti-6Al-4V/Al: $E = 207/115/70$ GPa
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *failure mode* in submersible engineering is defined as the **distinct, physically identifiable mechanism by which a structural or mechanical component of a submersible loses its ability to perform its intended design function** under the combined action of hydrostatic pressure, hydrostatic gradient, material degradation, cyclic loading, or environmental attack. In the context of pressure hulls, failure modes are classified as those leading to **loss of watertight integrity, loss of geometric stability, or loss of load-carrying capacity**.

For Module 4, the failure modes of primary interest are those of the **pressure hull (resistance against hydrostatic collapse)**, the **secondary structural members (frames, ring stiffeners, bulkheads)**, and the **interface systems (penetrations, viewports, hatch covers, ballast tanks)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Failure Modes

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to be conversant with the following hierarchical classification:

| S.No | Category | Specific Failure Mode |
|------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Geometric / Stability | Shell Buckling (Elastic, Inelastic) |
| 2 | Geometric / Stability | Ring Frame Local Buckling |
| 3 | Geometric / Stability | General / Overall Collapse |
| 4 | Material | Yielding / Plastic Collapse |
| 5 | Material | Fatigue (Low-Cycle, High-Cycle) |
| 6 | Material | Fracture / Brittle Failure |
| 7 | Material | Creep (for non-metallic hulls) |
| 8 | Interface | Seal / Gasket Blowout |
| 9 | Interface | Penetration / Viewport Failure |
| 10 | Environmental | Stress-Corrosion Cracking (SCC) |
| 11 | Environmental | Pitting / Galvanic Corrosion |
| 12 | Operational | Cavitation-Induced Erosion |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crushing Egg" Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a raw egg held between your palms and squeezed uniformly from both ends. The eggshell does *not* crack like a brittle ceramic. Instead, it **snaps inward in a wavy, multi-lobed pattern** before any visible material fracture occurs. This is *buckling*, not *crushing*. Now imagine the same egg but placed inside a thin-walled steel tin can and lowered to the bottom of a 4000 m ocean trench. The tin can does *not* collapse into a flat pancake — it develops a **dimple pattern (diamond-shaped lobes)** that suddenly snaps inward catastrophically. This is exactly what happened to the submersible **Titan** (OceanGate, June 2023). A *failure mode* is essentially the question: *"In what geometric pattern will the structure surrender to the ocean?"*

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Insight:**
> Across the past four university examination cycles under the 2024 Scheme, "Failure Modes" consistently fetches 7 to 14 marks (out of 100) in OENST723, either as a direct Part-A definition or as the backbone of a Part-B design question involving *critical buckling pressure calculation*. Students who confuse **yielding** with **buckling** invariably lose 2 to 3 marks per question.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Memorize)

- Density of seawater: **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
- Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Hydrostatic pressure gradient: **≈ 0.101 MPa per 10 m depth** (≈ 1 atm / 10 m)
- Typical yield strength of marine-grade steel (HY-80): **σ_y ≈ 550 MPa**
- Typical yield strength of titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V): **σ_y ≈ 880 MPa**
- Poisson's ratio for steel: **ν ≈ 0.30**
- Young's modulus for steel: **E ≈ 210 GPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pressure-Hull Loading: The Driving Force Behind Every Failure

A submersible pressure hull is essentially a **thin-walled cylindrical (or spherical) shell** loaded externally by hydrostatic pressure that increases linearly with depth. At any operating depth *h*, the external pressure is:

$$P_{ext} \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h \;+\; P_{atm}$$

For deep-ocean operations the atmospheric term is neglected, and the rule of thumb **1 dbar ≈ 1 m depth** is used.

## 2.2 Buckling — The Dominant Failure Mode

### 2.2.1 What is Elastic Shell Buckling?

Unlike a flat plate which buckles into a single half-wave, a **cylindrical shell under external pressure buckles into a checkerboard of diamond-shaped lobes**. The number of lobes *n* depends on the shell's geometry. This is **geometric instability**, not material failure — the stresses are well below yield when buckling initiates.

### 2.2.2 Logical Steps of Buckling Analysis

- **Step 1 —** Compute the external hydrostatic pressure at the operating depth.
- **Step 2 —** Identify the hull geometry: radius *R*, thickness *t*, unsupported length *L* between ring stiffeners.
- **Step 3 —** Determine the *critical buckling pressure* (P_cr) using the appropriate empirical/theoretical formula.
- **Step 4 —** Compute the **knock-down factor (KDF)** to account for real-shell imperfections (knock-down typically **0.7 to 0.9**).
- **Step 5 —** Apply the safety factor: **FOS = P_cr × KDF / P_ext ≥ 1.5 to 2.0** (depending on classification society).
- **Step 6 —** If FOS is insufficient, redesign — increase *t*, decrease *L* (add ring frames), or change material.

### 2.2.3 The "Why" Behind the Formulas

Each empirical buckling formula was derived by fitting experimental data to a particular *n*-lobe pattern. The classical derivations (Bresse, Mises, Bryan) trade off analytical tractability against accuracy, and each is valid only within a specific range of the dimensionless parameter **Z = L²/(R·t)**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Radius-to-thickness ratio | R/t | 20 – 200 |
| Length-to-radius ratio | L/R | 1 – 10 |
| Batdorf parameter | Z = L²/(R·t) | 50 – 5000 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important reference for KTU Part-B numericals**. Note: every absolute-value and conditional bracket uses `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Validity / Use |
|---|--------------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | External Pressure at Depth | $P_{ext} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Any depth *h* (m) |
| 2 | Hoop Stress (thin cyl.) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}$ | Membrane stress in cylindrical hull |
| 3 | Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2t}$ | End-cap stress in cylindrical hull |
| 4 | Von Mises (long cyl.) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{E}{4\sqrt{1-\nu^{2}}}\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{3}$ | Very long cylinders (Z > 5000) |
| 5 | Bresse-Geckeler | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}}\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{5/2}$ | Intermediate length cylinders |
| 6 | Bryan (intermediate) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{E \cdot t}{R \cdot n^{2}} \left[\dfrac{(n^{2}-1)^{2}}{12}\left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{(t/R)^{2}}{n^{2}-1}\right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\nu^{2}}$ | Optimised for *n* lobes, intermediate Z |
| 7 | Elastic Collapse Pressure (ring-stiffened) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left[\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{E}\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)\right]^{0.6}\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{1.6}$ | Inelastic regime (when $\sigma_{cr}>\sigma_{y}$) |
| 8 | Knock-Down Factor (DNV) | $P_{allow} = \alpha \cdot P_{cr}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.7$ (DNV-OS-C301) |
| 9 | Safety Factor (manned) | $FOS = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{P_{ext}} \geq 1.5$–$2.0$ | Manned submersible convention |
| 10 | Plastic Collapse Pressure | $P_{pc} = 2\sigma_{y}\ln(R_{o}/R_{i})$ | Spherical thick-walled hull |
| 11 | Ring Spacing Limit (Bryan) | $L_{max} = \pi R \sqrt{\dfrac{t/R}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}}}$ | Maximum frame spacing |
| 12 | Stress Intensity (Tresca) | $\sigma_{int} = \vert \sigma_{1} - \sigma_{3} \vert$ | 3-D principal stress comparison |

## 2.4 Engineering Significance of Each Failure Mode

- **Elastic Buckling** dominates in **thin, large-diameter** hulls (e.g., deep-sea ROVs with R/t > 100).
- **Inelastic / Plastic Collapse** dominates in **thick-walled** pressure vessels operating near the yield limit (e.g., human-occupied vehicles at moderate depth).
- **Fatigue Failure** is critical in **repeated-dive** vehicles (military submarines, scientific AUVs) where every dive cycle imposes one full pressure cycle.
- **Stress-Corrosion Cracking** is the silent killer of **titanium hulls in warm, oxygenated water** (rare but catastrophic — e.g., the HMS *Scylla* incident context).
- **Seal Blowout** is the most common *non-buckling* failure mode and is **statistically responsible for the majority of in-service submersible incidents** (e.g., blowout preventer philosophy borrowed from offshore drilling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Hull

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** Prove that the tangential (circumferential) stress in a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure equals **σ_h = PR/t** by the classical "free-body-cut" method. This is foundational for all subsequent buckling and collapse derivations.

### Step 1 — Geometry & Loading Setup

Consider a cylindrical pressure hull of mean radius *R*, wall thickness *t*, and axial length *L*, loaded internally by pressure *P*. The end caps are assumed to be flat and act on the cylinder only through axial reaction.

### Step 2 — Cut the Cylinder Along a Longitudinal Plane

Cut the cylinder with a diametrical plane running parallel to its axis. The resulting free body is a half-cylinder. The internal pressure acts on the projected area of this half-cylinder, which equals *2R·L* (diameter × length).

### Step 3 — Force Balance in the Hoop Direction

The internal pressure creates an outward radial force that must be resisted by the hoop tension in the two cut walls. Each cut wall has cross-sectional area *t·L*.

$$F_{pressure} \;=\; P \cdot 2R \cdot L$$

$$F_{hoop} \;=\; 2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot t \cdot L$$

### Step 4 — Equate and Simplify

Setting the resisting force equal to the driving force:

$$2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot t \cdot L \;=\; P \cdot 2R \cdot L$$

Dividing both sides by *2·L*:

$$\sigma_{h} \cdot t \;=\; P \cdot R$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{h} \;=\; \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

- For a constant internal pressure, hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress (which equals *PR/2t*).
- This is why pressure-hull **welded seams in the longitudinal (girth) direction** are the most critical welds.
- **Units check:** [Pa] = [Pa][m]/[m] → consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bresse-Geckeler Buckling Formula (Long Cylinder)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** Derive the classical elastic buckling pressure for a long unstiffened cylinder. This is the formula most frequently tested in KTU Module-4 Part-B.

### Step 1 — Shell Kinematics Assumption

A long cylinder buckles into *n* circumferential lobes and *m* axial half-waves. For a long cylinder (Z ≫ 1000), the axial wave number collapses to *m = 1*, and the buckled shape is given by a sinusoidal radial displacement *w*.

### Step 2 — Strain Energy of Bending

The bending strain energy stored in the shell per unit area is:

$$U_{bending} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{2} \left[\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{w}{R^{2}}\right)^{2} + 2\nu \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{w}{R^{2}}\right)\right]$$

where *D = E·t³/[12(1−ν²)]* is the flexural rigidity.

### Step 3 — Work Done by External Pressure

The work done by the external pressure *P* on the inextensional deformation is:

$$W_{ext} \;=\; -\dfrac{P}{2} \int_{A}\left[\left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right}\right)^{2}\right] dA$$

(The factor *P/2* arises because pressure acts on the projected area reduction, which is quadratic in *w*.)

### Step 4 — Assume Buckling Mode Shape

Let the buckled displacement take the form:

$$w(x,y) \;=\; A \sin\left(\dfrac{n y}{R}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and Integrate

After integrating *U_bending* and *W_ext* over the shell area and taking the stationary condition *dΠ/dA = 0*, the critical pressure emerges as:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{E \cdot t}{R \cdot n^{2}} \left[\dfrac{(n^{2}-1)^{2}}{12}\left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{(t/R)^{2}}{n^{2}-1}\right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\nu^{2}}$$

### Step 6 — Long-Cylinder Limit

For long cylinders (*L/R → ∞*), the first term inside the brackets vanishes (since *t/L → 0*). Optimising the remaining expression with respect to *n* (by *dP_cr/dn = 0*) yields the optimal lobe number:

$$n_{opt}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1-\nu^{2}}}\sqrt{\dfrac{R}{t}}$$

Substituting back gives the celebrated **Bresse-Geckeler formula**:

$$\boxed{\;P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}}\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{5/2}\;}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Sanity Check (Steel Hull)

Let *R = 1.0 m*, *t = 0.02 m*, *E = 210 × 10⁹ Pa*, *ν = 0.30*.

Plug in:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \times 210 \times 10^{9}}{\sqrt{3 \times (1 - 0.09)}} \times \left(\dfrac{0.02}{1.0}\right)^{2.5}$$

$$\sqrt{3 \times 0.91} = \sqrt{2.73} \approx 1.652$$

$$\left(\dfrac{0.02}{1.0}\right)^{2.5} = (0.02)^{2.5} = (0.02)^{2} \cdot (0.02)^{0.5} = 0.0004 \times 0.1414 = 5.657 \times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{cr} \approx \dfrac{4.20 \times 10^{11}}{1.652} \times 5.657 \times 10^{-5} \approx 2.542 \times 10^{11} \times 5.657 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 14.38 \; MPa}$$

This corresponds to an operating depth of approximately **1423 m** before collapse — consistent with industry data for thin-walled steel ROVs.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — Which Failure Mode Governs?

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix during the KTU exam to justify your chosen formula before substituting numbers.**

| Condition on Geometry | Condition on Stress | Governing Failure Mode | Formula to Apply |
|-----------------------|---------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| $Z = \dfrac{L^{2}}{R t} > 5000$ | $\sigma_{cr} < 0.5 \sigma_{y}$ | Elastic Overall Buckling | Bresse-Geckeler |
| $Z = \dfrac{L^{2}}{R t} \approx 100$–$5000$ | $\sigma_{cr} < 0.5 \sigma_{y}$ | Elastic Shell Buckling | Bryan / Von Mises |
| Any $Z$ | $0.5 \sigma_{y} < \sigma_{cr} < \sigma_{y}$ | Inelastic Buckling | Gerard / Plantema |
| Any $Z$ | $\sigma_{cr} \geq \sigma_{y}$ | Plastic Collapse | P_c = 2 σ_y ln(R_o/R_i) (sphere) |
| $N_{cycles} > 10^{4}$ | $\sigma < \sigma_{y}$ | Fatigue | S-N curve / Miner's rule |
| Aggressive seawater + sustained load | $\sigma < \sigma_{y}$ | Stress Corrosion Cracking | K_ISCC threshold |

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — KTU Style

**Problem (Self-Assessment):**
A cylindrical pressure hull has *R = 0.75 m*, *t = 0.025 m*, *L = 2.5 m* (between ring frames), made of HY-80 steel (*E = 210 GPa*, *ν = 0.30*, *σ_y = 550 MPa*). Determine:
(a) Whether elastic or inelastic buckling governs.
(b) The allowable operating depth using a knock-down factor of 0.75 and FOS = 2.0.

**Solution Outline (use as model for Part-B):**

Compute Z:

$$Z = \dfrac{L^{2}}{R t} = \dfrac{(2.5)^{2}}{0.75 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{6.25}{0.01875} \approx 333$$

Since *Z = 333*, we are in the **intermediate-length regime** → use **Bryan equation**. Try *n = 8* lobes (typical optimum for this geometry):

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{E t}{R n^{2}}\left[\dfrac{(n^{2}-1)^{2}}{12}\left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{(t/R)^{2}}{n^{2}-1}\right]\dfrac{1}{1-\nu^{2}}$$

Compute term by term:

$$\dfrac{E t}{R n^{2}} = \dfrac{210 \times 10^{9} \times 0.025}{0.75 \times 64} \approx 1.094 \times 10^{8} \; Pa$$

$$\left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{0.025}{2.5}\right)^{2} = 10^{-4}$$

$$\dfrac{(n^{2}-1)^{2}}{12}\left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{(63)^{2}}{12} \times 10^{-4} = 330.75 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0331$$

$$\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{0.025}{0.75}\right)^{2} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\dfrac{(t/R)^{2}}{n^{2}-1} = \dfrac{1.111 \times 10^{-3}}{63} \approx 1.764 \times 10^{-5}$$

Sum inside brackets ≈ 0.0331. Divide by *(1 − ν²) = 0.91* → 0.0364.

$$P_{cr} \approx 1.094 \times 10^{8} \times 0.0364 \approx 3.98 \; MPa$$

Allowable pressure = *(KDF / FOS) × P_cr = 0.75 / 2.0 × 3.98 ≈ 1.49 MPa*.

Operating depth:

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{P_{allow}}{\rho_{sw} g} = \dfrac{1.49 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81} \approx 148 \; m$$

(Students should redo the optimisation with *n = 7* and *n = 9* to find the *P_cr_max*.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Failure Modes in a Submersible Pressure Hull

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1 — Conceptual Map.** Maps the logical hierarchy from the broad *category* of failure (stability vs. material) down to the specific engineering mechanism.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA1["Submersible Failure Modes"]
    nodeB1["Stability Failures"]
    nodeB2["Material Failures"]
    nodeB3["Interface Failures"]
    nodeB4["Environmental Failures"]
    nodeC1["Elastic Buckling"]
    nodeC2["Inelastic Buckling"]
    nodeC3["Plastic Collapse"]
    nodeC4["Overall Collapse"]
    nodeC5["Yielding"]
    nodeC6["Fatigue"]
    nodeC7["Fracture"]
    nodeC8["Creep"]
    nodeC9["Seal Blowout"]
    nodeC10["Penetration Failure"]
    nodeC11["Viewport Failure"]
    nodeC12["Stress Corrosion Cracking"]
    nodeC13["Pitting Corrosion"]
    nodeC14["Galvanic Corrosion"]
    nodeD1["Bresse-Geckeler Formula"]
    nodeD2["Bryan Formula"]
    nodeD3["Gerard Equation"]
    nodeD4["Thick-Wall Formula"]
    nodeD5["Tresca Yield Criterion"]
    nodeD6["Miners Rule"]
    nodeD7["Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics"]
    nodeD8["Larson Miller Parameter"]
    nodeD9["O-Ring Compression Set"]
    nodeD10["DNV Penetration Rules"]
    nodeD11["ASME BPVC Section 8"]
    nodeD12["NACE MR0175"]
    nodeD13["Cathodic Protection Design"]
    nodeD14["Material Selection Chart"]

    rootA1 --> nodeB1
    rootA1 --> nodeB2
    rootA1 --> nodeB3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Flow of Failure Progression

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 2 — Engineering Decision Flow.** Use this to *visualise the analytical procedure* an engineer follows when evaluating whether a hull is safe.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Input: Depth of Operation h"]
    procA["Compute External Pressure P_ext"]
    procB["Identify Hull Geometry R t L"]
    procC["Compute Z Parameter L squared over R t"]
    decisionA{"Z greater than 5000?"}
    pathA1["Apply Bresse-Geckeler"]
    pathA2["Apply Bryan Equation"]
    procD["Compute P_cr from chosen formula"]
    procE["Compute Critical Stress sigma_cr"]
    decisionB{"sigma_cr greater than 0.5 sigma_y?"}
    pathB1["Elastic Regime"]
    pathB2["Inelastic Regime apply Gerard"]
    procF["Apply Knock-Down Factor KDF"]
    procG["Apply Factor of Safety FOS"]
    procH["Compute Allowable Depth h_allow"]
    decisionC{"FOS greater than 1.5?"}
    endA["PASS - Design Acceptable"]
    endB["FAIL - Redesign Required"]
    feedback["Increase t or add ring stiffeners"]

    startA --> procA
    procA --> procB
    procB --> procC
    procC --> decisionA
    decisionA -- YES --> pathA1
    decisionA -- NO --> pathA2
    pathA1 --> procD

    decisionB -- YES --> pathB2

    pathB2 --> procF

    procG --> procH
    procH --> decisionC
    decisionC -- YES --> endA
    decisionC -- NO --> endB
    endB --> feedback
    feedback --> procB
```
