# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Set 2]

**Differentiate between elastic buckling and plastic collapse as failure modes of a submersible pressure hull. State the governing empirical formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Elastic Buckling (1 mark):** A geometric instability that occurs when the compressive membrane stress in the shell wall reaches a critical value *before* the material yields. The buckled shape is a pattern of diamond-shaped lobes. Governing formula: **Bresse-Geckeler equation — P_cr = (2E/√(3(1−ν²)))·(t/R)^(5/2)** for long cylinders, or the **Bryan equation** for intermediate lengths.

- **Plastic Collapse (1 mark):** A material-based failure mode that occurs when the entire hull cross-section reaches the yield stress *σ_y*. It governs in thick-walled pressure vessels. Governing formula: **P_pc = 2 σ_y · ln(R_o/R_i)** for spherical hulls, and a Tresca-based equivalent for cylinders.

- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Elastic buckling is *geometry-dominated* (independent of σ_y), whereas plastic collapse is *strength-dominated* (independent of E). The crossover occurs at approximately *t/R ≈ 0.05* for marine-grade steel.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Set 1]

**List any six distinct failure modes relevant to a manned submersible pressure hull and identify the engineering parameter most critical to each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list 6 × 0.5 marks each):**

| # | Failure Mode | Most Critical Parameter |
|---|--------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Elastic Shell Buckling | Radius-to-thickness ratio **R/t** |
| 2 | Plastic Collapse | Yield strength **σ_y** |
| 3 | Fatigue | Stress range **Δσ** and cycle count **N** |
| 4 | Stress-Corrosion Cracking | Stress-intensity factor **K_ISCC** |
| 5 | Seal / Gasket Blowout | O-ring compression set (%), gasket hardness |
| 6 | Viewport Failure | Cone-angle (typically 40°–60°) and crown thickness |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a clear schematic, derive the **Bresse-Geckeler formula** for the elastic critical buckling pressure of a long unstiffened cylindrical pressure hull under external hydrostatic pressure. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A submersible pressure hull has mean radius *R = 0.80 m*, wall thickness *t = 0.022 m*, length *L = 3.0 m*, material properties *E = 210 GPa*, *ν = 0.30*. Using the appropriate buckling formula, determine **(i)** the critical buckling pressure and **(ii)** the maximum safe operating depth if the design uses a knock-down factor of 0.70 and a factor of safety of 2.0. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Long, perfectly cylindrical shell (no geometric imperfections).
- Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
- Buckling lobes are sinusoidal; membrane pre-buckling stresses neglected.
- Classical Donnell-type shallow-shell kinematics.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**
1. State total potential energy Π = U_bending + W_ext. (1 mark)
2. Express *D = E·t³/[12(1−ν²)]*. (0.5 mark)
3. Assume displacement *w = A sin(ny/R) sin(πx/L)*. (1 mark)
4. Integrate over area; equate *dΠ/dA = 0*. (1.5 marks)
5. Apply long-cylinder limit (*L/R → ∞*, hence *m = 1*) and optimise over *n* to obtain *n_opt² = (2/√3)·√(1/(1−ν²))·√(R/t)*. (1 mark)
6. Final result: *P_cr = (2E/√(3(1−ν²)))·(t/R)^(5/2)*. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Classify the cylinder (1 mark):**

$$Z = \dfrac{L^{2}}{R t} = \dfrac{(3.0)^{2}}{0.80 \times 0.022} = \dfrac{9.0}{0.0176} \approx 511$$

Since *Z ≈ 511* (intermediate, not very long), the **Bresse-Geckeler formula is the first approximation**. (For an exact answer, Bryan should be used — see below.)

**Step 2 — Apply Bresse-Geckeler (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \times 210 \times 10^{9}}{\sqrt{3 \times (1 - 0.09)}} \times \left(\dfrac{0.022}{0.80}\right)^{2.5}$$

$$\sqrt{3 \times 0.91} = \sqrt{2.73} \approx 1.652$$

$$(0.0275)^{2.5} = (0.0275)^{2} \cdot (0.0275)^{0.5} = 7.563 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1658 = 1.254 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{4.20 \times 10^{11}}{1.652} \times 1.254 \times 10^{-4} \approx 31.88 \; MPa$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bryan refinement (1.5 marks):**
For *n = 8* lobes, substituting in Bryan equation gives *P_cr ≈ 10.4 MPa*. (Students should verify by direct calculation.)

**Step 4 — Apply KDF and FOS (1 mark):**

$$P_{allow} = \dfrac{0.70 \times 10.4}{2.0} = 3.64 \; MPa$$

**Step 5 — Convert to depth (1 mark):**

$$h_{max} = \dfrac{P_{allow}}{\rho_{sw} \, g} = \dfrac{3.64 \times 10^{6}}{1025 \times 9.81} \approx 362 \; m$$

**Step 6 — State the answer with units and safety commentary (1 mark):**

> *The hull is safe to operate at depths ≤ 362 m under the assumed KDF and FOS. The Bresse-Geckeler value (31.88 MPa) over-predicts by ~3× because the hull is not in the long-cylinder limit; Bryan is the correct choice.*

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternate]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the four major categories of failure modes that can occur in a submersible pressure hull. For each category, name one practical real-world incident where it was the dominant cause. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A spherical submersible hull has outer radius *R_o = 1.20 m*, inner radius *R_i = 1.16 m*, fabricated from a titanium alloy (*σ_y = 880 MPa*, *ν = 0.34*). Compute **(i)** the plastic collapse pressure, **(ii)** the elastic critical buckling pressure using Von Mises formula with E = 114 GPa, and **(iii)** comment on which failure mode governs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Four categories (1.25 marks each, plus 2 marks for examples):**

1. **Geometric / Stability Failure** — Elastic shell buckling into *n* lobes. *Example:* the *Titan* submersible (OceanGate, 2023) catastrophically buckled under hydrostatic compression at ~3800 m depth.

2. **Material Failure** — Yielding and plastic collapse in thick-walled regions. *Example:* the *Byford Dolphin* diving bell accident (1983), where a pressurisation system failure caused explosive decompression and barotrauma.

3. **Interface / Joint Failure** — Seal and penetration blowout. *Example:* the *Johnson Sea-Link* (1973) fire, in which water ingress through a hatch seal contributed to the casualty.

4. **Environmental / Time-Dependent Failure** — Stress-corrosion cracking and fatigue. *Example:* the *HMHS Britannic* (1916) — though a surface ship, the mechanism of brittle fracture in cold seawater is illustrative.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Plastic Collapse Pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{pc} = 2 \, \sigma_{y} \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_{o}}{R_{i}}\right) = 2 \times 880 \times 10^{6} \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.20}{1.16}\right)$$

$$\ln(1.20/1.16) = \ln(1.03448) \approx 0.03390$$

$$P_{pc} \approx 2 \times 880 \times 10^{6} \times 0.03390 \approx 59.66 \; MPa$$

**(ii) Elastic Critical Buckling — Von Mises (2.5 marks):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{E}{4\sqrt{1 - \nu^{2}}}\left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{3}$$

With *t = R_o − R_i = 0.04 m*, *R = (R_o + R_i)/2 = 1.18 m*, *E = 114 GPa*, *ν = 0.34*:

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.34^{2}} = \sqrt{0.8844} \approx 0.9404$$

$$\left(\dfrac{0.04}{1.18}\right)^{3} = (0.03390)^{3} \approx 3.894 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{114 \times 10^{9}}{4 \times 0.9404} \times 3.894 \times 10^{-5} \approx \dfrac{1.14 \times 10^{11}}{3.7616} \times 3.894 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\approx 3.030 \times 10^{10} \times 3.894 \times 10^{-5} \approx 1.18 \; MPa$$

**(iii) Commentary on Governing Mode (2.5 marks):**

- The Von Mises elastic buckling pressure (*1.18 MPa*) is **~50× smaller** than the plastic collapse pressure (*59.66 MPa*).
- Therefore, **elastic buckling governs** for this thin spherical hull.
- Practical depth limit (with KDF = 0.70, FOS = 2.0): *P_allow = 0.70 × 1.18 / 2.0 ≈ 0.413 MPa* → depth ≈ **41 m**.
- *Engineering recommendation:* increase wall thickness, add external ring stiffeners, or transition to a thicker-walled cylinder if deeper operation is required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Failure-Mode Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (loses 1 mark):** Always keep MPa, GPa, m, mm consistent. Convert everything to SI base units *before* substitution.
> 2. **Formula Mismatch (loses 2 marks):** Bresse-Geckeler is for *long* cylinders only. Bryan must be used for intermediate *Z*. If the student blindly applies Bresse-Geckeler to an intermediate hull, full marks are forfeited even if arithmetic is correct.
> 3. **Forgetting the Knock-Down Factor (loses 1 mark):** Real shells have imperfections; textbook P_cr is always *over-optimistic*. KDF must be applied explicitly.
> 4. **Conflating Buckling with Yielding (loses 2 marks):** A common error is to compute σ_h = PR/t and compare it with σ_y. This is *yielding* check, not *buckling*. They are two different failure modes.
> 5. **Missing the 1 m ↔ 0.101 MPa Conversion:** At depth 1000 m, *P_ext = 10.1 MPa*, not 100 MPa or 1 MPa.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Failure Modes):**

- ☐ **Elastic shell buckling** is a *geometric instability* in thin shells — it occurs *before* the material yields.
- ☐ **Plastic collapse** is a *strength-based* failure in thick shells — it occurs at full-section yielding.
- ☐ **Bresse-Geckeler** → long cylinders (*Z > 5000*); **Bryan** → intermediate cylinders (*Z = 100–5000*); **Von Mises** → very thick cylinders.
- ☐ **Knock-down factor (KDF ≈ 0.7)** accounts for real-shell imperfections (out-of-roundness, eccentricity of load).
- ☐ **FOS for manned submersibles ≥ 1.5 to 2.0** depending on classification society (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's).
- ☐ **Hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress** in a thin cylinder — the *girth* welds are critical.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure** increases at **~0.101 MPa per 10 m depth** in seawater.
- ☐ **Fatigue** is dominant in *repeated-dive* vehicles; use Miner's rule for cumulative damage.
- ☐ **Stress-corrosion cracking** is *silent and catastrophic* — it can occur at stresses far below σ_y.
- ☐ **Seal/gasket failure** is the most common *in-service* failure mode (statistical majority).
- ☐ Always **draw a free-body diagram** before applying any formula — KTU examiners reward methodology as much as arithmetic.
- ☐ **Spherical hulls** are more efficient than cylinders (volume/surface ratio) but harder to manufacture — used in deep-rated submersibles (e.g., *Limiting Factor*, *Aluminaut*).
- ☐ **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V)** is preferred for deep-rated hulls due to high strength-to-weight ratio and corrosion resistance; **HY-80/HY-100 steel** is standard for military submarines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Hull Penetration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Hull Penetration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pressure Hull Penetration** is any engineered opening, aperture, or cut-out made through the pressure hull plating of a submersible (submarine, ROV, AUV, bathyscaphe) that allows the passage of mechanical systems, electrical cabling, piping, shafts, or personnel while maintaining the structural integrity of the hull under external hydrostatic pressure at operating depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Construct):** A pressure hull penetration is a localized discontinuity in the pressure hull shell, mandatorily compensated by reinforcement (ring-stiffened, pad-stiffened, or insert-stiffened) to restore the load-carrying capacity removed by the opening.

In design terms, every penetration is treated as a **stress concentration source** that must be analytically re-classified as a **reinforced discontinuity**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine punching a hole in a sealed, pressurized water tank. The moment the hole is created, the entire hoop-stress pattern that was previously uniform in the plate redistributes itself around the edges of the hole. The stress lines (called **stress trajectories**) bunch together at the sides of the hole, causing the local stress to **multiply by a factor of 3** for a small circular hole in an infinite plate under uniaxial tension.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a rubber sheet stretched over a bowl. Pinch the sheet with your fingers on opposite sides — you'll see the wrinkles (stress lines) crowd together at the pinch points. Similarly, around a hole in a stretched plate, stress "wrinkles" crowd at the horizontal edges (perpendicular to the load), and the local stress rises sharply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (Bold):** The theoretical **stress concentration factor (Kt)** for a small circular hole in a thin infinite plate under uniaxial tension is exactly **3.0** (Kirsch, 1898). For **biaxial (hydrostatic) loading**, the peak stress is **2σ** instead of **3σ** at the hole boundary, because the biaxial component partially cancels the uniaxial amplification.

### 1.3 Categories of Penetrations (KTU Board Classification)

Pressure hull penetrations are classified by KTU/NAVSEA convention into four principal classes:

1. **Small Penetrations (D ≤ 0.05·R)** — Cables, small pipes, sensor leads. Handled with packing glands and threaded bosses.
2. **Medium Penetrations (0.05·R < D ≤ 0.20·R)** — Valve stems, sonar transducers. Reinforced with welded rings or pads.
3. **Large Penetrations (D > 0.20·R)** — Main hatch, sail hatch, escape trunk. Requires internal ring frames, doubler plates, and insert rings.
4. **Shaft & Conical Penetrations** — Propeller shaft, bow plane shaft, stabilizer shafts. Use conical/cylindrical bosses to distribute load gradually.

*(Where D = penetration diameter, R = pressure hull radius.)*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Stress Concentration

When a hole is cut in a cylindrical pressure hull (modeled as a curved plate), three physical effects occur simultaneously:

- **Geometric Discontinuity:** The load-carrying cross-section is locally reduced by the projected hole area.
- **Stress Trajectory Reflow:** Stress lines cannot terminate at the hole edge; they must detour around the hole, increasing the local line density.
- **Bending / Membrane Coupling:** Near the hole, the through-thickness stress distribution (originally uniform) becomes non-uniform, introducing a secondary bending moment.

The combined effect is captured by the **net section efficiency (η)** of the hull at the penetration station.

### 2.2 Design Philosophy: The "Area Replacement" Method

The classical KTU/NAVSEA design approach to penetration reinforcement rests on one cardinal principle:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Axiom:** The material **removed** to create the opening must be **replaced** (or over-replaced) by reinforcement material, integrated over the effective length over which load is shed into the penetration.

This is implemented through the **Area Balance Equation** (see formula sheet below). The reinforcement can be provided by:

- **Doubler Plate / Pad (External or Internal):** A flat plate welded concentrically over the opening.
- **Insert Ring (Ring-Stiffened Penetration):** A heavier section ring fitted inside the hull at the cut-out, with full-penetration welds.
- **Boss-Type Penetration:** A forged cylindrical/conical boss welded to the hull plate, used for shaft and pipe penetrations.

### 2.3 The "2:1 Spacing Rule" (Board-Exam Favourite)

Penetrations must be spaced apart to prevent overlapping stress fields. The empirical KTU/NAVSEA rule is:

**Minimum centre-to-centre distance between adjacent penetrations ≥ 2 × (sum of their radii)**

That is, $S_{min} = 2(R_1 + R_2)$, and the minimum distance from any penetration edge to the nearest ring frame or bulkhead is also subject to the same 2:1 rule measured from the **edge of the reinforced zone**.

### 2.4 Effective Width Concept

Because load shed into a penetration spreads out at an angle (typically taken as 30° to the hull surface, or as a 2:1 slope), the reinforcement is considered effective only within a **frustum-shaped region** of axial length:

$$L_{eff} = \sqrt{D \cdot t_{hull}} \quad \text{or} \quad L_{eff} = 0.5 \cdot D \text{ (whichever is greater)}$$

This $L_{eff}$ defines the axial extent of hull plate that contributes to carrying the load around the opening, and it is the length over which the reinforcement area is integrated.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{t}$ | Hoop stress in intact cylindrical hull | Pa, m |
| 2 | $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{2t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress in intact hull | Pa, m |
| 3 | $K_t = 3$ (uniaxial) | Stress concentration factor, small circular hole in infinite plate | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_{peak} = 2\sigma_{hoop}$ (biaxial hydrostatic) | Peak stress at circular hole under equal biaxial tension | Pa |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{net}}{A_{gross}}$ | Net section efficiency at the penetration | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A_{removed} = D \cdot t_{hull}$ | Cross-sectional area removed by the hole (projected) | m² |
| 7 | $A_{reinf} = A_{removed} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{hoop}}{\sigma_{allow,reinf}}$ | Required reinforcement cross-section (Area Balance Eq.) | m² |
| 8 | $L_{eff} = \max\!\left(\sqrt{D \cdot t_{hull}},\; 0.5 D\right)$ | Effective axial length contributing to reinforcement | m |
| 9 | $S_{min} \ge 2(R_1 + R_2)$ | 2:1 Spacing Rule between adjacent penetrations | m |
| 10 | $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress (yield criterion) | Pa |
| 11 | $p_{collapse} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Critical buckling pressure of ring-stiffened cylinder (von Mises, 1914) | Pa |
| 12 | $F.S._{design} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{vM,operating}} \ge 1.5$ | Minimum factor of safety on yielding (KTU/NAVSEA SUBSAFE) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Tip:** In Part A questions, examiners expect you to recall at minimum Equations (1), (2), (3), and (7) by heart. In Part B derivations, at least one of (7), (8), and (12) will be required for a full 14-mark solution.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of pressure hull penetrations is foundational in:

- **Nuclear Submarines:** Reactor coolant pipes, control rod drive shafts, sonar arrays.
- **Deep-Sea Manned Submersibles (e.g., DSV *Limiting Factor*):** Viewport penetrations rated to **1100 atm ≈ 110 MPa**.
- **AUVs/ROVs:** Cable penetrations, pressure-tolerant electronics pods, thruster shafts.
- **Submarine Rescue Vessels:** Trunk openings, escape hatch geometry, mateable skirt penetrations.

The deeper the operating depth, the more dominant the penetration geometry becomes relative to the parent hull — at 6000 m (Trench depth), a 100 mm penetration behaves like a substantial structural feature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Peak Stress Around a Circular Hole in a Biaxially-Loaded Plate (Kirsch-Generalized Solution)

**Problem Setup:** A thin, isotropic plate of large extent is loaded by equal biaxial tensions $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_0$ (simulating hydrostatic pressure-induced equal bi-axial membrane stresses in a cylinder). A small circular hole of radius $a$ is drilled through the plate. Find the tangential stress $\sigma_\theta$ at the hole boundary.

**Step 1 — Superposition Principle:**
Biaxial equal tension is decomposed into two perpendicular uniaxial tensions of magnitude $\sigma_0$ each, applied simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Apply the Kirsch (1898) solution for a uniaxial tension $\sigma_0$ along the x-axis:**
For the uniaxial component acting along x-axis, the tangential stress at the hole boundary ($r = a$) is:

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{(x)}(a, \theta) = \sigma_0 \bigl(1 - 2\cos 2\theta\bigr)
$$

*Logic:* This is the well-known Kirsch result. At $\theta = 90°$ (top/bottom of hole, perpendicular to load), $\cos 2\theta = \cos 180° = -1$, giving $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_0(1 + 2) = 3\sigma_0$ — the famous Kt = 3.

**Step 3 — Apply Kirsch solution for the orthogonal uniaxial tension $\sigma_0$ along the y-axis:**
By rotating the coordinate system by 90°, we obtain:

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{(y)}(a, \theta) = \sigma_0 \bigl(1 - 2\cos[2(\theta - 90°)]\bigr) = \sigma_0 \bigl(1 + 2\cos 2\theta\bigr)
$$

*Logic:* At $\theta = 0°$ (right side of hole, perpendicular to y-load), $\cos 0° = 1$, giving $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_0(1 + 2) = 3\sigma_0$ — confirming the stress is amplified in the direction perpendicular to each uniaxial load.

**Step 4 — Superpose the two uniaxial contributions:**
Adding Step 2 and Step 3:

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{biaxial}(a, \theta) = \sigma_0(1 - 2\cos 2\theta) + \sigma_0(1 + 2\cos 2\theta)
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{biaxial}(a, \theta) = 2\sigma_0
$$

*Logic:* Under equal biaxial tension, the peak tangential stress at the hole boundary is **constant** at $2\sigma_0$ for every angle $\theta$. This is why biaxial hydrostatic loading is *less severe* than uniaxial: the peak-to-mean ratio drops from 3 to 2.

**Step 6 — Specialize to a cylindrical pressure hull:**
For a deep-diving submersible, the in-plane membrane stresses in the hull are:

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_{long} = \frac{pR}{2t}, \qquad \sigma_y = \sigma_{hoop} = \frac{pR}{t}
$$

These are **not** equal. To handle the general case, we treat each component separately and superpose:

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{peak}(a, \theta = 90°) = 3\sigma_x - \sigma_y = 3\!\left(\frac{pR}{2t}\right) - \frac{pR}{t} = \frac{pR}{2t}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{peak}(a, \theta = 0°) = 3\sigma_y - \sigma_x = 3\!\left(\frac{pR}{t}\right) - \frac{pR}{2t} = \frac{5pR}{2t}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion / Engineering Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\theta}^{max} = \frac{5pR}{2t} \quad \text{at the points of the hole on the longitudinal axis (sides of hole)}\;}
$$

This $5/2$ factor is the **governing design stress** at any unreinforced hull penetration. Reinforcement must bring the operating stress back below the allowable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Reinforcement Area (Area Balance Method)

**Problem Setup:** A circular penetration of diameter $D$ is cut into a cylindrical hull of thickness $t_h$. The hoop stress in the intact hull is $\sigma_{hoop} = pR/t_h$. The reinforcement pad has allowable stress $\sigma_{reinf}$ and is to be sized.

**Step 1 — Compute the membrane load removed per unit axial length of hull:**
The hoop force per unit axial length originally carried by the removed material is:

$$
f_{removed} = \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t_h
$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of penetration geometry:**

$$
f_{removed} = \frac{pR}{t_h} \cdot t_h = pR \quad \text{(per unit length)}
$$

But for a finite length $L_{eff}$, the total load that must be transferred around the opening is:

$$
F_{removed} = \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t_h \cdot L_{eff} = \frac{pR}{t_h} \cdot t_h \cdot L_{eff} = pR \cdot L_{eff}
$$

**Step 3 — Force that the reinforcement must carry:**
At the penetration station, the reinforcement cross-section $A_{reinf}$ (in mm²) carries the load at its allowable stress $\sigma_{reinf}$:

$$
F_{reinf} = \sigma_{reinf} \cdot A_{reinf}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the forces (Area Balance):**

$$
\sigma_{reinf} \cdot A_{reinf} = \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t_h \cdot L_{eff}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $A_{reinf}$:**

$$
A_{reinf} = \frac{\sigma_{hoop} \cdot t_h \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{reinf}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\sigma_{hoop} = pR/t_h$:**

$$
\boxed{\;A_{reinf} = \frac{pR \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{reinf}}\;}
$$

*Logic check (units):* Pa · m / Pa = m². Correct.

**Step 7 — Apply factor of safety:**
For KTU 2024 SUBSAFE-aligned design, $F.S. \ge 1.5$ is applied as a stress derating on the reinforcement allowable. Effective allowable becomes $\sigma_{reinf,eff} = \sigma_{yield}/1.5$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution Path)

**Given:** A submersible pressure hull has $R = 2.5$ m, $t_h = 0.04$ m, operating depth $h = 1000$ m (assume seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²). A circular cable penetration of $D = 0.10$ m is to be cut, reinforced by an internal insert ring of HY-80 steel ($\sigma_{yield} = 550$ MPa, $\sigma_{reinf,allow} = 550/1.5 = 366.7$ MPa).

**Step 1 — Compute hydrostatic pressure:**

$$
p = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1000 = 10{,}055{,}250 \; \text{Pa} \approx 10.06 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute intact hull hoop stress:**

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{pR}{t_h} = \frac{10.06 \times 10^6 \times 2.5}{0.04} = 628.9 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective reinforcement length:**

$$
\sqrt{D \cdot t_h} = \sqrt{0.10 \times 0.04} = \sqrt{0.004} = 0.0632 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
0.5 D = 0.05 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
L_{eff} = \max(0.0632,\; 0.05) = 0.0632 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute required reinforcement cross-section area:**

$$
A_{reinf} = \frac{pR \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{reinf,allow}} = \frac{10.06 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \times 0.0632}{366.7 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
A_{reinf} = \frac{1{,}589{,}480}{366.7 \times 10^6} = 4.334 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2 = 4334 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Size a rectangular insert ring of width $b$ and thickness $t_r$:**
If we choose $t_r = 0.025$ m (25 mm), then:

$$
b = \frac{A_{reinf}}{t_r} = \frac{4.334 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} = 0.173 \; \text{m} \approx 175 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification with 2:1 rule:**
If another identical penetration is 0.5 m away (centre-to-centre), the rule requires:

$$
S_{min} = 2(R_1 + R_2) = 2(0.05 + 0.05) = 0.20 \; \text{m}
$$

Since 0.5 m > 0.20 m, spacing is acceptable. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pressure Hull Penetration Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operating Depth and Design Pressure p] --> B[Hull Geometry: R and t_h]
    B --> C[Compute Intact Hoop and Longitudinal Stress]
    C --> D{Penetration Type and Size D}
    D --> D1[Small: Cable and Sensor D le 0.05R]
    D --> D2[Medium: Valve and Sonar 0.05R to 0.20R]
    D --> D3[Large: Hatch and Trunk D gt 0.20R]
    D --> D4[Shaft and Conical Boss]
    D1 --> E[Compute Peak Stress at Hole Edge]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Area Balance Method]
    F --> G[Compute Required Reinf Area A_reinf]
    G --> H[Select Reinf Type: Pad or Ring or Boss]
    H --> I[Verify 2 to 1 Spacing Rule]
    I --> J[Check Von Mises Yield Criterion]
    J --> K[Apply Factor of Safety 1.5]
    K --> L{FOS and Spacing Satisfied}
    L -- Yes --> M[Final Design Approved]
    L -- No --> N[Iterate: Increase t_reinf or b]
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section: Ring-Reinforced Circular Penetration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[External Sea Water Hydrostatic Pressure p] --> P2[Hull Plate Thickness t_h]
    P2 --> P3[Circular Cutout Diameter D]
    P3 --> P4[Insert Ring Thickness t_r]
    P4 --> P5[Insert Ring Axial Width b]
    P5 --> P6[Full Penetration Weld at Outer Edge]
    P5 --> P7[Full Penetration Weld at Inner Edge]
    P2 --> P8[Effective Length L_eff = sqrt of D times t_h]
    P8 --> P4
```

### 4.3 Penetration Type Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Pressure Hull Penetration Types] --> T1[Small: D le 0.05R]
    T --> T2[Medium: 0.05R to 0.20R]
    T --> T3[Large: D gt 0.20R]
    T --> T4[Conical Boss Type]
    T1 --> T1a[Threaded Boss with Packing Gland]
    T1 --> T1b[Conduit and Cable Gland]
    T2 --> T2a[Welded Pad Reinforcement]
    T2 --> T2b[Sonar Transducer Dome Mount]
    T3 --> T3a[Internal Ring Frame Doubler]
    T3 --> T3b[Insert Ring with Trunk Sleeve]
    T4 --> T4a[Propeller Shaft Boss]
    T4 --> T4b[Stabilizer and Plane Shaft]
    T4 --> T4c[Periscope and Mast Well]
```

### 4.4 Stress Flow Visualization Around a Circular Hole (Block Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Hoop Stress Lines Approaching Hole] --> S2[Stress Bunching at Sides theta equals 0 deg]
    S2 --> S3[Peak Stress equals 5pR over 2t]
    S1 --> S4[Stress Reduction at Top and Bottom theta equals 90 deg]
    S4 --> S5[Local Stress equals pR over 2t]
    S3 --> S6[Region of Reinforcement Required]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Effective Length L_eff]
    S7 --> S8[Insert Ring or Pad Placed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1.** Define a pressure hull penetration. State any two classifications based on size.

**Model Answer:**
A pressure hull penetration is any opening made through the pressure-resistant shell of a submersible to permit passage of mechanical, electrical, or hydraulic systems while preserving hull integrity under hydrostatic load. **Classification by size (relative to hull radius R):**
- *Small penetrations:* $D \le 0.05 R$ (cables, sensors).
- *Medium penetrations:* $0.05 R < D \le 0.20 R$ (valve stems, sonar).
- *Large penetrations:* $D > 0.20 R$ (hatches, trunks). **[3 marks]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2.** What is the stress concentration factor for a small circular hole in an infinite plate under uniaxial tension, and how does it change under equal biaxial tension?

**Model Answer:**
The stress concentration factor (Kt) for a small circular hole in an infinite plate under **uniaxial tension is 3.0** (Kirsch, 1898). Under **equal biaxial tension** (such as hydrostatic pressure on a closed pressure hull), the peak tangential stress at the hole boundary is reduced to $2\sigma_0$ (uniform around the hole), effectively a peak-to-mean ratio of 2, because the two orthogonal components partially cancel the uniaxial amplification. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Q8]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the peak tangential stress around a small circular penetration in a cylindrical pressure hull subjected to external hydrostatic pressure. State the underlying assumptions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A submersible pressure hull has inner radius $R = 3.0$ m, hull thickness $t = 0.05$ m, and is to operate at a depth of 1500 m. A circular penetration of diameter $D = 0.20$ m is to be cut. Compute: (i) intact hoop stress, (ii) peak stress at the penetration edge, and (iii) the required reinforcement area assuming a HY-80 insert ring with allowable stress $\sigma_{reinf,allow} = 367$ MPa. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**
1. The intact cylindrical hull under external pressure $p$ has membrane stresses $\sigma_{hoop} = pR/t$ and $\sigma_{long} = pR/(2t)$ in the longitudinal and circumferential directions respectively. **[1 mark]**
2. Treat the local plate around the hole as an infinite plate under biaxial stress $\sigma_x = \sigma_{long}$, $\sigma_y = \sigma_{hoop}$. **[1 mark]**
3. Apply Kirsch's solution for uniaxial tension to each direction and superpose: at the hole edge ($r = a$), the tangential stress is $\sigma_\theta(\theta) = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y) - 2(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta$. **[2 marks]**
4. The maximum occurs at $\theta = 0°$ (sides of hole on longitudinal axis): $\sigma_{\theta}^{max} = 3\sigma_y - \sigma_x = 3(pR/t) - pR/(2t) = \mathbf{5pR/(2t)}$. **[2 marks]**
5. *Assumptions:* small hole ($a \ll \sqrt{Rt}$), thin plate ($t \ll R$), membrane (no bending), far-field uniform stress, isotropic linear-elastic material. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Numerical solution:**

*Step (i) — Hydrostatic pressure:*
$$p = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1500 = 15.08 \; \text{MPa}$$

*Step (ii) — Intact hoop stress:*
$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{pR}{t} = \frac{15.08 \times 10^6 \times 3.0}{0.05} = 904.8 \; \text{MPa}$$

*Step (iii) — Peak stress at penetration edge:*
$$\sigma_{peak} = \frac{5pR}{2t} = \frac{5 \times 15.08 \times 10^6 \times 3.0}{2 \times 0.05} = 2.262 \; \text{GPa}$$

*Step (iv) — Effective length:*
$$L_{eff} = \max\!\left(\sqrt{0.20 \times 0.05},\; 0.5 \times 0.20\right) = \max(0.10,\; 0.10) = 0.10 \; \text{m}$$

*Step (v) — Required reinforcement area:*
$$A_{reinf} = \frac{pR \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{reinf,allow}} = \frac{15.08 \times 10^6 \times 3.0 \times 0.10}{367 \times 10^6} = 0.01233 \; \text{m}^2 = \mathbf{12{,}330 \; mm^2}$$

*Verification:* Peak stress exceeds the steel yield (~550 MPa), so reinforcement is mandatory. **[7 marks distributed as: 1+2+1+1+2]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Area Replacement Method** for the design of pressure hull penetration reinforcement. State the formula and the physical meaning of each term. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the step-by-step design procedure for a ring-reinforced circular penetration in a submarine pressure hull. Highlight the **2:1 spacing rule** and its significance. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Area Replacement Method:**
The Area Replacement Method is founded on the principle that the material **removed** by cutting the penetration must be **replaced** (in load-carrying capacity) by reinforcement material integrated over the **effective length** $L_{eff}$ over which load sheds into the opening. **[2 marks]**

The governing equation is:
$$A_{reinf} = \frac{\sigma_{hoop} \cdot t_h \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{reinf,allow}} = \frac{pR \cdot L_{eff}}{\sigma_{reinf,allow}}$$

Physical meaning of each term:
- $A_{reinf}$: cross-sectional area of the reinforcement (pad or ring) required to restore load capacity. **[1 mark]**
- $pR$: membrane hoop force per unit axial length of hull (units: N/m). **[1 mark]**
- $L_{eff}$: axial length over which the load is shed into the penetration; defined as $L_{eff} = \max(\sqrt{Dt_h},\; 0.5D)$. **[1 mark]**
- $\sigma_{reinf,allow}$: allowable stress in the reinforcement material, with factor of safety (typically 1.5 on yield). **[1 mark]**
- An additional over-strength factor of 1.1 is often applied to account for weld inefficiencies and stress risers at the weld toes. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Step-by-Step Design Procedure:**

1. Determine the design pressure $p$ from the operating depth: $p = \rho g h$. **[0.5 mark]**
2. Establish hull geometry: $R$, $t_h$, and the parent material (typically HY-80, HY-100, or titanium). **[0.5 mark]**
3. Compute intact stresses $\sigma_{hoop} = pR/t_h$ and $\sigma_{long} = pR/(2t_h)$. **[0.5 mark]**
4. Identify the penetration class (small/medium/large) and compute the peak stress $\sigma_{peak} = 5pR/(2t_h)$. **[0.5 mark]**
5. Calculate the effective reinforcement length $L_{eff} = \max(\sqrt{Dt_h},\; 0.5D)$. **[0.5 mark]**
6. Apply the Area Replacement Method to size $A_{reinf}$, then select a practical geometry (ring thickness $t_r$ and width $b$, or pad thickness and diameter). **[1 mark]**
7. **Apply the 2:1 Spacing Rule:** verify that the centre-to-centre distance between this penetration and any adjacent penetration (or ring frame) satisfies $S \ge 2(R_1 + R_2)$, where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the radii of the openings. This rule prevents **overlap of stress fields** and ensures each penetration behaves as an isolated discontinuity. **[2 marks]**
8. Verify the **Von Mises yield criterion:** compute $\sigma_{vM}$ at the most critical location (typically at the edge of the reinforcement on the parent plate side) and confirm $\sigma_{vM} \le \sigma_{yield}/1.5$. **[1 mark]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Do not* compute hoop stress as $pD/(2t)$ — this is the **longitudinal seam stress in a pipe**, not the membrane hoop stress. The correct form is $pR/t$.
> 2. *Do not* forget to apply the 1.5 factor of safety. The 2023 Dec board paper noted a 12% mark deduction for omitting F.S.
> 3. *Do not* use Kt = 3 for the biaxial hull case. The correct peak is $5pR/(2t)$, not $3pR/t$.
> 4. *Do not* compute $L_{eff}$ as $0.5D$ alone; you must take the maximum of the two forms.
> 5. Always specify whether the penetration is **internal** or **external** — the hydrostatic pressure reference changes the sign of the membrane force.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (must-memorize):** A pressure hull penetration is a discontinuity in the pressure hull that must be reinforced to restore load capacity.
- **Governing Peak Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{peak} = \mathbf{5pR/(2t)}$ at the sides of a circular hole in a biaxially-loaded cylindrical hull.
- **Kirsch Kt = 3** applies only to **uniaxial** loading; biaxial gives peak = $2\sigma_0$.
- **Area Balance Equation:** $A_{reinf} = pR \cdot L_{eff} / \sigma_{reinf,allow}$.
- **Effective Length:** $L_{eff} = \max(\sqrt{Dt_h},\; 0.5D)$.
- **2:1 Spacing Rule:** $S_{min} = 2(R_1 + R_2)$; absolute minimum.
- **Factor of Safety:** $F.S. \ge 1.5$ on yield (per SUBSAFE/KTU naval convention).
- **Von Mises Criterion:** Always use $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{0.5[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]}$ for combined stress states.
- **Penetration Classes:** Small ($D \le 0.05R$), Medium ($0.05R$–$0.20R$), Large ($D > 0.20R$).
- **Reinforcement Types:** Pad (doubler), Insert Ring (ring-stiffened), Boss (conical/cylindrical).
- **Assumption Checklist for derivations:** small hole, thin plate, membrane action, far-field uniformity, linear-elastic isotropic.
- **Numerical value to remember:** Hydrostatic pressure gradient ≈ **1 MPa per 100 m** of seawater depth.
- **High-yield constant:** $K_t$ for small circular hole in infinite plate under uniaxial tension = **3** (Kirsch, 1898).
- **Common error to avoid:** Confusing hoop stress ($pR/t$) with longitudinal seam stress ($pD/2t$).
- **Board-Examiner keyword triggers:** "area balance," "effective length," "Kirsch," "2:1 rule," "von Mises."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diving and Surfacing System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diving and Surfacing System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Diving and Surfacing System** of a submersible is a critical arrangement of ballast tanks, valves, compressed air reservoirs, and auxiliary hydrodynamic control surfaces that collectively govern the **transition of the vehicle between the surface (afloat) condition and the submerged (immersed) condition**, and vice versa. The system is governed fundamentally by **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy** combined with the controllable modification of the vehicle's **effective weight** through the admission and expulsion of seawater and high-pressure air.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the diving and surfacing system is classified as one of the three primary "vehicle-level" control systems of a manned or unmanned submersible, alongside the **trim and stability system** and the **propulsion and maneuvering system**. The proper design of this system ensures that the submersible can:
1. **Surface Safely** from any operational depth (including emergency blow).
2. **Dive and Hover** at any commanded depth.
3. **Maintain Reserve Buoyancy Margin** to compensate for floods, equipment malfunctions, and dynamic loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> According to Module 4 of the OENST723 syllabus, students are expected to understand the *principles of submerging and surfacing*, the role of *main ballast tanks (MBT)*, *trim tanks*, *variable ballast*, *diving planes (fairwaters)*, and *emergency blow systems*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a sealed plastic water bottle with a small hole near the cap. If the bottle is full of air and sealed, it floats high on the water surface because the air inside makes the *average* density of the bottle lower than water. Now, if you open the cap and let water fill in through the hole, the bottle gradually gets heavier as water replaces the air. At some point, the bottle will become **neutrally buoyant** and hover at a depth. If more water enters, it will sink (this is **diving**).

Now, to bring it back up (**surfacing**), you can either inject compressed air from a small pump. The compressed air pushes the water back out, making the bottle lighter again, and it rises. If you have a large high-pressure air tank and need to surface quickly in an emergency, you "blow" all the air at once to expel the water rapidly — this is the classic **emergency blow** used in submarines.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea:** A submersible "dives" by *deliberately making itself heavier* (negative buoyancy) and "surfaces" by *deliberately making itself lighter* (positive buoyancy) using the controlled exchange of water and air in dedicated ballast tanks.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are routinely used in diving system calculations:

- **Density of seawater (ρ_sw):** $\mathbf{1025 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ (at surface, average salinity)
- **Density of freshwater (ρ_fw):** $\mathbf{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** $\mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$
- **Specific weight of seawater (γ_sw):** $\mathbf{10050 \text{ N/m}^3}$
- **Atmospheric pressure at sea level (P_atm):** $\mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$
- **Pressure gradient with depth (seawater):** $\mathbf{\approx 10.05 \text{ kPa/m}}$ (or 1 atm per 10 m)

> [!NOTE]
> These constants are mandatory for all numerical problems that may appear in the **KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE)**. Memorize them — they appear in nearly every derivation-based question.

## 1.4 Classification of Buoyancy States (Foundational Concept)

| Buoyancy State | Condition | Net Force | Vehicle Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Buoyancy** | Buoyant Force > Weight | Upward | Rises to surface |
| **Neutral Buoyancy** | Buoyant Force = Weight | Zero | Hovers at constant depth |
| **Negative Buoyancy** | Buoyant Force < Weight | Downward | Sinks (dives) |

The diving and surfacing system is, in essence, an **engineered mechanism to switch the vehicle between these three states on demand.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of a Dive–Surface Cycle

The complete operational sequence of a submersible's diving and surfacing system can be broken down into three logically distinct phases. Each phase requires specific engineering considerations.

### Phase 1: Surface (Afloat) Condition
- The vehicle floats with a portion above the waterline.
- The **hull** is intact, dry, and pressurized at 1 atm.
- Main Ballast Tanks (MBT) are **empty** (filled with air at atmospheric pressure).
- The vehicle possesses a **Reserve Buoyancy** that keeps the hatches above water.
- Compressed air systems are charged and ready.

### Phase 2: Submerging (Diving) Sequence
- **Step 1:** Operate the **main ballast tank vents** to allow the trapped air to escape.
- **Step 2:** Open **flooding valves** at the bottom of the MBT. Seawater enters, replacing the air.
- **Step 3:** As the mass of the vehicle increases, the buoyant force is exceeded, and the vehicle **descends**.
- **Step 4:** Once the desired depth is approached, **trim tanks** are used to fine-tune the longitudinal and transverse balance.

### Phase 3: Surfacing (Emerged) Sequence
- **Normal Surfacing:** Compressed air at moderate pressure (typically 1.5–2.5 times the ambient pressure) is admitted into the MBT, displacing the seawater through the lower flooding valves (now acting as "blow valves").
- **Emergency Blow (Emergency Surfacing):** High-pressure air (typically stored at 20–30 MPa) is released rapidly into all MBTs simultaneously to expel water in seconds, ensuring crew safety in case of casualty.

## 2.2 Reserve Buoyancy — The Engineering Margin

**Reserve Buoyancy (RB)** is defined as the difference between the **buoyant force when the vehicle is fully submerged** and the **actual weight of the vehicle** (excluding the variable ballast). It is the *engineering safety margin* that guarantees the vehicle can return to the surface even if some compartments flood.

Mathematically, Reserve Buoyancy is expressed as:

$$
RB = W_{fl} - W
$$

where $W_{fl}$ is the buoyant force when the vehicle is fully submerged (the **full-load displacement** weight equivalent of the displaced water), and $W$ is the lightweight (empty) weight of the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** A well-designed submersible should have a reserve buoyancy of approximately **5% to 10%** of its full-load displacement. Submarines operating purely on positive buoyancy for surface transit may have a much higher reserve buoyancy (~25–30%).

## 2.3 The Role of Diving Planes (Fairwaters)

**Diving planes** (also called **fairwaters**) are small, controllable, wing-like surfaces mounted on the **conning tower** or **sail** of a submarine. They serve a critical function during the *transit through the periscope depth range* and *shallow water operations*:

- When the submarine is on the surface, the planes are flush with the sail.
- As the submarine submerges to periscope depth, the **fairwater planes** deploy.
- The planes generate **lift or downforce** based on the submarine's forward speed and the angle of the planes.
- This hydrodynamic force assists in **rapid depth changes** without requiring excessive ballast operations.

The lift force generated by the diving planes can be approximated using the standard aerodynamic/hydrodynamic lift equation:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_{sw} \, V^2 \, S \, C_L
$$

where:
- $L$ = Lift force (N)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = Density of seawater (~1025 kg/m³)
- $V$ = Forward speed of the submarine (m/s)
- $S$ = Planform area of the diving planes (m²)
- $C_L$ = Coefficient of lift (dimensionless, typically 0.4–0.8 for operation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Diving planes are an elegant example of *hydrodynamic control* in the absence of pure buoyant control. They allow a submarine to "fly" underwater using its forward motion — a key insight students must articulate in design-based questions.

## 2.4 Types of Ballast Systems in Submersibles

The diving and surfacing system is implemented through several distinct types of ballast tanks, each with a specific operational role:

| Tank Type | Function | Volume Fraction | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main Ballast Tank (MBT)** | Provides primary variable buoyancy for diving/surfacing | Largest (~70% of total) | External (saddle tanks) or Internal (free-flooding) |
| **Trim Tank** | Adjusts longitudinal and transverse attitude | Small (~10–15%) | Forward and aft, port and starboard |
| **Variable Ballast Tank** | Compensates for weight changes (payload, crew, water in/out) | Small (~5–10%) | Internal, adjustable pumps |
| **Safety/Buoyancy Tank** | Provides emergency positive buoyancy if flooded | Small (~5%) | Top of pressure hull, droppable |
| **Compensating Tank** | Compensates for changes in seawater density with depth | Tiny | Bilge area |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes every formula that is likely to be tested in the KTU ESE. Commit these to memory and practice their application.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g$ | Buoyant force on a fully submerged body | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of the submersible | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | $F_{net} = F_b - W$ | Net vertical force (positive = rise) | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | $RB = W_{fl} - W_{lh}$ | Reserve buoyancy (full load minus lightweight) | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | $m_{ballast} = (F_b - W)/\ g$ | Mass of ballast water required for submergence | kg |
| 6 | $P(z) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $z$ | Pascals (Pa) |
| 7 | $P_{blow} \geq P_{amb}(z) + \Delta P$ | Required blow pressure at depth $z$ | Pascals (Pa) |
| 8 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} V^2 S C_L$ | Lift force from diving planes | Newtons (N) |
| 9 | $C_B = \dfrac{W_{lh}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g}$ | Block coefficient (hull form fullness) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_{blow} = \dfrac{V_{MBT}}{Q_{air} \cdot \eta_v}$ | Time to empty a tank using air blow | Seconds (s) |
| 11 | $\dot{m}_{flood} = C_d A_{flood} \sqrt{2 \rho_{sw} \Delta P}$ | Mass flow rate of flooding seawater | kg/s |
| 12 | $a_{sub} = \dfrac{F_b - W}{M_{sub}}$ | Submergence acceleration | m/s² |

> **Notation Used:**
> - $\rho_{sw}$: density of seawater (kg/m³)
> - $V$: displaced volume (m³)
> - $g$: gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
> - $W_{fl}$: buoyant weight at full submergence (N)
> - $W_{lh}$: lightweight (empty) hull weight (N)
> - $z$: depth below sea surface (m)
> - $C_d$: discharge coefficient for flooding valve (typically 0.6–0.8)
> - $A_{flood}$: cross-sectional area of flooding valve (m²)
> - $Q_{air}$: volumetric air flow rate (m³/s)
> - $\eta_v$: volumetric efficiency of blow (typically 0.7–0.9)
> - $M_{sub}$: total mass of submerged vehicle (kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:** When writing these equations in your KTU answer sheets, always use the notation **$F_b$, $W$, $\rho_{sw}$** consistently. Many students lose marks by mixing up subscripts. Examiners are strict about this.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The diving and surfacing system is fundamental in the following practical domains:

1. **Defense Submarines:** Military submarines (e.g., Scorpène, Type 214) use a combination of **MBT flooding** and **fairwater planes** for rapid depth changes during tactical maneuvers.
2. **Oceanographic Research Submersibles:** Vehicles like *Alvin* (DSV Alvin) use **variable ballast** and **syntactic foam** for fine depth control.
3. **DSV/ROV Operations:** Deep-diving vehicles (e.g., *Limiting Factor*, *Shinkai 6500*) require **precision depth control** for sample collection, relying on adjustable ballast systems.
4. **Submarine Rescue:** The **emergency blow system** is a life-critical safety feature — the 2018 *Argentinian Submarine San Juan* incident highlighted the consequences of system failure.
5. **Civilian AUV/HOV Design:** Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) use miniaturized ballast systems, often employing oil–water displacement for depth control.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Ballast Water Required for Submergence

**Problem Setup:** A small submersible has a lightweight (empty) mass of $M_{lh} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg}$ and a fully submerged displaced volume of $V = 18 \text{ m}^3$. The density of seawater is $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine the mass of ballast water that must be taken in for the vehicle to be **neutrally buoyant** when fully submerged.

### Step 1: Identify the Buoyant Force When Fully Submerged

When the vehicle is fully submerged, the displaced volume equals the *total external volume* of the hull, $V = 18 \text{ m}^3$. By Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force is:

$$
F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g
$$

Substituting values:

$$
F_b = 1025 \cdot 18 \cdot 9.81 \text{ N}
$$

Let us compute this product step by step:

$$
1025 \cdot 18 = 18{,}450
$$

$$
18{,}450 \cdot 9.81 = ?
$$

Performing the multiplication explicitly:

$$
18{,}450 \cdot 9 = 166{,}050
$$

$$
18{,}450 \cdot 0.81 = 14{,}944.5
$$

$$
\therefore F_b = 166{,}050 + 14{,}944.5 = 180{,}994.5 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Lightweight Weight

The lightweight weight (weight of the empty vehicle) is:

$$
W_{lh} = M_{lh} \cdot g = 12{,}000 \cdot 9.81 = 117{,}720 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Net Upward Buoyancy

Even when the vehicle is "empty" (no ballast), the displaced seawater exerts an upward force greater than the lightweight weight (since the vehicle is designed to float). The net upward force in this state is:

$$
F_{b, net} = F_b - W_{lh} = 180{,}994.5 - 117{,}720 = 63{,}274.5 \text{ N}
$$

This corresponds to the **reserve buoyancy** of the vehicle.

### Step 4: Compute the Required Ballast Weight to Achieve Neutral Buoyancy

For the vehicle to be neutrally buoyant when submerged, the additional weight (ballast water) must exactly counterbalance the reserve buoyancy. The required ballast weight is:

$$
W_{ballast} = F_{b,net} = 63{,}274.5 \text{ N}
$$

Converting this to mass:

$$
M_{ballast} = \dfrac{W_{ballast}}{g} = \dfrac{63{,}274.5}{9.81} \text{ kg}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\dfrac{63{,}274.5}{9.81} \approx 6{,}449.7 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$
\boxed{M_{ballast} \approx 6{,}450 \text{ kg}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The submersible must take on approximately **6,450 kg** of seawater into its ballast tanks to become neutrally buoyant at full submergence. Additional ballast (≈ 500–1,000 kg) is typically added to ensure a small negative buoyancy for *controlled descent*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compressed Air Pressure Required for Emergency Blow at Depth

**Problem Setup:** A submarine is at a depth of $z = 200 \text{ m}$ below the sea surface. The total volume of its main ballast tanks is $V_{MBT} = 600 \text{ m}^3$. The submarine must surface in an emergency. Determine the **minimum compressed air pressure** required in the high-pressure air banks to successfully blow all the water out of the MBTs.

### Step 1: Compute the Ambient (Hydrostatic) Pressure at 200 m Depth

The hydrostatic pressure at depth $z$ is:

$$
P_{amb}(z) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
P_{amb}(200) = 101{,}325 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 200 \text{ Pa}
$$

Let us compute the hydrostatic term:

$$
1025 \cdot 9.81 = 10{,}055.25 \text{ Pa/m}
$$

$$
10{,}055.25 \cdot 200 = 2{,}011{,}050 \text{ Pa}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{amb}(200) = 101{,}325 + 2{,}011{,}050 = 2{,}112{,}375 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.112 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Practical Rule of Thumb

In real engineering practice, the high-pressure air bank must be at a pressure **at least 1.5 to 2.5 times** the ambient pressure to ensure rapid and complete tank emptying. The "1 atm over ambient" minimum is theoretical; in practice, a higher margin is required because of:
- Pressure losses in the air lines and valves.
- The need to overcome frictional losses.
- The dynamic head of the rising water column.
- The volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v < 1$).

Using a conservative safety factor of $\mathbf{1.5}$:

$$
P_{blow,min} = 1.5 \cdot P_{amb}(200) = 1.5 \cdot 2{,}112{,}375 = 3{,}168{,}562.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{blow,min} \approx 3.17 \text{ MPa}}
$$

### Step 3: Practical Engineering Recommendation

Submarines typically store emergency blow air at pressures of **20–30 MPa** in carbon-fiber-wound composite bottles, providing a massive safety margin and ensuring that even at the deepest operational depth (e.g., 500 m), the blow can be executed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When solving these problems, *show both* the theoretical minimum pressure and the practical (1.5x–2.5x safety factor) pressure. Examiners reward students who demonstrate engineering judgment, not just formula plugging.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Required for Emergency Blow

**Problem Setup:** Given the submarine from Section 3.2 (with $V_{MBT} = 600 \text{ m}^3$ at depth 200 m), and a high-pressure air system that delivers a volumetric flow rate of $Q_{air} = 12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at the tank conditions, with a volumetric efficiency of $\eta_v = 0.85$, compute the **time to empty the MBTs**.

### Step 1: Apply the Blow Time Equation

The time required to empty the MBT using compressed air is:

$$
t_{blow} = \dfrac{V_{MBT}}{Q_{air} \cdot \eta_v}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
t_{blow} = \dfrac{600}{12 \cdot 0.85} = \dfrac{600}{10.2} \approx 58.82 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3: Final Result

$$
\boxed{t_{blow} \approx 58.8 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** In a real emergency, this is the time the crew has to wait before the vehicle becomes positively buoyant and begins to rise. Note that the actual surfacing time is *much longer* because the vehicle must overcome inertia, hydrodynamic drag, and hydrostatic pressure differences as it ascends.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lift Force from Diving Planes

**Problem Setup:** A submarine is moving at a forward speed of $V = 8 \text{ m/s}$ (about 15.5 knots) with its diving planes deployed at a 10° angle, giving a coefficient of lift $C_L = 0.6$. The total planform area of the planes is $S = 4.5 \text{ m}^2$. Compute the lift force.

### Step 1: Apply the Hydrodynamic Lift Equation

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} V^2 S C_L
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot (8)^2 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.6
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
8^2 = 64
$$

$$
1025 \cdot 64 = 65{,}600
$$

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 65{,}600 = 32{,}800
$$

$$
4.5 \cdot 0.6 = 2.7
$$

$$
L = 32{,}800 \cdot 2.7 = 88{,}560 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3: Final Result

$$
\boxed{L \approx 88.56 \text{ kN}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A lift of nearly 9 metric tons from the diving planes alone allows a submarine to climb or dive rapidly *without* changing its ballast — a critical tactical capability.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Diving Methods

| Diving Method | Mechanism | Time to Operate | Energy Required | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Main Ballast Flooding** | Open vents + flooding valves | 2–5 minutes | None (gravity) | Normal dive from surface |
| **Diving Planes (Fairwater)** | Hydrodynamic lift from forward motion | Seconds (transient) | Propulsion power | Shallow depth changes, periscope depth |
| **Trim Tank Transfer** | Pump water between tanks | 30–60 seconds | Pump power | Fine depth control and attitude |
| **Emergency Blow** | High-pressure air expulsion | <60 seconds (tank empty) | Stored air | Surfacing only |
| **Variable Ballast Pumps** | Pump water in/out of internal tanks | Continuous | Pump power | Adjustable buoyancy for hovering |

## 4.2 Operational State Machine: Dive–Surface Cycle

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **state transitions** of a submersible as it progresses through a complete dive–surface cycle. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU conceptual questions on system sequencing.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    PrepForDive --> FloodingMBT: Open Flood Valves
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU:**
> - The **Top Buoyancy Tank** is a droppable safety tank that provides positive buoyancy even if the pressure hull is breached.
> - The **MBT (Port & Starboard)** are the *primary variable ballast tanks* — they handle the bulk of the diving and surfacing.
> - The **Flooding Ports** are at the bottom for ingress of seawater and serve as blow ports during surfacing.
> - The **Trim Tanks (Forward & Aft)** adjust the longitudinal pitch.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, 2 Marks]

> **Q: Define the term "Reserve Buoyancy" of a submersible and state its typical magnitude as a percentage of full-load displacement.**

**Model Answer:**

Reserve buoyancy is defined as the difference between the buoyant force experienced by the vehicle when it is **fully submerged** and the **lightweight (empty) weight** of the vehicle. Mathematically:

$$
RB = W_{fl} - W_{lh} = (\rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g) - (M_{lh} \cdot g)
$$

For a typical military submarine, the reserve buoyancy is approximately **25%–30%** of the full-load displacement, while for a deep-diving research submersible it is usually only **5%–10%** because these vehicles are designed to operate predominantly in the submerged state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Tip (2 marks):** 1 mark for the correct definition, 1 mark for the typical percentage range. Students often lose the second mark by being vague ("around 10–20%") — be specific.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, 2 Marks]

> **Q: Differentiate between a Main Ballast Tank (MBT) and a Trim Tank in a submersible.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Main Ballast Tank (MBT) | Trim Tank |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Provides primary variable buoyancy for dive and surface | Adjusts longitudinal and transverse attitude |
| **Volume** | Large (70% of total ballast) | Small (10–15% of total ballast) |
| **Operation** | Gravity flooding / high-pressure air blow | Pump-driven water transfer |
| **Location** | External (saddle) or internal (free-flooding) | Forward/aft, port/starboard pairs |
| **Time constant** | Minutes | Seconds |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, 14 Marks]

> **Q (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a typical diving and surfacing system of a submersible. Discuss the role of main ballast tanks, trim tanks, and emergency blow system. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **Q (b)** A submersible has a fully submerged displacement volume of $V = 25 \text{ m}^3$ and a lightweight mass of $M_{lh} = 18{,}000 \text{ kg}$. The density of seawater is $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine: (i) the reserve buoyancy, (ii) the mass of ballast water required to make the vehicle neutrally buoyant at full submergence, and (iii) the additional ballast mass required to give the vehicle a submergence acceleration of $a = 0.05 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks

The diving and surfacing system operates on the principle of **controlled alteration of the vehicle's effective weight** to switch its buoyancy state between positive, neutral, and negative. The three primary components are:

**1. Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs) — 2 Marks**

- The MBTs are the *largest* variable-volume tanks in the vehicle.
- On the surface, the MBTs are *empty* (filled with air at atmospheric pressure), and the vehicle floats with positive reserve buoyancy.
- To **dive**, the MBT vents at the top are opened, allowing the trapped air to escape, and the flooding valves at the bottom are opened, allowing seawater to enter. As water replaces air, the vehicle's mass increases, the buoyant force is exceeded, and the vehicle begins to sink.

**2. Trim Tanks — 2 Marks**

- Trim tanks are *small* tanks located at the bow, stern, port, and starboard of the pressure hull.
- They are filled or emptied using pumps, not by gravity.
- They allow fine adjustment of the vehicle's *longitudinal pitch* (fore-aft) and *transverse roll* (port-starboard), ensuring the vehicle is level at depth.

**3. Emergency Blow System — 2 Marks**

- The emergency blow system consists of *high-pressure air reservoirs* (charged to 20–30 MPa) connected to the MBTs via large-diameter quick-opening valves.
- In an emergency, the operator (or an automatic system) opens these valves, dumping massive quantities of air into the MBTs.
- The air displaces the seawater out through the bottom flooding ports, rapidly restoring positive buoyancy and causing the vehicle to surface.

**4. Sketch — 1 Mark** (Use the diagrams from Section 4.4 of these notes as a reference.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $V = 25 \text{ m}^3$
- $M_{lh} = 18{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $a = 0.05 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Buoyant Force at Full Submergence (1 Mark)**

$$
F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = 1025 \cdot 25 \cdot 9.81
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
1025 \cdot 25 = 25{,}625
$$

$$
25{,}625 \cdot 9.81 = 251{,}381.25 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_b \approx 251.38 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 2: Lightweight Weight (1 Mark)**

$$
W_{lh} = M_{lh} \cdot g = 18{,}000 \cdot 9.81 = 176{,}580 \text{ N} = 176.58 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Reserve Buoyancy (1 Mark)**

$$
RB = F_b - W_{lh} = 251{,}381.25 - 176{,}580 = 74{,}801.25 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{RB \approx 74.80 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4: Mass of Ballast for Neutral Buoyancy (2 Marks)**

For neutral buoyancy: $F_b = W_{lh} + M_{ballast} \cdot g$

$$
M_{ballast} = \dfrac{F_b - W_{lh}}{g} = \dfrac{74{,}801.25}{9.81} = 7{,}625.0 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{ballast} \approx 7{,}625 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 5: Additional Ballast for Submergence Acceleration (2 Marks)**

For the vehicle to descend with acceleration $a$, the net downward force must be:

$$
F_{net} = (M_{lh} + M_{ballast} + M_{add}) \cdot a
$$

We require: $F_b - (M_{lh} + M_{ballast} + M_{add}) \cdot g = -(M_{lh} + M_{ballast} + M_{add}) \cdot a$

Or equivalently, the net downward force in excess of gravity:

$$
F_{net,down} = (M_{lh} + M_{ballast} + M_{add}) \cdot a
$$

Let $M_{total} = M_{lh} + M_{ballast} + M_{add}$. Then:

$$
F_b = M_{total} \cdot (g + a)
$$

$$
M_{total} = \dfrac{F_b}{g + a} = \dfrac{251{,}381.25}{9.81 + 0.05} = \dfrac{251{,}381.25}{9.86}
$$

$$
M_{total} \approx 25{,}495.05 \text{ kg}
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_{add} = M_{total} - M_{lh} - M_{ballast} = 25{,}495.05 - 18{,}000 - 7{,}625 = -129.95 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The negative value of $M_{add}$ means that the mass of ballast water needed to give the vehicle an acceleration of 0.05 m/s² downward is *less* than that required for neutral buoyancy. In other words, the $7{,}625 \text{ kg}$ we computed for neutral buoyancy already provides slightly more than 0.05 m/s² of submergence acceleration. The student should report that the neutral-ballast condition already corresponds to a small descent acceleration. If the question demands an *increase* in ballast beyond neutral buoyancy, then:

For *additional* acceleration beyond neutral buoyancy, we need to add enough mass to increase the net downward force. If $M_{add}$ must be positive, the problem may have intended a different interpretation. We can also present the alternative:

For a small additional acceleration $\delta a$ from the neutral state, the additional mass is approximately:

$$
\delta M = \dfrac{M_{total} \cdot \delta a}{g + a_{existing}}
$$

where $a_{existing}$ is the acceleration at neutral buoyancy (which is zero in the ideal case). So practically, $M_{add} \approx 0$ for this small acceleration — the design margin in the ballast system already covers it.

For a *practical engineering* answer: report that **no additional ballast is required** for such a small acceleration, as the system design margin accommodates it. If the question demands a *theoretical* answer assuming a rigid neutral-buoyancy starting point:

$$
M_{add} = \dfrac{M_{total,neutral} \cdot a}{g} = \dfrac{25{,}625 \cdot 0.05}{9.81} = \dfrac{1{,}281.25}{9.81} \approx 130.6 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{add} \approx 130 \text{ kg} \text{ (to give 0.05 m/s}^2 \text{ downward acceleration)}}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, 14 Marks]

> **Q (a)** Explain the concept of diving planes (fairwater planes) used in submarines. Derive the expression for the hydrodynamic lift force generated by them, and explain their operational role during the periscope-depth transition. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **Q (b)** A submarine is at a depth of $z = 250 \text{ m}$ with an MBT volume of $V_{MBT} = 450 \text{ m}^3$. The high-pressure air system can deliver $Q_{air} = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ at tank conditions with a volumetric efficiency of $\eta_v = 0.80$. The density of seawater is $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Determine: (i) the ambient pressure at the MBT depth, (ii) the minimum theoretical air pressure required to initiate blowing, and (iii) the time required to empty the MBTs. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**

Diving planes, also called **fairwater planes**, are small, controllable, wing-like hydrodynamic surfaces mounted on the **conning tower (sail)** of a submarine. They are typically arranged in *X-configuration* (four fins) or *cruciform* configuration.

The key operational principle is that when the submarine is moving forward at speed, the diving planes generate a **lift force** (upward or downward) based on the *angle of attack* of the planes. This lift force acts on the sail, which is near the *center of pressure* of the vehicle, and translates into a **pitching moment** that causes the submarine to change depth.

**Why They Are Used (2 Marks):**

- During the *periscope-depth transition* (e.g., between 30 m and 12 m depth), it is tactically undesirable to use ballast operations because they are noisy and slow.
- Diving planes allow the submarine to change depth *silently* and *rapidly* using only its forward propulsion.
- The planes are deployed (extended) as the submarine submerges past the periscope depth, and retracted (flush with the sail) when the submarine is fully submerged to reduce drag.

**Derivation of Lift Force (2 Marks):**

The lift force is given by the standard hydrodynamic equation:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} V^2 S C_L
$$

where $\rho_{sw}$ is the density of seawater, $V$ is the forward speed, $S$ is the planform area of the planes, and $C_L$ is the coefficient of lift, which is a function of the angle of attack $\alpha$ and the plane geometry. For small angles, $C_L \approx 2\pi \alpha$ (in radians), but in practice, $C_L$ ranges from 0.4 to 0.8.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $z = 250 \text{ m}$
- $V_{MBT} = 450 \text{ m}^3$
- $Q_{air} = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\eta_v = 0.80$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $P_{atm} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Ambient Pressure at 250 m Depth (2 Marks)**

$$
P_{amb}(250) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z
$$

$$
P_{amb}(250) = 101{,}325 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 250
$$

Compute the hydrostatic term:

$$
1025 \cdot 9.81 = 10{,}055.25
$$

$$
10{,}055.25 \cdot 250 = 2{,}513{,}812.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_{amb}(250) = 101{,}325 + 2{,}513{,}812.5 = 2{,}615{,}137.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{amb}(250) \approx 2.615 \text{ MPa} \quad (\approx 25.8 \text{ atm})}
$$

**Step 2: Minimum Theoretical Air Pressure for Blow (2 Marks)**

The minimum theoretical air pressure to push water out of the MBT must be *at least equal to* the ambient pressure inside the tank (which equals the ambient hydrostatic pressure, since the tank is open to the sea at the bottom):

$$
P_{blow,min,theoretical} = P_{amb}(250) = 2.615 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{blow,min,theoretical} \approx 2.615 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Practical Minimum Air Pressure (Engineering Recommendation) (1 Mark)**

Applying a 1.5× safety factor (to overcome line losses, friction, and dynamic head):

$$
P_{blow,min,practical} = 1.5 \cdot 2.615 \approx 3.923 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Time to Empty the MBTs (2 Marks)**

$$
t_{blow} = \dfrac{V_{MBT}}{Q_{air} \cdot \eta_v} = \dfrac{450}{10 \cdot 0.80} = \dfrac{450}{8} = 56.25 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{blow} \approx 56.25 \text{ seconds}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to students:** In the answer sheet, present *both* the theoretical minimum pressure AND the practical (1.5×) pressure. Examiners specifically look for the practical engineering judgment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $P_{atm}$ in the hydrostatic pressure formula.** Many students write $P(z) = \rho g z$, which is wrong. The correct formula is $P(z) = P_{atm} + \rho g z$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this oversight.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Reserve Buoyancy" with "Total Buoyancy".** Reserve buoyancy is the *excess* buoyancy over the lightweight weight, not the buoyant force itself. State the formula explicitly: $RB = F_b - W_{lh}$.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in the final answer.** Always express mass in kg, force in N (or kN), and pressure in Pa (or MPa). Mixing units (e.g., kg-force with N) is a guaranteed mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Not stating the safety factor for blow pressure.** The theoretical minimum pressure is rarely the *practical* answer. Always mention the 1.5×–2.5× safety factor.
>
> 5. **Confusing MBT with Trim Tanks in sketches.** In diagrams, clearly label the MBT (largest), trim tanks (small, paired), and variable ballast (internal, smallest). Examiners check for proper labeling.
>
> 6. **Omitting the sketch in 7-mark questions.** Even a basic block diagram earns you 1 mark. Never skip a diagram.
>
> 7. **Mixing up $V$ (displaced volume) with $V_{MBT}$ (tank volume).** These are not the same. $V$ is the *external* volume of the hull, $V_{MBT}$ is the *internal* volume of the tank.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Diving and Surfacing System: High-Density Revision Summary**

- **Core Principle:** A submersible dives by *gaining weight* (flooding ballast) and surfaces by *losing weight* (blowing ballast with compressed air). The governing law is **Archimedes' Principle**.

- **Three Buoyancy States:**
  1. **Positive Buoyancy:** $F_b > W$ → Rises.
  2. **Neutral Buoyancy:** $F_b = W$ → Hovers.
  3. **Negative Buoyancy:** $F_b < W$ → Sinks.

- **Reserve Buoyancy (RB):** $RB = F_b - W_{lh}$. Typical: 5%–10% (research submersibles) and 25%–30% (military submarines).

- **Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs):** Largest variable-volume tanks; flood by gravity (open vents + flooding valves); emptied by compressed air blow.

- **Trim Tanks:** Small, pump-driven tanks for fine attitude control (longitudinal pitch and transverse roll).

- **Variable Ballast Tanks:** Internal tanks for fine depth control and weight compensation (e.g., for payload changes).

- **Diving Planes (Fairwaters):** Hydrodynamic control surfaces on the sail; generate lift $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$ to assist in depth changes during the periscope-depth transition.

- **Emergency Blow System:** High-pressure air reservoirs (20–30 MPa) dump air rapidly into MBTs to surface the vehicle in an emergency. Blow time: $t_{blow} = V_{MBT} / (Q_{air} \cdot \eta_v)$.

- **Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth:** $P(z) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z$. Pressure increases by ~10 kPa per meter of seawater depth (≈ 1 atm per 10 m).

- **Submergence Acceleration:** $a_{sub} = (F_b - W_{total}) / M_{total}$. The vehicle must have *excess* weight (negative buoyancy) to descend.

- **Block Coefficient:** $C_B = W_{lh} / (\rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g)$. Indicates hull form fullness (typically 0.4–0.6 for streamlined submersibles).

- **Top-of-Hull Safety Tanks:** Droppable buoyancy tanks that provide positive buoyancy in catastrophic flooding scenarios.

- **Free-Flooding vs. Variable Ballast:** Free-flooding tanks exchange water passively (MBT); variable ballast uses active pumping for fine control.

- **Key Engineering Constants to Memorize:**
  - $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
  - $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
  - Pressure gradient ≈ 10.05 kPa/m (seawater)

- **Critical Engineering Trade-offs:**
  - Higher reserve buoyancy = safer (can surface easily) but limits payload and depth.
  - Lower reserve buoyancy = greater payload and depth capability but riskier in emergencies.
  - Emergency blow pressure must be stored at 5–10× the operational depth pressure for safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Hydraulic System** in a submersible is a power transmission and control mechanism that uses a pressurised incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil, synthetic hydrocarbon, or water-glycol mixtures) to generate, transmit, and control linear or rotary mechanical energy for actuating sub-sea machinery such as ballast valves, manipulator arms, hatch covers, hydroplanes, and torpedo tube mechanisms. The system operates strictly on **Pascal's Law** of pressure transmission, which states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OENST723 - Module 4):**
> Hydraulic systems form the *primary actuation backbone* of every crewed and uncrewed submersible (HOV, ROV, AUV, DSV). Unlike electric or pneumatic drives, hydraulics deliver **very high power density** per unit volume — a critical advantage when space, weight, and payload are strictly limited inside a pressure hull.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **human muscular and circulatory system** as an analogy:
- The **heart** = hydraulic pump (generates pressure)
- **Arteries** = high-pressure hydraulic lines
- **Muscles** = hydraulic actuators (convert fluid energy to motion)
- **Veins** = return lines to reservoir
- **Brain signals** = solenoid-operated control valves
- **Blood** = hydraulic fluid

Just as your body moves your hand by sending pressurised blood into muscle fibres, a submersible moves its manipulator gripper by sending pressurised oil into a hydraulic cylinder. The deeper the submersible dives, the more the surrounding seawater pushes back — and the hydraulic system must overcome this **ambient hydrostatic pressure** to do any work.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following baseline constants and metrics govern all submersible hydraulic design:

- **Standard working pressure:** **210 bar (21 MPa)** for main systems; up to **350 bar (35 MPa)** for compact actuators.
- **Hydraulic fluid density:** **~870 kg/m³** for mineral oil; **~1000 kg/m³** for water-based fluids.
- **Bulk modulus of mineral oil:** **~1.4 to 1.7 GPa** (governs system stiffness).
- **Ambient seawater pressure gain:** **+1 bar for every +10.2 m** of depth (≈ **+0.0981 bar/m**).
- **Power transmission efficiency:** typically **75 % – 90 %** for well-designed circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why incompressibility matters:** Liquids change volume by less than **0.5 %** even at 200 bar, making hydraulics *stiff* (precise positioning) compared to pneumatics, which is *compliant* (cushioning).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Pascal's Law

Pascal's Law states:

> *"A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel."*

This is the *philosophical foundation* of every hydraulic circuit. It means a small force on a small piston can produce a large force on a large piston, with the *pressure* (not the force) being the constant quantity.

## 2.2 Major Sub-Systems of a Submersible Hydraulic Plant

A typical deep-rated submersible hydraulic system is organised into the following functional sub-systems:

1. **Power Generation Sub-System**
