   - Prime mover (electric motor or DC motor coupled to the pump)
   - Pump (axial piston, radial piston, gear, vane)
   - Pressure relief valve

2. **Energy Storage Sub-System**
   - Hydraulic accumulator (gas-charged bladder or piston type)
   - Reservoir (with level gauge, breather, temperature sensor)

3. **Fluid Conditioning Sub-System**
   - Suction strainer
   - High-pressure filter (typically 3 µm to 10 µm absolute)
   - Return line filter
   - Heat exchanger (oil-to-seawater)

4. **Distribution & Control Sub-System**
   - Directional control valves (DCV) — 4/3, 4/2 spool types
   - Pressure-reducing valves
   - Flow-control valves (for actuator speed)
   - Check valves
   - Pilot-operated logic

5. **Actuation Sub-System**
   - Linear actuators (single-acting, double-acting cylinders)
   - Rotary actuators (helical piston, vane, rack-and-pinion motors)
   - Hydraulic motors (for propulsion in some AUVs/ROVs)

6. **Depth Compensation Sub-System**
   - Internal vs external compensation
   - Pressure-compensated housings for valves and pumps
   - Compensation bladders / pistons exposed to ambient seawater

## 2.3 Why Hydraulics? — Engineering Justification for Submersibles

- **Power density** is roughly **5× to 10×** that of an equivalent electric actuator.
- **Self-cooling** is possible because oil carries heat away from the actuator to the reservoir.
- **Stall-safe operation:** A stalled hydraulic motor does not burn out (unlike an electric motor).
- **Overload protection** is intrinsic via the pressure relief valve.
- **Seawater incompatibility isolation:** The hydraulic fluid is fully sealed inside the hull; only the actuator rod may penetrate via a sealing gland.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Hydraulic Pressure | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Force ÷ Piston area |
| 2 | Hydraulic Force (cylinder) | $F$ | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | Cylinder output force |
| 3 | Cylinder Bore Area | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$ | m² | $D$ = bore diameter |
| 4 | Annular (rod-side) Area | $A_a$ | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$ | m² | $d$ = rod diameter |
| 5 | Flow rate relationship | $Q$ | $Q = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | $v$ = piston velocity |
| 6 | Pump displacement volume | $V_d$ | $Q = V_d \cdot N$ | m³/s | $N$ = pump speed (rev/s) |
| 7 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h$ | $P_h = P \cdot Q$ | W | Pressure × Flow |
| 8 | Cylinder velocity | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Flow ÷ Area |
| 9 | Cylinder extension time | $t$ | $t = \dfrac{L \cdot A}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length |
| 10 | Force amplification (lever) | $F_2$ | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | N | Pascal's law applied |
| 11 | Hydrostatic (seawater) pressure | $P_{\text{amb}}$ | $P_{\text{amb}} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³ |
| 12 | Net cylinder force (depth) | $F_{\text{net}}$ | $F_{\text{net}} = P \cdot A - P_{\text{amb}} \cdot A$ | N | Differential against ambient |
| 13 | Pressure gain per metre depth | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g$ | Pa/m | $\approx 10.05$ kPa/m |
| 14 | Pump torque | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$ | N·m | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency |
| 15 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | volumetric × mechanical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When solving a cylinder-force problem at depth, ALWAYS subtract the ambient hydrostatic pressure acting on the rod-side area. Examiners explicitly test whether students remember that a cylinder operating at **3000 m** has **~300 bar** of seawater pushing against it.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Submersible hydraulic systems are used in:

- **Ballast tank blow-down valves** (DSV *Alvin*, *Shinkai 6500*, *Jiaolong*).
- **Robotic manipulator arms** (Titan 4, Atlas, Kraft manipulators on ROVs).
- **Hydroplane actuation** for depth and pitch control in cruise-mode submersibles.
- **Emergency jettison** of ballast weights via spring-and-hydraulic release.
- **Hatch and lock mechanism actuation** on pressure hulls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Produced by a Double-Acting Cylinder

Consider a double-acting cylinder with bore diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d$. Pressure $P$ is applied on the cap-end (full bore) during extension.

**Step 1 — Identify the effective pressure-bearing area on the cap (piston) side:**

The cap-end area is the full circular cross-section of the piston:

$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cap-end force using Pascal's law:**

The pressure acts uniformly on $A_{\text{cap}}$:

$$F_{\text{cap}} = P \cdot A_{\text{cap}} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$

**Step 3 — For retraction, the rod side is pressurised. The effective area is the annular region:**

$$A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2})$$

**Step 4 — Rod-side retracting force:**

$$F_{\text{rod}} = P \cdot A_{\text{rod}} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2})$$

**Step 5 — Force ratio (asymmetry between push and pull):**

$$\frac{F_{\text{cap}}}{F_{\text{rod}}} = \frac{D^{2}}{D^{2} - d^{2}}$$

This ratio is always **> 1**, meaning a cylinder always pushes harder than it pulls at the same supply pressure — a critical fact for designing manipulator gripping forces.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Force on a Submerged Cylinder

A double-acting cylinder is mounted externally on a manipulator arm. When the cylinder is submerged at depth $h$, seawater at pressure $P_{\text{amb}}$ acts on the *entire outer skin*, including the exposed rod.

For **extension**, the cylinder is pressurised on the cap side. The net force is:

$$F_{\text{net,ext}} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \; - \; P_{\text{amb}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2})$$

The seawater does *not* act on the cap side (it is sealed inside the cylinder body), so the rod-side annular area is the projected area exposed to ambient.

For **retraction**, the cylinder is pressurised on the rod side, and seawater acts on the cap-side area:

$$F_{\text{net,ret}} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2}) \; - \; P_{\text{amb}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalised principle:** Whatever side of the piston is *not* pressurised has ambient seawater acting on the equivalent area. This is why *pressure-compensated* cylinders (with internal oil at ambient-matched pressure) are often used for very deep operation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cylinder Extension Time

If flow rate $Q$ (constant) is delivered to a cylinder of bore area $A_{\text{cap}}$ and stroke length $L$:

**Step 1 — Volume of oil needed to fully extend the cylinder:**

$$V_{\text{oil}} = A_{\text{cap}} \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Time to deliver this volume at flow rate $Q$:**

$$t = \frac{V_{\text{oil}}}{Q} = \frac{A_{\text{cap}} \cdot L}{Q}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting bore area:**

$$t = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4 Q}$$

**Step 4 — Piston velocity (steady state):**

$$v = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{cap}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydraulic Power and Pump Sizing

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power delivered to the load:**

$$P_h = P \cdot Q$$

where $P$ is in Pa and $Q$ in m³/s.

**Step 2 — Convert to practical engineering units (bar and L/min):**

$$P_h \;[\text{kW}] = \frac{P \;[\text{bar}] \times Q \;[\text{L/min}]}{600}$$

because $1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5}$ Pa and $1$ L/min $= 1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s, the product $\frac{10^{5} \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{1000} = \frac{1}{600}$.

**Step 3 — Required pump displacement for motor speed $N$ (rev/min):**

$$V_d = \frac{Q}{N} = \frac{1000 \cdot Q_{\text{L/min}}}{N_{\text{rpm}}}$$

(Units: cm³/rev if $Q$ in L/min and $N$ in rpm).

**Step 4 — Drive motor power needed (accounting for efficiency $\eta_o$):**

$$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{P_h}{\eta_o} = \frac{P \cdot Q}{\eta_o}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A double-acting cylinder with bore $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 45$ mm, and stroke $L = 400$ mm is to operate at a working pressure of **200 bar** at a depth of **2000 m**. The pump supplies **30 L/min**. Compute:
> (a) Cap-end extension force
> (b) Net extension force at depth
> (c) Extension time

**Given:**
- $D = 0.080$ m, $d = 0.045$ m, $L = 0.400$ m
- $P = 200 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 2 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- $h = 2000$ m
- $Q = 30$ L/min $= 30 \times 10^{-3} / 60$ m³/s $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(a) Cap-end extension force (at surface, no ambient):**

$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^{2} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{cap}} = (2 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.005 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N} \approx 100.5 \; \text{kN}$$

**(b) Ambient pressure at 2000 m depth:**

$$P_{\text{amb}} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000 = 2.011 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \approx 201 \; \text{bar}$$

$$A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \bigl((0.080)^{2} - (0.045)^{2}\bigr) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064 - 0.002025)$$

$$A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.004375) = 3.435 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{net,ext}} = P \cdot A_{\text{cap}} - P_{\text{amb}} \cdot A_{\text{rod}}$$

$$= (2 \times 10^{7})(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (2.011 \times 10^{7})(3.435 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= 100{,}540 - 69{,}080 = 31{,}460 \; \text{N} \approx 31.5 \; \text{kN}$$

**(c) Extension time:**

$$t = \frac{A_{\text{cap}} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.400}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.02 \; \text{s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** At 2000 m depth, the net extension force drops to **~31 %** of the surface value. This dramatic loss is *why* deep-submergence cylinders use **internal pressure compensation** — the oil is pre-pressurised to match ambient, eliminating the imbalance.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Compensation Strategy

| Depth Range | Compensation Strategy | Reason |
|-------------|----------------------|--------|
| 0 – 500 m | External, non-compensated cylinder | Low ambient; neglectable force loss |
| 500 – 2000 m | Pressure-compensated housing around DCV block | Valve spooling friction rises with $\Delta P$ |
| 2000 – 6500 m | Internally compensated cylinder + balanced spool valves | Avoids seal extrusion, reduces stiction |
| > 6500 m (Hadal) | Full oil-filled, pressure-balanced, seawater-displacement bladder | Reaches design pressure on first dive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Submersible Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[PRIME MOVER DC OR AC MOTOR] --> A2[AXIAL PISTON PUMP]
    A2 --> A3[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE]
    A2 --> A4[HP FILTER 3 MICRON]
    A4 --> A5[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE 4 BY 3 SPOOL]
    A5 --> A6[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER MANIPULATOR OR HYDROPLANE]
    A5 --> A7[ROTARY ACTUATOR HATCH OR VALVE GEAR]
    A6 --> A8[RETURN LINE FILTER]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[RESERVOIR WITH LEVEL AND TEMP SENSORS]
    A9 --> A10[HEAT EXCHANGER OIL TO SEAWATER]
    A10 --> A1
    A2 --> A11[ACCUMULATOR NITROGEN CHARGED]
    A11 --> A5
    A5 --> A12[DEPTH COMPENSATION BLADDER AMBIENT PRESSURE]
    A12 --> A9
    classDef power fill FFE0B2,stroke 333,stroke-width 2px
    classDef control fill C8E6C9,stroke 333,stroke-width 2px
    classDef actuator fill FFCDD2,stroke 333,stroke-width 2px
    classDef aux fill E1BEE7,stroke 333,stroke-width 2px
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 power
    class A5,A11 control
    class A6,A7 actuator
    class A8,A9,A10,A12 aux
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **orange band** is the *power generation* path.
- The **green block** is the *control and energy-storage* layer (the brain and capacitor).
- The **red blocks** are the *working actuators* (muscles).
- The **purple blocks** are the *auxiliary and compensation* loop (lungs and kidneys).

## 4.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Hydraulic Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN[OPEN LOOP CIRCUIT]
        O1[PUMP] --> O2[DCV]
        O2 --> O3[ACTUATOR]
        O3 --> O4[RESERVOIR]
        O4 --> O1
    end
    subgraph CLOSED[CLOSED LOOP CIRCUIT]
        C1[PUMP] --> C2[DCV]
        C2 --> C3[ACTUATOR]
        C3 --> C2
        C2 --> C1
    end
    classDef boxOL fill BBDEFB,stroke 000,stroke-width 1px
    classDef boxCL fill FFF59D,stroke 000,stroke 2px
    class O1,O2,O3,O4 boxOL
    class C1,C2,C3 boxCL
```

## 4.3 Hydraulic Cylinder Cross-Section (Functional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INLET PORT P] --> C1[CAP END CHAMBER]
    C1 --> P2[PISTON DIAMETER D]
    P2 --> C2[ROD SIDE CHAMBER]
    C2 --> P3[OUTLET PORT T]
    P2 -.-> P4[ROD DIAMETER d]
    P4 --> P5[STROKE L]
    classDef part fill B2DFDB,stroke 000,stroke-width 1px
    class P1,C1,P2,C2,P3,P4,P5,L part
```

**Notation used in the schematic:**
- **P** = Pressure inlet (4-way DCV pressurised port)
- **T** = Tank/return port
- **D** = bore (cap-side) diameter
- **d** = rod diameter
- **L** = stroke length

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Pascal's Law and explain its significance in the design of submersible hydraulic actuators.**

**Model Answer:**
Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction and acts with equal force on equal areas. Its significance in submersible actuators is twofold:
1. It allows a small input force at the pump piston to produce a much larger output force at the actuator cylinder, with the pressure remaining constant throughout the circuit.
2. It enables rapid, *stiff* (low-deflection) motion control, which is critical for precise positioning of manipulators and hydroplanes underwater where mechanical backlash is unacceptable.
[3 marks]

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting hydraulic cylinder, with one underwater application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **single-acting cylinder** is pressurised from one side only; the return stroke is achieved by an external spring, gravity, or the weight of the load itself. *Application:* spring-return hatch lock actuator on a pressure hull.

A **double-acting cylinder** is pressurised on both sides alternately via a 4-way directional control valve, allowing positive extension *and* retraction. *Application:* manipulator arm joint where gravity cannot be relied upon for return.
[3 marks]

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option 1*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> *(a)* A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm**, a rod diameter of **55 mm**, and a stroke of **500 mm**. It operates from a pump delivering **40 L/min** at a working pressure of **180 bar**. Calculate:
> (i) the cap-end extension force,
> (ii) the rod-end retraction force,
> (iii) the extension time, and
> (iv) the hydraulic power delivered to the cylinder.
> **[(a) = 7 marks]**
> *(b)* With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the function of a **pressure-compensated directional control valve** in a deep-rated submersible hydraulic system. Why is plain DCV spool actuation impossible below 2000 m? **[(b) = 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $D = 0.100$ m, $d = 0.055$ m, $L = 0.500$ m, $P = 180 \times 10^{5}$ Pa, $Q = 40$ L/min $= 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

**(i) Cap-end extension force:**

$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{cap}} = P \cdot A_{\text{cap}} = (1.8 \times 10^{7})(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.414 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{cap}} \approx 141.4 \; \text{kN}}$$

**(ii) Rod-end retraction force:**

$$A_{\text{rod}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\bigl((0.100)^{2} - (0.055)^{2}\bigr) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01 - 0.003025)$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.006975) = 5.479 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

$$F_{\text{rod}} = (1.8 \times 10^{7})(5.479 \times 10^{-3}) = 9.862 \times 10^{4} \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{rod}} \approx 98.6 \; \text{kN}}$$

**(iii) Extension time:**

$$t = \frac{A_{\text{cap}} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.500}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.89 \; \text{s}$$

**(iv) Hydraulic power:**

$$P_h = P \cdot Q = (1.8 \times 10^{7}) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.20 \times 10^{4} \; \text{W} = 12 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to forget that the rod-end area is *annular*, not a full circle. Marks are deducted for using $\frac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$ on the rod side. Always subtract $d^{2}$ from $D^{2}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A **pressure-compensated directional control valve (DCV)** is housed in a sealed chamber filled with hydraulic oil at a pressure equalised to the ambient seawater pressure by a flexible compensation bladder or piston. The spool is therefore *balanced* — there is no net $\Delta P$ across its lands, which would otherwise cause stiction and prevent shifting at depth.

**Why plain DCV spool actuation fails below 2000 m:**
- At 2000 m, ambient pressure ≈ **200 bar**.
- A standard DCV is sprung on one side and ports to tank on the other. If tank is at atmospheric pressure, the spool sees a **200 bar** differential across its end area.
- The solenoid pilot cannot generate enough force to shift the spool against this imbalance; the spool *locks* hydraulically.
- Furthermore, high differential pressure across spool lands accelerates **seal extrusion** and **erosion of land edges**, drastically reducing service life.
- The compensation chamber *eliminates* this differential, allowing the same low-force solenoid to shift the spool reliably to full ocean depth.

A labelled sketch should show: the valve body, the spool, the solenoid operator, the compensation chamber, the compensation bladder exposed to seawater, and the main ports P, T, A, B.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option 2*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]**
> *(a)* With neat diagrams, describe the construction and working of a **gas-charged hydraulic accumulator**. Explain its role in a submersible's emergency blow-down circuit. **[(a) = 7 marks]**
> *(b)* A submersible operates at a depth of **4500 m**. The seawater density is **1025 kg/m³** and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate:
> (i) the ambient hydrostatic pressure,
> (ii) the net force available on a single-acting plunger of diameter **60 mm** if the internal oil is pressurised to **280 bar**, and
> (iii) the percentage loss in thrust compared to surface operation. **[(b) = 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **gas-charged hydraulic accumulator** is an energy-storage device consisting of two chambers separated by a flexible elastomer bladder (or a floating piston):

1. **Pre-charged nitrogen chamber** — sealed, set to a pre-charge pressure $P_0$ (typically 0.7 × working pressure).
2. **Hydraulic oil chamber** — connected to the system via a poppet or spring-loaded check valve.

**Working principle:** When system pressure exceeds the gas pre-charge, oil flows in and compresses the nitrogen (Boyle's law: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ at constant temperature). The compressed gas stores energy as $E = \frac{1}{2} \Delta P \cdot \Delta V$. When system pressure drops, the gas expands and discharges the stored oil to maintain flow.

**Role in emergency blow-down circuit:** In a submersible emergency (e.g., power failure), the accumulator holds enough pressurised oil to:
- Open ballast tank vents to release water from buoyancy tanks → the vehicle becomes positively buoyant and surfaces.
- Actuate the jettison mechanism for drop-weights.
- Operate the manipulator to release a snagged tether.

Without the accumulator, a power loss would render all hydraulics inoperable — making the accumulator a **safety-critical** component certified by classification societies (DNV, ABS).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $h = 4500$ m, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $D = 0.060$ m, $P_{\text{oil}} = 280 \times 10^{5}$ Pa.

**(i) Ambient hydrostatic pressure at 4500 m:**

$$P_{\text{amb}} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4500$$

$$P_{\text{amb}} = 4.525 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \approx 452.5 \; \text{bar}$$

**(ii) Net force on the single-acting plunger at depth:**

The plunger has area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.060)^{2} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

Oil-side force pushing the plunger out:

$$F_{\text{oil}} = P_{\text{oil}} \cdot A = (2.8 \times 10^{7})(2.827 \times 10^{-3}) = 7.916 \times 10^{4} \; \text{N} \approx 79.2 \; \text{kN}$$

Ambient pressure acting on the rod-side projection opposes extension. For a single-acting plunger of full diameter exposed to seawater:

$$F_{\text{amb}} = P_{\text{amb}} \cdot A = (4.525 \times 10^{7})(2.827 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.279 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N} \approx 127.9 \; \text{kN}$$

Since $F_{\text{amb}} > F_{\text{oil}}$, the net force is *negative* — meaning the cylinder **cannot extend** at 4500 m. To make extension possible, internal pressure must exceed ambient, i.e.:

$$P_{\text{oil,req}} > 452.5 \; \text{bar}$$

**(iii) Percentage loss compared to surface:**

Surface thrust (no ambient):

$$F_{\text{surf}} = 79.2 \; \text{kN}$$

Depth thrust (negative):

$$F_{\text{depth}} = 79.2 - 127.9 = -48.7 \; \text{kN}$$

Loss percentage:

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{F_{\text{surf}} - F_{\text{depth}}}{F_{\text{surf}}} \times 100 = \frac{79.2 - (-48.7)}{79.2} \times 100 \approx 161.5 \;\%$$

The negative value indicates the cylinder cannot only not extend, but is actually pushed *inward* by ambient pressure. This is why at 4500 m a plain single-acting cylinder is unusable and **internal pressure compensation is mandatory**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert the given pressure to SI units (Pa). Always show the conversion $\times 10^{5}$.
> 2. Using the wrong cross-section area (full bore vs annular).
> 3. Sign convention — ambient pressure always *opposes* motion on the side of the cylinder that is *not* pressurised.
> 4. In part (b) of Question B, students often forget that a single-acting plunger at depth has the *full* bore area exposed to ambient (not annular), and the net thrust can become negative.
> 5. Not stating units explicitly in intermediate steps.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pascal's Law** is the *only* principle that makes hydraulics work — commit it to memory verbatim.
- **Pressure × Area = Force** — the single most-tested KTU formula for this module.
- Always distinguish **cap-end area** ($\frac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$) from **rod-end area** ($\frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2}-d^{2})$).
- At depth, **ambient pressure opposes the unpressurised side** of the piston — this is the source of nearly every "trick" question in the KTU paper.
- **Power** in kW from bar and L/min is $\dfrac{P \cdot Q}{600}$ — memorise this conversion.
- **Accumulator pre-charge** is typically 0.7 × working pressure; never exceed the maximum stated on the nameplate.
- **Closed-loop circuits** give finer control and faster response (used in ROV thrusters).
- **Open-loop circuits** are simpler and used for manipulator / hatch actuation.
- **Compensation is mandatory** below ~1000 m for any actuator whose rod penetrates the hull.
- **Pump torque** sizing: $T = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$ — required to size the drive motor.
- **Standard working pressure band** for submersibles: **200 – 350 bar**.
- **Seawater pressure gain:** **+1 bar per 10.2 m** of depth — quick mental arithmetic in the exam hall.
- **Bulk modulus of oil** governs system stiffness; if you see a question about "spongy" hydraulics, blame air entrainment (effective bulk modulus drops drastically).
- The **gas-charged accumulator** is *always* a safety-critical device in emergency circuits — examiners love asking its role in blow-down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** KTU 2024 scheme questions on hydraulic systems almost always test **three things**: (1) the basic force/pressure/area triangle, (2) the depth-compensation subtlety, and (3) a sketch-and-explain of a component (DCV, accumulator, or pump). Practice all three categories thoroughly before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Pressure Air Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** High Pressure Air Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **High Pressure Air (HPA) System:** A self-contained, redundant pneumatic energy storage and distribution network installed on a submersible/submarine, designed to store compressed air at elevated pressures (typically **200 bar to 350 bar**), and deliver it on demand for mission-critical functions including main ballast tank (MBT) blow, emergency surfacing, pneumatic actuation of valves, and starting/operation of the air-independent propulsion (AIP) plant. The HPA system is one of the **most safety-critical** auxiliary systems on a submarine, because the **ability to surface from a fully submerged, even "bottomed" state, depends directly on its functional integrity**. ## 1.2 Sub-System Classification (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping) A typical HPA system comprises four logical sub-systems: | Sub-System | Primary Function | |------------|-----------------| | Generation (HP Compressors) | Charge the air banks from atmospheric intake | | Storage (Air Flasks / Banks) | Store the high-pressure energy | | Distribution (Manifolds, Valves, Piping) | Route air to consumers at controlled pressure | | Safety (Relief Valves, Burst Discs, Isolation) | Protect personnel, hull, and equipment | ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Scuba Diver's Twin Tank Rig:** Imagine a scuba diver carrying two scuba tanks on their back at a pressure of 200 bar. The diver has a primary regulator (first stage) that drops the pressure to ~10 bar, and a second stage that further drops it to ambient so they can breathe. The HP air system on a submarine works on exactly the **same principle** — high-pressure air is stored in banks (the "tanks"), then stepped down through a hierarchy of pressure-reducing valves to the working pressure of each consumer (e.g., **5–8 bar** for valve actuation, **30–70 bar** for torpedo discharge, **ambient** for MBT blow). The "trick" is that the air is used not for breathing, but to **physically push seawater out of the ballast tanks** so the submarine becomes positively buoyant and rises to the surface. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (for KTU problems) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} \approx 1.013 \text{ bar}$ > - **Typical HPA Storage Pressure:** $P_{HP} = 200$ to $350 \text{ bar}$ > - **Submarine Operating Depth (modern Kilo / Scorpene / Virginia class):** $250$ to $500 \text{ m}$ > - **Hydrostatic Pressure Gradient:** $P_{hyd}(z) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z$, with $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - **Hydrostatic Pressure @ 100 m:** $\approx 11.0 \text{ bar}$ > - **Adiabatic Index for Air (diatomic):** $\gamma = 1.4$ > - **Gas Constant for Air:** $R_{air} = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ > - **Specific Volume of Air at STP:** $v_{STP} = 0.843 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — "Why HP and not LP?" A natural student question: *Why store air at 200–350 bar instead of, say, 10 bar?* - **Energy Density:** Energy stored is roughly $E \propto P \cdot V$. A 10× increase in storage pressure gives ~10× more surfacing energy in the same tank volume. - **Volume / Weight Budget:** Submarines are volume-constrained. Storing at 300 bar instead of 10 bar reduces the number of flasks by a factor of ~30. - **Faster Emergency Blow:** A high volume of air at 300 bar can be vented to ballast in seconds, producing rapid surfacing. ## 2.2 Core Theory — Boyle's Law & Ideal Gas Behaviour For an isothermal (slow) blow, the volume of seawater displaced by the air leaving a flask at pressure $P_1$ and volume $V_1$ (flask volume) and expanding to a back-pressure $P_2$ (hydrostatic + atmospheric + line losses) is given by: $$V_{\text{displaced}} = V_1 \cdot \left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} - 1 \right)$$ For an **adiabatic** (fast, emergency) blow, the same relation is: $$V_{\text{displaced,adiabatic}} = V_1 \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why two versions?** In a slow blow (routine), the air has time to exchange heat with the flask walls and surrounding seawater — essentially isothermal. In a fast emergency blow from depth, the expansion is so rapid that the gas has no time to absorb heat — adiabatic. The adiabatic case yields a *smaller* displaced volume, which is why **emergency blow systems are intentionally oversized** to compensate. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of an Emergency Blow 1. **Trigger:** Submerged submarine unable to surface via normal means. The Emergency Blow Valve (EBV) is actuated. 2. **Selection:** Operator selects "GROUP I" or "GROUP II" air banks (redundancy — never both at once, to leave a reserve). 3. **Pressurization:** The HP air passes from flask → manifold → through a quick-opening valve → into the MBT. 4. **Displacement:** Air at $P_1$ pushes seawater out of the MBT through the hull-mounted ports (kingstons/limber holes). 5. **Surfacing:** Displaced seawater reduces the submarine's mass → **positive buoyancy** → ascent. 6. **Termination:** When submarine broaches, vents open to release the air. ## 2.4 HP Compressor — Operating Principle A submarine HP air compressor is typically a **3-stage reciprocating, oil-free (or oil-injected with separation) compressor** with inter-coolers and after-coolers. For every stage $i$: $$\frac{T_{i,\text{out}}}{T_{i,\text{in}}} = \left( \frac{P_{i,\text{out}}}{P_{i,\text{in}}} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ The work input per kg of air (per stage) is: $$w_i = c_p (T_{i,\text{out}} - T_{i,\text{in}})$$ For a multi-stage compressor with inter-cooling back to inlet temperature and optimal pressure ratio per stage $(P_{ratio})^{(1/n)}$, the **minimum total work** is: $$w_{min} = n \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ (P_{ratio})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving Module 4 HPA numerical problems. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions. | # | Quantity / Parameter | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Volume of seawater displaced (isothermal) | $V_d = V_f \cdot (P_f/P_b - 1)$ | $V_f$ flask vol [m³], $P_f$ flask [bar], $P_b$ back-pressure [bar] | Routine blow sizing | | 2 | Volume displaced (adiabatic / emergency) | $V_d = V_f \cdot \left[ (P_f/P_b)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air | Emergency blow sizing | | 3 | Required HP flask volume | $V_f = V_{MBT,sw} \cdot \left( P_b / P_f \right)$ (isothermal) | $V_{MBT,sw}$ is seawater volume to be displaced | Reverse calc: flask sizing | | 4 | Ideal gas law (STP) | $P V = n R T$ or $P v = R T$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) or 287 J/(kg·K) | Mass of air stored | | 5 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $z$ | $P_{hyd}(z) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g z$ | $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Back-pressure at depth | | 6 | Total MBT back-pressure (depth) | $P_b = P_{atm} + P_{hyd}(z) + \Delta P_{line}$ | $\Delta P_{line}$ piping loss, typ. 0.3–0.8 bar | Blow valve load | | 7 | Single-stage isothermal work | $w = P_1 v_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | J/kg of air | Compressor power baseline | | 8 | Single-stage adiabatic work | $w = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{R T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | $\eta_c$ isothermal efficiency | Compressor design | | 9 | Pressure ratio per stage (optimal, $n$ stages) | $r_p = (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | $n$ = number of stages | Multi-stage design | | 10 | Time to charge a flask | $t = \frac{P_f V_f}{\dot{W} \cdot \eta_{mech}} \cdot \frac{1}{(\text{dimensionless})}$ | $\dot{W}$ compressor power | Mission planning | | 11 | Flasks required (integer rounding) | $N_{flasks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{V_{f,\text{req}}}{V_{f,\text{single}}} \right\rceil$ | Ceiling function | Procurement | | 12 | Buoyancy gained (from displaced water) | $\Delta B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_d$ | Newtons | Surfacing margin | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Submarine Life-Cycle Cost:** ~12–18% of a submarine's total auxiliary machinery cost is attributable to the HPA system, including compressors, flasks, valves, and instrumentation. - **Hazard Class:** The system is classified as **"High Energy"** in naval safety standards because a flask rupture releases energy equivalent to a small explosion (a 50 L flask at 300 bar stores $\approx 2.4$ MJ — roughly the kinetic energy of a 1-ton car at 100 km/h). - **Material Trend:** Modern non-magnetic submarines (e.g., Scorpene class) use **carbon-fibre-wrapped aluminium-lined flasks** to reduce magnetic signature and save ~40% weight compared to steel flasks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Volume of Seawater Displaced per Flask (Isothermal Case) We start from the **first law of thermodynamics** applied to a control volume (the MBT) where HP air enters and seawater leaves, assuming the process is slow enough that the air remains at ambient seawater temperature $T_{sw}$ (typically 4–15 °C in operating zones). **Assumptions:** - Negligible heat transfer with environment over the blow duration → quasi-isothermal. - Negligible kinetic energy of escaping seawater. - Air behaves as an ideal gas. - Hydrostatic pressure is constant (depth essentially unchanged during the blow — valid for short-emergency events). **Step 1.** Initial air state in the flask: $P_1 = P_f$, $V_1 = V_f$, $T_1 = T_{sw}$. After the air is released into the MBT, its final state (in the MBT, pushing the seawater out) is: $P_2 = P_b$, $V_2 = V_{air,MBT}$. **Step 2.** Apply Boyle's Law (isothermal ideal gas): $$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$ $$\Rightarrow P_f \cdot V_f = P_b \cdot V_{air,MBT}$$ **Step 3.** The volume of seawater displaced is the *additional* air volume in the MBT beyond what would be there at atmospheric pressure. Equivalently, since the MBT was initially filled with seawater at pressure $P_b$, the air entering at $P_b$ simply fills the volume vacated by displaced seawater. The mass of air leaving the flask is: $$m_{air} = \frac{P_f V_f}{R T_{sw}}$$ **Step 4.** At the MBT pressure $P_b$, this same mass occupies: $$V_{air,MBT} = \frac{m_{air} R T_{sw}}{P_b} = \frac{P_f V_f}{P_b}$$ **Step 5.** Net volume of seawater displaced (since the MBT was originally full of water at the same pressure $P_b$): $$V_d = V_{air,MBT} - V_{\text{air initially in MBT,if any}}$$ For a *fully flooded* MBT at the start of the blow: $$\boxed{V_d = V_f \cdot \left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} - 1 \right)}$$ **Step 6.** (Adiabatic extension) For an emergency blow, repeat Step 1–3 using $P V^\gamma = \text{const}$: $$P_f V_f^{\gamma} = P_b V_{air,MBT}^{\gamma}$$ $$V_{air,MBT} = V_f \left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} \right)^{1/\gamma}$$ $$\boxed{V_{d,\text{adiabatic}} = V_f \cdot \left[ \left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} \right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]}$$ With $\gamma = 1.4$, $1/\gamma \approx 0.714$, so the adiabatic displaced volume is **less** than the isothermal case for the same $P_f/P_b$ — confirming the need for **redundancy and oversizing** in emergency systems. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Required Number of HP Air Flasks for an Emergency Blow **Problem Setup:** A Scorpene-class submarine is at a depth of $z = 100$ m. The MBT (main ballast tank) holds a seawater volume of $V_{MBT,sw} = 250 \text{ m}^3$ when fully flooded. We want to **fully blow this tank** (i.e., displace all 250 m³ of seawater) using HP air flasks each of $V_f = 50$ L = 0.05 m³, charged to $P_f = 300$ bar (gauge). Assume adiabatic blow. Find the number of flasks required. **Step 1. Compute the back-pressure $P_b$ at 100 m depth.** The hydrostatic pressure (gauge) is: $$P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z$$ $$P_{hyd} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 100$$ $$P_{hyd} = 1{,}005{,}525 \text{ Pa} = 10.055 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$ Add atmospheric pressure to get absolute: $$P_{hyd,abs} = 10.055 + 1.013 = 11.068 \text{ bar (absolute)}$$ Now add the line and kingston losses. Take $\Delta P_{line} = 0.5$ bar: $$P_b = 11.068 + 0.5 = 11.568 \text{ bar (absolute)}$$ **Step 2. Apply the adiabatic displaced-volume equation to a single flask.** $$V_{d,\text{single}} = V_f \cdot \left[ \left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} \right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$ Note: $P_f$ here must also be absolute. 300 bar gauge = 301.013 bar absolute. Use $P_f \approx 301$ bar, $P_b = 11.57$ bar: $$\frac{P_f}{P_b} = \frac{301}{11.57} = 26.02$$ $$\left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} \right)^{1/\gamma} = 26.02^{0.714}$$ Compute $26.02^{0.714}$: $$\ln(26.02) = 3.258$$ $$0.714 \times 3.258 = 2.326$$ $$e^{2.326} = 10.24$$ Therefore: $$V_{d,\text{single}} = 0.05 \times (10.24 - 1) = 0.05 \times 9.24 = 0.462 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3. Number of flasks required for full blow of 250 m³:** $$N_{\text{flasks,ideal}} = \frac{250}{0.462} = 541 \text{ flasks}$$ **Step 4. Apply safety factor $SF = 1.25$ (naval practice — accounts for flask-to-flask delivery losses, partial-fill, and temperature effects):** $$N_{\text{flasks,design}} = 541 \times 1.25 = 676 \text{ flasks (round up to 680)}$$ **Step 5. Sanity check — convert to total air mass stored:** $$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{P_f V_{f,\text{total}}}{R T_{sw}}$$ With $V_{f,\text{total}} = 680 \times 0.05 = 34 \text{ m}^3$ and $T_{sw} = 288$ K: $$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{301 \times 10^5 \times 34}{287 \times 288}$$ $$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{1.0234 \times 10^9}{82{,}656}$$ $$m_{\text{air}} \approx 12{,}382 \text{ kg} \approx 12.4 \text{ tonnes of compressed air}$$ This is consistent with published data for the Scorpene class (~10–14 tonnes of HPA inventory for emergency blow). ## 3.3 Derivation III — HP Compressor Sizing and Charging Time **Problem Setup:** The HPA storage system above ($V_{f,\text{total}} = 34$ m³) must be recharged from $P_1 = 1$ bar (atmospheric, after a full discharge) back to $P_f = 301$ bar. A 3-stage reciprocating compressor with mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.85$ and overall isentropic efficiency $\eta_c = 0.7$ takes in air at $T_1 = 300$ K. Find the **compressor power** and **charging time**. **Step 1. Optimal pressure ratio per stage:** For $n = 3$ stages, total ratio = $301/1 = 301$: $$r_p = 301^{1/3} = 6.69$$ So each stage compresses from $1 \to 6.69 \to 44.7 \to 301$ bar. **Step 2. Stage outlet temperature (per stage, with inter-cooling back to $T_1$):** $$T_{out} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ $$T_{out} = 300 \times 6.69^{0.286}$$ Compute $6.69^{0.286}$: $$\ln(6.69) = 1.900$$ $$0.286 \times 1.900 = 0.5435$$ $$e^{0.5435} = 1.722$$ $$T_{out} = 300 \times 1.722 = 516.6 \text{ K} \;(\approx 243.5°\text{C})$$ This is why **inter-cooling and after-cooling are mandatory** — without them, the third stage outlet would exceed 700 K, destroying seals and lubricants. **Step 3. Work per stage per kg of air (adiabatic, with efficiency):** $$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{R T_1}{\eta_c} \left[ r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$ $$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \cdot \frac{287 \times 300}{0.7} \times (1.722 - 1)$$ $$w_{\text{stage}} = 3.5 \times 123{,}000 \times 0.722$$ $$w_{\text{stage}} = 3.5 \times 88{,}806 = 310{,}821 \text{ J/kg} \approx 311 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4. Total work for 3 stages:** $$w_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 311 = 933 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5. Compressor power needed to charge 12.4 tonnes of air in, say, 4 hours:** Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \frac{12{,}382 \text{ kg}}{4 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = 0.860 \text{ kg/s}$$ Power (input shaft): $$\dot{W}_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot w_{\text{total}}}{\eta_m} = \frac{0.860 \times 933}{0.85} = 943.7 \text{ kW}$$ Apply motor + drive efficiency of ~0.93: $$\dot{W}_{\text{motor}} \approx \frac{943.7}{0.93} \approx 1015 \text{ kW} \approx 1.0 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 6. Charging time for 1.0 MW motor:** $$t_{\text{charge}} = \frac{12{,}382 \times 933{,}000}{1{,}015{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 0.93} = \frac{1.155 \times 10^{10}}{8.024 \times 10^5} \approx 14{,}400 \text{ s} = 4.0 \text{ hours} \;\checkmark$$ This confirms self-consistency. In reality, modern submarines use two parallel 500 kW compressors. ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Buoyancy Margin and Surfacing Verification **Check:** With $V_d = 250$ m³ displaced, what is the buoyancy gained, and is the submarine guaranteed to surface? $$\Delta B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_d = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250$$ $$\Delta B = 2{,}513{,}812 \text{ N} \approx 256 \text{ tonnes-force}$$ A Scorpene-class submarine has a submerged displacement of ~1700 tonnes. The standard free-flooding MBT volume is sized to produce ~3–5% positive buoyancy at full blow, which is more than enough to overcome negative residual buoyancy and hydrodynamic drag during ascent. > [!NOTE] > **Important:** The *buoyancy gained* is independent of the depth (since it depends only on displaced water volume). However, the *air required* increases with depth because the back-pressure $P_b$ grows. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Submarine HPA System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Intake Louver
with Separator] --> B[1st Stage
LP Compressor] B --> C[Intercooler 1] C --> D[2nd Stage
MP Compressor] D --> E[Intercooler 2] E --> F[3rd Stage
HP Compressor] F --> G[Aftercooler and
Drier Assembly] G --> H[Charging Manifold] H --> I1[HP Air Flask Bank
Group I] H --> I2[HP Air Flask Bank
Group II] I1 --> J[Distribution Manifold] I2 --> J J --> K1[Pressure Reducer A
to 30 to 70 bar] J --> K2[Pressure Reducer B
to 5 to 8 bar] J --> K3[Emergency Blow Valve
no reduction] K1 --> L1[Torpedo Tube
Discharge System] K2 --> L2[Pneumatic Valves
and Actuators] K3 --> L3[Main Ballast Tank
MBT Blow Line] M[Safety Relief Valves
set at 1.05 times P design] -.-> H N[Burst Disc Assembly] -.-> I1 O[Pressure Transmitters
and Level Gauges] -.-> I1 O -.-> I2 P[Control Console
in Control Room] --> A P --> H P --> K3 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Air Flow Path (Generation to Consumption) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GEN[Generation Subsystem] direction TB GEN1[Atmospheric Intake] --> GEN2[Filter and Silencer] GEN2 --> GEN3[3 Stage Reciprocating
Compressor 500 kW x 2] GEN3 --> GEN4[Intercoolers and
Aftercooler] GEN4 --> GEN5[Refrigeration Drier
to minus 40 deg C PDP] end subgraph STORE[Storage Subsystem] direction TB ST1[Charging Manifold
300 bar rated] ST2[Group I Flasks
340 flasks x 50 L] ST3[Group II Flasks
340 flasks x 50 L] ST1 --> ST2 ST1 --> ST3 end subgraph DIST[Distribution Subsystem] direction TB D1[HP Manifold 300 bar] D2[First Reducer
to 70 bar] D3[Second Reducer
to 8 bar] D1 --> D2 D1 --> D3 D1 --> D4[Emergency Blow
Quick Opening Valve] end subgraph USE[End Users] direction TB U1[MBT Blow
full flow] U2[HP Air consumers
70 bar] U3[LP Air consumers
8 bar valves] end GEN5 --> ST1 ST2 --> D1 ST3 --> D1 D2 --> U2 D3 --> U3 D4 --> U1 ``` ## 4.3 State-Diagram of HP Flask During Mission Cycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> FullyCharged FullyCharged --> InService: Normal Operation InService --> PartiallyDepleted: Routine MBT cycle PartiallyDepleted --> FullyCharged: Compressor recharge PartiallyDepleted --> EmergencyBlow: EBV activated FullyCharged --> EmergencyBlow: Bottomed or flood casualty EmergencyBlow --> Depleted: Flask exhausted Depleted --> Servicing: At dockyard Servicing --> FullyCharged: Hydrostatic test and recharge Depleted --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Component-Level Schematic (HP Flask with Safety Fittings) ```mermaid flowchart LR F[Flask Body
Carbon Fibre or Steel] --> V1[Charging Valve
Normally Open] F --> V2[Discharge Valve
Normally Closed] F --> V3[Safety Relief Valve
set 315 bar] F --> V4[Burst Disc
set 330 bar] F --> V5[Drain Valve] F --> SG[Pressure Gauge
Local] F --> PT[Pressure Transmitter
to Control Room] F --> TT[Thermometer Pocket] ``` > [!NOTE] > All valve set pressures are illustrative and align with KTU 2024 representative problem values. Real naval systems are governed by exact design codes (e.g., Naval Engineering Standard NES 824 for pressure vessels). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the "absolute vs gauge" conversion** — losing 2–3 marks in every numerical answer. > 2. **Mixing isothermal and adiabatic formulas** — examiners explicitly test both. Read the question stem carefully. > 3. **Not including the line loss / kingston loss** $\Delta P_{line}$ in $P_b$ — the back-pressure is **never** just $P_{atm} + \rho g z$. > 4. **Forgetting the safety factor** in the flask count — examiners often accept 1.15 to 1.30; pick 1.25 unless told otherwise. > 5. **Using $\gamma = 1.67$ (monatomic) instead of $\gamma = 1.4$ (diatomic air).** > 6. **Failing to express final answers in SI units with proper rounding** — full marks require 3–4 significant figures and a unit. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B] **List and briefly explain any three functions of the High Pressure Air (HPA) system on a conventional submarine.** **Model Answer:** The High Pressure Air (HPA) system on a conventional (diesel-electric) submarine performs the following critical functions: 1. **Main Ballast Tank (MBT) Blow for Surfacing:** This is the *primary safety function*. HP air stored in flasks at 200–350 bar is admitted into the MBT to expel seawater overboard, creating positive buoyancy and surfacing the boat. In an emergency, the Emergency Blow Valve (EBV) can dump an entire group of flasks in a few seconds, raising the boat from "bottomed" state. 2. **Operation of Pneumatic Actuators and Valves:** Many hull-penetrating valves (sea-water valves, kingston valves, vent valves, drain valves) are pneumatically actuated because hydraulic systems are vulnerable to leaks. These require air at reduced pressure of 5–8 bar, supplied via pressure-reducing stations downstream of the HP manifold. 3. **Torpedo and Weapon Discharge:** Heavy-weight torpedoes (e.g., SST-4, Varunastra) and missile ejection systems use HP air at 30–70 bar for impulse discharge, providing the initial push to clear the weapon from the tube before the water ram takes over. 4. **(Bonus, for any 3-of-N) Air Supply to Air-Independent Propulsion (AIP) Plants:** Modern submarines (e.g., Scorpene with MESMA, Type 214 with HDW fuel cells) require HP air to start the AIP plant and to provide oxygen replenishment via the electrolysis product or external supply line. *(Any three functions explained in 2–3 lines each = full 3 marks.)* --- ### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam] **Differentiate between isothermal and adiabatic expansion of HP air during a submarine blow, stating the relevant equations.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Isothermal Expansion | Adiabatic Expansion | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | Heat exchange | Allowed (slow blow, gas exchanges heat with seawater-cooled flask walls) | Negligible (fast blow, no time for heat transfer) | | Time scale | Minutes (routine surface) | Seconds (emergency blow) | | Governing law | Boyle's Law: $P V = \text{const}$ | Poisson's Law: $P V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ | | Temperature | Constant, $T = T_{sw}$ | Drops during expansion | | Displaced volume equation | $V_d = V_f \left( \dfrac{P_f}{P_b} - 1 \right)$ | $V_d = V_f \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_f}{P_b} \right)^{1/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | | Engineering implication | Routine blow design | Emergency blow design (smaller displaced volume → requires oversizing) | *(3 marks: 1 for identifying the time scale, 1 for the correct equation, 1 for the engineering implication.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) format for Module 4 (out of 14 marks): a 7+7 sub-part structure is expected, with full internal choice (i.e., answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** [Modeled on KTU Dec 2022 University Exam Pattern] **(a)** A conventional submarine is submerged at a depth of **80 m**. The Main Ballast Tank (MBT) when fully flooded contains **180 m³** of seawater. Each HP air flask has an internal volume of **60 L** and is charged to **250 bar (gauge)**. Assuming **isothermal** conditions, **line loss of 0.4 bar**, and a **safety factor of 1.2**, determine: (i) The required total HP flask volume. (ii) The number of flasks required. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the same submarine data as in (a), repeat the calculation assuming the blow is now **adiabatic** ($\gamma = 1.4$). Compare and comment on the difference in flask count. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Isothermal Blow:** **Step 1: Compute the back-pressure $P_b$ at 80 m depth.** Hydrostatic pressure (gauge): $$P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot z = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 80 = 804{,}420 \text{ Pa} \approx 8.044 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$ Absolute: $$P_{hyd,abs} = 8.044 + 1.013 = 9.057 \text{ bar (absolute)}$$ With line loss $\Delta P_{line} = 0.4$ bar: $$\boxed{P_b = 9.057 + 0.4 = 9.457 \text{ bar (absolute)}}$$ **Step 2: Apply the isothermal displaced-volume equation (per flask).** Convert $P_f$ to absolute: $P_f = 250 + 1.013 = 251.013$ bar (use 251 bar): $$V_{d,\text{single}} = V_f \cdot \left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} - 1 \right) = 0.06 \times \left( \frac{251}{9.457} - 1 \right)$$ $$\frac{251}{9.457} = 26.54$$ $$V_{d,\text{single}} = 0.06 \times 25.54 = 1.532 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3: Required total flask volume for $V_{MBT,sw} = 180$ m³:** $$V_{f,\text{req,iso}} = \frac{V_{MBT,sw}}{V_{d,\text{single}}/V_f} = \frac{180}{25.54} = 7.05 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 4: Number of flasks (with $SF = 1.2$):** $$N_{\text{iso}} = 1.2 \times \frac{7.05}{0.06} = 1.2 \times 117.5 = 141 \text{ flasks}$$ Round up: $N_{\text{iso}} = 142$ flasks. **Part (b) — Adiabatic Blow:** **Step 1: Per-flask displaced volume (adiabatic).** $$\frac{P_f}{P_b} = 26.54 \text{ (same as above)}$$ $$\left( \frac{P_f}{P_b} \right)^{1/\gamma} = 26.54^{0.714}$$ $$\ln(26.54) = 3.278, \quad 0.714 \times 3.278 = 2.341, \quad e^{2.341} = 10.39$$ $$V_{d,\text{single,adiabatic}} = 0.06 \times (10.39 - 1) = 0.06 \times 9.39 = 0.5634 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2: Required number of flasks:** $$N_{\text{adiabatic,ideal}} = \frac{180}{0.5634 / 0.06} = \frac{180}{9.39} = 19.17 \text{ times the single-flask volume}$$ Equivalently: $$N_{\text{adiabatic,ideal}} = 19.17 \times 1 = \frac{180}{0.5634} = 319.5 \text{ flasks (ideal)}$$ With safety factor 1.2: $$\boxed{N_{\text{adiabatic,design}} = 1.2 \times 319.5 = 383.4 \to 384 \text{ flasks}}$$ **Step 3: Comparison & Comment.** | Parameter | Isothermal | Adiabatic | |-----------|------------|-----------| | Flasks required (design) | 142 | 384 | | Ratio | 1.0 | ~2.7× | **Comment:** The adiabatic blow requires **~2.7 times** more flasks than the isothermal case for the same displaced volume. This is because in adiabatic expansion, the gas does not absorb heat from the surroundings, so its temperature drops and its final pressure is reached at a smaller volume than in the isothermal case. **This is precisely why emergency blow systems are oversized and why dual redundancy (Group I + Group II) is mandated by naval safety codes — you must guarantee surfacing even in the worst-case thermodynamic regime.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** [Modeled on KTU May 2023 University Exam Pattern] **(a)** Describe the **HP air compressor subsystem** of a modern non-magnetic submarine, including its type, number of stages, and the role of inter-coolers and after-coolers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-stage reciprocating HP air compressor compresses air from **1 bar (abs)** to **300 bar (abs)**. The intake temperature is **300 K** and the isentropic efficiency of each stage is **0.70**. Compute: (i) The **optimum pressure ratio per stage**. (ii) The **discharge temperature** of the third stage. (iii) The **shaft power** required to compress **10 kg of air per minute**, assuming a mechanical efficiency of **0.88**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — HP Compressor Subsystem Description:** A modern non-magnetic submarine (e.g., Scorpene, Type 209) employs a **3-stage reciprocating, oil-free (water-lubricated or PTFE-rings)** HP air compressor subsystem. The configuration is: 1. **Type:** Reciprocating piston-type, typically arranged in an L- or W-configuration to balance forces. Three compression stages because a single-stage ratio of 300:1 would produce unmanageable discharge temperatures (~1100 K). 2. **Number of Units:** Two parallel compressors, each rated at ~50% of total demand, providing **redundancy** so that loss of one unit does not disable HPA charging. 3. **Inter-coolers:** Between successive stages, the compressed air is passed through shell-and-tube heat exchangers cooled by seawater. The air is brought back to (or close to) the intake temperature, **reducing the specific work** of subsequent stages and **preventing thermal damage** to seals, valves, and cylinders. 4. **After-cooler:** After the final (3rd) stage, the air at ~250 °C is cooled to ~40 °C before entering the **refrigeration drier** to reduce its Pressure Dew Point (PDP) to **below −40 °C**. This is critical because moisture in the HP flasks would condense, cause corrosion, and produce ice at the blow valves during emergency operation. 5. **Lubrication:** Water-lubricated cylinders (with demineralised water) or dry-running PTFE piston rings to avoid oil contamination of the air — important because oil + HP air + O₂ traces → explosion risk. 6. **Suction Filter/Silencer:** On the intake louver, to prevent salt spray and particulates from entering the compressor, which would destroy the close-tolerance cylinders. *(7 marks allocated across: type + stages 2M, redundancy & inter-coolers 2M, after-cooler & drier 2M, lube/oil-free & safety 1M.)* --- **Part (b) — Compressor Calculations:** **Step 1: Optimum pressure ratio per stage.** $$r_p = \left( \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{300}{1} \right)^{1/3} = 300^{0.333} = 6.694$$ $$\boxed{r_p \approx 6.69 \text{ per stage}}$$ Intermediate pressures: - $P_1 = 1$ bar (intake) - $P_2 = 6.69$ bar (after 1st stage) - $P_3 = 6.69^2 = 44.8$ bar (after 2nd stage) - $P_4 = 6.69^3 = 300$ bar (after 3rd stage) ✓ **Step 2: Discharge temperature of the third stage.** For an ideal-gas adiabatic process: $$T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ With $T_{in} = T_1 = 300$ K (inter-cooled back to intake temperature between stages), $\gamma = 1.4$: $$(\gamma - 1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$$ $$T_{out,3} = 300 \times 6.694^{0.2857}$$ Compute $6.694^{0.2857}$: $$\ln(6.694) = 1.9008, \quad 0.2857 \times 1.9008 = 0.5430, \quad e^{0.5430} = 1.7213$$ $$T_{out,3} = 300 \times 1.7213 = 516.4 \text{ K} = 243.3 \text{ °C}$$ **Note:** This is the *ideal* temperature; with the isentropic efficiency factor, the real temperature will be **higher**: Real (with $\eta_c = 0.7$): $$T_{out,3,\text{real}} = T_1 + \frac{T_{out,3,\text{ideal}} - T_1}{\eta_c} = 300 + \frac{516.4 - 300}{0.7} = 300 + 309.1 = 609.1 \text{ K} = 335.9 \text{ °C}$$ This is the **realistic discharge temperature** that justifies the **after-cooler** requirement. **Step 3: Shaft power for 10 kg/min = 0.1667 kg/s.** Work per stage (per kg of air, with efficiency): $$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{R T_1}{\eta_c} \cdot \left[ r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$ $$w_{\text{stage}} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times \frac{287 \times 300}{0.7} \times (1.7213 - 1)$$ $$w_{\text{stage}} = 3.5 \times 123{,}000 \times 0.7213$$ $$w_{\text{stage}} = 3.5 \times 88{,}720 = 310{,}520 \text{ J/kg} \approx 310.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Total work** (3 stages): $$w_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 310.5 = 931.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Mass flow power** (ideal-gas work): $$P_{\text{indicated}} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{\text{total}} = 0.1667 \times 931.5 = 155.2 \text{ kW}$$ **Shaft power** (accounting for mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$): $$P_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{P_{\text{indicated}}}{\eta_m} = \frac{155.2}{0.88} = 176.4 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{shaft}} \approx 176 \text{ kW per compressor}}$$ For two such compressors (full redundancy), total installed HP compressor power ≈ **352 kW**, consistent with published submarine specs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 4 / HPA Systems** - [ ] **Primary function of HPA system:** Emergency surfacing (MBT blow), pneumatic valve actuation, weapon discharge, and AIP support. - [ ] **Storage pressure range:** 200 – 350 bar (gauge); typical modern value = **300 bar**. - [ ] **Number of banks:** At least 2 (Group I + Group II) for redundancy — never both used simultaneously. - [ ] **Material of flasks:** Steel (magnetic boats) or carbon-fibre-wrapped aluminium-lined (non-magnetic boats like Scorpene). - [ ] **Displaced volume (isothermal):** $V_d = V_f (P_f/P_b - 1)$ — used for **routine blow**. - [ ] **Displaced volume (adiabatic):** $V_d = V_f [(P_f/P_b)^{1/\gamma} - 1]$ — used for **emergency blow**, $\gamma = 1.4$. - [ ] **Back-pressure formula:** $P_b = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g z + \Delta P_{line}$ — **all three terms mandatory**. - [ ] **Seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ (not 1000 kg/m³ — that's fresh water). - [ ] **Adiabatic index for air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (diatomic: N₂ + O₂). - [ ] **HP compressor type:** 3-stage reciprocating, oil-free, water- or PTFE-lubricated. - [ ] **Compressor stage rule:** Use optimum pressure ratio $r_p = (P_{out}/P_{in})^{1/n}$ — minimizes total work. - [ ] **Inter-cooling purpose:** Brings air back to intake temp between stages → reduces work and prevents thermal damage. - [ ] **After-cooler + drier purpose:** Prevents moisture in flasks → prevents corrosion & ice block at blow valves. - [ ] **Emergency Blow Valve (EBV):** Quick-opening, full-flow, normally isolated. Single actuation discharges a full group. - [ ] **Safety factor on flask count:** Typically **1.2 to 1.3** — use 1.25 if not specified. - [ ] **Buoyancy gained:** $\Delta B = \rho_{sw} g V_d$ — independent of depth. - [ ] **Air required:** Increases with depth because $P_b$ increases (deeper = more air needed). - [ ] **Energy stored in 50 L flask at 300 bar:** $\approx 2.4$ MJ (treat as "high energy" hazard class). - [ ] **Pressure hierarchy on board:** 300 bar (HP flask) → 70 bar (weapons) → 8 bar (valve actuation) → ambient (MBT blow). - [ ] **Failure mode mantra:** *"Redundancy, redundancy, redundancy."* Two compressor sets, two flask groups, dual pressure reducers, dual safety relief. - [ ] **Don't forget units:** Convert gauge to absolute for **all** gas-law equations — examiners deduct 1–2 marks per occurrence. - [ ] **Sanity-check numbers:** A real submarine carries 10–14 tonnes of HP air. If your answer is 0.5 tonnes or 500 tonnes, recheck the calculation.

topics.title: Water Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Water Systems" in a Submersible?

In the context of submarine / submersible design, **Water Systems** refer to the integrated network of piping, pumps, valves, tanks, and treatment units that manage every fluid stream on board — from producing drinking water to discharging sewage, from cooling vital machinery to flooding/emptying ballast tanks. They are the **circulatory system** of the vessel, without which no manned or unmanned submersible can operate safely underwater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> *Water Systems on a submersible comprise the fresh water (potable & service), sea water (cooling, ballast, firefighting), sanitary/sewage, trim, drainage, and high-pressure air (for ballast blow) sub-systems, all integrated to support habitability, machinery operation, depth control, and safety.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a submersible as a **small underwater city of ~50–150 people**, sealed inside a steel pressure hull. Just like a city, it needs:

- A **drinking water supply** (potable water plant) — analogous to a municipal water works.
- A **plumbing network** (fresh & salt water distribution) — like household pipes.
- A **sewage treatment & discharge system** — like a municipal STP.
- **Depth control** by flooding/emptying tanks — like a submarine "diving tank."
- **Cooling water** to remove heat from engines & electronics — like a building's HVAC chiller lines.
- **Fire-fighting mains** fed from sea — like industrial hydrants.

In short: **everything that water does in a city, a submersible must do it in a self-contained, pressure-tolerant way.**

### 1.3 Classification of Water Systems (KTU High-Yield Map)

| Sl. | Sub-System | Primary Function | Typical Source / Sink |
|-----|------------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Potable Water System** | Drinking & cooking | RO plant / fresh water tanks |
| 2 | **Service (Wash-Down) Water** | Showers, laundry, deck wash | Fresh water storage |
| 3 | **Sea Water Cooling System** | Cool main motors, electronics, A/C | Direct sea chest → pumps → heat exchangers → overboard |
| 4 | **Ballast System** | Flood/empty to control weight & trim | Sea → ballast tanks → HP air blow overboard |
| 5 | **Trim System** | Adjust fore-aft & athwartships balance | Dedicated trim tanks + pumps |
| 6 | **Sanitary / Sewage System** | Collect & discharge black & grey water | Sanitary tanks → MSD → ejector → overboard |
| 7 | **Fire-Fighting System** | Sea-water hydrants, sprinklers, AFFF | Sea chest → fire pump → mains |
| 8 | **Drainage / Bilge System** | Remove leakage & condensate | Low points → bilge wells → eductor/pump → overboard |
| 9 | **High-Pressure Air (HP Air) System** | Blow ballast & start main engines | HP air bottles → regulators → manifolds |
| 10 | **Desalination (RO) Plant** | Convert sea water to fresh water | Sea water feed → RO membrane → product water |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants you must remember:**
> - Density of sea water: **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
> - Density of fresh water: **ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard submerged specific gravity for ballast: **1.025 t/m³**

### 1.4 Why Water Systems are Critical in Submersibles

A submersible is *completely surrounded* by water, yet must *exclude* water from its interior while *using* the surrounding water for cooling, ballasting, and firefighting. The design challenge is thus:

1. **Pressure differential management** — internal systems at 1 atm vs. external hydrostatic pressure of up to 60+ bar.
2. **Contamination prevention** — sea water must never mix with potable or system water.
3. **Material selection** — cupro-nickel, super-duplex stainless steel, or titanium for sea water piping to resist corrosion.
4. **Redundancy** — military submarines demand 2×100% pumps for every critical system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System-by-System Operational Logic

### A. Potable Water System
**How it works (step by step):**
1. Sea water is drawn through a sea chest (below the light-waterline).
2. It passes through a **straining box / coarse strainer** to remove marine growth.
3. A **boost pump** raises pressure to ~3–5 bar.
4. Water enters the **Reverse Osmosis (RO) plant**, where a semi-permeable membrane rejects ~99% of dissolved salts.
5. Permeate flows to the **product water tank**; brine is discharged overboard.
6. From the product tank, water is **chlorinated / UV-treated** and pumped to galley & dispensers.
7. A separate **fresh water storage tank** holds reserve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule of thumb:** Allocate **15–20 litres/person/day** for drinking & cooking + 80–100 L/person/day for showers/laundry → ≈ **120 L/man-day** total fresh water.

### B. Sea Water Cooling System
1. Sea chest → **sea water cooling pump** (centrifugal).
2. Flow is split into **main cooling loop** (H.E. for lube oil, main motor, A/C condenser) and **auxiliary loop** (electronics, sonar dome cooling).
3. Heated sea water discharges overboard through a **discharge above the waterline** (for surfaced) or a **submerged discharge** (submerged mode).
4. A **temperature control valve (TCV)** regulates flow to keep coolant at ~28–32 °C.

### C. Ballast System
1. **Flooding** – Open sea inlet valve → pump water into main ballast tanks (MBT).
2. **Vent** – Air in the tank is pushed out through the **vent valve / mast / snorkel** to atmosphere.
3. **Blowing (emptying)** – HP air at 30–250 bar is admitted to the tank top, displacing water through the **lower Kingston valve** overboard.
4. **Emergency blow** – All HP air bottles dumped simultaneously → fastest surfacing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite concept:** The volume of water that must be moved to surface a dived boat is governed by **Archimedes' principle** — submarine weight < displaced water volume × ρ_sw × g ⇒ submerges.

### D. Trim System
- Small dedicated tanks at the bow and stern (and port/starboard).
- Moved by **trim pumps** or by gravity through a **trim manifold**.
- Required to maintain **even keel** when weight shifts (e.g., torpedo fired, crew moves).

### E. Sanitary / Sewage System
1. **Black water** (toilets) → **Sanitary Holding Tank (SHT)**.
2. **Grey water** (sinks, showers) → may combine with black water or treated separately.
3. **Marine Sanitation Device (MSD)** – Type II (biological) or Type I (chemical/chlorination) — kills 99% of coliforms.
4. **Discharge pump / eductor** ejects treated effluent overboard (permitted only beyond a regulated distance from shore).

### F. Fire-Fighting System
- **Sea-water fire main** at 7–10 bar pressure.
- Hydrants, AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam) proportioners, CO₂ flooding for machinery spaces.
- Diesel-driven **emergency fire pump** for redundancy.

### G. HP Air System (Ballast Blow)
- **HP air compressors** charge storage bottles to 230–300 bar.
- Reducing valves drop to **intermediate pressure (40–60 bar)** and **low pressure (10–20 bar)**.
- LP air is what actually blows ballast.

### H. Drainage / Bilge System
- Bilge wells at every low point in the pressure hull.
- **Bilge eductor** (no moving parts, uses HP air or sea water motive) preferred for flooded conditions.
- Discharge only if oily-water separator (OWS) indicates <15 ppm oil.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every year.**

### Fundamental Fluid Equations

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \rho \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from depth | Pa |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \, g \, d$ | Absolute pressure at depth $d$ | Pa |
| 3 | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes) | N |
| 4 | $\gamma = \rho \, g$ | Specific weight | N/m³ |
| 5 | $\dot{V} = A \, v$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 6 | $H_p = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) + h_L$ | Pump head (Bernoulli) | m |
| 7 | $h_L = f \, \dfrac{L}{D} \, \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m |
| 8 | $P_{shaft} = \rho g Q H_p / \eta$ | Pump shaft power | W |
| 9 | $Q = A_v \, C_v \, \sqrt{2 g \Delta h}$ | Orifice / valve flow | m³/s |
| 10 | $t_{blow} = \dfrac{V_{MBT} \times P_{LP}}{P_{HP} \times \dot{V}_{HP air}}$ | Approx. ballast blow time | s |

### Ballast & Stability

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|
| 11 | $W_{sub} = W_{light} + W_{ballast} + W_{fwd} - F_B$ | Submerged equilibrium: $W = F_B$ |
| 12 | $\Delta W = \rho_{sw} \, g \, \Delta V$ | Weight change per unit volume of water |
| 13 | $t_{flood} = V_{MBT} / Q_{flood pump}$ | Time to flood ballast |
| 14 | $GM = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{disp}} - BG$ | Metacentric height (stability) |

### Heat-Transfer for Cooling Systems

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|
| 15 | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T = \rho Q c_p \Delta T$ | Heat removed by sea water |
| 16 | $\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_{sw} c_{p,sw} \Delta T_{sw}}$ | Required cooling sea-water flow |
| 17 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Heat-exchanger log-mean temp. difference |

### RO Plant

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|
| 18 | $Q_{permeate} = A_{mem} \times J_w$ | RO product rate ($J_w$ in LMH) |
| 19 | $RR = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ | Recovery ratio |
| 20 | $E_{pump, RO} = \dfrac{Q_f \Delta P_{mem}}{\eta_{pump}}$ | RO high-pressure pump power |

> [!NOTE]
> In all rows, do **not** use the pipe symbol `|` — use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute value is required, to keep markdown tables valid.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval architects** use these equations to size pumps, tanks, and pipes such that the boat can dive in <90 s and surface in <5 min.
- **Marine engineers** use the cooling formulas to choose heat-exchanger area and to verify that sea water flow is sufficient.
- **Public-health / habitability officers** use the fresh-water allocation to certify the boat for 90-day patrols.
- **Environmental compliance officers** enforce discharge limits — directly tying design to OWS and MSD choices.
- **Cost engineering:** RO plants and HP air systems are *very* expensive; a wrong design wastes millions in displacement.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Sea-Water Cooling Flow

**Given:** A submarine's main motor & electronics dissipate $Q = 250 \text{ kW}$ of heat. Sea water enters at $T_{sw,in} = 28^\circ C$ and is allowed to rise by $\Delta T_{sw} = 8^\circ C$ before discharge. $c_{p,sw} = 4.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

**Required:** Mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{sw}$ and volumetric flow rate $Q_{sw}$.

### Step 1 — State the governing equation
The heat absorbed by sea water equals the heat rejected by the equipment:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_{sw} \, c_{p,sw} \, \Delta T_{sw}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for mass flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,sw} \, \Delta T_{sw}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{250{,}000 \text{ W}}{4000 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 8 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{250{,}000}{32{,}000} = 7.8125 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to volumetric flow

$$
Q_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{7.8125}{1025}
$$

$$
Q_{sw} = 7.62 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 27.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

### Step 4 — Sanity check
- Specific heat chosen is $4.0$ kJ/kg·K — appropriate for sea water (slightly higher than fresh water's 4.18).
- Mass flow of ~8 kg/s for 250 kW is **plausible** (typical for a small submersible).

> **Result:** $\dot{m}_{sw} \approx 7.81$ kg/s, $Q_{sw} \approx 27.4$ m³/h. The sea-water cooling pump must be sized to deliver at least this flow at the design head.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ballast Volume Required to Submerge

**Given:** A submersible has light weight $W_L = 1800$ tonnes. At periscope depth, it displaces $V_{surf} = 1850$ m³ (already partly flooded). To fully submerge, displaced volume must increase to $V_{sub} = 1900$ m³. Sea-water density $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Required:** Additional ballast volume to flood, $\Delta V_{MBT}$.

### Step 1 — Apply Archimedes
At the surface:

$$
F_{B,surf} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{surf} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1850
$$

$$
F_{B,surf} = 1.860 \times 10^7 \text{ N} \approx 18.6 \text{ MN}
$$

Buoyancy equals light weight at surface equilibrium: $F_{B,surf} = W_L \cdot g = 1800 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 = 17.66$ MN.

Wait — the surface is *not* in equilibrium here; the boat is **positively buoyant at periscope depth** (which is the normal condition). To submerge, ballast water is added to *increase weight* until weight = displaced buoyancy at the new depth.

### Step 2 — Equate weight with buoyancy at submerged displacement
At full submerged depth:

$$
W_L + \Delta W = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{sub}
$$

$$
\Delta W = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{sub} - W_L \, g
$$

Converting to **mass** of ballast water (divide by g):

$$
\Delta m = \rho_{sw} \, V_{sub} - m_L
$$

$$
\Delta m = 1025 \times 1900 - 1{,}800{,}000
$$

$$
\Delta m = 1{,}947{,}500 - 1{,}800{,}000 = 147{,}500 \text{ kg} = 147.5 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### Step 3 — Volume of ballast water

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{147{,}500}{1025} = 143.9 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 4 — Time required to flood
If the flood pump delivers $Q_{flood} = 60$ m³/h:

$$
t_{flood} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{Q_{flood}} = \dfrac{143.9}{60} = 2.40 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Result:** The submersible must flood approximately **144 m³** of sea water, taking **~2.4 hours** at a 60 m³/h flood rate. (Military boats use **emergency blow via HP air**, but normal dive is gravity flood → much faster if the MBT vents are large enough.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HP Air Required for Emergency Blow

**Given:** One main ballast tank of volume $V_{MBT} = 80$ m³ must be blown empty in $t_{blow} = 120$ s. Tank initial water level $h_1 = 0.95 V_{MBT}$ (95% full). LP air available at $P_{LP} = 8$ bar; HP bottles at $P_{HP} = 250$ bar, each bottle volume $V_b = 0.05$ m³, of which $n = 4$ are dedicated to this blow.

**Required:** Verify if 4 HP bottles are sufficient.

### Step 1 — Air required at LP (using isothermal blow, ideal gas)
For an isothermal blow (submarine blow is *adiabatic* but close enough to isothermal for design):

$$
P_{HP} V_{HP} = P_{LP} V_{LP}
$$

$$
V_{HP, equiv} = \dfrac{P_{LP} \times V_{LP, required}}{P_{HP}}
$$

### Step 2 — Volume of LP air needed
The water displaced is $V_w = 0.95 \times 80 = 76$ m³, and we need enough air at LP to fill the entire 80 m³ space and push water out:

$$
V_{LP} = V_{MBT} \times \dfrac{P_{LP}}{P_{LP}} = 80 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (plus displacement of water = 76 m}^3\text{)}
$$

But tank volume stays at 80 m³, so the *air* volume delivered at LP = 80 m³ (filling the tank as water leaves). So we need **80 m³ of air at 8 bar**.

### Step 3 — Equivalent HP air volume

$$
V_{HP, equiv} = \dfrac{P_{LP} \times V_{LP}}{P_{HP}} = \dfrac{8 \times 80}{250} = 2.56 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 4 — Bottles available
Total HP volume available = $4 \times 0.05 = 0.20$ m³ — this is the **stored gas** at 250 bar.

But **stored gas is already at 250 bar** — the equivalent 250-bar volume we need is 2.56 m³. Available = 0.20 m³ → **insufficient by factor 12.8**.

### Step 5 — Realistic correction
Actually, the air in 4 bottles at 250 bar expands isothermally to 8 bar. The *expansion ratio* is $250/8 = 31.25$. So each 0.05 m³ bottle becomes $0.05 \times 31.25 = 1.5625$ m³ at LP. Four bottles give $4 \times 1.5625 = 6.25$ m³ of LP air. To deliver 80 m³ at LP → still **insufficient** (need ~13 bottles of 50 L each, or larger LP receiver tanks).

> **Engineering Reality:** Submarines therefore carry an **LP air receiver** (typically 4–6 m³ at 8–10 bar) that is *recharged from HP bottles* during the blow. The 4 HP bottles only *top up* the LP receiver; the receiver does most of the work. The blow time of 120 s is **highly optimistic** — actual full emergency blow of an 80 m³ tank is closer to 4–6 minutes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pump Selection Summary

| Application | Typical Head (m) | Typical Flow (m³/h) | Pump Type | Drive |
|-------------|------------------|---------------------|-----------|-------|
| Sea-water cooling | 15–30 | 30–300 | Centrifugal | Electric |
| Ballast flood | 5–15 | 50–200 | Centrifugal / eductor | Electric |
| Trim pump | 10–20 | 5–20 | Centrifugal | Electric |
| Fire main | 70–110 | 60–150 | Centrifugal (multistage) | Electric + diesel backup |
| HP air compressor | 200–300 bar discharge | 50–200 m³/h free air | Reciprocating (multi-stage) | Electric / diesel |
| RO high-pressure pump | 50–70 bar | 5–30 m³/h | Positive-displacement / centrifugal | Electric |
| Sanitary discharge | 5–10 | 2–10 | Positive displacement | Electric |
| Bilge eductor | 10–25 (motive) | 5–20 | Eductor (no moving parts) | HP air / sea water motive |
