# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4]

**List the four major sub-systems of a submersible Environmental Control System and state the physiological limit of CO₂ partial pressure for an 8-hour mission.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major sub-systems are:

1. **Atmosphere control sub-system** — regulates O₂ and CO₂ partial pressures and total pressure.
2. **Thermal control sub-system** — removes metabolic, equipment and hull conduction heat using seawater as the ultimate sink via a refrigeration loop.
3. **Humidity control sub-system** — condenses and removes water vapour; keeps RH in the 30–70 % band.
4. **Air filtration and revitalization sub-system** — removes particulates (HEPA) and trace gases (activated carbon).

**Physiological CO₂ limit for 8 h mission:** **0.005 atm = 5,000 ppm = 0.5 % by volume.**
For an emergency 24-hour exposure, the limit rises to **20,000 ppm (2 %)**. Above 5 %, CO₂ becomes narcotic.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]

**Why is seawater used as the primary heat sink in a submersible ECS instead of ambient air?**

**Model Answer:**

Submersibles operate at depth, surrounded entirely by **seawater at 2–6 °C** which is:
- **Always available**, regardless of depth or mission duration (unlike air).
- **Thermally stable** — large thermal mass, narrow seasonal/diurnal swing.
- **Cold** — at depth, water is colder than internal air, providing a natural heat-pump-driving temperature difference.
- **High heat capacity** (cp ≈ 4.18 kJ/kg·K) and high density allow compact heat exchangers.
- Air is **not available** as a convective medium at depth, and surfacing to dump heat would compromise mission stealth and continuity.

Hence seawater-cooled shell-and-tube heat exchangers, backed by a vapour-compression chiller on a glycol secondary loop, form the standard architecture.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

**A 6-person submersible undertakes a 96-hour submerged mission. Each crew member consumes 0.5 kg of O₂ and produces 0.9 kg of CO₂ per 24 hours. Sweat and respiration add 0.06 kg/h of water vapour per person. Use LiOH (capacity 0.45 kg CO₂ per kg LiOH) for CO₂ scrubbing.**

**Solve in two parts:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the total LiOH mass required, and the O₂ cylinder count if HP cylinders are 50 L water volume, charged to 200 bar. Assume 10 % unusable gas reserve.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compute the total chiller cooling load (sensible + latent) given: 100 W/person metabolic sensible heat, spherical hull of inner radius 3 m with 30 mm steel (k = 50 W/m·K) and 50 mm insulation (k = 0.04 W/m·K), inside 22 °C, seawater outside 4 °C. Take h_fg = 2425 kJ/kg.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Mission totals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2} &= 6 \times 0.5 \times 4 = 12.0 \;\text{kg} \\
m_{CO_2} &= 6 \times 0.9 \times 4 = 21.6 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — LiOH mass**

$$
m_{LiOH} = \frac{21.6}{0.45} = 48.0 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — O₂ in standard volume**

$$
V_{O_2,\text{STP}} = \frac{12.0}{1.4286} = 8.40 \;\text{m}^3 = 8400 \;\text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Gas per cylinder at 200 bar**

$$
V_{\text{cyl, 1 bar}} = 200 \times 50 = 10\,000 \;\text{L} = 10 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Usable volume (90 %)**

$$
V_{\text{usable}} = 0.90 \times 10 = 9.0 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Cylinder count**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{8.40}{9.0} \right\rceil = 1 \;\text{cylinder}
$$

**Answer (a):** LiOH = **48 kg**, O₂ cylinders = **1** (rounded up).

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Sensible metabolic**

$$
Q_s = 6 \times 100 = 600 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Latent from respiration + sweat**

$$
\dot{m}_w = 6 \times 0.06 = 0.36 \;\text{kg/h} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_L = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.425 \times 10^6 = 242.5 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Hull conduction** (per the method in Section 3.3, r = 3.0 m; r_s = 3.030 m; r_i = 3.080 m; A_i = 119.2 m²)

$$
R_{\text{ins}} = \frac{1}{4\pi (0.04)}\left(\frac{1}{3.030} - \frac{1}{3.080}\right) = 0.01066 \;\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{\text{steel}} \approx 5.25 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K/W} \;\;(\text{negligible})
$$

$$
Q_{\text{hull}} = \frac{22 - 4}{0.01067} = 1687 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — Total chiller load**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 600 + 242.5 + 1687 = 2529.5 \;\text{W} \approx 2.53 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Answer (b):** Total chiller capacity = **~2.53 kW** (sensible 90 %, latent 10 %).

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled schematic, describe the operation of a **molecular sieve (Zeolite 13X) pressure-swing CO₂ scrubber** in a submersible ECS. How does it differ in regeneration energy from a LiOH canister?

**(b) [7 marks]** A submersible hull has a leak rate of 0.5 % of internal volume per hour. Internal free volume 200 m³, internal pressure held at 1.02 ATA, ambient seawater pressure 6.0 ATA at 50 m depth. How often must the lung tank pump run if it can deliver 5 m³ of make-up air per stroke?

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Schematic description (textual, for sketch in exam):**

A **PSA molecular sieve scrubber** has two beds (A and B), each containing Zeolite 13X granules. The cycle has four steps per bed:

1. **Adsorption (3–5 min):** Cabin air at ~1 bar is blown through Bed A. CO₂ and H₂O are adsorbed onto the zeolite; clean air exits and returns to the cabin.
2. **Equalization (10 s):** Bed A and Bed B are cross-connected to recover pressure.
3. **Regeneration (3–5 min):** Bed A is exposed to **vacuum (~0.05 bar)** while a small portion of product air is back-flushed through it. The lower partial pressure desorbs CO₂ and H₂O, which are dumped overboard.
4. **Re-pressurization (10 s):** Bed A is brought back to 1 bar with cabin air; meanwhile Bed B is in its adsorption step.

The two beds alternate continuously, giving **uninterrupted CO₂ removal**.

**Comparison with LiOH:**

| Aspect | LiOH Canister | Molecular Sieve (PSA) |
|---|---|---|
| Regeneration | **Single-use, discarded** | **Reused indefinitely** |
| Energy for regen | None (passive) | Moderate (vacuum pump + heater) |
| Weight on long mission | High (consumable) | Low (one-time hardware) |
| CO₂ removal efficiency | > 95 % | 90–95 % |
| Water by-product | Yes (adds humidity) | No (water also adsorbed) |
| Mission duration | Hours to a few days | Days to weeks |

> The PSA scrubber is preferred for missions exceeding ~48 h.

**Answer (a):** A PSA scrubber uses pressure swing over two zeolite beds to alternately adsorb and desorb CO₂. Unlike LiOH, it is regenerable, so for missions longer than ~2 days it carries less total mass.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**

- Leak rate = 0.5 %/h of free volume.
- Free volume V = 200 m³.
- Internal pressure = 1.02 ATA.
- Pump capacity = 5 m³ per stroke.

**Step 1 — Volumetric leak per hour**

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{leak}} = 0.005 \times 200 = 1.0 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Time between pump strokes**

$$
t_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{5.0 \;\text{m}^3}{1.0 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}} = 5 \;\text{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure-equivalent check**

Since the leak is from 1.02 ATA to 6.0 ATA ambient, the leak rate above is conservative; in a real hull with 1.02 ATA internal vs 6 ATA external, leakage is **into the water**, not out, so the lungs must **expel** gas, not ingest it. For an inward leak, lung pressure falls; the regulator must **add** air. The 5-hour frequency is on the **make-up-air** side (oxygen consumed, not leak).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU pitfall:** Many students confuse "leak" direction. At depth, hull pressure is below ambient seawater pressure, so any leak is **inward** — the lungs *lose* pressure, and the **lung tank pump injects** gas. The O₂-consumption correction adds ~0.21 m³/h of additional N₂+CO₂ loss to be made up.

**Corrected make-up including O₂ consumption** (6 crew, 0.84 m³ O₂/day each = 5.04 m³ O₂/day total = 0.21 m³/h; assume 1 O₂ in 4 make-up air):

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{make-up, total}} = 1.00 + 0.21 = 1.21 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

$$
t_{\text{cycle, corrected}} = \frac{5.0}{1.21} = 4.13 \;\text{h}
$$

**Answer (b):** Pump must run **once every ~4.1 hours** (or once every **5 h** if O₂ is replaced by separate cylinder feed rather than by lung pump).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Zones in ECS Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 10 % reserve** in cylinder-count questions — loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing mass-based and volume-based RQ** — RQ is conventionally a volume ratio.
> 3. **Ignoring the contribution of hull conduction** in heat-load problems — losses are dominated by *seawater-side*, not metabolic, heat.
> 4. **Wrong direction of leak** in lung-tank questions — inward leak at depth, outward at surface.
> 5. **Forgetting the regeneration cycle** in PSA scrubber sketches — beds must be drawn in pairs.
> 6. **Not stating partial pressure in kPa** — KTU expects SI units throughout.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 5-Minute Recall Sheet — Read Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- **ECS = Closed-loop life support** — atmosphere + thermal + humidity + filtration.
- **Human O₂ need:** 0.5 kg/day ≈ 0.84 m³/day.
- **Human CO₂ output:** 0.9 kg/day ≈ 0.75 m³/day.
- **CO₂ limit:** 0.5 % (5,000 ppm) for 8 h; 2 % for 24 h emergency.
- **RQ (volume basis):** 0.85 for mixed diet.
- **LiOH stoichiometry:** 1.091 kg LiOH per kg CO₂ (ideal); 2.22 kg LiOH per kg CO₂ (practical 0.45 factor).
- **Soda-lime stoichiometry:** 1.82 kg per kg CO₂.
- **Metabolic heat:** 100 W (rest), 200–300 W (work).
- **Sweat rate:** ~0.06 kg/h per person.
- **h_fg (water):** 2425 kJ/kg.
- **Air cp:** 1.005 kJ/kg·K.
- **Seawater is the cold sink** — chiller always on at depth.
- **Hull heat flow:** Q = ΔT / R_total; R_total ≈ R_insulation (steel negligible).
- **PSA molecular sieve** is regenerable, suited to long missions.
- **LiOH canister** is single-use, suited to short missions and emergency backup.
- **Lung tank** compensates for gas loss: leak + O₂ consumption.
- **Condensation forms** if inner wall temperature falls below dew point — solve by insulation, dehumidification, or vapour barrier.
- **Total pressure** held at 1 ATA ± 0.05; O₂ partial pressure 16–21 kPa.
- **Diagrams must show** two beds for PSA, or a single-pass train for LiOH.
- **Equation forms to memorize:**
  - $m_{O_2} = N \dot{m}_{O_2} t$
  - $m_{LiOH} = m_{CO_2}/0.45$
  - $Q_{\text{hull}} = \Delta T / R_{\text{ins}}$
  - $Q_L = \dot{m}_w \cdot h_{fg}$
  - $V_{\text{cyl, 1 bar}} = P_{\text{fill}} V_{\text{water}}$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Provision for Escape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Provision for Escape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Provision for Escape** in a submersible refers to the integrated set of engineered systems, structural openings (escape trunks/hatches), life-support equipment (escape suits, breathing apparatus, Steinke hoods), and procedural protocols that enable the crew to safely evacuate the vessel to the surface in the event of a catastrophic disablement, flooding, fire, or loss of power, where normal surfacing is impossible.

The "Provision for Escape" is a **mandatory safety subsystem** in the design philosophy of any human-occupied underwater vehicle. It is governed by classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register), naval design standards (e.g., U.S. Navy SUBSAFE), and international maritime conventions (IMO/SOLAS for rescue submersibles).

## 1.2 Key Design Drivers

| Driver | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Disability Survival** | Submersible must remain habitable long enough for crew to escape (e.g., 72–120 hours in some classes). |
| **Escape Depth** | Maximum depth from which escape is practicable, limited by physiological limits. |
| **Crew Size** | Number of personnel the system can evacuate in a single escape cycle or in sequence. |
| **Ascent Time** | Total time from hatch opening to surface arrival. |
| **Buoyancy Assist** | Whether natural or assisted buoyancy is provided to the escaping crew member. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The High-Rise Fire Escape:**
> Think of a submersible like a high-rise apartment building that has lost all its lifts and is on fire. The "Provision for Escape" is the building's fire escape stairwell — a separate, structurally hardened, fire-rated path that the occupants can use when the normal route (elevator = normal surfacing) fails.
>
> In a submersible, the "stairwell" is an **escape trunk** (a vertical shaft from the pressure hull to the sea surface), the "fire-rated door" is a **hatch with sea-pressure resistance**, and the "oxygen mask" is the **escape suit or Steinke hood** that lets the occupant breathe while climbing the shaft (swimming upward through the open ocean).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics used in escape system design:
> - **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar ≈ 10 m seawater (msw) of pressure.**
> - **Standard escape depth limit (untrained personnel with suit): 180 m (SOLAS) / 300 m (trained, suited).**
> - **Maximum practical escape depth (with Steinke hood): ~180 m.**
> - **Lung over-expansion threshold: 2 atm absolute pressure ratio (i.e., 10 msw → 20 msw absolute ascent) must be avoided by exhalation.**
> - **Standard ascent rate: 0.3 m/s (1 ft/s) for trained escape; up to 1.5–2.0 m/s for free ascent.**

## 1.5 Types of Escape Provisions (Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> Three principal modes of escape are used in modern submersible design:
> 1. **Free Ascent Escape** — Crew member exits the hatch and rises unaided by buoyancy. Used at shallow depths (<30–40 msw).
> 2. **Buoyancy-Assisted Escape** — Crew wears an escape suit with an inflatable life-jacket, providing controlled positive buoyancy. Used up to 180–300 msw.
> 3. **Rescue via Remotely Operated/Externally Mated Vehicle** — Crew remains inside the disabled vessel and is rescued by a Deep Submergence Rescue Vehicle (DSRV) that mates to the escape hatch. Used at any operational depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physiological Constraints Governing Escape Design

The human body is a **gas-bearing organism**. As ambient pressure changes during descent and ascent, the gas in the lungs, sinuses, and middle ear must be managed to prevent injury.

### 2.1.1 Boyle's Law (Foundational)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boyle's Law:** At constant temperature, the volume of a fixed mass of gas is inversely proportional to its absolute pressure.
> $$PV = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$
> This single law governs **lung over-expansion injury** during escape ascent and is the reason every escapee must **continuously exhale** during ascent.

### 2.1.2 Lung Over-Expansion Risk

If a diver/submersible occupant free-ascends from depth $d$ msw to the surface while holding a breath of air at ambient pressure $P_d$, the lung volume would expand by a factor of:

$$\frac{V_{\text{surface}}}{V_{\text{depth}}} = \frac{P_d}{P_{\text{atm}}}$$

**Critical limit:** Lung rupture (alveolar over-distension) occurs when the expansion ratio exceeds **~2.0** (i.e., when lung volume would more than double). This sets the **maximum safe free-ascent depth** at approximately **10 msw (1 atm → 2 atm expansion ratio) without exhalation**, or **~30 msw with trained continuous exhalation**.

## 2.2 Escape Trunk — Geometric Design

An escape trunk is a vertical (or near-vertical) cylindrical shaft fitted with:
- An **inner pressure-resistant hatch** (closed during normal operations).
- An **outer equalizing hatch** (opened to sea after equalization).
- **Flooding/venting valves** to allow water to fill/empty the trunk.
- **Ladders, handrails, and buoyancy aids** for the ascending crew member.

### 2.2.1 Trunk Diameter Selection

The minimum internal diameter is set by:
- **Crew member shoulder width + breathing apparatus** (typically **0.6–0.8 m** for a suited individual).
- **Buoyancy of escapee** must not wedge the occupant in the trunk.

$$D_{\text{trunk}} \geq W_{\text{shoulder}} + 2(t_{\text{suit}}) + 2 \cdot c_{\text{clearance}}$$

Where $c_{\text{clearance}} \geq 50$ mm is the safety clearance.

## 2.3 Breathing Gas Calculations

### 2.3.1 Gas Requirement for Buoyancy-Assisted Escape

The escapee needs a breathing gas supply that:
1. **Counteracts CO₂ buildup** (toxicity threshold: **PCO₂ < 0.05 atm / 5% by volume**).
2. **Maintains O₂ partial pressure** (**PO₂ > 0.16 atm** to avoid hypoxia).
3. **Lasts the entire escape transit time** at depth $d$ and ascent rate $v_a$.

The breathing gas volume required (in litres at ambient pressure) for a single escape:

$$V_{\text{gas}} = \dot{V}_{\text{breath}} \times t_{\text{escape}}$$

Where:
- $\dot{V}_{\text{breath}} \approx 30$ L/min (at ambient pressure) for a working/exerting person,
- $t_{\text{escape}} = d / v_a$.

The gas stored in cylinders must be at surface pressure, so the **storage volume** is:

$$V_{\text{storage}} = \frac{V_{\text{gas}} \times P_d}{P_{\text{storage}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Because gas compresses with depth, the *mass* of gas required is the same, but the storage cylinder volume can be modest because gases are highly compressible.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_d = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g d$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $d$ | Pa |
| 2 | $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 10$ msw $\approx 1$ bar | Sea-level atmospheric reference | bar |
| 3 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Boyle's Law — gas compression/expansion | — |
| 4 | $\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{P_1}{P_2}$ | Volume ratio from pressure change | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{\text{exp}} = \frac{P_{\text{depth}}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$ | Lung expansion ratio on free ascent | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t_{\text{escape}} = d / v_a$ | Escape transit time to surface | seconds |
| 7 | $V_{\text{gas}} = \dot{V}_{\text{breath}} \cdot t_{\text{escape}}$ | Total breathing gas consumed (at depth) | litres |
| 8 | $V_{\text{storage}} = \frac{V_{\text{gas}} \cdot P_d}{P_{\text{store}}}$ | On-board cylinder volume required | litres |
| 9 | $D_{\text{trunk}} \geq 0.6$–$0.8$ m | Minimum escape trunk diameter | m |
| 10 | $v_a = 0.3$–$2.0$ m/s | Standard escape ascent rate | m/s |
| 11 | $F_{\text{buoy}} = (\rho_{\text{suit,avg}} - \rho_w) \cdot V_{\text{suit}} \cdot g$ | Net buoyant force on escapee | N |
| 12 | $\text{PCO}_2 < 0.05$ atm | CO₂ toxicity threshold (8-hr exposure) | atm |
| 13 | $\text{PO}_2 \geq 0.16$ atm | Hypoxia threshold | atm |
| 14 | $N_{\text{cycles}} = N_{\text{crew}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Sequential escape time budget | hours |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Submarine Design:** Determines placement of escape hatches along the hull (typically 2 hatches in forward and aft escape trunks for redundancy).
> - **AUV Recovery (Manned Submersibles):** Influences free-flooding trunk layout to ensure no air pocket forms that could impede ascent.
> - **Deep-Sea Rescue:** DSRV mating seats and skirt designs are derived from escape-trunk geometry.
> - **Submarine Lifeguard Systems:** Set the rules for how quickly a rescue vehicle can reach and mate to a disabled boat — typically 2–4 hours in peacetime.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lung Expansion Ratio as a Function of Escape Depth

**Problem:** An escapee free-ascends from a depth of 30 msw. Determine the percentage increase in lung volume if he/she holds their breath throughout the ascent.

**Given:**
- Depth of escape, $d = 30$ msw
- Atmospheric pressure at sea surface, $P_{\text{atm}} = 1$ atm (approx. 10 msw equivalent)
- Hydrostatic pressure gradient: **1 atm per 10 msw**

**Step 1: Compute the absolute pressure at 30 msw depth.**

We use the relation:

$$P_d = P_{\text{atm}} + \frac{d}{10} \text{ atm}$$

Substituting $d = 30$ msw:

$$P_d = 1 + \frac{30}{10} = 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2: Apply Boyle's Law to the lung volume.**

At depth, the lung volume is $V_1$. At the surface, the lung volume is $V_2$. Boyle's Law gives:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

Solving for the volume ratio:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{4 \text{ atm}}{1 \text{ atm}} = 4$$

**Step 3: Convert ratio to percentage increase.**

$$\Delta V \% = \left( \frac{V_2 - V_1}{V_1} \right) \times 100 = (4 - 1) \times 100 = 300\%$$

**Conclusion:**
The lungs would expand to **4 times** their original volume — a **300% increase**. This far exceeds the **~100% (factor of 2)** rupture threshold, and the escapee would almost certainly suffer fatal arterial gas embolism. **Continuous exhalation is mandatory.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Escape Time and Breathing Gas Requirement

**Problem:** A crew member must escape from a disabled submersible at a depth of 180 msw using a Steinke hood. The hood provides a closed breathing environment supplied from a high-pressure cylinder. Calculate the total escape time and the minimum gas volume required at storage pressure of 200 bar.

**Given:**
- Escape depth, $d = 180$ msw
- Ascent rate (buoyancy-assisted, suit + hood), $v_a = 1.0$ m/s
- Breathing rate at ambient pressure, $\dot{V}_{\text{breath}} = 30$ L/min
- Storage pressure, $P_{\text{store}} = 200$ bar

**Step 1: Compute absolute pressure at 180 msw depth.**

$$P_d = 1 + \frac{180}{10} = 19 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2: Compute escape transit time.**

$$t_{\text{escape}} = \frac{d}{v_a} = \frac{180 \text{ m}}{1.0 \text{ m/s}} = 180 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Compute total ambient-pressure gas volume consumed.**

$$V_{\text{gas}} = \dot{V}_{\text{breath}} \times t_{\text{escape}} = 30 \text{ L/min} \times 3 \text{ min} = 90 \text{ L (at ambient pressure)}$$

**Step 4: Convert to storage volume at 200 bar.**

The storage volume $V_{\text{store}}$ is computed using the same Boyle's Law:

$$V_{\text{store}} \cdot P_{\text{store}} = V_{\text{gas}} \cdot P_d$$

Solving for $V_{\text{store}}$:

$$V_{\text{store}} = \frac{V_{\text{gas}} \cdot P_d}{P_{\text{store}}}$$

Note the unit conversion: 19 atm = 19.265 bar (since 1 atm ≈ 1.01325 bar). For simplicity in naval gas calculations, we use 1 atm ≈ 1 bar, so $P_d \approx 19$ bar.

$$V_{\text{store}} = \frac{90 \text{ L} \times 19 \text{ bar}}{200 \text{ bar}} = 8.55 \text{ L}$$

**Conclusion:**
Only **~8.5 litres of cylinder volume at 200 bar** is required to supply the escapee for 3 minutes of breathing at 180 msw. This is the small, compact emergency cylinder built into the Steinke hood or escape suit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Free-Ascent Depth (Lung-Rupture Limited)

**Problem:** Determine the maximum depth from which a free-ascent escape is physiologically safe, assuming the escapee exhales continuously such that the lung volume is allowed to expand by a maximum factor of 2.0.

**Step 1: Set the volume-expansion ratio equal to the rupture threshold.**

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{P_d}{P_{\text{atm}}} = 2.0$$

**Step 2: Solve for the absolute pressure at depth.**

$$P_d = 2.0 \times P_{\text{atm}} = 2.0 \times 1 \text{ atm} = 2 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3: Convert pressure to depth.**

$$d = (P_d - P_{\text{atm}}) \times 10 \text{ msw/atm} = (2 - 1) \times 10 = 10 \text{ msw}$$

**Conclusion:**
The maximum depth from which a **free ascent** (no suit) can be safely performed is approximately **10 msw** (33 fsw). Beyond this, even with continuous exhalation, the physiological strain (oxygen toxicity, narcosis, decompression sickness) makes free ascent unreliable, and a **buoyancy-assisted escape with breathing apparatus** is required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Depth vs. Escape Method

| Depth Range (msw) | Method | Equipment | Risk Profile |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0–10 msw | Free ascent (breath-hold) | None | Lung over-expansion if breath held |
| 10–40 msw | Free ascent with training | Hood with small gas supply | Narcosis, CO₂ build-up |
| 40–180 msw | Buoyancy-assisted ascent | Steinke hood or escape suit | Narcosis (N₂), O₂ toxicity, hypothermia |
| 180–600+ msw | Impossible by crew escape | Mating DSRV / Submarine Rescue Vehicle (SRV) | High-pressure rescue only |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Escape Sequence Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detected] --> B{Is Normal Surfacing Possible?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Normal Surfacing Procedure]
    B -- No --> D[Activate Escape Protocol]
    D --> E[Don Escape Suit and Hood]
    E --> F[Enter Escape Trunk]
    F --> G[Flood Escape Trunk]
    G --> H[Equalize Trunk Pressure with Sea]
    H --> I[Open Outer Sea-Pressure Hatch]
    I --> J[Buoyancy-Assisted Ascent]
    J --> K{Continuous Exhalation Active?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Rise at 0.3 to 1.0 m per s]
    K -- No --> M[Lung Over-Expansion Risk]
    L --> N[Surface and Deploy Beacon]
    M --> O[EMERGENCY - Medical Aid]
    N --> P[Recovery by Surface Vessel]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Escape System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pressure Hull] --> B[Inner Pressure Hatch]
    B --> C[Escape Trunk]
    C --> D[Flooding Valve]
    C --> E[Outer Sea Hatch]
    C --> F[Ladder and Handholds]
    G[Escape Suit Storage Locker] --> A
    H[Steinke Hood] --> A
    I[Emergency Breathing Cylinder 200 bar] --> H
    J[Personal Buoyancy Inflator] --> G
    A --> K[Trunk Pressure Indicator]
    A --> L[Communications and Tracking Beacon]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Escape Mode Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Disable Event]) --> Q1{Depth Less than 10 msw?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Free Breath-Hold Ascent]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Depth 10 to 40 msw?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Free Ascent with Hood]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Depth 40 to 180 msw?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Buoyancy Assisted Steinke Hood]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Depth 180 to 600 msw?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Escape Suit with Thermal Protection]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[No Crew Escape - Await DSRV Rescue]
    R5 --> End([Stay-aboard in Safe Compartment])
    R1 --> End2([Surface])
    R2 --> End2
    R3 --> End2
    R4 --> End2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define "Provision for Escape" as applied to submersible design. List the three principal modes of escape used in modern submersibles.**

**Model Answer:**
Provision for Escape is the integrated set of engineered systems — escape trunks, hatches, escape suits, breathing apparatus, and procedural protocols — that enable the crew to safely evacuate a disabled submersible to the surface when normal surfacing is impossible.

The three principal modes of escape are:
1. **Free Ascent Escape** — unaided buoyancy from shallow depths (<30–40 msw).
2. **Buoyancy-Assisted Escape** — using escape suits or Steinke hoods with inflated life-jacket, for depths up to 180–300 msw.
3. **Stay-aboard and Rescue** — crew remains in a habitable compartment and is rescued by an externally mated DSRV/SRV, used at greater depths.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**State Boyle's Law. Why is continuous exhalation mandatory during escape ascent from a submerged submersible?**

**Model Answer:**
Boyle's Law states that, at constant temperature, the absolute pressure of a fixed mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

Continuous exhalation is mandatory because, as the escapee ascends, the ambient pressure decreases and the air trapped in the lungs expands. Without controlled exhalation, the lung volume can exceed the ~2× elastic limit, causing **alveolar rupture**, **arterial gas embolism**, and potentially fatal injury. Continuous exhalation keeps the lung volume at or below the safe limit.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and operation of a **Steinke Hood** system used for emergency escape from a disabled submersible. Explain the role of buoyancy, breathing gas supply, and exhalation regulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A crew of 4 must escape sequentially from a disabled submersible at a depth of 120 msw. The escape suit provides a controlled ascent rate of 0.8 m/s, and the breathing rate is 25 L/min (at ambient). The on-board emergency cylinder is charged to 200 bar. Calculate:
- (i) The total escape time for all four crew members.
- (ii) The total breathing gas required (in litres, at ambient pressure) for the entire crew.
- (iii) The total cylinder volume required at storage pressure.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**(a) Steinke Hood System — Description:**

A Steinke Hood is a transparent, dome-shaped plastic hood (typically made of **polycarbonate** or **acrylic**) that fits over the head and seals at the neck. The crew member dons the hood while still inside the pressure hull, then enters the escape trunk.

**Key components:**
1. **Hood dome** — transparent, holds an air bubble that allows the occupant to breathe.
2. **Inflatable life-jacket** — provides controlled positive buoyancy at the chest, generating a head-up ascent attitude.
3. **Emergency breathing cylinder** — small, high-pressure (200 bar) cylinder feeding air into the hood bubble to compensate for breathing consumption and depth-pressure changes.
4. **Exhalation valve** — releases excess gas to the surrounding water to prevent over-pressurization of the hood bubble as the ambient pressure drops.
5. **Webbing harness** — secures the hood to the chest and back.

**Operating sequence:**
1. Crew member enters the escape trunk.
2. Trunk is flooded to ambient pressure.
3. Outer sea-hatch is opened.
4. Escapee exits the hatch into open water.
5. The life-jacket inflates, providing 50–80 N of net upward buoyancy.
6. As the escapee rises, the **exhalation valve** vents expanding gas; the **inlet valve** admits fresh gas from the cylinder.
7. **Continuous exhalation** is required to keep the lungs at safe volume.
8. The escapee surfaces and signals the recovery team.

**Sketch (textual description):**
- Hood dome (diameter ~30 cm) on top.
- Inflatable buoyancy collar around chest.
- Webbing harness passing over shoulders.
- Cylinder mounted on chest plate with regulator.
- Inlet and exhalation valves at the bottom of the hood.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Depth, $d = 120$ msw
- Ascent rate, $v_a = 0.8$ m/s
- Breathing rate, $\dot{V}_{\text{breath}} = 25$ L/min (at ambient)
- Storage pressure, $P_{\text{store}} = 200$ bar
- Crew size, $N = 4$
- Sequential escape — one crew member escapes at a time.

**Step 1: Compute absolute pressure at 120 msw.**

$$P_d = 1 + \frac{120}{10} = 13 \text{ atm} \approx 13 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Compute escape time per crew member.**

$$t_1 = \frac{d}{v_a} = \frac{120}{0.8} = 150 \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Total escape time for 4 crew (sequential, single trunk).**

Add a 5-minute hatch cycle time per person (re-flooding, equalization, hatch operation):

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = 2.5 \text{ min} + 5.0 \text{ min} = 7.5 \text{ min per person}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 7.5 = 30 \text{ min}$$

**(i) Total escape time = 30 minutes.**

**Step 4: Breathing gas per person (at ambient pressure).**

$$V_{\text{gas,person}} = 25 \text{ L/min} \times 2.5 \text{ min} = 62.5 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5: Total breathing gas for crew (at ambient pressure).**

$$V_{\text{total,ambient}} = 4 \times 62.5 = 250 \text{ L (at 13 bar)}$$

**(ii) Total breathing gas at ambient = 250 litres.**

**Step 6: Convert to storage volume at 200 bar.**

$$V_{\text{store}} = \frac{V_{\text{total,ambient}} \times P_d}{P_{\text{store}}} = \frac{250 \times 13}{200} = 16.25 \text{ L}$$

**(iii) Total cylinder volume required = 16.25 litres at 200 bar.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Free Ascent Escape**, **Buoyancy-Assisted Escape (Steinke Hood)**, and **Rescue via DSRV (Deep Submergence Rescue Vehicle)** in terms of:
- Maximum operational depth
- Equipment required
- Physiological risk to crew
- Time to reach the surface / safety

**(b) [7 Marks]** A free-ascent escape is attempted from a depth of 20 msw. The escapee accidentally holds their breath throughout the ascent. Calculate:
- (i) The absolute pressure at 20 msw.
- (ii) The expansion ratio of the lung volume.
- (iii) The percentage volume increase.
- (iv) State whether this expansion will cause lung rupture, with justification.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Free Ascent | Steinke Hood | DSRV Rescue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max depth | 10–30 msw | 180 msw (some up to 300 msw) | Full operational depth (up to 600+ msw) |
| Equipment | None (or small hood) | Hood, suit, cylinder, buoyancy | Rescue submersible, surface support ship |
| Physiological risk | High (lung rupture, CO₂) | Moderate (narcosis, hypothermia) | Low (crew remains inside pressure hull) |
| Time to safety | 1–5 minutes (ascent only) | 3–6 minutes (ascent only) | 12–48 hours (mating + transfer) |
| Crew preparation | Minimal | Moderate (hood donning) | None during escape |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Depth, $d = 20$ msw
- Breath held (worst case).

**Step 1: Absolute pressure at 20 msw.**

$$P_d = 1 + \frac{20}{10} = 3 \text{ atm}$$

**(i) Absolute pressure = 3 atm.**

**Step 2: Expansion ratio.**

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{P_d}{P_{\text{atm}}} = \frac{3}{1} = 3$$

**(ii) Expansion ratio = 3.0 (i.e., volume triples).**

**Step 3: Percentage increase.**

$$\Delta V \% = (3 - 1) \times 100 = 200\%$$

**(iii) Percentage increase = 200%.**

**Step 4: Will rupture occur?**

The lung elastic limit is ~2× (i.e., 100% expansion). At 3× (200% expansion), the rupture threshold is **exceeded by a factor of 1.5**. **YES — lung rupture and arterial gas embolism are virtually certain.** The escapee would suffer potentially fatal pulmonary barotrauma. Continuous exhalation is mandatory.

**(iv) Conclusion: Lung rupture will occur with high probability.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** (1 atm ≈ 10 msw; 1 atm ≈ 1.01325 bar) — always show the pressure in both atm and bar.
> 2. **Confusing absolute and gauge pressure** — depth calculations MUST use absolute pressure. Many students mistakenly use 20 msw = 2 bar instead of 3 bar.
> 3. **Ignoring sequential escape time** — in multi-crew problems, students often compute only one person's escape time and forget the hatch cycle time between crew.
> 4. **Omitting the "why" in physiological questions** — it is not enough to compute a number; you must state the consequence (lung rupture, gas embolism, death).
> 5. **Missing the exhalation rule** — the most heavily tested concept in this module; any escape-ascent question without exhalation discussion is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boyle's Law** is the foundation of all escape physiology: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.
- **Continuous exhalation** is mandatory during any escape ascent to prevent lung over-expansion.
- **1 atm ≈ 10 msw** of seawater pressure — memorize this conversion.
- **Free ascent** is safe only to ~10 msw without exhalation; up to 30 msw with trained exhalation.
- **Steinke Hood** is the standard escape device for **40–180 msw**.
- **Escape suits** extend safe escape to **~300 msw** (with thermal and narcotic protection).
- **Beyond 300 msw**, only **DSRV / SRV rescue** is viable; crew must stay aboard in a sealed compartment.
- **Escape trunk diameter** must be ≥ 0.6–0.8 m to accommodate a suited crew member.
- **Trunk flooding and pressure equalization** is the slowest step in escape; design must minimize this time.
- **Each escape cycle** includes: suit donning + trunk entry + flooding + hatch opening + ascent + recovery. Typically **5–10 minutes per crew member**.
- **Total escape time** for an N-person crew is roughly $N \times T_{\text{cycle}}$.
- **Lung rupture threshold** is a 2× volume expansion (i.e., ascent from 2 atm absolute, or 10 msw, with breath held).
- **CO₂ toxicity** limit: PCO₂ < 0.05 atm (5%) for short exposure; hood must vent continuously.
- **Hypoxia** limit: PO₂ > 0.16 atm; hood gas supply must be adequate.
- **Narcosis** (nitrogen) becomes significant beyond 30 msw and incapacitating beyond 60–90 msw — escapees need trained breathing discipline.
- **Differential pressure across an escape hatch** is a major hazard — never open a hatch unless pressures are equalized.
- **Redundancy** in escape provisions is mandatory: **at least 2 escape trunks** on a submarine (forward and aft), each with independent breathing gas supply.
- **Class rules**: DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, and SOLAS all require explicit escape analysis for any human-occupied submersible.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST723 - BASICS OF DESIGN OF SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Systems** in a submersible refer to the integrated network of power sources, distribution networks, conversion equipment, motors, lighting, instrumentation, communication, and control hardware that collectively deliver, regulate, and consume electrical energy to support the vehicle's mission envelope. These systems must operate reliably under extreme hydrostatic pressure (up to **110 MPa** at full ocean depth of **11,000 m**), corrosive saline environment, confined geometry, and strict safety constraints for human-occupied vehicles (HOVs).

The major functional blocks include:

- **Power Generation / Storage Module** (batteries, fuel cells)
- **Power Conversion Module** (DC–DC converters, DC–AC inverters, transformers)
- **Power Distribution Module** (main busbars, circuit breakers, fuses, junction boxes)
- **Propulsion Electrical Module** (thruster motors and drives)
- **Auxiliary Load Module** (lighting, pumps, hydraulics, life-support)
- **Mission Systems Module** (sonar, navigation, communication, cameras, sensors)
- **Emergency & Safety Module** (backup batteries, alarms, blow circuits, ground-fault detection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Students must be able to distinguish between the *prime power source*, *auxiliary power*, and *emergency power* architectures and identify the load categories with their typical demand values for shallow (≤ 300 m), medium (300–2000 m), and deep (> 2000 m) submersibles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a submersible as a **"self-contained underwater spaceship."** Just as an astronaut cannot plug a spacecraft into a wall socket, a submersible must carry its own electricity with it. The electrical system is essentially the **"circulatory system of a human body"** — the *batteries* are the heart (pumping out energy), the *cables* are the arteries and veins (carrying current), the *circuit breakers* are the safety valves, and the *thrusters, lights, sonar, sensors* are the muscles and senses that consume the energy to do useful work.

For an intuitive picture: think of a modern **cordless stick vacuum cleaner**. It has a Li-ion battery, a small inverter driving a brushless DC motor, control electronics, and over-current protection — all sealed inside a slim tube. A submersible is essentially the same architecture, but blown up to **kilowatt scale**, sealed against **110 MPa**, and floating in an environment that can instantly short-circuit any exposed conductor through seawater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Board-Favorite Constants):**
> - Sea-water resistivity: **ρ ≈ 0.20 Ω·m** at 20 °C, **~0.30 Ω·m** at 4 °C (deep ocean)
> - Hydrostatic pressure gradient: **~0.101 MPa per 10 m** of depth (≈ **1 atm / 10 m**)
> - Typical operating voltages in modern submersibles: **120 V DC**, **240 V DC**, or **440 V 3-phase AC**
> - Specific energy targets: Pb–acid ≈ **30–40 Wh/kg**, Ag–Zn ≈ **100–130 Wh/kg**, Li–ion ≈ **150–250 Wh/kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Power Source Hierarchy

The **prime power** source defines the endurance and depth-class of a submersible. Selection criteria: specific energy (Wh/kg), energy density (Wh/L), depth of discharge limit, safety (gas evolution, thermal runaway), and recharge logistics.

**Why each technology matters — the operational logic:**

1. **Lead-Acid Batteries** — Cheapest, robust, but heavy. Evolve **hydrogen gas** during overcharge → mandatory **explosion-proof compartments** and forced ventilation during surface charging. Specific energy ≈ **30–40 Wh/kg**. Used in older HOVs (e.g., *Alvin* pre-2012 upgrade).

2. **Silver–Zinc (Ag–Zn) Batteries** — High specific energy (**~120 Wh/kg**), low self-discharge, excellent for one-shot deep dives. Limitation: only **~50–100 cycles** and high cost. Preferred for military/work-class submersibles.

3. **Lithium-Ion Batteries** — Highest practical specific energy (**150–250 Wh/kg**), thousands of cycles. Critical safety issue: **thermal runaway** under pressure → mandatory Battery Management System (BMS) with cell-level monitoring. Used in modern AUVs (e.g., *Sentry*, *Autosub*) and deep submersibles.

4. **PEM Fuel Cells** — Continuous energy as long as H₂ and O₂ are stored. Specific energy of the *system* can exceed **400 Wh/kg** when cryogenic H₂ is used. Used in long-endurance AUVs (e.g., *URASHIMA*, *Bluefin*).

5. **Nuclear Power** — Used only in deep, long-endurance vehicles (e.g., Soviet *Pisces*-class, US *NR-1*). Practically obsolete for civilian submersibles today.

### 2.2 Electric Motors for Thrusters

Modern submersibles use one of three motor classes:

- **Brushed DC Motors** — Simple speed control, but brush wear limits maintenance intervals. Used in small ROVs.
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors** — High efficiency (**> 90%**), long life, ideal for deep operation. Most common choice in current-generation ROVs/AUVs.
- **Three-Phase AC Induction Motors** — Rugged, lower cost per kW. Used in larger work-class ROVs (e.g., *Schilling* systems).

The mechanical power delivered at the propeller shaft is:

$$P_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi\, n\, T}{60}$$

where $T$ is the torque in N·m, $\omega$ the angular velocity in rad/s, and $n$ the rotational speed in rpm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Utility:** The choice of motor technology directly impacts the *thruster efficiency curve* $\eta(n)$ which feeds back into the **resistance–propulsion balance** and hence into the **mission endurance calculation**. This is a recurring question in KTU ESE papers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Domain of Use |
|---|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P = V \cdot I$ | $P$ [W], $V$ [V], $I$ [A] | DC power, battery sizing |
| 2 | $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | line values, $\cos\phi$ power factor | 3-φ AC distribution |
| 3 | $P_{\text{mech}} = T\,\omega = \dfrac{2\pi n T}{60}$ | $T$ [N·m], $n$ [rpm] | Motor–propeller coupling |
| 4 | $E = P \cdot t$ | $E$ [Wh], $P$ [W], $t$ [h] | Mission energy budget |
| 5 | $C_{\text{Ah}} = \dfrac{E}{V_{\text{bus}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{conv}} \cdot \text{DoD}}$ | $C$ [Ah], $\eta$ efficiency | Battery bank sizing |
| 6 | $V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R = \dfrac{I\,\rho\,L}{A}$ | $\rho$ [Ω·m], $L$ [m], $A$ [m²] | Cable voltage drop |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{\text{drop}}}{V_{\text{bus}}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Design rule: $\Delta V\% \le 3\%$ |
| 8 | $P_{\text{hydro}} = \rho_s g h$ | $\rho_s \approx 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $h$ [m] | Pressure hull / penetrator sizing |
| 9 | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{E}{m_b}$ | [Wh/kg] | Battery technology comparison |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{drive}} \cdot \eta_{\text{prop}}$ | dimensionless | Mission endurance estimate |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oceanographic AUV survey planning** → Battery sizing drives mission profile.
- **Offshore oil & gas ROV inspection** → Power distribution design for **3000 m** rated tethers.
- **Cable joint design** for **sub-sea observatories** (NEPTUNE, India Ocean Network).
- **Defense submarine emergency blow systems** — guaranteed independent electrical power for emergency surfacing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Submersible Battery Bank Sizing for a Given Mission

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A small submersible operates at **500 m** depth with the following average electrical loads during a **6-hour** mission:

- Thruster motors (4 nos.): **1.5 kW** each, **40%** duty cycle
- Lighting (interior + exterior): **600 W**, continuous
- Life-support pumps & O₂ generation: **800 W**, continuous
- Sonar + navigation + control: **500 W**, continuous
- Emergency beacon: **100 W**, continuous
- Conversion / drive losses equivalent to **η_conv = 0.88**, depth-of-discharge limit **DoD = 0.70**, bus voltage **V_bus = 240 V DC**.
- Design with a **safety factor SF = 1.20** on capacity.

**Step 1 — Compute the average electrical load for each subsystem.**

For the thrusters, since duty cycle is **40%** over 6 h, average power is:

$$P_{\text{thr,avg}} \;=\; 4 \times 1.5 \text{ kW} \times 0.40 \;=\; 2.40 \text{ kW}$$

The continuous loads simply add:

$$P_{\text{light}} = 0.60 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{\text{LS}} = 0.80 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{\text{sonar}} = 0.50 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{\text{emerg}} = 0.10 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Total average electrical load (at the bus).**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{load}} &= P_{\text{thr,avg}} + P_{\text{light}} + P_{\text{LS}} + P_{\text{sonar}} + P_{\text{emerg}} \\
&= 2.40 + 0.60 + 0.80 + 0.50 + 0.10 \\
&= 4.40 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total mission energy required at the battery terminals** (divide by converter efficiency, since battery must supply losses too):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{batt}} &= \frac{P_{\text{load}}}{\eta_{\text{conv}}} = \frac{4.40}{0.88} = 5.00 \text{ kW} \\
E_{\text{miss}} &= P_{\text{batt}} \times t = 5.00 \text{ kW} \times 6 \text{ h} = 30.0 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert energy to battery capacity in Ah.**

For a **240 V DC** bus:

$$C_{\text{raw}} = \frac{E_{\text{miss}}}{V_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{240 \text{ V}} = 125 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 5 — Apply depth-of-discharge limit and safety factor.**

We must keep **30%** of capacity in reserve (DoD = 0.70), and add **20%** design margin:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{design}} &= \frac{C_{\text{raw}} \cdot \text{SF}}{\text{DoD}} \\
&= \frac{125 \times 1.20}{0.70} \\
&= \frac{150}{0.70} \\
&= 214.3 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final selection.**

Round up to the next standard rating: **240 Ah, 240 V** battery bank. This corresponds to **N = 40** cells of **6 V, 240 Ah** in series, or alternatively **N = 120** cells of **2 V, 240 Ah** in series (typical vented lead-acid or Li-ion configuration).

**Verification — check that the design point is conservative:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Usable energy} &= 240 \text{ V} \times 240 \text{ Ah} \times 0.70 = 40{,}320 \text{ Wh} \\
\text{Safety margin} &= \frac{40{,}320 - 30{,}000}{30{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 34.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

The ~34% margin (after DoD and SF) is a healthy KTU-acceptable design figure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cable Voltage-Drop Limit for a Thruster Feeder

A **3 kW, 240 V DC** thruster draws at full load:

$$I_{\text{full}} = \frac{P}{V \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}} = \frac{3000}{240 \times 0.92} = 13.59 \text{ A}$$

The feeder cable length (motor controller → thruster) is **35 m**, copper conductor ($\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$). The KTU design rule is **ΔV% ≤ 3%**.

Maximum allowable drop:

$$V_{\text{drop,max}} = 0.03 \times 240 = 7.20 \text{ V}$$

Total loop resistance budget (go + return):

$$R_{\text{max}} = \frac{V_{\text{drop,max}}}{I} = \frac{7.20}{13.59} = 0.530 \;\Omega$$

For a 2-conductor cable of length **2 × 35 m = 70 m**, minimum cross-sectional area:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{min}} &= \frac{\rho \cdot L_{\text{loop}}}{R_{\text{max}}} \\
&= \frac{1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 70}{0.530} \\
&= \frac{1.204 \times 10^{-6}}{0.530} \\
&= 2.27 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \\
&\approx 2.27 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Design decision:** Choose the next standard size, **2.5 mm²** (or for safety, **4 mm²**) to also satisfy current-carrying capacity under pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Submersible Electrical System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[(PRIME POWER SOURCE)] --> BMS[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS]
    BMS --> DCBUS[MAIN DC BUS 240V]
    DCBUS --> DCDC[DC-DC CONVERTER 240V to 48V or 24V]
    DCBUS --> DCAC[DC-AC INVERTER 240V DC to 440V 3-PHASE AC]

    DCAC --> THRC1[THRUSTER 1 BLDC DRIVE]
    DCAC --> THRC2[THRUSTER 2 BLDC DRIVE]
    DCAC --> THRC3[THRUSTER 3 BLDC DRIVE]
    DCAC --> THRC4[THRUSTER 4 BLDC DRIVE]

    DCDC --> AUXL[AUX LOADS LIGHTING PUMPS]
    DCDC --> MSN[MISSION SYSTEMS SONAR NAV CAMERA]
    DCDC --> LS[LIFE SUPPORT O2 SCRUBBER]

    BMS -.BACKUP LINK.-> EMG[(EMERGENCY BATTERY PACK)]
    EMG --> EMR[EMERGENCY BLOW CIRCUIT]
    EMG --> EMB[EMERGENCY BEACON]

    DCBUS --> CB[MAIN CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    DCBUS --> GF[GROUND FAULT MONITOR]
    CB --- GF

    subgraph CONTROL
        PLC[MAIN CONTROLLER PLC]
        PLC --> BMS
        PLC --> DCAC
        PLC --> MSN
    end
```

### 4.2 Power Flow Hierarchy (Load Categorization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prime Source] --> B{Criticality}
    B --> C1[PROPULSION THRUSTERS]
    B --> C2[LIFE SUPPORT]
    B --> C3[MISSION PAYLOAD]
    B --> C4[EMERGENCY LOADS]

    C1 --> D1[Must survive to surfacing]
    C2 --> D2[Must survive full mission]
    C3 --> D3[Sheddable on low battery]
    C4 --> D4[Independent backup bus]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every box labeled *THRCn* represents one independently driven thruster. The **EMG** (emergency) bus is galvanically isolated from the **MAIN DC BUS** so that a fault on the main side cannot disable blow or beacon circuits — a strict **SOLAS-submersible-equivalent** requirement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between *prime power* and *emergency power* in a submersible. Why must the emergency bus be galvanically isolated from the main bus?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Prime power** (1 mark) — The main on-board energy source (typically a rechargeable battery bank or fuel cell) that supplies all mission and propulsion loads for the duration of the dive.
- **Emergency power** (1 mark) — A small, independently housed battery pack dedicated *only* to the emergency blow circuit, locator beacon, and essential alarms. It is sized to operate the vehicle for **at least 72 hours** in a disabled state.
- **Galvanic isolation reason** (1 mark) — Prevents a short circuit, ground fault, or fire on the main bus from propagating to and disabling the emergency circuits. Isolation is normally achieved via a *separate battery, separate cabling, separate circuit breakers, and dedicated penetrators through the pressure hull.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Compare lead-acid and lithium-ion batteries for submersible applications on the basis of (i) specific energy, (ii) cycle life, and (iii) safety concerns.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lead-acid | Lithium-ion |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Specific energy | **30–40 Wh/kg** | **150–250 Wh/kg** |
| Cycle life | **300–500 cycles** | **1000–3000 cycles** |
| Safety concerns | **H₂ evolution** during overcharge → explosion risk; electrolyte spillage | **Thermal runaway** under abuse or pressure → fire; requires BMS |

Each correct row → **1 mark**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A — Battery Sizing for a Scientific Dive
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

> A scientific submersible must perform an **8-hour** dive at 1500 m depth. The estimated load profile is:
>
> - Four thrusters @ **1.8 kW** each, **30%** duty cycle
> - Interior + exterior LED lighting: **500 W** continuous
> - Life-support (O₂, CO₂ scrubber, pumps): **900 W** continuous
> - Multibeam sonar + INS + cameras: **750 W** continuous
> - Emergency beacon: **120 W** continuous
>
> The main DC bus is **300 V**, conversion efficiency of motor drives = **0.90**, depth-of-discharge = **0.65**, and a safety factor of **1.15** is required. Determine the **required battery capacity in Ah** and the **number of 2 V cells** in series.

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

**(a) [7 marks] Compute average power demand and mission energy.**

Average thruster power:

$$P_{\text{thr,avg}} = 4 \times 1.8 \times 0.30 = 2.16 \text{ kW}$$

Total bus power:

$$P_{\text{load}} = 2.16 + 0.50 + 0.90 + 0.75 + 0.12 = 4.43 \text{ kW}$$

Battery-terminal power:

$$P_{\text{batt}} = \frac{4.43}{0.90} = 4.92 \text{ kW}$$

Mission energy:

$$E = 4.92 \times 8 = 39.36 \text{ kWh}$$

**(b) [7 marks] Convert to capacity and size the battery bank.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{raw}} &= \frac{E}{V_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{39{,}360 \text{ Wh}}{300 \text{ V}} = 131.2 \text{ Ah} \\
C_{\text{design}} &= \frac{131.2 \times 1.15}{0.65} = \frac{150.88}{0.65} = 232.1 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}$$

Round up to next standard rating: **240 Ah, 300 V bank**.

Number of 2 V cells in series:

$$N = \frac{300 \text{ V}}{2 \text{ V}} = 150 \text{ cells}$$

**Final answer: 150 cells of 2 V, 240 Ah in series.** Energy stored = **72 kWh**, providing ~**45%** real safety margin.

---

#### Question B — Cable Voltage-Drop Design
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

> The **forward horizontal thruster** of an ROV is rated **4 kW, 240 V DC**, with motor efficiency **0.93**. The thruster is mounted **18 m** from the motor controller using a copper cable. KTU design rules mandate **ΔV ≤ 3%**.
>
> (a) Compute the **full-load current** and the **maximum allowable loop resistance**.
> (b) Determine the **minimum copper cross-sectional area** required, and select a **standard conductor size** from {1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.0, 10.0} mm².

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

Full-load current:

$$I = \frac{P}{V \cdot \eta} = \frac{4000}{240 \times 0.93} = 17.92 \text{ A}$$

Maximum drop:

$$V_{\text{drop}} = 0.03 \times 240 = 7.20 \text{ V}$$

Maximum loop resistance:

$$R_{\max} = \frac{V_{\text{drop}}}{I} = \frac{7.20}{17.92} = 0.4018 \;\Omega$$

**(b) [7 marks]**

Loop length (go + return): $L = 2 \times 18 = 36$ m.

For copper ($\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot$m):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\min} &= \frac{\rho \cdot L}{R_{\max}} = \frac{1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 36}{0.4018} \\
&= \frac{6.192 \times 10^{-7}}{0.4018} \\
&= 1.541 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \\
&\approx 1.54 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Next standard size above **1.54 mm²** is **2.5 mm²**.

**Selection: 2.5 mm² copper**, 2-core armoured submersible cable, oil-resistant and pressure-rated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often forget the **return conductor** and use single-run length in the voltage-drop formula → **lose 2 marks** every time. Always use $L_{\text{loop}} = 2 \times L_{\text{one-way}}$.
> - Battery sizing: students forget to **divide by $\eta_{\text{conv}}$** when converting load energy to battery-terminal energy. Common 2-mark deduction.
> - Always state the **bus voltage explicitly** in the answer; examiners specifically award 1 mark for naming it.
> - Round **up**, never down, when selecting the standard cell or cable size.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Prime vs. Emergency power** must be galvanically isolated; emergency bus powers blow circuit + beacon only.
- ✅ **Specific energy** ordering: Pb–acid < Ni–Cd < Ag–Zn < Li–ion < PEM fuel cell < nuclear.
- ✅ **Battery sizing formula:** $C_{\text{Ah}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{load}}\,t}{V_{\text{bus}}\,\eta_{\text{conv}}\,\text{DoD}} \cdot \text{SF}$
- ✅ **Cable sizing formula:** $A_{\min} = \dfrac{\rho\,L_{\text{loop}}\,I}{\Delta V_{\text{allow}}}$
- ✅ **Design rule of thumb:** $\Delta V \le 3\%$ for power feeders, $\le 5\%$ for lighting, $\le 10\%$ for electronics.
- ✅ **Three-phase AC power:** $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$.
- ✅ **Motor mechanical power:** $P = T\omega = \dfrac{2\pi n T}{60}$.
- ✅ **Pressure rule:** $P_{\text{hydro}} = \rho_s g h$; **1 atm per 10 m**.
- ✅ **Modern submersible bus voltages:** **120 / 240 / 300 / 440 V DC** — higher voltage → lower current → thinner cables.
- ✅ **Always round-up** standard sizes for cells (Ah) and cables (mm²).
- ✅ **Safety hierarchy:** Propulsion > Life-support > Mission payload > Emergency systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
